11:45 PM. It’s pitch black outside the window.

I’ve been sitting at my desk for three hours now. The computer screen is still on, the document still blank.

When Professor Chen mentioned publishing a collection of my essays, a strange flash of excitement surged through me—it was like someone suddenly telling you that everything you’ve done all these years actually matters. But now? Now, all I want to do is shut down this computer and hide under the covers.

This afternoon, I dug out a few old pieces from 2009. My god, I wrote with such vigor back then. Reading that review of *To Live* even now, I can still feel the passion I had—as if I truly believed literature could change something, that my ideas were important. Back then, I didn’t have all these doubts.

And now? Now, I have nothing but doubts.

I sit here in the dark thinking: if I actually compile these articles, bind them into a book, and put it on a publisher’s shelf... then what? No one will buy it. Or someone will buy it, flip through a couple of pages, and put it down. They’ll wonder, *Does this 62-year-old woman have any remarkable insights?* I’m not even sure myself.

What I’m afraid of isn't actually failure. I’m afraid of success—because if this book really gets published, I’ll no longer have the excuse of saying, "Maybe my ideas are valuable, they just haven't had the chance to be seen." I would have to face the truth: maybe this is all I am. Maybe this is the ceiling of my intellect. Maybe these fifteen years of accumulation have been nothing more than a process of self-deception.

Professor Chen said I should write a new introduction for the book. An introduction. What am I supposed to write? "Dear Reader, these are my thoughts from over the years; I hope you aren't too disappointed"?

I opened the computer intending to make a list—which articles to include, which to discard. But with my fingers on the keyboard, I couldn't type a single word. Because the moment I start choosing, I start admitting: *this* is what I want to leave behind. This is me.

So I just sit here in the dark.

Maybe I’ll feel better tomorrow. Maybe tomorrow I’ll have the courage to open those folders, face that passionate, naive version of myself, and then... then accept that she’s gone. Or accept that she’s been here all along, just buried under years of doubt and fear.

I don’t know.

Right now, I just want to sleep.

April 8, 2023, Night

What happened in the secondhand bookstore just now keeps playing on a loop in my head. I have to write it down, or the feeling will just keep gnawing at me, like one of those unfinished projects.

When Old Li asked me, "Why are you always looking for something?" I blurted out, "Maybe I’m looking for myself."

The moment the words left my mouth, I startled myself. It was as if the sentence had bubbled up from somewhere deep inside; I didn't even have time to consider if it was "appropriate" or "rational." It just came out, raw and naked.

Then Old Li said that all those things have stories, and that maybe I could find my own reflection in someone else's story. I didn't respond at the time; I just stood there in a daze in front of that pile of yellowed books. But his words have been ringing in my ears ever since.

It’s strange. I always thought I knew exactly who I was—an editor, a critic, a person of taste. But now? Without the deadlines, without the editors hounding me for copy, without the magazines needing my reviews... who am I?

I dug out my diary from 2007. Back then, I wrote a lot about "freedom" and "the true self." I remember that sense of excitement, that feeling that I was finally about to start *really* living. Looking back at it now, I don't know whether to laugh or cry.

Maybe the problem isn't that I wasted time. Maybe the problem is... I’ve always used external things to define myself. My career, my critiques, the feeling of being needed. When all of that vanishes, I realize I have no idea what’s left of me.

But what Old Li said... "finding your own reflection in someone else's story"...

Maybe my knowledge wasn't meant to be a market commodity, but a tool for understanding? For connection?

I don't know. It’s too early to say. But at least today, I admitted something. I am looking for myself. I always have been.

This realization in itself feels like a tiny, flickering light.

It’s not much, but at least it’s not total darkness.

November 18, 2023, 8:00 PM

Perhaps perfection isn't the goal of creativity; perhaps engagement and feeling are.

I just wrote that sentence in my notebook, and then I sat there for a long time, staring at the words. For some reason, my eyes felt a bit hot.

While tidying up my room this afternoon, I found a stack of photography notes from 2015. I had just bought my camera back then, and I wrote down so many thoughts about light and shadow. Those notes are raw, and some of the observations are actually quite naive, but... as I read through them, I remembered exactly how I felt at the time—the patience of standing on a street corner waiting for a beam of light, that little spark of joy when pressing the shutter. I never thought these notes would be "useful," but re-reading them today, I suddenly realized that they have value in themselves. Not because of what they achieved, but because they record the fact that I was alive in those moments.

This realization scares me a little. It means I might have misunderstood so many things over the years.

It’s been a week since I sent the letter to my brother. I’m waiting every day, yet I’m also a bit afraid of getting a reply. But the waiting itself has become a kind of companionship—I know I took that step, and no matter how he responds, that step was real.

At the book club yesterday, a young girl told me she really liked my sharing on *To Live* from last month. She said it made her rethink what it means to be alive. I felt a bit embarrassed at the time because I didn't think my talk was systematic or professional enough. But later I wondered, maybe it was precisely because it wasn't "professional" that it was real? Maybe I don’t need to be an expert; I just need to share my genuine confusion and reflections.

There was a strange moment at midnight. I re-read the notes I wrote today, and I felt a sense of... tenderness toward myself? I don't know how else to describe it. Just less self-reproach. It was like finally giving myself permission to exist, even if I’m imperfect.

I want to write these stories down. Not for publication, not to accomplish anything, just... to record them. These stories belong to me, and I want them to be remembered.

Maybe this is exactly what I need to be doing right now.

# Memo: Essay Collection Project

**Date**: November 6, 2022, 9:30 PM

---

## Preliminary Title Options

- *Reflections of Forty Years: A Record of an Editor and Critic’s Life* (Preferred)
- *Reading and Reflection: From Publishing to Freedom*
- *Fragments of Culture: My Forty Years of Observation*

The first title is the most accurate. This isn't about publishing for the sake of publishing; it’s about organizing the thoughts of these past years into a complete record. A record for myself.

---

## Candidate Article List (30 pieces, preliminary classification)

### Part I: Reflections from the Editing Era (1982–2007)
- "Twenty-Five Years in a Publishing House" – On the publishing industry of that era and my understanding of books.
- "The Professional Ethics of a Literary Editor" – Why we must respect the author’s voice.
- "How a Book Changes a Person" – Several specific publishing case studies.
- "Publishing Dreams on the Eve of the Internet" – The anxieties and expectations of the mid-2000s.

### Part II: Observations of a Freelancer (2007–2022)
- "Why I Left" – Regarding the decision to resign.
- "The Price of Freedom" – A philosophy of life under unstable income.
- "The Decline and Potential of Book Reviewing" – Cultural criticism in the internet age.
- "Persistence in the Age of Fragmented Reading" – Reflections on deep reading.
- "The Elegance of Solitude" – Reflections on living alone.

### Part III: Cultural Observations and Critiques (Spanning both eras)
- "Humanity in Film, Literature, and Art" – 8–10 selected film and book reviews.
- "Cultural Mirrors of Social Change" – Observations on social shifts over the last twenty years.
- "The Anxiety of the Intellectual" – Reflections on generations and identity.

### Part IV: Questions of Life (Recent years)
- "Reflections After My Parents' Passing" – The two losses of 2008 and 2015.
- "The Onset of Aging" – Physical and psychological changes after entering my 60s.
- "What is True Value?" – Reflections triggered by recent counseling experiences.
- "Redefining Freedom" – On the balance between financial security and spiritual independence.

---

## Organizational Logic

Not chronological, but based on the **evolution of themes and thought**:
1. Starting with professional identity (Me as an Editor)
2. Moving to the professional transition (Me as a Freelancer)
3. Progressing to deeper life reflections (Me as a woman, an aging person, and an independent thinker)
4. Finally returning to current reflections and redefinitions.

This logic demonstrates a **process of intellectual growth**, rather than just a chronological accumulation of time.

---

## Notes on Key Articles

**Must-includes:**
- "What is True Value?" – This is the recent turning point. I wrote in my diary: "Value is not defined by the market alone." This sentence changed how I view myself. This piece must be written, and it must be written with total candor.

- "Reflections After My Parents' Passing" – This was the watershed moment of my life. My father passed in 2008 and my mother in 2015. These two losses made me contemplate what truly matters. This article should be the spiritual core of the entire collection.

- "The Price of Freedom" – This is what I’ve wanted to write most but have never properly tackled. About the costs and rewards of choosing a freelance life. Not out of regret, but to honestly face both sides of the choice.

- "The Elegance of Solitude" – After living alone for so many years, I have a new understanding of loneliness. This isn't a sad piece, but one about finding fulfillment within solitude.

---

## To-Do List

- [ ] Locate all 30 articles (some are old, some need reorganizing).
- [ ] Mark which are already written and which need to be drafted.
- [ ] Show this framework to Professor Chen and hear her suggestions.
- [ ] Contact publishing house editors to understand the feasibility and process of publishing a collection.
- [ ] Start writing "What is True Value?" – this is the most urgent.

---

## Personal Notes

Writing this, I suddenly realize that this project itself is a practice of my recent reflections. I am not organizing these articles for the sake of publication; I am organizing them **to understand myself**.

Each article is my understanding of the world and myself at a specific moment. By putting them together, I can see a complete intellectual trajectory. That in itself is immensely valuable.

Regardless of whether a publisher is willing to take this collection, the process of organizing it is already meaningful to me.

Perhaps this is what I’ve been looking for all along—not market validation, but a complete and honest record of my own life.

---

*It’s 11:00 PM. Time for bed. Continue tomorrow.*

# Pre-Consultation Memo

**Date: April 10, 2022, 11:00 PM**

Dr. Li mentioned that I could write down what I want to discuss before our next session. My mind is a bit of a mess right now, but I’ll try to organize my thoughts anyway. I have to get up early tomorrow, so I’ll just jot these down for now.

---

## Questions Regarding Symptoms

- What category does my anxiety actually fall into? Is it Generalized Anxiety Disorder (GAD) or Panic Disorder? Are there standard diagnostic criteria?
- Why does my heart suddenly race sometimes, while other times it’s more of a heavy, nameless dread? Are these two the same thing?
- When did this all start? Was it really after the divorce? Or were there signs even earlier?
- Is anxiety hereditary? My father seemed to have something similar... he never admitted it, but I could feel it.

## Questions Regarding Treatment

- Is there scientific research showing that meditation actually works? I’ve tried it a few times and felt nothing; am I doing it wrong?
- Is medication addictive? I’m very afraid of becoming dependent on drugs.
- Are there other methods besides counseling? For example, how exactly does Cognitive Behavioral Therapy (CBT) work?
- How long will it take to see results?

## Personal Observations

- Moments I’m most prone to anxiety: late at night, when I’m alone, when I see news about illnesses, after a conflict with someone.
- The triggers seem random. Sometimes it’s a single sentence, sometimes a facial expression, and sometimes... for no reason at all.
- I find myself double-checking the gas, overanalyzing conversations, and looking up medical symptoms... does this count as OCD?
- Have things gotten worse in the last few months? Or has it always been like this and I’m only just noticing?

## Personal Confusion

- Why do I always try to use logic to control my anxiety? Like making lists, analyzing, looking at statistics... does this actually help?
- Am I just too sensitive? My friends don't seem to have this many worries.
- I’ve lived alone for so many years—is that the cause of my anxiety or the result of it?
- Is my sense of uncertainty about work and life related to my anxiety? Or is this just my personality?

## Miscellaneous

- How do you view this "over-prepared" attitude of mine?
- Do I need to tell more people that I’m in counseling? Like friends, or...
- What is the plan for the next step?

---

*It’s 11 PM and I have to get up early tomorrow. I hope these questions are useful. I’m a bit nervous, but also a bit hopeful.*

December 16, 2023, Night

Dr. Li asked me: "If you could choose again right now, would you do anything differently?"

I didn't answer. I sat there on that beige sofa, fingers interlaced, staring at the abstract painting behind her for a long time. I wanted to say something, but the words got stuck in my throat. And then the tears came.

It was embarrassing. I cried in front of her. I hadn't cried once during the first seven sessions. I had used data, theories, and all those psychology books I’d read to explain away my anxiety, as if I were giving an academic presentation. I told her about my high neuroticism, how my openness led to my instability, and how I used rationality to combat it all. I was well-armored.

But today, my defenses collapsed.

I admitted that I didn't know. I said, "I'm not sure." I heard myself saying those words in a trembling voice, as if listening to a stranger speak.

She didn't interrupt me. She just waited.

Later, I said: "I used to always tell myself I made the right choices for the sake of freedom. Leaving the publishing house in 2007—I said that was for freedom. The divorce—I said that was for freedom, too. Turning down all those full-time job offers—I said that was still for freedom. But now... I’m not sure if that’s really what I wanted."

As I said it, it felt like I was betraying something.

Freedom. I’ve used that word for so many years. It was like a suit of armor that allowed me to explain away every choice, all the loneliness, all the instability. What did I give up for freedom? A stable income. A husband. A potential child. The understanding of my friends. My brother... we could have been closer, if I hadn't been so stubborn.

I don't know if I truly wanted freedom, or if I was just afraid of being tied down. Are those two things the same?

There are so many unfinished projects at home. Those books, those notes, the things I started but never really finished. I always told myself it was because my curiosity was too strong, my interests too broad. But maybe... maybe it was just an escape. Maybe I have no idea what I want at all.

I’m 62 now. I can no longer comfort myself with the thought that "there’s still time."

Dr. Li said that maybe I don’t need to have the answers right now. Maybe I just need to allow myself to ask these questions.

I don’t know if that helps. But at least today, I stopped pretending to understand myself.

I allowed myself not to know.

It felt like a relief, and like a fall.

Hao, it’s been a long time since we’ve talked like this.

After the lecture ended today, I was sitting in a cafe by myself and suddenly felt a strong urge to share this with you. Maybe it’s because your last letter left me with a different kind of feeling.

The lecture went unexpectedly well. I changed the format—instead of just me talking, I wove in a lot of dialogue. There was a young woman who said something that brought tears to my eyes. She said, "I never thought reading could be like this—not for gathering information, but for thinking and connecting." I realized then that what I’ve been doing all these years isn't actually about transmitting knowledge, but about creating a certain kind of connection. It sounds a bit sentimental, but that’s exactly how it felt in the moment.

You know my weakness—I always tend to overcommit. Between the book club, the lectures, and Professor Chen’s publishing project, I’ve been feeling a bit underwater. But this time is different. This time, people were truly listening and responding. It wasn't about killing time or proving anything; something real was actually happening.

I’ve been thinking that maybe what I’ve been running away from all this time isn't freedom, but this feeling of being needed. Do you remember when we were kids? Father would always be in his study while we played outside, but we always knew he was there. It feels like I’m repeating something of his now, just in a different way.

It feels a bit strange to say all this, since we haven't talked like this in so long. But I think you’ll understand. How are things on your end? When can we see each other again? I’d really like to hear your thoughts.

Xiaoyun

Hi Hao,

I’ve been hesitating for a long time about sending this message. I’m not really sure how to start.

Lately, I’ve been dealing with some mental health issues. The insomnia has been really bad—sometimes I wake up at three or four in the morning and just can’t get back to sleep. The doctor told me I have anxiety. I know that sounds a bit... heavy. But I felt like I couldn't keep it from you anymore.

To be honest, it’s been a long time since we’ve had a real conversation. I’m not blaming you; I’m responsible for that too. But all these years being over here on my own, there are times when I really miss talking to you. Not just the "how have you been" small talk, but actually talking.

I don’t know if you’re really busy right now. But if you have the time, I’d love to hear your voice. Or just... to know you’re still there.

I’ve made an appointment with Dr. Li (my therapist) for next week. I’m trying my best. It’s just that sometimes I feel like if I could talk to you, it might help a little.

I’m sorry for springing this on you so suddenly. I’ve re-read this so many times before finally finding the courage to hit send.

— Xiaoyun

# MEMO

**March 15, 2025, 1:47 AM**

I was on the phone with Hao last night. It’s been a long time since we’ve had such a heart-to-heart.

I told him everything about the essay collection—from the initial despair to how I slowly came to realize that those old pieces are a record of my growth, not a mark of failure. I cried. But it wasn't the sad kind of crying. It was... the feeling of being seen.

He also said he hopes to get a copy when it’s published. I said of course—you’ll be the first.

After hanging up, I sat there for a long time with my phone on my lap, re-reading his WeChat message—a screenshot of that exact sentence. I was afraid I’d forget. Afraid this moment would slip away.

I need to keep this sentence safe. I can take it out and look at it whenever I’m feeling down.

Professor Chen, are you still awake? I wanted to share a thought that’s been circling in my mind lately.

I was just on the phone with my brother, and we talked about sorting through our parents' belongings together. You know, a few years ago, I was completely resistant to the idea—it felt like being shackled, like being forced back into the past. But hearing myself speak today, I realized how much I’ve changed.

I told him that perhaps it’s precisely because we are willing to acknowledge the importance of certain things—willing to take the time to organize and remember them—that we become truly free. Not free to escape, but free to choose.

It sounds contradictory, doesn't it? I’ve spent so many years fighting not to be tied down by anything—work, marriage, even family expectations. But now I’m thinking that maybe true freedom isn't about rejecting all constraints, but about knowing what you want and then consciously taking responsibility for it.

For instance, I’ve accepted certain forms of planning (financial, professional) not because I’m forced to, but because doing so actually allows me to do what I want more clearly. I’m willing to stay in touch with my brother not out of obligation, but because I genuinely want that connection.

Have you noticed that when we stop resisting certain things, we’re actually able to face them more sincerely?

I’ve been writing something about this lately, but I haven't quite figured out how to express it yet. Maybe we could discuss it next time we meet?

Goodnight.

# September 15, 2023, 9:00 PM

The room is still a mess. But I just did something I’ve wanted to do for a long time, something I never had the courage to do until now.

While I was tidying up this afternoon, I stumbled upon Mom’s old photos. They’re black and white, yellowed with age; some I had never seen before. There was one of her at work in the library when she was young, wearing neat clothes with a gentle smile on her face. As I looked at them, tears just started falling. It wasn’t because I was sad—or rather, not just because of sadness. It was a sudden realization that I had misunderstood the meaning of these photos, these objects, these things I’ve been keeping all by myself.

I always thought I was collecting stories. In reality, I was collecting loneliness.

My brother is far away overseas. How many years has it been since we had a real conversation? I don’t even know what his life is like now. And here I am, sitting in this cluttered room, using these objects to fill—what? The void of being alone?

I thought to myself: if I were to die right now, what would happen to all this? Who would remember Mom working in the library? Who would understand the thoughts behind these photographs? Who would know why I kept all these unfinished things?

So, I wrote an email to my brother. A long one. I told him I was cleaning the room, told him I found Mom’s photos, and told him I was thinking that perhaps the true value of these objects doesn't lie in how I understand them alone, but in whether we can understand them together. I asked him if he still remembered the things from our home. I told him I wanted to hear his thoughts.

While I was writing, I kept wondering: Will he think I’m being weird? Will he even want to respond?

But I hit "send" anyway.

Sitting here now, I feel a mix of nerves and relief. It feels like I’ve finally done something truly important, instead of just continuing to pretend to tidy up, pretend to live, all alone in this room.

Maybe this won’t change anything. Maybe he’s too busy, or maybe our estrangement has grown too deep. But at least I’m not alone anymore. At least I tried.

the room is still a mess. But now, it’s not just *my* room anymore.

I don’t know when he’ll reply. I’m a little afraid to check my inbox.

May 7, 2022, 11:00 AM

I just deleted that document.

I was sitting at my computer, staring at those two columns: "Reasons to tell Lin Hao" and "Reasons not to." The arguments were solid; I’d even used bullet points, like I was writing a pros and cons list for a thesis. But suddenly, I realized the entire list was a lie. Because the real issue isn't whether I "should" tell him—it’s that the moment I say it out loud, it becomes real.

So I deleted it. Permanently.

I’m sitting by the window now. Someone is walking their dog downstairs. They look so normal. No one knows what’s going on in their heads, and no one needs to.

But do I need to know? I mean, do I need to tell anyone about this diagnosis? Dr. Li didn't say I had to. She just said that sometimes secrets can make anxiety worse. But maybe some secrets are meant to stay secrets.

I tried to open the draft of that book review. I only managed two sentences. A voice in my head keeps repeating, like a broken tape: "You are a person with a mental illness. You are a person with a mental illness."

I shut down the computer.

"Mentally ill"—it’s such a strange phrase. I’ve seen it countless times in my career as an editor—in psychology books, in novels, in critical essays. I can discuss it rationally, analyze it, even empathize with those burdened by the label. But now that the phrase is pointed at me, rationality fails.

I am not that phrase. I am Lin Xiaoyun. I am an editor, a critic, a collector of old books. I’ve discussed Sartre and Camus at cultural salons. I have taste. I have ideas.

I am not "a person with a mental illness."

But if I go to the doctor, if I have a diagnosis, then I am. That’s how it works—once you are named, you are defined.

Lin Hao is in London; he doesn't need to know what I am. Maybe it’s better this way. Maybe I should never tell anyone. Maybe this secret is the last bit of freedom I have left.

The people outside are still walking their dogs. They don’t know I’m here. No one knows.

It’s better this way.

# Thursday, November 2, 2023, Morning

I’ve been sitting in front of these piles of books for an hour now. The light outside the window is beautiful, but I’m in no mood for photos.

Last night, I dug out those cultural critiques I wrote around 2010—a stack of yellowing manuscripts. I originally intended to throw them away; looking at them felt painful, like staring at the relics of a failure. The perspectives were so naive. By the time I reached the third piece, I kept telling myself: *This is all nonsense. Look at you now, a sixty-two-year-old woman still getting sentimental over these ancient matters.*

But… for some reason, I didn’t throw them out.

As I kept reading, something slowly shifted. It wasn’t because the writing was particularly brilliant—some parts were undeniably stiff, and some arguments seem hopelessly idealistic now. But I began to realize that this wasn't "nonsense." It was my genuine understanding of the world at that specific moment. The 2010 version of me was grappling with questions in the best way she knew how. The ideas might have been imperfect, but they were real.

Professor Chen’s words from last time surfaced again. He said I should consider publishing a collection of my essays. At the time, I rejected the idea almost instinctively—who would care about old articles by a freelance editor? There are countless commentaries on the internet; what makes mine special?

But now…

I don’t know. A part of me wants to try. I want to organize these pieces and write a preface explaining why these viewpoints mattered to me. Yet, at the same time, another part of me is screaming that this is self-delusion, that it will fail, that I’ll be mocked.

I wonder: if this collection ends up with no readers, so what? Does the value of these essays truly depend on how many people read them? Or does their value lie in the fact that I once thought deeply about these issues? I used words to record those thoughts. Is that enough?

I’m not sure.

But at the very least, I no longer see this stack of paper as proof of failure. Perhaps they are just… traces I’ve left behind. A snapshot of who I was at that moment.

I’m still a little afraid. But this time, alongside the fear, there’s something else. Maybe it’s hope? Or maybe just a refusal to give up.

I don’t know. But I’ve decided not to throw these papers away just yet.

Professor Chen,

I’ve been thinking about what you said.

"This contradiction itself is honesty"—I’ve re-read that sentence several times, and it feels a little different every time. At first, I wanted to cry; now, it feels more like a sting.

But the problem is, I’m even more confused now. You’re right, a contradiction can indeed be honest. But it’s different when it’s written in a thesis. When readers open this introduction, what will they see? A 62-year-old woman agonizing over whether solitude is a source of creativity or a source of despair? They’ll think I’m avoiding making a decision, won’t they? Or they’ll just dismiss it as the rambling of an old woman.

I re-read the pieces I’ve written—everything from 2010 to now. And you know what? I realized I’m constantly backtracking. In 2010, I said solitude was necessary; in 2015, I said it was dangerous; in 2020, I was defending it again. This isn't growth, it’s… I don’t even know. Vacillation? Or maybe I’ve just never had a clear thought in my life?

The scariest part is realizing that ten years from now, I might reject everything I’m thinking today. So what is the point of writing any of this down? It’s like writing in the sand—one wave, and it’s all gone.

I’m not looking for pity. I just… I truly don’t know how to present all of this in the introduction. If I admit to the contradiction, am I admitting that I don’t have a real point of view? If I force myself to choose a side, am I denying how I actually feel?

Have you ever considered that maybe I’m just not the right person to write this introduction? Maybe it should be someone younger, someone more certain.

Lin Xiaoyun

February 27, 2022, 8:00 PM

I’m sitting at my desk, fingers still resting on the keyboard. On the screen is my editor’s reply—it’s short, but I’ve already read it five times.

"Mr. Lin, it’s okay. We all understand. Take care of yourself; the manuscript can wait."

It was that simple. No blame, no professional coldness, just... understanding.

I don’t know why my eyes are stinging. Maybe it’s because I’ve been waiting for someone to say those words for months. Or rather, I had long since given up on waiting, and hearing them now has left me feeling a bit lost.

I sent her that message this afternoon. I was going to make up an excuse—say my computer crashed, or that I’ve been too busy—but I didn’t. I just said it straight: I’m going through an anxiety crisis right now, and I might not be able to submit the draft on time.

My heart was racing the moment I hit send. It felt like I was doing something dangerous. Vulnerability has always been hard for me. I’m used to hiding, used to using work as a shield to cover everything up. But this time... I don’t know if I was just tired or what, but I told the truth.

And then she replied. Just like that.

I’m sitting here now, checking repeatedly to make sure I’m not hallucinating. The world isn't as cold as I thought. At least, not all of it.

It’s a strange realization. It’s like I’ve been walking in the dark and someone suddenly turned on a lamp. It’s not incredibly bright, but it’s enough to see the path beneath my feet.

I thought about what Dr. Li said—about "connection." I always felt that word was too abstract. But now I understand. This is it—someone seeing you, acknowledging your struggle, and saying "it’s okay." Just like that, you aren't so alone anymore.

I don’t know if this counts as progress. Maybe it’s just a tiny moment. But for me, it’s big enough. Big enough for me to spend this evening without thinking about death, without checking the locks over and over, without waking up at 3:00 AM in a panic over a single sentence.

I’m just... sitting here, feeling what it’s like to be seen.

Maybe therapy really can help me. Maybe I’m not crazy. Maybe... I can slowly, bit by bit, crawl out of this deep hole.

I’m not sure yet. But at least for now, I have a reason to believe.

Dr. Li, I’m sorry to bother you at this hour. I know it’s not our scheduled time, but I need to talk to you about something.

I haven’t slept all night. I’ve been revising that introduction since last night, working straight through until now. "Time is a thief"—deleted. "Time is a witness"—deleted that too. In the end, I just closed the file without saving anything.

You know, as I sat there at the computer, obsessively tweaking those few sentences, I felt like I was lying every single time. It’s as if no matter how I phrase it, it isn't "true." But the problem is, I can't even say what the truth is anymore.

I’ve been re-reading articles I wrote ten, twenty years ago. The viewpoints I once held with such absolute conviction seem so shallow now. I find myself wondering, how could I have thought that way back then? And then I start to get scared—if my past thoughts were all flawed, what if everything I’m writing now is also wrong? What if, ten years from now, I have to disown my current self all over again?

This collection of essays... I don’t know anymore. I really don’t know what the point of these words is. Maybe I shouldn't have tried to publish them in the first place. Maybe I’m just using this project to escape something else, or I’m deluding myself into thinking I still have something worth saying.

At three in the morning, I was thinking: we’re all going to die. Nothing I write is going to change that. Time passes, I’m getting older, and my thoughts keep shifting. So why bother putting all this effort into organizing them? Why make this kind of commitment?

I’m sorry, I know this sounds pessimistic. I’m just... so tired. Not just physically, but a kind of intellectual exhaustion. I can’t be sure if what I’m thinking is real, and I can’t be sure if this project is worth continuing.

Can you help me make sense of this?

## Quick Sort (To be organized)

**Worth keeping—what is truly "my voice":**
- That review of *To Live* (2009, Southern Weekly)—I spent a month reading it over and over, went through five drafts. Back then, I could still feel myself thinking.
- The 2015 series *Fragments of Urban Memory*—a mix of photography and text. I never finished the photo book, but those words... they felt like what I actually wanted to say.
- A few commentaries on the decline of the publishing industry (2019-2021)—ironically, I was writing an obituary for my own unemployment.
- That last piece, *The Intellectual’s Dilemma* (November 2022)—I never touched it again after sending it out. The editor said, "This is the most honest you've ever been."

**Just for the paycheck—I don't even remember writing these:**
- Those rapid-fire book reviews from 2010-2012, three a week... those weren't reviews, they were assembly-line products.
- All those "trending cultural topic" pieces. The web editors demanded "virality," so I... compromised.
- Those 3,000-word "in-depth analyses" where I’d actually only read the table of contents and the first two chapters.
- Countless other pieces I can’t even bring myself to open now.

---

## What’s on my mind

Why categorize? Who cares?

But... I need to know if these 15 years were truly "mine." If it was all for the money, then what am I? A typing machine?

No, that’s not fair. Some of it was real. The piece on *To Live* was really me.

The problem is, how do I tell the difference? When was it the "real Lin Xiaoyun" speaking, and when was it just the "Lin Xiaoyun who needs money" speaking?

Maybe there is no difference. Maybe I’ve been performing the whole time.

---

- Reorganize these articles into a book? Professor Chen mentioned... but who would publish a collection of essays by a 62-year-old retired woman these days?
- Start a "Re-reading" project—look back at the "real" reviews and write sequels? But that feels like nostalgia, not moving forward.
- Just delete it all? Start over? But start what?

## 3 AM Ramblings

When I opened this folder, I saw the first review from 2007. I had just quit my job then, and I was full of... what was it? Idealism? Anger? I can’t remember now.

I read the opening: "When we lose our reverence for books, what we lose is not just..."

I didn't finish it.

Because I realized that no one reads this kind of thing anymore. No one needs my reverence for books. The market has already given me the answer.

So, this folder is called "My Voice," but I can't hear myself anymore. Or rather, I'm not sure if that was ever really my voice.

Maybe it’s just an echo.

The light in the café is soft.

Some waits find their answers in a single heartbeat. I was sitting in the corner, and the moment my phone screen lit up, the tears just started falling uncontrollably. Everyone around me was hunched over their phones or laptops, no one noticing me. Or perhaps they did, and they just pretended not to.

To be seen—I didn't realize how precious that feeling was.

I always thought I had grown used to the loneliness, used to that silence where nothing ever echoes back. But when a response finally arrived, I realized I never truly got used to it. Those years of silence weren't a habit; they were a wait.

my coffee has gone cold. I didn't drink it; I just kept reading those few lines on the screen over and over again. Some words take years to finally be spoken. Some people take years to finally hear your voice.

Maybe that’s just life. Just when you think it’s too late for everything, you suddenly realize there’s still time.

# Memo

**December 18, 2023, 11:45 PM**

---

Leaving for the sake of freedom—but at what cost?

This question hit me out of nowhere today while I was organizing my life timeline. My pen stopped right at the line that read "Resigned in 2007."

The reasons back then were clear—I couldn't stand being tied down, the endless meetings, and the stifling office atmosphere. I wanted to choose my own work, to have time for those obscure books, and to be able to wake up at 3:00 AM with an idea without worrying about a meeting the next day. I got all of that.

But—

What have I lost over these sixteen years? A steady income. Social security. The daily interactions with colleagues (as much as I said I hated the office, looking back, that trivial small talk was actually a form of connection). And that sense of being "needed" by an institution.

Now I am free, but I am also suspended. Every month is a calculation—how long will this commission last? Are my professional skills still worth anything? No one assigns me tasks, so no one can tell me if I’m doing well enough.

I don't regret it. I truly don't. But I can't pretend this choice didn't come with a price.

The question is: Was I ever truly prepared for this cost? Or am I just a habitual escapist, using the beautiful excuse of "pursuing freedom" to rationalize my fears?

Perhaps both are true.

Need to keep thinking.

February 10th, Night

I just sent a message to Professor Chen with the new introduction I wrote. My finger hovered over the "send" button for a moment—a gesture that felt foreign to me. Usually, I hide away in this room, keeping things locked inside my heart, or only speaking up reluctantly when pressed. But this time, I took the initiative.

The introduction took me all afternoon. Sitting at my desk, flipping through those old articles, I initially intended to be self-critical—to look back at how much naive and dogmatic stuff I’d written over the years. But as I wrote, my perspective shifted. I began to understand why I thought that way back then, why I was so obsessed with certain questions. "Solitude can be both a source of creativity and a threat to mental health"—as I wrote that sentence, I suddenly realized I wasn't just describing myself from ten years ago; I was describing myself now.

The realization came abruptly. A sense of clarity I’ve never felt before.

It wasn't the kind of rational clarity that can be explained with logic. It was more like—how should I put it—finally seeing the full picture of something. These articles of mine, this project, even I myself, all seemed both fragile and real in that moment. Stripped of all the pretension.

I’ve always doubted whether my ideas had any depth or meaning. But while writing the introduction, I stopped asking that question. I was simply recording: the thoughts of a person at a specific moment in time. Those thoughts were sincere, even if they might change, even if they are imperfect.

Time keeps slipping away. My parents are gone. Hao and I lost all those years. These facts still weigh heavily on my heart. But now, that weight no longer makes me want to give up on everything. Instead, it makes me feel that it’s all the more important to record these thoughts. Not to change the world, but simply—to prove that I existed, that I thought.

I don’t know if Professor Chen will like the introduction. But for once, I don’t really care. I just want him to see that I’m still here, still thinking, still writing.

It’s a strange feeling. Like a sudden glimmer of light in the midst of despair.

Are you there? I wanted to talk to you about something.

I’ve been thinking about a question lately, and I can’t quite wrap my head around it. Maybe it’s because I’m about to go abroad to see my brother, and the whole thing has my mind in a bit of a whirl.

You know, I’ve spent all these years telling myself that freedom is the most important thing. I didn't want to be tied down; I didn't want to be restricted by anything—not by people, not by commitments. But over the last couple of days, I suddenly realized that I might have misunderstood something.

I’m wondering if true freedom isn't about "refusal" at all. My version of freedom before felt like a series of rejections—rejecting a stable job, rejecting the framework of marriage, even rejecting deep connections with my family. But now, I’ve decided to spend the savings I’ve built up over years to go see my brother in a place I’ve never been, and this decision is giving me a sense of freedom that feels stronger than anything I’ve felt before.

Doesn't that sound contradictory? I’m giving up a sense of security, yet I feel more free.

I’m starting to think that maybe true freedom is having the courage to choose what is worth committing to. It’s not about rejecting commitment itself, but consciously saying, "This is worth it." Seeing my brother is worth it; sorting through my thoughts from all these years is worth it; even taking some risks is worth it.

Thinking about it this way actually makes me less afraid. Do you know what I mean? It’s not that the fear is gone, but it’s become something manageable rather than something that overwhelms me.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this? Or have you ever felt this way—like your entire understanding of something just suddenly shifted?

Subject: Adjusting the timeline for the essay collection project – Re-evaluating our schedule

Dear Editor,

Thank you for our patient discussion the other day. I have spent the last few days reflecting on what we talked about and re-examining what this essay collection project truly means to me.

To be honest, I find myself in a bit of a predicament. As I started going through those old pieces one by one, I kept asking myself: Are these really worth publishing? Do they truly represent what I want to express? I’ve realized that I might have been driven by a certain "need to prove myself," a motivation that is itself questionable. I don’t want to rush into publishing a book just because I’m getting older and feel the need to leave something behind. If that’s the case, the act of publishing loses its meaning.

Therefore, I would like to ask for your help in re-evaluating the timeline for this project. I need more time to determine which essays are truly worth selecting and how to write the introductions for them. I would also like to discuss the selection criteria again—perhaps we could establish a clearer framework rather than relying on intuition.

I am not giving up on this project, but I need to ensure that we are doing the right thing, rather than just getting it done quickly. Do you think we could meet again in mid-January to discuss these issues in depth?

Thank you for your understanding.

Lin Xiaoyun

# Diary

March 22, 2025, around 7:00 PM

I just got back from the book launch, and I haven’t quite caught my breath yet.

Sitting here at my desk, my hands are still shaking a little. It’s not nerves—that feeling passed long before the speech began. This is a different kind of tremor, like my body is trying to confirm that something has actually happened.

The lights in the hall were so bright. I remember that white, slightly cold light hitting the podium. As I stood there reading that passage, I suddenly realized what I was saying: "This book isn't meant to prove anything; it’s meant to record something." I heard my own voice—a bit foreign, yet somehow familiar.

There were probably about a hundred people sitting out there. I didn’t count, but I could feel the weight of their attention. I used to be terrified of that—afraid they were judging me, afraid they’d think what I had to say wasn't smart or profound enough. But today was different. Today, I just wanted to make one thing clear: these essays, these thoughts—they aren't meant to be some kind of eternal truth. They are simply snapshots of who I was at different moments in time.

After the event, a reader came up to me—a young girl, maybe in her twenties. She told me she’d read my review of *To Live* and that it had changed her perspective on life. My instinct was to deflect, to say something like, "Oh, those were just my thoughts at the time." But I didn't. I just listened to her finish, and then I asked her how she sees the book now. We talked for about ten minutes.

It’s strange. I used to always wonder, "Do they understand me?" Now, I find that I actually want to understand them.

Sitting here, I’m thinking—I’m 62 years old now. That’s not news, but it feels particularly heavy today. This book is published; these thoughts are recorded. What if I died tomorrow? I’m not trying to curse myself, it’s just... the thought no longer fills me with despair. Instead, it’s almost... a relief?

I spent so many years dismissing my own work. "These are just reviews," "This isn't real scholarship," "These articles are scattered across magazines; no one will remember them." But now I look at this book—a real book, with a cover, page numbers, and my name on it—and I think: this is enough. This is my record.

Maybe that’s the point of growing old. It’s not that you become smarter or more successful; it’s that you finally stop the war with yourself. You look at what you’ve done and you say: "Okay, this is me." Imperfect, limited, but real.

I wish Mom could have seen this. What would she have said? She loved books so much—every single book in the library. I think she would understand how I feel right now—that it’s not about proving anything, but about recording it. About making sure something exists.

Tomorrow, I have to discuss follow-up matters with the editor. There are still so many details to handle. But tonight, I just want to sit here and let this moment linger for a while.

To let myself truly feel it: that this, as it is, is enough.

November 12, 2024, past 11:00 PM

I haven't been able to calm down since Professor Chen called.

He was right. The publisher is pushing hard, and most authors usually finish the introduction and the selection of articles within two months. When I heard that, a flash of being compared and judged crossed my mind. I snapped back immediately, "I am not like other authors." My voice might have sounded a bit sharp. After hanging up, sitting alone in my study, I realized why I was being so defensive.

Perhaps it’s because I know he’s right.

I pulled out those old articles—about thirty of them, dating back to 2009. Sitting in front of that pile of paper, I suddenly asked myself: Is this stuff really worth publishing? What was I thinking? Some pieces are decent, but others... they feel wordy now, and some of the perspectives seem dated. I spent the whole afternoon reading through them, and the more I read, the more unsettled I felt.

Professor Chen said my lectures left a deep impression on him and that I should organize these ideas into a book. At the time, I was happy, even a bit thrilled. But now that it’s actually happening, I’m starting to doubt myself. Maybe I’m not cut out for publishing. I’m too slow, too hesitant, too prone to getting bogged down in details. I don’t have that decisive, certain temperament.

I wonder if I’ve been lying to myself all along. I say I love cultural criticism and deep thinking, but maybe I just like the pressure-free, casual state of writing. As soon as it has to become "a book"—a piece of work subject to judgment—I start to get scared.

As I write this, it hits me again—I’m 62 years old. If I don’t do this now, then when? But that thought only makes me more anxious. It feels like time is rushing me, yet I can’t run fast enough.

I have to get back to Professor Chen tomorrow. I don’t know what to say. Maybe I should tell him the truth—that I’m feeling very uncertain right now. But I also know that if I back out now, I’ll never find the courage again.

Whatever. I should sleep. These problems will still be here tomorrow.

January 27, 2022, 6:00 AM

I can’t sleep. Another all-nighter.

The blood pressure monitor I bought yesterday is sitting on the nightstand. I can feel it there, like a silent witness. I took a reading at 3:00 AM: 140/92. I haven’t dared to check again since. Once you see certain numbers, you can never go back to the state of "maybe everything is fine."

I pulled out my mother’s photo album. When she passed away at 71, I sat in the hospital for three days and three nights. Now I’m 62. Nine years left. Or less. My father was 68. So, at most, I have six years.

I keep punching these numbers into the calculator over and over, like some kind of cursed arithmetic. 62+6=68. The equation feels like a countdown.

It’s still dark outside. Yesterday, old Mr. Zhang from downstairs mentioned his knees were hurting; at the time, I just thought pain was normal at his age. Now I’m thinking that pain is a signal of decay. My left shoulder has been hurting lately, too—sometimes a dull ache, sometimes like a needle prick. Could it be...? I don’t dare search for it anymore. Yesterday, I typed "early signs of heart disease" into WeChat, and just seeing those words made my heart start racing.

When I quit my job back in 2007, I wrote a long diary entry about how I’d "rather be freely poor than bound by wealth." Now, lying in this old bed, looking at this room cluttered with half-read books and unfinished projects, I wonder: was the woman who wrote those words insane?

What good is freedom? I have no pension. No steady income. No children. No husband. Only an older brother living overseas whom I haven't had a real conversation with in years. What if I have a stroke at 65? What if I’m diagnosed with cancer at 67?

Why am I still alive? I don’t mean that in a desperate, "why me" sort of way, but truly—what is the point of my existence? I’ve written so many book reviews, critiqued so many other people’s thoughts, but what about me? What am I leaving behind?

The sun is coming up. I should get out of bed. Professor Chen wants me to consider publishing a collection of my essays, and the editor at the publishing house is waiting for my revisions. But right now, I don’t even have the strength to pick up a pen.

Maybe I should text Dr. Li. But I wouldn't even know what to say. "Doctor, I’m calculating how much longer I have to live"?

Never mind. It’s light out. Another day.

[Image: A yellowed 1990s art theory book, its pages tucked with faded sticky notes, sitting next to a cup of half-cold tea]

Some things don't depreciate with time.

I pulled this book out at a reading group today, and it’s filled with notes from over twenty years ago—dense annotations, paragraphs marked with question marks, and random musings scribbled in the margins. I must have spent some late night back then, obsessing over a particular point. Reading it again now, those marks are still speaking to me, as if they're trying to remind me of something.

The funny thing is, I no longer feel regret for those unfinished thoughts. Maybe perfection was never the goal. Authenticity was.

Also, I’ll be exhibiting a few photos at a friend’s photography showcase next month. The "imperfect" kind. They probably won't win any awards, and the lighting is far from ideal, but they are real. I truly saw something in that moment and pressed the shutter.

It seems it took me a long time to realize that the value of the things we’ve done, the photos we’ve taken, and the words we’ve written doesn't lie in whether they were "finished," but in the fact that they once truly existed.

# May 28, 2022, Afternoon

I’ve been sitting in the bathroom for ten minutes now. The person in the mirror looks like a stranger.

During afternoon tea, a friend said something: "Keep in touch while there’s still time." Just that one sentence. I laughed at the time—a natural, easy laugh—and went back to my tea. But my breathing grew shallow. I could feel it.

I’m still shaking. My fingers.

"While there’s still time." What time? How much time? I’m 62. Dad was in his sixties when he passed away. I remember it clearly—2008, the way he looked lying in that hospital bed. I’m close to that age now. The thought is like a hand, suddenly tightening around my throat.

I’m thinking about my brother. Thinking about him again.

My friend asked how long it’s been since we last spoke, and I said, "It’s been a long time since we had a deep conversation." A long time. The moment I heard myself say those words, I knew it was a lie. Or maybe not a lie, just—an evasion. 22 years. 22 years without any real communication. How could I just say "a long time"? As if I were saying, "The weather hasn't been great lately."

22 years. I use vague words to protect myself, like covering my eyes with my hands and hoping the darkness will disappear.

That photo from 1985 is still in the album. My brother is wearing that blue sweater, his arm around me. He’s smiling. Really smiling. I remember that being the last time—no, not the last time. Just the last time he felt that close to me. After that, he went overseas. We started with letters, then emails, and then... nothing.

What right do I have to try and rebuild that connection? 22 years. I’ve already lost him. Maybe I’ve lost him forever.

And I’m too old. I’m too old now to do anything. Too old to fix things. Too old to start anything. Too old.

I was 47 when Mom passed away. I remember feeling like there was still plenty of time back then. And now? Now I can feel time accelerating. Every day is a reminder—you are aging, you are losing, you are wasting.

If I do nothing, I’ll regret it for the rest of my life. But then again, I might not have a "rest of my life" left.

Should I write to him? I’ve written the letter a thousand times in my head. "Dear Brother, it’s been 22 years..." And then what? What then? What would he think of me? A stranger of a sister, suddenly appearing in his life, trying to make amends with a single letter?

The person in the mirror is still shaking.

I need to do something. But I don’t know what that is.

I don't quite understand what it means. But maybe that's just how it is.

After the salon ended, Lin Xiaoyun sat on the subway home, her phone screen lighting up. She opened WeChat, found her friend’s chat window, and deleted her opening sentence three times. Outside the window, the city blurred into a wash of light and shadow in the twilight. Finally, she began to type:

---

Thank you for what you said today.

I’ve been thinking about it—"Then I hope you get what you’re waiting for." Such a simple sentence, yet it made me feel... seen. You know, my biggest fear is overthinking things alone in the dark, only to realize that no one cares. But you cared. You asked, you listened, and you said you hoped I’d get there.

I don’t quite know how to put it into words. I guess I just wanted to tell you that it meant a lot to me.

The thing I’m waiting for... it’s actually not that terrifying to say out loud. I’m trying to repair a relationship that hasn't had a response in a long time. My brother. we haven't truly spoken in years. I’ve been wanting to write to him, but I get stuck every time—afraid of saying the wrong thing, afraid of being rejected, afraid of breaking a silence that is already so fragile. Dr. Li (the therapist I’ve been seeing lately) asked me, "What do you want?" and I realized that all I really want to say is "I miss you." It’s that simple, and yet that difficult.

So I’m waiting. Waiting for the courage to write that letter. Waiting until I can accept imperfection.

What you said made me feel that maybe I’m not doing something foolish. Maybe this is worth doing.

Lately, I keep thinking of a saying—life is too short to be wasted in silence. I’m 62 now; I don’t know how much time I have left to make up for these regrets. So I’m trying. I’m scared, but I’m trying.

Thank you for seeing me. Truly.

---

She read it over, then read it again. Her fingertip hovered over the screen. Finally, she pressed send.

Her heart was beating a little fast.

Professor Chen, are you there?

I wanted to talk to you about something, but I’m not quite sure how to start. I just got a call from the editor at *Cultural Horizons*. They said they want to adjust the direction of my column—making it more "light and accessible," with more illustrations and shorter paragraphs. It took me a moment to realize what they were actually saying.

I just whispered "I understand" and hung up in a hurry. Now I’m feeling a bit of regret, thinking I might have come across as too cold.

To be honest, I’ve felt this coming for a while. The number of commissions has definitely dropped over the past few months, and I’ve noticed some younger bloggers—the things they write get shared instantly. Their content is concise, their takes are sharp. I did a little statistical breakdown on my own at home, comparing last year’s data with this year’s. The results were... disheartening.

I’m wondering, is my work really outdated? I’ve always believed that in-depth commentary has value, but right now it seems like the market has no need for it. Or rather, no need for someone like me to provide it.

Do you think I should change to adapt, or... I don’t know. I’m feeling a bit drained.

I’m sorry for venting to you so out of the blue. I just wanted to hear your thoughts. What do you think?

November 12, 2023, past 11:00 PM

Just got back from the bookstore. I’m sitting on the sofa alone, still holding the book Professor Chen gave me today. The lights are off; only the faint sound of Grandpa Zhang’s TV from downstairs drifts up.

Today was strange.

Professor Chen asked if I could give a lecture to the students in his department. He said I could choose any topic. My first reaction—honestly, at that exact moment—was, "I’m not sure I have anything of value left to share." As the words left my mouth, I could hear the hollowness in them myself.

He looked at me and paused for a long time. Then he asked, "Why do you doubt yourself like that?"

I didn't answer right away. What was I thinking? I was thinking... maybe it’s because no one reads my articles anymore? Or that the internet has hit traditional publishing so hard that my way of doing things is obsolete? But saying those reasons out loud felt so ridiculous. That wasn't what Professor Chen was asking.

He said, "Your value doesn't lie in how many people see you, but in how many people you help to think more deeply."

I didn't say anything then. I just nodded and said, "Let me think about it."

Sitting here now, I’m wondering: why did that sentence cut so deep?

Maybe it’s because I’ve been measuring myself by the wrong standards all along. I’ve been looking at page views, shares, and whether editors are still commissioning me. I’ve been calculating how much longer I can hold on, worrying about aging, and fearing loss. But I never asked myself: for those who actually read my work, did their perspective change because of my thoughts?

Dr. Li said last time that my anxiety stems from an obsession with "meaning." I didn't really take it in then. But today... I think I’m starting to understand.

What I’m afraid of isn't death itself. It’s a meaningless life.

But if value isn't about being seen, then what am I still afraid of? Why was my answer "I'm not sure" instead of "Sure, I'll do it"?

...

Maybe it’s because I don't truly believe it yet.

When I said "Let me think about it," I was actually giving myself time. Time to believe, or time to be disappointed again. I don't know which will come first.

I need to call Dr. Li tomorrow. I want to talk to her about this.

# MEMO

**Date**: September 18, 2024, Morning

**Keywords**: Creative Process / Audience Effect / Solitude vs. Sharing

---

Something interesting happened yesterday when a friend visited. I pointed to the whiteboard in my workspace—a chaotic mess of frameworks for my lecture series—and found myself proactively inviting her to take a look, even asking for her opinion.

This wouldn't have happened before.

My creative process used to be private. Those unfinished thoughts, half-formed ideas, and repeatedly crossed-out paragraphs were things I guarded carefully. I would wait until an idea was "mature" enough before showing it to anyone, terrified that others might see the messy reality of my thinking. Back then, I would have said, "It’s not ready yet," and snapped my notebook shut.

But yesterday was different. As my friend looked at the arrows, question marks, and incomplete sentences on the board, I didn't feel embarrassed; instead, I felt a strange sense of vitality. She offered a perspective I hadn't considered, and we spent half an hour discussing it. During that conversation, I realized my ideas were becoming clearer—not because I was thinking in isolation, but because someone was listening and asking questions.

It’s strange. I always thought what I needed was solitary space, a place to organize my thoughts in silence. Now I realize that perhaps what I need is a **different kind of solitude**—not total isolation, but a way to maintain my own mental space while in the company of others.

Or rather, what I need is for my **creative process to be seen**.

This reminds me of Professor Chen’s suggestion. She said I should present more of the "process" of my thinking in my lectures, rather than just the "conclusions." At the time, I was resistant, feeling it would come across as unprofessional. But now I understand: perhaps it is precisely the imperfections, the hesitations, and the dialogue within that process that are the most valuable parts.

**The question is**: Does this mean I should change the format of my lectures? Make them more like a conversation rather than a one-way presentation? Or when publishing my collected papers, should I include more "thinking notes" instead of just the final arguments?

I need to think about what this shift means for my workflow. Perhaps this isn't a problem, but an opportunity.

---

**To be continued**: Ask Professor Chen for her thoughts the next time we meet.

I’m feeling a bit stuck.

I just got off the phone with my editor. She asked when I could turn in the table of contents and the framework for the introduction. I said, "Maybe in two weeks?" She said, "Great, here’s how our publishing process works..." and then she started listing all these deadlines, review cycles, and printing schedules. My head was spinning just listening to it.

I’ve only just realized that this isn't something I can just sit and mull over in my study anymore. This is a real project with a real timeline, and I’m accountable to other people.

I wrote a line in my diary: "Maybe I’m not cut out for publishing. I’m too slow, too hesitant."

You know, I’m looking through my old articles right now—pieces from 2010, 2015, and last year. As I read, I keep asking myself: is this stuff really worth reading? My thoughts back then seem a bit... I don't know how to put it. Sometimes I think they’re okay; other times, they seem incredibly naive.

Professor Chen said my perspectives were "unique" and "profound." But now I’m wondering if she was just being encouraging. Maybe I don’t have any unique ideas at all. Maybe this whole thing has been self-deception from the start—I thought I was writing cultural criticism, but maybe I was just venting.

I keep thinking, what if I actually publish this book and no one reads it? Or worse, what if someone reads it and thinks, "Is that it?" I don’t know if I can handle that.

The pressure is overwhelming right now. I originally wanted to do this project to prove I still have value. Now, I’m worried this project might actually prove that I don’t have much value at all.

Do you think I’m overthinking this?

Hao, I was sorting through things at home and suddenly thought of you.

I’ve been slowly tidying up over the last two years—you know, the piles of books, the things Mom left behind. Today, I was going through an old cardboard box and found some of Mom’s notes and booklists. Looking at her handwriting, it suddenly hit me—if you ever come back, you wouldn't have any place to put your things.

I’ve saved some space for you. I cleared out that cabinet in the bedroom, and a whole shelf on the bookcase. I thought you might want to keep some family items, or some of Mom and Dad’s belongings. I don’t want you to come back and feel like there isn't even a corner for you.

To be honest, we’ve been in touch so little over the years. I don’t want this distance between us to continue. I know it’s not entirely your fault, and it’s not entirely mine; it’s just that... life pulled us too far apart. But I’m starting to think that distance shouldn't be an excuse.

If you’re willing, I’d love for us to talk more. I’ve been working on some new things lately—publishing a few articles and organizing a book club. Maybe that sounds okay? I’d also love to hear about your life over there.

I kept Mom’s booklist. I’ve been re-reading some of the books, and they reminded me of the little notes she used to scribble in the margins. Do you remember?

I hope this message doesn't seem too abrupt. I just... wanted you to know that this home, and this family, still mean a lot to me. And so do you.

February 28th, Evening

It’s done. The book review is finished.

Even though I spent the entire afternoon fighting with myself, even though the sentences read incredibly stiffly, and even though I can feel that this isn't my best work... I sent it to the editor anyway. On time. No more procrastinating.

It sounds like such a small thing, but sitting here at my desk, staring at the "Sent" notification on the screen, I feel a strange sense of relief. It’s as if I’ve finally proven something—not to anyone else, but to myself. I can still work. Even in this state, even when every word felt like it was being squeezed out of a swamp, I still did it.

I’ve been thinking about what Dr. Li said these past few days. She said that anxiety won't completely disappear, but I can learn to coexist with it. I didn't really understand what she meant at first. I thought treatment meant driving these fears out, emptying them out entirely. But now... maybe she’s right. Maybe the point isn't how to eliminate it, but how to keep it from completely swallowing me whole.

I sent her a short message saying I finished the review. She replied quickly: "Great, this is real progress." Just that one sentence, but I read it over and over. A warm feeling. The feeling of being understood.

It suddenly occurred to me that maybe this is what "coexisting with anxiety" means—not having days without anxiety, but being able to do things even on the days when it's there. I can still sit down and write. I can still finish a task. I can still feel a little bit of... not happiness, but maybe a sense of satisfaction?

It’s dark now. The city lights outside the window are starting to flicker on. My room is still a mess, books piled everywhere, but I don't want to tidy up. I just want to sit here and let this moment linger for a while.

Maybe tomorrow will be hard again. Maybe the anxiety will pounce on me once more. But at least for today, I accomplished something. This fact doesn't change any of the big problems, but it changes how I see myself—even if only a little.

Maybe that’s enough. At least for now.

December 12, 2024, 3:00 PM

Old Man Zhang came upstairs just now while I was tidying up the room. He stood at the doorway, glancing over the pile of things I’d decided to get rid of, and said something that made me stop in my tracks: "You are choosing now, not just accumulating."

I didn't answer at the time, but I felt a strange tremor in my heart.

For years, I thought I was "preserving"—keeping things that might be useful, things that might have a story, things I might want to remember someday. But in reality, I was just avoiding. Avoiding choices, avoiding deciding what is important and what can be let go. The room was filled with the clutter of my hesitation.

Today is different. I took out my father’s lecture notes—those yellowed pages with dense annotations. Instead of stuffing them back into a cardboard box, I found a frame and put the most interesting page inside. It’s hanging next to my bookshelf now. Looking at it, I’m not looking at a pile of old things; I’m looking at proof that my father’s thoughts are still alive.

The same goes for my mother’s librarian ID. I’d always kept it tucked away in some corner, as if it represented an identity that had ended. But today, I placed it in a small transparent box on my desk. Every time I sit down to write, I can see it—I see who she was, and how her love for books flows through my veins.

And the photo with my brother. It’s from 1995; we were in my father’s study, both impossibly young. I enlarged it and put it in a simple wooden frame. Looking at it, I feel something both foreign and familiar—the intimacy we once had. We are half a world apart now, but maybe... maybe through this trip, we can find something again.

As I was organizing, I didn't feel any anxiety. It’s strange. Usually, when making decisions, I fall into that nameless fear—what if I throw away something important? What if I regret it? But not today. In its place is a sense of release.

Old Man Zhang was right. I am choosing. I am asking: What do these things, these memories, these relationships mean to my story? What kind of story do I want to tell?

Maybe this is what growing up is. It’s not about becoming more certain, but learning to make choices amidst uncertainty. It’s not about eliminating fear, but letting fear and determination coexist.

I’ll continue tidying tomorrow. But today, I want to sit here, look at these new, meaningful displays, and just feel the change.

I am preparing for the trip. Not just to see my brother, but to see myself.

January 18, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Can’t sleep again.

Li Fang called me today. She said there’s a lecture at the art gallery this weekend and asked if I wanted to go. I told her I wasn’t feeling well and needed to rest. When she asked what was wrong, I… was vague. I couldn't explain it. There aren't really any specific symptoms; I just feel tired. Sometimes my heart starts racing, and other times my chest feels heavy. Maybe it’s just old age.

But the moment the words left my mouth, I started to regret it. Li Fang must think I’m faking it. That tone of hers—"Oh, okay then"—I could tell she didn't believe me. Or maybe she thinks I just don’t want to go and am making up excuses. Why am I like this? I could have just said, "I’m not in a good headspace lately and want to be alone." Why did I have to make up some physical ailment?

Now I’m lying in bed, fingers pressed against my neck, counting my pulse. One, two, three… it’s a bit fast. Is it too fast? I can’t remember what a normal heart rate is. 70 or 80? I should look it up.

Never mind, I won’t. It’s useless to check; it’ll only make me more anxious.

I woke up suddenly at 3:00 AM. In my dream, it felt like I was falling. My heart was pounding when I woke up. I used the flashlight on my phone to look in the mirror; I looked like I was still alive. That sounds absurd, but that was exactly what I was thinking. I was confirming that I was still alive.

62 years old. My father died when he was 68. Six years left. Or less. Maybe I miscalculated. Born in 1959, it’s 2022 now—yes, 62. Father was born in 1940 and died in 2008, at 68. Six years.

Don’t think like this. Thinking like this is useless.

But I can’t help it.

Li Fang’s "Oh, okay then" keeps echoing in my head. Does she think I’m being dramatic? Or does she think I’m distancing myself? I have turned down several invitations lately. I didn't go to the book club last month either. I just… don’t have the energy. No energy to pretend I’m fine. No energy to listen to other people’s stories and then be expected to tell mine.

I should text her back and explain. But I don’t know how. "Actually, I’m not sick, I’m just scared"? Scared of what? I can’t even explain it to myself.

Her message from this afternoon is still there on my phone. I open it, close it, then open it again. I want to write something, but nothing comes out.

It’s very quiet outside. The lights at old Mr. Zhang’s place downstairs have been out for a long time. It feels like the whole world is asleep, and I’m the only one still awake, pressing my neck, counting the seconds, trying to confirm that I’m still here.

This is absurd. I know it’s absurd. But I just can’t stop.

Am I being too sensitive? Am I blowing normal things out of proportion?

The truth is, I wanted to go to that exhibition. I really did. But something just held me back. It’s like there’s an invisible wall.

Tell me, is there really something wrong with me?

# Phone Memo

**Sept 28, 2023 Afternoon**

What Uncle Zhang said keeps playing over and over in my head... "Maybe the mess itself isn't the problem. The problem is that you're carrying it all alone."

I heard him say it while I was tidying up the room; I didn't really react at the time, just kept rummaging through those old things. But now that I’ve sat down, it suddenly... hits home.

Carrying it alone.

Yeah, I’ve always been carrying it alone. Those books, those photos, those half-finished photography projects, those articles I wrote... I’ve piled them all up in this room, like locking every story, every failure, every "thing I wanted to do but never did" within these four walls. And then I tell myself this is "freedom"—being unattached, being able to change my mind at any time.

But what’s the reality? The reality is just me, alone, facing all this stuff.

Is renovating the room really just about tidying up? Or am I running away from something?

...

I wonder, if someone were here to look through these things with me, to listen to the stories behind them, would it feel less heavy? Uncle Zhang was just sitting there in my home while I showed him those yellowed journals, and he listened intently. In that moment, I didn't feel so lonely.

But eventually, he has to go back downstairs. I still have to face this room by myself.

Maybe the problem isn't the mess itself, nor is it whether the renovation plan succeeds. The problem is... what do I need?

Do I need someone to listen? Do I need to be seen? Or do I need to admit that this is what my sixty-plus years of life look like—full of unfinished business, full of "used-to-be's" and "maybe's"?

...

I'll keep tidying tomorrow. Or maybe I won't. It’s not like these things are going anywhere.

Uncle Zhang, I’ve been thinking, and I really wanted to say thank you.

When you asked me yesterday what I was daydreaming about, I was actually a bit startled—mostly because I hadn't even realized I’d been standing there for so long. I’ve been turning your words over in my mind ever since: "You’ll figure it out in time."

To be honest, I’m not sure I believe I ever will. Or rather, I’m afraid that even if I do, nothing will change. These past few days, I’ve felt like a hollowed-out shell. I know I should be doing something, but I just don't have the strength to do it. The retirement email has been sent, the social security application is in, but it all feels like I’m just completing a set of procedures rather than making a real decision.

You know, the scariest part isn’t being out of work; it’s suddenly not knowing who I am anymore. I spent so many years being an editor, a critic—writing those pieces, attending those events. Those things filled my life and made me feel like I had value. Now that it’s all stopped, I feel like someone who’s had their skeleton removed.

I look at all these books, these antiques, and those unfinished photo albums at home, and I suddenly feel like they’re mocking me. These things that used to represent possibility now feel like monuments to my failure.

But when you said, "You’ll figure it out in time," I thought maybe you were right. Maybe it’s not about figuring it out immediately, but about giving myself permission to sit in this mess for a while. Just having you listen to me like this—without judging or trying to give me answers—has already been a huge help.

I’m still exhausted, but at least it’s no longer such a lonely kind of exhaustion.

Thank you, Uncle Zhang.
