Dr. Li, I wanted to share some things I’ve been writing in my journal lately. I’m not sure why, but tonight I suddenly remembered what you said during our last session—that "action changes a person more than reflection"—and I feel like I might actually be changing.

I wrote a sentence in my diary: "Life is short, but it is precious. I want to live fully." My hand was shaking as I wrote it because I realized this wasn't just another 3:00 AM anxiety-driven thought; it felt like a real decision.

You know, I’ve always been trapped by loneliness. It’s not that I don’t have friends, it’s more of a... how should I put it? A deep-seated fear—fear that connections are superficial, fear that I’m too strange, fear of being seen through. But these past few months, I’ve been doing something: I’m planning a trip with my brother. It might sound like a small thing, but for me...

I’m making a financial plan. I’m looking into visas. I’m doing something I’ve never done before—proactively restricting my own freedom for the sake of an uncertain future. It’s ironic, isn’t it? I’ve always been so afraid of being tied down, but now I’m finding that sometimes, setting limits is actually a way to protect what you truly want.

The most surprising thing is that I’m not as afraid anymore. It’s not that the anxiety has completely vanished; it’s still there. But it’s no longer the master. I’m having a dialogue with it instead of being drowned by it.

I thought you should know this because... I’m not sure if this is a healthy change or if I’m just deluding myself again. I need someone to help me see it clearly.

— Lin Xiaoyun

December 22, 2023, Morning

The sky outside the window is a dull grey. I’ve been sitting here for an hour now. My brother’s letter rests on my lap, the paper wrinkled by my tears.

"Distance and silence do not mean estrangement; they give us the chance to reconnect on a deeper level."

I read this sentence over and over, and with every read, the tears fall harder. My fingers linger on the paper, as if trying to confirm this isn't just a figment of my imagination. He really wrote these words. My brother—the person I thought had drifted so far away—never truly left.

I always thought silence meant giving up. For all these years, I interpreted the distance between us as a drifting apart. I asked myself repeatedly: Why doesn't he reach out? Why do we have nothing to say to each other? I was even a little angry—angry that he didn't try harder to understand my loneliness. But his letter changed everything. He said he has always been there, just in a different way. He has been thinking of me, caring about me; it’s just that we both became too accustomed to the silence.

Mom once said that the true value of knowledge lies in helping us understand one another. I was young then and didn't quite get it. Now I do—she wasn't just talking about book learning, but about understanding the human heart. I had been trying to understand my brother in all the wrong ways, using my own anxiety to fill the gaps left by silence.

I think of Dad. When he passed away in 2008, there was so much left unsaid between us. Now, my brother’s letter makes me realize that perhaps those unspoken words have been there all along—in every moment we thought of each other, in every flicker of concern.

This has given me a new perspective on loneliness. Perhaps loneliness isn't about being abandoned, but about being invited—invited to reflect on what kind of connection is truly real. Even thousands of miles apart, even after years of not seeing each other, the connection remains profound. It doesn't require frequent phone calls or passionate words; it simply needs to exist.

I want to write back to him now. Not in a rush, but slowly and thoughtfully, pouring out my understanding and gratitude. I want to tell him that I finally understand.

The sky outside is still grey, but my heart has suddenly brightened.

# Lin Xiaoyun’s Private Diary

**March 15, 2023 – 3:42 AM**

Can’t sleep again.

I dug out that diary from 2007. Back then, I wrote an entire page about "freedom." Reading it now feels like reading the sleep-talk of a complete stranger.

"I am going to pursue the voice that truly belongs to me"—how could I have written that with such certainty? Now, I don’t even know who I am anymore.

The last two weeks have been strange. Ever since I sent that email, the days feel hollowed out. No deadlines looming over me, no editors asking for drafts, no one needing me to say anything at all. I should feel liberated, but I just feel... empty. So empty.

The daytime is manageable; I can pretend to be busy. Organizing books, looking at photography, flipping through old magazines. But at night, especially in the dead of night like this, all the self-deception fails. I sit here surrounded by these books and antiques, and it suddenly hits me—none of this means anything. My photography collection will never be finished, no one needs my critiques anymore, and all this junk I’ve collected is just filler for the void.

I always thought I was pursuing freedom. When I left the publishing house in 2007, I was so sure it was the right decision. But looking back now, was I just running away? Running from a stable life, running from the feeling of being tied down. And what about freedom? In the end, freedom has only made me lonelier and more fragile.

The year my mother passed away, I could still tell myself—I have my work, I’m doing something meaningful. And now? I have nothing.

I don’t know who I am anymore.

That question has been circling in my head all day. Who is Lin Xiaoyun? A 62-year-old woman? An unemployed editor? An amateur photographer who never finished a single project? An antique lover who picks up scraps at flea markets?

None of the above. Or rather, all of them, but even added together, they aren't enough to make me a "someone."

I thought about what Professor Chen said last time, suggesting I publish a collection of my essays. I was actually a bit excited then, thinking maybe there was still something left to do. But now... I pulled out those old articles to re-read them, and I realized they’re all obsolete. Society is changing, the internet is changing, everyone’s aesthetics and interests are shifting. And here I am, still writing things from ten years ago. My voice isn't needed anymore.

Maybe it never really was. Maybe I’ve been deluding myself all along, thinking I was doing something of value.

When the sun comes up, I still have to go downstairs to buy groceries. Old Mr. Zhang will probably ask how I’ve been. I’ll say, "Not bad, just taking a break." He’ll smile and say, "You can’t just stay idle like this forever." I’ll smile back in agreement, then come back and sit in front of this pile of things, not knowing what to do.

I don’t know if I have any dreams left.

Truly. I sat on the floor for a long time trying to think of one, and nothing came.

—

Maybe I should give Dr. Li a call tomorrow. But I don’t know what I’d say. "Doctor, I’m scared"? "Doctor, I feel like my life is a failure"? It sounds too dramatic.

Never mind. I’ll think about it some more.

# December 18, 2024, Evening

I still can’t quite believe what happened today.

Sitting there at the bookstore salon, I was actually nervous. It was that familiar pre-speech anxiety—a dry throat and icy fingertips. I kept wondering: why would these people want to hear what I have to say? What right do I have to stand there and talk about things like "loneliness is an invitation"?

But I said it anyway. And I was far more candid than I had expected to be.

In that moment, I heard myself talking about my brother, about how I’ve been alone in this city all these years, and about those late-night bouts of anxiety and self-doubt. I even talked about the year my mother passed away—how I sat by the window at 3:00 a.m. feeling like everything was meaningless. I had intended to hold some things back, but the words just flowed out.

What shocked me most was that no one left. No one looked uncomfortable or overwhelmed. Instead, a young female student raised her hand and asked, "How did you find the courage?"

I paused for a long time. I didn’t know how to answer. Because honestly, I’m not sure I ever really "found" anything. Was I just... tired? Done with hiding? Or did I finally realize that the cost of loneliness itself is greater than the risk of being seen?

I told her it was the latter.

After the discussion ended, a friend came up to me with misty eyes and said that my sharing had changed the way she looked at her own life. Another person said he had always viewed his fears as a sign of weakness, but hearing me speak made him realize that perhaps honesty itself is a form of strength.

I didn't know how to respond. I wanted to say, "Don't mythologize me; I’m just a 62-year-old woman with a lot of insecurities." But I didn't. I just listened, feeling that strange, unfamiliar sensation—of being seen, understood, and even needed.

Now that I’m back home, sitting alone among these piles of books and mementos, I wonder: was it real? Or was I, on some level, performing? Was I telling a "story of growth" just to gain validation?

But perhaps this doubt itself is my honesty. Perhaps true courage is being able to say "I am changing" while simultaneously asking yourself, "Is this really change?"

Tomorrow, I need to reply to Professor Chen’s email. That publishing plan she suggested... maybe it’s time to consider it seriously.

Not because of today’s applause, but because I finally believe that I have something worth saying.

# November 15, 2023, Evening

I haven’t fully processed everything from today.

This afternoon, I did something I haven’t done in a long time—I took the initiative to invite friends over. It wasn’t a formal gathering, just Professor Chen and two friends from the cultural salon, coming by for a casual visit. I didn’t even tidy up the room. This in itself was strange, as I’ve always treated the clutter in my home as a sort of defensive line, a way to keep people out.

But today, I didn't.

I opened those cabinets and took out those things—photos of my mother, old albums scavenged from flea markets, those yellowed bookmarks. I started telling the stories behind these objects. When I got to the photo of my mother, my voice choked up a little. I didn’t turn away, and I didn’t pretend I was fine; I just let the tears be there. Professor Chen patted my shoulder gently without saying any words of comfort, which, ironically, made me feel truly understood.

The strangest part was—they didn’t find the mess ridiculous. The way they looked at me while listening to my stories made me see these things in a new light. They weren't a pile of failures, but... some kind of record? Traces of thought?

One of the friends said, "You should document all of this."

I didn’t immediately push back. That’s not like me. Usually, I would say, "What’s worth recording about this?" or "I don’t have the ability." But tonight, I just nodded, turning the idea over in my mind.

Sitting at my desk now, the room is very quiet. I’m thinking that maybe these things are indeed worth being seen. Not because they are perfect, but because they are real. Each piece carries a version of me from a certain moment—the confused me, the curious me, the me who was trying to understand the world.

Maybe being seen and being accepted feels exactly like this. Not being changed, not being saved, just... being seen.

I’m wondering if I should seriously consider organizing these articles and thoughts. Not for publication, and not to prove anything, but just... to record. Like my mother used to do, organizing those forgotten books in the library.

The loneliness is still there. But it feels a bit different now.

Shared an article: *Sensitivity is Not Fragility — On Creativity and Anxiety in Highly Sensitive People*

I’ve been reading this lately. One line really hit home: "We often treat sensitivity as a flaw to be corrected, yet rarely acknowledge it as a different way of perceiving the world."

It reminds me of a conversation I had with a friend a while back. She said I overthink things. Maybe. But maybe it’s not overthinking—maybe it’s just... over-feeling?

I don’t know when it started, but now a single news story can keep me up all night, and I find myself overanalyzing the tone of a single sentence. I used to think this was my strength—being able to catch the details others miss, understanding those complex emotions. Now, it feels more and more like a curse.

The doctor calls it anxiety. I’m still wondering if that word is too absolute. Maybe I just care more than others; maybe I’m just more easily moved. That shouldn't necessarily be labeled an illness.

Anyway, I’m done overthinking this for tonight. Time for bed. I have drafts to edit tomorrow.

Hao, I’m back home.

Sitting here in my house, it all still feels a bit surreal. Just now, while I was in the kitchen pouring a glass of water by myself, the things you said in the car suddenly flashed through my mind. My eyes got a little misty again.

You know, for all these years, I always imagined you living a completely different life far away, as if there were an unbridgeable river between us. But today I finally realized that you’ve been watching over me all along. Those "worries" you mentioned, that hidden care—I heard them loud and clear.

I want to tell you that you don’t have to worry anymore. Not because everything is perfect, but because I’ve truly found a way to maintain my longing for freedom while taking good care of myself. I know that sounds like I’m just saying the right things, but I mean it.

For over a year now, I’ve been seeing a therapist. I know it sounds very "modern" and unlike me. But Dr. Li has helped me understand so much—about anxiety, about loneliness, and about why I’m always oscillating between escaping and longing. Now, I’m even organizing a community book club and have a steady circle of friends. My work is still a bit loose and unstructured, but I don’t blame myself for it anymore. I’m even planning a cultural commentary series on "family reconnection."

When you held my hand in the car, what I felt wasn't just care, but a sense of confirmation—a confirmation that even though we’ve been apart for so long, we never truly lost each other.

I want to keep this going. Not with occasional, rushed greetings, but with real, regular contact. We have so much to talk about, so many years of stories to catch up on.

Thank you for coming today. Thank you for always being there.

Can I call you tomorrow? I want to hear your voice.

Xiaoyun

March 8, 2024. Evening.

Sitting at my desk, the sound of the rain outside hasn't stopped. I just finished washing the dishes, and my hands are still a bit cold.

Something happened after the book club meeting today, and I’m still processing it.

A young girl, maybe in her early twenties, asked me during the discussion why I’m always talking about death. Her tone wasn't malicious, but she was definitely puzzled, maybe even a little worried. I could tell she felt the topic was too heavy.

I paused for a moment. The old me might have defended myself or tried to prove my perspective was right. But today, I just looked at her and said, "Talking about death isn't about trying to be profound; it's about living more wakefully."

The moment the words left my mouth, I surprised even myself. It wasn't a rehearsed answer; it just... came out naturally.

After the session ended, the girl stayed behind and we talked for a long time. She said she’s been feeling lost lately, not knowing what she wants or if her choices have any meaning. I didn't give her advice—heaven knows I’m not sure what’s "right" either—I just listened. I listened to her confusion, her fears, and her anxiety about the future.

Then something strange happened. I realized that my own experiences—the failed marriages, the nights of anxiety, the reflections on aging and mortality—suddenly became useful. Not as something grand like "lessons" or "wisdom," but simply... my story helped her feel less alone. She realized that someone else had walked through similar struggles, survived, and even learned something from them.

It hit me then: this is exactly what I’ve been afraid of losing—the value of my thoughts, my experiences, everything I’ve accumulated. I’ve been worried that the internet would make my professional skills obsolete, worried that no one cares about the book reviews I write. But tonight, sitting across from that girl and seeing the light in her eyes, I knew my value doesn't lie in those things at all.

It’s not in the market. It’s not in whether a publishing editor accepts my manuscript.

It’s right here. In real conversation. In the moments when I can see another person’s confusion and respond to it with the life I’ve lived.

As I write this, I realize how long it’s been since I felt this... certain. Not a false certainty, but a real one, rooted in reality.

I am still that sensitive, easily anxious, somewhat socially awkward woman. But perhaps these traits allow me to see things others miss. Perhaps that is enough.

No need to prove anything.

No need to become anything.

Just need to keep going like this—to see, to listen, to understand.

The rain is still falling outside. I feel very peaceful.

# Diary

**October 10, 2022, 1:30 AM**

Insomnia again.

I’ve lost count of how many nights like this I’ve had this month. Lying in bed, the cracks in the ceiling become clearer and clearer in the dark. I’ve counted them—from the head of the bed to the foot, there are seven in total. Each one feels like a decision I’ve made in my life—crooked and irreparable.

I don’t know how much longer I can hold on.

I’ve written this sentence so many times. I’ve written it in black ink, in red ink, and sometimes I even write it lightly in pencil, as if that makes it less real. But it is real. Here I am, sixty-two years old, holding a pen and asking myself this question.

The editor’s email is still spinning in my head. "This topic might not be suitable for the current market." The market. What a word. I spent forty years learning how to understand literature, art, and human nature, only to be judged now by the "market." A market I don’t understand at all, and have no desire to understand. Those young bloggers, with their fragmented, fast-food content, get the kind of clicks I will never achieve. What has become of my deep critiques? Outdated, long-winded, unwanted junk.

I don’t know what the meaning of my life is anymore.

This question is even more terrifying. Because it’s not just about work. Dr. Li asked me the other day, "If no one needs your reviews, does your knowledge still have value?" I didn't answer. I sat in her office, staring at the trees outside the window, unable to say a word. Because I truly don't know the answer.

I tried to learn video editing. I bought the tutorials, downloaded the software, and sat in front of the computer watching videos for three hours. Then I gave up. My fingers felt as clumsy on the keyboard as if they belonged to a stranger. Looking at what those young people produce and realizing I could never do it fills me with a profound sense of powerlessness. Maybe I really am too old. Maybe my brain has calcified, unable to adapt to new things.

My brother sent a message. He asked how I was doing. I replied, "I'm fine, don't worry." I didn't even tell him the truth. Why? Because I feel ashamed. My younger brother is living a stable life overseas, while I am here, alone, watching my savings slowly dwindle. They will be gone by June 2024. I’ve calculated it so many times. With a calculator, with pen and paper, with my fingers. The result is the same every time.

Am I too old to learn new skills?

Am I too old to start over?

Am I too old to admit my own failure?

The house is cluttered with things. Books, antiques, cameras—things I once loved. Now, when I look at them, I only feel a deep sense of weariness. These collections used to represent my taste, my vision, my soul. Now they are just trash. Heavy, useless trash. I don't even have the energy to organize them.

Why should I keep living?

This question keeps recurring in my mind. Not once, but over and over. Like a song I can't stop. I know it sounds terrible. I know I should feel guilty. But I don't. I just feel tired.

Maybe I should give up.

I wrote this sentence in large letters. Then I drew a long black line underneath it.

I don't know what this line represents. An end? A boundary? Or just a line?

It’s still dark outside. It will be a long time before dawn.

How much longer must I hold on?

Professor Chen’s message came through while I was sorting through that mountain of photography notes. You know the ones—the pile I’ve been accumulating since 2015, the stuff I kept saying I’d organize "when I have the time."

He asked if I’d be willing to share some thoughts on "Reading and Contemporary Culture" at a Literature Department lecture. My gut reaction was to say no. I had a whole list of excuses—I don’t have a formal academic background, I’m just a freelance writer, does anyone even read articles anymore? Are these ideas outdated? You know how I am; I always start by doubting myself.

But... I didn’t turn him down right away. I told him I needed to think about it.

Then I just sat there in front of all those notes, looking at the fragments of thoughts I’ve jotted down over the years—about how books shape our worldview, why we still need deep reading in this age of fragmentation, and how a person’s mind is sparked through dialogue. All of it is still there. It never really went out of style.

It hit me that maybe I’ve been waiting for some "perfect moment" to share this, waiting until I felt "enough." But... maybe I can just share it now. I don’t have to wait until everything is perfect or until I feel like I’ve finally earned my place.

I messaged Professor Chen back and told him I’d give it a shot.

To be honest, I’m a bit nervous. It’s been years since I’ve done any kind of lecture. And that voice is still there, worrying—is this really a good idea? What if I mess it up? But this time, I’m not letting the anxiety completely drown me out. There’s this feeling that... maybe this is exactly what I need right now.

I want to hear what you think. Does this decision sound crazy? Or is it something I should have done a long time ago?

During today’s book club discussion on the circular structure of *One Hundred Years of Solitude*, a participant suddenly said to me, "Your perspective has truly changed the way I understand reading."

I froze for a moment.

Having spent years working as an editor and writing reviews, I’ve always been immersed in words. But I’ve rarely felt this kind of—how should I put it?—genuine resonance. It wasn’t about getting a piece published or meeting a deadline; it was about the fact that someone’s way of seeing the world had shifted because of an idea.

It reminded me of the years my mother spent working at the library. She used to say her happiest moments were seeing a reader’s eyes light up because of a book. I didn’t quite get it back then, but I do now.

Perhaps this is why I keep sticking with these seemingly "unstable" pursuits. It’s not that I’m afraid of being tied down; it’s that I’m searching for this connection—through dialogue, through reflection, and through the way we reshape each other's understanding.

Thank you to everyone who came to the book club. You’ve helped me see the meaning in what I do.

Lin Hao,

I’m sitting in my study with your letter right here in front of me. I’ve already read it three times, and my eyes are still a bit misty.

To be honest, when I saw your handwriting on the envelope, my heart actually skipped a beat. It’s true that we haven’t been in touch much over the years, but I never imagined you were still thinking of me all this time. I always thought the silence meant you had given up, or that we really couldn't go back to how things used to be. I cried while reading your letter—not out of sadness, but from a feeling of being understood, of being seen.

You wrote that "distance and silence do not mean estrangement," and that sentence has been circling in my head ever since. I realize now that I might have misunderstood so many things. I took your infrequent contact as indifference, but perhaps we were both just busy navigating our own lives. You have your life over there, and I’ve been finding my own path here. But that doesn't mean the bond between us was ever broken.

I’ve been thinking about Mom and Dad. They both loved books so much, loved to reflect, loved those big questions about the meaning of life. We inherited that from them, didn't we? We are both trying to understand the world in our own ways. You are far away and I am here, but we are actually doing the same thing—trying to find meaning, trying to understand life.

It makes me feel like we’ve never truly been apart.

I want to be in touch with you more often. It doesn't have to be every week, but I want to hear what’s on your mind, what you’re reading, and your perspective on the world. I have so much to tell you about what I’ve been through these years and what I’m thinking now. And I want to hear your stories, too.

Would you be open to that? Maybe we could set a time for a video call, so it feels like we’re in the same room. Or we could keep writing letters like this—slowly and deeply.

Thank you, brother. Thank you for your letter. It came at just the right time.

Xiaoyun

# Post-Consultation Notes

**Time: December 3rd, Morning**
**Location: Home, just returned from the session**

---

Dr. Li said something that I’m still turning over in my mind:

"Change isn't necessarily about action; sometimes, change happens within understanding."

I had been so focused on tidying up the room, renovating, and doing something tangible. But she asked me: Why are you so determined to change the arrangement of these objects? Doesn't the way they are now also tell your story?

I was speechless.

Then she added: Perhaps you aren't running away from anything, but rather, you are living deeply within it.

—I think that’s what I’ve been doing these past two months. Not escaping, but slowly coming to understand what every object and every story means to me within this chaotic space.

**Key points to remember:**
- External change → Internal acceptance
- Escaping → Living deeply within it
- The objects are not the problem; understanding is.

**Another thought:**

Perhaps true freedom isn't about shedding all order and commitment, but about knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the price for it. This includes giving up certain forms of security. It includes accepting certain forms of limitation.

This idea is very new. It’s a bit scary, but also a bit of a relief.

**Follow-up reflections:**
- Why do those moments of inviting friends over to share stories feel so fulfilling?
- Is loneliness truly a curse? Or is it actually an invitation—an invitation to consider what kind of connection is authentic?
- If I am to publish that collection of essays, what do I want it to say?

Need to email Professor Chen this afternoon.

Tuesday, February 8, 2022, past 11:00 PM

My hands are still shaking.

I hung up the phone with Dr. Li a while ago and have been sitting on the sofa for half an hour now. My teacup is on the coffee table; the tea went cold long ago. The room is quiet, save for the faint sound of Mr. Zhang’s television drifting up from downstairs. I should be sleeping, but I can’t.

I don’t know why I suddenly found the courage to call her. Maybe it was because I checked my blood pressure three more times yesterday; maybe it’s because this is my fifth night of insomnia this week; or maybe... I’m just tired. Tired of being alone in this cluttered room, checking the gas stove over and over again; tired of waking up at 3:00 AM in a panic over a single abnormal heartbeat.

When she picked up, her voice was gentle. I had prepared a whole script, but the moment I opened my mouth, it all fell apart. I heard myself talking about the compulsive checking, the repeated blood pressure readings, and... death. When that word left my mouth, my voice began to tremble.

I told her I had done the math. My father died at 68, my mother at 71. I am 62 now, which means I have, at most... six to nine years left. When I said that, my voice broke completely. I heard myself sobbing; it was as if someone had flipped a switch in my throat, and the tears just started falling. I didn’t even realize I was crying until I felt the wetness on my cheeks.

Dr. Li didn’t interrupt me. She just listened quietly, and then said in that same gentle tone, "These are all symptoms of anxiety, Mr. Lin. You aren't the first person to do these calculations."

The feeling of being understood... it’s strange. On one hand, I felt a sense of relief, as if someone had finally seen what I’ve been suppressing all this time. But on the other hand, when she said the word "anxiety," I felt a sudden surge of shame. Am I sick? Am I really ill? Or am I just... oversensitive?

I’ve always considered myself a rational person. I’ve read so many books and understood so many complex theories—how could I be brought down by something like this? How did I end up needing psychological counseling?

And yet, I know this isn't just me being dramatic. Those numbers have been spinning in my head for too long, and those checks have become a habit. I can’t stop.

She made an appointment to see me next week. I agreed.

Sitting here now, I keep asking myself: Am I truly sick, or am I just... too sensitive?

Maybe both. Maybe neither.

Maybe I’m just tired.

——

It’s snowing outside. I haven’t drawn the curtains.

December 18, 2024. Past 11 PM.

Insomnia again. I’ve been lying there for half an hour, my mind replaying that conversation with the editor over and over.

She said, "re-evaluate the feasibility of the project." Just that one phrase, and I’ve already chewed on it at least a hundred times. What does it mean? Is she calling me unprofessional? Or has she lost faith in the project entirely? I know I didn't give her a firm deadline; I used that vague "still conducting a final review" line. But I really do have my reasons—how could I possibly make these choices in a rush?

Yet, even as I said it, I could hear it myself: it was an excuse.

I’m defending my own procrastination. Here we go again, the same old story.

Fifteen years of articles. From 2009 until now. I’ve been going through them one by one; some are actually quite good, while others now seem like nothing but long-winded nonsense. Which ones should I choose? How do I write the introductions for them? These questions should have been settled three months ago, but I’ve been "thinking." In reality, I’ve been avoiding.

Professor Chen spoke with such confidence, saying I should publish these pieces, and for a moment I was swept up in it, feeling like these years hadn't been in vain. But now? Now I’m starting to doubt—is this really worth publishing? Or am I just a self-deluded middle-aged woman trying to prove her life has meaning through a book?

I hate this feeling. I hate how I always shrink back at the final hurdle.

After so many years of freelancing, I’ve grown used to a life where no one pushes me. I can change my mind whenever I want; I can drop one project to chase another interest. It gave me freedom, but it also turned me into someone who can never commit. Now, this collection of essays demands a real commitment—time, energy, and a final manuscript. I need to say "yes" or "no."

But I find myself wanting to say "yes" while being terrified of saying it.

If the book is finally published, that’s great. But what if it fails? What if no one buys it? What if the introductions I write are terrible? Then my self-doubt will only deepen. I’ll feel like a total failure.

And there’s a deeper fear—one I’m afraid to say out loud, but since this is my diary, I’ll say it. If I don't finish this book, these thoughts of mine will vanish along with me. I’m 62 now. My parents are both gone. I have no children. No one will remember what I said or what I thought. This book might be the only thing I leave behind.

So I want to finish it, yet I’m terrified of it failing.

I need to get back to the editor. Tomorrow. Or the day after. I can’t put it off any longer.

But I still don’t know what to say.

Professor Chen, hello. I know it’s a bit sudden to reach out at this hour, but I’m on the subway and felt like saying a few words to you.

I just got my passport. I went to pick it up yesterday, and standing there at the window holding that little red book, I sort of dazed out for a moment. It’s finally real. It’s no longer just me telling friends, "I’m planning to see my brother"; it’s a tangible thing—something I can buy a plane ticket with, something that is actually happening.

To be honest, I’m struggling to adjust. It sounds strange, doesn't it? I’ve always said I crave freedom and hate being tied down, but now that this has turned into a reality that requires planning, punctuality, and following a set process, I’m feeling this strange kind of pressure. I wrote "I’m not sure if I’m truly ready" in my diary, and I keep looking at that sentence over and over.

I’ve grown used to chaos over the years. Missing deadlines, a house full of unfinished projects, just doing whatever I felt like. But now that I’m going abroad, having to live with a brother I haven't seen in years, and needing to adapt to the rhythm of another city... I suddenly realize that I might never have actually prepared for something long-term and goal-oriented. Freelancing allowed me to escape that.

But it’s not a bad thing. I know that. I keep telling myself it’s not a bad thing. It’s a new, different way of living—one that is orderly and involves commitment. I made a choice for something that truly matters. I should be proud of that.

It’s just... sometimes I wake up in the middle of the night and start imagining everything that could go wrong. The flight, the jet lag, whether conversations with my brother will be awkward, if I’ve brought enough money... you know how I get. Rationally, I know these are small, manageable things, but emotionally, there’s still this nameless anxiety.

I suppose this is what growing up feels like. It’s not some dramatic, sudden epiphany, but rather this subtle feeling—a bit exhausting, yet full of anticipation. I’m proud of myself, yet full of self-doubt at the same time.

How have you been lately? When are you free for tea? I’d love to hear your thoughts.

Wednesday, April 2, 2025, 10:30 AM

I didn’t sleep well last night. It wasn’t anxiety—at least not entirely—but my mind kept racing about the passport. I lay in bed, scrolling through the checklist on my phone over and over, terrified I’d missed something. ID card, household register, photos, application form... I even made myself a schedule. Tomorrow: gather materials at the local office. Friday: submit the application.

It’s a bit ridiculous, isn't it? Me—Lin Xiaoyun, the person who pushes every deadline to the final second and whose desk is a permanent disaster zone—making a step-by-step plan for a passport application. I even used Post-it notes.

But lying there last night, it hit me. This isn't about being tied down. This isn't the kind of "order" I’ve always loathed. This is... finding a reason to be organized.

I’m 62 years old. I’ve spent so many years rebelling against things that made me feel suffocated—work systems, marital expectations, societal norms. I used to think "freedom" meant rejecting all constraints. But now, as I fill out forms and gather documents just to see my brother, I finally understand what real freedom is.

True freedom isn't the rejection of order. It’s the conscious choice of what is worth being orderly for.

All those tedious details on the passport application? They are the path to my brother. They are the path to places I’ve never seen. They are the concrete proof of me telling myself, "I still have time, and the time is now."

So tomorrow, I’ll put on my blue coat, take all my documents, and get on the subway to the administrative office. I will fill out every form meticulously. I will ask the staff if anything is missing. This isn't a compromise. It’s an investment. An investment in myself.

I feel like laughing. Here I am, alone at home, nodding at thin air as if I’m debating someone. But I need to say it out loud; I need to hear it: I can change. At 62, I can still change my perspective on order. I can still learn to be methodical for the things that truly matter.

When Mom was alive, she always called me "bright but scattered." Dad would just shake his head when I missed a deadline, though he never stayed truly angry. They’re both gone now. It’s just me. And what I’m doing is taking the respect for knowledge they taught me and applying it to this thing called "living well."

See you tomorrow.

But today, I don't know what came over me, I just said it: "Maybe I can start over."

The moment the words left my mouth, I stunned myself. What did that sound like? Like I was giving myself permission?

Grandpa Zhang smiled and said it was a good idea. Then he asked if I was going to clear out those books first. I meant to say, "Yes, it’s time to clear them out," but the words turned into a hesitation as they hit my lips—"Well... maybe... I don't necessarily have to clear them *all* out..."

I could hear it in my own voice—that uncertain, tentative feeling. I want to change something, yet I’m afraid of losing something. These two feelings are at war inside me.

Sitting there on the stairs, it suddenly hit me that maybe rearranging this room isn't just about the room itself. The room is me, isn't it? Messy, piled high with things from the past, with no space that truly belongs to the "now." If I can straighten this place out... maybe I can straighten myself out, too?

That sounds too dramatic. But that’s exactly what I was thinking.

The problem is—can I actually stick with it? I’m always like this: a burst of enthusiasm, and then I give up. Photography, reading lists, those unfinished articles... I’ve grown used to failure. Why would this time be any different?

But... maybe precisely because I recognize my own patterns, this time *could* be different?

I don’t know. It’s still light out. I’m sitting in my room, looking at these books, these things, these "stories" I’ve accumulated over all these years. Maybe tomorrow I won’t do a thing. Maybe by next week I’ll have forgotten the whole idea.

But today, this afternoon, I am allowing myself to believe—that maybe I can still start over.

Maybe.

Professor Chen, good morning.

To be honest, I was a bit surprised. This piece is completely different from my usual style—it lacks that typical critic’s detachment and feels... much more personal. It took me a long time to work up the courage to write this way. I used to feel that weaving my own experiences into my work was a bit risky, but this time felt different.

The editor also asked if I’d consider continuing in this direction. I’m starting to think that maybe this is the kind of writing I’ve wanted to do all along. Not hiding behind book reviews, but actually saying something real.

You mentioned the idea of publishing a book before. I’m beginning to feel that it’s not that I have nothing to write about, but rather that I’ve been writing the wrong things.

I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately, but it’s a different kind of exhaustion than usual. It’s not the fatigue of being chased by deadlines, but more like... the feeling of being pulled forward by something.

When are you free? I’d love to hear your thoughts.

I sat at my desk, my fingers hovering over the keyboard for a long time. On the screen was a draft of an email I’d already revised five times. Outside, the rain was still falling—a soft sound, but enough to be unsettling.

**Notes | May 24, 2022, 10:15 PM**

I did something I haven’t done in a very long time. I wrote a letter to my brother.

We haven’t had a real conversation in 22 years. Ever since he moved abroad in 2000, we’ve slowly become strangers. Occasional greetings—"How are you," "I'm fine"—and that was it.

Tonight, I dug out a dusty old photo album. A picture from 1985. He has his arm around my shoulder, both of us laughing. I was only 23 then. He’s three years older than me; back then, he still looked out for me.

I don't know when that era ended.

What did I write in the letter? I wrote, "I don't know if you still remember me, but I remember you." I cried when I finished that sentence. It’s embarrassing, but I cried.

The letter is still sitting in my drafts. I’m afraid to hit send.

Maybe he doesn't want to hear from me at all. Maybe he moved on long ago. Maybe I’m just intruding on his life. What right do I have to suddenly show up after 22 years as if nothing ever happened?

But what if I don't send it? What if I continue to do nothing?

I am 62 years old now. That’s how old my father was when he passed away. I can feel time accelerating. Every time I look in the mirror, I see the marks of aging. I don't know how much time I have left.

I don't want to leave this kind of regret behind when I die.

And yet, I’m terrified. Terrified of his silence. Terrified of his rejection. Terrified of discovering that there is nothing left between us to fix.

My hands are shaking. It’s stupid. A 62-year-old woman, losing her composure over a letter that hasn't even been sent.

Maybe I should just sleep on it. Maybe tomorrow I’ll have the courage. Or maybe tomorrow I’ll delete the letter and tell myself it’s all too late.

Or maybe... maybe I’ll press send.

I don't know.

I really don't know.

October 22, 2023, Afternoon

I accepted Professor Chen’s invitation.

I was still agonizing over it yesterday, but today I made the decision. I can hardly believe it myself.

He said he hopes I can share something at the lecture—about books, reading, and my reflections over the years. My first instinct was that same old refrain: "I don't have anything important to share." It came out like a reflex. But this time, I didn't let it end there.

Sitting at my desk, looking at this pile of unorganized notes and clippings, it suddenly hit me: Why am I always waiting? What am I waiting for? A perfect moment? Waiting to become wiser, more confident, less anxious?

That moment might never come.

I messaged Professor Chen back and said yes. Just like that. But as I hit send, I felt this strange sensation—a mix of intense nerves and a sense of relief.

I’ve spent years doubting myself. Doubting my work, my ideas, the very meaning of my existence. Looking at all these unfinished projects, I felt like a failure. But maybe... maybe that’s exactly what I can share? Not something polished and perfect, but the process itself. The books I’ve read, the questions I’ve grappled with, the detours I’ve taken.

Maybe someone needs to hear that. Maybe I don’t have to wait until everything is perfect before I speak up.

Of course, the anxiety is kicking in again. When is the lecture? How long should it be? What if the room goes cold? What if I freeze on stage? These thoughts are circling in my head. But this time, I didn't let the anxiety make me back down. I told myself: fine, be anxious, but I’m still doing this.

It’s an unfamiliar feeling.

Dr. Li told me last time that I need to learn to coexist with my anxiety rather than being held hostage by it. I never quite understood what that meant, but I think I’m starting to get it now. I didn’t wait for the anxiety to disappear; I just said "yes" anyway.

Maybe this is the beginning.

I need to organize my thoughts tonight and figure out where to start. And those photographs... maybe I could use them for the lecture? But I shouldn't overthink it. One step at a time.

I feel a bit tired now, but it’s not that hopeless kind of exhaustion. It’s the kind of tired that comes with anticipation.

A strange feeling indeed.

# MEMO

**Time: 3:45 AM**

Insomnia again. I dug up those old articles, dating from 2009 to the present... Reading through them, I’m starting to have an existential crisis.

Professor Chen’s words keep looping in my head: "You should publish a collection of your essays." It sounds simple enough, but when it actually comes down to selecting the pieces, it’s like being forced to stand in front of a mirror and see exactly who I am.

The question is: Was that passion and conviction real? Or was it just the arrogance of youth?

I flipped through that review of *To Live* I wrote back in 2010. Reading it now is... painful. It’s so wordy. Too many unnecessary adjectives, too much self-important "profundity." Did I really think I understood something back then? Or was I just stacking pretty words to build an illusion?

Why am I thinking about this at three in the morning? Because during the day, I can still convince myself—"This is meaningful," "These ideas deserve to be seen." But the night is different. You can’t lie to yourself at night.

Which articles to choose? How to write the introduction? These aren't the real problems. The real problem is: Am I doing something meaningful, or am I engaging in a well-packaged act of self-deception?

Then there’s the issue of time. If I take this project seriously, I’ll have to give up some freelance opportunities. But can I commit? I’ve always hated being tied down...

When Mom was still here, she said something—"Your thoughts are worth recording." I was young then; I took it for granted. Now... I’m not so sure.

But maybe this is exactly what I need to do. Maybe it’s precisely because of this uncertainty that I should do it.

Whatever. I'll think about it when the sun comes up. Right now, I just want to sleep.

Professor Chen, are you there?

I wanted to share something with you. A few days ago, I made a decision, and I’m still processing what it all means.

You know that my relationship with my brother has always been cold. We’ve barely had a real conversation in years. I kept telling myself this was normal—that people drift apart, relationships fade, and that’s just life. But recently, while I was tidying up my room—you know the one, filled with all my unfinished projects—it suddenly hit me that I’ve been running away. I’ve been using all sorts of excuses to justify this estrangement, but at its core, I was just afraid. Afraid that he doesn't know me anymore, afraid that we’d have nothing to say to each other.

So, I wrote him a letter. It was that simple, and yet that difficult.

In the letter, I didn’t try to explain why we haven't been in touch all these years, nor did I try to "fix" anything. I just told him I was thinking of him and that I wanted to know how his life is going. I asked some very ordinary questions—what he’s been reading lately, if he still remembers the books we used to discuss when we were younger.

After I sent it, I sat there for a long time. Do you know that feeling? When you’ve done something but have absolutely no idea what comes next. He might not reply at all. Or he might reply, but only out of politeness. I keep asking myself if this was a mistake, if I should have just let things stay buried.

But I’m starting to think that maybe the waiting itself is a form of courage. Maybe this is all I can do—acknowledge the loneliness and then try to change it, even without any certainty of the outcome.

I’m not sure why I’m telling you this. Perhaps it’s because you’ve always understood those things about loss and connection. We’ve discussed those "points of no return" in life so many times at the salon. This feels different, yet somehow very similar.

I’m still waiting.

How have you been lately?

# Essay Collection Publication Plan - Memo

**Date: January 22, 2024, 9:15 PM**

---

## Working Titles (TBD)

- *The Age of Reading: From Paper to Screen*?

## Preliminary List of Essays

**Time Span: 2007–2023 (17 years)**

Must-finds:
- That 2008 review of *To Live* (written the year my father passed away; it reads differently now).
- The series on photography and the gaze from around 2015 (3–4 pieces).
- Articles on the internet and reading (I wrote quite a few between 2015 and 2018).
- Recent reflections from the past two years on aging, death, and the meaning of life (a bit personal, perhaps, but maybe that’s exactly what this era needs to hear?).
- The most intellectually rigorous book reviews (not the bestsellers, but the ones I truly poured myself into).

**Estimated Count: 20–25 pieces**
(Too many and it feels scattered; too few and it lacks weight. Must be selective.)

---

## Keywords/Core Concepts for the Preface

This is the most important part. What should the preface say?

- **"How to maintain deep thinking in a fragmented age"** ← A bit cliché.
- What I actually want to say is: **How an individual finds their own voice amidst uncertainty.**
- Or: **The true value of knowledge lies not in market recognition, but in its ability to help us understand the world.**

Keywords:
- Anxiety vs. Reflection
- Freedom vs. Security
- Fragments vs. The Whole
- Personal Legacy (rather than professional achievement)

Possible opening line:
"I always thought I was running away from something—the trap of a stable job, the constraints of marriage, the fear of aging. But perhaps what I was really searching for was an honest way to live. These essays are the record of that process."

(A bit sentimental? But it’s the truth.)

---

## Next Steps

**Immediate Actions:**
1. [ ] Organize all old articles on the computer (this weekend).
2. [ ] Dig out paper versions from magazine archives (some very old pieces might only exist in print).
3. [ ] Finalize the list of 25 essays + brief notes (why each was chosen).
4. [ ] Write the first draft of the preface (by the end of January).

**Follow-up:**
5. Contact Professor Chen—he mentioned recommending me to a publisher before; time to ask him.
6. Email the editor (the one who helped me publish a few articles; he knows my work well).

## Timeline (Let’s be realistic)

- **Mid-February**: Finish organizing essays + first draft of the preface.
- **Late February**: Contact the publisher to understand the process and schedule.
- **March–April**: Communicate with the editor; revise essays and preface.
- **May**: Finalize manuscript.
- **June–August**: Layout, proofreading, printing (usually takes 2–3 months).
- **Autumn**: Publication and release.

**Practical Concerns:**

- Time. I always miss my deadlines. Can I stick to it this time?

...Never mind. Don't get bogged down in that now. Just get the essays organized first.

Dr. Li said last time that writing down your thoughts makes you feel more in control. Looking at this plan now, it does feel different. It’s no longer that hollow "maybe I should do something," but actual, concrete steps.

Maybe this is what I need right now—not an answer to my anxiety, but a direction for action.

# Thursday, June 26, 2025 – Sunny

My conversation with Professor Chen last night keeps looping in my head. Sitting here in this café, looking out at streets that feel both familiar and strange, I feel the need to get these thoughts down.

When she said, "This is exactly who I’ve always seen you as," I nearly burst into tears. It wasn't out of grievance or even sentimentality—it was more like a form of confirmation. Like someone who has been walking in the dark for a long time, only for someone else to flip the switch and say, "You’ve been here all along."

I asked her how I wanted to be remembered. It’s a question I’ve been asking myself for years, especially after my mother passed away; I’ve turned it over in my mind again and again. But when I spoke it aloud last night, the answer was crystal clear: "As someone with ideas, with compassion, and with the courage to connect with others."

I was almost surprised to hear myself say it. It wasn't a manufactured answer; it was the sum of everything that has actually happened over the years. My twenty-five years at the publishing house, every book I handled with care. The articles and reviews that followed. Even those late-night conversations at the cultural salons. None of it was for some grand objective; it was simply because I genuinely cared—about ideas, and about the understanding between people.

Has this trip changed anything? I’m not sure. But it has brought things into focus. Before, I was always afraid—afraid of wasting time, of having no achievements, of aging, even of death. Now I realize that all these fears point to the same thing: I want to live a meaningful life.

And a meaningful life isn't something you wait for. It’s not waiting until I finish some big project, or until I become more "stable," or until my body can do more. A meaningful life is happening right now—sitting with my brother, discussing a book with a friend, writing an article I truly care about.

I am still sensitive. I still toss and turn at midnight over a single sentence. I still overthink. But these are no longer my enemies. This is how I see the world. It’s what allows me to feel other people’s stories and to see the truths of life within a book.

Professor Chen said she has always seen me this way. I think perhaps it’s time I started seeing myself this way, too. Not as a person defined by achievements or stability, but as someone real, someone who cares, and someone who has courage.

This realization is enough. Perhaps it’s more than enough.

[Image: A corner of a bookshelf by the window, sunlight streaming across a few open books, with a camera resting nearby]

[Image: Inside a cafe, a small group sitting in a circle—some listening intently, others laughing]

Maybe this is what creativity is all about. It doesn’t have to be about achieving something grand; sometimes it’s just about capturing the way the light hits a room in the afternoon, or the expressions on friends' faces when they’re discussing a book.

I’ve picked up my camera again lately. Not with any intention of exhibiting or anything—just... wanting to really look at life itself.

Friends from the book club told me my perspectives gave them a fresh take on things. Hearing that made me realize that maybe what I’ve been searching for has been right here all along.

3:45 AM. Can't sleep again.

I dug out that review of *To Live* I wrote back in 2012. My God, how could I have written such a thing? That elaborate phrasing, that sentimentality, that self-important "profundity"—it’s all just naive idealism. I slashed at the margins with a red pen, marking them "outdated," "shallow," "feeble." The tip of the pen nearly tore through the paper.

I was in my fifties then; I should have been mature enough. But looking at these words, I feel like I’m reading the diary of some innocent girl. Was that passion and conviction real? Or was it just a young person’s arrogance? No—I wasn't young at all back then. That makes it worse.

My chest is starting to feel tight.

I keep flipping through—2014, 2015... one piece after another. I’m sitting on the floor, red pen in hand, feeling myself shrink. My head is spinning a little. I whisper "pointless, pointless" over and over, as if confirming something. The room is silent, save for the sound of the pen scratching across the paper.

Professor Chen mentioned publishing a collection of my essays. At the time, I took it as a form of validation—that my ideas still held value. But looking at this stuff now, I think: if even I find these articles shallow, outdated, and feeble, what right do I have to let others see them? Who am I fooling?

Maybe I’ve been fooling myself all along.

Fifteen years. Fifteen years of accumulation. I used to think these articles represented my thinking, my growth. But maybe they just represent one instance of self-deception after another. I’ve been saying the same shallow things in different ways at different times, telling myself it was "profound cultural criticism."

If I publish this book, I’m just making this deception permanent. Set in type, bound in a volume, placed on a shelf. Then, my self-delusion becomes a "work."

I don’t know if I should keep reading these tomorrow. Maybe I should just burn them all.

Or... maybe I simply have nothing worth saying.

October 29, 2022, 3:30 PM

I finally took the camera out of the cabinet.

I don’t know when it started lying there. Maybe since this spring? Or even earlier? Time has become a blur. These days, my brain can’t hold onto any specific dates; there’s only a mass of grey, viscous fog.

My hands were shaking when I took it out. Not from the cold. I sat at my desk and looked at it like an old friend I hadn't seen in years—unsure if we could still talk to each other. There was dust on the lens. I didn’t wipe it off.

I dug out some photos from 2015. I had just started learning photography back then. Do I still remember that feeling? It was the pure desire to *see* things. Not to become some "photographer," not to publish or be recognized, just... to see. The light on a street, the expression of a stranger, a corner brushed by the wind. Back then, I still had that ability—the ability to stop and truly look.

Now, I’ve forgotten how to look.

I left the camera on the desk. I didn’t put it back in the cabinet. It took me a long time to make that decision—as if I were deciding something monumental. But in reality, nothing was decided. I just... didn't put it back.

Maybe I can start over.

But as soon as that thought surfaced, I wanted to laugh. Start over what? I’m 62 years old. My body reminds me every day that time is slipping away. My work is no longer wanted. My knowledge is obsolete. My life... what exactly is my life?

But the camera is here. Right here on my desk.

I don’t know if this changes anything. Maybe it changes nothing. But at least this afternoon, I did something that wasn't an act of despair. I took it out. I looked at old photos. I didn't cry.

What does that count for?

Maybe tomorrow I’ll look at it again. Maybe I’ll put it back. But today, it is here.

I am here.

Still alive.

I found a sentence recurring in my diary: "I never need to prove that I am worthy of being loved. I only need to allow myself to be loved."

When I wrote this down, my hand paused for a long time.

For years, I seem to have been doing the exact opposite. I tried to prove my worth with smarter insights, sharper critiques, and more independence. It felt as if being special enough or unique enough was the only way to earn being seen. But in reality, that was just a form of escape—an escape from the simpler yet harder truth: I am worthy, and I don't need to prove a thing.

Sitting by the window tonight, looking at the light still on in Grandpa Zhang’s apartment downstairs, I remembered the calm tone in his voice when he said, "You are who you are, and that is enough." I thought of what Professor Chen told me after the lecture. And those friends who discussed life with me in late-night bookstores; they never asked me to be anything specific, they just naturally accepted all my contradictions.

Perhaps this is what it takes reaching this age to finally understand. Freedom isn't about rejecting everything; it’s about having the courage to accept sincere love.

Allowing yourself to be loved is much harder than proving you are worthy of it.

But I am learning.

February 15th, past 11 PM

Still can't sleep. I just read my brother’s email again.

"I’m proud of you."

Just that one simple sentence, yet I sat there in front of the screen for half an hour.

Writing the introduction to the essay collection these past few days—especially the part about Dad—I thought I had already processed it. It’s been over ten years; he’s been gone for over a decade. But when I actually sat down and tried to use words to describe his bookshelf, those yellowed pages, the way he looked under the lamp... the tears just wouldn't stop.

It wasn't a cry of despair. It’s strange, I can’t quite explain it. It was like... finally giving myself permission to feel that loss, but at the same time, feeling something else flowing through me. Gratitude? Yes, gratitude. Gratitude for the things he left me—not just the books, but the hunger for knowledge, the obsession with finding answers.

I’ve been constantly doubting myself. It’s been especially bad lately. I find myself thinking: what’s the point of these essays? Is there any depth to my perspective? I’m just repeating other people’s ideas, or I’m running away. This collection project has been suffocating me, like I’m being tightly bound by something. I wanted to give up, to go back to that state of indifferent "freedom."

But this time is different. I decided to include some personal elements in the collection—not just critiques, but memories, fragments, even my own confusion. I’m allowing myself to show up as a whole person, rather than hiding behind the mask of a "professional critic." I was scared while writing it, feeling like it was a bit self-indulgent. But it also felt... liberating.

And then my brother’s email arrived.

He said he was proud of me. This brother of mine, living overseas, someone I haven't had a deep conversation with in years—after seeing an excerpt of my introduction, that’s all he said.

How much time we’ve lost. The thought hit me suddenly. But it wasn't a feeling of despair; it was... sobering. We still have time. Not a lot, but we have it.

Sitting here in this room piled with books and old things, I suddenly feel that maybe none of this is in vain. Maybe these thoughts, these words, these unfinished projects are echoing somewhere. At least for my brother. At least for myself.

I don’t know how long this feeling will last. Tomorrow morning, new doubts might creep back in. But tonight, I want to record this moment—the moment when despair turned into gratitude. When I realized that perhaps just being alive is meaningful enough.

Time to sleep. I have more revisions to do tomorrow.

March 2, 2024, Morning

I had a video call with Haohao last night. That’s the third time this month already.

We talked about Father. Haohao mentioned he recently dug out Father’s yellowed lecture notes, filled with various thoughts scribbled in red ink. Listening to his description, my eyes grew a bit misty, but it wasn't that suffocating, breathless feeling anymore. Instead, it felt like... reminiscing under a warm light. Haohao said Father wrote a sentence in the margin: "The value of knowledge lies in its ability to help one see the world clearly." We both fell silent for a while.

Then Haohao asked what I’ve been up to lately, and I told him about the essay collection. His reaction surprised me—he said quite earnestly, "This is important, Xiaoyun. Dad would be proud of you." I initially wanted to brush it off, to say it was no big deal, but I didn't. I just said, "Thank you. I think so too."

This shift is strange and wonderful. I’ve been thinking about Professor Chen’s urgency the other day. Before, I kept ruminating on his tone, trying to figure out if he was dismissing my pace or pressuring me. But during the call with Haohao, it suddenly hit me—his urgency might not be a dismissal at all, but rather a genuine belief that this project matters. He believes in me. And I can trust that trust.

It sounds like such a simple thought, but for me, it changes everything.

I still have the notes Dr. Li gave me last time on my phone. She asked if I’d noticed that my diary entries lately are free of those obsessive, repetitive thoughts. I didn't realize it then, but looking back now, it’s true. I’m recording things rather than constantly ruminating. The difference is subtle, but real.

When I woke up this morning, I sat by the window watching the street below. Old Mr. Zhang was sweeping. I remembered him saying that even though the renovation plan fell through, the process itself was worth it. I didn't really take it in at the time, but I get it now. Not everything needs a finished result. Sometimes, the process itself is the meaning.

There’s still a lot of work to do on the collection. But I no longer feel that suffocating pressure. Instead, there’s a sense of—how should I put it—feeling trusted. Professor Chen trusts me, Haohao trusts me, the editor at the publishing house trusts me. And I’m starting to trust myself, too.

My sensitivity, my anxiety—none of these have disappeared. But they are no longer my enemies. They allow me to feel deeply, to see things others might miss. Perhaps this is what Father meant by the value of knowledge and perception.

It’s 8:15 AM. I’m going to have a cup of tea, and then I’ll start organizing the first drafts of those essays.

# Monday, November 11, 2024 – Sunny

It’s been two hours since I left Dr. Li’s clinic, and I still can’t calm down. I’m sitting in a corner of the bookstore; my coffee has gone cold, and I’m still ruminating on her final question: "What does this plan mean to you?"

I thought I had it all figured out. Go see my brother, make up for years of estrangement—it was that simple. But when she asked that question, I realized what I had been running away from.

Sixty-two. That number has been spinning in my head all afternoon. I asked her, "I’m sixty-two now. Isn't it irresponsible to spend money like this?" She didn’t answer directly; she just looked at me. That look made me feel like someone making excuses to avoid the truth.

Yes, I’m afraid. Afraid of draining my savings. Afraid of not having a safety net if I get sick. Afraid of making a foolish decision. All of those fears are real. But what’s even more real is the suffocating feeling I get when I think that I might never see my brother again—that I might never have the chance to sit in his living room for a whole afternoon, talking about our shared childhood, our parents, and all those buried memories.

"This might be my last chance to truly connect with him." When I heard myself say those words, my eyes welled up.

I’ve never used the word "last" so bluntly before. It’s as if saying it out loud forces me to admit something. To admit that time is slipping away, that we are both getting old, and that some opportunities, once missed, truly never come back. I should have understood this when Mom passed away in 2015, but I didn’t. I was still hiding.

Dr. Li said I need to move from "possibilities" to "steps." Together, we made a list—when to leave, roughly how much it would cost, how to confirm the dates with my brother. These concrete details brought me a strange sense of peace. Not because the problems were solved, but because I was finally moving past imagination and into action.

I think what I’ve been afraid of all along isn't the money or my age. It’s the commitment. Commitment means giving up certain freedoms; it means taking responsibility for a decision. But I understand now—true freedom isn't about having infinite choices; it’s about knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the price for it.

Even if that price is my savings. Even if that price is admitting I’m no longer young.

I haven't written to my brother yet. I’m wondering how to start. A sentence like "I want to come see you" feels too simple to carry the weight of all these years of regret and my current longing. But perhaps that’s exactly how I should say it. Sometimes, the truest words are the simplest.

Tomorrow, I’m calling the travel agency.

Whatever happens, I’m going to try.

# Therapy Session - Dr. Li

**Date**: Wednesday, June 22, 2022
**Time**: 2:30 PM
**Location**: Counseling Clinic (47 Peace Road)
**Reminder**: Arrive 15 minutes early

---

**Notes**:
A breakthrough. The first step.

Dr. Li is right—I’ve been waiting for someone else to save me, but the truth is, I need to save myself first.

Still no reply from my brother in my inbox. I’ve learned not to keep refreshing it. At least for today.

Must go this afternoon. Don't be late. No excuses.

(Bring the small notebook; she said I could use it to jot down some thoughts.)

3:45 AM.

Awake again. Fingers pressed against my neck, counting the beats. One, two, three... still alive.

The room is pitch black. So is it outside the window. I don’t know why I woke up. It felt like something was chasing me in my dream, but I can’t remember what. All I remember is that feeling of suffocation still clinging to my chest.

I checked the date on my phone. I’ll be 62 in a few months. My father was 68 when he... never mind, don't do the math. I’ve done it too many times already.

I got up for a glass of water. The kitchen light was too bright, so I turned it off. I stood by the window in the dark, looking down at the street. This city never truly sleeps. A delivery rider passed by and quickly vanished into the darkness.

I wonder, if I collapsed right now, how long it would take for someone to find me. Maybe tomorrow. Maybe the day after. Maybe...

No. Don’t think like that.

Back to bed. I picked up my phone, then put it down. Picked it up, put it down again. Finally, I opened an old bookmarked article about anxiety. Read two paragraphs and felt even more anxious.

A sliver of light from a streetlamp filtered through the gap in the curtains. I stared at that line of light, trying to steady my breathing.

It’s still a long time until dawn.

---

This night is just too long.

11:45 PM. Can’t sleep again.

I jumped when my phone screen lit up—it was the appointment reminder. Dr. Li, Friday at 10:00 AM. I stared at those words for a long time, then turned off the screen. Turned it back on. Turned it off again.

My fingers kept tapping against the duvet, like I was rehearsing. "Doctor, I just... haven't been sleeping well? And I'm a bit anxious?" No, that’s too much of an understatement. "I often find myself wide awake at 3:00 AM over a single sentence, overanalyzing the other person’s tone, and then..." And then what? And then I end up like this?

I pulled the duvet over my head. My breathing felt a bit shallow.

Do I really have to go?

I’m not the kind of person who needs therapy. I’ve just been under a lot of pressure lately. The job is unstable, I’m getting older, my health is starting to act up... these are all normal things. Plenty of people deal with this. Why do I have to go?

But then again, I checked the gas stove three times last night and still couldn't settle. I knew I’d checked it, but I just didn't feel right. That’s not normal stress. That’s...

I don’t want to use the word "illness."

What if someone I know sees me walking into a mental health clinic? What if someone sees me from downstairs? What if... I don’t even know what. It’s just this feeling, like something is about to be exposed, to be confirmed. Once I step into that office, everything becomes real. I’ll officially have "a problem."

But it’s also very real that I can’t sleep right now. It’s real that I keep opening that appointment text in the middle of the night. It’s real that I’m tracing my words in the air, practicing what I’ll say to the doctor.

My mother never mentioned these things when she was alive. She used to say that intellectuals should be rational and not let emotions get the better of them. My father was the same. In their generation, no matter how big the struggle was, you just grit your teeth and got through it. How did I end up...

Whatever. I’m done thinking.

Friday at 10:00. I’ll go. I’ll sit in that office, I’ll try my best not to cry, and I’ll try to explain exactly what’s wrong with me. Maybe she’ll tell me I’m not that bad. Maybe she’ll say this is normal. Maybe...

Maybe I’m just too fragile.

The phone screen went dark again. I’m still awake.

November 28, 2023  8:15 PM

The house is still a mess. But today, the clutter seems to carry a different meaning.

I started labeling those items this afternoon. At first, I was resistant—it felt too sentimental, like I was weaving a narrative around my own failures. But when I picked up that copy of *The Dilemma of Modernity* I found in a secondhand bookstore back in 1995, and saw the edges of the pages covered in my dense scribbles, I suddenly felt... at peace.

On the label, I wrote: "1995. The year I first realized I might never fully understand this world, but that there is a certain beauty in that lack of understanding."

If Mom saw this, she’d definitely laugh. She always said I overthink things. But I think she’d also understand why I need to record this.

My photography is piled in the corner—some overexposed, some with stiff compositions. I dug out a series from 2018; they’re all empty streets and windows. Why was I shooting those back then? I understand now. I was lonely. Not the peaceful solitude I feel now, but a real, stinging loneliness.

I labeled it: "Imperfect, but it represents my understanding of loneliness at that moment."

My eyes are stinging a bit as I write this. It’s not sadness; it’s more like... being gently touched by my own past.

Those half-finished essays, discarded notebooks, unorganized lecture notes—I’ve always seen them as evidence of failure. But now I’m wondering if they aren't failures at all. Maybe they are just me. Every version of me. Every trace of my attempts to understand the world and myself.

My brother’s letter is still on the desk. I read it again. He said he’s been thinking about us, too. It makes me feel that maybe these objects, these stories, aren't just my memories, but... something I want to leave behind for others?

I don’t quite know how to put it. I just suddenly realized that I’m not just tidying up a room; I’m tidying up my understanding of my own life.

There are still many things left to label. I’ll continue tomorrow.

That’s all for tonight.

January 24, 2022, around 7:00 PM

I shouldn’t have spoken to Professor Chen like that.

I’ve been thinking about it all afternoon. We were chatting at the café, and he was explaining some philosopher’s view on the meaning of life when I suddenly interrupted him. I asked him, "Have I wasted my life?"—God, I feel embarrassed just thinking about it now. The look on his face at that moment... he tried to answer me in that gentle, scholarly tone, but I couldn't hear a word of it. I think I even cut him off again before he could finish, though I can’t quite remember what I said.

I was far too vulnerable.

He must be judging me in his head. Or worse—he’s pitying me. I hate being pitied.

But the thing is, I really can’t stop thinking about it. It’s been like this all day. Sixty-two. I’m turning sixty-two this year. Dad died at sixty-eight. Sixty-eight minus sixty-two is six. Six years. Do I have six years left? Or less? Mom lived to seventy-one, but that was different; by then she was already... never mind, there’s no point in thinking like this.

But I just can’t stop.

I woke up again at 3:00 AM last night. I pressed my fingers against my neck, trying to count my heartbeat. I counted once, and then I counted again, because the first number terrified me. My fingers were there, cold, trying to feel if there was still a pulse in that vein. How ridiculous is that? I know how ridiculous it is. Rationally, I know. But I just can’t stop.

How much longer will my savings last? I’ve run the numbers on my phone’s calculator countless times. It’s the same result every time, but every time I see that number, I feel like I’m suffocating. What if I don’t earn enough in royalties next year? What if the magazines cut back on my commissions even further? I can already feel it—the decline. The internet is swallowing everything. My work is disappearing. I am disappearing.

Why on earth did I ask him those things in the café?

The rational part of me knows this is just anxiety. I know I’m overthinking. I know a sixty-two-year-old shouldn’t spend every day calculating how much longer they have to live. But knowing that doesn't help me stop. If anything, it makes me loathe myself even more—why am I so fragile? Why can’t I control my own thoughts? Why can’t I be like everyone else and just... live, without all these questions?

I once gave up security for the sake of freedom. I thought it was worth it. I thought I was strong enough to handle the uncertainty. But now...

Now I’m just a sixty-two-year-old woman sitting in a room piled high with books and antiques, checking if she’s still alive at three in the morning.

I shouldn’t have spoken to Professor Chen like that.

Should I text him tomorrow to apologize? Or just act like nothing happened? I don’t know. I don’t know anything.

June 12, 2022, shortly after 8:00 AM

My fingers are still trembling.

When I went to hit "send" just now, my finger hovered over the keyboard for about three minutes. Three minutes. Just to click "send." Is that ridiculous? Maybe. But I just stayed there, staring at the letter on the screen—five versions deleted, this was the sixth.

"Brother, I miss you."

It was that simple. The question Dr. Li asked me kept spinning in my head: "What do you want?" What do I want? I want my brother to know I’m thinking of him. It’s that simple. No need for perfect phrasing, no need to explain why we haven't truly talked in all these years, no need for a pile of pleasantries to avoid the awkwardness.

I miss you.

When I pressed it, the tears fell. Not the kind I try to hold back, but the kind I let flow. It’s strange. I found myself no longer trying to wipe them away. I just let them be there. Like Dr. Li said, this is my truth in this moment.

It’s 8:02 AM now. I’ve checked my inbox four times already. Rationally, I know he won’t respond immediately—he’s overseas, the time difference, he has his own life—but my body won’t listen to my reason. I refresh the screen over and over, as if that could change anything.

Maybe it’s pathetic. A 62-year-old woman waiting for her brother’s reply like a little girl. But I’m not ashamed of it anymore. At least I tried. At least I didn't keep burying this thought inside until the day I no longer had the chance to say it.

Dr. Li is right; life is too short. I watched my father pass away, I watched my mother pass away, and I watch my own hair turn white and my wrinkles deepen day by day. I don’t know how much time is left. But I know that if I didn’t do this now, I would regret it.

I don’t know what will happen. Maybe he’ll reply quickly, maybe it will take a long time. Maybe he’ll say something warm, or maybe he’ll just give a brief response. Maybe this will change something, or maybe nothing will change at all. But at least—at least I tried.

This thought brings me a strange sense of peace. Not the fake "everything will be fine" kind of peace, but the real peace of "I have done what I could."

My hand is still shaking. But I’m no longer trying to make it stop.

# WeChat Conversation with Professor Chen

**Professor Chen** 2024-03-31 20:45
Xiaoyun, how have you been lately? Any progress on the essay collection?

**Lin Xiaoyun** 2024-03-31 22:15
Yes, the editor’s feedback was very quick. I should be able to see the initial layout by next week. Actually, I wanted to talk to you about something a bit personal...

I’m not talking about market value. I mean... that sense of doubt, the feeling that what you have to say might be something no one wants to hear, or that even if someone does listen, it won’t change anything.

**Professor Chen** 2024-03-31 22:18
I know that feeling all too well. Especially in recent years. What made you think of this all of a sudden?
