Because I’ve been making some changes lately. Counseling, reorganizing my thoughts, organizing book clubs... all of this has made me rethink one question: why am I doing this?

I used to think the answer was "because I like it" or "because it’s what I’m good at." But recently I’ve realized that maybe the real answer is—I want to share something with others. Not to prove anything, just... to share.

**Professor Chen** 2024-03-31 22:25
That sounds like a very significant shift.

**Lin Xiaoyun** 2024-03-31 22:28
It is. I used to be so afraid—afraid of being sensitive, afraid of anxiety, afraid that my ideas were too weird or impractical. Now I’m starting to feel that maybe these traits are exactly what allow me to see things that others don’t.

You know? When I was discussing a book with those young people at the book club, one girl said, "I’ve never thought about it that way before." In that moment, I finally understood—my value isn't in how much I know, but in my ability to help others see a new perspective.

The value of life isn't in its length, but in its depth and meaning. I truly believe that now.

**Professor Chen** 2024-03-31 22:32
Xiaoyun, what you just said reminds me of some of the confusion I felt when I was your age. I’m so glad you’ve found this answer.

Once these dates are written down, they become real. It’s no longer just Prof. Chen saying, "You should publish"; it’s me telling myself, "Okay, I’m doing this."

Sometimes I wonder if this is my old habit acting up again—overcommitting, only to regret it at 3:00 AM. But this time feels different. These ideas are the genuine accumulation of my last ten-plus years. Perhaps they truly deserve to be organized and shared.

I just... hope I can stick with it.

May 25, 2022, 3:00 AM

I can’t sleep. Still can’t sleep.

The letter is gone. Yesterday afternoon, I stood in front of the post office for five minutes. Five minutes. Just standing there, staring at the address written in my own handwriting on the envelope—Lin Hao, Toronto. My hands were shaking; I could feel it. It was cold inside the post office, the air conditioning was blasting, but my hands were still shaking. A staff member asked if I needed help, and that’s when I realized I’d been standing there for a long time.

I went in. I walked to the counter and handed the letter to her. Just like that. No more thinking, no more re-reading the contents (I’d already read it five times), I just let it go.

It was a strange moment. When the letter disappeared from my hand and she placed it on the sorting carousel behind her, I felt a sense of... how do I put it... relief? But at the same time, terror. The two feelings were so tangled together I didn’t even know which was stronger. My legs felt a bit weak. I stood in the post office for a while longer, watching that carousel spin until the letter was out of sight.

Twenty-two years. We haven't truly communicated in twenty-two years. That photo from 1985—he had his arm around my shoulder, and we were both laughing. Back then he was still here, really here. Now he’s in Toronto and I’m here, with half the world between us.

What did I write in the letter? I don’t even fully remember. I wrote about that photo. I wrote about how I’ve been thinking of him. I wrote... I don’t know. I wrote a lot about regret. About time.

I sent a message to Zhou Yu saying, "I sent it." She replied with a hug emoji. But she doesn't understand. No one can understand what those five minutes felt like.

It’s 3:00 AM now. I’ve checked my inbox five times. Five times. I know there’s no way he could reply this fast. He’s probably still sleeping. Or maybe he won’t reply at all. Or he’ll reply, but it’ll be cold, or angry, or... I don’t know.

I’m sixty-two this year. My father was this age when he passed away. I’ve always remembered that number. I remember the way he looked the last time he saw me. I don’t want to be like him... I don’t want to... without having spoken to my brother.

No. Don’t think like that.

But I can’t help it. My phone is right beside me. I’ll keep checking my email until... until what? Until he replies? Until I’m sure he received it? Until I’m sure he isn’t angry?

What am I doing? This is so stupid. It’s so... so late.

Maybe. Maybe not.

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

It’s still dark outside. It’ll be a long time before dawn.

Good morning, Dr. Li.

I wanted to give you an update on how things have been going this week. I’ve kept that one sentence in mind—"Maybe I just need to learn to coexist with it"—and I even circled it in my journal. Sometimes, when I can’t sleep in the middle of the night, I think of those words, and it makes me feel a little less afraid.

I downloaded that meditation app and stuck with it for about three weeks. To be honest, there were a few times I gave up halfway through, feeling like I was just wasting my time. But I started again this week. I don’t know if that counts as progress; maybe I’m just kidding myself.

Also, I’ve started tracking when my anxiety gets particularly bad in my journal. The day before yesterday, it was a news story about aging; yesterday, it was an email about a deadline. Once I write it down, things seem a bit clearer.

It’s just… is this actually working? I’m still waking up at 3:00 AM, sometimes even more frequently than before. Maybe the improvement is just in my head.

Is there anything else I should pay attention to before our next session?

I’ve been wondering whether I should share this thought with you.

While I was chatting with Professor Chen yesterday, a sentence suddenly slipped out: "I’m considering publishing a collection of essays to document my forty years of reflection." I surprised even myself the moment I said it, because while the idea has lived in my heart for a long time, I had never actually uttered it aloud.

His reaction caught me off guard. He said, "These reflections of yours represent the profound thinking of a generation of intellectuals on culture. This is important."

My eyes welled up a bit right then. It might sound like an exaggeration, but in that moment, I realized that perhaps I have been measuring my work by the wrong standards all along. I’ve been thinking that the book reviews and critiques I’ve written over the years might have no market value—traditional publishing has been hit hard by the internet, and my professional skills might have become obsolete long ago. But what Professor Chen said made me realize that perhaps value isn't defined solely by the market.

In these forty years, from my time as an editor to my years as a freelancer, I have encountered so many ideas and so many works. I have constantly been pondering what culture is, what meaning is, and what is worth being recorded. These reflections are real to me; they have shaped how I see the world.

I’m wondering now: if I truly organize these writings—not for the market, not to prove anything, but simply to document the thought process of an intellectual in this era—would there be meaning in that?

I know my energy isn't what it used to be, and my financial situation has always been tight. But perhaps, this is exactly the time I should do this. While I still remember, and while I can still think.

What do you think?

# Phone Memo

**March 25, 2022, 9:30 PM**

---

Dr. Li said, "Anxiety isn't your failure; it's your signal."

I sat in her office, my fingers twisting incessantly. It took me a moment to realize—I’ve never treated anxiety as a "signal." I’ve always treated it as a secret, a piece of evidence proving I’m not strong enough.

She asked when my sleep started falling apart. I couldn't say for sure. Maybe last year? Maybe earlier? Time has all blurred together. She didn't offer any "solutions." Instead, she asked me, "If it wasn't for the sake of being 'normal,' what is it that you actually need?"

I didn't have an answer.

---

I walked for a long time after the session. The convenience store at night looked strange—under those pale lights, all that exhaustion became somewhat transparent. I sat there with three unread messages on my phone, none of which I checked. I just watched the traffic outside and felt my body slowly sinking. Not falling, but... relaxing?

It’s a foreign feeling.

---

I slept deeply tonight. No dreams. No waking up at 3:00 AM. None of that feeling like something was pressing down on my chest.

When I woke up, it wasn't light out yet, but I wasn't afraid. It’s strange.

---

Maybe change isn't a grand event. Maybe it’s just sitting in a convenience store, admitting you’re tired, and then actually falling asleep.

Maybe that’s all it is.

---

Remember this night. Remember this feeling.

Just stepped out of the Exit and Entry Administration office. My passport application is finally submitted.

To be honest, I felt a bit nervous walking into that office—not because of the paperwork itself, but because of what it represents. It’s no longer "maybe I’ll go see my brother"; it’s actually happening. At 62, I’m going to fly solo to see a brother I haven't had a deep conversation with in years.

When the officer asked for the purpose of my trip, I said "family visit." My throat tightened a little as those two words came out.

The funniest part is that I’ve always prided myself on "hating plans and constraints," yet here I am with a memo on my phone, listing flights, hotels, and packing items. If my friends saw my to-do list, they’d die laughing—this doesn't look like Lin Xiaoyun at all. But that’s exactly how I know how much this means to me.

It’ll take 15 working days to get the passport. During this time, I’ll probably keep double-checking if I put the right address (I’ve already checked three times) and waking up in the middle of the night wondering, "Did I forget something?" But at the same time, I’m looking forward to it. Looking forward to seeing that blue booklet and the possibilities it represents.

It feels strange yet good. That’s probably the best way to describe how I feel right now.

# Reading Group Notes & Publication Plan Feedback
**Date**: September 15, 2024  
**Attendance**: 8  
**Book Discussed**: *Methods and Ethics of Cultural Criticism* (Selected Chapters)

---

## Core Feedback Snippets

**Keywords**:
- "The critic's stance" ← Recurring theme; needs further depth.
- "Expression of generational differences" ← A particular focus for younger participants.
- "Balance between personal experience vs. theoretical frameworks" ← The most tense/dynamic point of discussion.
- "The 'warmth' of criticism" ← An interesting term used by a student.

**Most Valuable Discussion**:
A professional in the group asked: Why does the critic’s personal background matter so much? Do we read criticism for objective analysis, or for a flesh-and-blood voice?

My response might not have been clear enough. The published manuscript should explicitly state that objectivity and personal perspective are not opposites; they complement each other. This point needs to be supported by more concrete examples.

---

## Implications for the Publication Plan

**Chapter 2 (The Ethics of the Critic)**:
- Add a subsection on "The Personal in Criticism."
- Use specific cases mentioned in today’s discussion (after obtaining consent).
- Include a practical tip on "how to maintain a personal voice while avoiding subjective dogmatism."

**Chapter 4 (Methods for Different Cultural Forms)**:
- Participants mentioned generational gaps—consider adding a section on "How to Critique Youth Culture."
- This is an angle I hadn't fully considered before.

**Overall Structure**:
- The "conversational feel" of the reading group made me realize the manuscript might be too academic.
- Consider adding a "FAQ" or "Dialogue Box" at the end of each chapter.
- This would maintain academic rigor while preserving the vitality of the discussion.

---

## Specific Participant Feedback

A retired teacher noted: "I’ve encountered exactly what you’re talking about in my own teaching. When students ask, 'Professor, what’s your take?', I realize I need to give them both a method and a glimpse into how I actually think."

This is crucial. It suggests the book’s practical utility might be stronger than I anticipated.

---

## Next Steps

- [ ] Transcribe and organize today’s recording notes (consent obtained).
- [ ] Email Professor Chen to share the "personality vs. objectivity" discussion and get his thoughts.
- [ ] Revise the Chapter 2 draft to incorporate today’s feedback.
- [ ] For the next session (mid-October), choose a "Criticism in Practice" theme. Have participants try critiquing a contemporary cultural phenomenon using the methods I’ve proposed.
- [ ] Consider inviting two participants (a student and a professional) to be "beta readers" for more in-depth feedback before publication.

---

## Personal Observations

This session made me realize something I’d overlooked: I’ve been worried about whether my knowledge is "systematic enough," but the most valuable parts are actually the "imperfect" insights drawn from real-world experience.

The participants aren't looking for a flawless theoretical treatise; they are looking for a thoughtful, human voice.

This shifts my understanding of the publication project. It’s not about "completing a book"—it’s about "sharing a way of thinking."

There’s a big difference.

---

**Next Review Date**: October 1, 2024 (After draft revisions)

# Phone Memo

**April 2, 2023 | AM**

Found these while organizing my photos—20 shots worth keeping.

---

**Urban Loneliness Series**
- A silhouette on the subway platform (backlit, sharp outlines) — captured that feeling of being submerged yet seen.
- Reflections in a storefront window on a rainy night — the world in the glass feels more real than reality itself.
- A bicycle parked in front of an old building, unridden — the act of waiting is a story in its own right.

**Portraits**
- Auntie Wang’s profile at the wet market — forty years of life etched into her wrinkles.
- That girl sitting in the corner of the bookstore — what was she reading? I’ll never know, but there’s a beauty in that mystery.
- Uncle Zhang’s hands as he repairs a bike — the movement of his fingers speaks more than his face ever could.

**Evidence of Light and Shadow**
- The stairwell at 6:00 AM — light slicing through space, and through time.
- The shadow of a coffee cup on white paper — as simple as it gets, so why is it so powerful?
- Dust motes dancing in the sunlight on a bookshelf — even the forgotten things are glowing.

---

**Why these work:**
- Not because of technical perfection (my skills are far from perfect).
- But because they captured a genuine moment — not something I imagined, but something that truly existed.
- Every shot asks a question rather than providing an answer.
- Some photos showed me things I’d never noticed before — it proves the lens was working, and I was working.

---

Maybe this is enough.

No need for an exhibition, no need for anyone else’s validation.

Just knowing that these photos exist, and that they are real.

Brother, after we hung up just now, I sat there for a long time.

In that silence, I finally understood what a real connection feels like. We didn't need to say a word to feel each other’s presence. I think you felt it too.

I’ve always kept my nephew and niece’s faces in my heart. Seeing them there by your side, it suddenly hit me—I’ve missed every single moment of them growing up. But you know what? Thinking about it that way actually brought me peace. It’s not regret, it’s... knowing that there is still a chance.

You said, "You changed everything." I want to tell you this: you changed my understanding of loneliness. All these years on my own, I thought this was just my fate. But tonight I realized that loneliness was never a curse. It was just waiting for a true connection. And you were always there.

About going abroad—I’m serious. I want to see the life you’ve built, and I want to truly sit and spend time with my nephew and niece. How does next spring sound? I can start saving up and request time off in advance. If that works for you all.

Also, let’s not wait this long to talk again. Once a month, okay? A fixed time. I need this.

Thank you for not giving up on talking to me tonight.

#HomeTreasures #MaybeThisIsEnough

May 14, 2025, Afternoon

While tidying up the room just now, I pulled a yellowed envelope from behind the bookshelf. Inside was a photo of my brother and me—probably around 1995. We were in our father’s study; he was sitting in a chair, and I was leaning against his shoulder. That was before he moved abroad, back when we would still discuss books together and laugh at our father’s opinions.

Looking at the photo, my first reaction was that familiar sense of regret—how did we end up like this? Years without a real conversation, only perfunctory greetings during holidays. But this time, it felt different. I didn’t spiral into that repetitive self-reproach. Instead, I felt... a gentle acceptance of the fact. We have our separate lives now, but this photo is proof that we once truly existed in each other’s worlds. That is enough.

This afternoon, I started preparing the things I’ll bring to see him. I finally decided: a book on contemporary Chinese photography (he’s always been interested in my work), a tin of Wuyi Rock Tea I bought, and—this makes me a bit nervous—prints of a few selected photographs of mine. They were all taken over the last two years—cityscapes, portraits—some of which I’m not even fully satisfied with yet. But I want him to see who I am now, not just hear what I have to say.

This is completely different from my previous obsessive state of checking my passport over and over and scouring travel guides. Back then, I was drowning in details, constantly thinking, "What if something goes wrong?" What I’m doing now comes from a place of love. I’m thinking about "what I want him to see," rather than "whether I’ll mess this up."

To be honest, I’m still nervous. That pre-meeting jitters. But it’s not that suffocating anxiety. It’s more like... anticipation? Or rather, the kind of tension that says, "This matters." I’m 62, and he’s not young anymore; how many more chances will we have to meet like this? That thought used to wake me up in the middle of the night in a panic, but now it only makes me more certain that this trip is the right thing to do.

The year my mother passed away, I wrote in my diary: "We only learn to cherish things after we’ve lost them." Now, I want to change that—we can learn to cherish things before they are completely lost.

I’ll continue packing tomorrow. I still need to double-check the flight details. But I wanted to record how I feel today, so that when I look back, I can remember this shift—that the distance from anxiety to love is, in fact, just a turn away.

Sometimes we lose each other in silence, and sometimes, we find each other in it again.

I had a long phone call with my brother today. When he mentioned his plans to move back home, I didn't rush to argue or question him like I usually do. Instead, I asked "why" many times—but this time, I truly wanted to listen.

We talked for a long while. About our parents, about those missing years, and about our lives now. He said he’s been thinking a lot about the distance that grew between us over the years. I told him I had been, too.

After hanging up, I was sorting through some old things and found a family photo from a long time ago. We were both so young then, still living under the same roof. Looking at his face in the photo, I suddenly realized something:

**The deepest connections sometimes come right after the longest silences.**

I don’t know why, but I feel a profound sense of peace tonight.

---

#Family #Time #NewBeginnings

# May 20, 2025, Evening

Dr. Li said something today that I’m still turning over in my mind.

She asked me, "What do you think anxiety means?" My gut reaction was to say, "Something is wrong." Then she asked, "Wrong for whom?" I froze. She continued, "Maybe anxiety isn't a signal of a malfunction, but proof that you care."

I sat there, feeling something in my mind shift ever so slightly.

For the past two weeks, I’ve been obsessively checking my passport, my tickets, my itinerary—even checking the weather forecast from Shanghai to Beijing at three in the morning. I thought this was my "condition" acting up again—that compulsive, uncontrollable anxiety. But Dr. Li said that maybe I’m just taking something seriously because it matters to me.

I had never thought of it that way.

Going to see my brother this time is no small thing. For over ten years, we’ve only exchanged occasional greetings, like two strangers maintaining a polite distance. I always told myself it was because we were both too busy, or because we simply had nothing to say. But the truth is, I was afraid—afraid that when we finally met, I’d discover we really have nothing left to talk about. Afraid to confirm that the estrangement is real and irreparable.

So, I am anxious. And that’s normal.

Dr. Li also mentioned that my decision to reduce the frequency of our sessions isn't just a sign of "improvement," but a sign that I’ve started to trust myself to take care of myself. My eyes welled up when I heard that. When did I stop believing in myself?

Maybe it started when my father passed away. Maybe it was the divorce. Or maybe it was just all those years spent alone in this room, watching the stacks of books grow higher, watching the days slip by, watching the person in the mirror slowly grow old... I began to believe that I was fragile, something easily broken.

But here I am at sixty-two, making a decision like this—spending a portion of my savings to see someone I want to see, to experience something I want to experience. That isn't fragility. It’s... what is it?

Maybe it’s courage. Maybe it’s just what it means to be alive.

I’m looking out the window now at the evening sky as it grows dark. Tomorrow, the preparations continue. My anxiety won’t disappear, but I no longer see it as an enemy. It’s just saying, "This is important. You care. Go do it right."

I think I can live with that.

# Preliminary Chapter Framework for *Notes on Cultural Observation*

**Date:** Evening of September 5, 2024
**Location:** Living Room (Lecture notes spread across the floor)

---

## Part I: Dialogue Between Reading and the Era

### Chapter 1: "Classics Reread"
- **Related Lecture:** Autumn 2023 Salon: "Why Do Classics Go Out of Style?"
- **Core Argument:** The value of classic literature lies not in its permanence, but in the fact that every era can discover new meaning within it. Take *Dream of the Red Chamber* as an example: readers from different decades see entirely different things.
- **To Supplement:** The impact of contemporary web literature on traditional classics—a new idea mentioned in the September lecture.
- **References:** Find that old commentary on "literary generational renewal"; it should be in the 2015 notes.

### Chapter 2: "The Mirror of Publishing—We Are What We Read"
- **Related Lecture:** 2022 discussion with Professor Chen.
- **Core Argument:** Publishing choices reflect social psychology. From my 25 years of observation in the industry, the bestsellers of any given period are projections of that era's anxieties.
- **To Supplement:** The transformation of publishing in the internet age—I’ve written a commentary on this, but the data needs updating.
- **To Ponder:** Why did I shift from traditional publishing to freelance writing? What was the underlying logic behind that choice?

### Chapter 3: "The Privacy and Publicity of Reading"
- **Related Lecture:** Multiple discussions at the Book Club.
- **Core Argument:** Reading is both the most private act and the most public one. My book club is currently exploring this tension.
- **To Supplement:** Participant feedback—how they rediscover personal reading experiences through collective discussion.
- **Citations:** Look for relevant works of literary theory.

---

## Part II: Methodology of Cultural Criticism

### Chapter 4: "The Position of the Critic"
- **Related Lecture:** Early 2024 notes on "The Ethics of Criticism."
- **Core Argument:** A critic should not be a high-and-mighty judge, but an honest reader. My biggest shift over the years has been moving from "judging" to "dialogue."
- **To Supplement:** Specific case study—that review of a certain work where I later changed my mind.
- **Reflection:** How my "low agreeableness" manifests in my reviews—bluntness is sometimes honesty, sometimes a wound.

### Chapter 5: "Cultural Representations of Contemporary Syndromes"
- **Related Lecture:** Social Issues Discussion Series.
- **Core Argument:** By observing cultural products (film, literature, art), we can see the anxieties, desires, and contradictions of an era.
- **To Supplement:** Recent observations—changes in cultural products post-pandemic.
- **To Deepen:** How does this relate to my own anxious tendencies? Am I observing the era, or am I observing myself?

---

## Part III: The Responsibilities and Dilemmas of the Intellectual

### Chapter 6: "A Parental Legacy—Knowledge and Conscience"
- **Related Lecture:** Never lectured on this, but have been thinking about it constantly.
- **Core Argument:** What does it mean to grow up in an intellectual family? My father’s academic spirit, my mother’s love for culture—they taught me not just knowledge, but a persistence for truth.
- **To Supplement:** Family memories of the Cultural Revolution—how that experience shaped my vigilance toward institutionalization.
- **Emotional Layer:** My parents have passed, but their voices remain in my thoughts. This chapter might be very personal.

### Chapter 7: "The Dance of Freedom and Insecurity"
- **Related Lecture:** Career choices discussed with Professor Chen.
- **Core Argument:** Why did I choose to go freelance? It wasn’t just a desire for freedom, but a fear of the system. Yet, freedom itself brings insecurity.
- **To Supplement:** Financial anxiety over the years, changes in professional identity.
- **To Ponder:** Have I now found a new sense of security through my lectures and publishing plans? One that comes from meaning rather than an institution?

### Chapter 8: "The Value of Solitude"
- **Related Lecture:** "Solitude and Connection" mentioned by someone at the Book Club.
- **Core Argument:** My solitude is not forced, but chosen. But what is the price of this choice? Estrangement from my brother, having no children, living alone...
- **To Supplement:** Recent communication with my brother—we are reconnecting; what has that changed?
- **Reflection:** A new equilibrium between solitude and connection.

---

## Part IV: Present Observations

### Chapter 9: "The Cultural Imagination of Aging"
- **Related Lecture:** Still in the conceptual stage.
- **Core Argument:** Entering my 60s, I’ve begun to notice how impoverished society’s imagination of aging is. We need new narratives.
- **To Supplement:** Personal physical changes, thoughts on mortality (not fear, but contemplation).
- **To Deepen:** How does this relate to my sensitivity and anxious tendencies?

### Chapter 10: "Cultural Anxiety in the Internet Age"
- **Related Lecture:** Recent Salon discussion.
- **Core Argument:** The professionalism of traditional cultural criticism seems to have failed in the face of the internet. But perhaps it’s not a failure, but a transformation.
- **To Supplement:** How do I find my own place in this era?
- **Reflection:** Is there still a market for my skills? Or do I need to redefine what "market" means?

---

## Scattered Ideas to Organize

- [ ] Photography and Seeing—How does this relate to criticism? (Photography notes from 2015 are still in a corner somewhere.)
- [ ] The Philosophy of Antique Collecting—Why do we keep old things?
- [ ] The Democratization Experiment of the Book Club—How has participant empowerment changed the quality of discussion?
- [ ] Conversations with Dr. Li—How has psychotherapy changed my understanding of myself?
- [ ] Uncle Zhang’s Story—Why is the support of a neighbor sometimes more important than that of a friend?

---

## Next Steps

1. **This week:** Re-read lecture notes from 2015–2020 to identify missing themes.
2. **Next week:** Discuss the framework for Chapters 6 and 7 with Professor Chen.
3. **By end of month:** Complete a detailed outline and citation list for each chapter.
4. **With Publisher Editor:** Confirm if this framework is viable.

**Memo:**
Looking at these scattered notes tonight, I suddenly realized this isn't just a draft for a book—it’s a map of my thoughts over the years. Every piece of paper is a moment, a question, something I haven't fully understood yet. Perhaps that is what I have been doing all along—not completing something, but understanding something.

Now, I need to turn that understanding into something others can read.

Professor Chen, something interesting happened at the reading club today that I wanted to share with you.

A college student asked me, "Professor Lin, how do you think we should be preparing for the future right now?" I was taken aback for a moment. Then, I said something that even surprised me: "I no longer view time as preparation for the future. What I am doing now *is* the life I want to live."

The room went silent for a few seconds after I said that. Later, one of the participants told me that it completely changed her perspective on the present.

I’ve been reflecting on that sentence ever since. You know, it really gets to the heart of what we’ve been discussing—about time, about meaning, and about why we need to live in the moment. I used to be so anxious, always feeling like everything I did was just a stepping stone for some "more important" moment later on. But I realize now that this mindset is, in itself, a waste of life.

I’m thinking this idea deserves to be expanded upon. Perhaps it could be a chapter in the book? Or at least a theme for the lecture series. It’s not just a personal realization; judging by everyone's reaction, it seems to have struck a chord with a lot of people.

What do you think? Is this a direction worth pursuing further?

— Lin Xiaoyun

I sat in my room, the phone screen glowing and then fading to black. It was already dark outside, and I hadn't turned on the lights.

After leaving the salon earlier, I bought a bottle of water at a convenience store and sat in the corner for twenty minutes, just staring into space. Now that I’m back home, my finger has been hovering over the screen for a long time.

In the end, I opened WeChat anyway. I found her name—the older woman from the salon. We had a pretty deep conversation last time when we were discussing *Life and Death Are Wearing Me Out*.

I deleted my draft three times.

What I finally sent was:

---

I’m sorry about earlier... I lost control of my emotions a bit.

I haven’t been sleeping well lately, and with work piling up, I’ve been feeling a little overwhelmed. It wasn’t any of you, really. You’re all wonderful, and the discussion was very interesting. I think I just need some time to recalibrate.

Thank you for your concern back there. Truly.

---

I looked at the message and felt a bit awkward. It sounded too much like I was making excuses. But I didn't have the courage to change it.

Resting the phone on my lap, I sank back into silence.

From outside, I could hear the sound of Grandpa Zhang’s TV downstairs—distant and muffled. It reminded me of the last time he asked how my home renovation plans were going. I still haven't answered him.

Maybe tomorrow. Or the day after.

Right now, I just want to sit here and let the night pass slowly by.

Subject: Inquiry regarding the publication of a collection of essays

Dear Professor Chen,

I hope this email finds you well. I am writing to you because the suggestion you made after your lecture some time ago has been on my mind ever since. At the time, when you mentioned that I should consider compiling my articles from over the years into a book, I didn't take it too seriously; I felt they were merely fragmented thoughts scattered across various magazines. However, while sorting through my old files recently, I came across the reviews and essays I’ve written over the years and realized that they might indeed form a coherent thread of thought.

I am a freelance editor and cultural critic. Since 2008, I have written book reviews, film critiques, and observations on social phenomena for various magazines and cultural websites. These pieces cover a wide range of fields, including literature, film, art theory, and contemporary culture. I am now looking to reorganize and revise these articles from the past decade or so into a collection of essays. The theme of this collection is "Reading and the Times"—a record of my reflections through reading and viewing in this rapidly changing era.

I would like to ask if the University Press has any plans to publish this type of cultural criticism collection. If there is a possibility, I would very much like to learn about the specific process and requirements. I currently have about 15 to 20 relatively polished articles on hand, and I am continuing to supplement and revise them.

Thank you again for your previous encouragement, and I look forward to hearing your advice.

Sincerely,

Lin Xiaoyun

# MEMO

**Time: April 24, 2025, 10:30 PM**

---

Connecting isn't as hard as I thought it would be.

That sentence has been playing on a loop in my head all day. After talking with Dr. Li this afternoon, I sat in the cafe for a long time, just thinking about it.

We’re trying. Is that enough? My brother’s email was short, but he said, "I'm glad you're coming." It wasn't some deep, emotional confession, but... why does it feel like enough?

Maybe it’s because I finally understand that a "perfect" connection never existed. We’re just trying. He’s been overseas all these years, and I’ve been here. The time difference, the distance, all the excuses I used to explain why we couldn't be close—they all seem less important now.

About the anxiety. Dr. Li was right; I don’t need to eliminate it. I just need to... walk with it. I was definitely nervous when I bought the plane ticket, but it wasn't that suffocating feeling from before. It was just there. I knew it was there, but I wasn't drowning in it. It’s strange.

Can we really make up for lost time?

I don’t know. But maybe that’s the wrong question to ask. Maybe we aren't making up for anything, but creating something new instead. Who we are now, and what we want to say now—that’s what matters.

Why am I not as afraid anymore?

Because I’ve already made the decision I feared most—spending the money, leaving this city, and going to see someone who is a stranger, yet not a stranger at all. Once the decision was made, I actually felt a sense of relief.

My passport should be ready next week. The flight is booked. I’m still looking at hotels.

Two months to go.

No more procrastinating this time.

---

*p.s. I found an old photography book tonight that I haven't looked at in ages. It reminded me that I’ve always wanted to document things through a lens, but I never actually did it. Maybe this trip can change that. To see my brother, and to see the place where he lives. Through the lens.*

# MEMO

**Date**: April 8, 2024, Afternoon
**Subject**: Methodological Reminders for Reading Groups and Lectures

---

## Core Observation

Professor Chen said something today that I need to write down, or I’ll forget it again.

"You aren't teaching; you are inviting."

When he said this, I was busy defending this month’s book selection for the reading group using a barrage of academic jargon. I could hear my own voice becoming authoritative—a habit honed over 25 years as an editor in publishing. Then I stopped and rephrased the same idea, but this time I said: "I want to invite everyone to think about this question together, rather than provide the answer."

The air in the room seemed to let out a sigh of relief.

## Action List

**In Reading Groups:**
- Use more open-ended questions and fewer declarative statements.
- Say "What do you think?" instead of "I believe..."
- Embrace silence and differing opinions; don't rush to fill the gaps.
- Remember: I am a facilitator, not an authority.

**In Alumni Lectures:**
- Emphasize "Let’s think together" rather than "I’m here to tell you."
- Share more doubts and fewer definitive answers.
- Invite students to question and challenge.
- Be honest and say, "I’m not sure, but let’s explore this."

**In Cultural Columns:**
- Start with "Let us..." instead of "I found..."
- Write as if discussing with a friend, not issuing a manifesto.

---

## Why This Matters

I’ve been terrified of becoming obsolete. But Professor Chen helped me see that perhaps the problem isn't whether my knowledge is "new" enough, but rather how I choose to share it.

A sincere invitation is more powerful than a perfect answer.

The next time I’m lying awake at 3:00 AM replaying a conversation in my head, I will remember this.

**Evening Addendum (21:15):**

Just looked at the conversation again.

I woke up again at 2:00 AM.

My heart was racing; I could feel it thumping against my chest. My hands were shaking as I picked up my phone to check my vitals—heart rate 88, blood pressure 140/92. I know these numbers might not mean much, but I just… I can’t let it go.

I rushed to the bathroom and gulped down half a glass of water. The person in the mirror looked like a stranger.

It’s 2:45 AM now. I have the TV on—some documentary I watched a long time ago. I need the background noise. The darkness is just too heavy.

I keep thinking about how my father was 68 when he passed away. My mother was 71. I’m 62 now. That number just keeps spinning around in my head…

It’s not like I’m thinking about anything in particular. It’s just… lying there in bed, it suddenly hit me. Thinking about how much time I have left if I follow that same pace. What I can still do. All the things I haven’t done yet.

Maybe I’m just exhausted lately. My drafts are overdue again, and the editor has followed up twice. I haven't been sleeping well. That’s just how it is when you get older—your body starts telling you things you don’t really want to hear.

I don’t know why I’m writing this. Maybe I just… need a place to put it all down.

It’s raining outside now. I can hear the sound of the rain mixing with the TV. It’s still better than the silence of the dark.

5:15 AM. Awake again.

My heart is still racing—that feeling of something being wedged in my chest, pushing upward. I counted my breaths, trying to calm myself down, but my fingers are shaking. Even with this thick duvet, I still feel cold.

I have to go today. 2:00 PM. Dr. Li’s clinic is over on Changning Road. I’ve written the address on a sticky note, then copied it again, and again. Afraid of getting it wrong. Afraid of being late. Afraid of… what am I afraid of?

I got up at 3:00 AM to change my clothes. The black sweater felt too gloomy, so I switched to dark blue. Then I felt the blue looked too deliberate, like I was trying to hide something. I changed back to black. I stood in front of the mirror for a long time, looking at my face. Dark circles under my eyes, corners of my mouth drooping. I poked my cheeks with my fingers, trying to make myself look more alive. It didn't work.

Do I really have to go?

Maybe I’m just being too sensitive. Maybe this is just a normal reaction to getting older. Maybe I don’t need to see a therapist at all. I can adjust on my own. I’ve always managed to pull myself through.

But… my sleep has completely collapsed. Lying in bed last night, those endless conversations kept replaying in my head. A single sentence, a look, a tone of voice. At 2:00 AM, it suddenly hit me—maybe what I said at that salon was offensive? Maybe Professor Chen doesn't actually want to stay in touch? Maybe I shouldn't have...

Stop. Stop thinking about it.

But I can’t stop.

Maybe there really is something wrong with me. Maybe I need a diagnosis. Maybe the moment I step into that clinic, the doctor will use a specific word to define me, and then I’ll officially be "broken." Right now, I can still say it’s just stress, just mid-life anxiety. But what if there’s a diagnosis? What if she tells me I have some kind of…

No. Don’t think about that.

I don’t want to trouble anyone. I really don’t. But I’m also… I’m so lonely.

It’s still dark outside. I checked the address one more time. 1247 Changning Road, 5th Floor. I keep walking the route in my mind—leaving the building, turning the corner, entering that office block. Will I see someone I know? Will someone ask me what I’m doing there?

What am I thinking? It’s so early; there’s no one on the streets.

But I’m still worried.

My fingers are still shaking. I press my hands down under the duvet, but I can feel them trembling. It’s like they don’t belong to my body. Like something I can’t control.

Maybe I should wait a bit longer. Maybe in a few days, I’ll feel better. Maybe…

No. I’ve already made the appointment. Dr. Li said she had time, and I said I could make it. I can’t run away anymore. I’ve been running for so long.

But is it really that serious?

5:30 AM. Still dark. I have plenty of time. I could try to sleep a bit more. But I know I won’t.

I’ll just lie here. Lie here and wait for dawn. Wait for 2:00 PM.

Wait for the courage to walk into that clinic.

# Memo

**What Dr. Li said (must remember):**

"Look at what has already changed—you are no longer silent."

---

I was sitting on the sofa in her office, and my eyes suddenly welled up when I heard those words. It wasn't out of sentimentality, but because—I had never thought of it that way.

I had been waiting for the perfect letter, the perfect phrasing, the perfect moment. I thought change had to be some grand outcome—a reply from my brother, a mended relationship, some concrete result. But that’s not what Dr. Li meant.

She meant: **You are already doing it.**

The five versions of the letter I deleted over and over. The crying during therapy. Saying the words "I miss you." Admitting my fear. These *are* the changes. Not the result, but the act itself.

---

And that thing I said myself (remember this too):

"I will still live. I will still do what I want to do."

As I said it, I felt something loosen. It wasn't that the anxiety vanished, but rather—anxiety was no longer an excuse. Death was no longer an excuse. Silence was no longer an excuse.

Time is slipping away, that’s true. But I am still alive, and that is also true.

---

Maybe I should just send the letter now.

Maybe the perfect letter doesn't exist.

Maybe I just need to tell the truth.

February 3, 2022, Afternoon

Xiao Wang sent me a message today. She said there’s a photography field trip this weekend and asked if I wanted to head out to the suburbs with them. The moment I saw the message, a barrage of thoughts flashed through my mind—what if my chest gets tight on the bus? Who could help me if my heart acts up in a strange place? What if I faint out in the wild?

I know it’s ridiculous. I know it perfectly well. But I just can’t stop.

In the end, I messaged her back saying I couldn’t make it because I’m behind on a book review project, maybe another time. She replied instantly with "Okay." Just those two words.

Now I’m staring at those two words over and over. "Okay." Does she really not mind, or is she already disappointed? When she invited me to the book club before, I turned her down then, too. Back then she said, "No worries, see you next time," and her tone felt warm. But this "Okay" feels… different?

I’m wondering if she’s starting to find me annoying. Always refusing, always having an excuse. The truth is, there is no book review project. I’m just… afraid. Afraid of something happening while I’m out. Afraid of my body failing me.

Then I started thinking: if Xiao Wang is losing patience with me, what about the others in the photography circle? What about my friends from the cultural salons? Am I pushing everyone away, one by one?

God, I’m doing it again. One rejection spirals into a future of total isolation.

My father was still lecturing at 68. My mother was still organizing her library at 71. Here I am at 62, hiding at home, making up excuses, overanalyzing a two-word reply from a friend, and imagining the worst possible endings.

How much longer do I have to live?

No, I shouldn’t think like that. It’s too… I don’t know how to put it. It’s too much like I’m cursing myself.

But I really do feel exhausted. Not a physical exhaustion, but… a nameless fear that clings to my skin. It’s been there since this morning, and now it’s growing thicker.

Should I send Xiao Wang another message? Explain things? But explain what? Tell her I’m actually afraid to leave the house? Tell her I’m picturing myself collapsing in the middle of nowhere? What would she think of me?

Forget it. Let it be. I’ll get back to my book review. The deadline is coming up again. I always wait until the last minute to start. It’s become a habit.

Just like how I always wait until the last minute to realize I’ve pushed everyone away.

Yesterday's reading club ran over time again. It was supposed to be two hours, but the discussion kept going until nearly 3:00 PM before everyone finally headed out. A student asked about my interpretation of the ending of *To Live*, which led us from Yu Hua to the meaning of suffering, and then to how each of us finds that sense of "living" in our own lives.

To be honest, I didn't expect so many people to show up. Nor did I expect everyone's thoughts to be so rich. Some people brought up perspectives I’d never even considered; I just sat there listening, sometimes taking notes, sometimes just nodding. Later, I realized that I don't need to provide any "standard answers." We were just thinking together, and that was enough.

It’s a wonderful feeling. It feels more grounded than my years as an editor at the publishing house. Back then, I was always rushing through manuscripts and revisions, constantly racing against some deadline. Now, things have finally slowed down, and I actually have a clearer sense of what I’m doing.

Thank you to all the friends who came. Your questions and ideas showed me that these discussions truly mean something to everyone. Next month, we’ll be reading *One Hundred Years of Solitude*. If you're interested, you're more than welcome to join us.

(Also, Grandpa Zhang said he wanted to come and listen. I told him, "Then just come!" and he laughed. Sometimes the simplest invitations are the warmest.)

Dr. Li, I... I’m sorry to bother you at this hour. I wasn't going to say anything, but I just spent some time talking to my neighbor downstairs. He said some comforting things, but I... I snapped at him. I used a really harsh tone. I’m still thinking about it now.

I told him, "Nobody cares about these stories." After I said it, I just turned toward the window, wanting the conversation to end. He meant well, but I just... I couldn't take it.

Actually, I’ve been looking at everything in my home these past two days. The photography, the unfinished notes, the antiques and old books I’ve collected... Looking at all of it, I’ve started to doubt myself. I can’t change anything. Not just the room—I mean... me. It feels like I’ve never truly finished anything in my life.

I’m wondering if I need... I don’t know how to put it. Is this feeling normal? Or is there something genuinely wrong with me?

I’ve been like this lately—just sitting here among all these things, asking myself the same questions over and over. If one day I’m gone, what happens to all this? Who will remember? Who will understand the meaning behind these stories?

I’m sorry, I know this sounds heavy. I just... I wanted to ask, should I come in and talk to you?

October 12, 2023, 7:00 PM

Everyone has left. The room is still filled with the scent of tea and the lingering warmth of their laughter.

I’m sitting on the sofa, looking at the objects that have been rearranged—the photographs, the old books, the mementos. They are no longer just my private secrets.

To be honest, I felt a pang of regret the moment I decided to invite them over to see these things. What was I thinking? Exposing my own mess to others? Opening up this room—this place filled with my failures and unfinished dreams?

But they came. Professor Chen picked up that yellowed photo album and flipped through it intently. I was about to explain that these were taken many years ago and weren't anything special by today's standards. But he didn't ask about that. He simply said, "Every one of these has a story."

I suddenly realized that I’ve been judging these things through the lens of "success" and "failure," as if only perfect work deserves to be seen. But as they looked, I heard a different voice—they were asking what I was thinking in those moments, why I composed a shot that way, what was written in that notebook.

They were listening to my story.

Sharing these things made me feel less lonely. It’s strange. I always thought loneliness was a choice I made, the price of freedom. But tonight I understood that perhaps loneliness and freedom are not the same thing. Maybe I’ve been confusing them all along.

Before leaving, Professor Chen said, "You should write these stories down. Truly."

I didn't give him a direct answer. But sitting here now, I’m thinking—maybe he’s right. Not for publication, not for some goal. Just... to record them. These moments, these thoughts, these things I was once so passionate about.

I look at that photo of the city nightscape on the wall. I used to think it was imperfect—the composition was off, the lighting wasn't ideal. But now I remember: it was the winter of 2016, and I was contemplating the meaning of true beauty. When I pressed the shutter, I was there. I was alive.

Maybe the mess and the imperfection are what’s real.

The room is still a mess. The renovation plan hasn't truly begun. But tonight, I no longer see this as a failure.

Tomorrow, I’m going to write down how I feel tonight. Not for anyone else. Just to remember this evening, to remember this feeling—the first time someone entered my world and listened to my stories, and I was no longer so afraid of being seen.

Professor Chen, I have a question for you. It might come across as a bit abrupt.

I re-read the 18 selected articles yesterday. To be honest, I’m feeling something strange right now—it’s not relief, but rather a deeper sense of exhaustion. I cut 12 pieces, thinking it would make things easier, but instead, it feels like I’ve discarded a part of myself.

You know, looking at the rejected drafts, I’ve started to doubt whether the remaining 18 are actually worth it. Re-reading them, I realized some of the perspectives seem so superficial now, and some of the arguments feel a bit weak. I can't help but wonder: if I’m this dissatisfied with what I thought five years ago, will the same happen to the pieces I’ve chosen now? Will I end up disavowing my current self again in ten years?

This cycle scares me.

And frankly, I’ve started asking myself—does this project really have any meaning? I don't mean the act of publishing itself, but rather, do these articles, these ideas, actually leave anything behind? Or am I just trying to find a justification for my own existence?

I’m not looking for self-pity. I just want to hear your thoughts. As a scholar, how do you see these 18 articles? Is there truly something there worth preserving? Or am I just deluding myself?

By the way, my energy levels aren't great right now, so this might sound a bit heavy. But I really just need a voice of reason at the moment.

September 8, 2024, Evening

I had a long phone call with my brother.

He brought up the publishing project himself and asked how it was going. Usually, I would have brushed it off with a casual "I'm still organizing things," but for some reason, I just couldn't bring myself to gloss over it today. I told him that the biggest hurdle is actually financial. Publishing a collection of papers like this requires a significant upfront investment, and my savings... my voice trailed off and cracked a little as I said it.

It’s been many years since I’ve been this honest with him.

My brother didn't offer the usual comforts I expected, like "It's okay, take your time." Instead, he said, "Xiaoyun, you’ve always thought you needed to be completely independent, but independence isn't the same as loneliness."

I didn't respond right away. On the other end of the line, I could hear his breathing, mixed with the sound of old Mr. Zhang downstairs fixing something.

"Independence isn't loneliness"—I’ve been turning that sentence over in my mind ever since.

For so many years, I’ve understood independence as a form of absolute self-sufficiency. To be independent meant I couldn't accept help, couldn't show weakness, and couldn't rely on anyone. But my brother said he could help me—not because I’ve failed, but because it’s something he wants to do as my brother. He said he’s been reading my articles and thinks they deserve to be seen by more people.

I was unexpectedly moved.

Before hanging up, he told me to send over the plan and budget if I needed to. I didn't refuse. That in itself is very unusual for me.

I’m sitting at my desk now. It’s completely dark outside. The room is as messy as ever, with books and notes piled everywhere, but for once, I don't feel that familiar sense of anxiety. Instead, there’s a strange, unfamiliar sense of relaxation.

Maybe I’ve been misunderstanding something all along. Maybe accepting help isn't a sign of weakness, but another form of courage.

Or rather, a form of growth.

November 25, 2023, Night

Sitting at my desk, still holding the notebook I brought back from the book club today. It’s pitch black outside. The lights are on at Old Man Zhang’s place downstairs; I can hear the sound of his TV. It’s a very quiet night.

But my mind won’t stop racing.

I keep thinking about that moment today. When I said, "What’s truly worth fearing isn't death itself, but never having truly lived," I startled even myself. Those words didn't come from my head—they came from... somewhere deeper. Hearing myself say them was like listening to someone else speak.

And then, seeing that retired woman’s eyes well up. I didn't know how to react. What did I think I was doing? Sharing an academic viewpoint? In that moment, it wasn't that at all. I was just voicing the raw, honest fear of a 62-year-old woman.

It’s strange. I’ve always thought my value lay in saying something insightful, in writing some profound critique. But today wasn't like that. I wasn't critiquing anything; I was just... living. Speaking my real fears. And someone heard me, and was moved.

Does this change anything? I don’t know.

But I’m starting to wonder if I’ve actually been living well all these years. I swing between busyness and idleness, tangle with freedom and fear, rush to meet deadlines the night before, and lose sleep over a single sentence in the middle of the night. I collect books and antiques, attend salons, and discuss philosophy with friends. All of this is real, but... am I living, or am I escaping?

Dr. Li asked me last time, "What do you think you’re running from?" I didn't give a real answer. Now I think, maybe what I’m escaping is the question itself—whether I have truly lived.

62 years old. It’s not an abstract number. My parents are gone. My marriage is over. My brother is overseas, and we barely have real conversations. I have so many unfinished projects, so many half-read books. Sometimes I wonder, if it all ended right now... what would I leave behind? A pile of notes? A few critiques? Or the fact that a person once lived, and once made a stranger cry at a book club?

I don’t know which is more meaningful.

I have a draft due tomorrow. The editor has already nudged me twice. I should go to sleep, but I know I’ll just keep mulling over these questions in bed. Maybe this is just my way—thinking through anxiety, living through insomnia.

Maybe that’s not so bad after all.

Hey, I’m still on the subway, just heading back from the salon.

I shared our story there tonight. You know, about all those years apart, about that phone call. I didn't expect to be so open, but once I started talking, the words just found their way. I said that loneliness can be a choice—a choice to connect, or a choice to find meaning within the solitude.

After I finished, a friend asked me, "So, what happened next?" And I realized that I’ve been talking about the past, but we are changing the story right now.

Sitting here on the train, I’m wondering—is this actually possible? I mean, is it really possible for me to come see you guys? Not as some distant dream, but as something we can actually plan. To see your life as it is now, to meet your family.

I didn't realize I’d miss you this much. For all these years, I thought I’d accepted the distance, but after that call, everything changed. You still remember me; you remember us growing up together. That means so much to me.

When are you free? I think we should talk regularly—not just once in a while, but really regularly. Maybe every month? Or what do you think would work best?

Also, do you think I could come see you this year? I know it takes planning and money, but I want to try. I want to see you in person, not just on a screen.

I’ll think about it more when I get home. But right now, I just feel like time is limited, and connection is possible. I don’t want to wait anymore.

Thank you all so much for tonight.

Sitting here and listening to your shares, I found myself deeply moved. Sometimes I think I’m just "telling" a story, but in reality, every conversation changes my own understanding of those stories. Your questions and insights helped me see things I hadn't noticed before.

A special thanks to those who took the initiative to share, as well as those who listened quietly and asked such profound questions at the end. This is exactly why we gather—not to find definitive answers, but to discover new possibilities through each other's reflections.

See you at the next book club! 😊

It's raining outside again. The streetlight downstairs catches the raindrops perfectly—I can see them falling, one by one.

A friend asked me how I’ve been lately. I didn’t know how to answer. "I'm okay" feels like a lie these days, but the truth is just too heavy. So I didn't reply.

Maybe it’ll clear up tomorrow.

It’s not like I can go out anyway.

Thank you for your persuasion last time. I know you’ve said it many times and I kept putting it off, but... I went today.

But she didn't make me feel embarrassed. She just handed me some tissues and said, "It’s okay. You don’t need to rush." Just that one sentence actually made me relax.

We talked a lot. About the years since the divorce, about my parents passing away, and about why I always find myself overanalyzing some trivial comment at three in the morning. She said there are reasons for all of this, that it’s not because I’m "too sensitive" or "too fragile." It might not sound like much, but to me... it felt like being understood, a feeling I haven't had in a very long time.

We’re meeting again at the same time next week. I didn't hesitate when I nodded in agreement, which is a miracle in itself for me.

I don't know where this is going yet, but at least for now, I no longer feel like admitting I need help is a failure. Maybe that in itself is a start.

Thank you again. Truly.

June 24, 2025, 10:15 PM

The guest room is quiet. From downstairs, I can hear my brother and sister-in-law laughing at some variety show. I’m sitting on the edge of the bed with my notebook on my lap, looking out at the night view of an unfamiliar city.

Today, I had a sudden realization.

I used to think that freedom meant escaping—escaping marriage, the office, and everything that could tie me down. I was proud of that freedom, but it also exhausted me. It was an empty kind of freedom; it left me floating in mid-air, with nowhere to land.

But after spending these few days with my brother, I’ve realized that true freedom is having the courage to choose what is worth committing to.

I chose this trip. I chose to spend the money I had originally set aside for my retirement. I chose to come here at the risk of losing my job. I chose to reconnect with my family at sixty-two. These choices come with costs and risks, but that is exactly why they are meaningful.

I’m no longer tangled in anxiety. It’s strange. The thoughts that used to keep me tossing and turning at 3:00 AM—about aging, money, whether anyone cares about me—don't feel as sharp here. It’s not that they’ve disappeared; it’s just that I’m now filled with something more real.

Yesterday, my brother asked me why I only came now. I didn’t give him a direct answer. But I think maybe the old me wasn't ready yet. I was still running away from something, rather than moving toward something. It’s different now.

I’ve found the peace of having a direction. It’s an unfamiliar feeling, but I like it.

Tomorrow, I’m going to the art museum with my sister-in-law. She said there’s a photography exhibition I’d be interested in. I started learning photography back in 2015, but it’s been off and on ever since. Maybe here, while I’m with family, I’ll remember why I started in the first place.

If Mom were still alive, she would love to see me like this. Dad would, too. They were the kind of people who believed life should be fully experienced. It took me so long to realize that I’ve been inheriting that from them all along.

Anyway, I hear my brother calling me from downstairs. They want me to come down for tea.

I find that I don't want to hide in my room anymore.

Hao,

I don’t know how to start this letter. Maybe I’ll start with what happened a few hours ago—I was tidying up the room and opened a dusty cardboard box.

It was full of old things. Photos of Mom, my diaries from when I was younger, some pictures I took, and a lot of books. So many unfinished books. I sat there on the floor, looking at these things, and suddenly felt a wave of sadness. Not because of the objects themselves, but because… I realized that all these years, I’ve been here alone collecting, preserving, and organizing these things, but there has never been anyone to truly share their stories with.

Did you know? I have a photo of Mom when she was young, working at the library. She’s wearing a very elegant outfit, standing in front of the bookshelves, smiling so happily. Looking at that photo, I remembered how she taught me to read and told me stories from those books. But these memories only exist in my head now. What about you? Do you still remember?

How long has it been since we’ve had a real conversation? Not the "how are you" or "how have you been" kind of small talk, but a real exchange. I’m not even sure what you’re doing now or what your life is like. And here I am, living alone in this old house, a house filled with stories but with no one to listen.

Maybe this sounds self-pitying. I know you’re busy and far away overseas. But I wanted to tell you—the true value of these objects doesn't lie in the things themselves, but in their power to help us understand each other. Mom’s photo is a memory for me, but if you saw it too, maybe we could remember her together. Maybe we could understand together where we came from and who we are.

I don’t know how you’ll respond to this. Maybe it feels abrupt. But I want to take that risk. I keep wondering, if one day I’m no longer here, what will happen to these stories? Who will remember? That’s why I want to share these things with you while there’s still time. Not just the objects, but my thoughts and my feelings.

Could you write back? Even if it’s just to tell me what’s on your mind right now.

Xiaoyun

---

Notes on Generalized Anxiety Disorder (Summary of Search History)

**Core Concepts:**
- Neurotransmitter imbalance (Serotonin, Norepinephrine, GABA)
- Neurobiological changes caused by chronic stress
- Amygdala overactivation → Dysregulation of the threat detection system
- Risk factors for poor prognosis: Age of onset, duration, comorbid depression

**Impact on Career:**
- Distractibility, impaired decision-making
- Freelancers are more prone to the "work-anxiety-avoidance" cycle
- Deadline procrastination may be linked to anxiety symptoms (not just "laziness")
- Unstable income → Financial anxiety → Worsening symptoms → Further decline in productivity

**Social Issues:**
- Stigmatization: A tendency to view those with anxiety as "fragile" or "not strong enough"
- Concealment among intellectuals (my father’s generation would never admit to having "psychological problems")
- Age factor: Anxiety in women over 60 is often misdiagnosed as menopause or physical illness

**What Dr. Li said is still echoing in my head:**
"You are using understanding as a substitute for feeling."

# June 16, 2022, 6:30 AM

Awake again.

No alarm. Just my eyes snapping open, heart racing, that feeling that something is wrong. My fingers were trembling when I reached for my phone—I noticed it, but I couldn't stop it. 6:32 AM.

The first thing I did was open my email.

No reply. Of course not. I know it’s irrational, but I opened it anyway. Then I opened it again. Then I re-read the email I sent.

"Dear Brother..."

My finger hovered over the screen for a long time. Was the tone right? Was it too formal? Or too casual? I read it again, and then again. Every word made me second-guess myself. "We haven't been in touch much over the years"—does that sound like I’m accusing him? Will he think I’m bringing up old grudges?

Dr. Li said perfection isn't the goal. But right now, my brain is searching for nothing but perfection.

He’s over there. I tried to calculate the time. He’s in Europe, so it should be... afternoon? Or evening? I pulled up the clock app on my phone, then opened a world time website. It’s just past 2 PM in London. No, wait, he’s not in London. He’s in... I suddenly realized I’m not even sure which city he’s in exactly. That thought made me even more anxious.

I refresh my inbox over and over. I know it’s useless, but I keep doing it.

My fingers tap against the quilt. Over and over. Fast. I can feel my own heartbeat. The room is quiet, save for the hum of the air conditioner and my breathing.

But... I did it. I actually did it.

The thought broke through suddenly, like a beam of light. I sent it. That email I’ve been rewriting for three weeks—I finally hit send. My fingers were shaking, but I pressed it. Not the perfect version, not the fiftieth draft I kept obsessing over, but that version. The real one.

I don’t know how he’ll respond. Maybe he’ll be happy. Maybe he’ll feel awkward. Maybe he won’t know what to say at all. Maybe he’s busy right now and hasn’t even seen it. Maybe...

But none of that matters anymore, does it?

I’ve broken the silence.

Now all I can do is wait. It’s hard. My body is telling me to do something, to check, to confirm, to control. But I can’t. All I can do is sit here, let the anxiety exist, let it flow through my body, and then... keep living.

Maybe that’s enough.

Maybe that’s everything.

I looked at the email one last time. Same words. Same me. Then I put my phone down and closed my eyes.

Outside the window, the sky is getting brighter.

Another new day.

# October 12, 2023, around 7:00 PM

I just got off the phone with Xiao Zhou, and I still feel a bit dazed.

To be honest, it took a lot of courage for me to say it—"I’ve been seeing a therapist lately." My palms were sweating as the words came out, as if I were admitting to something shameful. I was braced for sympathy, pity, or the inevitable "What’s wrong?" But Xiao Zhou just said, quite casually, "That’s great, I’ve been thinking about it too."

That was it. No shock, no interrogation. It was as if I’d just said, "I’ve been watching movies lately."

I don't know why that hit me so hard.

We talked for a while longer. She told me she struggles with insomnia too, that she lies awake at night thinking about the most random things, wondering if there’s something wrong with her. Listening to her, it suddenly hit me—I’ve always viewed my anxiety, my restlessness, and my fear of death as personal failures. I saw them as proof that I was uniquely fragile or broken. But everything Xiao Zhou described was almost identical to what I’ve been going through.

She isn't a particularly "fragile" person. She has a stable job; her life seems perfectly organized. Yet, she also finds herself wide awake at 3:00 AM.

It feels strange. It’s as if my anxiety isn't a private brand of shame anymore.

I keep wondering why I felt that seeing a therapist was so disgraceful—like it was proof that I couldn't handle my own life, that I was a loser. But thinking about it now, maybe it’s just that I need help. Just like you’d see a dentist or an eye doctor. Why is my attitude toward mental health so different?

Sitting here, looking at the night sky outside the window, I suddenly feel less lonely. It’s not because any of my problems have been solved, but because I realized I’m not the only one who gets scared in the middle of the night.

Xiao Zhou asked me how Dr. Li is. I told her she’s pretty good—gentle, and she doesn't make me feel like there's something "wrong" with me. We’ve only met twice, but that feeling of being heard... I’d forgotten what that felt like.

Maybe I should keep going.

Maybe I don’t need to be ashamed of this.

# December 3, 2023  Sunny

Just got back from Dr. Li’s clinic. My hands are still shaking a little.

Sitting on the sofa, she asked me what I’ve been up to lately. I told her about the room renovation—the piles of books, the objects, the unfinished projects. I told her I wanted to clear them all out, that I wanted a "clean space," a fresh start. She listened for a long time, then asked, "Where do you think the change actually happens?"

I didn't catch her meaning at first. Then she said, "Real change happens in how you understand these objects and the stories behind them."

I sat there for a long time.

That sentence felt like a key, suddenly unlocking something. I had been thinking so much about how to escape this room—to escape the clutter, the chaos, the unfinished business. It was as if by clearing it all away, I could become someone else. But in reality... in reality, I was trying to escape myself.

I said to her, "Maybe I don't need to escape my life; maybe I need to live more deeply within it."

As I said it, my eyes welled up. It’s strange—I’ve thought that sentence a thousand times in my head, but saying it out loud felt completely different. It was like my logic and something much deeper finally aligned.

Walking home, I thought about those books, the antiques, the half-finished photography projects. They aren't failures. They were my thoughts in those moments. They represent my perspective, my curiosity, a certain stage of my life. Why have I been so ashamed of them?

Maybe true freedom isn't about escaping commitment or order. Maybe freedom is knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the price for it—including giving up certain forms of security, or accepting certain limitations.

The room is still the same. The books are still there. The objects are still there. But the way I look at them has changed.

I don’t know how long this realization will last. But at least for today, I feel a sense of peace.

Tonight’s book club has come to an end. Eight of us sat in a corner of the bookstore, spending two hours discussing *The Meaning of Life*. We had students, office workers, and even some retired friends.

What surprised me most was a girl who joined us for the first time. As she was leaving, she said, "Thank you for creating a space like this."

I didn't actually do much—I just posed a few questions for us to think about together. But in that moment, I realized that perhaps what truly matters has never been what I say, but what we are all contemplating together.

It’s a feeling that’s hard to describe. It doesn't come from being in control of the room, but from that authentic, undisturbed connection.

We’ll be doing this again next month. If you’re interested in conversations like these, you’re more than welcome to join us. Let’s explore together.
