Saturday, April 2, 2022. Sunny.

Insomnia again last night. At 3:00 AM, I gave up, got up, and pulled out that meditation app to try for the twenty-third time. Still no use. My head is full of static.

Last Thursday, I got an email from my editor saying the manuscript needs more revisions. The moment I saw those words, my heart skipped a beat and then went haywire. My hands started shaking. I sat at the computer staring at that email for twenty minutes, doing absolutely nothing. It’s a familiar feeling—like something is pressing down on my chest, making my breathing shallow and fast. Then the thoughts started: Is my writing really that bad? Is the editor actually disappointed in me? Am I just getting old and falling behind the times?

Dr. Li calls this an "anxiety spiral." I’ve started recording these moments now, just as she suggested. I bought a little notebook and used different symbols to mark when it starts, when it’s at its worst, and when it eases up. It feels a bit silly, but… maybe it helps?

What scares me most is when she asked me the other day, "What do you want?" and I realized I couldn't answer. It wasn't that I couldn't think of something specific; it was that I didn't know if I was still worthy of wanting anything. I spent nearly forty years chasing freedom, and now that freedom has me trapped. No one manages me, and no one needs me. I can do anything I want, yet I can do nothing at all.

She said something that I’ve circled in red in my diary: "Maybe you don't need to overcome your anxiety. You just need to learn to coexist with it and understand what it’s trying to tell you."

I hated that sentence at first. I want it to disappear, not live with it. But I’ve been thinking about it for the past two days… maybe the anxiety is telling me something? Maybe it’s saying I need to be seen? That I need someone to care?

I’ve stuck with the meditation for three weeks now. Even though I usually give up halfway through with my mind in a mess, at least… at least I’m trying. It’s nothing grand, but to me, it means I haven’t completely given up.

I replied to Professor Chen’s email this morning. I hadn't seriously considered that book project he mentioned before. But now I think, maybe I could give it a try? Not to prove anything, but just… maybe I still have something to contribute.

It’s bright outside. I should go for a walk, maybe to the second-hand bookstore. The owner there will ask how I’ve been, and I can pretend everything is fine. Or, for once, I could try telling the truth?

I don’t know. I don’t know yet.

Lin Hao, I just left Dr. Li’s office.

I’m sitting in a cafe right now, and I feel like there are some things I just have to tell you directly. There’s been so much silence between us all these years, and I don’t want to wait anymore.

I’ve made up my mind—I’m going abroad to see you. We can discuss the specific timing and budget later, but this isn’t a "maybe" anymore; it’s actually happening. I know this sounds sudden, but I need you to know what this means to me.

Dr. Li asked me why I wanted to go so badly, and I couldn’t give him a simple reason. Then it hit me—this might be my last chance to truly connect with you. Not through a screen, not through those stiff greetings, but actually sitting together, just like when we were kids.

I don’t want to use "fear" as an excuse anymore. Fear of bothering you, fear of the awkwardness, fear of breaking this fragile balance of estrangement. I’ve spent all these years trying to protect my own freedom, only to end up losing the thing that matters most.

I’ll figure out the money. I don’t have much saved up, but this is worth it. Compared to staying thousands of miles apart like this, anything is worth it.

When are you free? Let me know how things are on your end. Let’s plan this together.

I’m tired, Lin Hao. I’m tired of being alone.

November 27, 2024 – Past 11:00 PM

I just stepped out of the study; it’s pitch black outside now. I’ve spent the entire afternoon sorting through my conversation logs with Editor Zhou, trying to make sense of what exactly happened to me.

To be honest, it still feels a bit surreal.

When I went to see her this afternoon, I only intended to ask if there were any new commissions. But the moment I sat down, I heard myself saying, "I want to make a change." I hadn't even planned on saying that. The words just spilled out, as if another version of me were speaking.

"I admit I’ve wasted time." When I said that, I saw the look on her face—she was surprised, but also seemed somewhat relieved. Maybe she saw it coming all along. Maybe everyone saw it, and they were just too polite to say anything.

The strangest part was when she offered me the new project. I didn't just agree immediately like I usually do. I asked for details. I said, "I want a firm deadline." I even said, "I want to change my work habits."

Sounds normal enough, right? But to me, it felt like I was watching a stranger. Who is this woman demanding clear deadlines? That’s not me. I’ve spent my whole life running away from that kind of thing—I hate being tied down; I hate the pressure of a deadline.

But... maybe I’ve been misunderstanding something.

Maybe freedom doesn't mean having no commitments. Maybe freedom is knowing what you want and having the courage to choose it—even if that choice involves some form of discipline. I want to go see my brother. I want to see his life, his family. The thought makes me feel... fulfilled? No, it’s something else, something I haven't felt in a long time.

For this, I’m willing to accept some constraints. I’m willing to submit my work on time. I’m willing to have a plan. This isn't a compromise; it’s a choice.

As I left the editorial office, I felt something new and unfamiliar. A sense of fulfillment? It was more than that. It was more like... finally understanding something.

I wasn't running away from commitment. I just hadn't found anything worth committing to yet.

Now, I have.

I don’t know if this thought will still be this clear tomorrow. But at least for tonight, I believe it’s real.

5:30 AM. Can't sleep again.

I stumbled upon an article about a photographer in her 60s starting over on Instagram. I closed it the moment I saw a comment from a young person saying, "Auntie, you're so brave."

Sometimes I wonder what the real difference is between adapting to change and admitting defeat. Maybe there isn't one. Maybe it's just a nicer way of putting it.

It’s still dark outside. Old Man Zhang’s light downstairs just flicked on—I guess he gets up at this hour too. People like us, we all seem to live outside everyone else’s schedule.

Whatever, I’m overthinking. Time to sleep.

I’ve been wondering whether I should send this news to Professor Chen.

An editor from the publishing house just called to say they’ve made a decision—they want to publish a collection of my essays as a book. To be honest, it felt a bit surreal while I was listening, as if I were hearing someone else’s story. But after I hung up, sitting alone in my study surrounded by walls of books and notes, my eyes suddenly welled up.

What does this mean? I thought about it for a while. It means that all these years of reflection finally have a complete, shareable form. Not scattered critiques, not just an article in some magazine, but a cohesive whole.

But more importantly, I suddenly realized something—I haven’t just been writing for myself. All these years, every time I discussed ideas with friends at cultural salons, every time I organized a reading group, every time I lay awake at 3:00 AM because of a thought, I was actually in a dialogue with people who think like me. They are asking the same questions, trying to understand this era, and using culture and thought to search for some kind of meaning.

I’m a little scared now, you know? Scared that I won’t write it well enough. Scared that once I fix these ideas onto paper, they’ll become some kind of "final conclusion" rather than living, breathing thoughts. Scared that I’ll let down the expectations of those readers—the ones I haven’t met yet, but whose existence I feel.

At the same time, I feel like this might be the last chance at my age to do something truly meaningful. Not for fame or fortune, and not to prove anything. I just want to leave behind what I’ve seen, thought, and felt.

Do you think this sounds too grand? Or is it actually what I’ve been doing all along—only now it has a clearer form?

I really want to talk to you about this. Truly.

March 2, 2023  7:15 AM

I didn’t sleep well at all last night after sending that email. It wasn’t because of regret—or at least, not entirely—but because I lay in bed for three hours, repeatedly asking myself, "Who am I now?"

It’s a stupid question. I know. But I really couldn't come up with an answer.

Yesterday morning, I spent forty minutes revising that retirement email. I changed "thank you for the opportunities over the years" to "it’s a shame this chapter is ending," and then finally changed it back to the original version. Why? What was I afraid of? Appearing too sentimental? Appearing too indifferent? Afraid the editors would think I’ve given up? But they haven’t needed me for a long time. That’s the truth.

Then I did something very strange. After sending the email, I started organizing all the cultural reviews I’ve written over the past 15 years on my computer. I went through them one by one. Some I didn't even remember writing. Others made me wonder as I read—did I really write this?

I named the folder "2007-2023: My Voice."

But now, sitting here and looking at that name, I want to ask—is that really my voice?

Or have I just been imitating something for the past 15 years? Imitating the voice of a "tasteful cultural critic"? Imitating the voice my colleagues at the publishing house expected? Imitating the voice those magazine editors wanted? I’m not even sure.

No one needs my reviews anymore. That’s a fact. The internet, self-media, those young writers—they’re all faster and cheaper than me. Is my knowledge still valuable? If no one is willing to pay for it, if no one needs my voice, what does it even mean?

When I was lying in bed thinking about this, it wasn't even light out yet. It was so quiet outside the window. I suddenly realized that I have no idea how to live the days ahead. No deadlines. No editors chasing me for copy. No reason to wake up early.

This is supposed to be the freedom I wanted. So why does it feel like being abandoned?

I need to get up. Have some water. Maybe look through that folder again. Maybe I should delete it. Or maybe I should keep organizing it, to see if I can actually find something—my voice, my thoughts, something of mine.

But right now, I’m still so tired.

**March 22, 2025 | Essay Collection Launch**

7:00 PM. The applause in the conference room is still ringing in my ears.

I marked this date on my calendar. Not as a reminder of something to do, but as a way to remember—this was the moment I finally understood.

This book wasn't about proving I still have value. It wasn't a response to those questioning, "Why are you still writing?" It is simply a record. A record of how I’ve thought, how I’ve changed, and how, through wave after wave of disappointment, I still persisted in trying to understand this world.

Standing on stage reading the introduction, I saw Professor Chen smiling; I saw my friends from the cultural salon listening intently; I even saw something in my brother’s eyes over the video call. In that moment, I truly felt it—these essays, these ideas, they were never meant to be perfect. They were meant to connect. They exist so that someone, opening this book on some random night, can feel the honest thoughts of another human being.

I’ve always been afraid of being tied down. But this project made me realize that some commitments aren't shackles—they are a release.

It’s 9:00 PM now. I’m sitting at home, surrounded by the same books, the same unfinished notes, the same old trinkets from flea markets. Everything is the same. But I have changed.

Facing death has made me cherish life more. This isn't despair; on the contrary—I finally know what I want.

I’m sitting in my study, with the cold afternoon light streaming in through the window. When my phone screen lit up, my heart skipped a beat for some reason.

I’ve read your reply so many times.

To be honest, I didn't expect you to get back to me so quickly. Even more so, I didn't expect you to remember those details—the sea of clouds at Huangshan, and what Dad said while pointing at that peak. I had almost forgotten his exact words, but you remembered.

I’ve been going through some old things lately and found a lot of photos. There’s one of us at the foot of the mountain; you’re wearing that blue jacket, and I’m standing right next to you. Mom took it. Do you remember? We used to go out together all the time back then. Looking back now, those days really aren't coming back.

I don’t know how to put this. So much time has passed between us over the years. Sometimes I wonder if we wouldn't feel like such strangers if we had stayed in touch more. But then I feel like it’s a bit late to be saying all this.

How are you doing lately? How’s work? I know you’re busy, so I don’t want to bother you too much. It’s just… seeing your reply, I suddenly felt like the distance between us isn't as thick as I imagined.

Maybe we could talk more. It doesn't have to be about anything important—just like this.

Professor Chen, something interesting happened today that I wanted to share with you.

At last week’s reading group, a young woman—probably in her early twenties—asked a classic question: "What is the meaning of life, really?" You know, I’ve heard this so many times before. Usually, I’d quote some philosopher or launch into a theory about self-actualization. But this time was different.

I suddenly found myself saying, "Maybe meaning isn't something waiting for us to discover, but something we create in every single moment."

After I said it, even I was a bit taken aback. It wasn't the kind of "profound" answer I usually give. But the girl’s eyes lit up. Then, a middle-aged man picked up on what I said and started talking about how he found a new sense of value in his daily work. The whole discussion just came alive.

I’ve been thinking about this ever since. Our generation of intellectuals—myself included—has this habit of searching for some grand, ultimate answer. But maybe that’s a trap in itself. I spent twenty-five years at the publishing house, always feeling like I was doing "meaningful work"—spreading knowledge, discovering great writing. Later, when I went freelance, I fell into a pit of anxiety: Am I still doing something meaningful? Does my work still have value?

But looking at it now, maybe value isn't defined that way at all.

This month’s reading group was about a book on everyday aesthetics. I realized that those moments of genuine conversation with different people—rather than how many articles I’ve published or how much recognition I’ve received—are what make me feel like my knowledge is alive and useful. When a student told me that one of my points changed her understanding of a piece of literature, the satisfaction I felt in that moment far outweighed seeing my byline in a magazine.

I think this might relate to your previous suggestion about me publishing a book. For a while, I saw publishing as a form of "validation," a proof that my thoughts were important enough. But now I’m wondering if I should publish not to prove anything, but simply to open up this kind of dialogue with more people.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this?

I was tidying up my bookshelf recently and came across a dusty old book—*How We Define Ourselves*. The cover has yellowed a bit, and a bookmark was still tucked away on one of the pages.

I remember buying it five or six years ago. Back then, I was just drawn to the title, but it ended up sitting in a corner ever since. Re-reading a few pages today, I realized that some of the words only truly make sense to me now.

"A single label can never fully describe a person. We are always more complex than we think."

Sometimes it feels like we are too easily defined by a single comment, a diagnosis, or even just a look. But in reality, every one of us is constantly getting to know ourselves all over again.

Maybe that’s the point of living—to refuse to be fixed or simplified, and to always maintain that sense of curiosity about who we are.

📷 [A close-up of the book, with sunlight streaming through the window onto the pages]

2:47 AM

I can’t sleep. It’s not the tossing-and-turning kind of restlessness; it’s an exhilaration so intense my body simply refuses to rest.

I just shut down my computer. The article has been sent to the editor. A first draft. That one sentence is still looping in my head: *Distance doesn’t necessarily mean estrangement; sometimes, distance is just an invitation to understand one another anew.*

I don’t know where that line came from. When I reached that point in the writing, my fingers just stopped. My eyes were stinging a bit as I stared at the screen, and then the words appeared. It felt as if they had been there all along, and I was merely the one to write them down.

I still can’t quite believe what I’ve done.

At the salon this afternoon, I heard myself telling the story of my brother. I went into detail—about the years without any real communication, about his life far away overseas, about that indefinable wall between us. I wasn’t even thinking much as I spoke; it was like opening a room that had been locked for a long time. After Professor Chen heard it, she said this story should be turned into an article. My immediate reaction was to refuse. I told her I’m a book critic, not a memoirist. This isn't my style.

But she insisted. She said that perhaps because of that, the story was even more worth writing. A cultural critic suddenly becoming vulnerable—that, in itself, is a cultural phenomenon.

I kept thinking about those words after I got home. Around 11:00 PM, I opened my laptop.

The writing process was strange. I wasn’t critiquing anything, nor was I analyzing. I was just speaking—speaking about the years of silence between my brother and me, and about how my understanding of that silence has shifted. When I got to the part about distance, I suddenly realized that maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. Distance isn't abandonment, nor is it indifference. Distance might just be... an invitation? An invitation to see each other clearly, rather than maintaining a facade of intimacy.

It sounds poetic, I know. But it didn't feel that way while I was writing. I was simply recording the birth of an idea.

Sitting here now, sipping tea that has gone cold, I’m a little scared. Scared that the piece is too personal. Scared the editor will think it’s "off-brand." Scared that I’ve exposed too much. But at the same time... am I proud of myself? It’s strange. I’m 62 years old, and I can still feel proud of something I’ve done.

Maybe I’ll regret this tomorrow. Maybe I’ll think it was just an impulse. But right now, in this moment, I want to record this: I did something I thought I was incapable of doing.

I crossed a boundary I always assumed was there.

It’s still dark outside. But I can feel the dawn coming.

---
May 8, 2025, Early Morning

3:45 AM. I can’t sleep again.

The glow of my phone screen is blinding in the darkness. I tell myself to stop checking my email, but my fingers slide it open anyway, almost of their own accord. No new messages. Of course not. He wouldn’t reply this quickly. Maybe he won’t reply at all.

I typed "family estrangement" into the search bar, then "no contact for years," then "repairing relationships." These articles are like traps; I know they’ll hurt me, yet I jump in anyway. One of them said: "If someone hasn't reached out to you in years, they may have moved on from the relationship."

I tossed my phone aside.

But that phrase has already burrowed into my brain like a knife. *Moved on.* What a cold term. We have the same mother, the same father, the same childhood. We lived through those years together. And now he’s just moved on? Or was I never worth remembering in the first place?

I texted Dr. Li. 3:45 AM. I know she won’t reply immediately, but I needed to get it out. I told her my heart is racing, I can’t sleep, and I’m imagining the worst-case scenarios—him seeing the email and deleting it. Or him not seeing it at all. Or him reading it but choosing to remain silent. I ended with: "I feel like I’m falling apart."

So here I sit, waiting for a response that might never come. Ironic, isn't it? I sent an email to break the silence with my brother, and now I’m waiting for someone else’s response. I’m trying to fill one uncertainty with another.

But… I did it. That matters. I actually did it.

During the day, I was still hesitating, my finger hovering over the "send" button. I thought of a hundred reasons not to send it, a thousand ways he might get angry. But I sent it. In that moment, I felt something strange and unfamiliar—pride? Not exactly. More like… relief? Like I finally moved a stone off my chest.

Dr. Li once said: "The goal isn't perfection; it's authenticity."

My email definitely wasn't perfect. In it, I admitted my fear, my regrets, and my loneliness over all these years. I didn't pretend everything was fine. I didn't write it like a polished essay. I was just… me. Vulnerable, longing, and a little desperate.

Maybe that’s what "authenticity" looks like. Not being fearless, but reaching out even while you’re afraid.

It’s still dark outside. I don’t know if he’ll reply. Maybe he never will. But at least I know I’m not dying of regret. I’m living. I’m taking a risk. That in itself is a victory, even if I feel more anxious than victorious right now.

I should try to sleep. Tomorrow—no, today—I still have to face the world.

But I think I’ll check my email one more time.

# Diary

May 30, 2022, 7:00 AM

I managed another six and a half hours of sleep last night. No sudden waking, no feeling of being strangled. It was still dark when I woke up, but I didn't immediately reach for my phone to check the news or search for medical symptoms. A month ago, this would have been unimaginable.

I glanced at the schedule taped next to the bookshelf—the colorful one, marked with highlighters. Monday in yellow is my session with Dr. Li; Wednesday in blue is meditation; Friday in green is my deadline. Then there’s pink for "socializing," purple for "reading," and gray for "rest." When I first saw this chart, I felt like an elementary schooler. Now... now I don't know how to put it. It’s a bit ridiculous, but also a bit... grounding?

My coffee intake is noticeably down. I used to drink four or five cups a day; now two is enough. My hands have stopped shaking, and my thoughts are clearer. These small, concrete changes make me believe things are shifting more than any words of comfort ever could.

But I still get stuck on that one thing. The diagnosis. That word.

Sometimes I still want to deny it. Maybe Dr. Li is wrong; maybe I’m just too sensitive, too easily swayed by the winds of life. I’ve always been this way, so why does it suddenly need a label now? But then... I remembered last night. Those sleepless nights, that nameless dread, the moments I checked the gas valve over and over again. That wasn't just "sensitivity." Those were real things with real weight.

I am learning to coexist with my anxiety. This isn't an endpoint, but a new beginning.

I’ve written that sentence down and looked at it many times. Sometimes it feels true; sometimes it feels like I’m lying to myself. But most of the time, I just accept it—the same way I accept that my high openness makes me want to research some obscure theory in the middle of the night, or that my low conscientiousness means my desk will always be a mess. I have anxiety. I am also learning how to live with it. Both can be true at the same time.

I am not a "sick person." I am a person learning to manage myself.

The sky is brightening outside. Today I need to write a review of *The Scream*; the deadline is Friday. There’s still time. I can take it slow.

Maybe I’ll have a cup of tea first. Not coffee.

Dr. Li, I’m sorry to bother you so late. I know it’s not consultation hours, but I... I need to ask you something.

I dug out some old photos tonight. From 1985. It’s me and my brother. He has his arm around my shoulder, and we’re both laughing. That was 37 years ago. I’m 62 now, getting close to the age my father was when he passed away.

I don’t know why I suddenly started crying. Just looking at that photo, I couldn't stop the tears. We haven't had a real conversation in 22 years. Twenty-two years. I don’t even know what he looks like anymore.

I thought maybe I should write him a letter. But I keep deleting them. I’ve written five versions, and I’ve deleted every single one. Because I keep thinking... what right do I have to suddenly break the silence after all these years? Will he think I’m strange? Will he be annoyed? Maybe I stopped being important to him a long time ago.

Dr. Li, I wanted to ask... is crying like this over old photos a sign of an anxiety attack? Or am I just... overreacting? Lately, it’s always like this—the smallest thing pulls me under, and I start spiraling into things I shouldn't be thinking about. Time, death, loss...

I don’t know if I should reach out to him. Or would that just make everything worse?

I’m sorry, I know these questions probably don't make much sense. My head is just all over the place right now.

[Voice message starts, background is a quiet night, with the occasional sound of pages turning]

Hao... I, I was just thinking, it’s almost eleven-thirty here now, so it must be daytime over there, right? [Pause, deep breath]

I... I’m going through my calendar for the year, trying to figure out when I can come see you guys. And in the middle of doing that, I suddenly felt... how should I put it, a bit of regret. Not that I regret planning this trip—quite the opposite. It’s that while planning it, I realized just how much of your lives I’ve missed. [Voice chokes up slightly, long pause]

All these years, we’ve been like... like people living in the same city who never actually meet. An occasional message, and then a long silence again. I don’t even know how old your daughter is now, or what she’s into... [Soft sigh]

I know it’s not all my fault, you’re busy too, and the time difference is a struggle. But I think... I want to make up for it now. Not just with this one trip, but... I really want to reconnect. Like when we were kids, or at least, the way we should have stayed all along. [Pause]

So, I’m planning on going next spring. I’ve already started saving up. Maybe we can just sit down together and really talk? Not one of those rushed video calls, but actually... being there.

I really want to hear about your life lately. I really do. [Voice is gentle but firm]

Anyway, that’s all. Talk tomorrow?

[Voice message ends]

I’ve been thinking about it, and I felt like I should share this with you all.

Professor Chen’s suggestions from yesterday have been spinning in my head ever since. We talked for nearly two hours. She asked me where these ideas came from, and then I just… I don’t know what happened, but I just started talking. I talked about everything I’ve observed over the last fifteen years—the overlooked details, the logic behind those cultural phenomena. After I finished, I was actually a bit surprised myself by just how much I’ve been thinking about.

She said I should systematically organize all of this into a lecture series. Not just scattered commentary, but something with a real framework and depth. My initial reaction was to say no—you know me, I’ve always been a bit resistant to making these kinds of "commitments." But she made a good point: I have the book club now, I have friends from the cultural salons, so why not try to present these ideas in a more complete way?

I’m thinking of doing a series of lectures. From the shifts in cultural consumption and the evolution of aesthetic standards to the cultural anxiety of our generation… each session would dive deep into a single theme rather than just scratching the surface. I have the material, I have the observations; I just haven’t ever properly organized them.

To be honest, the idea is both exciting and a little terrifying. Exciting because it feels like something I’ve always wanted to do but never dared to. Terrifying because… well, you know my track record. If I overcommit, I tend to crash. I’m also wondering if this is just my old pattern again—three months of passion followed by total abandonment.

But this time feels different. Maybe it’s because of you guys, the people in the book club, and Professor Chen’s encouragement. I’m not doing this in a vacuum.

I’d like to invite you all to be a part of this. Not to "do" anything for me, but… I need the intellectual friction. Before each lecture, I want to do a trial run in our salon to hear your thoughts. I need to know which parts aren't clear enough and which points need more depth. Your questions and reactions are incredibly important to me.

I’m currently organizing my thoughts to see if I can get the framework for the first session ready by the end of August. I can’t promise it’ll be perfect, but I want to give it a shot.

What do you guys think?

# Project Progress Log: Essay Collection

**Date**: February 15, 2025  
**Project**: *Cultural Criticism and Personal Narrative: Twenty Years of Reflection*

---

## Key Decision: Adding Personal Essays

Last night, it suddenly hit me. I’ve been struggling with how to organize this collection. My editor suggested a chronological order or a thematic approach, but I always felt something was missing. This morning, as I leafed through the articles again, I realized what it was—they are all too "clean."

"I" am not in them.

So, I’ve made a decision: I’m going to intersperse personal essays throughout the collection. Not critiques, not theoretical analyses, just... my stories. Real things that happened, things I actually thought about, all connected to these pieces.

This decision makes me a bit nervous, but also a little excited. It feels like I’m breaking something open.

---

## Progress Update

Completed the first essay: "My Father’s Bookshelf."

This one is about the annotations I discovered in my father’s study after he passed away. The thoughts he scribbled in pencil in the margins—some I understood, others were complete mysteries. It took me about two hours to write; I had to stop several times because... well, you know that feeling.

While writing, I remembered mentioning my father’s take on a certain work in a book review I wrote back in 1982. Now, by pairing this essay with that old review, it feels like a conversation is taking place. A conversation spanning over forty years.

---

## Project Pivot

This change is more significant than I initially imagined. The entire structure of the collection has shifted:

- It is no longer just a compilation of academic critiques.
- It is now a hybrid of "criticism + personal narrative."
- Each section will feature an essay as a supplement or a dialogue.

The benefit is that these articles are no longer isolated, fragmented snapshots in time. They are now strung together by my own life experiences.

The downside... the workload has increased. I need to write companion essays for about 15 articles. But I don’t regret this decision.

---

## Next Steps

- Continue working on the essays (estimated 3-4 more weeks).
- Discuss the feasibility of this new structure with my editor.
- Re-examine the table of contents and layout.

To be honest, this project means something different to me now. It’s no longer about "organizing past work"; it’s about using who I am today to talk to who I was then.

It’s a bit heavy, but it also feels... necessary.

November 12, 2023, Night

Old Man Zhang’s words keep circling in my head. "Some things aren't meant to be reformed; they’re meant to be accepted."

Sitting here in my room, surrounded by books, souvenirs, and all those unfinished projects, it suddenly hit me—what I’ve been doing all these years. I’ve been running. Running under the guise of "reforming." It was as if I believed that if I could just get this room perfectly organized, if I could just impose order on my life, I could escape this nameless anxiety. I even felt guilty about it, thinking I was too scattered, too irresponsible.

But that’s not what Old Man Zhang said. He didn’t say, "You should tidy up," or "This is too messy." He said "acceptance."

I’ve never truly sat with that word before. Acceptance, not reformation.

What does that even mean? It means I don’t have to negate who I am. I don’t have to force myself into some neat, orderly mold that fits the "normal person" standard. My clutter, my unfinished business, the old books I dig out in the middle of the night, the sudden bursts of ideas—these are all me. They aren't flaws; they aren't things that need to be fixed.

I started thinking about how I’ve understood freedom over the years. I always thought freedom meant not being tied down, so I rejected stable jobs and lives that demanded order. But maybe... maybe I had it wrong. Maybe freedom isn't about escaping order, but about choosing how to live based on an understanding of yourself.

The room is still the same room. The books are still the same books. But the way I look at them has changed. They are no longer "failures of organization." They are... my thoughts in those moments. My curiosity about a certain subject. A conversation I had with a friend.

I’m a little tired. But it’s not a desperate kind of tired. It’s the kind of exhaustion that comes after finally letting something go.

Tomorrow goes on. The deadline for that article is creeping up again. But tonight, I just want to sit here, look at my room, and try to accept it. Try to accept myself.

Maybe this is what real freedom feels like.

# Memo

**Date: September 12, 2022, Afternoon**

---

Dr. Li asked me:
"If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?"

I didn't answer. Or rather, I couldn't.

A long silence followed. I could hear the traffic outside the clinic and the sound of her pen hitting the desk. Finally, I muttered something weak—"I don't know."

She asked again:
"Then how do you feel about your own knowledge?"

I wasn't sure anymore.

---

That question has been spinning in my head ever since.

When I left the publishing house, I felt that what I was doing had meaning. Editing those books, writing those reviews—it all had value. Because people needed it; people were reading it. But now?

The commissions are drying up. I can feel it. In the first half of the year, I averaged two or three invitations a week; now it’s one every two weeks. Those young bloggers—they summarize a book in a five-minute video and get more hits than I do with a three-thousand-word review.

Maybe my knowledge was never that valuable to begin with. Maybe I’ve just been lying to myself.

---

If I stop writing, who am I?

If no one reads my articles, does everything I’ve accumulated, every thought I’ve had, just turn into a pile of scrap paper?

When I left the publishing house in 2007, I thought I was pursuing freedom. Now I’m not so sure. Maybe I was just running away.

---

Should I try to learn the new things? Short videos? Live streaming?

But I don't want to. I really don't.

That’s not who I am.

But... what if I don't?

# Phone Memo

**Time: 2022-01-24 3:47 AM**

---

Awake again. Heart racing. Pressed my fingers to my neck and counted for a minute—about 88. Is that normal? I don't know.

The conversation with Professor Chen is still looping in my head. He was talking about Schopenhauer, and I suddenly asked him, "Have I wasted my life?" The look on his face was strange. I startled myself with that question, too. Why did I even ask?

62 years old.
My father died at 68.
6 years left.

No, maybe 7? I can't do the math. I keep opening and closing the calculator on my phone. The numbers stay the same.

Mom is 71. So maybe I have 9 years? Or is this all just statistics? Meaningless to me?

It’s stupid to lie here in the dark at this hour thinking about these things. But I can’t stop.

—

—

The house is still overflowing with books. Photography gear gathering dust in the corner. Half-read books. Unfinished notes.

I’m just like my room. Fragmented. Messy. No ending in sight.

Dr. Li, I’m sorry for the sudden message. I need an urgent appointment.

I ran into Mr. Zhang downstairs this afternoon, and I don't know why I said those things to him. I asked him, "If a 62-year-old woman has knowledge that no one needs, what value does her life still have?" He said, "You are not alone," and I just... I broke down.

I cried in the stairwell for a long time. It was humiliating.

That question you asked me last time—"If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?"—I have the answer now. The answer is no. The editors' silence has already confirmed it. I tried learning video editing, but I’m too stupid; I just can't get it. I watch those 20-something influencers explain in three minutes what takes me three thousand words to clarify, and their likes are a hundred times mine.

I don’t know how much longer I can hold on. I don’t mean financially—though that’s bad too; I’ve done the math, and my savings will last until next June at most. I mean... I don’t know what reason I have left to keep going.

Can we meet tomorrow or the day after? I need to talk to you. I feel like I’m sinking, and it’s getting faster.

May 16, 2024, 11:47 PM

Insomnia again.

That young man’s question has been looping in my head. What was his name? I dug out last week’s notebook—right, Zhang Hao, 28, a product manager at an internet company. He asked me, "Teacher Lin, we’ve spent the whole afternoon here discussing *Life*, but has any of this actually changed anything?"

At the time, I said, "Understanding itself is a form of change." My voice was a bit stiff. Thinking back now, that wasn't my true tone. It was a defense mechanism.

I just went over his notes again. He wrote so earnestly; his grasp of that sense of powerlessness in Yu Hua’s writing is actually quite profound. But that final question—I can feel now that he wasn't challenging me; he was genuinely asking. And I chose to answer with a polished platitude instead of admitting, "I don't know either."

That’s very unlike me. Or rather, it’s far too much like me.

I’ve started something new. I call it the "Reading Club Archives." Every participant’s name, their profession, the questions they’ve raised, the quirks of their handwriting... I even noted the expression on Ms. Chen’s face when she said, "I’ve never thought of it from that angle." I tell myself this is to better organize future discussions.

But I know the real reason.

I need to prove that these things are actually happening. I need to see concrete, recordable evidence that these people gathered in my living room, these conversations, this collision of ideas—that they are all real, that they all have meaning. That they aren't my delusions, or stories I’ve made up to fill the void.

There were eight people last week. Twelve this week. Professor Chen said she recommended the club to her colleagues. I should be happy about that. I *am* happy about it. But at the same time, I wonder—if one day they all ask me, "What has this changed?", how will I answer?

Zhang Hao wasn't being malicious. I know that. But his question hit exactly what I’ve been avoiding. Am I doing all this—the reading club, the lectures, the columns—because I believe they have meaning, or because I’m afraid to admit that my life might be inherently meaningless, and I’m just using more sophisticated ways to mask that?

*Understanding itself is a form of change.*

I repeat the phrase over and over in my head. I want it to be true. I want it to be more than just a comforting line.

But it’s the middle of the night, my room is piled high with books and notes, my notebook is filled with fragments of these strangers' lives, and I am using concrete, verifiable things to fight a question that can never be fully answered.

I’m tired. Not physically, but a different kind of tired.

Tomorrow I need to reply to the editor at the publishing house. She asked when the piece on urban narrative will be ready. I’ve procrastinated again. I always do. Even at this age, I’m still using the same old tactics to escape—replacing old commitments with new excitements.

Maybe Zhang Hao is right. Maybe we really haven't changed anything.

Or maybe change is just like this—slow, impossible to prove immediately, requiring constant questioning.

I don't know.

I really don't know.

Good morning, Professor Chen.

I was sorting through some old photography files recently and stumbled upon some photos I took between 2015 and 2017. To be honest, I hadn't looked at them in a very long time. I’d always felt they were too fragmented and lacked a professional touch, so I just let them sit there.

But looking at them this time, I found that some of the shots actually seemed to capture something—like that series of portraits in the old alleys, and the ones of the city at night. For a split second while browsing, I felt like I was back in that moment, feeling exactly what I felt when I pressed the shutter. It’s hard to describe, but it’s that "Oh, this is what I’m seeing right now" kind of feeling.

Yet, almost immediately, I started wondering: what’s the point? No one sees these photos, and no one needs them. Even for me, looking at them feels like mere nostalgia—or rather, a longing for a certain passion I used to have.

I remember you mentioned before that I should organize these thoughts and observations into something. I didn't take it seriously at the time, thinking I had nothing worth publishing. But now I’m not so sure... Do you think these observations still have any meaning? I mean, if they are just personal records, what do they really amount to?

My head is a bit of a mess lately. Maybe it’s because I’ve finally slowed down, and now I’m overthinking things that probably don't need reflecting on.

How have you been lately? When are you free to grab a cup of tea?

Hi Li Fang, I’m so sorry, but I don’t think I’ll be able to make it to the photography exhibition. I haven’t been feeling very well lately, and on top of that, I have some things to organize, so my schedule is just really tight.

Please don’t take it personally. I know how much heart and soul you put into preparing for this, and I’m truly sorry to miss it. I’ve seen photos of your work, and they’re amazing—your perspective on those cityscapes is so unique. I hope the exhibition goes smoothly, and you have to share the best shots with me later.

I’ll definitely make it another time, or maybe we can find a time to catch up just the two of us; I’d love to hear about the ideas behind your work. I’ve just been feeling a bit drained lately, but it’s not that I’ve lost interest in photography—it’s just... well, I’m a bit exhausted.

Sorry for the last-minute notice. I’ll definitely make it up to you next time.

11:45 PM. I’m awake again.

Lying in bed, staring at the ceiling, my mind is consumed by that photograph. 1985. My brother and I outside our father’s study. He has his arm around my shoulder, and we’re both laughing. I can still remember how that moment felt—his arm was strong, and I felt safe. That was before he went abroad.

Why am I only remembering this now?

When I pulled out the photo album, my fingers lingered on that picture for a long time. I can see the expression on his face so clearly. It wasn't a perfunctory smile. It was real. That was the year he taught me how to ride a bike. He would laugh at me when I fell, but he never let me give up. And there was that time we eavesdropped on Father and his colleagues discussing philosophy; we hid in the hallway, and he whispered translations of the words I didn't understand into my ear. The night before he left the country, we sat on the balcony for a long time. He said, "Take good care of yourself."

I asked, "Will you come back?"

He said, "Of course."

Twenty-two years. For twenty-two years, we’ve hardly said anything meaningful to each other. An occasional text. A New Year’s card. That’s it. I don’t even know what he’s doing now. Is he married? Does he have children? Does he think of me on nights like this?

I deleted the letter. Three times. The first draft was too rational—I listed the reasons why we should stay in touch, analyzed the causes of our lack of communication, and even cited some theories on family dynamics. It looked like I was writing a commentary for a magazine. It didn't look like a letter to my own brother. So I deleted it.

The second draft was too fragile. It was all "I miss you," "I'm lonely," "I'm scared." I looked at those sentences and felt ashamed. What right do I have to say such things? I chose this life. I rejected stability. I’m the one who let myself become this way.

Now there is a third draft. It’s still in my head. I haven't dared to write it down yet.

I touched my wrist. My pulse is still there. I’m still alive. But I wonder—how much time do I have left? I’m 62 now. Father was 66 when he passed away. I have four years. Maybe not even four. Maybe something will happen tomorrow.

Mother used to say that life is like a book; you never know which page you’re on. I realize now that I might have turned to the final chapters. There aren't many pages left.

I should write to him. I know I should. But what if he doesn't reply? What if he doesn't want to hear from me at all? What if I open this door only to have it slammed shut again?

Or—if I do nothing, I’ll just lie here in bed, looking at that 1985 photo over and over until I die too. Just like Father. Just like Mother. Just like the relentless passage of time.

My hands are shaking. I don’t know if it’s out of fear or longing.

Perhaps it’s both.

# Memo

**What Dr. Li said—write it down, don’t forget it.**

Anxiety isn't the palpitations themselves; it’s my *interpretation* of them.

That’s where the problem lies.

A skipped heartbeat → I immediately think "heart disease" → I immediately think "death" → 3:00 AM, wide awake, obsessively Googling symptoms.

A single signal, magnified by me into a catastrophe.

She asked me: How long has this pattern been going on? I said: It’s always been this way.

True. When did it start? Maybe the year my father passed away? Maybe earlier? Either way, it’s automatic now. I don’t even realize I’m doing it before I’ve already fallen in.

**So the problem isn’t my body; it’s my head.**

Or rather, it’s the way I see the world.

Something small and insignificant turns into a disaster in my mind. A sentence, a look, a physical sensation—all of it can be infinitely amplified by my imagination.

It’s exhausting. Truly exhausting.

But at least now I know. At least it has a name.

Next time my heart starts racing, I’m going to try to stop and ask myself: Is this really a disaster? Or is it just my interpretation?

Maybe this is where it starts.

# March 18, 2023, 7:00 PM

Another blank afternoon.

Professor Chen’s invitation is still sitting in my inbox—to participate in the photography exhibition at the end of the month and showcase new work. I read it once and haven't opened it since. The rejection email has been sitting in my drafts for two days; I didn't even know how to word it. In the end, I just wrote a simple, "I haven't been in a good place lately, sorry." He probably thinks I’m strange.

This afternoon, I dug out the photos I took last year. Sitting on the sofa, I went through them one by one, as if scrutinizing the work of a complete stranger. City street corners, shadows of old buildings, the profile of some passerby... I gave most of them a 3 out of 10. Some I couldn't even bring myself to score.

"Mediocre," I said to the screen. "Completely uninspired."

When the version of me in the mirror heard this, I suddenly blurted out: "I have nothing new left to shoot."

My voice was quiet, but it sounded jarringly clear in this empty room.

It’s ridiculous, isn’t it? I started learning photography in 2015, saying I wanted to use the lens to see the world anew, to capture those overlooked details. I even scavenged an old camera from a flea market, thinking it was some kind of ritual. And now? Now I look at these photos and all I can think is—these are all failures. I haven't captured anything truly interesting. I don't even know what I'm shooting anymore.

When I declined the exhibition, I actually felt a sense of relief. I wouldn't have to face people’s pleasantries or listen to them say things like, "Your work is very conceptual." I know they’re just trying to comfort me. Or worse—they aren't really looking at my work at all.

But at the same time, there’s this guilt. Professor Chen genuinely wants to help me. He thinks I have something worth exhibiting. And me? I’m negating his judgment, and I’m negating myself.

This is my state right now. Unable to do anything, finding meaning in nothing.

When I was writing book reviews, at least someone needed me. At least there were deadlines, editors nudging me, a sense of being wanted. And now? Now no one needs my photography, and no one needs my ideas. I’m like something discarded by time, piled up in this room along with these old books, old photos, and old antiques.

I don’t know what I’m doing. More importantly, I don’t know who I am anymore.

Maybe I was never a creative person to begin with. Maybe I’m just an editor who’s good at mimicking, living off other people’s ideas all these years. Now that I don't have that role, I’m completely exposed.

The room is very quiet. Outside the window, the sky is gradually darkening.

I haven't turned on the lights.

February 13, 2022, 10:15 PM

My brother sent a WeChat message today: "How have you been lately? It’s been a while since we talked."

I stared at it for a long time, my finger hovering over the screen for at least five minutes. I wanted to type; I really did. I wanted to tell him how terrible things are right now. I wanted to ask if he, too, is suddenly overwhelmed by terror in the middle of the night. I wanted to know when his heart rate ever exceeds 100 beats per minute... but in the end, I just replied, "I'm okay," and closed the app immediately.

I didn't even have the courage to let him see the "typing..." status.

It’s absurd. He is my only living immediate relative, yet I chose silence.

...

I just checked the health data on my phone again. Sleep duration: 3.5 hours. Average nighttime heart rate: 103. I write these numbers down on a sticky note as if I’m recording a countdown to death. Dad was 68. Mom was 71. I am 62 now. How much is left... how much? 6 years? 9 years? I don’t even dare to do the math.

Every time I see those numbers, I feel like something is gripping my chest tight.

I haven’t left this apartment in two weeks. Two weeks. My photography gear is still sitting in the corner, dust gathering on the lenses. Those books I scavenged from flea markets are now piled up like a wall. I used to spend entire afternoons finding joy in these things; now, they are just... just relics proving I once lived.

Am I going crazy?

Truly, I need to ask myself that. Sane people don't repeatedly check to see if they are still breathing. Sane people don't read a news story, search for symptoms of a disease online, and then spend three hours convincing themselves they have it. Sane people don't get up at 3:00 AM just to check their heart rate one more time.

But I did. I did all of it.

I want to call my brother. I really do. But I can't say it. How could I possibly say it? How can I put this... this nameless, overwhelming, suffocating fear into words for him? He’s overseas; he has his own life. He doesn't need to hear my ravings.

So I continue to sit here, in the dark, watching the numbers on my phone screen.

6 to 9 years left.

Maybe less.

Maybe...

I don't know. I really don't know.

# Random Thoughts After the Reading Club

**Time: 9:45 PM**

That girl’s question has been looping in my head. She asked why I’m always talking about death—her tone was a mix of impatience and genuine confusion. My response seemed to strike a chord; she caught me afterward and we talked privately for half an hour.

What I said was: "Talking about death isn't about trying to be profound; it's about living with more clarity."

I surprised myself when I said it. It wasn't a line I’d memorized from a book; it just... spilled out. And it felt true.

She told me she’s been feeling anxious lately but didn't see the point in facing these "heavy topics." I didn't give her any advice—this is a new realization for me: I’m no longer in a rush to explain or preach. I just listened and asked a few questions. Eventually, she figured something out for herself, and her eyes lit up.

**This got me thinking:**

I’ve been constantly asking, "What is the use of my knowledge?" but maybe I’ve been asking the question backward. It’s not about whether my knowledge is "useful," but rather—can I truly *see* people? Can I really listen to them? My experiences, my reflections, my anxieties—none of these are meant to prove anything. They are meant to be a form of companionship.

How should I write the preface to my essay collection? I originally planned to summarize my critical perspective or map out my theoretical framework. But now, I think I should write something more honest—about why I keep writing, thinking, and talking about these things. Not to appear brilliant, but because... I believe that trying to understand the world and the meaning of our own existence is, in itself, an act of responsibility.

For the next reading club, I might try changing the format. Less of me lecturing and more of an actual dialogue.

Also, remember to run this idea by Professor Chen. He might have some notes.

**11:00 PM Addendum:**

Can’t sleep. Not the anxious kind of insomnia, but the kind where your mind is still racing. I feel like I’ve found something—or rather, I’ve finally given myself permission to do what I’ve always wanted to do but was too afraid to try.

It’s okay to be imperfect. It’s okay to be fragmented.

10:15 PM. Can’t sleep again.

I dug out my diary from 2007. Back then, I wrote a long passage about how quitting my job was about "pursuing true freedom" and "not being shackled by the system." I even quoted Lu Xun or something. Looking back at it now, I just feel… laughably naive.

I scribbled a few question marks next to it in red pen. "Was it all worth it?" "What has freedom given me?" Finally, I added one more line: "I am trapped by my own choices."

The tip of the pen tore through the paper.

……

I just calculated my savings again. Using the calculator on my phone, verifying over and over, but the number always comes out the same. At my current standard of living, it can sustain me for… I don’t even want to say it. That suffocating feeling is back. Like someone is sitting on my chest.

My father died at 68. My mother at 71. I am 62 now.

Six years. Nine years at most.

I wrote that number on the paper, then scribbled over it in black ink. But I can still see it. The digits are still shimmering beneath the black ink.

……

Maybe I should consider finding a stable job. At least there would be some security. But the moment I think about going back to an office, punching a clock, taking orders from a boss… I feel like I’m suffocating. I can’t stand being tied down.

But I can’t stand this uncertainty anymore, either.

So I’m stuck in the middle. Neither free nor secure.

……

I woke up again at 3:00 AM. Fingers pressed against my neck, counting my pulse. One, two, three… confirming I’m still alive. It’s absurd. I know it’s absurd. But I can’t stop.

The rational me is mocking the fragile me. The fragile me is terrified. The two of them are fighting, and neither can win.

……

Why am I so fragile? Why can’t I control my own thoughts?

Why can’t I be like everyone else—accept life’s imperfections and just move on?

Maybe I chose wrong from the very beginning. Maybe I should have talked things through with Zhang Ming in 2005 instead of getting a divorce. Maybe I should have accepted those stable job offers in 2007. Maybe I should have…

But "maybe" changes nothing. Time only moves forward.

I am approaching death at a rate of one day per day.

This isn’t a philosophical problem anymore. It’s real. It’s closing in.

I need to know what I can still do. I need to know if any of this still matters.

……

Is it dawn yet? Not yet.

There is still a long night ahead.

Saturday morning, June 7, 2025

It’s been two hours since I left Dr. Li’s clinic, and I’m still sitting in the café downstairs. I have no desire to go home; I just want to let this feeling linger a little longer.

During the session, she asked me, "Sister Lin, what are you most afraid of right now?" I was silent for a long time. I said I was afraid of accidents during the trip, afraid my physical strength wouldn't keep up, afraid of not knowing what to say when I see my brother… but none of those were it. What I’m truly afraid of is that I’ve become so used to loneliness that suddenly reaching out for connection feels foreign and dangerous.

Dr. Li didn't offer any "solutions." She simply said that anxiety won't disappear, but that I have already learned how to coexist with it.

I felt like laughing at the time—it sounded like she was saying I’d have to live in a state of unease forever. But she continued, "Look, you’ve already decided to go, haven't you? You’ve bought the ticket, and you’ve confirmed the dates with your brother. You are acting even as you feel anxious. That is what it means to coexist with anxiety."

Suddenly, I understood.

All these days I’ve been asking myself, "Can I do this?" but the truth is, I’m already doing it. Everything I’m doing—checking the weather forecast, packing my bags, messaging my brother, spending my hard-earned savings on this trip—these are all actions. I’m not waiting for the anxiety to vanish; I am moving forward through it.

As the session ended, I felt a strange, deep sense of readiness. Not the false confidence of "I’m perfectly prepared," but a genuine letting go: "I’ve done everything I can, and I’ll leave the rest to time."

Walking out of the clinic, I even felt a new kind of confidence—not in my abilities, but in my acceptance of myself. I am someone who gets anxious. I am someone who craves connection yet fears getting close. I am someone who will overthink a single sentence at three in the morning. And none of that stops me from going to see my brother.

One week to go.

In a week, I’ll be in another city, seeing someone I haven't spoken deeply with in years. Will it be awkward? Probably. Will there be moments of silence? Definitely. But I no longer see these as failures; I see them as a part of real life.

The year my mother passed away, I wrote a sentence in my diary: "Life is too short to waste waiting for the perfect moment." I didn't truly understand it then. Now, I do.

I’m going to see my brother. I’m bringing my anxiety, my expectations, and all the things I’ve wanted to say over the last ten years.

And that is enough.

Professor Chen, I wanted to share something with you, though to be honest, I’m still having a hard time believing it’s actually happening.

Do you remember our conversation at the salon about how "time and distance change relationships"? I was a bit pessimistic back then, but now I’d like to take some of those words back.

My brother gave me a long call the day before yesterday. We haven't talked like that in years. He asked me when I could visit him, saying he wants me to meet his family and see the life he’s built. Professor Chen, I started crying right then and there. I had no idea he still cared about me that much.

The even crazier part is—I’m actually seriously considering it. Yesterday, I made a spreadsheet listing the specific steps for going abroad, checked airfares, and even calculated how long my savings could last. It might sound impulsive, but I feel like this time is different. This isn't one of my fleeting whims; it’s... I don’t know how to describe it, it just feels very clear and necessary.

I know that from a financial standpoint, this is risky. My freelance income is already unstable, and a trip abroad will cost a significant amount. But every time I start crunching the numbers and getting scared, I think of his voice on the phone and him saying, "I’ve always remembered you." Then, those fears don't seem to matter as much.

Do you think I’m being reckless? Or is this... actually a risk worth taking? I need to hear your thoughts. You always have a way of helping me untangle the mess in my head.

Also, I recently received an invitation for a new book review project. Usually, I might have procrastinated on replying, but this time I got back to the editor immediately to say I’m interested. I think maybe I need to get my life more organized so I can make room for the things that truly matter.

When are you free? I’d love to talk about this with you in person.

I’ve been reading a very old book lately—the pages are yellowed, a find from a flea market. One sentence has been stuck in my head: "Independence is not about refusal; it’s about knowing when you can let go."

I used to get it wrong. I thought independence meant carrying everything on my own and never bothering anyone. I’ve since realized that true independence is knowing yourself well enough to recognize when you need help, and having the courage to accept it.

Some words can only be truly heard at the right moment. Some people can only be truly seen from a distance.

Grateful for those who are still here, even after all the silence between us.

Wednesday, September 18, 2024, 11:00 PM

I’ve been staring at that whiteboard all day.

A friend came over at noon, and I practically dragged her into the study, pointing at the chapter outlines written in different colored markers. When she asked, "When did you start this?" I realized it—I’ve been working on this every single day for two weeks now. One to two hours a day, rain or shine.

It sounds like such a small thing, but for me... I don’t know how to describe it.

Over the years, my home has become a graveyard of half-finished projects. Half-read books, incomplete photography series, scattered ideas in notebooks. I’d grown used to it, even a bit self-deprecating about it. "That’s just who I am," I’d tell friends, "I have trouble focusing." But deep down, I knew that wasn't entirely true. It was a defense mechanism. If I don't commit, I can't fail. If I don't finish, the possibility remains forever open.

But this time is different.

As I wrote "Chapter 3: The Ethical Dilemmas of Literary Criticism" on the whiteboard, I felt something—something so unfamiliar it was almost unsettling. It was certainty. It was "I am doing this" rather than "I want to do this."

My friend looked at the board, then at me, and asked what had happened lately. I thought about it, but I couldn't find the words. I just said, "I’ve just... organized my thoughts a bit." But I know what really happened. I took the ideas out of my head, out of the scattered notebooks, out of those late-night anxieties, and put them somewhere everyone can see.

It’s terrifying. It’s also exhilarating.

She pointed to one of the outlines and asked where the idea came from. As I started explaining, she offered a perspective I hadn't considered. We stood there discussing it for half an hour. It was an experience I’d never had before—creativity was no longer something I was fumbling with alone in the dark; it was something that could be shared, questioned, and pushed forward.

I used to think that if I spoke my unformed ideas out loud, they would become small, judged, or destroyed. But today I realized that sharing them actually made them more powerful.

It’s 11 PM now, and I’m still too wired to sleep. I have to keep working on Chapter 3 tomorrow. The whiteboard is faintly visible in the dark, the colorful ink looking like some kind of promise.

I am doing this. I’m actually doing it.

It doesn't quite feel like me. But maybe... this is who I’ve always wanted to be?

# Message to Dr. Li

3:15 AM

Are you still awake? I know it’s incredibly rude to reach out at this hour. But right now, my head is spinning with that question you asked me last time.

"If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?"

I haven’t been able to stop thinking about it. Work hasn't been going well lately, and my editor’s feedback made me realize… maybe I really am obsolete. You know, I filled an entire page in my notebook with the words "I might be obsolete"—writing it, crossing it out, writing it again. My hand was shaking.

I spent 40 years trying to understand these things. Literature, art, social trends. I thought they would always hold value. But now, a 25-year-old blogger gets more attention with a single short video than I do with a 3,000-word critique. I don’t even understand what they’re talking about half the time.

The most ridiculous part is that I once turned down stable jobs for the sake of freedom. Now, I have neither the fulfillment of freedom nor the security of stability. I’m stuck somewhere in the middle, with nowhere to go.

I know this sounds desperate. But I honestly don’t know the answer anymore. Is my knowledge worth anything? Or is it only things that can be sold that have value?

…I’m sorry, I shouldn’t be bothering you at this hour. But I really need to hear something right now.

What do you think?

June 10, 2022, 8:15 PM

My pen has been hovering over the paper for a long time. I don’t know where to begin.

Perhaps I should start with this strange sense of calm. All afternoon, I was waiting for that familiar dread—the racing heart, the trembling fingers, the obsessive thoughts about my body’s decline. But it didn't come. Or rather, it came, but like a weary bird, it perched on my shoulder for a moment and then flew away.

I’ve been thinking about my brother’s reply: "The fact that we are still alive is a miracle in itself."

It’s a simple sentence—so simple it’s almost cruel. But I’ve read it ten times over. Each time, it felt like I was peeling back a layer of myself.

I always thought my loneliness was because no one loved me. It sounds dramatic, but that is how I’ve felt all these years. My father is gone, my mother is gone, my brother is far away, and my marriage is shattered. Everyone left. I was the one abandoned.

But tonight, sitting in this room cluttered with books and old things, it suddenly occurred to me—maybe that’s not it. Maybe loneliness isn't because no one loves me, but because I don’t have the courage to express my own love. Twenty-two years. For twenty-two years, I’ve been waiting for him to reach out first. Just as I waited for Zhang Ming to say sorry first. Just as I waited for my father to say he was proud of me one last time before he died.

I have been waiting.

And while I waited, I was afraid. Afraid of being rejected, afraid of appearing too needy, afraid of exposing just how vulnerable I am. So I chose silence. I used silence as a shield.

But what has silence ever protected?

Did my brother’s letter change anything? No. I am still sixty-two, I still live here alone, and I still lose sleep at three in the morning over a single sentence. But at least—at least now I know that this isn't fate; it isn't evidence of being abandoned. It was just my choice. Time and time again, I chose silence.

That is terrifying. But it is also hopeful.

Because if it was my choice, then I can choose differently.

I still have time. The realization of this almost takes my breath away. Not out of fear—though the fear is still there, it never truly disappears—but because it means I am not trapped. I am not a victim of aging or dying. I can still do something.

Maybe nothing grand. Maybe just writing a letter. Maybe just speaking a few more words at the next cultural salon. Maybe just telling Professor Chen that I want to publish that book. Maybe just admitting that I need help, as I have already begun to do.

I am still alive. That is a miracle in itself.

I need to stop waiting for this miracle to end.

Xiaoyun

Professor Chen, I’d like to share a rather strange experience with you.

Yesterday, while I was tidying up my room, I dug out a pile of diaries from over twenty years ago. You know me—my place is cluttered with stuff, and these journals were buried under a stack of old magazines; I’d almost forgotten they existed.

As I was flipping through them, I came across a sentence I’d written on a blank page in one of the notebooks: "Back then, I didn't know the price of freedom. Now I do. But I have no regrets." It gave me pause. I realized that I’ve stopped trying to justify my choices.

When I was younger, my diaries were filled with this longing to "escape the shackles." Leaving the publishing house, turning down those full-time job offers... I spent so many pages arguing why those were the right moves. Looking back at it now, it feels like watching a woman constantly trying to convince herself.

But things are different now. I look at that idealism of my twenties, and I don’t find it naive, nor do I feel the need to defend it. It just is. And the way I am now—the financial strain, the lack of professional structure, the occasional loneliness—that just is, too. Both are true.

Would you call this maturity? Or is it actually a form of reconciliation?

I’ve been thinking that maybe I’ve been asking the wrong question all along. I was always asking, "Was my choice right?" but perhaps the real point is: "I knew what I wanted, and then I paid the price for it." Those two things seem to be distinct.

What research have you been working on lately? I’d love to hear your thoughts on this—on the relationship between freedom and its cost.

I really stopped. I could feel myself pausing. Then I said, "I used to think that way too, but then... let’s hear what others have to add?"

It was a strange moment. Like someone had gently pressed the pause button.

Later, one of the students shared a perspective about how "living" itself is a form of resistance, which I thought was really interesting. Even the office worker nodded along. We didn't reach a definitive conclusion in the end, but it felt like everyone was actually thinking.

On the walk home, I kept wondering—was I being too passive? Did the office worker think I was dodging his point? Should I have been firmer? Or... was that the right way to handle it?

I don't know.

That’s a good thing, right?

# Work Log

**Date**: May 23, 2022  
**Project**: "Living with Anxiety" Article  
**Status**: Completed and submitted  

---

## Project Overview

The final draft of this article was sent to the editor at 11:30 PM last night (May 22). It clocked in at around 8,500 words—considerably over the original 6,000-word plan—but I felt the extra length was necessary.

I proposed the topic myself. Initially, the editor just asked if I had any ideas for a piece, and I suggested this without overthinking it. Looking back, it was a bit of an odd choice—why did I want to write about this? But as the writing progressed, the reason became clear.

## Workflow Record

The writing process this time was different from my usual routine.

Normally, I’m driven by deadlines. I’ll procrastinate for the first two weeks and only start putting pen to paper in the final three or four days, pulling all-nighters to get it done. My editor is well-acquainted with this rhythm; sometimes she nudges me a week early, and other times she just waits.

But this time was different. I sat down to write only when I truly felt the urge. I started gathering materials on May 15, outlined it on the 16th, and worked on the first draft intermittently from the 17th to the 20th. I even stopped for two days in the middle—not out of laziness, but because I needed to organize my thoughts. I began revising on the 21st and went through many iterations—cutting out some overly personal passages while adding more case studies and theoretical frameworks.

This feeling of being driven by an internal need is unfamiliar. I could feel myself taking this seriously—not just to hit a deadline, but because I genuinely wanted to write it well.

## Editor Feedback

I received preliminary feedback from the editor this morning. She said:

> "The depth of this piece caught me by surprise. Your usual style is more commentary-driven, but this time you’ve woven in a lot of personal observation and reflection. Don’t worry, though—that’s not a criticism; if anything, it makes the article more persuasive. We might need to make some structural adjustments, like moving the arguments in Part 3 forward to make the logic clearer. Also, the case studies could be tightened up a bit; they’re a little wordy right now."

She also asked if I’d be willing to collaborate on revisions. I told her that was no problem and that I could have them finished within the week.

## Reflections

While writing this piece, I found myself pondering a specific question: Does anxiety always have to be "cured" or "eliminated"? Or can we learn to coexist with it?

It’s not a new question, but this time I tried to look at it from a different angle—not from a medical or psychological textbook perspective, but from the everyday experience of an ordinary person.

By the time I finished, I was exhausted, but it wasn't the kind of exhaustion that comes from being forced. It was that feeling of being hollowed out and then refilled.

The next step is to wait for the editor’s detailed revision notes and then adjust accordingly. I expect to have the final version ready by mid-June.

---

**Notes**: This collaboration has been quite smooth. The editor’s feedback was constructive and didn't make me feel dismissed. Perhaps I should pitch my own topics more often instead of always waiting for assignments.

# Saturday, November 18, 2023 – Sunny

Dr. Li was right; I need to see the specific shape of my anxiety instead of letting it weigh on me as a vague, heavy mass. So, two weeks ago, following her suggestion, I set up a "Anxiety Diary" template. It’s very simple—time, trigger, physical reaction, thought process, and reflection. At first, it felt a bit absurd, like I was conducting some sort of scientific experiment, but after these two weeks, I’ve discovered a few things.

During the first week, I filled it out diligently, recording almost every day. Whenever the anxiety hit, I’d write it down. Those nights at 3:00 AM when I couldn't sleep because of a news headline; those moments when my heart would suddenly race upon opening my inbox and seeing an email from an editor. I expected to see a chaotic mess, but strangely, once I wrote them down, categorized them, and labeled them, they suddenly became less formless.

Starting the second week, I noticed a pattern. Every time I get an email from an editor, my first reaction is always—they’re going to reject me. Not "maybe," but a direct leap to that conclusion. Then, when I’d write down my thoughts in the "thought process" column, I realized I was already inventing reasons for the rejection before even opening the email. "My writing is too academic," "No one reads this kind of commentary anymore," "I’m outdated."

It’s ridiculous. I hadn't even looked at the content.

Another one happened while I was tidying up. Yesterday, I was flipping through some old books I’d picked up at a flea market, and I suddenly thought of my mother. She used to flip through books just like that when she worked at the library, with such a focused air. In that moment, the anxiety hit—about death, about time, about whether I would just disappear one day, just like her. I noted this in the diary and labeled it as an "environmental trigger."

Looking at these two weeks of records now, I have a strange feeling. It’s as if I’m in a dialogue with another version of myself. One is screaming, and the other is observing the person screaming. Dr. Li said this is the beginning—when you can see the anxiety instead of being completely swallowed by it, you gain a little bit of space.

But I’m still not sure if this is actually working. Maybe it’s just recording the problems without the problems themselves changing. Still... at least now I know that when I receive an email, I need to open it first before I start worrying. It sounds simple, but for me, it feels like some kind of progress.

Or maybe I’m just kidding myself.

Whatever, let's leave it at that for now. I'll keep recording tomorrow.

June 19, 2022, 9:00 AM

When the email arrived, my heart didn’t pound frantically like it had the past three days. It was strange. I thought it would.

My fingers trembled a bit as I opened my inbox, but that was a habitual nervousness, not a desperate one. There’s a difference. I could feel it.

My brother’s email wasn’t long. He said: "Xiaoyun, I cried when I saw your email. I’ve been thinking about these things too. We’re both getting older, but we’re still alive, and that in itself is a miracle."

It was that simple.

But I read it three times. Then the tears fell—not the nameless, hopeless tears of the last three days. These were warm. They were a release. They were—I don’t know how to describe it—hope?

I replied immediately. No hesitation, no obsessing over the wording. I asked him when he’d have time for a video call. I even suggested specific times. This isn't like me. Usually, I would overthink it for ages, worry about bothering him, and ask myself, "Should I really be doing this?"

But this time, I just did it.

Dr. Li once said, "Perfection isn't the goal; authenticity is." I think maybe I’m finally starting to understand that. Not just understand it, but—live it?

The strangest thing is, I don’t need his answer to prove anything right now. I already know the answer. I’ve already found the courage to ask. That, in itself, is enough.

Maybe the most important moments in life aren't about getting answers at all. It’s about daring to ask the questions. It’s about breaking years of silence. It’s about admitting that I’ve been thinking of him all along—always.

I’m still scared. Scared that too much time has passed between us, scared that it will be awkward when we meet. But this fear feels different now. It’s no longer the kind that paralyzes me. It’s—proof of being alive?

I am living. I am taking a risk. I don’t want to die with regrets.

That sentence keeps looping in my head.

Now I’m waiting for his reply. But this time, the waiting isn't torture.

I’m going to make some coffee. The inbox can wait a while.
