Sunday, October 8, 2023 – Just past 11:00 PM

I just got back from Dr. Li’s. I’ve been sitting on the sofa for half an hour now, and I still haven't turned on the lights.

She said something that I’m still turning over in my mind: "Your anxiety doesn't seem to come from the palpitations themselves, but from your interpretation of them."

I’ve always been like this.

It was only when I said it out loud that I realized—this didn't just start today; this is what I’ve been doing my entire life. A racing heart, and I immediately think "heart disease." A friend doesn't reply to a message right away, and I start overanalyzing the tone of my last text, convinced they must be angry. I see a news story about some illness, and at 3:00 AM, I’m on my phone searching for symptoms, convincing myself I have them all.

I take small signals and amplify them into catastrophes.

It sounds so simple, but saying it out loud brought on a strange sensation—a mix of shame and a kind of relief. It was like finally seeing something clearly that had been haunting me in the dark; it wasn't as mysterious or uncontrollable as I had imagined. It has a name. It has a pattern.

Dr. Li asked me when this started. I couldn't say. Maybe middle school? Maybe earlier? All those magnified fears, the self-interrogations at 3:00 AM, the over-interpretation of every bodily signal—it all follows the same logic. I’m an expert at this, truly. I’m incredibly good at turning a possible threat into a certain disaster.

But that also means—I might be just as good at doing the opposite.

I didn't share that thought with Dr. Li because I haven't fully processed it yet. But sitting here in the dark, I’m wondering: if I can see the pattern, does that mean I can do something about it? Not eliminate the anxiety—I know that’s unrealistic—but... change how I interpret it?

It sounds too simple. And too hard.

I’ll think about it tomorrow. For now, I just want to sit here and feel this strange moment—the first time I’ve truly seen my own thought traps, rather than just listing symptoms. It feels both vulnerable and empowering.

It’s a very unfamiliar feeling.

From my time as an editor to my current work in cultural criticism, I’ve essentially been doing the same thing: trying to understand this era, what people are reading, and what they are thinking. My editor suggested I consider putting together a collection of essays to organize these thoughts into a book.

I’ve been writing for years—across different magazines and websites, from book reviews to cultural essays—but I never thought about gathering them all together. I always felt each piece was independent, like vintage finds from a flea market—each with its own story, but seemingly disconnected when placed side by side.

But perhaps the connection lies in this: I’ve been asking the same question all along. Cultural criticism isn't just about critiquing; it’s a way of deeply understanding the world.

So, it’s time to start organizing my notes. All those half-finished drafts and scattered thoughts lying around the room finally have a purpose.

I’m a bit tired, but I’m also looking forward to it.

Just now, while I was tidying up my bookshelves, I stumbled upon my college diary from 1982. We had just started university back then, remember? I had written all these manifestos in it—things like "changing the aesthetics of the publishing world" or "ensuring more thought-provoking works are seen." It had that sense of absolute certainty that only young people have.

Then, I looked through my notes from the past few months. And you know what? I’m still writing the exact same things. I’m still discussing publishing, culture, and what ideas are worth spreading. Only now, the tone has shifted to—"Am I still doing the same thing? What does this even amount to?"

I sat there, looking at these two notebooks, and suddenly felt a bit lost. It’s been forty years. My ideas haven't changed, but my confidence in them is entirely different. When I was young, I saw them as a mission; now, I see them as... I don't even know.

I’ve been wondering: when we say someone has "wasted their potential" or "failed to realize their ideals," what are we actually measuring? Is it the quantifiable stuff—how many books were published, how many awards were won? Or is it something else?

Because if it’s the former, then I have certainly failed. But what if... what if there’s another definition of success?

I’m not just trying to comfort myself. I just feel that this is a complex question, and I need to talk to someone who has truly thought about these things. Would you be willing to listen to my ramblings?

# Lecture Notes | May 10

**On the subway, 18:30**

Just left campus, and my brain is still buzzing. I need to get this down now, before it gets buried under the weight of daily trivia.

---

## That Opening Moment

I asked them: "Why do you think a person chooses to read?"

The classroom went silent for about three seconds. I could feel the awkwardness—they were waiting for me to provide the answer. I didn't. I just sat on the edge of the lectern and waited.

Then a girl raised her hand. She said, "Because they want to escape reality." Then a boy added, "Or they want to understand it." Someone else said, "To find resonance."

Once someone started, it was like a dam breaking. The discussion that followed was unstoppable. People were actually arguing—really arguing!—about the relationship between fiction and reality.

This is exactly what I wanted. But I didn't expect it to feel so... natural.

---

## That One Comment

As class ended, a boy with glasses walked up to me. He said, "Professor Lin, you weren't giving us answers today. You were inviting us to think."

I didn't know how to react at the moment. By the time I did, he was already gone.

I’m still thinking about that sentence now.

---

## Core Insight (Must remember this)

**Facilitation is more powerful than instruction.**

Not because it’s more "humble" or "democratic," but because genuine thinking must happen from the inside out. I can’t cram my understanding into their heads. If I do, all they get is my secondhand goods.

But if I ask the right questions, they’ll start digging for themselves. Those are their own discoveries. It’s a completely different thing.

---

## But... (Subway Reflections)

On the way home, another voice started popping up:

Am I just avoiding my responsibilities? As a lecturer, shouldn't I be presenting knowledge more systematically? Shouldn't there be a clearer framework instead of this... "facilitated" ambiguity?

That’s how traditional lectures work—the lecturer speaks, the students listen. There’s a clear logical chain; there’s authority. Was what I did today too loose?

Or... is this just my old mindset acting up? That anxiety of "I must prove I know enough"?

(I don't know. I need to figure this out.)

---

## Details to Use (For future writing)

- The shift from the opening silence to the enthusiastic participation.
- "You were inviting us to think"—this feedback is interesting and worth exploring further.
- The fiction vs. reality debate—the questions the students raised themselves had more vitality than any I had pre-planned.
- The power dynamics between facilitation vs. instruction.

---

## Current Mood

Tired. But not the "drained" kind of tired. It’s the kind of exhaustion that comes from actually using your brain.

A bit excited. A bit uneasy.

I'll keep thinking about it tomorrow.

Lin Hao, it’s been a long time since I’ve written to you like this.

Actually, I’ve been wanting to find a chance to have a real talk with you. Not the usual polite greetings during holidays, but truly letting you know what’s been on my mind and what I’ve been up to lately.

My collection of essays is about to be published. You might not fully realize what this means—it took me a long time to understand it myself. Over the years, I’ve written so many book reviews and commentaries, scattered across various magazines and websites. For a while, I felt they were just fragments, nothing of any particular value. But recently, while organizing these pieces, I suddenly saw things differently. These articles aren't proof of failure; they are my genuine thoughts and concerns from different periods of my life. They change, but that change itself is a testament to my growth.

You know, it reminds me of Dad. He was so dedicated to his scholarship, not because he had something to prove, but because he truly wanted to understand. And Mom, all those years in the library—she was helping people access knowledge. I think I finally understand a little bit of why they did what they did.

Lately, I’ve also been thinking a lot about time. Not in an anxious way, but... with a clearer sense of awareness, I suppose. We’re all going to get older; that’s just a fact. But because of that, I want even more to create something meaningful while I still can. It doesn’t have to be perfect, but it has to be real.

I’ve always regretted that we’ve stayed in touch so little over the years. I know it’s not all your fault; I’m responsible too. I was so obsessed with my independence that I sometimes came across as cold. But I’ve only recently realized that true freedom isn't about rejecting everything or hiding in your own world. Maybe freedom is knowing what you want and having the courage to pursue it—including admitting that you need your family.

I want to share this collection with you. Not just the work itself, but the whole process—the reflections, the changes, and the ideas I’ve agonized over alone late at night. I want to hear your thoughts, and I want to know what you’re doing and thinking about these days.

Maybe we could start talking more often? It doesn’t have to be about anything major, just... like siblings.

I’ll send you a sample copy of the collection once it’s ready.

Xiaoyun

**Location**: At my desk, home

My head has been a bit of a mess lately. I decided to dig out the last six months of bills and see what the numbers actually look like. Otherwise, this vague feeling of "not having enough money" will just keep weighing on me.

**September Bill Summary**:
- Rent: 2,800
- Utilities (Water, Electricity, Gas): 320
- Phone & Internet: 128
- Groceries & Food: 1,200 (I was more disciplined this month)
- Books & Flea Markets: 680 (Went to the flea market again... bought a collection of poems from the Republican era and two small folk-kiln plates)
- Medical Check-up: 450 (Ultrasound; still worrying about that small nodule)
- Miscellaneous (Coffee, cultural lecture tickets, etc.): 380

**Subtotal**: 5,958 RMB

August was about the same. July was a bit higher (over 6,200—I attended a few salons and had dinner gatherings with friends that month).

Looking back at the figures for June and May, my average monthly expenditure is between 5,800 and 6,200.

**The problem is**:
How has my freelance income been these past few months? Let me calculate... July's manuscript fees were 4,500, August was 5,200, and September was only 4,800. That averages out to just over 5,000. This means I’m actually dipping into my savings every month.

How much did I have saved at the end of last year? I need to find my bankbook... (searched for a while) Roughly 38,000. That’s everything I’ve managed to save over the years.

**If I go to see Lin Hao**:
- Airfare (Round trip): Approx. 4,000–5,000
- Visa: Around 500
- Living expenses for a two-week stay: 5,000–6,000
- Insurance, miscellaneous: 1,000

**Total needed**: 10,500–12,500

Based on my current savings, it’s doable. But the problem is...

If I spend this money, my remaining savings will drop to 25,000–28,000. At my current rate of loss (spending 6,000 while earning 5,000), that money will only last 4 or 5 months at most.

**I need to increase my income immediately, either before or after the trip.**

Or... I have to start cutting costs.

Looking at these numbers makes me feel a bit sick. Those books, those antiques... every piece has a story, and I can't bear to part with any of them. But right now, it feels like these "stories" are eating my future.

The most painful realization is this: if I don’t go see Lin Hao, this money will just be drained away by daily life anyway. In two years, I’ll find myself with nothing—I won't have seen my brother, and I won't have saved any money.

So... I have to go.

**The Plan**:
- Starting this month, spend a maximum of 1,000 on books and antiques (it used to be 1,500–2,000).
- Bring the food budget down to under 1,000.
- Coffee and lectures... let's put those on hold for now.
- Aim to take on 3–4 freelance assignments in both November and December, at least 800 per piece.

If I can pull this off, I should have the travel money saved by the end of December.

But honestly, I don't have much faith in my own self-discipline. I always plan things perfectly, and then some new idea distracts me over the weekend.

But this time is different. This isn't about buying something; it's about seeing someone. Someone I should have gone to see a long time ago.

September 23, 2024 – A little past 10:00 PM

Still can’t sleep. I just finished my shower and I’m sitting on the edge of the bed, my mind still racing.

The moment I said it, I was a bit stunned myself. It felt as if those words weren't something I’d thought up, but something that had bubbled up from somewhere much deeper.

Then the girl said, "That changes how I look at the present." The atmosphere of the entire room just went still for a moment.

I’m sitting here, turning my own words over and over in my mind. Is that really what I think? Or am I just lying to myself?

But no, I think it’s true.

I remember the year Mom passed away; I spent every day wondering "how much time do I have left" and "what should I be rushing to do." That anxiety followed me like a ghost. And now? I’m writing this book, hosting the book club, having discussions with Professor Chen, hunting for old books at the flea market... these things themselves make me feel alive. It’s not for some future achievement, it’s just... this, right now, is enough.

I kind of want to call my brother, but it’s too late now. And besides... we haven't had a real talk in so long. I don’t know if he’d understand this feeling.

I still have to revise the first draft of that book review tomorrow. The editor said they need feedback by next week.

But for now, I’ll just sit here and let this feeling linger for a while.

October 27, 2024  Evening

Sitting on the sofa, still holding that book of poetry I brought back from the bookstore. That sense of fulfillment from the subway hasn't quite faded; instead, it feels like something warm slowly spreading through my chest.

I haven't fully processed what happened at the salon today.

I don’t know why I suddenly started talking about my brother. Maybe the discussion topic just hit a nerve—something about loneliness and connection. I just started speaking, saying more than I usually do, and more bluntly. I said that loneliness can also be a choice, not necessarily something we passively endure. The moment the words left my mouth, I was a little surprised myself.

But what happened afterward was even more surprising.

After the discussion ended, a young man—a student, I think—came up to me. He told me that because of what I said, he was rethinking his relationship with his father. He said they hadn't had a real conversation in a long time. His eyes were a bit red.

I didn't know what to say at that moment. I just stood there, looking at him, and suddenly felt seen. Truly seen. Not in a judgmental way, but—understood. My words had actually affected him. My experience, my pain, could actually help someone else reflect on their own life.

It’s so strange.

For all these years, I’ve been writing reviews, analyzing other people’s books, and dissecting other people’s thoughts. But I’ve never felt my words truly touch something like they did today. Not in an academic sense, but something—vivid, human-to-human.

On the subway, I suddenly felt like crying. Not out of sadness, just... fullness. Like a cup being filled to the brim.

I thought about that phone call with my brother. He remembers me. He cares about me. We’ve been apart for so many years, with so much silence and distance in between, but none of that erased anything. If anything, it made the connection more precious. I used to think that loneliness was a form of freedom. But maybe I had it backward. Maybe true freedom is having the courage to connect, even if it might hurt.

Maybe true freedom is choosing to love.

I’m sitting here now, thinking about that boy, thinking about my brother, and thinking about how my parents are gone. Time really is limited. This doesn't scare me anymore. Instead, it makes me see very clearly—what I can do right now. I can go see my brother. I can keep organizing these salons, listening to other people's stories, and sharing my own.

I can stop using loneliness as an excuse.

But for now, I just want to sit here and savor this feeling of being full. I’ll figure the rest out tomorrow.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something with you. Professor Chen called out of the blue today to give me some feedback on my article. It wasn't anything major, but he said one thing—"We all need rest. Sometimes it takes more courage to stop than to keep running"—and I just burst into tears.

It was strange. I didn't even realize what was happening. The tears just came and wouldn't stop; I probably cried for about ten minutes. Then I made up an excuse about someone knocking at the door and hung up in a hurry. Thinking back on it now, it’s a bit embarrassing.

I’m wondering if this is that pattern you mentioned last time? I use logic to escape, but sometimes a single sentence can pierce right through that shell. I’ve been constantly analyzing why I’m anxious and why I can’t stop, as if mastering the theory would give me control over everything. But in that moment, I couldn't control anything.

What confuses me even more is why I felt the need to hide it. Why did I make up an excuse? I was talking to a friend, and what he said was so warm; I should have felt understood. Instead, I felt more afraid—afraid of being seen, afraid of him knowing that I’m actually not that strong.

Does this count as progress? Or am I just finding new ways to avoid the same old problems?

I’d like to discuss this during our next session.

I’ve been revisiting an old book I bought ages ago, and one line has been playing on a loop in my head: "Vulnerability is not weakness; it is honesty."

I used to think I had to polish myself until I was flawless before I could face the world—as if admitting I was scared, exhausted, or lost was synonymous with failure. I’ve since realized that this "perfection" is, in itself, the greatest lie.

Lately, I’ve been learning to let myself cry. To let myself say, "I’m not okay." To let myself be seen, even when I’m not at my best.

It’s actually been a release.

Sometimes I think we spend so many years hiding who we are, only to end up feeling more alone. True connection never happens because of how perfect we are; it happens because we dare to be real.

Goodnight. Back to my book tomorrow.

At tonight’s salon, a young man’s eyes welled up after hearing the story of my brother and me. He told me he felt like calling his father, who lives far away.

It suddenly hit me that perhaps we are all trapped in the same predicament—stuck in distance and time, unaware that connection is actually within arm's reach.

A friend asked me if all those years of loneliness were a waste. I said no. Loneliness taught me how to listen and what true companionship really means. Perhaps it’s precisely because of those years of silence that every word I share with my brother now feels so precious.

Not every connection requires being together every day. Sometimes, a single phone call or a sincere conversation is enough to prove that we never truly lost each other.

Time is limited, but that isn’t a reason for sadness; rather, it’s a reason to cherish what we have.

July 22, 2024, 9:00 PM

Sitting in my study, watching the city lights flicker on one by one outside the window. I’ve finished half a cup of cold tea, and my hands are still trembling slightly.

I don’t know where to start. Or rather, I know exactly where to start, but I don’t want to admit how much this has affected me.

My brother said, "This means you’re smart enough to accept support."

I’ve been chewing on those words over and over. For over twenty years, I told myself that independence was dignity. That accepting help was failure. I was even proud of it—look, I can live perfectly well on my own; I don’t need anyone. But hearing him say that today, I suddenly realized that maybe I’ve had it backwards all along.

He also said he wanted to "invest in my sister’s talent." Not out of pity. Not as charity. An investment.

I cried at that moment. I didn't expect to. I thought I was past the age of being so easily moved. But the tears just fell, and I couldn't even explain why. Maybe it was the feeling of being seen. Maybe it’s because for the first time in all these years, I heard someone say that my knowledge, my ideas, and my experiences have value. Not just "you work hard," but real value—the kind worth investing in.

I remember the year my mother passed away, how I sat alone in this room for a long time. I remember how, after my father left, I told myself I had to be even stronger. I remember how, after the divorce, I poured all my energy into work, as if being busy enough would keep the loneliness at bay.

And now? Now I have the book club, the lectures, Professor Chen’s publishing advice, my brother’s support... I should feel content. I do feel content. But at the same time, I feel exhausted. There are too many commitments. I wonder if I’m repeating the same pattern—using busyness to fill some kind of void.

But this time is different. This time, someone is truly looking at me.

I need to learn how to say "no." And I also need to learn how to say "okay, I accept." Both are hard.

There is so much to do tomorrow. But tonight, I’m just going to sit here and let myself feel what it’s like to be understood.

To let myself believe that maybe I don’t have to carry everything alone.

Maybe this isn't failure.

Maybe this is just growing up.

# Phone Memo

**The Anxious Visitor**

Insomnia again last night. At 3 a.m., sitting on the sofa, it hit me—anxiety is like an uninvited visitor. When it knocks, you can choose not to open the door, but it will keep knocking. Sometimes it’s a faint tap; sometimes it’s a violent pounding. The worst part is, you never know when it’s going to show up.

This metaphor seems to capture something. It’s not that it will disappear, but... maybe I can learn to coexist with it? Like dealing with a nuisance neighbor—you know he’s coming, but you get to decide how to respond.

Dr. Li’s words keep circling in my head: "This is treatable." I keep chewing on that sentence, like sipping a very bitter tea. Bitter, but perhaps nourishing.

**A Small Discovery**

Yesterday at the secondhand bookstore, I found a collection of poems from the 1980s. I flipped through a few pages, and one poem said: "The light in the darkness doesn't come from outside; it's that you've learned to open your eyes."

October 15, 2022, 3:47 AM

Can’t sleep again.

Professor Chen’s words keep circling in my head. "Your reviews are very influential in academic circles"—her eyes were sincere when she said it; I could tell. But what did I hear? I heard a white lie. Or more accurately, a eulogy for the past.

"Influential." Past tense.

When I asked her, "Are you sure? I feel like no one reads my work anymore," she probably thought I was being modest. I wasn't. I was stating a fact. A bloody, visceral fact that I live through every single day.

The editor’s reply from last week is still stuck in my throat. That phrase, "market response fell short of expectations"—I know what that means. It means nobody wants it. It means the things I write, the words I spend entire afternoons, nights, and sometimes whole weekends polishing, are unwanted. Like trash.

And now Professor Chen says she wants to publish a collection of my essays.

I should be happy, right? This is what I’ve dreamed of. But sitting across from her, all I could feel was a deep, nameless sense of powerlessness. I know that even if I say yes, I might not be able to finish it. I know I don’t have the energy to comb through those old articles, the passion to write a new preface, or the confidence to believe this publication would change anything.

So I said, "I’ll think about it."

That was the most honest thing I could say.

I’m thinking about 2007. The invitations. The stable, full-time jobs. I was so certain back then that I needed freedom. I felt that being tied to an office would kill my soul. And now? Now I have my freedom, but my soul hasn't come alive. Instead, it’s dying, bit by bit.

Freedom didn't give me fulfillment. It only gave me fear.

"Is my knowledge still valuable?" Dr. Li asked that question once. I never gave an answer. Because I know what the answer is.

If no one needs my commentary, then it has no value. That is the logic of this era. The things I spent forty years building became obsolete overnight. Those young bloggers—they write shallowly, quickly, and for "instant gratification"—but they have traffic. They have attention. They have a sense of presence.

And me? What do I have? I have a room cluttered with books and antiques. I have some reviews that are "influential" in academic circles. I have savings that are about to run out.

I have until June 2024.

I’ve done the math so many times. It’s the same number every time.

Maybe I don’t understand what this era needs at all. Maybe I was never as valuable as I imagined. Maybe my entire life’s choices have been a mistake—from choosing to be an editor, to choosing to freelance, to choosing to persist even now.

Maybe I should have given up a long time ago.

But I don’t know what to give up. I don’t know what else I could do.

All I know is that dawn is coming, and I’m still not asleep.

All I know is that Professor Chen’s invitation feels like a trap—a kind, sympathetic trap. it shows me an exit that I might never be able to reach.

All I know is that I am afraid.

And this fear is something I can no longer manage with logic.

# Video Call with Lin Hao | Rebuilding

**Date**: September 29, 2024, 8:00 PM  
**Tags**: #Milestone #Family #Conversation

---

Finally, it wasn't just awkward small talk.

I had a video call with Lin Hao last night, and he took the initiative to ask about my publishing plans. It wasn't a polite formality; he genuinely wanted to listen. I talked about my reflections over the years and why this book means so much to me. To my surprise, he said, "You’ve been working toward this all along. I’ve seen it. I’m proud of you."

Proud. When that word came out of his mouth, my eyes welled up a little.

We talked for over two hours. We went from the content of the book to our views on society today, and then to our thoughts on aging. He mentioned Dad, saying that if Dad could see my work now, he would be proud too. And Mom would be, as well.

For so many years, it felt like we were living in parallel universes. He was overseas, and I was here. Just the occasional text, a "How have you been?" But last night felt different. It felt like finding something we had lost.

He said that once the book is published, I must send him a copy.

I said I would.

---

*This wasn't just a phone call. It was the beginning of a restoration.*

Friday, November 25, 2022 | Sunny

Yesterday, after leaving Dr. Li’s office, I sat in the cafe downstairs for a long time. She told me we could switch to bi-weekly sessions now. My reaction was strange—I should have been happy, but I felt a sense of loss. It was only later that I realized these weekly sessions over the past seven months have become the most stable thing in my life.

Dr. Li said I’ve "learned to coexist with anxiety." I didn't quite understand that phrasing at first; I always felt anxiety should be eliminated, like curing a symptom. But as she explained it yesterday, I suddenly remembered something I wrote two years ago: "A perfect life doesn't exist; there is only making peace with an imperfect one." I wrote that while reviewing a movie back then, but reading it now feels like a conversation with my past self.

I’ve been measuring myself by market standards all this time. If my articles don't get views, I say I’m worthless; if my job is unstable, I say I’m a failure; if my anxiety symptoms haven't fully vanished, I say I’m not "better" yet. But all these standards come from the outside. Dr. Li asked me, "Do those reviews you write mean something to you?" I said they did. She said, "Then that’s enough."

That answer made me a little angry. It’s too simple. And yet, I couldn't find a reason to argue with it.

My sleep has been much better these past few days, and the chest tightness isn't as frequent. I’m even thinking about reorganizing the house. Not for any specific goal, just... because I want to. This thought in itself is strange, because the old me did everything with a purpose.

Old Man Zhang asked me the day before yesterday how the room renovation was going. I told him it was on hold. He smiled and said, "Take your time, you can't rush these things." We stood by the stairwell and chatted for a while; he told me it took him three years after retiring to figure out what he actually wanted. Three years. It’s only been seven months for me.

Maybe this is what true freedom is—not total financial independence, not the absence of anxiety, but the ability to live at your own pace. My limited savings, my unstable job, and this anxiety that will never entirely disappear. But all of this is the real me.

Next week, I’m going to the seminar organized by Professor Chen. I think I’ll go. Not for any "opportunity," just because I want to.

# June 16, 2022 Afternoon

I escaped again.

While we were discussing that topic at the book club, I felt like someone had suddenly flipped a switch inside me. That girl was sharing how she finally gathered the courage to text a cousin she hadn't spoken to in years, and the whole room started applauding for her. As I listened, my throat began to tighten.

I tried to cover it with a cough, but it didn't work. My eyes welled up anyway.

I said I wasn't feeling well and left early. Now I’m sitting at home, and my fingers are still shaking.

The moment the email notification chimed, my entire body froze. For a split second, I couldn't hear anything; there was only that sound echoing in my ears. I didn't check my phone immediately—I told myself to stay calm, but the truth was I hadn't been listening to a word anyone said. My attention was completely sucked away, as if tethered by an invisible rope.

I actually did it. I actually sent that email to Lin Hao.

Now I’m waiting. Every minute feels an eternity.

Dr. Li said, "Perfection is not the goal; authenticity is." Thinking of those words makes me want to cry. I lost control at the book club; I let that vulnerability show in front of everyone. It wasn't perfect. But maybe that’s what being authentic is.

I’m scared. Scared that he won’t reply. Scared that he’ll reply but only with something perfunctory. Scared that all these years of silence have completely severed the connection between us. At three in the morning, I would obsess over the wording of that email, imagining the look on his face when he reads it, imagining every possible worst-case scenario.

But I’m also proud of myself. It’s strange, because I don’t even know the outcome yet. But I’ve reached out. I’ve broken years of silence. Regardless of how he responds, I’ve done it.

I don’t want to die full of regrets.

Now, all I can do is wait. My fingers keep tapping on the table. I’ve checked my inbox five times already.

I’m terrified. But I’ve done it.

# Memo

I dug out my diary from 2007. Back then, I wrote: "I choose freedom—not as an escape, but because I believe I can live more authentically in another way."

Looking at those words now, it feels a bit ironic.

Freedom didn’t save me. It merely delayed the inevitable—aging, loneliness, the sense of uselessness. I thought that without anyone rushing me, without the shackles of an office, I could become a better version of myself. But in reality, I just swapped one form of anxiety for another. I went from fearing confinement to fearing being forgotten.

The question is: Do I actually have anything to say? Or have I just been using "freedom" and my identity as an "intellectual" to mask a mundane and lonely truth?

Perhaps the truth about freedom is this—you must bear its consequences alone. No one else is responsible for your choices.

I should tidy up the house tomorrow. Or maybe I won't. It’s not like anyone is coming over anyway.

February 23, 2022, Evening

My finger lingered on the screen for a long time. I didn’t delete the message.

"I am here."

Just those three words.

I don’t know what I’m waiting for. For him to say something more? Or for this promise to vanish, just like all the others?

I sent him that message this afternoon, saying things have been a bit difficult lately and that I’m seeking help. My hand was shaking when I hit send. I was even prepared to be ignored—he might be busy, he might not have seen it at all, or he might have seen it and just didn't know what to say. It’s been so long since we truly spoke. How long? Five years? Ten? I can’t even remember.

But he replied. And quickly.

"I am here."

My eyes are a bit blurred. I sat on the bed, reading those three words over and over, trying to grasp their meaning. He is here? Has he been here all along? Or does it mean that from now on, he will be here?

I thought back to when we were children. During the Cultural Revolution, we hid at home while our father was being denounced and our mother lived in fear. Back then, my brother would sit by my bed; he wouldn't say a word, he would just stay with me. Later, we grew up and moved on to our own lives. He went overseas, and I chose the life of a freelancer. We became like two parallel lines, occasionally talking on the phone about trivial things.

I don’t know when I started to become convinced that I was abandoned. Not by him—but by everyone. I chose loneliness, and then loneliness truly arrived. I accepted it as a fact, an unchangeable fate.

But maybe...

No, I don’t dare think "but maybe." I’m too used to disappointment.

I read it once more. "I am here."

Maybe he really is here. Maybe I don't need to understand exactly what it means; maybe I just need to believe it. But believing in something is dangerous. If I believe, and then I lose it again?

I placed my phone by my pillow. I’ll keep looking at this message. At least for tonight, I won’t delete it.

Maybe... I’ll reply to him tomorrow. I don’t know what to say, but I’ll try.

Maybe.

I lay in bed, the glare of my phone screen stinging my eyes. 5:15 AM. Still haven't slept.

I deleted and rewrote the message three times.

---

**Message Content:**

Dr. Li, I’m sorry to bother you so early. I know you’re probably not awake yet.

I just wanted to say... I still want to come to this afternoon’s appointment. I was hesitating before, but my sleep has gotten even worse these past two days. I spent all of last night thinking about meaningless things. My mom was like that too back then; I remember her saying that insomnia makes you strange, that you end up obsessing over tiny details over and over...

Never mind, I’m not trying to scare you or make it sound like I’m in a bad way. It’s probably just the stress lately—work stuff, and other things. I just wanted to confirm if I can still come at the scheduled time?

I know this is a hassle. You really don’t need to reply specifically; I just needed to confirm the time.

Actually, I’m a little scared. I don’t know what I’m supposed to say. But I think I have to go.

---

I stared at the message for five minutes, my finger hovering back and forth between "Delete" and "Send."

In the end, I didn't press send.

I turned off the screen, placed the phone on my chest, and went back to staring at the ceiling.

Outside the window, the sky was still dark. I could faintly hear old Mr. Zhang snoring downstairs.

My heart was still racing.

Hey, I’m at the airport.

My flight doesn’t take off for another two hours. I’m sitting in a cafe, watching the planes come and go outside the window, and I suddenly felt like sending you a message. Maybe it’s nerves, or maybe it’s anticipation—I can’t quite put my finger on it.

I’ve spent the last few days packing, sorting through things I want to show you. Old photos, a few articles I wrote recently, and a book I think you’d like. When Uncle Zhang was helping me move my stuff, he said something that stuck with me: "Changing direction isn't giving up; it’s truly beginning." I think that’s exactly what coming to see you means to me.

I know we haven't been in touch much over the years. Sometimes I think of you in the middle of the night—I remember us flipping through books together in Dad’s study when we were kids, and those conversations we had before you moved abroad. I’ve always felt a sense of regret, but I’ve also been waiting for a chance for us to finally sit down and really talk.

This trip is very important to me. Not just because I get to see you, but because I feel like I’m finally ready—ready to face the things I’ve been avoiding, and ready to reconnect with the people who matter most to me. I’m not as afraid anymore.

I want to hear your stories from all these years and see what your life is like now. I also want to tell you what I’ve been thinking, what I’ve been doing, and how I’ve made my way to where I am today, step by step.

Boarding is starting soon. I’m a little nervous, but mostly just excited.

Wait for me.

Xiaoyun

2:30 AM. Can’t sleep again.

I dug out my diary from fifteen years ago. It was written during my final days at the publishing house. The pages have yellowed. Underneath the sentence "I want to be a truly independent cultural critic, unbound by any institution," I had scribbled a line in red ink, pressing down hard: "But what about now? I have my freedom, but I’ve lost my sense of security."

The tip of the pen tore through the paper.

Sitting at my desk, I take out the calculator. I run the numbers again.

Savings divided by monthly expenses. It went from 60 months the year before last, to 48 months last year, and now it’s 36. I keep pressing the buttons, as if hitting them enough times could change the result. 36 months. Three years. If the commissions continue to dwindle, if no new collaborations come through... maybe it’s only 24 months. Two years.

I write these numbers down, then cross them out. Write them again. Cross them out again. My hand starts to shake.

Maybe I should have accepted those full-time job offers back in 2007. I still had a chance then. But now? Who would want a 62-year-old woman? Physical strength fading, reflexes slowing down, and stubborn as hell.

The editor said, "Market demands have changed." That sentence felt like a knife. Everything I spent forty years building—my understanding of literature, my taste in art, my observations of society—turned into something obsolete overnight. A young blogger posts a few random sentences and gets tens of thousands of likes, while my long-form critiques go unread.

Do I still have value?

I’ve asked myself this question a hundred times. Every time, there is no answer.

I cover my face with my hands. My fingertips are ice-cold.

Maybe I should never have believed in freedom. Maybe freedom was always a luxury meant for the young. I used it to escape, never imagining that one day it would become a trap—no security, no way out, only shrinking opportunities and growing fear.

Five years ago, I could still say, "I love this work." Now, I can only calculate how much longer I can hold on.

I have to get up when the sun rises. I have to pretend everything is fine. I have to wait for emails that might never come.

Am I really obsolete?

Or was I never worth being seen in the first place?

# Phone Memo

**May 31, 2023 10:47 AM**

I dug up a review of *To Live* from 1997. I wrote it.

Back then, I said—the suffering in Yu Hua’s writing isn't meant to fish for sympathy; it’s questioning how a person maintains dignity within the void. Reading that sentence now... I still feel the same way.

Why have I been denying all of this?

Just now, as I was organizing things, my fingers brushed over these old articles, and it suddenly hit me—these aren't proof of failure. These are records of how I think. They are evidence that I existed.

That follow-up piece on *To Live* I wrote after my father passed away in 2008. I remember staying up all night, sitting in the study, revising those few paragraphs over and over. Looking at them now, those traces of revision... that was me thinking. That was me living.

It wasn't a waste of time.

Yesterday, I called myself a failure because I haven't published a book, I don't have a stable job, I don't have... I don't have anything. It was all "don'ts." But what about these articles? What about these thoughts?

Maybe I’ve been measuring myself with the wrong yardstick.

Money. Titles. The things you can see.

But is a person’s value really limited to just those things?

I don’t know. But I know that I was once capable of conceiving these ideas. I could see things others couldn't. That ability... it hasn't disappeared. I haven't disappeared.

I’m just tired.

Maybe failure isn't failure. Maybe it’s just... not finished yet.

—

Should I compile these articles? Professor Chen once said...

Never mind. Don't think about that yet.

Just... get it down. I was once capable of thinking these things.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share what happened today with you before our next session.

This morning, I tried making a list—you know, that thing I always do, breaking problems down into bullet points as if that would help me make sense of them. It was about whether or not to tell my brother. I had three columns: reasons, risks, and possible reactions. I stopped halfway through. I deleted the entire document.

I realized what I was doing—I was using analysis as a way to escape. That thing you said last time has been stuck in my head.

But Dr. Li, I’m stuck on something else now. The word "diagnosis." I know you haven't given me a formal diagnosis, but even just in the process of our discussions, I’ve started to feel like I’m being labeled. "Anxiety disorder," "neurotic tendencies"—these words feel like a mark that changes how I see myself.

If I tell my brother, how will he look at me? Will he see me as someone who is "broken"? We’re already so distant; maybe this will just become another reason for that distance. Or maybe he’ll reach out out of pity, but that kind of pity would feel even worse, because it wouldn't be based on who I am, but on what’s wrong with me.

Am I overreacting? Does a diagnosis really change how people see me? Or is this all just in my head?

I know these questions might sound naive. But I’m just really confused right now.

February 26, 2022. Late at night.

I don’t know how to start writing this. My hands are shaking.

I went to see Dr. Li this afternoon. By the time I left the clinic, it was already dark. I stood on the street corner for a long time, watching people pass by, feeling like a ghost.

She said something. I’m still replaying it in my head.

"It’s not because you are too fragile; it’s because you are going through a major life transition."

I cried. Right there in her office, in front of a stranger. I didn’t even think I would cry.

For all these years, I’ve been at war with myself. Wondering if I was going crazy. Wondering if I was too sensitive, too dramatic, too... something. But she used a word—"transition." It was as if all my fears suddenly had a name. A reason. It wasn't my fault. It wasn't that I was too weak.

I said it out loud during the session. Things I had never put into clear words before. "I’m afraid there’s something wrong with my heart. I’m afraid I’m going to die. I’m afraid I’ve wasted my life." Saying it felt like confessing some terrible secret. But she didn’t dismiss me. She didn’t say, "You’re overthinking it." She just listened. Really listened.

And then I started doing the math.

My father died at 68. My mother at 71. I am 62 now.

62.

How much do I have left? Six years? Nine? What am I thinking? Why am I calculating this? But I can’t stop.

Dr. Li said this is called "catastrophizing." I’ve heard the word before, but I never thought it applied to me. Now I know. That is exactly what I’m doing.

She said it’s treatable. She said it so calmly, like she was talking about the weather. "This is treatable."

I want to believe her. I really do.

But it’s the middle of the night now, the room is silent, and I can hear my own heartbeat. I’m counting it. One, two... no, don’t do this. Don’t start again.

I’m going to see her again tomorrow. And next week. She said we need to do some "work." I don’t know what that means, but at least... at least there’s a plan?

Maybe this isn't permanent.

Maybe.

I keep repeating that word to myself. Maybe.

Maybe I’m not crazy. Maybe this is just... something. Something that can be named. Something that can be changed.

I don’t know.

But this afternoon, when she said those words, for the first time I felt understood. Truly understood. Not pitied, not comforted, but seen.

It’s a strange feeling.

It hurts.

But maybe... maybe it’s a good start?

I should sleep. But I know I won't. I’ll lie in bed, replaying her words until dawn.

Maybe things will be clearer tomorrow.

Maybe.

May 24, 2025, 9:00 PM

I sat on the floor and cried while going through those old photos. It wasn’t a cry of sadness, but more of a... how should I put it? A cry of finally seeing something clearly.

There’s a group photo from 1985. My brother and I are standing next to our father, his hand resting on my shoulder. We’re all smiling so brightly. Back then, he was five years older than me and he was my entire world—he’d teach me how to climb trees and tell me stories about what happened at school. I remember how desperately I wanted to grow up to be just like him.

As I kept flipping through, the distance in the photos slowly widened. In the photos from high school, we started facing different directions. After college, there were even fewer. In the one taken before I went abroad, we’re both smiling, but there’s a sense of estrangement in those smiles.

I asked myself—how did this happen? How did we go from being so close to where we are now, without a real conversation in years?

My mother’s letters were tucked between the photos. In a letter she wrote to me in 1998, she mentioned that my brother was working very hard overseas and hoped I would write to him more often. I can’t find my reply from back then. What was I so busy with? Work? Or had I already started feeling like I had nothing left to say to him?

I understand now. It wasn’t his fault, and it wasn’t mine. We just... each chose our own paths, and then got a little too used to walking them alone. I chose freedom, living alone, and a lifestyle where I could move entirely at my own pace. He chose distance. We were both protecting ourselves in our own ways.

But it wasn’t an escape. At least, not entirely.

I’ve decided to take these photos with me when I go to see him. Not to change anything, but to tell him—I understand now. I understand that the closeness never really disappeared; it was just buried too deep by our choices and by time. I want to look at those photos with him again, hear about what he’s been through all these years, and tell him what’s been on my mind.

This isn’t about making up for regrets. It’s more like... finally giving him and myself a chance to understand why things turned out this way.

Touching these photos, I feel a strange sense of peace. All these years, I’ve lived alone, worked alone, and thought alone. I used to think this was the freedom I wanted. Now I realize that freedom isn’t about escaping connections, but having the courage to choose the ones that truly matter.

My brother’s invitation changed something. Or rather, it finally made me stop and see things for what they are.

Two weeks to go. I need to prepare myself mentally. Not for the trip itself, but for this meeting. For the things we need to say out loud.

I feel alive right now. Truly alive.

Monday, May 8, 2023  Rain

When I walked out of Dr. Li’s office yesterday, I stood downstairs for a long time. The May weather was muggy, but I just stood there anyway, staring at the construction site across the street. She had asked me, "When did these thoughts begin?" and I realized I had never actually given that question a proper answer.

I always assumed my breakdown was a recent development. But it wasn’t.

Digging through those old diaries—the ones from the 1990s and early 2000s—I saw a different version of myself. What was that person writing about? Expectations for the future. "I want to be an influential editor," "I’m going to change the publishing industry." I was only in my thirties then; I still believed in those things.

Now I’m 62. I haven’t changed a thing. If anything, I’m the one who has been changed.

The plan to renovate the room was actually the trigger. Standing in front of that pile of clutter, it suddenly hit me—this is my life. Accumulation, chaos, unfinished business. I thought I was pursuing freedom, but I was really just running away. Running from failure, running from being seen, running from commitment.

And then those words just slipped out: "My entire life has been a mistake."

I startled myself when I said it. But that was how I felt in that moment. It wasn't melodrama; it was genuine despair.

Dr. Li didn't contradict me, nor did she offer comfort. She simply asked that question: "When did these thoughts begin?"

I started tracing back. Was it after Mom passed away in 2015? Not entirely. Was it after I left the publishing house in 2007? Not quite. Was it after the divorce? Or even earlier?

The truth is, I don’t know. But I’m starting to realize that maybe the problem isn't "whether my life is a mistake," but rather "what standards have I been using to judge myself?"

I see the eyes of those young people at the book club. They listen intently as I talk about *To Live*. One girl said, "Ms. Lin, your interpretation changed how I see this book." In that moment, I didn't think about "having no achievements" or "wasting my life." I just felt—seen.

But I’m still not sure. How long can something so fragile last? Can I stop doubting myself?

Old Man Zhang asked me about the room renovation again today. I told him I’m still thinking it over. I know I’m procrastinating. But maybe procrastination isn't necessarily a bad thing. Maybe I need time to figure out what it is I truly want.

It’s not freedom, and it’s not security.

Maybe it’s just—to be allowed to exist.

# MEMO

**Time: Afternoon, June 11, 2023**

I just got back from the book club, and my mind is still wandering.

Today we discussed a book about the meaning of life. A young girl—probably in her early twenties—asked me, "Professor Lin, what do you think is the meaning of life?" I’ve heard this question many times. In the past, I would have given a very "intellectual" answer—something about self-actualization or contributing to human civilization. But today, I suddenly said something that didn't quite sound like me: "Perhaps meaning isn't some ultimate answer, but rather the act of creating meaning in every single moment."

I was a bit surprised by my own words as I said them.

The girl nodded, looking genuinely moved. Later, a middle-aged man joined the discussion. He said he’d been working for twenty years and only recently realized he had no idea what he actually wanted. We talked for a long time—about choices, about regrets, and about how to keep living amidst imperfection.

As I was leaving the bookstore, I sent myself a memo: "My knowledge revealed a new kind of value in these conversations."

Sitting at home now, I’m still thinking about that sentence.

I’ve always felt that my value should come from the number of articles I publish, the influence of my critiques, or some form of official recognition. But today I saw that—in that afternoon, in that slightly crowded corner of the bookstore—my thoughts, my questions, and my observations of life were truly useful to those people. Not because I am particularly brilliant or professional, but because I was willing to think honestly and admit that I, too, am confused and uncertain.

Maybe this is why I keep organizing this book club, and why I’m still flipping through books and taking notes at three in the morning. It’s not to prove anything; it’s because this kind of authentic connection is meaningful in itself.

This realization brings me a strange sense of relief.

Tomorrow, I’ll organize the notes from today’s discussion. I might turn them into a short commentary. Not for publication, just... because I want to capture this feeling.

It stopped me from sleeping well, it stopped me from focusing on my books, it stopped me from... whatever, that’s just how I felt at the time.

Day 2: Anxiety didn't stop me from replying to Professor Chen’s email.
Day 3: Anxiety didn't stop me from going to the flea market and finding a vintage 1950s magazine.
Day 4: Anxiety didn't stop me from attending the bookstore salon.
Day 5: Anxiety didn't stop me from finishing that book review, even though the deadline had already passed.
Day 6: Anxiety didn't stop me from chatting with old Mr. Zhang downstairs; he said I looked like I was in "good spirits."
Day 7: Anxiety didn't stop me from... living.

Looking at these seven entries, my eyes suddenly welled up. It wasn't a breakdown, just... a bit of a sting.

# Thursday, June 22, 2023 – Sunny

Yesterday afternoon, I dug that Nikon camera out from a dusty corner of the bookshelf.

There was a thin layer of dust on the lens, so I wiped it off with the hem of my shirt. I wasn’t thinking about entering any exhibitions, nor was I trying to take "pretty" photos. I just... wanted to shoot. The thought was so simple it felt almost foreign.

When was the last time I picked up the camera like this? Last year, maybe? Or even longer ago? I remember back then I was always overthinking—is this professional enough? Is this angle too mundane? Why can’t I get the same results as everyone else? And then, I just put it down.

But today was different. I went downstairs and took a photo of an old man repairing a bicycle. His fingers were rough, yet his movements were meticulous. I didn’t tell him I was shooting; I just pressed the shutter. Then I photographed the windows of the old building across the street, where a pot of ivy was trailing down from a windowsill. I also captured my friends from the morning book club sitting in a circle discussing *To Live*—even though that one was taken on my phone, I love that moment. Everyone was so deep in thought.

When I looked back at the photos, I didn't judge them. I didn't think "this composition isn't good enough" or "the lighting could be more perfect." I just looked at them and felt a gentle, quiet sense of inner satisfaction.

It’s strange. Or maybe not strange, just something I haven't felt in a very long time.

I’m starting to think that maybe creativity isn't about producing some great masterpiece. Maybe it’s just... recording life. Reflecting on life. Expressing life. My life contains the old man’s rough fingers, the green of the ivy, and the expressions on my friends' faces as they discuss life and death. That’s enough. Those things are meaningful in themselves.

I don’t know why, but thinking this way made my shoulders relax a little.

Dr. Li sent me a message tonight asking how my week has been. I want to tell her about this. But I’m not sure how to put it into words yet. Maybe during our next session.

For now, I just want to write it down—I picked up my camera again. Not for any particular reason, just to see.

February 3, 2024, Evening

My hand trembled when I found my brother’s postcard.

It was an old postcard, probably from over a decade ago, that slipped out from a dusty, long-forgotten book. His handwriting was just as I remembered—square and firm. There were only a few lines: "I’ve been reading *Being and Nothingness* lately, and it reminded me of your comments. When shall we discuss it?"

Just those few simple words. But as I sat there, my eyes began to well up.

I realized that all these years, I’ve been running away. Not just from him, but from the kind of intimacy that demands commitment and a response. I gave myself grand excuses—that I needed freedom, that I didn't want to be tied down. But the truth? I was avoiding potential disappointment; I was avoiding the responsibility of continuously investing in a relationship. Like him, we both hid in our own separate worlds, using "distance" as a shield.

I decided to write him a long letter.

My fingers shook as I wrote. I told him about Dr. Li, about the anxiety I’ve been dealing with all these years, and about my fears of aging and death—things I had never shared with him. I wrote about my regret over our estrangement and about my new understanding of myself. Finally, I wrote: "I only realize now that I’ve been running away—not just from security, but from intimacy with you. I don’t know if it’s too late to start over, but I want to try."

After finishing, I sat in my room for a long time.

It was a strange feeling. It felt like both putting down a heavy burden and jumping into deep, unknown waters. Relief and trepidation were tangled together; I wasn't even sure how he would respond. Maybe he moved on long ago. Maybe this letter will only make things more awkward.

But I know that this time, I didn't run away.

This isn't just about trying to repair my relationship with him. I feel like I’m making peace with myself. For years, I’ve been justifying my choices—the divorce, the freelancing, the refusal to settle down. I told myself it was all for the sake of freedom. But in reality, I was paying a price for my avoidance, and I was never willing to admit it.

Now, I’m beginning to allow myself to see both the cost and the gain. It’s hard. But it’s much more honest than continuing to lie to myself.

The letter has been sent. Now, I wait.

I think this is what growth is. It’s not about becoming stronger or more certain; it’s about learning to stay open in the midst of uncertainty.

I came across a collection of 1980s art criticism at a second-hand bookstore yesterday. One sentence has been looping in my head ever since: "We spend our entire lives asking who we are, yet we rarely ask why we are asking the question in the first place."

At that moment, I thought: that hits hard.

Flipping through the yellowed pages, it suddenly dawned on me that some questions don't actually have answers. Or rather, the answer lies within the process of asking. It’s like the vintage items I collect—their value isn't in being seen, but in—well, in what, exactly?

Feeling a bit drained. Haven't been sleeping well lately. But maybe that’s not a bad thing. Sometimes, things become clearer when you're wide awake in the middle of the night.

June 11, 2025, 2:47 AM

I can’t sleep. What Uncle Zhang said keeps looping in my head.

"Sometimes we think we’ve failed, but we’ve actually just changed direction."

When he said it, he was holding that rag doll—a worn-out little bear with faded fabric. He said it belonged to his daughter when she was little and that he’d kept it all these years. After the renovation plan fell through, he thought of it.

I didn’t catch his meaning right away. It wasn't until he placed it in my hands and said, "You need this," that it suddenly clicked—he wasn't talking about the house; he was talking about me.

I cried. Truly. Sobbing in the arms of a retired worker downstairs—this would have been impossible six months ago. Back then, I was still drowning in guilt over the collapse of the renovation project, feeling like I’d failed yet again, defeated once more by economic reality. But now... now I suddenly see another possibility.

True freedom is having the courage to pay the price for what you want.

I used to think freedom meant being unburdened, being free from any restrictions. But that’s just escapism. Real freedom is this: I chose this life, even if it’s imperfect, even if it’s risky, even if I sometimes worry about money. I chose it, and I bear the cost of it—that, in itself, is freedom.

The renovation didn't happen, but I didn't die. I’m still here, still working, still attending the book club, still discussing philosophy with friends. I haven't failed; I’ve just changed direction. Just like Uncle Zhang said.

Giving me that doll was his way of saying—you are strong enough now. You don’t need a perfect home; you already have a real life.

I put the little bear on the bookshelf, right next to my father’s copy of *The Meaning of Life*.

I’m leaving to see my brother the day after tomorrow. I think I’m finally ready now. Not just in terms of packing, but mentally. I finally understand that the "security" I’ve been chasing actually comes from accepting my own choices. I’m 62 years old, and I can still take risks, change direction, and reconnect.

This isn't bravery. This is just living.

Living truthfully.

There are moments when you suddenly realize just how precious time is.

At a book club meeting today, a woman said, "Family is what matters most." It’s a simple sentiment, one I’ve heard countless times. But hearing it today, my eyes welled up.

I thought of my brother. We haven’t had a real conversation in years. The years have just slipped away, like sand through my fingers. I used to think there was plenty of time—that we could take it slow and mend things eventually. But our parents are gone now, and I’m 62. How many "take it slows" do I have left?

He recently invited me to visit him. At first, I refused—the passport, the flights, the money... I had plenty of excuses. But sitting there today, listening to others share their family stories, it suddenly hit me: perhaps true courage is taking a risk for the things that actually matter.

I’ve made up my mind. I’m going.

There are some people you simply cannot afford to miss out on again. There are some connections worth breaking your safe, familiar, and lonely habits for.

I’m going to start getting my passport ready tonight.

2:47 AM.

The post is out.

I’m sitting at my desk, the screen’s glow hitting my face, fingers still resting on the keyboard, unsure where to put them. That piece on *One Hundred Years of Solitude*—I revised it three times. The first draft was buried in academic jargon; it read like I was reciting a thesis. The second time, I added a lot of personal observations, but it felt too rambling. In the final version, I stripped away every "it is worth noting" and "from this perspective," leaving only the most honest sentence: "In reading, I found the meaning of solitude, and the possibility of connecting with a stranger."

My heart raced a little when I hit "publish."

There are three comments now. One of them says: "You said exactly what I’ve been wanting to say but couldn't find the words for."

My eyes are welling up. Truly. I’m sitting here, staring at the screen, and my eyes are just... wet. This feeling—of being understood, of being truly seen—I haven't felt it in a long time. Not during those years at the publishing house, and not during these years writing book reviews just for the paycheck. This time is different. This time is real.

But...

Why am I starting to count the comments again? Three. Only three. I’m wondering if that’s enough. Was the topic fresh enough? Will anyone even see it?

I hate this about myself.

I really do. Dr. Li once said that anxiety tries to protect us through quantification, making us feel like we’re still in control. I thought I had let go of this habit. I thought I finally understood that real value doesn't lie in the number of comments or shares. I thought I got it.

But clearly, I don’t.

Or rather... I understand it, but I’m still learning. It’s not a one-time epiphany; it’s a recurring lesson. I’ll let go of this habit over and over, and then pick it back up over and over. Maybe this is just who I am.

That comment is still there. "You said exactly what I’ve been wanting to say but couldn't find the words for."

I remember now—this is why I write. Not for the numbers. Not to prove anything. Just for this—to have a moment of genuine connection with a stranger in the middle of the night through words.

I can believe two things at once right now: these three comments are enough, and they are also not enough. Being understood is real, and the anxiety is real too.

I don’t have to choose one.

I can just sit here, feeling both satisfied and unsettled. My eyes can well up for that one sentence, even as my heart quickens over the comment count. This is me. This is who I am right now.

Imperfect, but real.

I’ll think about all this tomorrow. For now, I want to read that comment one more time.

Li Lan, I’ve been sitting here by myself for a long while.

That thing you said has been playing on a loop in my head: "Is his silence also a way of waiting?" I had never thought of it that way. For all these years, I just assumed he didn’t want anything to do with me, or that he’d simply gotten used to life without a sister. But you’re right—I’ve been making assumptions about what he’s thinking without ever actually asking him.

I just re-read his last email. "It’s good to stay in touch"—when I first read that, it felt so polite and distant. But now I’m wondering, maybe he was testing the waters too? Maybe he’s just as lost as I am when it comes to how we’re supposed to be with each other?

The video call is in a few hours. I’ll admit, I’m a bit nervous. But it’s different this time—it’s not that "he’s going to be disappointed" kind of nervous. It’s... I don’t know how to put it. It just feels like this really matters. It’s like I’ve finally realized that for all these years, I might have been angry at an imaginary version of my brother, rather than the real him.

Thank you. Truly. Sometimes we just need someone else to poke a hole in our assumptions.

I’m going to really listen to him. Not to judge, just... listen.

# Wednesday, November 8, 2023 – Overcast

Organizing my room again. This time, it wasn't driven by Uncle Zhang’s renovation plan, nor was it out of a sudden surge of guilt. It was just... sitting on the floor, looking at the pile of photography work in the corner, and suddenly wanting to dig through it all.

I came across that set of cityscapes from 2016. I remember taking them on Nanjing Road on a rainy evening. The neon lights in the photos are blurred, the raindrops are blurred—you can barely make anything out. At the time, I was so frustrated; I saw it as a failure. Not enough technique, poor composition, not enough of anything.

But looking at that photo today, I suddenly remembered how I felt that evening. What was I thinking? What was I waiting for? Why did I press the shutter at that exact moment?

Maybe... the photo itself represents my thoughts at that time. It wasn't a failure, but a record. A testament to "how I once saw the world."

Then there’s the 2017 portrait series. That’s the one I wanted to give up on the most. I stopped shooting after that because I felt I had no talent. But flipping through them, I found a profile of an old woman looking out a window. I had written a few lines for each photo—what I was thinking at the time, why I chose that angle, what that person made me think of.

As I was doing this, I realized something. I’ve always said my room is a mess, that my life is a mess, and that this chaos is "freedom." But actually... chaos isn't freedom. Chaos is an escape.

Real freedom, perhaps, is being able to see through the clutter and consciously decide where everything belongs. Not passively letting things pile up, but actively organizing—not to make the room look "tidy," but to let every item return to its rightful place.

I’m re-numbering these photos and labeling them. Not to make them "useful" or "valuable," but because... I want to remember. I don't want these moments to be lost in the chaos.

Maybe this is all I can do right now. Not completing some grand project, not becoming some great photographer, but simply this: seeing these fragments, naming them, and letting them exist in order.

My brother hasn't replied yet. I wonder what he’ll think of the things I wrote to him. Will he think I’m being melodramatic? Will he think I’m wallowing in self-pity?

But I sent it. That in itself is a change.

It’s 10:30 now. The sunlight is streaming through the window, hitting the photos. I’m going to keep organizing. No rush. There’s plenty of time.

# Diary

**June 20, 2022 Afternoon**

My hands are still shaking.

I’ve checked my inbox five times already. No new reply. I know he might still be busy, the time difference, or that he needs time to gather his thoughts—I understand all that, I really do. But my heart is still racing.

I’ve been waiting for this moment for so long that sometimes I felt it would never come.

Now that it’s here, I’m actually more afraid. It’s ridiculous, isn’t it? I spent all these years imagining reconnecting with him, and now that it’s actually happening, I’m thinking—what if he reads my letter and realizes I’m not the sister he remembers? What if he thinks I’m too sensitive, too dramatic, too… Lin Xiaoyun?

I deleted five versions before I finally hit send. Five. The first was too cold, the second too humble, the third tried to pretend everything was fine—which was just fake. The fourth… I don’t even remember what the fourth one was. The fifth was the one I finally sent, but even now I’m second-guessing the phrasing. "I’ve been waiting for this moment"—that’s too revealing. Will he think I’m accusing him?

We’ve wasted too much time.

That thought keeps circling in my head. I’m 62 this year. How old is he? I actually have to stop and think about it. That in itself says everything. We’ve missed so much—when Father passed away, when Mother passed away, all those moments I should have been able to share with him. What if he doesn't respond again? What if this is just another false start?

Breathe. I need to breathe.

My chest is tight. It’s a familiar feeling—that uncontrollable, trapped sensation. I can’t control whether he’ll reply again. I can’t control the time difference. I can’t control anything. I can only sit here, staring at this empty inbox, imagining every possibility.

Maybe he’s forgotten. Maybe he doesn’t care at all. Maybe for him, our estrangement became the norm long ago, and I’m the only one still aching over it.

No, that’s not fair. He replied. What does that mean? That he cares? That he’s been waiting too?

Or is it just politeness?

I hate this uncertainty. Hate it.

My fingers keep tapping the table—tap, tap, tap, tap—I didn't even realize I was doing it. Now it’s stopped. Now it’s started again.

What if he doesn’t respond? Then I’ve lost him forever. I’ve already lost my parents; I can’t lose him too. I can’t. But it’s not something I can control, is it? That’s the lesson I’ve been trying to learn—I can’t control anyone else, only my own reaction. But my reaction right now is… on the verge of a breakdown.

Maybe I’m not alone.

Maybe he’s waiting too. Maybe he’s checking his inbox. Maybe he’s afraid, too.

Maybe?

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

I need to stop checking my email. I need to do something. Read? No, I can’t focus. Photography? My hands are shaking. Maybe I should…

Maybe I should…

May 20, 2022, 11:45 PM

My hands were shaking when I pulled it out.

That photo. Summer of 1985. My brother had his arm around my shoulder; we were both laughing. My fingers kept tracing his face—his face in that photo. My fingertips rubbed against the cellophane over and over, as if by doing that, I could... could what? I don’t even know.

37 years. From 1985 until now. 37 years.

He left in 2000. I was 45 then. Now I’m 62. 22 years. 22 years without a real conversation. The occasional text. The occasional birthday greeting. That’s it.

I’ve been sitting on the floor, sitting here all night. My legs are numb.

Does he still remember me? We were so close back then. And now? What am I now? Am I just a distant memory to him? Or am I nothing at all?

How old was my father when he passed away? I looked it up. 65. I’m 62 now. I’m getting close to that number. Close to the number where my father stopped forever. I’m calculating. I can’t stop calculating. If I also... at 65... No. Don’t think like that.

But I can’t help it.

Once the thought gets in, it won’t leave. Time is devouring me. I can feel it. Every minute is being squeezed out of my body. I’m losing. I’m losing my brother, losing time, losing... losing what? Losing what I could have had?

Maybe I should write to him.

I’ve written it five times in my head. Deleted it five times. Every time, I get stuck at the same spot. What right do I have to suddenly break the silence after 22 years? What would he think of me? Would he think I’m just causing trouble again? Or would he think...

I don’t know.

I really don’t know.

My hands are still shaking. I put the album back, but I didn’t really put it back. It’s still right here beside me. I keep wanting to look at it one more time. That photo. His smile. How close we were.

Maybe it’s already too late.

Maybe... maybe not.

But if I do nothing, I’ll regret it. I’ll regret it forever. Until... until I end up like my father.

I need to sleep. I need to stop thinking like this. But I can’t.

I can’t.

November 6, 2023, Afternoon

My hands are still shaking.

My brother’s letter is sitting right in front of me. I’ve read it three times now. Every time, I stop at the same spot—"Perhaps these objects are not symbols of chaos, but the traces of your life."

I don’t know what to do. My eyes are stinging, which is strange. I thought I had grown used to his silence, used to that faint sense of regret. But for him to say this... He didn't criticize my mess; he didn't say "you should tidy up" or anything like that. Instead, he called these things my traces.

With a trembling hand, I wrote in my notebook: Maybe I’ve been misunderstanding myself all along.

Now I’m sitting here, asking myself over and over—what exactly have I misunderstood?

All these years, I’ve looked at my room, my life, and my unfinished projects as proof of failure. As evidence of my incompetence, my lack of discipline, my unworthiness. Every time someone visited, I felt ashamed of these piles of books, these half-finished photographs, these antiques scavenged from flea markets. I felt I should be more organized, more like a "successful person."

But my brother said... these are my traces.

I’m thinking that maybe chaos doesn’t equal failure. Maybe my understanding of myself has been wrong this whole time. Those unfinished books aren't a sign of laziness, but of my curiosity pointing in different directions at different moments. Those half-completed photography projects aren't failures, but genuine explorations I made at a certain point in time. Those antiques and second-hand books—each one has a story, each represents my love for something in that specific moment.

Maybe... there is value in the chaos itself?

I don’t know. I really don’t know. But something in my heart has loosened. It’s like something I’ve been clutching tightly has suddenly been allowed to let go.

My brother still cares about me. He cares. Realizing this makes me feel a little less alone.

I need to sit for a while. I need to let this thought slowly unfold in my mind. Maybe tomorrow I’ll feel differently. Maybe this is just a fleeting illusion. But for this moment, I am allowing myself to believe—maybe I don’t need to change my chaos; I just need to change how I understand it.

Maybe... that is enough.

# Memo

**Time: 8:45 PM**

Ten minutes. I hovered over the "send" button for ten minutes.

My finger was suspended above the screen. The email was already written—not long, just a few sentences. But in those ten minutes, every worst-case scenario flashed through my mind: He won’t respond. He’ll think I’m strange. He probably doesn’t even remember me. Or, worse, he’ll respond, but only out of politeness.

I even thought about deleting it and starting over. Or just not sending it at all.

But I pressed it anyway.

---

Dr. Li asked me, "What do you want?"

I didn't understand the question at first. What do I want? I want a perfect letter, I want to find the exact right words, I want to predict his reaction, I want to protect myself from rejection...

But that’s not what she meant. She was asking: stripping away all the tactics and defenses, what do I want most?

I want him to know I’m thinking of him.

It’s that simple.

---

After the email went out, I felt a strange sense of release. Not because I’m expecting a certain response, but because... I finally did it. I stopped waiting for the perfect moment. I stopped revising. I just said it.

Maybe that’s what matters most. Not his response, but the act of reaching out itself.

---

My phone is sitting next to me. I’m waiting, but not entirely waiting.

If he responds, I’ll be happy. If he doesn’t, I... I don’t know. But at least I tried. At least I’m no longer that person who just imagines things over and over late at night but never acts.

Dr. Li said, "Life is too short to be wasted in silence."

I understand that now.

---

Maybe I should do this more often. Maybe I should write to him more. Maybe I should stop pretending there’s nothing left to be said between us.

Maybe.

For now, I’m just sitting here, drinking cold tea, waiting to see what happens next.
