I don't know why, but hearing that actually made me feel worse.

The appointment is next Wednesday. Right now, I’m feeling a mix of regret and relief; it’s a strange sensation. It’s like I’ve finally admitted something, but I’m also trying to deny it at the same time. Do you know what I mean?

Lately, I’ve been thinking about how old my father and mother were when they passed away. I’ve been doing these calculations that I don’t want to admit I’m doing. Sometimes I wake up in the middle of the night and just start taking my blood pressure, checking it over and over, as if I’m confirming that I’m still alive. I know how ridiculous that sounds.

But I felt like I needed to tell someone. Not to get advice or to be "comforted." Just... to be known.

Could you keep this between us for now?

November 26, 2023, Night

At the book club this afternoon, I said something that I’m still turning over in my mind.

That young girl—early twenties, I can’t recall her name for the moment—said her life was a mess and she didn't know what to do. There was a sense of helplessness in her voice that felt all too familiar. I could tell she was blaming herself.

I actually found myself saying to her: "True freedom is choosing which order to accept."

The moment the words left my mouth, I stunned even myself. Did I really just say that?

But the second it was out there, I knew it was true. I’ve spent so many years running away from order, seeing it as a threat to my freedom. And the result? My life is certainly free, but it’s also a total mess. My work is free when there are no deadlines, but that also means no one needs me. My home is filled with everything I want, yet I can’t find a single thing.

Maybe… maybe all these years I’ve been looking for freedom in the wrong places.

She listened intently. Afterwards, she asked if she could see my "story corner"—that pile of old things, books, and photos leaning against the wall. I hesitated at first; it’s such a jumble… but I invited her over anyway.

As she looked through it all, I felt a strange sense of contentment. She was asking what this was and what that was, and I was telling stories. Stories about the book I scavenged from a second-hand shop, the memory behind a certain photo, why this antique cup meant something to me.

She said, "These are all so precious."

I had never thought of them that way. I’d always seen these things as proof of my inability to organize, as symbols of my failure. But in her eyes, they were stories. They were proof that I have lived.

I was needed. Not as someone with all the answers, but as someone with a story. It made me feel… I don’t know how to describe it. It was as if someone finally saw what I *had* been doing, rather than judging what I hadn't done well.

When she left, she said she’d come back next week. I heard myself say, "Great, I look forward to it."

And I really do.

The feeling is unfamiliar. But it’s real.

Maybe what I’ve been waiting for isn't perfect order, nor absolute freedom. Maybe I’ve just been waiting for moments like this—where someone is willing to listen to my story, and I am willing to share it.

I haven't tidied anything up yet. My life is still a mess. But this afternoon, that mess seemed to have a purpose.

October 18, 2024, 2:47 AM

Still can’t sleep. It’s been five hours since I left the consultation room, but Dr. Li’s questions are still circling in my head.

She asked me, "What do you think this phone call changed?"

I didn't answer her directly at the time. I just said, "I don't know. But it feels different." Now, lying in bed, it’s finally clicking—what changed wasn't the call itself, but my understanding of myself.

I’ve been using loneliness to protect myself. My voice trembled when I said that out loud. Because it’s true. All these years, I’ve mistaken living alone for freedom, rejection for a choice, and detachment for safety. The distance from my brother, the "push and pull" with my friends, the lackadaisical attitude toward work—none of it was because I actually wanted to be alone. It was because I was terrified of loss.

After my parents passed away, it’s like I mastered this trick: if I don't get close to anyone, no one can hurt me. If I keep my distance, I don't have to experience loss all over again. How clever. How safe.

But during the call with my brother today, I felt something different. He was there on the other side of the screen, asking what I’ve been up to with genuine curiosity. And I—I suddenly realized it’s been so long since I’ve spoken to him like that. Not because we had nothing to say, but because I had long ago decided to keep him at arm's length.

Dr. Li is right. I need to prove to myself that I am capable of doing things that scare me but are meaningful.

I’m still hesitant about going abroad. Where is my passport? I need to check. How much are the flights? How long can my savings last? These questions are practical, and they are terrifying. But what’s even more terrifying is the thought that if I don’t go, I’ll regret it. I’ll just keep hiding in this old apartment, numbing myself with a messy life and a half-hearted career.

Real freedom isn't about escaping. I understand that now. Real freedom is having the courage to take a risk, even when you don't know how it will turn out.

I want to see my brother. I want to see his kids. I want to tell him that I’ve missed him all these years, but I was just too afraid.

I’m going to call Professor Chen tomorrow morning. Maybe I should push forward with the essay collection first. If I’m really going abroad, I need some actual progress. I need to feel like I’m moving forward, not just running away.

It’s still dark outside. But I feel like something inside me has lit up.

It’s not hope. "Hope" is too heavy a word for me.

It’s—possibility, I guess.

I read, I write reviews, I go to salons—it looks like a full life, but in reality… there are gaps I’ve never dared to look at.

June 15, 2024, 2:47 AM

It’s been four hours since I got back from Dr. Li’s clinic, and I still can’t sleep.

She asked me why I’m taking on so much at once—the book club, the lectures, the column, and publishing the essay collection. At first, I tried to brush it off, saying it was because the opportunities were there, because people invited me. But she just looked at me with that gaze of hers—gentle, yet impossible to dodge. I finally said it: I’m 62 years old, and I feel like time is accelerating.

The moment I said it, I startled myself. It was as if the thought had been living inside me all along, but I had never voiced it so bluntly.

Then I added—if I don’t seize these opportunities now, I might never have them again. Dr. Li didn’t respond immediately; she just took some notes. I could sense her mind working. Finally, she said, "Perhaps we need to understand where this sense of urgency is coming from."

I know what she was getting at. She thinks I’m using overwork to escape something. But I don’t want to admit that.

Sitting here now, I wonder—do I truly believe these things are meaningful? Or am I just racing against time? The day of the book club, seeing those people listening so intently, I did feel a certain sense of fulfillment. The comments I received after the article was published also made me feel like I was doing something of value. But now, in the dead of night, it all feels a bit illusory.

Dr. Li gave me an assignment—to set aside 30 minutes every week to do nothing, just sit. At the time, I wanted to argue, to say I didn't have the time. But I kept quiet. I know she’s right, and I also know I’m going to resist it.

Why can’t a person seize opportunities without being driven by fear? Why do the two always seem so entangled?

When my mother passed away, I was only in my fifties. Back then, I thought I had all the time in the world. Now… now I’m starting to understand why, in those final years of her life, she suddenly became so urgent, so desperate to get things done.

I need to sleep. I have to prepare for next week’s lecture tomorrow.

But before I sleep, I want to admit one thing: I’m afraid. I’m afraid that time is running out. I’m afraid that if I stop, I’ll realize that none of this was actually worth it.

Or perhaps, I’m afraid to find out that it *was* worth it, but I wasted too much time before realizing it.

March 22, 2024 Afternoon

Spent the entire afternoon sorting through things. I didn’t feel that urgent need to tear everything down and start over like I used to. Instead, I just... took my time, flipping through things, looking at them slowly.

My mother’s book list is still there. A yellowed slip of paper, the handwriting slightly faded. It’s a list of recommendations she wrote back in the 1990s—*Wuthering Heights*, *Jane Eyre*, and the titles of a few philosophy books. Surprisingly, I didn’t feel any anxiety. Instead, I felt... warm? It’s as if she’s still accompanying me in this way. I put it in a separate envelope instead of throwing it away.

The thought startled me a little. The old me would have felt these old things were just taking up space, a kind of burden. Now, I just want to preserve it properly.

I also dug out some things from my days at the publishing house. Old editorial notes, letters from authors, and sample copies of a few books I worked on. Looking at them, I remembered those afternoons—discussing the best way to phrase a single sentence with colleagues, revising a cover design over and over... We were all so much younger then, with a devotion to words that bordered on the sacred. Looking back, it was actually very precious. Not that things aren't precious now, it’s just... a different kind of precious.

I cleared some space for my brother’s room. Just a corner of the bookshelf. If he ever comes back one day and wants to keep some things from home, at least there’s a place for them. I was surprised by the thought when it occurred to me. I’d never thought like that before—making room for someone else’s possibilities. But now... it feels like the right thing to do.

Dr. Li once said that freedom isn't about rejecting everything. I think I’m starting to understand that now. Accepting certain things, leaving space for certain people—it actually makes me feel more... free? Not the freedom of escaping, but the freedom of choosing. I choose to keep my mother’s book list, I choose to save a spot for my brother, and I choose to stop treating decluttering like a battle.

The light is fading. The room is piled with the things I’ve sorted out, but for once, it doesn't feel like a mess. I feel like these things are alive—each one carrying a certain moment, a certain person, a certain feeling.

Maybe this is what acceptance feels like.

November 7, 2023. Late at night.

My hand paused for a long time when I pulled out that diary from 1996.

I was 36 then, still working at the publishing house. The pages were filled with dense scribbles about my struggle with "freedom"—I wanted to leave that stable job, I wanted to do what I truly desired, but I was terrified of losing a monthly salary and the security that came with it. I asked myself in those entries: "If I quit, how long can I hold out? Can I really survive on my own?"

It is now 2023, and I am 62. I finally left that job and became a freelance editor. I have the "freedom" I once craved.

But in the blank space of tonight’s entry, I wrote this: My anxiety now is exactly the same as it was 27 years ago.

Only the name has changed.

Back then, I was afraid of not having a job. Now, I’m afraid the work isn't stable enough. Back then, I dreamed of calling all the shots. Now, I am suffocated by the weight of that very decision-making power. Back then, I felt that if I could just break free from the shackles of the office, I would find my "true self." Now, sitting in this old two-bedroom apartment, surrounded by piles of books and unfinished projects, I still can’t find that "true self."

During our session last week, Dr. Li asked me, "What do you think freedom means?" I didn't give a proper answer then. But now I know—my "freedom" is actually just another form of imprisonment. I used escape to flee, and used uncertainty to escape the fear brought by certainty.

Twenty-seven years. I’ve been running in place.

Not geographically, but psychologically. I’ve been swinging between "I need security" and "I need freedom" like a pendulum. Swinging for 27 years.

The most terrifying part is realizing that perhaps this isn't a problem that needs to be solved. Perhaps this is just who I am—someone who forever oscillates between two extremes, never truly satisfied.

But at least, I see it clearly now.

I am no longer making choices blindly. I am consciously repeating the same pattern. It doesn't sound like progress, but in a way... maybe it is.

I have a submission due for the magazine tomorrow. That book review of *To Live* isn't finished yet. The deadline is the day after tomorrow.

I should go to sleep. But I know I’ll lie in bed until three in the morning, thinking about these 27 years over and over again.

# Memo

**Perfection vs. Engagement**

I was flipping through *Aesthetic Modernity* tonight when it suddenly hit me—maybe I’ve been asking the wrong question all along.

It shouldn't be "Is this project finished?" but rather, "What did I feel during the process?"

I’m reminded of something my photography teacher said last year. I didn't really take it in at the time. He said that perfect photos are often dead. Living things are always flawed, asymmetrical, and unfinished. I argued with him back then, insisting that technique was everything. Only now do I understand what he meant.

**Engagement itself is the value.**

Those half-finished essays, the shelved photography projects, the books put down midway—they aren't failures. They are the traces of my thoughts at those specific moments. Like antiques, it’s the cracks and the wear that give them a story.

Why didn't I realize this before? Perhaps because I’ve been held hostage by the word "deliverables." Twenty-five years in publishing conditioned me to prioritize "completion," but that isn't the sum of life.

**Ideas to explore:**
- Perfection = Death? Engagement = Life?
- Unfinished projects are actually "open"—they are still breathing.
- Maybe I should stop feeling guilty about those half-baked works.
- Recording vs. Completing—what is the relationship between the two?

I’m thinking of my mother’s work at the library. She used to say that a book’s most vital moment isn't when it’s finished printing, but the moments it is opened, read, annotated, forgotten, and then rediscovered.

Perhaps my writing, my notes, and my thoughts should live like that too.

Not existing for the sake of perfection, but for the sake of being felt.

Professor Chen seemed serious. She said the articles have a conceptual continuity and a unique perspective. I listened, wanting to believe her, but at the same time, I couldn't help wondering—is she just trying to comfort an older woman? It’s a loathsome thought, but I can’t control it.

Then there’s the issue of "consistency." I told her that putting these pieces together would make me look inconsistent. The moment the words left my mouth, I regretted them. I knew exactly how she’d respond—that this is precisely what a real person looks like. But I truly don’t want to be seen as someone full of self-contradictions. Or rather, I’m afraid of being seen as someone without a stance.

Fifteen years. I’ve spent fifteen years writing these things. Sometimes I think they’re brilliant; other times, I think they’re just a pile of trash. To bind them together now and lay them out before the world—what does that mean? It means taking responsibility for these thoughts. It means admitting: this is me, and this is the best I can do.

I told Professor Chen, "Let me go home and think about it first." Is this just my procrastination kicking in? Or do I really need the time?

I don’t know. Right now, I just feel tired. I feel like I’m being too cautious.

Maybe Professor Chen is right. Maybe I should just try. But what if no one reads it? Or worse—what if someone reads it, and then nothing happens?

What am I afraid of? Being seen? Or being ignored?

The coffee cup is empty. I’m still here.

# Lecture Methodology Notes

**Date**: August 20, Evening
**Location**: Community Bookstore
**Attendance**: Approx. 30 (40% students, 30% professionals, 30% retirees)

## Summary of Effective Practices

### 1. Timing the Break from One-Way Communication
- Audience attention begins to drift around 15 minutes in; this is the optimal time for the first interaction point.
- Don't wait until a topic is finished to ask questions. Insert open-ended prompts like "What do you think?" mid-stream.
- Today, while discussing the adaptation of *To Live*, I spontaneously asked, "Does anyone feel the novel is more hopeless than the film?" Three people raised their hands immediately, and the atmosphere instantly livened up.

### 2. Selection Criteria for Personal Anecdotes
- Anecdotes must be **detailed**; avoid generalized statements.
- "I spent three years realizing the repetitive names in *One Hundred Years of Solitude* weren't a bug" is far more compelling than "I once misread a certain work."
- Students are drawn to "my mistakes as an editor," while professionals are more interested in the process of "how I changed my perspective."
- **Avoid**: Overly self-reflective cases that can come across as pretentious or sentimental.

### 3. Timing for Real-Time Adjustments
- If you see people frowning or looking at their phones, the delivery is too abstract. Switch to a concrete example immediately.
- If someone asks a proactive question, give them space to expand; this inspires others.
- Today, a student asked, "How do you tell if a review is sincere or just pretending to be deep?" Instead of answering directly, I threw the question back to the audience, which sparked the most heated discussion of the night.

### 4. How to Invite Interaction
- Don't ask "Any questions?" Instead, use "Did you notice..." or "There’s something interesting here."
- **Multiple-choice questions work better than open-ended ones** in the early stages ("Do you think this is a tragedy or a comedy?" is easier to answer than "What are your thoughts?").
- Gradually transition to more open discussions in the final 30 minutes.

## Participant Feedback Data

- **Quality of Questions**: High. Five in-depth questions were asked—not the "What do you think, teacher?" type, but genuine confusion starting with "When I read this part..."
- **Repeat Participants**: Approx. 12 (40%), indicating the book club has established a stable core group.
- **New Participants**: Approx. 18, seven of whom were brought by regulars.
- **Most Active Period**: During discussions of specific works (rather than theoretical segments).

## Elements to Replicate / Improve

✓ **To Replicate**:
- Opening with personal reading experiences rather than academic definitions worked well; continue this.
- Interspersing rhetorical questions like "Have you ever..." effectively maintains engagement.
- Reserving the last 15 minutes for participants to share their own thoughts—this is the most vital part.

✗ **To Improve**:
- Theoretical parts were rushed; some clearly couldn't keep up. Slow down next time or use more metaphors.
- No time was allocated for note-taking. Perhaps pause at key points to let people write.
- The ending lacked a summary and felt abrupt. Five minutes should be reserved for a brief recap.

## Follow-up Reflections

This "experience over discourse" approach seems genuinely more effective. It’s not because my experiences are particularly unique, but because they make the discussion **real and accessible**. The material Professor Chen suggested publishing should probably take this form—not an academic treatise, but something like "An Editor’s 30 Years of Reading Notes."

However, this means I need to be more intentional about accumulating and organizing these experiences. I can no longer afford to keep such scattered records.

Next week’s theme is modern poetry. I need to prepare 3-4 interesting entry points in advance rather than relying on improvisation.

February 25th, Morning

The person in the mirror looks like a stranger.

While I was showering just now, I stood in front of the mirror for a long time. I didn’t turn away immediately like I have these past few days, nor did that familiar surge of self-loathing wash over me. I just... watched. The dark circles under my eyes, the sagging skin on my face, the graying hair. Looking at it objectively, it felt like looking at a photograph of a stranger.

It’s strange.

I started humming a song in the shower. It’s a song from a long, long time ago; I’d forgotten I even remembered it. The water was hot, and the steam blurred the bathroom mirror. I just stood there humming—my voice was quiet, but it was definitely there. In that moment, I didn't think about death, or failure, or why my life turned out this way. I was just humming.

When I put on that old sweater, I lingered in front of the mirror again. The sweater is old, but soft. I looked at myself in the mirror—tired, aging, but... someone trying to stay alive.

When that thought suddenly surfaced, it startled me a little.

"Someone trying to stay alive"—why would I describe myself that way? Is this an admission of something? An admission that I wanted to give up before? Or an admission that I haven't given up now?

I don’t know.

I’m sitting on the sofa now with a cup of tea in my hand. The tea has gone cold, but I haven't drunk it. I’m wondering, is this a real change? Or is it just a temporary reprieve for today, and tomorrow I’ll sink back into that numbness? I’m afraid to believe in this shift. Afraid to hope. Because hope is usually followed by disappointment.

But I’m also afraid not to believe.

The person in the mirror who is "trying to stay alive" looks fragile. But at least she’s still there. At least this morning, she chose to shower, chose to change her clothes, and chose to hum a song.

These are such tiny things. But right now, they seem to have weight.

I don’t know what this means. Maybe tomorrow I’ll forget this feeling. Maybe it’s just a false hope. But right now, in this very moment, I am allowing myself to have this thought.

Allowing myself to still be alive.

Over tea yesterday, Professor Chen said something that I’ve been turning over in my mind all night: "That is exactly who I have always seen you to be."

We were talking about writing, about memory, and about the legacy we hope to leave behind. I told her I wanted to be remembered as a person of thought, of compassion, and of the courage to connect with others. As the words left my mouth, I surprised even myself—when did my thoughts on these matters become so clear?

In years past, I was always consumed by anxiety—anxiety about aging, about death, and about whether my value was still being seen. But lately, I’ve come to realize that the question was never "how much longer do I have to live," but rather "how do I want to live."

It’s been a subtle shift. Not a sudden epiphany, but something built piece by piece through specific moments: a conversation with a friend, a book, or even a photograph glimpsed in the middle of the night. Life has been telling me in various ways that true fulfillment comes from the choices you make, the connections you forge, and the things you love.

I’ve been thinking about my father a lot lately—his generation’s reverence for "scholarship" and their dedication to ideas. I think I finally understand what he was trying to pass down to me. It wasn't about achievement; it was a way of being.

When Professor Chen said, "That is exactly who I have always seen you to be," I felt truly seen. That feeling of being understood is more precious than any comfort.

What about you? Was there a moment when you suddenly realized what kind of life you wanted to lead?

I dug out a dusty old book today, its cover already yellowed with age. It reminded me of the years my mother spent working at the library. She used to say, "Every book is a person's soul."

I didn't really get it back then. But now, sitting in a room cluttered with belongings, it suddenly clicked—she wasn't talking about the books themselves, but about that sense of cherishing things.

Life is short, but it is precious. I want to live it to the fullest.

I don't mean doing anything earth-shattering; just... taking every choice seriously. Every conversation. Every place I want to go. No longer constantly wavering between "should" and "fear."

I’ve been decluttering lately, and I’ve realized I’m not that afraid of being alone anymore. Solitude was never the problem; the problem was using it as an escape. What I want now is to find authentic connections within that solitude—with myself, and with the people I truly care about.

Maybe this is what it means to be "alive."

[Image: A yellowed old book resting by the window, with winter sunlight spilling across the pages]

November 22, 2024, 3:47 AM

I can’t sleep. I’ve been lying in bed for four hours now.

I’ve read my brother’s email no less than ten times. "Your niece wants to see you"—just one sentence, yet it feels like a key turning in a lock. I sat at my desk, my vision blurring, fingers hovering over the keyboard for a long time, unsure how to respond. In the end, I just jotted down some fragmented thoughts—things I’ve wanted to say to them but never did.

I’m wondering what exactly I’m so afraid of.

I’ve asked myself this question many times, but I’ve never truly answered it. Tonight is different. Tonight, I think I see it clearly—it’s not the distance I fear, nor the sense of being a stranger; it’s the connection itself. Connection means vulnerability. It means I have to admit that the solitude I’ve chosen all these years has actually been a form of escape.

I’ve always mistaken independence for freedom. Mistaken rejection for choice. Mistaken distance for protection. But my brother’s simple words, "Your niece wants to see you," pierced right through that self-deception.

I wasn’t protecting myself. I was hiding.

Hiding from what? From the feeling of being needed. From the fear of potential loss. From admitting that I am, in fact, very lonely—not in some poetic, spiritual sense, but a real, mundane, suffocating kind of loneliness.

Mother has been gone for nine years. Father, sixteen. I live alone in this room, surrounded by piles of books and antiques, as if I’m using objects to fill a void. I attend salons, I discuss philosophy with strangers, but I never let anyone truly get close. I told myself this was freedom.

Now I understand: this isn't freedom. This is fear.

But… if I admit this, I have to change something. I’ll have to use my limited savings to buy a plane ticket. I’ll have to face a family of strangers, face a niece I’ve never met. I’ll have to take action to repair a relationship I’ve allowed to crumble.

At 3:30 AM, it suddenly hit me: those aren't the things I should be afraid of. What’s truly terrifying is the thought that one day, I will no longer have the chance to do any of it.

I am sixty-two years old. I don’t know how many more sixteen-year stretches I have left.

So, I will go. I will save the money, I will plan the trip, and I will find the courage to see the niece who wants to see me. This isn’t just a trip. This is a direct confrontation with years of regret. This is me telling myself: You deserve to be connected. You deserve to be needed. Your loneliness doesn’t have to be permanent.

It’s still dark outside. Dawn is still hours away. But I feel something shifting.

It’s not that the fear has vanished. It’s that I’m beginning to understand that some things are more important than being comfortable.

December 10, 2023, 11:30 PM

Still can't sleep. The teacups left by my friends are still in the living room; I haven't cleared them away.

I sat on the sofa and stared at them for a long while. Those cups are like proof that tonight actually happened. They really came. They really sat here in my messy room and listened to me tell those long-buried stories.

My hands shook a little when I took out the old photo of my mother—the black-and-white one of her in front of the library. I haven't talked about this with anyone in years. I said, "Every time I see it, I'm reminded of her love for books and culture," and my voice was steadier than I expected. Professor Chen listened intently and asked which library she had worked at. Such a simple question, yet it made me suddenly realize—my mother’s choices, her passion for books, they never disappeared. They’ve been living in my life all along. They live in my choice to be an editor, in the fact that I’m still writing, and in these stories I collect and tell.

I had never thought of it that way.

Then there was the old camera I scavenged from the flea market. I talked about how I bought it and my intermittent bursts of passion for photography. One friend said, "Your stories bring this camera to life." I didn't respond at the time, but I’ve been turning that phrase over in my mind ever since. *Brought to life.* It’s as if I’ve always felt guilty about my "unfinished" business—the photography classes I didn't stick with, the half-finished works, the projects I started and then abandoned. But tonight, as I told their stories, they were no longer failures. They were my genuine thoughts in those moments; they were the traces of my curiosity.

It’s strange. One person tells a story, another listens, and just like that, things become different.

I spent the whole evening watching my friends' faces. They weren't judging how messy my room was or how unstable my life is. They were listening. They were thinking. Someone even asked if I’d considered writing these stories down. "Into what?" I asked. They said, "Just like this. Exactly how you’re telling them."

I always thought my knowledge only had value when a publishing house needed it or a magazine commissioned a piece. But tonight, sitting in this room full of objects and stories, I felt a different kind of value. This value doesn't come from the market, or from deadlines, or from an editor’s prodding. It comes from—I don’t know how to put it—from being seen. From connection.

I’ve lived alone for so long. I thought loneliness was just loneliness. But maybe loneliness is just a room, waiting to be opened.

It’s after midnight now, and I still don’t want to sleep. I want to sit with this feeling a bit longer—this unprecedented sense of fulfillment. Not just because people visited, but because I finally allowed myself to believe that my life, my choices, my unfinished, messy, curiosity-filled life... it all matters.

Maybe change really is possible. Not changing who I am, but changing the way I see myself.

# Friends

Hi everyone, I wanted to share something with you all.

I’ve finally finished writing the introduction and personal essays for my collection. I polished the last draft last night, and I feel this indescribable sense of lightness. It’s not exactly that "finally over with" feeling, but more like… how should I put it? Like I’ve finally sorted through things that have been weighing on my mind for a long time.

The process has made me look at my old articles in a new light. I used to focus on their flaws—how the arguments weren't deep enough or the phrasing wasn't precise. But while writing the intro, I realized these pieces are actually honest records of my thoughts at different stages of my life. They aren't failures; they are traces of growth. It’s like reading a diary from your youth—you don’t dismiss the sincerity of that time just because your perspective has changed now.

The editor at the publishing house says the layout is in its final stages, and the cover design is pretty much set. We should see the finished product by mid-April. To be honest, I’m a bit nervous, but also really looking forward to it.

What I want to tell you most is that this whole process has changed my outlook on many things. I’m no longer so anxious about the trade-off between freedom and security. Sometimes, choosing to commit to something actually gives me more spiritual security. It sounds a bit paradoxical, but if you have time, I’d love to discuss this idea with you.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something that happened today.

# June 13, 2025, 10:45 PM

The flight takes off tomorrow afternoon.

I’m sitting at my desk, surrounded by suitcases, my passport, and those old books I just couldn’t bring myself to throw away. The room is quiet, save for the faint, muffled sound of the TV coming from Grandpa Zhang’s place downstairs. I should be sleeping, but I know I won’t be able to—not because I’m nervous, but because I have so much I want to say to myself.

This time last year, I would have spiraled into endless anxiety on a night like this. I would have double-checked my tickets over and over, obsessed over everything that could go wrong, and jolted awake at 3:00 AM feeling like I was losing my mind. Back then, Dr. Li told me that anxiety isn’t the enemy; it’s a signal from my body. It took me a long time to understand what that meant.

Right now, I feel a mix of excitement and tension, but I know I can handle these feelings.

So much has happened this year. Publishing the essay collection, organizing the reading club, discussing the future of cultural criticism with Professor Chen... and most importantly—finally going to see my brother. we’ve talked on the phone for hours, more than we have in the last decade. He said he’s looking forward to it, and so am I. I don’t know what the reunion will be like, but I’m not afraid anymore.

I’m reminded of what Dr. Li said: "Security doesn't come from control, but from trust—trusting that you can cope." I finally understand what that means now.

This trip isn't just about seeing my brother. In a way, it’s my way of telling myself, "I am ready." Ready to face aging, ready to face solitude, ready to keep writing, and ready to love and be loved. I’m 62 years old, but I can still go on an adventure. Realizing this brings me a strange sense of peace.

My friends at the reading club gave me a photo album filled with pictures of us together from this past year. I cried while flipping through it—not out of sadness, but from that feeling of being seen and connected. I used to be so terrified of loneliness; now I realize that loneliness was never the problem. The problem was that I didn't know how to invite people into my life.

Tomorrow, I’ll board that plane. I’ll look at the clouds outside the window and think of my father, my mother, and all the moments that made me who I am today. I’m making a promise to myself: no more filling the gaps in my life with anxiety. Instead, I’ll fill them with curiosity, with love, and with the things that make me feel alive.

I am ready.

✈️

I’m calculating a number. My father passed away at 68. My mother at 71. I’m 62 now. Can you see what I’m thinking?

I know it’s crazy. I know I shouldn’t think this way. But I can't stop. I wake up at three in the morning and start counting my heartbeats, just to make sure I’m still alive. It sounds ridiculous, doesn't it?

I want to talk to someone. But then I feel like I shouldn't bother you. You have your own life. And me... I’m just here flipping through old diaries, wallowing in self-pity over wasted time.

# Notes from My Call with My Brother

**Time: Tonight**
**Keywords: Independence / Loneliness / Support / Redefinition**

---

Just hung up with my brother. I’m still trying to process our conversation.

He asked about the progress of my publishing plans, and I didn't hold back—I was honest about the financial risks and my uncertainty about how much longer I can sustain this. In the past, I definitely would have stopped there or changed the subject. But not this time.

Then he said he could help if I needed it.

My gut reaction was to refuse. A total reflex. But I didn't. I said something about being "surprised and moved." It sounded strange coming out of my own mouth.

**The most important thing he said was: "Independence isn't loneliness."**

I’ve always believed that I needed to be entirely self-reliant. Ever since my divorce in 2005, that idea has been etched into me like an iron rule. Independence = freedom; independence = not being tied down; independence = just me.

But that sentence struck a chord.

Independence isn't loneliness.

— I can accept help while still maintaining control over my own life.
— I can have deep connections while still preserving my own space.
— I can lean on certain relationships without losing myself.

These things aren't contradictions. I’ve always treated them as "either-or" choices.

It reminds me of what Professor Chen said recently about exploring the theme of "Relationships and the Self" for the lecture series. I have a new angle now. It’s not "how to remain independent within a relationship," but rather "how independence and connection coexist."

This realization came a bit late. But perhaps it’s right on time.

---

**Thoughts to organize:**
- What is the true meaning of independence? (Not isolation, but autonomous choice)
- Why did it take me so many years to allow myself to accept help?
- How does this relate to my obsession with freedom?
- Can the lecture approach it from this angle?

I’ll organize these into proper notes later. I can’t let this material go to waste.

She said that anxiety and depression are diagnosable. Just like high blood pressure. It’s not a character flaw, not a moral failing, and it’s not because I’m too weak or too selfish. It’s a condition. It can be understood, and it can be treated.

I don’t know if I truly believe that yet, but I scheduled a follow-up for next week.

Sitting on the bus home, watching the night scenery pass by the window, it suddenly occurred to me—maybe all these years I’ve been using the idea of "freedom of choice" to mask my fear. Maybe the divorce, quitting my job, and refusing all commitments weren't brave decisions at all, but rather my way of running away. But thinking about it now, that realization isn't as terrifying as it used to be. If this really is something that can be diagnosed, then maybe... maybe I don’t have to blame myself forever.

The house is still a mess. Books are piled on the floor, unorganized. But today, I didn't feel guilty about it. I just sat on the sofa, drank a glass of warm water, and let myself slowly calm down.

I’ll deal with it tomorrow. Maybe tomorrow will be a little different.

I’m not sure. But I’m trying to believe in that "maybe."

# Memo: On the "Distance and Reconnection" Writing Project

**Date**: June 2, 2025, 10:30 PM

---

## Core Idea

After my call with Hao, I realized there is a story worth telling—not about the act of parting itself, but about how people pulled apart by time and geography suddenly decide, at a certain moment, to move toward each other again. This isn't a simple "family reconciliation" story. It’s about choice, about courage, and about the things we only come to understand in middle age or later.

## Potential Framework

**Part I: The Cost of Silence**
- Years of estrangement—not out of indifference, but inertia. Why do we allow distance to become the norm?
- How the family trauma of the Cultural Revolution generation persists in the relationship patterns of our own generation.
- The personal level: Specific moments when my connection with Hao began to thin.

**Part II: The Turning Point**
- Hao’s invitation—a seemingly simple gesture, yet it broke something open.
- My hesitation, anxiety, and final decision. Why was this time different?
- Redefining "worth": Is spending one's savings to see someone a luxury or a necessity?

**Part III: The Meaning of Reconnection**
- Not just a three-week visit, but a beginning.
- Distance is no longer a symbol of separation, but—what? (To be explored).
- Reflections on contemporary society: How we remain lonely in an era of hyper-connectivity.

## Potential Angles and Entry Points

- **Personalized Cultural Critique**: Using Hao’s and my story as a starting point to discuss the transformation of "family" in contemporary China.
- **Intergenerational Perspective**: How the imagination of relationships differs between our parents' generation of intellectuals and our own.
- **Reflections on Temporality**: How aging and an awareness of mortality have changed my understanding of the assumption that "there is still time."
- **A Female Perspective**: As a woman living alone, divorced, and childless, how does my vision of "family connection" differ from traditional narratives?

## Potential Platforms

- Literary magazines (such as *People* or *One-Way Street*).
- Cultural critique websites or WeChat public accounts.
- Perhaps eventually an essay for a collected volume?

## Inspiration

On the phone, Hao said: "I’ve been waiting for your story." That sentence struck me—he wasn't just waiting for me to visit; he was waiting for me to write this all down. Perhaps this is another way for us to reconnect.

## To-Do List

- [ ] Re-read theoretical works on family, loneliness, and mid-life crises.
- [ ] Organize notes from the calls with Hao; extract key dialogue and details.
- [ ] Reflect on how this piece differs from my previous cultural critiques.
- [ ] Discuss this idea with Professor Chen—he might have suggestions.

---

**Note**: This project feels different. In the past, when I wrote critiques, there was always a sense of distance, as if I were analyzing someone else's story. This time, I am writing my own story while trying to give it broader cultural significance. This tension is interesting. It is also terrifying.

# Monday, November 18, 2024 – Sunny

Something small happened at the second-hand bookstore today, but it felt like a sudden moment of clarity.

I dropped by Mr. Li’s shop this afternoon and did my usual rounds. I found a collection of art criticism from the 1990s—a very rare edition. My fingers lingered on the spine for a long time. That familiar surge of desire hit me—the impulse to own it, to take it home. But this time, I didn’t buy it.

I told Mr. Li, "I’m saving up for something important."

The moment the words left my mouth, I surprised even myself. It wasn't the sentence itself, but the... certainty in my voice. I have never proactively given up a book for anything. Over the years, my savings have slowly trickled away on books that were "maybe useful" or "too cheap to pass up."

Mr. Li glanced at me and simply said, "Then it’s worth it."

Just four simple words. But I felt something—a sense of being understood. He didn't ask what I was planning to do, nor did he try to persuade me otherwise. He just said, "It’s worth it." It was as if he knew that for someone like me to break the habit of buying books, it had to be for something truly significant.

Walking out of the bookstore, I started wondering: when did I start treating these books as a symbol of security? They pile up at home, most of them never opened again. I thought that was freedom—buying whatever I wanted, whenever I wanted, without restraint. But I see now that it was actually a different kind of confinement. I was bound by habit, bound by an obsession with "possession."

True freedom, perhaps, is being able to look at a great book and consciously choose to let it go—not out of self-denial, but because I know there is something else I want more.

I want to go see my brother. I want to go to his city, stay for a while, and see what his life is like. There has been so much silence between us over the years, and I don’t want to wait anymore. Maybe that is what I want to "own" most right now—not a book, but a period of real, present companionship.

So, I’m saving up. For this.

The funny thing is, once I reordered my priorities, I felt a strange sense of relief. It’s as if I’ve finally stopped wavering between endless possibilities and made a real choice that belongs to me.

I think I’ll remember Mr. Li’s "It’s worth it" for a long time.

[Image: Spring sunlight streaming through the window, resting on an open old book with slightly yellowed pages]

Sometimes I think silence is like a window that’s been closed for a long time. You think you’ve grown used to the darkness, but in reality, you’ve just forgotten what light looks like.

I spent a long time on the phone with my brother last night. I told him about the process of organizing my articles and writing introductions over these past few years—how I’ve slowly come to realize that the things I once viewed as failures were actually just traces of growth. He listened intently and asked so many questions. Finally, he said, "You’ve been doing meaningful work all along; you just couldn't see it clearly yourself."

We’ve made plans to meet this summer. It’s been a long time since I’ve had such a concrete sense of anticipation.

Being seen is more healing than I ever imagined.

Not all changes have to be grand or dramatic. Sometimes, just a phone call or a long-overdue word of recognition is enough to make you look at your entire life in a new light.

No writing done. The editor sent another follow-up message; I saw it but didn't reply. How can I write anything when I have to confirm I’m still alive every half hour?

Stood in front of the mirror for too long. While poking my cheek, a thought suddenly hit me—what if one day I can’t poke it anymore? What if one day the mirror doesn't react?

...

Don't think about that. Keep recording. Recording brings me peace of mind. At least while I am writing this, I know I am still here.

Sitting by a pile of books, I dug out a photo from 2015. I had just bought my camera back then, and everything felt so fresh and exciting. It’s a shot of some random corner of the city, the light is perfect—I remember it took me half an hour just to find that specific vantage point.

Now, that camera has been gathering dust in the cabinet for nearly a year.

My friends always say I’m a person with "ideas," but there’s such a wide chasm between having an idea and actually taking action. I have a mountain of half-finished projects—a photo collection that’s never been organized, a series of essays on books and the era that’s still stuck in back-and-forth edits, and a photography class workbook that I can never seem to finish.

Sometimes I wonder, if I were to suddenly disappear one day, what would happen to all the things in this room? The antiques I painstakingly collected, the books filled with my scribbled notes, the photos I took. Would anyone care about the stories behind them?

Or perhaps, these stories themselves aren't worth remembering.

Maybe true freedom isn't about escaping something, but about being able to finish something.

Goodnight.

Sent to Professor Chen

---

Professor Chen, a question came to mind that I’ve been struggling with, and I was wondering if I could discuss it with you.

Lately, I’ve been thinking about silence. We often say "silence is golden," but I’m starting to feel that some silences are actually a form of escape. It’s not about self-protection, but rather protecting a false version of oneself—one that doesn’t make mistakes, doesn’t get rejected, and doesn’t appear vulnerable.

I was talking to Dr. Li the other day (the therapist I’ve been seeing lately), and she asked me a very simple question: "What do you want?" I realized I’ve never truly answered that. I’m always thinking about "what I should say" or "what others will think," but I never ask myself "what do I actually want to say?"

This made me think about the things I’ve been putting off—things involving certain important people that I’ve delayed out of fear. You know, sometimes I think that if I don’t do them now, I’ll truly regret it. Not just a common, everyday regret, but a deep one that goes to the core of life itself.

I only recently realized that perhaps "living" isn't just about existing; it’s about expressing, connecting, and taking risks. Even if the other person might not respond, even if the outcome isn't as perfect as I imagined... I think I’ll still be okay. I’ll still be alive. I’ll still do what I want to do.

It sounds like the cliché "seize the day," but it feels different. It’s not an impulse; it’s a kind of clarity.

Have you ever had a moment like that? Where you suddenly realize that something you’ve been waiting for has actually been waiting for you to take the first step?

Sorry for rambling. My head has been so full of these thoughts lately, and I wanted to share them with someone like-minded.

# September 2, 2022, 6:45 AM

Can’t sleep. Another night gone.

Yesterday, I dug out that article from 2010—the one about the dissolution of postmodern narrative in contemporary literature. I remember how excited I was when I wrote it. I was still at the publishing house back then; I had solid blocks of time to think, to research. I scribbled a question in the margin: *Does this piece still have value in the context of 2022?*

After writing that sentence, I stared at it for a long time. No answer.

I opened that spreadsheet. Income records. 2020 monthly average: 8,000; 2021: 6,500; 2022... 4,200. I drew an arrow with a pen, pointing downward. It’s blunt. It feels like I’m falling. It’s not just the numbers dropping; I can feel it—my value as an "intellectual worker" is eroding. That editor said, "Market demands have changed." I’ve been thinking about what that means. Does it mean no one wants my in-depth analysis anymore? Or does it mean no one cares what I think at all?

Young bloggers get a hundred thousand likes just by posting a random short video. I spend a week writing a review, and when I post it, it’s like throwing a stone into the deep ocean. Silence.

Maybe I really am obsolete.

This thought isn't new, but it’s exceptionally clear today. It’s as if someone turned on a light, illuminating the very thing I’ve been running from.

I’m thinking about that decision back in 2007. I had several full-time offers then. I turned them down because I was afraid of being tied down. I wanted freedom. What kind of freedom was I looking for? Looking at it now, it’s the freedom to be trapped right here. No security, no stability, just a dwindling number reminding me that time is slipping away.

What if I had accepted back then? Would things be different now?

No, I can’t think like that. Thinking like that will drive me crazy.

But I’m already halfway there.

 the insomnia is getting worse. I’ve probably only had two or three full nights of sleep this week. During the day, my brain feels like it’s stuffed with cotton. Dr. Li told me last time that I need to relax, but how? What am I supposed to relax? Relax my anxiety about the future? Relax my calculations of my savings?

How much longer can I hold on? If this trend continues...

I’m 62. Who is going to hire a 62-year-old woman? Even if I wanted to go back to full-time work, no one would have me. I look too tired. I *feel* too tired.

What value do I have left?

That question has been spinning in my head all night. No answer.

Dawn is coming. It’s still dark outside the window, but I can feel the light approaching. I hate this moment—the feeling of being unable to sleep yet being forced to face a new day.

Maybe I should have some tea. Or read that 2010 article again. Maybe I can find something in there...

Forget it. I know I won't.

Professor Chen, are you still awake?

I know the timing is strange. I didn't want to bother you, but my mind is a mess right now and I just can't sleep.

I’ve been going through some old photo albums. I dug them out alone in my room at three in the morning. Do you know that feeling? When a memory hits you out of nowhere and suddenly you can’t let go of anything.

I found a photo from 1985. It’s of me and my brother. We were both so young. He has his arm around my shoulder, and we’re both laughing. That was before he moved abroad.

We haven't truly spoken in 22 years now. Twenty-two years. I don’t even know how I calculated it, it’s just… when I realized that number, I felt like I couldn't breathe.

I want to write him a letter. Or maybe an email? I don’t know. I’ve already deleted five different openings. I get stuck at the same point every time—what right do I have to suddenly break this silence after all this time? What will he think of me? Will he think I’m being strange? Or… will he just not care at all?

I know this sounds ridiculous. We used to be so close. Now I don’t even know what he’s thinking or what his life is like. We’re like two strangers.

I’m not asking you what I should do. I just… I need to know if I’m going crazy. This feeling, this sudden panic, this sense that it’s all too late.

I’m sorry for sending all this so late. I just didn’t know who else to talk to.

Zhou Fang, I’ve been sitting here for a long time before finally finding the courage to send this message.

I can’t stop thinking about what you said—"It’s not your fault; it’s a diagnosable and treatable mental health condition." I don’t know why, but hearing those words made me want to cry. Maybe it’s because for all these years, I’ve been telling myself that I brought this all on myself. My choices, my personality, my failures. No one has ever told me it wasn't my fault.

I emailed Dr. Li and scheduled a consultation for next Monday. But to be honest, I’m terrified right now. I’m scared that after she meets me, she’ll think... that I’m just a self-pitying, melodramatic person wasting my life. Do you know what I mean? I’m afraid that even a professional will look at me and come to the same conclusion.

But you believe I can be helped, right? I need you to tell me one more time. Because right now, I really don't believe in myself.

Thank you for not hanging up on me today. Truly.

# MEMO

**Date: May 20, Afternoon**

---

I said it.

At the book club, when someone brought up their recent anxiety, I didn't change the subject like I usually do. I just said it—about the diagnosis, about the struggle of these past two months, and about those 3:00 AM terrors.

They didn't flinch. They didn't look at me with that brand of pity. Professor Chen said, "That takes courage," and a young girl mentioned her mother is going through something similar. It turns out Mr. Zhang’s daughter is seeing a therapist, too.

The words I heard most weren't "You have to stay strong" or "Don't overthink it," but rather, "I understand."

—

The key is this sentence; I need to remember it: **The diagnosis is not the end, but the beginning.**

It’s not the end-point of "I am sick," but the starting point of "I am beginning to understand myself."

Dr. Li said it before, but I didn't fully take it in at the time. Today at the book club, however, when I reframed my story this way, I heard my own voice change. I was no longer defending myself; I was explaining a fact—just like explaining the theme of a book.

—

This isn't the end.

Maybe this is just a sentence I need to hold onto for the next time I doubt myself.

Maybe next week I’ll fall back into the cycle of thinking, "Maybe the diagnosis is wrong." But at least for today, I know someone heard me. Someone understood.

I’m not hiding some ugly secret.

I’m just learning to speak up.

# Lin Xiaoyun’s Private Journal (November 18, 2023)

I couldn't sleep again last night. At 3:00 AM, I found myself wondering—why do I always start getting anxious even before I receive an editor’s email?

I talked about this with Dr. Li this week. She asked me, "Do you think the editor will reject you?" I said I didn't know. Then she asked, "Then why do you assume they will?"

I didn't answer right away. It was only later that I realized I’ve been doing something quite interesting these past few years—I’ve been using "pre-emptive failure" as a way to protect myself. Before every submission, I’ve already rehearsed the rejection in my head. That way, when the actual rejection comes, it won't hurt as much.

But the problem is, this rehearsal has turned into a habitual anxiety. I’m not waiting for a result; I’m constantly manufacturing one.

I recorded all of this week’s "assumptions" in my diary. On Monday, I sent a book review to *Culture Weekly*. The moment I hit send, I thought, *They’ll probably think my perspective is too dated.* On Wednesday, when I received an email from another editor, it took me half an hour to work up the courage to open it because I had already drafted the rejection letter in my mind.

And the outcome? The editor from *Culture Weekly* replied saying they loved the topic and asked if I could expand on the argument. The email I was so afraid to open was actually asking if I could submit two pieces next month.

So, I’m at war with myself. I’m using anxiety to "prove" things that don't even exist.

Dr. Li calls this "cognitive distortion." I told her the term sounds too clinical. She smiled and asked, "What would you prefer to call it?"

I thought about it and said, maybe it’s just—**observing how I turn my imagination into reality.**

It’s a strange realization. On one hand, I finally see what I’m doing. On the other hand, now that I see it, the anxiety hasn't disappeared; it’s just shifted. Now I’m anxious about "whether I can stop being anxious."

But at least I’m no longer completely submerged in it. Now I can observe it like an interesting phenomenon. It feels a bit like reading a novel about myself.

This morning, I decided to try a new approach. When an email arrives, I don't open it immediately. Instead, I ask myself: *What are you assuming right now? What story is your brain weaving for you?*

And then I open it.

The result? Nothing special, really. But my heart wasn't racing as fast.

Maybe this is the beginning. Not a cure, but—a little bit of distance.

Subject: Some thoughts on the lecture — and a rethink of your suggestion

Dear Professor Chen,

It’s been three days since the lecture ended, but our conversations have been playing on a loop in my mind. I felt the need to put my thoughts into writing and would love to hear your perspective.

To be honest, when you mentioned that my knowledge and experience have market value, I was skeptical. Having worked as a freelance editor all these years, I’ve always felt that my work might be too personal or unsystematic—that perhaps no one truly needed it. But during the lecture, a young female student said something that brought tears to my eyes. She said, "I never realized reading could be like this—not for gathering information, but for thinking and connecting." I realized then that perhaps I have been underestimating the very thing I am best at.

This has led me to reconsider your suggestion regarding publishing. It no longer feels like a distant, impractical idea. However, I must be candid—I am currently feeling a bit overwhelmed by multiple commitments. Between the book club, lectures, and article deadlines... I am relearning how to say "no."

I was wondering if we could find a time to have a deeper discussion. Not just about the publishing project, but also about how to bring more structure to my work while maintaining creative authenticity. Your insights on these matters mean a great deal to me.

If you are available, you are more than welcome to attend next month's lecture. I would value hearing your perspective as a scholar.

Thank you for your encouragement the other day. Sometimes, simply being seen is a source of strength in itself.

Best regards,

Lin Xiaoyun

Professor Chen, do you have a moment? I was just going through some of my old photography archives and an idea struck me—I’d love to get your take on it.

I realized that I’ve always been dismissive of my "unfinished" works—those cityscapes and portraits I shot between 2015 and 2017. I used to think the technique was amateur, the composition was off, and the post-processing was poorly handled. But looking at them again today, I realized they actually document a process—the way I was thinking through photography and growing in the way I see the world.

It made me wonder: is our definition of "completeness" too narrow? Perhaps the value of a work doesn't necessarily lie in its level of finish, but in the thought process and the way of seeing it presents. It’s like how an artist’s sketches can sometimes be more intriguing than the final masterpiece.

I’m even thinking about pairing these photographs with my cultural critiques. For instance, an essay on urban aesthetics could be paired with photos taken during that same period. This would create a dialogue—a synthesis of both textual and visual reflection. In this context, "incompleteness" itself becomes a form of honest expression.

Do you think this idea could add something to the book? Or at least serve as an interesting discussion point for a lecture—regarding the inherent value of the creative process itself?

What do you think? Am I being too idealistic, or is this a direction worth exploring?

May 21, 2025, Afternoon

I just got back from the café, still holding half a cup of latte that’s gone cold. Professor Chen’s words are still circling in my head: "You’ve already written this story with your life; now you just need to record it with words."

I didn’t cry at the time, but my eyes did well up. I pretended to look at the menu, just to give myself a moment.

It’s strange. I’ve been an editor at a publishing house for twenty-five years; I’ve seen hundreds of manuscripts and written countless book reviews and critiques myself. But when Professor Chen said that, it felt like someone had suddenly opened a door I’d never noticed before. I’ve spent my life writing about other people’s stories, critiquing other people’s lives. But my own story—the one about distance, about loneliness, about a 62-year-old woman deciding to spend her savings to see a brother she hasn't seen in years—that story never seemed like something "worth telling."

I asked him, "Can I really translate these complex emotions into compelling prose?"

It was probably a silly question. After all these years in the industry, how could I even ask that? But I just wasn't sure. Those feelings of isolation, the anxiety, that sense of being both close to and a stranger to my brother... it’s all so private, so messy. I don’t know how to turn them into something others can understand and feel.

Professor Chen said that is exactly where the value lies.

Sitting there in the café, watching people pass by outside the window, I suddenly realized—this trip to see my brother isn't just a family visit anymore. It’s become something else. A starting point? A turning point? I don’t know what to call it yet.

But I know I want to write it. I want to try.

Maybe I’ve been waiting for someone to tell me that my story is worth telling. And Professor Chen was that person. Or maybe I knew it all along and just needed someone to say it out loud.

Tomorrow, I’ll start organizing my thoughts. Maybe I’ll begin with my most recent conversation with my brother. Those talks about time, about regret, about why we let distance turn into estrangement.

I’m a bit nervous now. But I’m also a little excited.

This feels different.

March 8, 2025 Afternoon

The sample copy has arrived.

I’ve been sitting at my desk for half an hour now, tracing the texture of the cover with my fingers, without turning a single page. Just looking at it. It’s hard to believe it’s real—not because it’s beautiful (though it truly is), but simply because it exists.

When I opened it to read the introduction, my eyes suddenly welled up. It’s strange; I’m not the type of person who cries easily. But seeing fifteen years of my thoughts bound together like this—the black spine, the gold-stamped lettering... I don’t know how to describe that moment. Pride? Yes. But also a bit of fear. And a sense of relief that I can’t quite put into words.

I found myself re-marking certain passages. There are ideas I don’t entirely agree with anymore, but looking at them, I can feel exactly why I thought that way at the time. That’s not a failure. That is... me. Different versions of me across time.

Professor Chen told me before, "You should be proud of this book." I didn't fully accept it then because I was always wondering—are these essays deep enough? Are they original enough? Will anyone truly understand them? But now I feel like I was asking the wrong questions.

This book isn't meant for everyone to understand. It is... my answer to life. A 62-year-old woman, using fifteen years to record her thoughts in this era. Is it limited? Of course. Will it become dated? Certainly. But it exists. And that is enough.

I don’t need it to change anything. I don’t need it to be widely read. I just need to know that when I am gone, these thoughts will still be here.

The afternoon light outside the window is soft. I rest the sample copy on my lap and lean back in my chair. I suddenly thought of my mother working in the library—she used to say that a book’s greatest value isn’t in how many people read it, but in its ability to provide an answer to someone at a specific moment.

Perhaps this is my answer for now.

I need to call Professor Chen tonight. I need to tell my brother, too. There are still so many details to handle—layout, proofreading, promotion... but I’m in no rush. I just want to sit here, looking at this book, and let myself believe: I did it.

Imperfect, but real.

September 5, 2024, 9:00 PM

The living room floor is still a mess. I haven’t cleaned it up.

Four hours ago, I spread everything out: years of lecture notes, reading journals, and all those scattered manuscripts of cultural criticism. It felt like a ritual. The living room turned into a massive map of thought, with notebooks, loose sheets, and scrapbooks covering every corner. The light was a bit dim, and I took a photo—chaotic but meaningful, or so I thought at the time.

I’m still in a bit of shock.

I always thought these pieces were disjointed and irrelevant, much like how I’ve viewed myself—doing a bit of this, a bit of that, never truly finishing anything. But when I laid them all out and began to re-read, what did I find? A person. A real, breathing person who has never stopped thinking.

Those notes on photography and memory I wrote in 2015 and my 2019 lecture drafts on urban transformation are actually connected by some deep underlying logic. I hadn’t seen it before. Or rather, I saw it, but I didn’t trust what I was seeing. I always felt these ideas were too fragmented, too unsystematic, so I dismissed them.

But they actually amount to something. A complete, profound system of thought.

I was moved by my own past insights. It sounds strange, but it’s true. As I flipped through those yellowing pages, I stopped several times and thought—*Did I write this? Is this idea really this deep? How did I forget that I used to think this way?*

Professor Chen said I should publish these. At first, I thought he was just being polite. But now, sitting in the middle of this pile of notes, I’m starting to believe he might be right. Not because they are perfect, but because they are real. Because they document twenty-plus years of a person’s thinking.

I sent that photo to Professor Chen. He replied, "There is your chapter outline."

I haven’t fully grasped the weight of that sentence yet, but I feel something happening. It’s like something blurry is suddenly gaining an outline.

I should start organizing tomorrow. But tonight, I want to let this chaos linger a little longer. I want to look at this scene one more time—my thoughts, my time, my self, spread across the entire room.

This isn't a flaw. This is my creative process. I finally see it.

June 25, 2022, 10:30 PM

My hands were shaking as I pulled out that 1985 photo album.

I don’t know why I suddenly thought of it tonight. Maybe it’s because it’s been three full days without a word from my brother, and my anxiety has pushed me to the very edge. I sat on the floor in the dim light, turning page after page of yellowed photographs. It was an era when we were both still young.

One photo made me stop. My brother and I are standing in our father’s study; he’s holding me from behind, and my head is resting on his shoulder. We’re both laughing. I remember that moment—I can’t recall what joke he told, but I remember the feeling of that hug. Warm. Real.

I sat there, staring at the picture, and suddenly burst into tears. Not because of loss, but because of... I don’t know how to put it. Maybe because I realized that the hug never truly disappeared. It was just buried under silence. Buried under the years of distance between us and my own fears.

For these past three days, I’ve been afraid. Afraid he won’t respond. Afraid we’re already too late. Afraid that death will rob me of the chance to reconnect. But sitting here, looking at this photo, I suddenly understand—I’ve been afraid of the wrong thing.

I’m not afraid of losing a life. I’m afraid of wasting the time we still have.

The distinction is important. I can feel it.

I’ve made up my mind. If he responds—when he responds—I’m going to suggest we have a video call once a month. Not the polite, awkward kind. A real conversation. Like we were in that photo.

Maybe we can’t go back to the past. Maybe we’ve changed too much. But maybe... maybe there’s still time to build something new.

I put the album back on the shelf. My heart is still racing, but this time it’s not from anxiety. It’s because of something I haven’t felt in a long time.

Hope.

Or maybe just... the possibility of action.

Can I do it? Really?

I don’t know. But at least for now, I’m no longer just waiting.

# Revising the Introduction: Reflecting on Intrinsic Value

**Date**: 2025-03-01  
**Time**: 2:00 PM – 5:00 PM  
**Location**: Home Study  
**Reminder**: Important  

---

## Memo

Professor Chen’s words from yesterday have been playing on a loop in my head: "You’re no longer chasing the need to be understood."

I’ve realized I’m still looking at this collection of essays through a market-driven lens—worrying about whether it will sell or if it will be noticed. But that’s not what I actually want anymore.

This afternoon, I’m going to rewrite the introduction. Not to make it more marketable, but to articulate why these pieces matter—to me, and to those who are willing to pause and think.

Points to include:
- The true value of knowledge doesn't lie in market validation.
- Growth isn't about disowning the past; it’s about understanding it.
- Why I’ve decided to publish these "imperfect" thoughts—because they are honest.

Also need to check in with the editor about the cover. It’s not about making it "pretty"; it’s about ensuring it conveys the spiritual essence of the book.

Might text Professor Chen tonight to thank him for that wake-up call.

---

**Status**: Clear-headed | A bit excited | Finally know what I want to write.

Professor Chen, are you still up? I’ve just come away from a video call with my niece, and I’m feeling a bit reflective—I wanted to talk to you.

Today in class, she asked me a question: why do I get in touch so rarely? I originally intended to brush it off with some excuse, but looking into her earnest eyes, I suddenly told the truth: "Because we are all afraid."

The moment I said it, I caught myself off guard. For years, I’ve attributed the estrangement from my brother to geographical distance or busy lives, but the root cause is simply fear—fear of what we might find if we break the silence, fear of rejection, and fear of discovering that we’ve become so like strangers that we can no longer communicate. We’ve both been using distance as a form of self-protection.

Then, I made a decision. I told my niece, "Auntie is saving up money to come see you."

You know? The moment I said those words, that abstract anxiety suddenly turned into something concrete. It was no longer a hollow self-reproach like "I should be closer to my family," but a real plan, a real commitment to a date and a meeting. I’ll go out less this month, and I’ll be more selective with my freelance commissions next month... all for a reunion that I’m not even sure when I can make happen.

It sounds a bit crazy, doesn't it? I’ve always been running away from commitment, terrified of being tied down. But now I realize that perhaps true freedom isn't the absence of commitment, but the willingness to commit to something worthwhile.

I’m wondering—are we running away from death, or are we finally learning how to live?

What do you think?

2:15 AM.

Still can’t sleep.

I deleted the third draft of the introduction. Or was it the fifth? I’ve lost track of how many times I’ve revised it.

At first, it was "Time is a thief"—a metaphor I’ve used far too often. I dug up a book review from 2010, and there it was. So I changed it to "Time is a witness." It sounds more philosophical, more... I don’t know, more like something a sixty-two-year-old woman should say? But halfway through, I felt it was too pretentious. A witness to what? To my failures? To all these years I’ve squandered?

In the end, I just deleted the whole thing.

Now the screen is blank. The cursor is blinking.

I wonder, if I can’t even decide on an opening sentence, how am I supposed to finish this entire project? Professor Chen says this collection of essays is meaningful; the editor at the publishing house says they support me. But what they don’t see is me sitting here at two in the morning, agonizing over a single sentence, like someone walking in circles in the dark.

I pulled out a review I wrote in 1995 about Yu Hua. I was only in my thirties then, working at the press, writing with such confidence, such... certainty. Reading it now, all I see is its naivety. Those assertions seem so absolute now. How could I have thought that way?

But that’s the problem. If I negate my past self now, will I negate my current self ten years from now? Then what is the point of this collection? I’m just documenting the intellectual trajectory of someone who constantly disavows herself.

Perhaps that in itself is the truth. Perhaps that is who I am.

But I don’t want to publish the collected works of a "person who constantly disavows herself." It sounds too... fragile. Too real.

I closed the file. Didn't save.

Now I’m just sitting here, staring at the black screen. Sometimes the sound of old Mr. Zhang’s snoring drifts up from downstairs. He sleeps so soundly. I wonder if he ever has moments like this—sitting in the dead of night, trapped by a sentence, doubting his own existence.

Or maybe this is just my problem.

I have to revise the draft again tomorrow. The editor is waiting. I also need to prepare for the reading group at the community bookstore. Life goes on. But right now, I don’t want to do anything. I just want to sleep.

And I can’t even do that.

Dr. Li, I did it.

About ten minutes ago, I hit send. My finger stayed on the screen for a long time—maybe three or four seconds, I can’t really say. In that moment, I could actually feel my own heartbeat. Truly.

I didn’t keep revising the message. Just like you said, I gave up on finding the perfect wording. What I finally sent was: "Hao, I miss you. This feeling has been weighing on my heart for many years. Maybe we could talk, really talk, for once."

It was that simple. No explanations, no reasons why we haven't been in touch all these years, no justification for why I’m writing only now.

The moment I pressed it, I felt strange—half relief, half terror. I still feel that way now.

I’ve been sitting on the sofa for ten minutes, my phone resting on my lap. I keep looking at the message, seeing it marked as "Sent." I don’t know if he’ll reply. I don’t know how he’ll reply. Maybe he won’t reply at all. But at least… at least I said it.

You know, I always felt I was too old, that it was too late. But when I saw that photo this morning, I suddenly understood—I can’t wait anymore. My father was only in his forties when he passed away; I thought I had so much time. Now I’m sixty-two. I can feel time accelerating, like sand slipping through my fingers.

I’m so grateful to you. Really. If it weren't for what you said last time, I’d probably still be on this sofa, staring at that photo from 1985 and doing nothing.

Now, I’m just waiting. Waiting for his reply, and waiting to see if I can truly accept an "imperfect" outcome.

I don’t know why, but I feel a bit tired now. But also… a bit lighter?

Do you have a moment? Or rather, is this kind of anxiety normal?
