# Cultural Salon Chat Group

**Lin Xiaoyun** 10:32

I’ve been reflecting on something lately and wanted to throw it out here to get your thoughts.

What is the real standard by which we judge a work, an article, or an idea? Is it the market response—how many people see, cite, and discuss it? Or is it its ability to help us, or others, understand the world more deeply?

Sometimes these two standards overlap, but more often than not, they don’t.

**Lin Xiaoyun** 10:35

While organizing my articles from over the years, I suddenly realized I’ve been using the former to measure their value. Which pieces went viral? Which ones were ignored? It’s as if that alone determined whether they were worth including in my collection. But thinking this way actually made me feel lost.

Then a friend pointed out that I no longer need to chase being understood. That really hit home. Why am I still using a market yardstick to measure my own thoughts?

**Lin Xiaoyun** 10:38

My current take is this: even if an article is only read by three or five people, if it helps them see something clearly in a certain moment, or makes them feel understood, then it has value. And that kind of value doesn’t depreciate over time.

What do you all think? Or rather, how do you handle this issue in your own creative work?

---

**Prof. Chen** 10:42

That’s a great question. It reminds me of something Pascal said—we don’t write to be remembered; we write to make a moment clearer.

**Prof. Chen** 10:43

I can sense this shift in you. Last time we met, you were still agonizing over the "market potential" of your essay collection. You sound much more at peace now.

**Lin Xiaoyun** 10:45

Ha, you caught me. I definitely feel more at ease. It was a long process, though; I couldn't wrap my head around it at first.

**Friend** 10:48

I completely agree. I went through the same thing with my photography. At first, I was obsessed with view counts, but I realized that just wears you out. Now I just shoot what I want. When someone occasionally says, "This photo changed how I see this place," that sense of fulfillment is on a whole different level.

**Lin Xiaoyun** 10:51

Exactly! That’s the feeling. That genuine connection carries so much more weight than numbers.

**Friend** 10:52

So, are you planning to include all your articles in the collection now?

**Lin Xiaoyun** 10:55

Not all of them. There’s still a selection process, but the criteria have changed. Now I ask myself: "Do I still believe in this piece? Does it capture a moment of my authentic thinking?" If the answer is yes, it’s worth keeping. Even if my views have changed since then, that change itself is meaningful. It shows growth.

**Lin Xiaoyun** 10:56

Thinking this way actually makes things easier. I don’t have to pretend I was never wrong, and I don’t have to disown my past self just to look "progressive."

2:45 AM.

I don’t know why I’m still awake. Or maybe I do—I know exactly why. That diagnosis is looping in my head like a player I can’t turn off.

I’ve replayed what Dr. Li said this afternoon over and over again. "Generalized Anxiety Disorder." Just like that, a label has been slapped on me. I know it’s not some terminal illness, and I know plenty of people have this diagnosis, but knowing it intellectually is one thing; feeling it is another.

I spent two hours searching online. I started with medical definitions, then patient forums, then how professionals cope with anxiety, then… I don’t even know what I was looking for anymore. Maybe I was looking for proof that I’m not actually sick. Or conversely, proof that I really am. The more information I gathered, the deeper I sank, until I was just mechanically clicking, reading, clicking… until my eyes ached.

The worst part was when that thought suddenly popped up: What if people find out? What if the editors find out? A cultural critic with an anxiety disorder—how does that sound? Unstable? Unreliable? Would I lose those collaborations?

I walked over to the mirror. At two in the morning, the person in the reflection looked like a stranger. I tried to find some sign of being "sick" on my face. What is a person with anxiety supposed to look like? I don’t know. I look the same as I did yesterday, yet completely different.

It’s ridiculous. I know it’s ridiculous. I can analyze all of this rationally—I know a diagnosis doesn’t change who I am, I know many brilliant people struggle with anxiety, and I know this might even explain why I’ve always been so… high-strung. But logic isn't helping me right now. Logic is like a key, but I’m not facing a locked door; I’m facing something invisible, pressing down on my chest.

Dr. Li said I need to "accept" it. Accept. As if I could just welcome anxiety in like a friend.

I don’t know how to do that.

Now I’m sitting at my desk, surrounded by half-read books and messy notes. This is me—always somewhere, never fully anywhere. Maybe that’s part of the anxiety, too?

I’m exhausted. But I can’t sleep.

May 12, 2023 – Past 11 PM

I’ve been lying here for a long time. There’s a crack on the ceiling I never noticed before. Or rather, I’ve noticed it many times, but I forget every time. Just like I’ve noticed so many things, only to let them slip away.

Old Man Zhang came knocking again this afternoon. He asked about the room renovation. Just hearing his voice made me feel exhausted. "I’m not in the mood for this right now," I told him. He looked a bit hurt, but I didn’t have the energy to comfort him. Whatever. Let it all be.

The room is cluttered with things that have been moved around. Books, old camera gear, junk scavenged from flea markets. They’re just piled there, like some kind of visual reminder of my life. I look at these things and can’t remember why I ever thought changing the room would change anything. How foolish.

I searched for some strange things tonight. "How to gracefully disappear from life." I don’t even know why I searched for it. It’s not that I’m actually planning anything, it’s just... sometimes I wonder, if I just vanished one day, would it be simpler? No more deadlines to worry about, no more looking at this aging face in the mirror, no more pretending I still have some kind of value.

It’s absurd. I know it’s absurd.

But sitting in this room, looking at this mess, I really can’t think of a reason to keep going. The book club? Maybe. But that’s just another project I’ll eventually give up on. I’m always like this. I start with such passion, and then slowly, the fire dies out, leaving behind nothing but one unfinished thing after another.

I thought about this when my mother passed away. I was a bit younger then; I still had the strength to think. Now, I barely have the energy to even do that.

Maybe this is just who I am. Someone who can’t stick to anything. Someone who wasted their freedom, wasted their time, and is now wasting space. This room is me. Messy, stale, and no one truly cares.

I have to reply to that editor’s email tomorrow. Another deadline is looming. I don’t know how much longer I can hold on.

April 22, 2022, 11:30 PM

I just hung up the phone with Wang Fang. Now I’m sitting alone in the study, the city lights glowing outside the window, and my hands are still shaking a little.

I don’t know where to start. Maybe it was just… suddenly remembering her message from this afternoon, asking how I’ve been. I was going to brush it off, change the subject like I always do. But for some reason, I just didn’t have the energy to keep up the act tonight. I told her I’ve been seeing a therapist.

The moment the words came out, I thought I’d feel ashamed. Or that I’d be waiting for her judgment. But there was nothing. She just listened quietly as I talked—about Dr. Li’s clinic, the late-night anxiety, how I sometimes overanalyze people’s tones over a single sentence. I talked for a long time, sharing things I’ve never told anyone.

She didn’t interrupt me, and she didn’t rush to give advice. She just listened. Then she said, "Xiaoyun, you’ve been carrying this alone for far too long."

I almost cried. But I didn’t. I just… didn't speak for a long time.

Later, she said that seeking help isn't a sign of weakness. When she said it, her tone was so matter-of-fact, like she was stating the most normal truth in the world. It didn't feel like she was just trying to comfort me.

After hanging up, I sat by myself for a long while. The room is quiet, save for the occasional sound of a car passing below. I’m thinking that maybe… maybe all those judgments I’ve held about myself were wrong. Maybe I’m not alone because I’m "too independent." Maybe I’m just scared. Scared of being a burden. Scared that if I spoke up, people would look down on me.

But Wang Fang listened, and she didn't look down on me. If anything, she seemed to understand me even more.

It’s a strange feeling. Being seen. Being accepted. Not because of how successful I am, but just… being accepted.

It’s almost midnight now, but I don’t feel that usual sense of anxiety. Instead, there’s this strange, indescribable calm. Not a fake sense of relaxation, but… true peace. Like something inside me has finally loosened its grip.

I have to keep working on that book review tomorrow; the deadline is looming again. But right now, I don’t want to think about that. I just want to sit here and let Wang Fang’s words sink in. "You’ve been carrying this alone for far too long."

Maybe… maybe I can try not to be so alone. Maybe it’s not weakness.

Maybe.

[Image: A ragdoll sitting by the window, with the city skyline silhouetted against the morning light]

Sometimes, changing direction is the best way to move forward.

I spent a long time chatting with old Mr. Zhang from downstairs yesterday. He said that instead of dwelling on what you’ve given up, it’s better to think about where the new possibilities lie. Such simple words, yet they brought a slight sting of tears to my eyes.

Living alone all these years, I’ve grown used to my own rhythm, and sometimes, used to the loneliness. But I’ve realized that true loneliness isn't about having no one around; it’s about having no one understand why you do what you do. Fortunately, I’ve met people who truly get it—they don’t pressure me to give up my dreams for a "stable" job, nor do they mock my stalled renovation plans. They just stay by my side, watching me pick myself up from disappointment.

I’m heading out next week. I’ve packed my bags, and I’ve packed away my old state of mind. At 62, I’ve finally grasped a simple truth: the best way to live is to do the things that make you feel alive.

Thank you to everyone who has stood by me. Your warmth gives me more peace of mind than any plan ever could.

**To discuss in the next session:**

1. Why do I always use "analysis" to dodge my feelings? Wang Fang said that on the phone, I kept explaining my anxiety, wrapping it in various theoretical frameworks as if that would tame it. Hearing her say that, I suddenly realized—yes, that’s exactly what I’m doing. Using terms like "Big Five" or "neuroticism" to talk about myself, as if by doing so I can maintain a distance and not have to actually feel the fear.

2. Why am I so afraid of being seen? Wang Fang said I’ve never talked to her like this before. I thought about it, and she’s right. I’m always changing the subject or using clever observations as a cover. Tonight was different; I cried. On the phone. I actually startled myself a bit.

3. How does this relate to my "independence"? I’ve always felt that seeking help is a failure, a betrayal of freedom. But talking to Wang Fang tonight, I felt a strange sense of relief—the feeling of being heard. Isn't that weakness? Or have I had the definition of "weakness" wrong all along?

---

**Observations:**

I noticed my tone on the phone was different than usual. Normally, I use a lot of "buts" and "on the other hands," as if I’m placing qualifiers on all my feelings. Tonight, I was more direct. Wang Fang said, "You sound different."

It made me think—maybe my anxiety isn't just a psychological issue, but a lifestyle. I use analysis, independence, and keeping a distance from everything to protect myself, but it also keeps me constantly running.

---

**Another thought** (albeit a bit messy):

If I’m willing to see my defenses, what’s next? Should I tell more people I’m in therapy? Or should this remain private?

Or maybe that’s not the point. Maybe the question is—why do I feel like this needs to be hidden?

---

What would Dr. Li think of this call? I imagine she’d ask me: "When you were crying on the phone, what were you feeling?"

I still don't know how to answer that.

Dr. Li, I wanted to check my pulse this morning, but this time I stopped myself.

Just as my fingers were about to press against my neck, I remembered what you said last time, and... I let go. Even though that urge to confirm I’m still alive was still there, I didn't give in to it.

This might sound like a small thing, but it felt different to me. It felt like the first time I was actually fighting back against this thing, instead of being completely led by the nose.

I don’t know if this means anything. Maybe I just happened to have more willpower today. But I wanted to tell you because... I’m not sure if this is what I’m supposed to be doing. Am I moving in the right direction?

It’s another week until our next session, which feels like a long time.

Hey, something interesting happened at the secondhand bookstore today.

You know how I am—every time I visit Mr. Li’s shop, I end up rummaging through the shelves for ages. Today, I spotted a book on the aesthetics of photography that I really wanted. Since I started learning photography back in 2015, I’ve always wanted to study the theory systematically. In the past, I wouldn't have hesitated for a second to buy it.

But this time, I didn't. I told Mr. Li, "I’m saving up for something important," and I put the book back.

Guess what he said? He just said, "Then it’s worth it." Four simple words, but they actually made me want to cry. To have a near-stranger understand my decision just like that... it meant a lot.

It hit me then that all the choices I’ve made over the last two years—quitting my stable job, living alone, hunting for old books—they all seemed to be in pursuit of some kind of freedom. But that freedom is actually very lonely. It wasn't until you called that I realized some things are more important than freedom.

Now that I’m saving up to come see you guys abroad, what’s giving up a book I like? It just makes me more certain that I’m making the right call.

When are you free to chat more about the itinerary? I want to plan this properly and not just wing it like I used to.

# Shot List: "Forgotten Corners"

## Core Theme
Capturing places forgotten by time and the city through the lens. No grand purpose—just wanting to see.

---

## Locations

### Tier 1 (Winter Priority)
- **Windows in Old Hutongs** (Near Yuyuan Road, Jing'an District)
  - Time: 7:00–8:00 AM, backlit is best
  - Focus: Texture of window frames, dust on glass, reflections
  - Note: Go in mid-November while the leaves still have color

- **Abandoned Shop on Yongkang Road** (That closed-down record store)
  - Time: 2:00–3:00 PM
  - Focus: Faded signage, door frames, light leaking through the cracks
  - Note: Move fast; heard it’s slated for demolition

- **Old Alleys on Tianping Road**
  - Time: 5:00–6:00 PM
  - Focus: Long corridors, clotheslines, lights glowing in windows
  - Note: Go when it’s quiet; don't disturb the residents

### Tier 2 (Around Winter Solstice)
- **Old Pier by Suzhou Creek** (Changning Section)
  - Time: 30 minutes before sunset
  - Focus: Rusted chains, water reflections, buildings on the opposite bank
  - Note: Check the light; backlighting can easily overexpose

- **Back Gardens of Old Villas on Julu Road** (If accessible)
  - Time: 12:00–1:00 PM
  - Focus: Abandoned flowerbeds, stones, moss
  - Note: Might need to ask for permission

---

## Technical Reminders
- Check shutter speed; light is weak in winter
- Try Black & White mode; might be more interesting
- Don't just shoot head-on—try crouching, low angles, and side profiles
- Details, details, details—not the whole, but the overlooked fragments

---

## Core Reminder
**This isn't for publishing or exhibition. This is about seeing clearly the things you usually walk past but never truly notice.**

Every place has a story, but I’m not the one writing it—time is. I’m just here to photograph it.

---

## Logistics
- Reschedule if the weather is bad; don't force it
- Bring spare batteries (stop forgetting this)
- Shoot if inspiration strikes; don't wait for the "perfect" time
- Visit at least one location a week, or this project will stall again

---

Dr. Li, I wanted to tell you about something that happened today.

This afternoon, my editor nudged me for my draft. Instead of making excuses or just ghosting her like I usually do, I paused for a long time... and then I just told her. I told her straight out that I’ve been going through an anxiety crisis lately and it’s been affecting my work.

My hands were shaking as I typed that. I expected her to be impatient, or to just say "get it to me as soon as possible." But she said she understood and asked if I needed an extension. She even told me to let her know if there was anything she could do to help.

I was honestly a bit shaken. I’ve always assumed people wouldn't care, or that they’d just think I was making excuses. But it really felt like she was listening.

It reminded me of what you said last time about "allowing yourself to be seen." I’ve always found that so hard; I felt like being honest was the same as exposing my weaknesses. But today felt different. She didn't dismiss me because of my struggles; if anything, she was more empathetic.

This small moment has made me start to believe that maybe the world isn't as cold as I imagined. Maybe I can try being this honest more often.

I’m still a bit nervous, though. Can I actually get the draft finished now? I don’t know. But at least I haven't spiraled into total self-loathing like I used to.

Do you think this counts as progress?

Sometimes we think loneliness stems from a lack of understanding, only to realize later that it’s because we never truly invited anyone in.

While tidying up my room today, it hit me—these books, these objects, these yellowed notebooks, they’ve been here all along, waiting to be seen. Not to be displayed, but to be truly seen.

Friends came over. I told them the stories behind these things. Some were stories I had almost forgotten myself. But as I spoke them aloud, they seemed to come back to life.

Maybe this is how change begins. Not by making the room tidy, but by letting it breathe.

# June 4, 2022, 11:20 AM

The moment I pressed send, my fingers were trembling.

It wasn’t because of the cold. The room was stuffy and hot, and I was dressed in summer clothes, yet my fingers wouldn't stop shaking. It was probably because my heart was racing. I could feel it thumping against my chest, like something trying to break free from the inside.

I stared at that message on the screen for ten minutes. Ten minutes. I kept tweaking the wording, deleting, and retyping. "Brother, I miss you." — It felt too simple. Too naked. But Dr. Li said that maybe "imperfect" is exactly what I need. She said I’m always trying to construct a perfect explanation, an airtight reason, as if I owe him some kind of apology.

But do I really owe him?

Twenty-two years. We have been silent for twenty-two years.

I think back to that photo from 1985. We were downstairs; he was on his bicycle, and I was on the back seat, both of us laughing. His arm was around me, terrified I might fall off. He was only a teenager then, and I was even younger. Later, we would eavesdrop on Father’s lectures in the study, huddled outside the door, and he would hold my hand whenever I got scared.

When did it start to change? When did such a vast distance grow between us?

Maybe it was the moment he left. Maybe it was because I didn't ask him to stay. Maybe it’s because, for these twenty-two years, I’ve been waiting for him to speak first, while he was waiting for me.

I pressed send.

It was a strange moment. It wasn't relief. It wasn't entirely fear, either. It was both feelings happening at once, like two currents colliding in my chest. Part of me felt light—finally, finally, I had done *something*. Not a perfect something, just... something. But the other part of me immediately began to regret it. What if he doesn't reply? What if he sees it but chooses to ignore it? What if I’ve only made everything more awkward?

I’m afraid to look at my phone now.

It’s sitting on the coffee table, screen-side down. I’ve checked it three times already, pretending to look for something else. No notification sound. No reply.

I am sixty-two years old. My father was around this age when he passed away. I remember thinking back then that he still had plenty of time, but he didn't. Neither did Mother. Now it’s my turn, and here I am, sitting alone in this room cluttered with books and odds and ends, waiting for a reply that might never come.

How much time do I have? How much time is really left?

If I do nothing, I’ll regret it for the rest of my life. But if I do this and he rejects me, I’ll regret that too. So maybe regret is inevitable either way. Maybe I should just accept that.

Maybe I should stop checking my phone.

But I can't. I’m going to look one more time. Right now.

March 31, 2024, 10:15 PM

No insomnia this month. Or rather, only once, but that was because I was buzzing with excitement over the preface for the essay collection—my mind just wouldn't stop—not because some nameless dread dragged me out of sleep at 3:00 AM. The distinction matters far more than I imagined.

I’m sitting at my desk, flipping through this month’s diary. We held three book club sessions, each with new faces. Last week, a girl—about twenty-five—told me that my commentary on *To Live* made her cry. I didn't know how to react at the time; I just nodded. Later, Uncle Zhang ran into me downstairs and said he’d heard about that session and wanted to come listen too. I never imagined my words would ripple out like that.

I spoke with Lin Hao twice on the phone. The first time, we were both stiff, like two strangers trying to find common ground. But the second time was different. He brought up Dad, saying he’d been sorting through some old letters and was reminded of Dad’s endless rambling about life. We talked for a long time. It didn’t solve anything, but it felt like something was being reconnected. I don’t know how long it will last, but at least for now, it’s no longer that silent regret.

Editing the essay collection is moving faster than expected. The editor at the publishing house said she loved the preface I wrote, calling it "warm." I had to read it three times before I believed it was true. I used to think my ideas were too scattered, too unsystematic, too "unprofessional." Now I realize that perhaps it’s exactly this scattered, personal quality that gives the writing life.

The most interesting thing is that I’ve started decluttering the house. Not the compulsive, "this is how it should be" kind of tidying, but a genuine desire to see what I’ve accumulated over the years. The half-read books, the unfinished photography projects, the old trinkets from flea markets... they don't make me feel guilty anymore. They are my traces; they record my curiosity and my various attempts at living.

I used to fear that my sensitivity would destroy me. Now I understand that it is a part of me. It is because of this sensitivity that I can hear the girl’s tears at the book club, feel Lin Hao’s hesitation and longing on the other end of the line, and see the meaning of an entire life within a single sentence.

The value of life lies not in its length, but in its depth and meaning.

I am creating that depth.

This realization has made me feel entirely at ease.

Hao, I’m sitting here in my study as the sky darkens outside. It suddenly hit me that it’s been a long time since I’ve written anything to you with such sincerity.

I’ve been busy preparing for the trip lately—passports, tickets, all the little details. I realized I’m doing something I never dared to do before: spending my savings just to see you. To be honest, it’s kept me up at night, double-checking everything and wondering if this is a mistake. But every time that thought crosses my mind, I think back to our last conversation.

You know, that call changed how I look at a lot of things. It wasn’t some grand philosophy; it was just… you asking me why I didn’t just come visit. In that moment, I realized I’d been using every excuse to keep myself trapped. The finances, the age, the distance—those are all real, but they weren’t the real reason. The real reason was fear. Fear that we’d find we’ve become strangers, fear of wasting time and money, fear of… fear of admitting that I’ve been running away all this time.

I’m 62 now. It sounds like just a fact, but to me, it’s suddenly become a reminder. I still have time to do these things, time to see you, and time to experience the things I’ve been putting off. I thought about how short life is the year Mom passed away, but it’s only now that I’ve truly started to believe it. I understand now that there is still so much in life worth experiencing and cherishing.

So, I want to thank you. Thank you for your patience on the phone, and for not giving up on me after all these years of silence. I know I’m not an easy person to get along with. I ask "why" too much, I’m stubborn, and I’ll overanalyze a single sentence in the middle of the night. But you seem to understand all of that.

I’m leaving at the end of May. I’m still a bit nervous, but this time it’s not the "I want to give up" kind of nervous; it’s the "I’m ready, but still a little scared" kind. That feeling actually isn't so bad.

We can really talk when we meet. I want to hear your stories—the real ones, not just the few greetings at the end of the year. I think we both owe each other a real conversation.

Thank you, Hao.

Xiaoyun

December 28, 2024, 10:00 PM

Sitting here by a pile of books, the tea in my hand has already gone cold. Outside the window is the city’s night view. I’ve lived in this old apartment for so many years; every corner is filled with my stories.

The year is coming to an end. I find myself repeatedly asking one question—who am I, really? Not as an editor, not as a critic, just... me.

That phone call with my brother changed something. I don’t know how to describe it, it’s just... something has been awakened. Maybe it’s because we’re both getting older, or maybe because I’ve finally admitted to my own loneliness. Not a sad kind of loneliness, but a sober, honest one. I’ve been running away from it, filling it with work, salons, and various hobbies. But this time I stopped. I looked at it and asked: what do you want?

The answer is: connection. But not the superficial kind used to escape solitude. A real, meaningful connection. Seeing my brother might be exactly that. How many years has it been since we had a real conversation? I don’t even know what his life is like now. The thought scares me, but it also excites me.

As for the trip, I’m still doing the math. Every time I open the calculator, my anxiety spikes a little. Not enough money, not enough time, and I’m not sure if my body can handle it. But... I’ve realized I’m no longer asking "can I do it," but rather "do I want to do it." The shift is subtle, but it changes everything.

I remember what my mother used to say—when she worked at the library, her favorites were the most worn-out books, because those were the ones that had been truly lived, truly read. I understand what she meant now. I don’t want a perfect, safe, meticulously planned life. I want a life that has been fully lived.

For years, I’ve been terrified of death. In the middle of the night, I would search for symptoms and repeatedly imagine the worst-case scenarios. But that fear is slowly transforming into something else—not the fear of dying, but the fear of wasting my life. I realize I still have time. Maybe not much, but enough. Enough to see my brother, enough to take a risk, enough to create something.

Regarding freedom, I think I finally understand it. I used to think freedom meant "not being tied down," so I rejected all commitments and plans. But true freedom, perhaps, is knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the price for it. To travel, I need to save money, which means taking my work more seriously. To see my brother, I need to make arrangements in advance. These aren't threats to my freedom; they are the safeguards of it.

I still have so many uncertainties. I don’t know how the trip will go, I don’t know what I’ll say to my brother, I don’t know about my health, and I don’t know if work will get worse. But I’ve learned to coexist with uncertainty. I no longer need all the answers before I can start moving.

I’ll be 63 next year. That number used to panic me. But now... now I just want to see what this year will bring.

I want to live. Truly live.

# May Schedule Planning

**Reorganizing after the editor’s email**

Spent the whole afternoon at the cafe marking up my calendar in red ink. My editor said we’re ramping up the frequency starting next month—moving from bi-weekly to weekly. I didn't say no right away; it’s an old habit of mine. I said, "Sure, I'll do my best," and then immediately started doing the math.

**Time allocation for the next three months:**

- **Every Tue & Thu evening** → Reading Group (Fixed, non-negotiable)
- **Every Wed afternoon** → Lecture prep (A commitment to Professor Chen)
- **Weekends** → Writing (Originally planned for one article every two weeks)
- **New: One article per week** → What does this actually mean?

I’ve marked all the deadlines in red on the calendar. June 7th, June 14th, June 21st... all the way through the end of August. The dense cluster of red dots looks like some kind of warning.

**The conflict is obvious:**

With Wednesday lecture prep and weekend writing, I used to have some breathing room. To hit a weekly pace, I’ll have to squeeze writing time into Mon, Tue, Thu, or Fri. But Tue and Thu are for the reading group, and Wed is for the lecture...

I scribbled a small question mark in the margin of the calendar.

**Other uncertainties:**

- How long can I sustain this pace?
- Will the quality drop? (This is what makes me most anxious.)
- Will the reading group members feel neglected because I’m stretched too thin?

It’s 11:30 PM, and I’m still sitting here staring at this red-marked calendar. I slightly regret agreeing to the editor while I was still at the cafe. I should have said, "Let me think about it." But then again, I feel like this might be an opportunity—if I can prove I can handle a weekly column, maybe...

But I shouldn't overthink it right now.

**I’ve set a "checkpoint" for myself: mid-June.**

I’ll see how I’m feeling then. If I really can’t hold it together, I’ll have to be honest with the editor. Even though that will be hard.

The calendar is in my phone notes now, and a paper version is taped next to my bookshelf. Every time I see it, I feel a bit of tension, but also... a sense of being pushed forward.

Maybe this is exactly what I need?

At that time, I was still contributing regularly to *Cultural Horizons*, writing a series of critiques on modernist literature. It was a strange feeling. I didn't feel that usual sense of self-deprecation—the "look, nobody wants this stuff anymore" feeling. Instead, it was... I don't know how to describe it. It was a sudden realization that there was something real in those words. I don't mean whether they still have market value today; I mean that what I was thinking back then, how I interpreted a work—those things actually happened. They were real.

I’ve been thinking that maybe I’ve been measuring my work by the wrong standards all along. I’ve equated value with market demand, with whether anyone is reading it, with whether it can be converted into income. But the sense of fulfillment I felt reading those articles today—that feeling didn't come from "this piece is useful," but from "I once thought about these questions with total sincerity."

This thought makes me a little uneasy. It’s not that I’ve suddenly become optimistic; I haven’t. But... something is slightly different. It’s like a tiny crack has opened up.

I’m not sure how long this feeling will last. Tomorrow I might sink right back into it. But I felt I should tell you about this, because you’ve been asking me if I’ve noticed anything different. This discovery is small, and it might not mean anything, but it did happen.

I haven't quite figured out what this means yet.

September 27, 2024, past 8:00 PM

I just stepped out of Dr. Li’s clinic. I’m sitting in the convenience store downstairs having a cup of hot tea, and my hands are still shaking.

Today was supposed to be just a routine consultation. I had prepared myself for the usual routine—I talk about my recent anxiety, she takes notes, and she gives me some advice. But then she suddenly said, "Lin Xiaoyun, I’d like to change the format of our sessions."

My heart sank the moment she said it. All sorts of thoughts flashed through my mind—Am I too dependent on her? Does she feel like I’m not making progress? Is she giving up on me? That feeling of being abandoned rushed over me instantly, and the tears just started falling. I didn't even realize why I was crying.

But what she said next stunned me. She said, "I’ve noticed that through your book club, the lectures, and your discussions with Professor Chen, you’ve already built a real support system. You are no longer an isolated patient who needs to be looked after. You already know how to take care of yourself."

I didn't process it at first. She continued, "We can switch to once every two weeks, or you can just come in whenever you need to. You don't need me to give you 'answers' every week anymore."

I cried again, but this time it felt completely different.

Sitting here in the convenience store, I finally understand the difference between those two bouts of tears. The first was fear—the fear of loss. The second was... relief? Or surprise? I’m not entirely sure. But it definitely wasn't despair.

I’ve always thought of myself as lonely. Living alone, divorced, parents gone, my brother far away overseas. I got used to seeing myself as someone who needed to be rescued. But what Dr. Li said allowed me to see things from a different perspective—I’ve actually been building connections all along. The people at the book club, Professor Chen, even Uncle Zhang. These are real.

I wrote this down in my notebook: "Independence does not equal loneliness."

That sentence means something different to me now. Before, I understood independence as an escape—escaping my marriage, escaping the office, escaping anything that might bind me. But now... now I understand that independence can be a choice—choosing who to be with, choosing how to live, and choosing to believe that I am capable of taking care of myself.

The publishing plan, the lectures, this book... none of these things are about filling a void of loneliness. They are my way of expressing something. They are my way of saying, "Look, I have something to say, and I want to share it with you."

I’ve never thought about it like this before.

Tomorrow, I need to email Professor Chen to tell him my recent thoughts. I also need to organize the discussion notes from this week’s book club. These things feel different now—not like I’m just killing time, but because... because these things truly matter to me.

My fingers are still shaking, but not from fear.

## Event Description

I spread out all my lecture notes, cultural critiques, and reading journals from over the years across the living room floor. For four hours, I sat in the middle of this sea of paper, revisiting my past thoughts like an archaeologist.

It was quite moving. I realized that these seemingly scattered and disorganized pieces—a late-night essay on *Dream of the Red Chamber*, notes from last year’s salon on contemporary art, arguments from a book review written two years ago—are actually all addressing the same core question: a question about meaning, about our era, and about how we find certainty amidst uncertainty.

Professor Chen was right. These aren't just isolated fragments; it’s a system. I hadn't realized it, but it’s been there all along.

I’ve managed to map out the initial framework, and the thread connecting the five chapters is much clearer now. There are still many details to fill in, but at least I finally know what this book wants to say.

# June 28, 2025, Evening

Sitting in my brother’s study, watching the sunset over an unfamiliar city. I filled an entire page of my notebook with plans, only to cross out half of them. It’s not that the plans were bad; it’s just that I suddenly realized—what am I doing?

I am planning my life for when I return.

The thought made me pause for a long time.

In the past, I was always running. Running from the constraints of the publishing house, from a failed marriage, from every commitment that made me feel trapped. I told myself this was "freedom," but in reality... I was just running. Running to the next interest, the next project, the next thing that made me feel alive. And then running away again.

This trip was supposed to be the same. I bought the ticket, dropped several deadlines, and told myself, "I need to escape for a while." But here, being with my brother, watching his life, seeing how he finds balance between stability and change... I finally realized I’ve had it backwards all along.

True freedom isn't escaping commitment. True freedom is **choosing** commitment.

I wrote in my notebook: "These aren't plans for escape; they are plans for return."

Looking at those words, my eyes grew a bit misty.

When I get back, I want to keep writing cultural critiques. Not because I’m forced to, but because I truly love it. I want to organize more book clubs—not to kill time, but because the ideas of the participants fulfill me. I want to call my brother regularly—not just for holiday greetings, but to truly share our lives. And maybe... I really should consider publishing that collection of essays. Professor Chen was right; I have something to say.

These are all commitments. But I’m not afraid of them anymore.

Because I’ve understood something—the opposite of loneliness isn't "the absence of solitude," but "meaningful connection." I can live alone, work freely, and change my plans at a moment's notice. But I don't have to be lonely. I can choose to build deep relationships with certain people and certain things. That isn't being bound; it’s being seen.

I am 62 years old now. My parents are gone. My brother lives half a world away. I used to think this meant I had to learn to live entirely on my own. But this trip has shown me that I can choose again. I can take the initiative to connect, rather than passively accepting loneliness.

The strangest thing is that this proactive choice makes me feel freer than I ever have before.

Not the freedom of escape. The **freedom of return**.

Tomorrow, my brother is taking me to the art museum he frequents. I want to take some photos and perhaps write a review of the city's art scene. It’s been so long since I felt this way—being completely immersed in something while simultaneously doing it for my work.

It feels good. It feels like being alive.

# Phone Memo

**Feb 1, 2022 – 2:15 AM**

Awake again. Heart is racing. I touched my neck; it feels like it’s going to jump out. Can’t find the blood pressure monitor—probably buried in the piles of books.

Why do I always wake up at this exact time?

Read an article yesterday saying arrhythmia is a sign of heart disease. Is my heartbeat irregular? How can I tell?

Dad was 68 when... no, Dad died at 68. I’m 62 now. Six years left.

Mom was 71. Nine years left.

Why am I calculating this?

**2:47 AM**

Pulled out my phone and searched "waking up at dawn with racing heart."

Should I go to the hospital? Or am I just tired?

I haven't been sleeping well lately, that's true. Missed another deadline. The editor at the magazine is probably furious.

**3:01 AM**

When will I die?

Why does this question keep coming up?

I don’t think about this during the day. Only at night, only in the dark, the question crawls out like an insect. Asking over and over. Over and over.

Has my life had any meaning?

Was I right to quit my job?

Why did I start being afraid at 62?

**3:15 AM**

Got out of bed. Had a glass of water. My fingers feel a bit numb. Poor circulation? Or just from holding my phone too long?

**3:22 AM**

Tomorrow, I must get some sleep.

---

**3:41 AM**

Still awake.

I stared at my phone screen for a long time.

My brother’s letter was lying right next to me; I’d already read it three times. That one sentence—"Maybe these items aren't symbols of chaos, but traces of your life"—I kept turning it over and over in my mind.

I opened WeChat and found Lin Hao’s chat window. The cursor blinked in the input box.

I deleted it.

Opened it again.

"Thanks for the letter."

No, too cold.

"Lin Hao, I read your letter. Read it several times. You’re right, I’ve been misunderstanding myself all along."

I stared at those words for two minutes. My finger hovered over the send button.

Is it too cheesy? Will I seem too vulnerable?

But... he said he understood. He didn't criticize my mess; instead, he called it my "traces." All these years, I thought he stayed away because he didn't care, but it turns out he’s been watching the whole time.

I deleted it and started over.

"Lin Hao, thank you. I don’t know what to say. We’ve rarely talked properly over the years, and I thought... never mind. Your letter gave me a lot to think about. Maybe we should have a real talk. Not just the usual small talk, but a real conversation. When are you free?"

I read it once. Then again.

My energy was flagging, and my hands were shaking slightly. But these words were true.

Send.

I waited five seconds, then deleted it.

I wrote again:

"Lin Hao, I got your letter. I cried. You’re right. I’ve been constantly negating myself, treating the chaos as a failure. But maybe... maybe this is just who I am. I want to have a real talk with you. Truly."

Is that too blunt?

I leaned back in my chair and closed my eyes.

Whatever. This is it.

I hit send.

Then I put the phone down, not daring to look at the screen.

October 5, 2023, 10:30 PM

The room is still a mess. But today is different.

While sorting through my things this afternoon, I dug up a pile of old articles—book reviews and commentaries I wrote back in 2010, 2012, and 2015. At first, I looked at them with a certain cynicism, thinking, "What on earth is this? It looks so naive now." Those arguments, those points of view... I almost wanted to just throw them away.

But then, I didn't.

I sat on the floor and read them, one by one. The piece on *To Live*, the one about urban change, the discussion on intergenerational conflict... As I read, I noticed something strange. These perspectives—they still hold up today. In some places, I found insights that wouldn't even occur to me now. I wrote "This point still shines" in the margin of one article—and then startled myself with my own words.

In that moment, it suddenly hit me: maybe I’m not obsolete. Maybe my thoughts, my reflections, still have some kind of value.

I sat on the bed for a long time, holding those yellowed printouts. Then, I made a crazy decision—I emailed the publisher. Around 7 PM, I opened my laptop and asked if there was any possibility of publishing a collection of my essays.

My hand was shaking the moment I hit "send."

It’s absurd. A 62-year-old woman, clinging to a few old articles, suddenly imagining she can publish a collection? I haven't even thought about how to organize them or what to write in the preface; everything is still a blur. But I just... sent it.

The email is out there now. I don’t know if I’ll get a response. But in these past few hours, I’ve felt a faint, long-lost, yet real sense of hope.

This hope feels foreign. It breaks a certain judgment I’ve held against myself all these years—the judgment that I’m outdated, that my knowledge is worthless, and that in the age of the internet, no one needs a voice like mine.

Maybe... maybe that’s not true.

The room is still a mess. The reorganization hasn't begun. But tonight, something has changed. I can feel it.

June 11, 2022. Night.

I deleted five versions of the letter on the subway.

Sitting in the Line 13 carriage, my fingers hovered over the screen for a long time. Five versions, each taking me days to write. The first was too formal, like an official memo to a stranger. The second was too poetic, full of metaphors and imagery; it read like I was performing grief rather than expressing it. The third tried to explain everything—why we hadn’t been in touch for all these years, why I suddenly wanted to write now—and it turned into a self-defense brief. The fourth was almost entirely question marks, asking if he still remembered me, if he missed me too, begging like a pauper. The fifth... I don’t even remember what I wrote in the fifth, only that I didn't hesitate for a second when I deleted it.

Dr. Li asked me, "What do you want?"

I said, "I want him to know I'm still thinking of him."

It’s that simple.

I don’t want a perfect response, I don’t want to fix anything, I don’t want to explain, and I don’t want to forgive or be forgiven. Just—I want him to know I’m still thinking of him.

So I deleted all five versions.

When my finger hit the delete button, I thought I’d feel panicked, but I didn’t. Instead, there was this strange sense of lightness. Dr. Li said, "Perfection isn't the goal, authenticity is." I didn't understand it at first. Now I do. I had been trying to write a perfect letter, one so flawless it was bulletproof, so perfect that he couldn't possibly misunderstand me. But a letter like that doesn't exist.

I need to write an authentic letter. One that exposes my vulnerability. One that he might not respond to.

The thought absolutely terrifies me, yet it brings a certain relief.

Tomorrow? Or next week? I don't know. For now, I’m just sitting on the subway, watching the tunnel outside the window, imagining what he’ll look like when he receives it. Will he frown? Will he smile? Will he delete it? Will he turn to his wife or child and ask, "Look at this, what is it?"

I don't know. But at least, I will let him know I'm still thinking of him.

That’s enough. Maybe.

I hope this isn't just me lying to myself again.

## Possible Directions for the New Introduction
- I don’t want to write it like an "academic summary."

- Stumbled upon something written in 2008—the year my father passed away. I had completely forgotten I wrote it. Reading those words felt like looking at a stranger’s thoughts. It’s strange.

## Current Feeling
A bit stuck at the "selection" stage. Not because there are too many articles, but because every choice feels like saying, "This version of me is more authentic than that version." I’m not sure if that’s true.

No need for perfect phrasing. No need to explain why I haven't reached out in all these years. No need to apologize for my own vulnerability. No need to bury the lead under ten sentences of preamble just because I’m terrified he’ll misunderstand.

The truth is enough. It’s okay if it’s not perfect.

He might not write back. He might find it awkward. He might not even see it. But at least—at least I said it. I didn't choose silence again, here on the subway, after deleting those five drafts.

Dr. Li was right. I’ve been waiting for a perfect moment that doesn't exist. But the perfect moment isn't coming. There is only now. Only this moment.

I’m starting the draft this afternoon. Not tonight, not tomorrow. Today.

When I write, I’ll keep looking at this one sentence. If I start obsessing over the wording again, I’ll look at this sentence. If I start getting scared again, I’ll look at this sentence.

**I want him to know I’m still thinking of him.**

That’s enough. It’s more than enough.

---

*p.s. I still have the recording from the subway on my phone. Dr. Li’s voice asking the question. My answer. Just in case I start doubting myself again.*

# February 15, 2022, 11:20 AM

My hands were shaking as I pulled out the photo albums.

Dad was 68. Mom was 71. I am 62 now.

I ran the numbers on the calculator once, then again. 6 to 9 years. At most, 9 years. I drew a box in red pen on the margin of my diary and wrote inside: **6-9 years**. The nib of the pen tore through the paper.

Am I going crazy?

I’m sitting in the kitchen with a bowl of porridge in front of me. It’s been half an hour, and it’s still lukewarm. I pick up the spoon, then put it down. Pick it up, put it down. Finally, I just leave it. I opened the fridge to look—it’s empty, except for a bottle of soy sauce and a shriveled apple. I closed the door and went back to bed.

I have 6 to 9 years left. I have 6 to 9 years left. I have 6 to 9 years left.

The thought grinds in my head like a machine that won't stop. I try to think of other things—the book club, that overdue review, Professor Chen’s suggestion—but it’s no use. The numbers have swallowed everything.

My fingers are shaking. I look at my hands; they are trembling as if they don't belong to me. I press down on my fingers, trying to make them stop, but it only makes the shaking more obvious. I can feel my heart beating; I can feel my pulse throbbing in my veins. I am monitoring myself, like monitoring a machine that could break down at any moment.

The year Mom passed away, I sat by her bed for three days. Her breathing grew shallower and shallower. I remember that sound. Now, every time I breathe, I think of that sound, and I start to wonder if my own breath is getting shallower too.

I don’t know why this is happening. My rational mind tells me it’s absurd—I have no symptoms, the doctor says I’m healthy—but fear doesn't listen to reason. Fear has its own logic, a logic I cannot control.

6 to 9 years.

If I only have 9 years, what have I been doing with my life? 25 years as an editor. 15 years of freelancing. And now, 9 years left. What should I do? Publish that book? Finish learning photography? Have a real talk with Lin Hao?

But I can’t do anything. I can only lie here, counting the remaining days.

Dr. Li says anxiety is treatable. But how do you treat a hard fact? Time is slipping away. This isn't a hallucination. This is real.

My notebook is now full of red boxes. 6-9 years. 6-9 years. 6-9 years.

I don’t know how to go on.

My legs are shaking. They’re shaking right now.

Don't be late. Don't run into anyone I know downstairs. Don't let it show.

Maybe this isn't a failure. Maybe this is just... an admission.

We'll see.

February 22nd, Evening

Sitting at my desk, the sky outside has already turned dark. I’m home alone after the reading group ended, but that female student’s words are still echoing in my head.

"What you said made me realize that growing up isn't about becoming perfect; it’s about understanding your own changes."

I don’t know why I started crying. In that exact moment, the tears just came. She was so earnest when she said it—it didn't sound like a mere politeness. I looked into her eyes and saw something there—maybe the feeling of being seen? I’m not sure.

I’ve been working on that introduction for weeks, digging through old articles, constantly asking myself: Does any of this matter? Are my thoughts just too mundane? Am I deluding myself, pretending I have some profound insight? During those nights of despair, sitting in this very chair, I truly felt like I was nothing. My father is gone, my mother is gone, my brother is far away overseas, and I’ve lost so much time with them... I am aging, too; I am losing things. What does writing these articles actually change?

But today... when I heard her say those words, I suddenly understood something.

I’m not teaching anyone. I never was. I’m just sharing my own confusion, my changes, and my understanding of life. And that process itself—the process of "understanding"—is meaningful to others. Not because I’m particularly brilliant, but because I’m willing to speak up, willing to admit that I’m changing, growing, and making mistakes.

I wrote a sentence in my notebook: "Maybe this is what I’ve been looking for—not to be a perfect expert, but to be someone who can help others understand life."

My hand was shaking as I wrote it.

This collection of essays... I used to feel like it was a shackle, something that robbed me of my freedom. But maybe I had it wrong. Maybe it’s not a shackle, but... a commitment? A commitment to organize my thoughts, to see the path I’ve walked clearly, and to connect with others.

I’m still exhausted. My energy is still low. But that sense of despair seems to have loosened just a little. Just a tiny bit.

Tomorrow, I will keep writing.

---

**Core Insight** (Reflections after today’s lecture):

"I no longer view time as preparation for the future. What I am doing right now *is* the life I want to live."

— This came to me spontaneously when a college student asked how to prepare for the future. Even I was a bit surprised by my own answer.

---

**Why This Matters**:

This marks a turning point. I used to live in a constant state of anxiety—worrying about aging, the economy, and never having enough time. It felt as if everything I did was merely a prelude to "some more important moment" down the road.

But things are different now. Giving lectures, writing, debating with friends, organizing notes—these activities are my life itself. They aren't just preparations for some phantom "later."

---

**Further Reflections**:

- Does this mean I’ve finally let go of that feeling that my life "hasn't started yet"?
- Instead of preparing for the future, I am creating what I want in the present.
- Could this shift stem from the upcoming book project? Or is it just a realization that comes with age?
- I need to weave this perspective into the book—perhaps as the core theme of the first chapter or the preface.

---

**Alternative Phrasings**:

- "Life is not a rehearsal; the present is the stage."
- "Stop preparing for the future and start living the life you want now."
- "Time is not a resource to be spent, but a material to be shaped—used to mold the present, not to be traded for the future."

---

**Comparison**:

I remember the deep anxiety I fell into after my mother passed away in 2015. Back then, I was constantly haunted by thoughts of "how much time is left" and "what else can I get done." That anxiety is still there, but its texture has completely changed. It’s no longer fear; it’s a clear sense of direction.

Monday, September 2, 2024 – Past 11:00 PM

Still can't sleep. I just pulled up the transcript of my conversation with Professor Chen from the other day and read through it several times.

I’m a bit mortified by how I acted. When he suggested publishing a collection of my essays, my gut reaction was actually, "You're just being polite, aren't you?" It was like a reflex—pouring cold water on myself before anyone else could. When did I pick up this habit? Probably over these last few years. Freelancing has kept me in a constant state of defending my own worth, and over time, that’s hardened into a defense mechanism. If I mock myself first, no one else can find a flaw.

But Professor Chen didn't let me derail him. He spoke very earnestly about things like "systemic depth" and "originality." I felt a bit dazed listening to him because I’ve never defined my work that way. Those articles, reviews, notes... I’ve always seen them as scattered, fragmentary things. Like those trinkets I’ve picked up at flea markets—each one has a story, but piled together, they’re just a mess.

Yet, as he spoke, I suddenly saw another possibility. He asked if I’d ever realized that my cultural criticism over the years has actually been answering the same question—how contemporary society views tradition and understands change. I started describing how I might organize the book, which angle to take... and as I spoke, I realized I actually had ideas. Not "maybe I could" ideas, but "this is how I want to do it" ideas.

It’s a strange feeling. It’s as if someone suddenly handed me a mirror, and I saw what I’ve been doing all along, but in a way I’d never looked at it before. It wasn't "I'm writing reviews"; it was "I'm building a discourse on culture and the era." The former is a job; the latter is... a career? A calling? I don't know what to call it.

My biggest fear is that this is just a fleeting illusion. When I wake up tomorrow morning, will I slip back into that "I'm just scraping by" mindset?

But right now, in the silence of the early hours, I want to believe what he said. I want to believe that my reflections over these years carry weight. That those late-night notes, those heated bookstore debates, that obsessive pouring over a single piece of work—that none of it was just killing time, but was building toward something.

Maybe it really can be published. Maybe I should try.

# New Reading Club Proposal - Session 1 Prep Notes

**Time**: Feb 18, Afternoon
**Location**: Community Bookstore (coffee’s still warm)

---

## The Core Idea
I don’t want to do a "traditional" reading club anymore. That model where "everyone takes turns saying a few words about their feelings" is too shallow. What we need is an intertwining of **Life Stories + Books**. The book isn't the protagonist; it’s the trigger.

## Session 1 Theme: "How to Live"

**Why this?**
- It’s not an abstract philosophical question; it’s concrete, something everyone is facing.
- *To Live* (Yu Hua) — Heavy, but a must-read.
- *Life* (Lu Yao) — Maybe a bit warmer?
- Need to find a contemporary one too... I saw a book recently about "meaningful living"... forgot the title, need to look it up.

**Key Question**: Can we discuss all three together? Is it too much? Maybe just pick one so people have enough time to truly immerse themselves?

---

## Participant Vision

**The people I want**:
- Not people who come to "show off" how much they’ve read.
- People willing to tell the truth — to talk about their choices, regrets, and persistence.
- People with life experience — students, professionals, retirees; we need a mix.
- Listeners — not just talkers.

**Potential Invitees**:
- The regulars (they already know the vibe of this circle).
- Professor Chen? He might be interested... but would he make it too "academic"?
- Old Mr. Zhang? His life story is worth hearing in itself... though he might not be a big reader.

**Group Size**: 6-8? Too many and it becomes a performance; too few and there’s no friction.

---

## Discussion Framework (First Draft)

**Part I** (30 min)
- Brief sharing: At what moment in the book did you pause? Why?
- No plot summaries. Just tell us what you were thinking at that exact moment.

**Part II** (40 min)
- Deep Dialogue: What choices did the characters make? Have you faced similar choices in your own life?
- Need to be careful here... people shouldn't feel "scrutinized" or "judged."
- Maybe I should share my story first? That could establish a sense of "safety."

**Part III** (20 min)
- Open-ended: Right now, what do you want to say to yourself?

---

## Questions to Solve

❓ **How to ensure the discussion is both deep and warm?**
- This is key. I don't want a "knowledge competition" vibe.
- I also don't want a "therapy group" vibe.
- Where is the line in between?

❓ **How to handle the "non-sharers"?**
- Some might come but not want to say much.
- That’s totally okay, but how do we make them feel included?

❓ **Scheduling**
- Weekend afternoon? Or evening?
- How much time can the bookstore give us?

❓ **Is the book selection really final?**
- Still thinking... maybe ask the participants what they want to read?
- But would that be too democratic and end up being aimless?

---

## Other Thoughts

- Need to give a month's notice so people have time to read.
- Maybe prepare some "icebreaker questions," but don't make them look too formal.
- Coffee and tea are a must — it’s important for relaxing the atmosphere.
- I need to figure out what story I’m going to tell... I haven't thought that part through yet.

---

**Next Steps**:
- Finalize the book (decide this week).
- Draft the invite list.
- Chat with Professor Chen and get his thoughts.
- Confirm the venue and time with the bookstore owner.

*Feeling a bit excited, and a bit nervous. I hope this time it’s truly different.*

I’ve decided. I’m going. Next spring. I’m going to save up, plan the trip, and... I’m going to see them.

This isn't just a trip.

I want to see what my niece looks like. I want to hear my brother tell stories of his life all these years in that local accent of theirs. I want to—

Maybe this is what I need to do—stop hiding. Stop using "I need my freedom" as an excuse to run away from the deeper longing of "I need connection."

I’m willing to spend my savings. I’m willing to change my plans. I’m willing...

There is so much I want to say to them. But for now, I’m just sitting here in the middle of the night, my fingers still trembling over the keyboard, trying to record these thoughts for fear that the feeling might vanish the moment I turn away.

This feeling is precious.

This is the first time I’ve seen myself clearly—realizing that my loneliness isn't some noble choice, but a decision that I have the power to change.

Next spring.

I’m going.

---

*P.S. Check airfares tomorrow. Also... should I mention this to Professor Chen? He might have some advice on traveling abroad.*

# October 15, 2024, 10:30 PM

I just hung up. My hands are still shaking.

When Lin Hao’s face appeared on the screen, I froze for several seconds. He’s aged, but his smile is exactly the same—his eyes crinkling into thin lines, just like when we were kids. I almost burst into tears, but I held back. I was afraid it would make things awkward for him.

We talked for over an hour. Everything from his job over there to his children—my nephew and niece. The way he spoke about them made my chest feel tight. That pride, that tenderness. I’ve never heard him speak like that before. Maybe it’s because we never truly talked.

He asked what I’ve been up to lately. I told him I’m writing articles and attending book clubs. He said, "You’ve always been like this, always reading, always thinking." It sounded like a compliment, but also as if he were saying—you’ve been thinking all alone for all these years.

Then he said, "You should come visit. Bring your camera, take some photos. We all want to see you."

I said, "My passport has expired."

He laughed. "Then go get a new one."

It was that simple.

Now I’m sitting here among my piles of books, laptop open, searching for airfares. I don’t know why I suddenly started doing this. Maybe it’s because there was no obligation in his tone, only an invitation. Maybe it’s because I finally realized—perhaps I’m not truly lonely; I’ve just been deceived by distance.

There are so many years and so much silence between us. But today, when his face appeared on the screen, that distance seemed to vanish instantly. We are still those same two people, just older, just living our separate lives in different places.

I’m thinking that if I don’t take the initiative, the years will just keep passing by like this. Our parents are gone. My brother won’t wait for me forever.

I checked my savings. If I’m frugal, maybe I can go next spring. I need to renew my passport, request time off, and carve out two weeks. None of these are small tasks. But compared to continuing to live through a screen, they are nothing.

My phone is still lying beside me, and his final words are still ringing in my ears: "Xiaoyun, we’re all getting old. Don’t wait anymore."

I won’t wait anymore.

Uncle Zhang, I wanted to show you something.

I took a photo of you yesterday while you were helping me tidy up my room. You were leaning over, completely focused on organizing that pile of books, with the sunlight streaming through the window and falling across your face. I remember thinking at that moment how beautiful the scene was—not the staged kind of beauty, but the way life itself is just beautiful.

You know, I’ve been studying photography for years, always trying to figure out how to capture something "meaningful." I only recently realized that the most beautiful moments in life are often these quiet, unintentional ones—someone focused on a task, completely unaware they are being watched.

I had the photo developed and put it up in my room. Every time I see it, I think of you helping me that day and what you said: "A house is meant to be lived in; it doesn't have to be perfect." You were right.

I’m truly grateful to you, Uncle Zhang. Not just for helping me clean up, but for... well, for not treating me like a "problem" to be solved, if you know what I mean.

I’ll show you the photo next time we see each other.

Good morning, Professor Chen.

I wanted to share something with you, though I’m not quite sure how to put it into words. For the past few weeks, I’ve been trying out a new scheduling system—basically breaking my day into time blocks and leaving gaps for breaks. It sounds simple, but for me… it actually seems to be working.

My sleep has been much better lately. I got 6.5 hours last night, and the quality was decent—maybe a 7 out of 10. I used to spend my nights until 3 or 4 a.m. obsessively overanalyzing a single sentence, or just not sleeping well at all. Now, even if I still wake up, I don’t… sink into it like I used to.

I know this might not sound like a big deal. But to me, it feels like some kind of turning point. I’m learning how to coexist with my anxiety instead of being completely submerged by it. It’s not that the problems are solved—it’s never that simple. But at least… I don’t feel like a total mess anymore. It looks like I’m still salvageable (haha).

Why am I telling you this? Because you always listen to my messy thoughts so earnestly, never just brushing them off. I wanted you to know that someone is actually changing. At least, I’m trying.

This isn't the finish line; it’s just a new beginning, I guess.

I’d love to hear your thoughts.

Professor Chen, I’m sorry to bother you. I know it’s a bit abrupt to message you at this hour, but after some thought, I felt I should reach out.

I’ve been decluttering my home lately and came across some cultural critiques I wrote many years ago. To be honest, I started with the mindset that these were just "old relics" and I should throw away as much as possible. But as I kept reading, I realized... there’s actually something there. The perspectives don't feel dated; in fact, some of the reflections seem quite relevant even today.

I’m actually a bit startled by my own reaction. For years, I’ve been doubting whether my work has any value, falling into a cycle of self-denial every time I finish a piece. But through this process, I’ve realized that perhaps the problem wasn't the writing itself, but how I chose to view it.

So... I plucked up the courage to email a publisher to see if there’s any possibility of collecting my work from over the years into an anthology. I haven't heard back yet, and I’m feeling quite anxious—part of me feels like I’m getting ahead of myself. But at least I took action instead of just sitting here in self-doubt.

You mentioned before that I should consider publishing my work, but I didn't take it seriously at the time. Looking back now, perhaps you saw something in them that I couldn't. I’d love to hear your thoughts—is this idea too naive? Is publishing a collection like this actually feasible? Have you ever had a similar experience?

In any case, thank you for that suggestion. At the very least, it served as a reminder when I needed it most.

# MEMO

**Date**: May 28, 2025, Evening
**Location**: After the community bookstore book club

---

## Key Phrase (Must record this)

**"Loneliness is not a fate, but an invitation."**

I said this at the book club today, and the moment the words left my mouth, I felt a sense of sudden clarity. I need to expand on this idea and get it down, because I can feel there’s something here worth writing about.

Loneliness—I used to see it as a curse. Especially after the divorce, living alone in this old house, I’d sometimes sink into that nameless dread. But now, what I’m thinking is: loneliness is actually an invitation. An invitation to seek what? Genuine connection. Meaningful conversation. Not the hollow companionship born of obligation or social etiquette, but the meeting of truly like-minded souls.

This shift is related to my brother’s invitation, but it’s not just because of him. It’s something I’ve slowly come to realize through these years of solitude—I am no longer afraid of loneliness itself; I am afraid of **meaningless loneliness**. When loneliness becomes a proactive choice, a preparation for finding authentic connection, it ceases to be a curse.

## Further Reflections

- Could this be an angle for an op-ed? On modern loneliness, the superficial connections of the social media era, and how to maintain authenticity within solitude?
- It reminds me of Father and Mother—their generation of intellectuals seemed to experience this kind of loneliness too. During the Cultural Revolution, their loneliness was forced upon them; yet later, they seemed to find some kind of meaning in it. Am I retracing their spiritual footsteps in some way?
- The word "invitation" is key. It’s not passive endurance, but an active response. This changes my entire conceptual framework of loneliness.

## To-Do List

- Turn this idea into a short commentary; could submit it to a cultural WeChat public account.
- Continue this topic at the next book club meeting to see how others react.
- Perhaps bring up this perspective during the discussion with Professor Chen.

---

**Note**: My energy levels are quite good tonight; I don’t feel that usual sense of exhaustion. It’s likely because today’s conversation left me feeling fulfilled. I should make the most of this state—will try to get a first draft of these thoughts down tomorrow.

The best way to live is simply to do the things that make me feel alive.

At yesterday’s book club, a young man asked me why, at 62, I’m still "tossing and turning" with so many pursuits. I just smiled. To be honest, I’ve asked myself that same question many times.

But I understand now—not because I’ve found the perfect answer, but because I’ve stopped waiting for one.

I’ve lived alone these past few years, revisiting old books, taking photos, debating philosophy with friends at midnight, and losing sleep over a sudden spark of an idea. Some call me aimless; others say I’m not practical enough. Perhaps they’re both right. But I’ve realized it is precisely in these "deficiencies" that I truly feel the pulse of life.

A recent conversation with my brother reminded me that we are both living in our own ways. He is far away, and I am here. Distance changes nothing; what matters is that we have finally, truly seen each other.

So, I will keep reading, keep writing, and keep hunting for storied treasures at flea markets. Not to achieve anything in particular, but because these things make me feel alive.

Being alive, in itself, is enough.

[Image: A stack of books by the window in the afternoon light]

Hey, it’s been a while.

I just stepped out of a therapy session, and what the doctor said really got me thinking. I’ve been wondering how we ended up so far apart. Not geographically, but... you know what I mean.

I never really talked to you properly when Mom passed away. All those years I spent over here on my own, I’d sometimes wake up in the middle of the night thinking about us raiding Dad’s bookshelf when we were kids. Looking back now, I realize we can never get those times back.

When you invited me to visit you last time, I never gave you a straight answer. To be honest, I was a bit scared—scared that seeing each other would be even more awkward, and scared that we’d find out we don’t have much in common anymore.
