I’m seriously considering the idea. Looking at my passport, checking the time and money... it’s still a bit of a stretch, but I want to try. I want to see you, and see your kids. I want to know how you’re really doing—not the polite "how are you" kind of small talk, but I actually want to hear it from you.

This message might feel a bit out of the blue. I’m not great at expressing myself like this to you. But I feel like we can't go on like this anymore.

How have you been lately?

Professor Chen, I hope you don’t mind me reaching out at this hour. I’ve been wrestling with something lately and felt you might be able to help me clear my head.

The feedback from the reading groups and lectures over the past two months has been great, but to be honest, I’m starting to feel trapped by my own commitments. Between the monthly reading groups, column deadlines, and the essay collection you mentioned... I’ve realized I’m juggling too much at once, and my efficiency is actually suffering.

I’ve laid out three options: first, keep doing everything but accept that the quality will fluctuate; second, pause the column to focus on the reading group and the book; or third, scale the reading group back to once every two months. But truth be told, I’m reluctant to let go of any of them—each one feels meaningful in its own way.

As a professor, I’m sure you’ve faced similar dilemmas. I’m curious, how do you balance these kinds of commitments? Or rather, how do you determine which ones are truly worth the investment?

I think I’m being a bit greedy, trying to do everything at once. But perhaps that is exactly where my problem lies.

Professor Chen, I hope you don’t mind me bothering you so late. I was sorting through some things and you came to mind; I’d love to hear your thoughts.

I just dug up a pile of old diaries and photos, and I ended up just sitting on the floor, going through them one by one. You know, I’ve never systematically looked back at my life choices. It’s always been fragmented—one decision after another, without ever asking "why."

When I got to the entries from 2007, I paused for a long time. I wrote in my diary: "Leaving for the sake of freedom, but at what cost?" I didn't write down an answer then, and I’m still asking it now.

All these years, I’ve been defending this choice of "freedom," haven’t I? Not being tied down, being able to do what I want. But tonight, I suddenly realized that I’ve never truly admitted it—this choice did come with a price. Financial instability, professional uncertainty, even a certain sense of isolation. I’m not saying I regret it, it’s just that... I’m finally allowing myself to see the full picture.

When you suggested I publish my work, my initial reaction was resistance. I felt like I was being "defined," being boxed into expectations. But now I’m wondering if it’s not about market value or professional achievement at all. Maybe the real question is: where does the value of everything I’ve accumulated over the years—those ideas, perspectives, and my understanding of books and culture—actually lie?

It’s not about proving anything. It’s just... I want to know if a person’s knowledge and reflections are truly complete if they only ever exist in scattered articles and late-night conversations.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this? Or is this a genuine question?

Professor Chen, I wanted to ask you something.

A few friends came over this afternoon, and while we were chatting, they noticed those loose articles and notes on my bookshelf. They said, "You really ought to get these down on record." I didn't think much of it at the time, but later, sitting there by myself, I suddenly remembered what you said before—about publishing a collection of papers.

To be honest, my initial reaction was a bit resistant. You know me; I’ve always felt that my writing isn't systematic enough—just a bunch of fragmented thoughts. But hearing them say that today made me wonder: maybe... maybe there’s value in those fragments themselves?

I’ve been thinking, what is the real purpose of a personal collection or a memoir? Does it have to be strictly academic and rigorous, or can it simply be a record of one person’s reflections on various issues at different stages of life? I’m not quite sure.

Were you being serious back then? Or was it just a polite word of encouragement? I need to hear your honest thoughts. If there is genuine meaning in this idea, I might... I might actually give it serious consideration.

It feels a bit strange, though—that I’m only starting to think about these things at my age.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something with you about tonight.

I just finished up at the community bookstore’s sharing corner. Now, I’m sitting in a cafe, having tea by myself, and I suddenly feel a bit lost. Do you know that feeling? The event went well—about twenty people showed up, and the discussion was quite lively—but now that it’s all over, I’ve fallen into this strange sense of stillness.

The most interesting part was when a participant asked me, "Why do you do all this? Why organize book clubs and write columns when there’s no pay?" My first instinct was to say, "To publish a book" or "To build some kind of influence"—the things I used to say. But as the words reached my lips, I suddenly realized they weren't true. What I ended up saying was, "Because I want to share these stories."

It was that simple.

The moment I said it, I felt something I’ve never felt before—not excitement, not a sense of achievement, just... a pure sense of "rightness." Like I had finally found the correct answer. My whole body just relaxed.

But that’s the problem.

This sense of peace is so fragile. Sitting here now, I’ve started thinking: What if fewer people show up next time? What if someone criticizes me? What if no one reads what I write? I’ll start doubting whether any of this is actually meaningful again. I can feel that familiar anxiety lurking in the corner, as if it never really left.

I know it sounds contradictory. It’s like I’ve found the answer, yet I haven't truly found it. The drive of "wanting to share" is real, but why am I still so easily shaken by data, feedback, and external validation?

I wanted to ask you—how can I make this peace more stable? Or rather, will I always be like this? Will I always be oscillating between affirmation and doubt?

By the way, you mentioned before that you wanted me to observe my own patterns. I see it now—I seem to have a constant need to re-confirm that what I’m doing is meaningful. Is this just part of my sensitivity? Or is there something else I’m not seeing clearly yet?

Lin Xiaoyun

# Phone Memo

**January 25, 2025, 3:00 PM**

---

Dr. Li said "pause."

She said it very gently, but I heard it—she thinks I’m breaking down. Maybe I am.

Pause. Stop. And then what?

I walked for a long time. The walk from the clinic to my house usually takes twenty minutes, but it took me nearly an hour. I passed that old bookstore and thought about going in, but I didn't. I passed the cafe and saw someone writing; their pen was moving so fast across the paper. I just stood outside the window and watched for a while.

Why am I always like this?

---

**Regarding the project:**

- Reread that 2015 piece, "The Roots of Contemporary Cultural Anxiety." My god. How was I ever that confident? The views are too absolute, the arguments insufficient. Looking back now, it’s just a mediocre long-form op-ed.
- Then there’s the 2018 one about urban memory. At the time, I thought it was profound. And now? Still mediocre. Just some pretty words.
- If these are all mediocre, what’s the point of publishing them?

---

**Dr. Li’s words are circling in my head:**

"You don’t have to decide right now. Give yourself some space."

But I’m afraid. I’m afraid that if I stop, I’ll never be able to go back. Just like when I left the publishing house in 2007—I thought I was chasing freedom, but what happened? I’ve been drifting all these years. No direction. No security.

With this project, at least there’s a deadline, at least there’s an editor waiting for me. If I give this up, I’ll have nothing.

---

**But—**

Maybe I never had anything to begin with?

These articles, these ideas, everything I’ve accumulated over the years... do they actually have any value? Or have I just been lying to myself, pretending I’m doing something meaningful?

---

**Time is slipping away.**

I’m 62 now. Looking at my past writing feels like looking at something written by a stranger. Who was that person? What was she thinking? Where is she now?

Maybe she’s dead. Maybe we’re all in the process of dying, just at different speeds.

---

**Need to email Professor Chen tomorrow.**

He’s asking about the progress of the essay collection again. What should I say? That I’m questioning everything? That I might give up?

Never mind. I won't reply yet.

---

**To-do list:**

- ~~Organize articles~~ (Don't know if I should bother anymore)
- ~~Write the preface~~ (Don't know if a preface is even necessary)
- Think through Dr. Li’s suggestion (But I can't think straight)
- Decide whether to continue or stop this project (I can't make this decision)

---

Will I be unable to sleep again at 3 AM?

Probably.

Dr. Li, I’m sorry to bother you so late. I know you’re probably already asleep.

I’m sitting in bed right now, and I haven’t slept for three hours. My brother just sent me a WeChat message, simply asking, "How have you been? It's been a while." It was just a simple sentence, but when I saw it... I don’t know how to put it, I was just suddenly terrified. I replied "I'm okay" and then closed it.

Now I’m repeatedly checking my pulse. I’ve been pressing my fingers against my neck and counting—I've done it five times now. Every time the number is different, which makes me even more scared. I know this is pointless, and I can feel that I’m doing something ridiculous, but I just can’t stop.

I keep thinking... my father passed away at 68, and my mother at 71. I’m 62 now. That number keeps spinning in my head. I’m calculating. I’m wondering how much longer I have to live. I even Googled "average life expectancy for a 62-year-old woman," and then I started reading articles about heart disease, and then I got even more frightened.

I feel like I might be going crazy. The rational part of me knows these symptoms probably mean nothing, but the other part of me just can’t believe it. I want to call my brother, but I feel like that would be too weak. We haven’t had a real conversation in such a long time. I don’t know how to start.

Could I... could I come see you this Monday? Or would that be too frequent? I really need help right now. I feel like I’m sinking, and the speed is getting faster and faster.

Sorry, I know this message is a mess. I’m just a mess right now.

# MEMO

**Date: May 18, 2023, Afternoon**

Dr. Li asked me, "Where are these thoughts coming from? Are they facts?"

I didn't answer right away. She was waiting for me.

"It’s pointless"—I said the renovation plan was pointless. But what does "pointless" even mean? Who gets to define it?
"None of my efforts will change anything"—Is that true? There were 15 people at the reading club that day. They were listening to me. Isn't that a change?

Dr. Li asked me to write these thoughts down. So, here they are.

---

**Question List (or rather, the questions Dr. Li left me with):**

1. The renovation plan failed, but what does failure mean? Does it mean I have nothing left, or does it mean I need to adjust the plan?

2. When I say "it’s pointless," what am I actually saying? Am I saying the action itself has no meaning, or am I saying I feel I’m not worthy of taking action?

3. The reading club, photography, these bits and pieces—why do I feel they are important one moment, and then feel they are all just self-deception the next?

4. If value isn't defined by results, then what defines it? Do I actually believe that, or am I just trying to comfort myself?

---

**Fragmented Thoughts:**

- Old Mr. Zhang said, "Tweak the plan and try again." Was he being serious? Or was he just trying to console me?
- Professor Chen mentioned publishing a collection of papers. I never thought about that. Or rather, I thought about it but dismissed it immediately. Why?
- My photographs are there. They exist. No matter how much I deny them, they exist.
- Someone was taking notes at the reading club. I saw it. That young girl, she was writing down what I said.

---

**But:**

I still feel exhausted. Not just physically. It’s that... indeterminate exhaustion. Like walking in the dark, unsure of what lies ahead with every step.

Maybe I need to learn to ask "Why not?" instead of "Why bother?"

Professor Chen,

I wanted to share some thoughts with you following our meeting earlier.

Sitting here on the subway home, I’ve been turning your words over and over in my mind: "Add your own voice." To be honest, hearing that made me a little nervous. As you know, I’ve spent so many years in the academic world that I’ve grown accustomed to hiding myself behind footnotes and citations. Anything personal? That always felt somehow "unprofessional."

But you’re right. I realize now that what I’ve been doing is a form of evasion—hiding behind academic armor and pretending that objectivity is synonymous with sincerity. Yet, looking back at what I’ve written over the years, the passages that truly move me, the ones I want to read again and again, are never the most "academic" ones. Instead, they are the observations where I accidentally let my guard down, where a bit of my own color shows through.

For the preface this time, I want to try something different. Not by abandoning academic rigor, but by allowing myself to breathe within it. I want to talk about why these essays matter to me, and how, over these past twenty-odd years, I have come to understand the world through reading and reflection. I want to talk about how it feels, as a sixty-two-year-old woman, to see my own ideas actually help others.

I don’t know what the final result will look like. It might seem insufficiently detached; it might feel too personal in places. But I want to try. I want to see what I am capable of writing when I stop apologizing for my own existence.

Xiaoyun

February 24, 3:00 PM

Back at the desk again. The notebook is still there; my fingers trembled a little when I opened it.

I tried writing the opening for "The City and Solitude," just as Dr. Li suggested last time—"Don't wait for perfection, just start with the imperfect." But by the third paragraph, I stopped. It wasn't for a lack of ideas, but... how should I put it? I just didn't have the strength to go on. The words were in my head, but the distance from my brain to my fingers felt like a vast river.

I didn't throw the notebook back into the corner in a fit of rage like I used to. I didn't delete those three paragraphs either. I just left them there, right in the most prominent spot on the desk. Then I found a sticky note, scribbled the word "Maybe" on it, and stuck it on the cover.

Maybe what? Maybe I can continue tomorrow? Maybe this isn't a total failure? Maybe I haven't completely rotted away yet?

I don't even know myself.

Sitting here staring at that "Maybe," I wonder if I'm just lying to myself again. Just like I’ve done all these years—lying to myself that choosing freedom was courage, lying to myself that leaving the publishing house was a pursuit, and now lying to myself that "Maybe" actually means something.

But... I didn't throw it away.

That’s the most ironic part. If I were truly desperate, truly finished, I should be able to make a clean decision. Either keep writing or give up entirely. But instead, I do this—neither continuing nor quitting, just leaving it there, using a "Maybe" to trick myself into thinking there's still a possibility.

The afternoon light outside the window is cold. I can hear the sound of old Mr. Zhang downstairs hammering at something. Life goes on; everyone’s life goes on. Only I am sitting here, staring at two syllables on a sticky note.

I don't know if this counts as progress.

Maybe this is all I’m capable of right now. Not breaking down, but not truly living either. Just suspended somewhere in the middle, propping myself up with a "Maybe."

I sat on the sofa, watching my phone screen light up and dim again. The appointment card was right there on the coffee table, with "Next Wednesday, 3:00 PM" scribbled in blue ink.

November 13, 2024, 8:45 PM

I just closed the budget spreadsheet. My eyes are a bit sore after staring at numbers all afternoon.

There isn't much to look at, really. It’s just simple arithmetic: 8,000 for airfare, 3,000 for accommodation, 2,000 for food and transport... roughly 15,000 in total. My current savings can cover it, but if I spend it this way, my safety net will become very thin. Very thin.

This afternoon, I discovered that flight prices have jumped 30% compared to two weeks ago. My immediate reaction was—forget it. It’s not realistic. What am I doing, still chasing these impractical dreams at 62? I even opened the exit page.

But I didn't close the tab. I just let it sit there.

Then I did something strange. I took a deep breath. Truly, just like Dr. Li taught me. And then I thought: if I don’t go, what will I regret? It’s not the money—it’s that, once again, I would be giving up out of fear. Fear of not being safe enough, fear of losing control, fear of...

I don't know how to put it. Just that old habit.

I called my brother. It was brief; I just said one thing: "I'm looking at flights." He laughed on the other end. He said, "Little sister, you’re finally coming."

And so... I went back to the budget.

The funny thing is, I found myself thinking not "Can I afford this?" but "Which month should I leave?" When did that shift happen? Have I actually made the decision?

Maybe it was the moment I spoke to him. Or even earlier. Perhaps the moment I started writing down these numbers, I already knew.

I used to think freedom meant having no constraints. No full-time job, no marriage, no commitments. But now I understand—that wasn’t freedom; that was avoidance. Real freedom is knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the price for it. Even if that price includes losing some sense of security.

I want to see my brother. I want to see his family. I want to walk through their city. I want to see how much time I have left to do these things.

So, 15,000 yuan... it’s actually not that expensive.

I’ll keep saving tomorrow. Buy fewer books. This month’s writing commission should come in on time.

I am making a real decision. It feels unfamiliar, and also... right.

Dr. Li, sorry to bother you so late. I know you might have already turned in for the night.

I was wondering if we could reschedule our session for next week. My state of mind has been pretty poor these last few days, and the insomnia is back. Last night, I didn't fall asleep until 3:00 AM, and then I was wide awake by five.

It’s nothing major, really. It’s just... lately I’ve been dwelling on these meaningless questions. About choices, about time. That question you asked me last time—I’ve been chewing on it over and over. "If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?" I realized I simply can’t answer it.

I stood in front of the mirror for a long time, just looking at myself. It felt a bit strange. Like I was looking at a stranger.

Anyway, maybe I’m just exhausted lately. Things at work are a bit of a mess, and I’m trying to recalibrate. Perhaps we could meet this Friday instead? Or we can stick to the original plan; that works too.

You don’t need to reply. I just... needed to say it out loud.

Goodnight.

I just stepped out of Dr. Li’s clinic, and I’m still chewing over what she said.

You know, I always thought anxiety was just anxiety—there was no point in distinguishing between different types. Tossing and turning at 3:00 AM, obsessively checking passport procedures, imagining every possible way a flight could be delayed… I just threw it all into one basket and figured I was just a high-strung person, and that was that.

But today, Dr. Li mentioned something I’d never considered before. She talked about the difference between "healthy preparatory anxiety" and "uncontrolled catastrophic thinking." They might look the same on the surface, but they’re actually completely different.

The former is when you’re doing something important (like going abroad to see your brother), and your body and brain are trying to help you prepare. So you think a few steps ahead and double-check things. That has a purpose. The latter is the real problem—that infinite loop of "what if" traps that ends up paralyzing you with fear.

As I was listening, it suddenly hit me: even though I’ve been researching and feeling nervous this time, I’m actually *doing* things. I’m applying for my passport, looking at flights, and confirming dates with my brother. I’m not just lying in bed spiraling over worst-case scenarios. That feels… different?

The most amazing thing is that once I could put this distinction into words, the anxiety became less terrifying. It’s like I can finally see its shape instead of being drowned by it.

I don’t know if this counts as progress, but it feels like I can finally have a conversation with my anxiety instead of being held hostage by it.

Professor Chen, I wanted to talk to you about my call with the editor earlier.

She mentioned a three-month timeline, saying that other authors usually finish in two. I got a bit defensive and probably didn't use the best tone—I told her, "I’m not other authors." Looking back, I’m wondering if I overreacted.

But honestly, three months is incredibly tight for me. It’s not because I’m slow—I want to be clear about that—it’s because I need time to revisit these essays. They were written at different stages and reflect my thinking at those specific times. Bringing them out now, I need to ensure every single piece still represents what I want to say. Some parts might need rethinking; some arguments might need a new introduction to frame them properly. It’s not a matter of typing; it’s a matter of thinking.

I’m worried that I’m just making excuses for myself. Maybe I am just slow. Maybe I’m simply not capable of completing a project like this within the deadline.

You told me that publishing this book is important for me, and I believe you. But now I’m starting to ask myself—are these essays actually worth publishing? What if I spend three months, six months, or even a year on this, only to find out they don’t really have much value?

I’m not trying to back out. I just… need to know if you think this project is worth it. Even if it takes more time?

Sorry for rambling. I know how busy you are.

# Private Chat Log

**Recipient: A friend from the cultural salon (the one who understands her best)**

---

I’ve had something on my mind lately that I’ve been wanting to talk to you about.

Have you ever wondered why we’re always measuring ourselves by other people’s standards? For instance, how many views an article gets, whether it can sell, if it has "market value"... I realized I’ve been doing this for years. Measuring myself with a market yardstick.

A couple of days ago, I dug up some old reviews I wrote. They were actually quite good, but my first instinct wasn't "this is well-written"; it was "no one wants to read this anymore." How absurd is that?

Then a thought occurred to me—maybe I don’t need to be recognized by the "spirit of the age." Maybe I only need to be recognized by myself and by those who truly understand me. Maybe that’s enough.

It sounds a bit like "chicken soup for the soul" when I say it out loud, but when I actually started thinking this way, I felt this massive sense of relief. Not the "escaping reality" kind of relief, but... how should I put it? Like I’ve finally rescued myself from a pointless competition.

The things I’ve written over the years might be useless to this era. But what about to me? Those reflections, those insights into books and life—they mean something to me. This isn't just self-consolation; it’s the truth.

What about you? What’s your take on this? Have you ever felt... held hostage by this idea of "usefulness"?

---

*8:00 PM, home alone, rain starting to fall outside. Suddenly remembered what you said recently and felt like I just had to talk to you.*

Friday, November 3, 2023, past 11:00 PM

Just got back from Dr. Li’s. I haven’t even washed up yet; I’m just sitting on the sofa, holding a teacup that has already gone cold.

Today she asked me a question—so simple it couldn't be simpler, yet it felt like a needle pricking through something.

I realized that when I talk about my anxiety, I’ve started using the language of neurobiology—things like "amygdala overactivation" or "serotonin imbalance." Listening to myself talk, I sounded like I was reading a research paper. Dr. Li just looked at me and asked, "If it really is just a neurochemical imbalance, what does that change for you?"

I froze.

I truly froze. Because I suddenly realized that I’ve been using knowledge to protect myself. If anxiety is just a biological issue, then it’s not *my* issue. It’s just my brain having a glitch, rather than something being wrong with me as a person. Do you see? It’s more comfortable to think that way.

Dr. Li said, "Protection can also become imprisonment."

I didn't respond at the time, but I’m still chewing on those words now.

I think about all the choices I’ve made over the years. Was leaving the publishing house an act of freedom? Or was it an escape? I say I’m afraid of being tied down, but am I actually using the "pursuit of freedom" as an excuse to dodge responsibility? Am I using knowledge, rationality, and an obsession with independence to protect myself—to keep anyone from truly getting close?

Even for the anxiety itself, I have to find a sophisticated justification.

It’s not "I’m terrified"; it’s "My neurotransmitters might be malfunctioning."

It’s not "I’m lonely"; it’s "I require intellectual independence."

It’s not "I don’t know what value I have left"; it’s "This era no longer needs critics like me."

I’ve armed myself with knowledge the way I’ve piled books up in my room. Eventually, the room is full, but I’m still sitting in it alone.

Dr. Li didn’t say I was wrong. She just watched me, waiting for me to say it myself. And then I did. I said, "I’m using knowledge to protect myself." It sounded like a confession of some sin.

Sitting here now, the tea is cold. Outside the window, I can see the light from old Mr. Zhang’s place downstairs. Is he still awake? Is he thinking about something, too? Or did he let go of all the "whys" a long time ago?

I don’t know.

I only know that I need to stop using knowledge to answer every question. But I also don’t know what’s left of me if I put that down.

Dr. Li,

I’ve been thinking about that question you asked yesterday.

I didn't sleep well. I woke up at three in the morning and started obsessing over those words: "If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?"

To be honest, I still haven't figured out the answer. Or rather, I’m afraid I already know it, but I don’t want to admit it.

I’ve spent the last two days looking through things I’ve written over the years. I went through some archived articles, some from ten years ago. Back then, I wrote with such vigor; I felt like I was saying something important. Reading them now, some of the points are still decent, but I realized I’ve been arguing the same things over and over—as if I were constantly trying to prove something to some invisible judge.

When you asked me, "What is your own opinion of your knowledge?" I said I wasn't sure. Now I want to add to that—it’s not that I’m unsure, it’s that I’ve never actually asked myself that question. I’ve always been asking what others think of me; I’ve never asked what I think of myself.

It’s terrifying.

I’m thinking that maybe I need to turn this question around. But I don’t know how. Could you help me think through this? Not to give me an answer, but... to help me see what it is I’m actually asking.

Lin Xiaoyun

Perhaps the most important moments in life aren't when we find the answers, but when we find the courage to ask the questions.

I was up until 3:00 AM last night, restless and tapping my fingers on the desk. This morning, I’ve been checking my inbox over and over like a fool. But I’ve realized that this restlessness itself says something—it means I care, I’m taking a risk, and I’m doing something that truly matters to me.

Dr. Li once said something that has always stuck with me: "Perfection isn't the goal; being real is."

I think we’re always waiting for that perfect moment to make a change, waiting for all our fears to vanish, waiting until we feel "brave enough." But the truth is, bravery is reaching out while you're still afraid. No matter what happens next, at least I’ve done that.

Whatever the outcome, I don't want to grow old wondering "what if."

[Image: A yellowed page from an old book with handwritten pencil notes in the margins]

Professor Chen,

I just got home, and my mind is still spinning a little.

Sitting here surrounded by my books, I felt I had to send you a message. Not just a simple "thank you," but to tell you that those questions you asked—especially the last one, "If your time were limited, what would you want to do most right now?"—it was as if I suddenly heard my own true voice.

For years, I’ve been asking myself "What else can I do?", as if I were constantly retreating. But when you made me say it out loud today, I realized I’ve known the answer all along. I just didn't have the courage to believe that I was worth the cost of those answers. Do you know what I mean? Not a financial cost, but the cost of admitting what I really want, and then actually going for it.

I’ve been pacing around the house alone, thinking about what you said, and suddenly I’m not so afraid anymore. It’s not that the anxiety has vanished, but I seem to understand now that anxiety and courage can coexist. I can be nervous and determined at the same time.

This conversation meant a lot to me. Probably more than I let on at the time.

Thank you for not just listening, but for asking the questions I truly needed to be asked.

Xiaoyun

3:15 AM. I’m sitting among piles of books, my phone screen still glowing with Ms. Wang’s last message.

I don’t know when I started crying. Maybe it was when she said, "You should go see a therapist." Maybe it was earlier.

I asked her: Who am I now? She didn’t answer directly. She just said, very gently, that perhaps I should talk to a professional. As if I were something that needed to be fixed.

My knowledge is worthless. I repeated the phrase, as if saying it enough times could turn it into an absolute truth. She tried to argue, saying my work has value, but I couldn’t hear it. How could it have value? No one needs me. Not the publishers. Not the magazines. Not this era.

As I lie in bed, my mind is stuck in 2007. I remember the feeling of the day I quit—that rush of freedom, that delusion of "I can finally do what I want." Looking back now, it was just an escape. I fled from one set of shackles into another abyss.

And then there’s 2005. Zhang Ming said I was unrealistic. He was right. I chose a marriage destined for failure, just as I chose a career destined for failure. Every decision was wrong. Quitting was wrong. The divorce was wrong. Choosing to go freelance was wrong. I’ve spent over a decade proving it.

What have I accumulated in this room? Books. Souvenirs. Unfinished projects. The ruins of a 62-year-old woman.

Ms. Wang was quiet on the phone. When I said, "Maybe you’re right," I could barely hear the words myself. I don’t even know what I was agreeing to—agreeing to see a doctor? Agreeing that I really do need help? Or agreeing that I am a total failure?

It’s still dark outside. I don’t know what to do when the sun comes up. I don’t know how to face the people at the book club tomorrow. They think I’m some kind of mentor. What kind of mentor am I? A fraud. A woman who wasted her life.

I should sleep. But I know I won't.

I’m just sitting here, looking at these books, wondering why they are still here. Wondering why I am still here.

Something small happened at home today, but it’s left me thinking for a long time.

A young girl from my book club came over. When she saw my wall—cluttered with old books, antiques, photos, and all sorts of mementos—I felt a bit embarrassed and thought I really should tidy it up. But she stopped and earnestly asked me about the story behind each item.

So, I told her. I told her which flea market I’d scouted a book from, the history behind a certain ceramic shard, and the moments captured in those photos. She listened intently, her eyes shining.

Later, she said, "This isn't a mess. This is a 'story corner'."

I had never thought of it that way. For years, I’ve been dismissing these things—viewing them as symbols of my scatteredness, my lack of order, and my unfinished life. But today, watching her find meaning in this "chaos," I suddenly realized that perhaps I’ve been misdefining myself all along.

Every object was chosen for a reason. Every story deserves to be told. Not because they are perfect, but because they are real.

Sometimes, you need someone else’s eyes to see the value in what you’ve been doing all along.

**Internal Monologue**
I’ve realized I’m doing two contradictory things: frantically buying books and courses as if investing in some blurry future, while compulsively checking my bank balance to calculate how much longer I can survive.

Yesterday at the bookstore, I overheard two young girls discussing a Douyin influencer’s book review. They sounded so happy, but I felt like a stranger. The things I spent 40 years learning mean nothing to them.

Maybe I should stop buying books. Every new book feels like a promise, and I’m no longer in a position to fulfill any promises.

But I’ll probably buy another one tomorrow.

August 5, 2022. Late at night.

I’ve been sitting in the dark for two hours now. No lights on.

I looked at that blogger’s account this afternoon. 432 followers versus 2.57 million. My 3,000-word book review: 47 likes. Her 8-minute short video: 120,000. I kept swiping back and forth on my phone, staring at those numbers over and over again, as if reading a verdict.

Perhaps this is the answer of our times.

I struck that sentence out with force. But it’s still there.

Forty years. Ever since I started at the publishing house in 1982, this is what I’ve been doing—reading, thinking, writing reviews. In those years, I was as meticulous as one could possibly be with every work. I could see layers others couldn't; I could understand an author’s intent and convey it through words. I took pride in that. My editors used to say so, too.

But now?

Now, that editor says "market demands have changed." She was polite, but the meaning was clear. Young bloggers are more attractive. Fast, easy, fragmented. My "depth" is too heavy for them.

I’m not angry. I wish I were. Anger is at least a form of strength. What I feel now is a cold, irrefutable clarity—that perhaps I’ve had it wrong all along. Perhaps the "depth" I believed in isn't worth a cent in this era. Perhaps my knowledge, my taste, and everything I’ve accumulated are nothing more than an old woman’s obsession.

I am 62 this year. How long will my savings last? A year? Two? I’ve checked the data, but I only get more frightened every time I do. I start calculating repeatedly, verifying with a calculator, then deleting it all. As if the problem doesn't exist as long as I don't look at the final number.

When I got back in touch with Hao-ge two months ago, I thought things would take a turn for the better. We talked for a long time, and I felt a sense of connection I hadn't felt in ages. But that seems to have been just a brief respite. Now the pressure is back, and it’s heavier than before.

I think back to those full-time job offers I rejected in 2007. I was so certain then that I was making the right decision—pursuing freedom, refusing to be bound. And now? Now I’m starting to think maybe I should have accepted. Maybe my idealism was always impractical. Maybe I was just using the word "freedom" to justify my own cowardice.

No. I don’t know what I’m thinking anymore.

I can’t see anything in the dark. The light from my phone screen reflects on my face as I continue to scroll through those numbers. 2.57 million. 120,000. 47. 432.

What are these numbers telling me?

Maybe this is just how it is. The times have changed, and I haven't. Or rather, I’ve changed too slowly. I tried to learn video production, and it was a disaster. I don’t even know how to edit. Watching those young people’s fingers fly across their screens, I just knew—I am already too old.

Too old.

The thought echoes in the darkness.

I should sleep. I have to reply to those editors' emails tomorrow. But I know I won't be able to. I’ll lie in bed, staring at the ceiling, asking myself the same question over and over: What value do I have left?

I don’t know the answer.

Have you guys ever thought that freedom and constraint are sometimes just two sides of the same coin?

I’ve been reflecting on something lately. We often talk about pursuing freedom and escaping frameworks and expectations. But… what happens once you actually get that freedom? Those empty stretches of time, those days when no one is pushing you to do anything—sometimes, they leave you feeling even more adrift.

Maybe freedom isn't the destination, but the starting point. It’s not about what you’re escaping from, but what you’re creating.

For example… have you noticed that our conversations in this group are actually a form of creation? We’re here discussing books, ideas, and questions that have no standard answers. It’s not forced, and it’s not for any practical reward. It’s just… pure connection.

I feel like this is what freedom truly looks like—not running away, but choosing to create dialogue and create meaning.

This is a bit out of the blue, but I was digging through an old notebook tonight and saw something I wrote five or six years ago, and this just clicked. What about you guys? How do you define freedom?

# Visiting Lin Hao Abroad — Prep Checklist

**Date: May 14, Afternoon**  
**Departure Date: Mid-June (One month to go)**

---

## I. Gifts to Bring

- [ ] **Book** — *Ten Lectures on Jiangnan Culture* (Recommended by Professor Chen; it’s about regional culture, something Lin Hao might find interesting).
- [ ] **Book** — An English book on photography (The new edition of *On Photography*; I’ve been reading it lately and want to discuss it with him).
- [ ] **Tea** — Longjing from back home. Small packets, won't take up much space.
- [ ] **My Photography** — Select 5–6 of my best shots, print them, and put them in an album. The "Urban Series" from last year, plus the portraits. These are things I want to show him—not to show off, but... I want him to know what I’ve been doing all these years.

**Thoughts:** None of these are expensive, but they are all meaningful. Books, tea, photos—they are what I can offer, and they all represent a connection.

---

## II. Potential Conversation Topics

**Things to think through beforehand (not to memorize a script, but to have a sense of direction):**

- His current work and life? How long has it been since we had a deep conversation?
- Family updates (his friends and colleagues over there).
- About our parents—we’ve both experienced their passing. It might be heavy, but it’s important. How does he view these past years?
- My career changes—the ups and downs of being a freelancer. He might have a different perspective, but this time I want to share sincerely.
- Photography, reading, my recent interests—let him see what I’m *doing*, not just that I’m alive.
- **What NOT to ask:** Why have we been out of touch for so long? It makes things sound accusatory. Let the meeting itself do the talking.

**Tone Reminder:** Be sincere, but don’t over-explain. He’s my brother, not a stranger.

---

## III. Travel Logistics

- [ ] Passport check (Is it still valid?)
- [ ] Flight — Confirm times, airline, and baggage policy.
- [ ] Visa — How far in advance do I need to apply?
- [ ] Accommodation — Confirm the location with Lin Hao, or book a hotel myself?
- [ ] Insurance — Travel insurance; check the rates.
- [ ] Bank — Notify the bank I’m going abroad so my card doesn't get frozen.
- [ ] Medication — Common meds for colds, stomach issues, and insomnia (my anxiety meds).
- [ ] Clothing — Check the weather; pack accordingly.
- [ ] SIM card or international roaming — To stay in touch with Lin Hao.

**Note:** This list looks trivial, but I find that writing it down puts my mind at ease. It’s not that I’ve become more organized; it’s just so I don’t wake up at 3 a.m. thinking, "Oh god, I forgot to check my passport."

---

## IV. Mental Preparation

**To be honest, how I’m feeling right now:**

- Excited but nervous. Going abroad at 62 to see a brother I haven't spoken deeply with in years feels both foreign and necessary.
- Am I worried it will be awkward? Probably. But the regret of not going would be worse.
- I’m spending my savings on this, which makes me a bit anxious. But I also know some things are more important than money. This trip is one of those things.
- I’m learning to trust my own decisions. That’s not easy for me.

**Final Thoughts:**

This memo itself is what I’m doing—organizing messy expectations and unease into a list so I can feel like I’m in control of something. But in reality, I know the most important things can’t be listed in advance. It’s the conversation that will happen when we meet; it’s the moment the years of silence between us are finally broken.

I’ve made up my mind. Now is the time to prepare.

# Phone Memo

---

When I dug out that photo, I stared at it for a long time.

That was me in 1995. Wearing that blue sweater, standing at the entrance of the publishing house. I remember the weather was beautiful that day. I remember what I was thinking—wondering if the job would be boring, wondering if I’d meet anyone interesting.

Back then, you had no idea what was coming.

Now I’m sitting here, thirty years later, looking at that face. She looks so certain. So full of hope. I want to ask her: Was it worth it? Was all of this worth it?

—

They will leave you.

I’m only now truly understanding what my father said back in the winter of 1996. I didn’t get it then. I get it now. He was telling me about the things you cannot hold onto.

—

Re-reading those articles, I find myself wondering: Did I really write this? What was that person thinking? How could she have interpreted the work this way?

The "me" of today would write it completely differently.

So what about ten years from now? Thirty years? Will I be sitting somewhere, looking at what I’m writing now and thinking it’s absurd?

—

What is time doing? Where is it?

—

Maybe I shouldn't have dug up these old things. Maybe some things are meant to stay in cardboard boxes, stay in memory—not be brought out to be compared, measured, and judged.

But I’ve already dug them up.

Now, I can’t bear to look at that photo, yet I can’t bring myself to put it back.

February 5, 3:30 AM

Insomnia again.

I just took my blood pressure for the fifth time. 142/94. My hands are shaking—I don’t know if it’s out of fear or just the vibration of the monitor. I wrote the numbers down on a sticky note and circled them heavily in red. Why did I do that? I don’t even know. It’s as if by writing it down and circling it, I could somehow expel the number from my body.

I can feel my heart beating. Every single thud. It’s like someone is drumming inside my chest. Will I die in my sleep? I’ve heard of people who did... just gone, like that. No warning at all.

My father was still lecturing at 68. My mother was still organizing her book collection at 71. I am 62 now. I’m calculating. I’m always calculating. 68 minus 62 is 6. 71 minus 62 is 9. Do I have six years left? Or nine? Or none at all?

It’s crazy. I know it’s crazy. But I can’t stop.

I dug out my diary from 2007. What did I write back then? "I want freedom. I don't want to be tethered to an office, drowned by boring meetings and office politics. I want to explore, to write what I truly want to write." I re-read those sentences in blue ink. Then I took my red pen and wrote in the margin: "Was it all worth it? Did I waste my life?"

My pen poked several holes through the paper.

Freedom. I kept talking about freedom. But what has freedom given me? An unstable income. No health insurance. No pension. And now here I sit, at three in the morning, checking my blood pressure and calculating how much time I have left, while I don't even know if my money will last as long as I do.

Maybe I should have found a steady job. But... I can’t stand being tied down. I’m trapped in a dilemma. Caught between a rock and a hard place.

I want to call Dr. Li. But it’s three in the morning. I can’t do that. I never do things like that.

My heart is still beating. One thud. Then another. And another.

I try to take deep breaths. But I just end up counting them. That doesn't help either.

Maybe I should sleep. But I’m afraid to sleep.

Maybe I should get up. But I don’t have the strength.

So I just sit here. In the dark. At 3:00 AM. A 62-year-old woman, measuring her blood pressure, flipping through a diary from fifteen years ago, calculating how much time she has left.

This is my freedom.

Professor Chen, I’ve been thinking about our conversation ever since I got home.

To be honest, when you asked me, "What is it that you want to say?", I was a bit caught off guard. I’ve grown used to using self-deprecation as a shield—saying things like, "Who would want to read the ramblings of a 62-year-old woman?" makes me feel safer. But you didn't let me hide behind that; instead, you pushed further. It was in that moment I realized that I actually do have something to say.

"How a woman finds meaning in solitude"—even I was surprised when those words came out. I’ve decided that I want to move forward with this project. I’m still a bit nervous, but that nervousness actually makes me feel certain that this is the right thing to do.

When is that editor you mentioned available? I’d like to discuss the feasibility with him/her as soon as possible. I know publishing something like this won’t happen overnight, but I want to at least take that first step.

Thank you, truly. Not just for your advice, but for asking me that question.

# Memo

**Date: May 20, 2025, Evening**

Dr. Li asked me, "How did this shift happen?"

I sat there and thought about it for a long time. There wasn't really a single defining moment, but if I had to say—it started with a conversation with my brother.

He asked me how I was doing in an email, a simple enough question. I was going to give him the usual brush-off, but this time I didn't. I told him I was anxious, told him I was making some changes. He wrote back: "You’ve always been making changes; you just never tell anyone."

That hit me. I realized I’d been treating anxiety as evidence of some kind of failure, as if it proved something was wrong with me. But the truth is—the anxiety itself isn't the problem. The problem was how I understood it.

I told Dr. Li that I now see anxiety as a signal that "I care about something." I care about this trip, I care about my relationship with my brother, I care about what I’m still capable of. Anxiety isn't a sign that I’m broken; it’s a sign that I’m doing something that actually matters to me.

She nodded, then asked if I still needed to see her every week.

I said no. Not because the problems have vanished, but because I finally know how to take care of myself. I can recognize the patterns now—the 3:00 AM overthinking, the obsessive checking of details—but now I know what they are. They won't go away, but I’m not afraid of them anymore.

One more session next month. That’s it.

**Remember this: Anxiety ≠ Failure. Anxiety = I am doing something meaningful.**

When those familiar fears return next time, come back to this.

# October 7, Afternoon

My hands are still shaking.

I was tidying up the room just now and stumbled upon that cardboard box full of old photos—it was tucked behind the bookshelf; I’d almost forgotten it was there. I only meant to clear some space, but I ended up sitting on the floor, going through them one by one.

There’s one photo from high school. Hao and I are in our uniforms, arms linked, both laughing. We look so young, leaning into each other with such effortless certainty. I don’t remember exactly when it was taken, but I can feel the intimacy in the picture—it wasn’t forced; it was just natural.

I didn’t even realize I was crying until the tears started hitting the photos. But this isn't like the anxious, nameless crying from before. This is... warm? Or is it regret? No, not quite. It feels more like something is being awakened.

Sitting there, looking at those photos, it suddenly hit me—we still have a chance. We really do.

So I sent Hao an email. I didn’t overthink it; I just typed it out: *When we talk, should we look at these photos together?*

My heart was racing when I hit send. It felt like I was doing something... risky? No, not risky. I was taking initiative. Instead of just passively waiting and accepting the distance like I’ve done for all these years, I was actually creating something.

He might think the idea is strange. Or maybe he’s been wanting to see these photos too. I don’t know. But at least I tried.

It’s getting dark outside. I checked the internet connection, the camera angle, and my face—a bit puffy, but this is the real me, right?

Our parents are gone now. That thought feels especially sharp today. Hao is so far away, and I’m here. If we don’t start being proactive, then these years really will have been wasted. Not wasted on life, but wasted on each other.

Maybe this time will be different. I keep telling myself that, but today feels different—it’s not that desperate "maybe" where I’m trying to convince myself; it’s... a genuine possibility.

The call is in a few hours. I should probably look for more photos worth sharing. Maybe I should gather my thoughts and figure out what to say. But right now, I don’t want to do anything. I just want to sit here and let this warmth linger a little longer.

My sensitivity can be so exhausting sometimes. But right now, I’m actually grateful for it. Because it’s allowing me to feel the weight of this moment.

I had a long call with my brother last night. He mentioned that autumn has fully arrived where he is, and the leaves have all turned red. It suddenly hit me—when was the last time we saw each other? It feels like it’s been years.

We’ve made a promise: I’m going to visit him next spring.

I’ve been reflecting on something lately—when did we start becoming afraid of connecting with people far away? Maybe it’s not the distance we fear, but the things we’ve left on hold for too long. But I don’t want to put things off anymore.

I dug out my dusty passport; it’s still valid. I’ve started making lists, crunching numbers, and handling the practicalities. This feels very much like me, yet not like me at all. But perhaps this is what growing up is—finding that balance between freedom and commitment.

Life is shorter than I thought. So, there are some things that need to be done right now.

June 12, 2025, past 11:00 PM

Just got back from the bookstore. My hands are still shaking a little.

Today’s book club was about Yu Hua’s *To Live*. Ironically, we weren't discussing the novel itself, but rather the meaning of being alive. Someone asked me—I think it was that girl, Xiao Li—she asked, "Mr. Lin, aren't you afraid?" "Afraid of what?" I asked her. She said, "Afraid of growing old, afraid of dying."

I didn't have to think long before I answered. I said, "I understand now that the best way to live is to do the things that make me feel alive."

When I said that, the whole room went silent. It wasn't an awkward silence, it was... I don't know how to describe it. It was the kind of silence where everyone is digesting something. I saw a few people’s eyes well up.

Later, a friend told me that what I said today reminded her of why she wants to live.

I’m sitting here, re-reading the sentence I just wrote. Do I really believe it? I think I do. Yes, I really believe it.

It’s not because I’ve suddenly become an optimist—I still obsess over a single sentence in the middle of the night, and I still look up medical news that makes me anxious. But none of that matters anymore. Or rather, those things are still there, but they are no longer the entirety of my life.

I am 62 years old. I am divorced. My parents are gone. My savings are modest. My work is unstable. All of this is true. But I am still here, still reading, still thinking, still discussing life with friends. I still feel needed because of a stranger’s question.

This is what it means to live.

I need to email that publisher’s editor tomorrow. Another deadline is approaching. I’ll probably wait until the very last minute to submit. But this time, I won’t feel guilty about it. Because I know I am doing the thing that makes me feel alive.

My brother sent a photo from his place last week. I should write back to him. Maybe I can tell him that our family seems to share this trait—a curiosity about life, a quest for meaning. It’s not a flaw. It’s our gift.

The light in the room is still on. The books on the shelves are still waiting for me. There is much to do tomorrow.

But for now, I just want to sit here and feel this moment. To feel that I am still alive.

Professor Chen, I wanted to share a small recent experience with you. It might seem a bit out of the blue, but I feel like you’ll understand.

The day before yesterday, I dug out my camera, which had been gathering dust in the cupboard. You know, I started learning photography back in 2015, but I eventually set it aside. I always felt my photos weren't good enough—I’d never been in an exhibition, nor had I ever completed a proper series. So, I just left it there.

But when I picked it up this time, my mindset was completely different. I wasn't thinking about creating a "work of art," nor was I considering exhibitions or submissions. I just wanted to document—to capture the way old Mr. Zhang downstairs fixes his flowerpots, the slant of afternoon light in the bookstore, or the expression on someone’s face when they suddenly fall into deep thought during a book club meeting.

Looking at these photos, I realized I wasn't judging their quality. Instead, I felt a gentle sense of contentment. It’s as if... I finally understood what "creative expression" really means. It’s not about being seen; it’s about truly seeing life itself.

This reminded me of your previous suggestion about publishing my work. At the time, I was actually a bit resistant, feeling that my writing wasn't "heavyweight" enough. But recently, while organizing the book club, I noticed something interesting happening—a point I made, or a question I raised, would make people stop and think. In that moment, I realized that perhaps I had been looking for value in the wrong places all along.

The value of my knowledge and my ideas isn't determined by an editor at a journal; it reveals itself in those real, authentic conversations.

I don’t know if this counts as an "epiphany" (that word feels a bit too grand), but I think you’ll understand the feeling—that moment when you suddenly realize the thing you’ve been chasing isn't actually what you need.

What do you think?

Hey, I’m still thinking about that question you asked me earlier.

"Are you really going to write it?"

Yes, I really am. But I’m only just realizing now that it’s not just because there’s something special about the photo itself—though there is. It’s more because... being here with you this time has allowed me to see things I couldn't see on my own.

The distance in that photo has actually always been there. But it’s never felt like this before—like the distance itself is a way of connecting. Do you know what I mean? It’s not that the distance has disappeared, but that we can finally face it honestly, and even find something meaningful in it.

I want to write it down, not to solve anything, but to remember this moment—us being here, looking at this unfamiliar place together, yet feeling so close.

What’s your take on "distance"? I mean, all these years, you’ve been over there and I’ve been here, but now we’re sitting together... have you ever thought about what that means?

I’m glad you asked me that question. It made me realize more clearly why I want to write this.

I negate market recognition, I negate the significance of publishing, and I negate the possibility that my knowledge could be useful to others. But this constant rejection is, in itself, a form of passivity.

I noticed something at the reading group recently. A student raised a question about literary ethics, and I shared a few thoughts off the cuff. I saw that girl’s eyes light up. She said, "I’ve never thought about it that way." In that moment, I realized my ideas were actually creating a connection. Not because they were published, but because they were heard and understood.

But that also got me thinking—what if these ideas could reach more people? Not to prove how smart I am, but because there might actually be people out there who need this perspective.

What do you think? Am I just making excuses for myself to publish, or have I finally found a genuine reason to do so?

Also, I wanted to ask—what does publishing a book mean to you? How do you decide which ideas are worth sharing?

I haven't made up my mind yet, but I’d love to hear your thoughts.

After today’s book club session, a participant came up to me and said she was grateful I created a space where everyone could just... talk. She mentioned how rare it is these days to have such deep conversations with strangers.

You’re all welcome to join next month’s session. Not because I have any authoritative insights to share, but simply because... I think we all need moments like this.

I’ve always believed my value lies in what I accomplish—the published book reviews, the reading groups I organize, the photos I take. I use these things to measure myself. A month ago, looking at the pile of unfinished projects in my house, I reached a conclusion: I have nothing. I am a failure.

But today... they didn't say "we like your knowledge" or "we admire your achievements." They said "we missed you." "Your return matters to us."

What does that mean?

Maybe... maybe value isn't what I always thought it was?

I’m sitting here, trying to make sense of this shift. My brain is still operating on the old logic—I should feel relieved, but at the same time, I’m doubting: was it just politeness? Or am I actually... needed?

He shared so many details about his life now—the kids’ schools, the street they live on, the books he’s been reading lately. It suddenly hit me how little I actually knew about his life. Yet he remembers me; he remembers so many things I thought he’d long forgotten.

I pulled out an old spreadsheet and started crunching the numbers. Airfare, visas, how much I need to save. It’s been a long time since I’ve planned something with this much focus.

Maybe some connections don't require being together every day to stay alive. But if there’s a chance, you should go see them. While there’s still time.

I don’t know when it’ll happen, but this time, it’s not just a dream.

June 15, 2025, 8:20 PM

Sitting on the flight back, the sky outside the window is a deep, dark blue. My brother has fallen asleep, his head resting against the window frame. Looking at his profile, I still find it hard to believe that all of this actually happened.

That moment in the car this afternoon—I don't think I’ll ever forget it.

When he said, "I’ve been so worried about you," my eyes suddenly welled up. For so many years, we’ve barely had a real conversation; I thought he didn't care at all about what I was doing. But he told me he’s been reading my articles all along, constantly wondering if I was doing okay. He said he knew I was living alone after the divorce, knew my work was unstable, and knew I often suffered from insomnia over the smallest things in the middle of the night. He knew everything.

I didn't burst into tears immediately, but the tears just wouldn't stop. I took his hand and didn't say a word for a long time.

Later, I told him, "I’ve found a way to keep my longing for freedom while still taking good care of myself." I was a bit surprised by my own words as I said them. I realized that this truly is my state of mind now. I’m no longer afraid of losing my sense of security, nor do I punish myself for craving freedom. I’ve found a balance.

After he heard this, he just looked at me in silence. It was a long silence, but not at all awkward. We just looked at each other, as if confirming that we were both still alive, that we both still cared. His eyes were a little red, too.

I think all our years of regret, our hidden affection, and all those unspoken words were held within that silence.

Now, sitting on this plane, I finally understand what this trip truly meant to me. It wasn't just about reconnecting with my brother; it was a validation of myself. At 62, I can still take risks, I can still mend broken relationships, and I can still create new meaning. Death no longer frightens me; instead, it makes me see more clearly that every day I’m alive should be spent doing things that make me feel real.

Going home tomorrow. I want to give Professor Chen a call and tell her about the trip. Maybe I’ll buy something for old Mr. Zhang to thank him for his company lately. And my friends from the reading club—I want to see them, too.

Life suddenly feels very clear. Not because all my problems have been solved, but because I finally understand that loneliness is not a curse. It is an invitation—an invitation to build authentic, meaningful connections. And I have already begun to answer it.

April 5, 2022. Night.

Sitting on the subway, my phone screen is still glowing. I’ve re-read that WeChat message I sent to Lin Hao over and over, terrified that I might have used the wrong word.

"I’ve been dealing with some mental health issues lately and wanted to talk to you about it."

I must have edited that sentence at least ten times. Delete, retype. Delete, retype. In the end, I hit send anyway. My heart started racing the moment my finger pressed down, and it still hasn't quite settled.

After the lecture at the art museum this afternoon, we were sitting in a teahouse. I don’t know how it came out—"I’ve been sleeping terribly lately, waking up all through the night with my head full of messy thoughts." I was going to just brush it off, but Ms. Li (what was her name again? I’ve seen her at a few salons) suddenly said, "Me too. I saw a psychiatrist last year and was on medication for a while. I’m doing much better now."

Then Professor Chen mentioned he has an anxiety disorder and has been in counseling for two years.

I felt so strange in that moment. It wasn't relief, but a sense of... being seen? No, that’s not the word. It was the feeling of realizing I’m not a monster. They are all living their lives just fine—continuing to work, attending lectures, thinking—and yet they also wake up in the middle of the night, and they’ve all been afraid.

I never thought I’d tell Lin Hao any of this. We haven't had a real conversation in so long. He’s abroad, I’m here; we’re like two parallel lines. But for some reason today, I just wanted him to know. Not to seek comfort, but... maybe I just wanted him to know that I’m not "all okay." Maybe I wanted to break that silence of pretending everything is normal.

Maybe he won’t reply. Maybe he’ll say something dismissive. But at least I tried.

This is the first time I’ve used the words "I have anxiety" to describe myself. Saying it out loud didn't feel that shameful. Instead, it felt a bit like admitting a fact, like admitting "I’m nearsighted."

Dr. Li gave me her clinic address. Next Tuesday afternoon. I’ve already written it down.

I’m still scared. But maybe... maybe I’m not quite so alone anymore.

February 14, 2024

July 5, 2024. Evening.

I was just sitting in the study alone, still replaying that one sentence in my head.

When the book club ended this afternoon, a girl—the finance major, I think—walked up and said, "Mr. Lin, thank you for sharing today. I never thought about this book from that perspective." Her tone was sincere, not just polite. Two others said something similar.

My reaction at the time was strange. It wasn't happiness, but rather a sense of... unease.

Because I suddenly realized that I hadn't given them any profound insights at all. We just read some passages together; I shared some thoughts, and they shared theirs. In some parts, I even said, "I'm not entirely sure what this means either." I didn't act the way an "expert" is supposed to act.

But they seemed to like it better that way?

I remember what I said this afternoon: "Perhaps we are all learning to embrace uncertainty." It just slipped out because we were discussing a particularly vague philosophical point, and I was truly just expressing my own confusion. I wasn't trying to say anything profound.

Sitting here now, I’m asking myself: Is this sincerity? Or am I just putting on another kind of performance—performing the role of the "vulnerable intellectual"?

Back when I was at the publishing house, I knew exactly how to speak to sound authoritative. I would cite theories and propose frameworks. Back then, I felt I had value. After going freelance, I started to doubt all of it. Now... I seem to have found a new way, yet I find myself wondering if this new way is just another disguise.

But... if it’s a disguise, why did everyone look so relaxed? Why did they take the initiative to ask about coming back next month?

Maybe I’m just overthinking it. Dr. Li mentioned this trait of mine last time.

Forget it, I’ll stop thinking. I still have to revise that column draft tomorrow. The editor said the reader feedback has been great and told me to keep this style.

But I feel a bit tired right now. Not physically tired, but... I don't know how to describe it. It’s that "am I doing the right thing?" kind of tired.

Fifteen people. Why would fifteen people come to my home on a Friday afternoon just to hear me talk?

I need to figure this out.

Found another 1987 art criticism collection at the second-hand bookstore today. Opening the first page, there’s a name written in pencil by the previous owner—now faded beyond recognition.

It reminds me of what Old Li used to say: "Every old object tells a story. Sometimes, we see ourselves in the stories of others."

Perhaps. I wonder what I see in these traces left by strangers. I see the earnestness they once had, their markings, and the silence left behind after they vanished.

Sometimes I’m not sure what I’m still doing. But at least I’m still turning pages, still reading. Still asking questions.

Am I running away from something, or am I pursuing something?

If I scrap this project, will I regret it? Or will I feel relieved?

September 12, 2023 | Past 11 PM

Sitting at my desk, the room is as messy as ever. But what happened today has left me with a different kind of feeling.

During the reading group this afternoon, a new girl asked me what I thought of the book. I said something offhandedly—"Help isn't measured by market value. This book helped me understand myself"—and as soon as the words left my mouth, I froze. It was as if I had suddenly heard what I was actually saying.

I’ve been constantly wondering if my writing is useful, if anyone cares, or if it can be sold. But in that moment, I realized that’s not the point. The point is... while I’m writing these things, I am thinking; I am understanding the world. That, in itself, is enough.

Then Professor Chen mentioned the photography showcase. My reflex was to say, "The work isn't finished yet"—I’ve said that a thousand times. But this time, I didn't. I heard myself say, "Okay, I'll do it." My voice was quiet, but certain.

The photos are indeed imperfect. Some have composition issues; some themes aren't fully thought out. But... they are there. They all represent what I saw and what I felt at a specific moment. Why must I wait until they are "finished" to show them? When is anything ever finished?

I’m thinking about the things piling up in this room. The photography, the unread books, the half-written notes. I’ve always seen them as evidence of failure. But maybe they are just... my traces. Proof that I have lived.

The year my mother passed away, I found her diaries. They were full of fragmented thoughts, many without conclusions. But I cried while reading them. Not because they were complete, but because they were real. Because they were *her*.

I understand a little better now. I don’t need to wait until everything is perfect, useful, or has market value before I allow myself to exist.

Tomorrow, I’m going to organize my photography. Not to "finish" it, but just to... see it clearly.

And those articles. Maybe I really should publish them as a collection. Not to make money, but just to... leave my thoughts behind.

I'm a bit tired, but things feel different.

May 26, 2022 – Past 11 PM

My hands are still shaking.

I’m sitting on the edge of the bed, the lights dimmed low. The room is cluttered with books and piles of things I haven't organized, but today, it’s as if I can’t see any of it. I keep checking the time on my phone—11:37. I’m thinking that if I don’t sleep now, I’ll be exhausted tomorrow. But I can't sleep.

I need to write this down. Otherwise, the feeling will dissipate, or be swallowed whole by my anxiety again.

At three o'clock this afternoon, I dialed that number. My finger hovered over the keypad for a long time. I thought about hanging up. I thought about saying, "Forget it, I'm fine." But I didn't. I heard the receptionist’s voice, and then the appointment time—next Tuesday at 2:00 PM.

My heart was racing. After I hung up, I sat on the sofa, not quite sure what I had just done.

Dr. Li’s clinic is in an old building. My palms were sweating when I walked in. She sat across from me, listening quietly as I spoke. I said a lot—about that photograph, about my brother, about these 22 years of silence. It was a mess; I kept stopping because I didn't even know what I was saying. I even cried. I hate crying in front of strangers.

But she didn't try to comfort me. She just asked one question: "But you are here now, aren't you?"

I paused for a long time. A really long time.

I didn't know how to answer. I was thinking—yes, I am here. I walked in of my own accord. I admitted that I needed help. It sounds like such a small thing, but to me... I don't know. It felt like something shifted inside of me.

Walking down the street after leaving the clinic, I felt something strange. The feeling of being seen. For all these years, I’ve sat at home alone, flipping through old photo albums, ruminating over things that cannot be changed. No one saw what I was afraid of. Or rather, I didn't let anyone see.

But today, Dr. Li saw. She didn't say, "You should do this" or "Why do you think that way?" She just... saw.

I’m still scared. I’m still wondering how my brother will respond to that letter (I haven't sent it yet, but I will). I’m still wondering if I’m being too fragile. But... maybe this isn't fragility. Maybe this is courage. Maybe I finally did something right.

I don't know. I really don't know.

But I am here now. I am writing this. At least I tried.

Tomorrow, I need to email Professor Chen back. And then maybe I’ll look at that 1985 photo one more time. But this time, I won't just look. I’ll do something.

I will.

I definitely will.

It took me a long time to decide to send you this message.

I was tidying up my room today when a thought suddenly hit me—maybe I’ve had the wrong idea about "freedom" all these years.

You know, I’ve always understood freedom as "rejecting all constraints." That’s why I quit my job, why I turned down those stable offers, and why I even, in a way, rejected commitment. I thought that was freedom. But looking at these piles of books, unfinished projects, and scattered notes now, I realize—that’s not freedom; that’s avoidance.

Real freedom, perhaps, is "knowing what you want, and then having the courage to pay the necessary price for it."

It sounds simple, but it took me all these years to finally figure it out. Freedom isn't the absence of choice; it’s making a decision that is truly your own among many options, and then bearing the consequences of that decision—whether good or bad. I’ve been running away from the "bearing" part.

I don’t know if it’s just me, or if you’ve ever felt the same way. But I’d like to hear your thoughts. How do you define freedom?

It’s almost midnight as I’m sending this, and I know you’re probably asleep. No rush to reply.

# MEMO

**Date: Friday, December 22, AM**

---

I received my brother’s letter.

I haven’t fully processed it yet. I’m sitting at my desk with the pages spread out in front of me; I’ve already read them three times.

He wrote: "Distance and silence do not mean estrangement; they give us the chance to reconnect on a deeper level."

—That sentence.

I always thought the silence of these past years was a failure. Two people living on opposite ends of the earth, occasional greetings, but mostly just blanks. I interpreted those blanks as a source of regret. But his meaning is entirely different.

Perhaps I have been misreading distance all along.

It reminds me of what Father used to say—he never emphasized the physical form of companionship, but rather that "true understanding is the deepest form of company." Mother, after all those years working in the library, also said that the best connections often happen in silence, when two people are contemplating the same things.

My brother and I, we read the same books. We inherited the same anxieties, the same curiosity. What has he been thinking about over there all these years? What have I been thinking about here? Perhaps that, in itself, is a conversation.

True connection isn't found in frequent phone calls. It’s not in "how are you" platitudes.

It’s in the mind. It’s in the things we share—what our parents left us, the questions about life and meaning that we are both still digesting.

—

What’s next?

Write back to him. Not a polite reply, but a real dialogue. Tell him what’s on my mind. Tell him what books I’ve read and what questions I’ve pondered alone in this room all these years. Perhaps that is our true reconnection.

Maybe I should organize these thoughts? Not just for him, but for myself.

Distance is merely physical. Our connection lies elsewhere.

---

May 3, 2023, 11:00 AM

My hands were shaking when I pulled it out.

It’s a diary from 1982. The pages have yellowed, and the cover still has a photocopy of Van Gogh’s *Sunflowers* that I bought back then. I wrote it in my college dorm with a fountain pen; the handwriting is heavy, as if I were carving something into the paper.

"I want to be an editor with ideas. I want to change the publishing world. I want to make those suppressed voices heard."

I read it three times. Then I looked around the room I’m sitting in now—piles of books, magazines, unorganized photo negatives, and all sorts of things scavenged from flea markets. What have I done for forty years?

I’m still doing the same things. Still reviewing other people’s books. Still writing for magazines. Still—

I cried. Sitting in the middle of all this stuff, I really cried. Not the kind of crying that can be comforted, but a deep, nameless weeping.

I asked myself: What have I done? Where did my life go?

What about that girl? That girl in the dorm who wrote down those grand ideals? What did she become? A freelancer? Someone who spends all day anxious about deadlines? Someone who organizes book clubs at a community bookstore—a what?

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

Sometimes I feel like I’m making progress. Lately, when I organize the book clubs and see those young people listening to me intently, I think: maybe this is where the value lies. Maybe change doesn’t have to mean changing the entire publishing industry; maybe it’s just reading a book with a dozen people in a small bookstore. Maybe that’s enough.

But sitting here now, looking at these diaries, I feel like I’m lying to myself.

I haven’t changed anything. I’ve just grown old. My ideals haven't been realized; my ambitions have been worn smooth by time. The things I once wanted to do now look like nothing more than the naive dreams of a young person.

Or... or maybe I’ve just failed in a different way.

I don’t want to think about it anymore. My head hurts. Outside the window, the May sunlight is blinding.

Maybe tomorrow I’ll be able to figure it out. Maybe I never will.
