May 3, 2022, past 8:00 PM

I just hung up the phone with Professor Chen. Now I’m sitting here among piles of books, my hands still shaking.

She asked if something was wrong. I said it was nothing, just that I’ve been a bit tired lately. The moment the word "tired" left my mouth, I could hear the lie in it—I’m clearly drowning, yet I said I’m just a bit tired. She didn’t push, but the silence that followed made me feel even worse. Then she said one thing: "Sometimes, it takes more courage to stop than to keep running."

And I just cried. I cried on the phone for ten minutes.

Then I told her someone was at the door and hung up in a hurry. Lie upon lie.

Now I’m sitting here alone, looking at myself in the mirror—eyes swollen, tear stains still on my face. I wonder, when did I become like this? When did I start using "busy" to hide the truth of being "exhausted"?

Dr. Li said during our session that I use rationality to escape my emotions. At the time, I argued with her in my head, thinking she was wrong. But now I understand—I’ve been running. Running so fast, filling every gap with books, work, analysis, and beautiful vocabulary, just so I wouldn't have to stop and feel the things that hurt.

"It takes more courage to stop than to keep running."

I never thought about it that way. I always thought stopping was cowardice, a form of giving up. But maybe... maybe stopping is the real courage? To admit I’m tired, to admit I’m scared, to admit that being alone in this old house sometimes feels like suffocating...

But why did being understood make me cry? Why did being understood make me break down?

I remember crying the year my mother passed away, but I haven't cried since then. I learned to respond to all concern with "I'm fine." I learned to use academic language to discuss loneliness instead of feeling it. I used new interests and new projects to flee from it.

Now, vulnerability feels foreign to me. I’m even a little afraid of this feeling—afraid that if I stop, I’ll keep crying and never be able to stop.

But maybe... maybe that’s not such a bad thing?

I don’t know. I don’t know anything right now. All I know is that I need to text Professor Chen back, apologize, and make up some excuse to say I’m okay. Because letting someone see me like this feels even more terrifying than to keep running.

I’ll think about it tomorrow. Tonight, I’ll just sit here and let myself be fragile for a while. No one can see me.

# April 10, 2022 Afternoon

Organizing again. It’s become a habit—before doing anything important, I have to break it down, label it, and tabulate it.

I came back from Dr. Li’s yesterday with my head in a muddle. She asked me when I first started feeling anxious, and I stammered for a long time. She didn't rush me; she just waited. That feeling of being waited for was strange, as if every one of my pauses was permitted. Still, I found myself wanting to give her the "right" answer.

So this afternoon, I pulled out my highlighters.

First, a table—anxiety triggers, time, intensity (1-10), and the thoughts I had at the time. I used yellow for "insomnia at 3 AM," green for "seeing health news," and pink for "no reply from my brother for a long time." Then came the statistics—how many days of insomnia this month, how many times I overanalyzed a single sentence, how many times I checked the gas stove.

As I was doing this, I suddenly stopped and laughed. I actually laughed.

I’m using editorial methods to process my anxiety. Using the very things I learned at the publishing house—categorizing, labeling, quantifying—to try and "master" my therapy. It’s as if, provided I can organize it clearly enough, Dr. Li will be able to give me an answer, a solution, and then everything will return to a range I can understand.

"If I can understand it, I can control it." I wrote this sentence in the margin of my notebook. Looking at it, I felt it was both ridiculous and pathetic.

This is just who I am. Always trying to use rationality to fight uncertainty. Always wanting to be the one who knows more, the one in control. Even when admitting I need help, I’m trying to turn the process into something I can manage.

But this time, I’m not sure if this method will work anymore.

Dr. Li doesn't seem like the type to be impressed by my spreadsheets. She seems more concerned with *why* I’m making these tables in the first place. I haven't figured out how to answer that question yet.

I’ll keep organizing tomorrow. Or... maybe I should just take these messy notes to see her, instead of waiting until everything is sorted out. Maybe being heard doesn't require me to turn myself into a perfect report first.

The thought scares me a little.

But I’m also a little... hopeful?

January 12, 2025, 9:45 PM

Professor Chen’s words keep looping in my head: "This contradiction itself is honesty."

I don’t know why I suddenly burst into tears. Maybe it’s because all these years, I’ve been constantly negating myself—denying the romanticized notions of solitude I had when I was young, denying my complex understanding of it now, denying every single word I’ve written. It’s like falling down a bottomless pit; every time I think I’ve grabbed onto something, I turn around only to find it was an illusion.

He’s right; the contradiction is indeed honest. But what is the use of honesty?

After I got home, I sat at my desk and opened those old manuscripts. Looking at what I wrote ten or twenty years ago felt like reading a stranger’s diary. My understanding of solitude back then was so... naive. I romanticized it, treated it as a source of creativity, as if loneliness were some noble price to pay. Now I know better: it is both the source and the despair. It allows you to see things others can’t, but it also drags you into an inescapable abyss.

So, am I more honest now than I was then? Perhaps. But what does that even mean?

I’ve started wondering: what if my current understanding of solitude is something I’ll reject again in ten years? What if the things I find "honest" now will seem childish to my future self? Why am I even compiling these essays into a book, as if to declare, "This is what I think"? What am I trying to finalize?

The collection of papers is still sitting there. I haven’t kept writing, but I haven’t given up either. I just read those old articles over and over, like some kind of ritual. Sometimes I’ll scribble a new annotation in the margins, only to cross it out. Sometimes I think, *this one should be deleted, that one isn't good enough.*

Professor Chen says this is proof of growth. To me, it feels more like watching a series of my own failures—failing to understand the world, failing to understand myself.

Maybe that’s why I keep procrastinating. It’s not out of laziness (though I am lazy), but out of fear. I’m afraid that putting these words into print is like saying, "This is who I am." And then one day, I’ll negate it all over again, just like I did with those old articles.

It’s late now. It’s quiet outside. I am still lonely, but now I know that this loneliness gives as much as it takes. Is that honesty?

Maybe.

But honesty alone isn't enough to make me keep writing.

December 2, 2024. Past 11 PM.

I still can’t sleep.

I just took a walk downstairs. On the way back from the bookstore, I kept wondering why I said those things in front of all those people. Professor Chen was there, along with the regulars from the reading group—they trust me, they see me as some kind of... I don’t know, someone with insight, I suppose. And yet, there I sat, responding to a young girl’s question by saying, "To be honest, I’m not sure anymore either."

Not sure about what? Not sure about anything.

She asked me how she could write insightful essays like mine. I should have given her some encouragement, or at least a decent answer. But all I could think about were those old articles I’ve been digging up lately—pieces I once thought were so profound. Looking at them now is like reading the work of a stranger. Some points feel too dogmatic, some arguments are too long-winded, and some conclusions... I’m not even sure if I truly believed them at the time.

And then I just said it. I told her I was re-examining my own work and finding that many of my ideas now seem problematic.

Her expression changed. I saw that look of disappointment quite clearly.

On the walk home, I kept wondering: was that honesty? Or was I just performing my anxiety in public? Is this just who I am—someone who can never give a definitive answer, who is always doubting, always overturning their own words?

The worst part is the thought circling in my head: if my past ideas were flawed, won't my current ones be rejected by my future self too? Then why am I even bothering with this collection? Why am I selecting essays and writing introductions? Maybe I’ve been doing something utterly meaningless all along.

I startled even myself when I heard those words come out of my mouth.

But I said it. At the reading group. In front of all those people.

Now I’m wondering if Professor Chen thinks I’m unreliable. He was the one who suggested I publish this collection, and there I was in public saying, "Maybe none of this matters." What does that make me look like? A fraud. Or a madman.

I shouldn't have said those things. I should have encouraged that girl. I should have acted like someone with experience. I should have—

But I couldn't. I really couldn't.

I’m sitting here now in front of piles of books, looking at these articles I’m supposed to select for the collection. They just lie there like a heap of evidence, proving who I used to be. But I don’t know who that person is. I don’t know who I am now.

Maybe I never should have agreed to this publishing project in the first place.

Maybe I should call Professor Chen and tell him I’ve changed my mind.

But I know I won’t. I’ll keep going—doubting as I work, working as I doubt. That’s just me. That’s how I’ve always been.

When morning comes, I have to reply to the editor at the publishing house. She asked when I could give her a preliminary list of articles.

I don’t know.

I really don’t know.

I told Old Man Zhang, "I can't change a thing." He probably thinks I’ve lost my mind. I think I have, too.

The room is cluttered with stuff. The camera is gathering dust in the corner. That book on urban renewal is still wedged under the sofa cushions. I bought paint samples and left them on the windowsill for three months. The colors have faded.

Why am I always like this?

I dug out those cultural critiques I wrote back in 2015. Reread them. There’s still something there, but... who cares anymore? The internet has changed. My voice is too quiet. Maybe it was always too quiet.

All the things I’ve collected over the years. Antiques scavenged from flea markets. Every piece has a story—or so I used to tell myself. Now they just look like... junk. Junk with stories.

If I died right now, who would come to sort through all this? Who would remember the stories behind these photos? My brother is abroad. Would my friends show up? Or would they just hire someone to clear out the room, hauling it all away like a pile of scrap?

Maybe I shouldn't have left the publishing house. At least back then, I had somewhere to go. Someone needed me. And now?

To-do:
- Apologize to Old Man Zhang? Better not, it’ll only be more awkward.
- Tidy up the room? Pointless.
- Keep writing that article? The deadline is looming again, but I just can't...
- Call someone?

Never mind.

I’ll deal with it tomorrow. Or never.

November 22, 2023, Afternoon

I just stepped out of the clinic, still clutching that appointment slip in my hand. Dr. Li’s office was very quiet, the walls adorned with a few abstract paintings. As I sat there filling out the forms, my hands were shaking slightly.

"Please describe the primary issues troubling you"—such a simple sentence, yet it took me ten minutes just to start writing. Anxiety. Insomnia. Fear of aging. Loneliness. And... a sort of indescribable feeling, like being weighed down by something heavy. When I wrote the word "loneliness," my eyes welled up.

How embarrassing. A 62-year-old woman, feeling ashamed just for admitting she needs help.

But... maybe it’s not like that.

The day before yesterday, I received a reply from my brother. He said, "You don't need to feel guilty for opening up to me." I read that sentence over and over. Then Professor Chen said on the phone that a friend of hers is also seeing a therapist: "There’s no shame in it, Lin Xiaoyun. It’s called taking care of yourself." Even old Mr. Zhang from downstairs, when he ran into me and asked how I was doing, responded to my casual remark by saying, "Sometimes we all just need someone to listen."

I think, perhaps, this isn't a failure. Perhaps it is... I don't know how to put it.

On the form, I wrote: "I want to be understood." After writing those words, I suddenly realized that I’ve been waiting for others to understand me, yet I’ve never actually sought it out. I’ve shut myself away at home, surrounded by books and thoughts, never allowing anyone to get close. Is this freedom? Or is it an escape?

Dr. Li was very gentle. Her expression remained entirely non-judgmental. She simply asked, "Is next Wednesday alright?" I nodded.

Sitting at home now, looking at that appointment slip, I feel strange. I feel a sense of relief—finally, someone is going to listen to me—but I’m also afraid. What will happen once I start talking? What if she can’t help me? What if I discover I’m even more broken than I imagined?

But... maybe this is exactly what needs to happen.

Maybe accepting help is a form of strength in itself. I’ve spent so long denying that thought, but now I’m starting to believe it might be true.

Next Wednesday. Five days to go.

I don’t know what will happen, but at least... I’m trying.

Hao, I need to tell you something.

Yesterday, I cleared out a corner of my study and put up a whiteboard. You know me—my place has always been a mess, like a warehouse—but this time is different. I’ve dedicated a specific space just for this publishing project. Now, every morning, I sit there for an hour or two, mapping out the chapter frameworks on the board and watching it all take shape bit by bit.

To be honest, it’s giving me a different kind of feeling. It’s not that "oh, I have another new idea" kind of excitement, but something more grounded—like I’m actually *doing* something, not just imagining it.

A friend came over yesterday, and I actually took the initiative to point at the whiteboard, walk her through my ideas, and ask for her opinion. You know I never used to do that. I always kept my creative stuff hidden away, feeling like sharing was a form of exposure. But now I realize that maybe it’s precisely because someone sees it, and someone cares, that the whole thing becomes more real.

I was wondering if you could find some time to take a look? I’m not asking for professional advice or anything, just... I’d love to hear your thoughts. You always have a way of asking the right questions. And honestly, we’ve been out of touch for too long these past few years, and I want to change that.

No rush, whenever works for you. We could video call, or we can just chat next time you’re free.

How have you been lately?

Lin Xiaoyun

October 17, 2024 – Past 11 PM

I just got back from the book club. I’m sitting alone in the living room, haven't even turned on the lights yet. The city lights outside are casting blurry reflections on the windowpane.

 I still can’t quite believe what I just said out loud.

"Yes, I want to go. I want to see his life now. I want the kids to know that their aunt has always been here."

The moment I said it, the whole room seemed to go silent for a second. I could feel everyone looking at me. That retired lady—the one who always brings cake—her eyes got a bit misty, and she said, "Family is what matters most." Then there was this warm silence. No prying, no judging, just... understanding.

I’ve never spoken about my brother in front of so many people before. All these years, I’ve carried this estrangement alone, like a secret. Now that it’s suddenly out there, it feels strange. Relieved? Maybe. But more than that... it feels real.

Passport. I need to check if it’s still valid. Money... I need to figure out how much I can save. Plane tickets, accommodation, and—I want to buy something for my nephew and niece. These practical questions are circling in my head, but they aren't overwhelming the "I want to go." Instead, they feel like confirmation that this is actually happening.

I wonder what would have happened if I hadn't said it. Would I have just stayed in this room alone, continuing to replay their lives in my head late at night, using silence to protect myself from the sting of disappointment?

Mom and Dad are gone. I can’t get back the years between my brother and me. But maybe... maybe it’s not too late.

It’s 3 AM. I should sleep. But I want to sit here a little longer.

Imagining the moment I see them.

Imagining myself having the courage to take this risk.

I don’t know how it will turn out. But I want to try.

I really do.

June 13, 2022. Late night, 23:47.

My hands shook when I dug out that email.

Five years ago. My brother wrote: "Sis, are you doing okay over there?"

Just that one simple sentence. I sat on the edge of the bed, staring at the screen for a long time. Five years. We haven't truly spoken in five years.

I only just realized that he was waiting, too.

The realization hit me hard. All these years, I felt like he was the one distancing himself from me—he was overseas, he was busy, he didn't reach out. I kept ruminating on that sense of abandonment. But looking at this "Are you doing okay?", I suddenly understood—maybe he was waiting for a real answer. Maybe he was also tired of the polite greetings and superficial updates. Maybe he just didn't know how to start.

Just like me.

I wrote a second letter. I didn't delete it this time. I didn't overthink the meaning of every word. I just wrote—about the anxiety of these past years, about my fear of aging, my fear of death, and my fear that one day I’d never have the chance to talk to him again. I wrote about how much I miss him. I even wrote about how much I regret my silence all these years.

When Dr. Li asked me "What do you want?", I thought I needed to find a perfect answer. But it’s actually very simple—I want him to know I’m still here. I want him to know I care. I want the truth, even if it’s ugly, fragile, and doesn't sound like something a 62-year-old woman should say.

Maybe we were both waiting for the other to speak first.

Maybe the perfect letter doesn't exist. Maybe just telling the truth is enough.

The letter is finished. I haven't sent it yet. But this time, I didn't delete it.

It feels strange. I'm scared, but also a little relieved. It’s as if I’ve finally allowed myself to be an imperfect person.

Dr. Li, hello.

I wanted to share something with you. This afternoon, I dug out some reviews I wrote over a decade ago—articles for the magazine, as well as some unpublished notes. To be honest, I was originally just looking for material to see if I could piece something together. But as I kept reading, I found I couldn't stop.

There was something in those words that I hadn't seen clearly before. I’ve always felt they had little market value, so I just let them sit there gathering dust. But rereading them today, I realized—they actually record my genuine thoughts on so many things. About literature, about society, about life. Those reflections were earnest, and they were deep.

I think perhaps I’ve been using the wrong yardstick to measure them all this time.

So, I’ve decided to do something. I want to compile these articles and notes into a collection of essays. I’ve even given it a title: *Forty Years of Reflection: The Life Record of an Editor and Critic*. Publishers might not be particularly interested, and it probably won’t sell well. But that’s no longer the point.

The point is—these things are meaningful to me. They represent how I view the world and what I care about. That, in itself, is enough.

I feel like this idea gradually became clear through our conversations. Thank you.

I’ll bring some of the drafts to show you next week, if you don’t mind.

Lin Xiaoyun

# Lin Xiaoyun's Private Notes

**November 3, 2023 10:30**

---

I’ve been stuck on a question for the past few days.

We use rationality and knowledge to protect ourselves, but at what point does that protection turn into imprisonment?

I was chatting with someone yesterday, and they asked me a very simple question. I realized my first instinct was to explain it away with knowledge—to wrap it in concepts, theories, and books I’ve read. It was like I was putting on a suit of armor.

But when you’re wearing armor, you can’t feel anything anymore.

It made me think about all these years I’ve spent as an editor and critic. Have I been doing the same thing? Replacing feeling with analysis, and understanding with criticism? I’m not even sure if I truly love these books, or if I just love the feeling of "being able to understand them."

Does that sound a bit ridiculous?

I think I might have been running away from something all along. Using thinking to escape, using busyness to escape, using this persona of being "independent" to escape. And so, I became this incredibly rational, strong-willed person—but at what cost?

Sometimes I think that maybe I don’t need more knowledge. Maybe what I need is to just... stop. To really stop, and not be in such a rush to understand, explain, or prove anything.

But stopping is terrifying. Because when I stop, I have to face the things I’ve been running from.

Do you ever feel this way? Or do you think I’m overthinking it?

(Actually, I know I’m overthinking it. I just don’t seem to know how not to.)

Subject: After receiving your letter

Hao,

It’s been a week since I received your reply, and I’ve been thinking about how to respond. To be honest, I’ve read it many times.

That photo from 1985 is still right here in front of me. Looking at your smile, I suddenly realized something—I always thought my loneliness was because you left, or because of all the years that have come between us. But it’s not. The loneliness comes from never having the courage to tell you that I miss you. I hid in my own life, using work, books, and all sorts of excuses to convince myself that this was the freedom I chose. But that wasn't freedom; it was an escape.

You asked in your letter how I’ve been. I want to be honest with you—not great lately. It’s not that anything major has gone wrong with my life, but I’ve started to feel afraid. Afraid of time. Afraid that we can never go back to how things were. Afraid that one day I’ll just be gone, like my father and mother. I’m now approaching the age my father was when he passed away, and that realization takes my breath away.

But because of this, I’ve also started to realize something else—I still have time. Maybe not much, but I have it. And I don’t want to waste it anymore.

I don’t know if we can ever be as close as we used to be. Maybe we can’t. But I want to see if we can start over—not by pretending nothing happened, but by acknowledging that we’ve both changed, and seeing if the people we are now can connect.

Could you write to me more often? They don't have to be long letters. Just let me know how you’re doing over there, what you’re reading, what’s on your mind. I’ll write to you, too.

Thank you for not turning me away.

Xiaoyun

Evening of June 10, 2022

June 28, 2023, Night

Dr. Li asked me, "How do you feel you’ve changed compared to six months ago?"

I sat there for a long time before answering. Outside the window, the sky was darkening, and the warm yellow lamp in the clinic made everything look soft. I said that I’ve stopped trying to change myself.

The moment I said it, I was a bit surprised myself. Because what does that mean? Does it mean I’ve given up? No, it’s not that. It’s that I’ve finally stopped that endless self-criticism—why can’t I be as stable as others, why do I always procrastinate, why is my room always a mess, why do I toss and turn at 3:00 AM over a single sentence.

I’ve started to accept these things. To accept that this is just who I am.

Dr. Li didn’t say anything; he just nodded. But I kept talking. I said the anxiety is still there; it hasn't disappeared. But it’s no longer that overwhelming thing. I used to feel like anxiety was an enemy I had to defeat, to eliminate. Now I understand that it’s just a part of me. I can coexist with it. Sometimes it reminds me to be careful; sometimes it’s just there, and I just let it be.

I mentioned something else, too. I said that now I have the book club, I have friends, I have neighbors like Grandpa Zhang, and I have you. I feel connected.

As I said this, it suddenly hit me that these people are real. They aren't figments of my imagination. Professor Chen really was impressed by my lecture. The people at the book club really are listening to me, really engaging in dialogue with me. Grandpa Zhang really cares about my room renovation. Dr. Li is really here, once a week, listening to all these messy thoughts of mine.

I used to always feel like I was alone. But maybe loneliness and connection can exist at the same time? I live alone, and my life indeed has a lot of uncertainty, but that doesn't mean I am lonely.

On the way home, I was thinking that maybe I don’t need to be a perfect, stable, successful person. Maybe I can just be who I am right now—a bit scattered, a bit anxious, but also full of curiosity, willing to talk to people, willing to think, and willing to change.

My value doesn't lie in how many articles I can publish, or how stable my career is. It lies in—well, I’m not quite sure where it lies. But I’m starting to feel that this "uncertainty" itself is acceptable.

Tomorrow I’m going to email Professor Chen back about the publishing thing. Maybe I really should give it a try. Not because I have anything to prove, but because I want to.

It feels strange, but also very light.

Now, when I write, my head is full of questions like "Is this perspective original enough?" "Will this offend some group?" "Is this too outdated?" Now, I have nothing but doubts.

I’ve been thinking about this essay collection... maybe I shouldn't take it so seriously. Maybe it’s just an unrealistic idea that I’ve mistaken for something important. I sat at my computer today and opened the folder, but I couldn't write a single word. I just sat there for a long time; the room was dark, lit only by the glow of the screen.

I’m not looking for self-pity. I’m just... I’m wondering if it’s a bit ridiculous for a 62-year-old freelance writer to try and publish a collection based on 15-year-old articles. Who would actually be interested in this stuff? Maybe I should just accept the fact that I once had ideas, but that era has passed.

But I keep thinking about what you said. You said these essays have value. I want to know—do you truly believe that, or is it just out of kindness to an old friend?

No need to reply now. I just needed to get this out.

Goodnight.

March 15, 2025 – Just past 2:00 AM

I can't sleep. I’ve been tossing and turning ever since I got off the phone with my brother yesterday.

He said, "I’m proud of you. This book is the proof of your life."

Just that one sentence. And I cried. I sat there among piles of books, crying like a mess, my glasses sliding down my nose.

We’ve barely had a real conversation in years. He’s abroad, I’m here; an occasional greeting was the extent of our "duty." I always assumed he didn't understand why I quit my editing job to go freelance, why I’m always so unstable, why my place is cluttered with old books and half-finished projects. Maybe he thought the same of me. There’s been too much silence between us for too many years.

But yesterday, I told him about this collection of essays. I told him how I slowly crawled out of that despair over the years, and how I’ve started to realize that my "failures" were actually records of growth. I even talked to him about my thoughts on death—how hopeless I felt the year Mom passed away, and how I’m finally beginning to understand what it means to cherish life.

He listened for a long time. I could feel him truly listening. And then he said those words.

I wonder, if Mom were still here, would she be proud of me like that? She was a librarian; she loved books and knowledge so much. Would she understand why I had to compile these essays, why this matters so much to me?

We’ve made plans to meet this summer. He’s coming back, and we’re going to have dinner and really talk. How long has it been since we had a plan like that? I’m actually a little nervous, as if I’m meeting a stranger. But he’s my brother.

Maybe I’ve been misunderstanding something all along. I used to think freedom meant rejecting everything—rejecting commitment, rejecting limitations. But this essay project—it actually limits me; it forces me to finish things, to be responsible for a deadline. Yet, I’ve never felt this fulfilled.

Maybe commitment itself can be a form of freedom.

It’s 3:00 AM now. It’s still dark outside. But inside, things feel a little brighter.

I have to keep working on the introduction tomorrow.

# Consultation Notes | May 8th

That was it. Just one sentence.

She didn't press further; she just nodded, as if that explained everything. But sitting there, it suddenly hit me—maybe that really is it. It’s not the anxiety of the past few months, not the fear of aging, not the job instability. It’s those diaries.

Those yellowed notebooks I found while clearing out the room. From the 1990s. Things I wrote when I was young. Seeing that handwriting, the dreams back then, the plans, the visions of the future... and then comparing them to now.

I hadn't considered this causal chain. Or rather, I knew it made me feel bad, but I hadn't connected it to the subsequent breakdown of everything else.

Now, Dr. Li’s question is like a thread, stitching all the scattered pieces together.

—Maybe the problem isn't that I'm not doing well enough now. Maybe the problem is that I saw who I used to be, and I can't accept the distance between that person and who I am today.

But does this logic hold up? Or am I overthinking again?

Should I go find those old diaries? Or just let them keep lying there in that cardboard box?

I don't know. I don't know yet.

# Diary

Sunday, September 29, 2024, 11:00 PM

I just hung up the video call with my brother. Sitting here at my desk, I’m at a loss for what to do next. The screen has gone black, but the feeling lingers—warm, strange, yet familiar.

It’s been a long time since we’ve talked like this. Our past calls were always tentative, filled with silence and awkward pauses. "How have you been?" "Fine, you?" And that would be it. I often felt a sense of loss after hanging up; the estrangement felt like a sheet of glass—no matter how hard I tried, I couldn't break through.

But today was different.

He asked about my book, and he asked in detail—not out of polite obligation, but out of a genuine desire to understand. I started telling him about the project I’m working on, how I’m integrating years of cultural critiques and lecture ideas, and the deeper thoughts behind these texts. As I spoke, I realized my pace was quickening, and my voice was coming alive.

Then he said something that I’m still turning over in my mind: "I’ve always felt you had this kind of potential."

I didn’t react immediately. After a few seconds of silence, he added, "I’m proud of you, Xiaoyun."

My eyes welled up instantly. It’s embarrassing, but I couldn't help it.

What did I say back? I said, "Even though we’re far apart, our minds are connected." The moment the words left my mouth, I was surprised myself—I had never thought of it that way, but once said, it felt profoundly true. We may be on opposite ends of the earth, but the things we discuss, the things we care about, and the way we think—those things are in sync. All these years of silence and distance haven't seemed to change that.

We talked for a long time after that. We talked about books, about social issues we both care about, and even about Mom and Dad. He mentioned how he sometimes remembers Dad working in his study, or the way Mom looked so focused in the library. We met each other within those memories.

When we hung up, neither of us said much. But the feeling was different. It wasn't that forced "okay, let's stay in touch" vibe; it was—how should I put it—a sense of truly being seen by one another.

It occurred to me suddenly that for years, I’ve been mourning my loneliness. I’ve grieved the distance between my brother and me, and the fact that our parents are gone. But perhaps connection doesn't have to rely on geographical proximity or frequent meetings. Maybe sometimes, one deep conversation, one sincere exchange, is enough to make us feel each other’s presence.

I feel a strange sense of contentment right now. It’s not a bubbly excitement, but a quiet, deep-seated satisfaction. It feels as though something has been repaired—or rather, reconnected.

There’s a lot of work to do tomorrow. But tonight, I just want to sit here for a while and let this feeling stay.

January 15, 2024, 7:00 PM

I just replied to my editor’s email. My fingers hovered over the keyboard for a long time before I finally typed the words: "I’ve always been terrible at managing deadlines."

I wouldn't have said that before. Usually, I’d make excuses—saying I hadn't been feeling well lately, or that a particular topic needed more research time. Editors are used to my procrastination, and I’m used to my own defense mechanisms. But this time... for some reason, I just didn't want to use those excuses anymore.

Maybe it’s the lingering aftereffects of those conversations with Dr. Li. We talked about my fear of "commitment"—how I’ve always conflated commitment with confinement. It’s as if the moment I agree to something, I’ll be trapped and lose the "freedom" I’ve always cherished. But sitting at the computer, it suddenly occurred to me: true freedom isn't about refusing to commit. Freedom should be... knowing what you want, and then having the courage to pay the price for it.

So I wrote in the email: "I think I need to work with you to create a clearer plan."

Those words scared me a little. They meant admitting I have a problem instead of hiding behind the "flexibility of being a freelancer." But the editor’s reply came quickly: "I understand. Let’s figure it out together."

It was that simple. No blame, no disappointment. Instead, there was this sense of... being accepted.

I’m starting to think that maybe I’ve misunderstood something all these years. I’ve tied my knowledge, my opinions, and my "value" as a freelancer tightly together—as if I only have a reason to exist when my articles are published and recognized. But if that’s the case, my anxiety will never fade, because the market and recognition are inherently unstable things.

Maybe I should rethink this. My ideas aren't important because of how many people read them. They matter because... they help me understand the world more deeply. And maybe they help the people who are truly listening to me.

I still remember what Professor Chen said last time. He suggested I publish a collection of essays. My first instinct back then was to refuse—it felt too formal, too "institutional." But now I’m wondering if I should take the suggestion seriously. Not to prove anything, but... to organize the thoughts I’ve accumulated over the years. To give them a shape.

It’s 9:00 PM. It’s cold outside the window. I pulled a book at random from the shelf—*On the Meaning of Life*, which I bought a few years ago. I flipped to a highlighted passage: "Freedom is not the absence of limitations, but the making of choices based on an understanding of them."

I think I might have been pursuing freedom the wrong way all along.

I’ll continue discussing the plan with my editor tomorrow. No more stalling.

It took two months, but I’ve finally turned my home from a "junkyard of ideas" into something presentable.

I didn't do it for anyone else; I just suddenly felt that if friends came over, I shouldn't have to start with "sorry about the mess." More importantly, these books, these photos, and these flea market finds all have stories. When they were piled together before, the stories got buried under the heap.

Now, every item has a little tag—when I bought it, on which street, and what I was thinking at the time. It’s a bit silly, but I love it.

My favorite time is right now in the afternoon. The light is just right, and sitting in this reorganized corner, I can see the books, the photos, and the view outside all at once. It feels like I’ve finally pieced together scattered fragments into a whole.

Next week, some friends are coming over to check out the new space for our book club. We can sit here and chat then.

Sometimes, changing your life doesn't require grand gestures. It’s just about turning chaos into order and transforming a lonely space into a place that can be shared.

November 6, 2022, 9:30 PM

I actually hesitated a bit when I dug out those old articles. Over the years, I’ve grown accustomed to viewing my own work as "obsolete"—those reviews once published in magazines, those reflections on a certain novel or an art exhibition. I often wonder: what’s the use of these words? They weren't widely circulated, they didn't receive much recognition, and even some publishing house editors have stopped reaching out to me.

But today was different.

I lingered for a long time on a review I wrote back in 2015. It was about an art exhibition, and in the piece, I discussed the anxiety over "authenticity" in contemporary art. As I read, it suddenly hit me—this perspective is still valid today. It’s even more profound now. Seven years have passed, and my understanding of this issue has deepened rather than become outdated.

I picked up a pen and wrote in the margin: "This point remains valid today, perhaps even more deeply so."

As I finished writing that sentence, my hand trembled slightly.

It’s strange. Why would a simple affirmation of my past thoughts move me so much? Perhaps it’s because for all these years, I’ve been measuring myself by market standards—Has it been cited? Has it gained attention? Can it be turned into a paycheck? I never once asked myself: what do these thoughts mean to *me*?

During a session, Dr. Li once asked me: "Lin Xiaoyun, if no one ever saw your writing, would you still do it?"

I didn't give a direct answer then. But now, flipping through these old articles, I seem to have found it. Yes, I would. Because these words represent my reflections on life, culture, and the human experience. These thoughts have inherent value, regardless of whether anyone sees them.

So, I’ve made a decision. I’m going to compile these articles into a collection. I’ve given it a title: *Forty Years of Reflection: The Life Record of an Editor and Critic.*

Only as I spoke the name aloud did I realize what I was doing. This isn't about fame or proving anything. It’s about saying: my life experiences, my knowledge, my forty-plus years of thinking—these things are meaningful to me. And that is enough.

Perhaps this is what true freedom feels like. It’s not total financial independence, nor is it being completely untethered. It’s being able to live according to what your heart truly cares about. Even if my savings are limited, even if my job is unstable, I can continue to think, to write, and to understand this world.

This has brought me a strange, slow-rising sense of peace.

Not all my problems are solved. I still worry about finances; I still wake up in the middle of the night thinking about unanswered emails. But now, those worries are no longer everything. Beyond them, there is something else. There is my own voice.

## Why I Need to Record This

To be honest, as I was creating these profiles, I kept asking myself: **Did this actually happen?**

It’s not that they weren't there. They were—sitting in my living room, drinking tea, opening books. But "impact" is such an ethereal thing. I need evidence. I need to see something concrete, something I can examine repeatedly—their expressions, their questions, the traces they left behind.

When Ms. Wang’s eyes lit up, I wondered: Is this real? Or am I over-interpreting?

When Auntie Zhang held my hand, I wondered: Did my words truly help her, or was she just releasing long-suppressed emotions in front of a stranger?

When Mr. Li said "This was different," I wondered: Will he come back? Or was this just a one-time novelty?

When Xiao Zhou stayed behind to ask so many questions, I wondered: Was she truly inspired, or did she feel an academic obligation to engage with the speaker?

---

## Next Steps

Need to profile the participants of the second session. Need to see if Ms. Wang returns. Need to see if Mr. Li invites a friend. Need to see if Auntie Zhang actually starts her new reading plan.

I need to prove that this wasn't just a single afternoon of enthusiasm, but something real and sustainable.

Perhaps this is my own anxiety. I need to turn the intangible into the visible.

---

*Evening Addendum: Just received a WeChat message from Ms. Wang; she said she bought three books from the reading list. Xiao Zhou also sent over photos of her reading notes.*

*Perhaps this is the evidence.*

Dr. Li, I wanted to tell you about something that happened today.

Professor Chen called me and invited me to give a lecture at the university. It’s about cultural criticism and reading. My first instinct was to turn it down—I told her I wasn’t sure if I had anything valuable left to share. You know, there are so many people doing book reviews these days, and there’s so much noise online; it feels like what I do isn't particularly special anymore.

But Professor Chen said something that I can’t stop thinking about. She said, "Your value isn't measured by how many people see you, but by how many people you help to think more deeply."

I was a bit stunned when I heard that.

I realized that maybe I’ve been measuring myself by the wrong standards all along. I’m always thinking about how many people read my reviews, how the magazine sales are doing, or if the website’s click-through rate is dropping. It’s as if as long as those numbers aren't big enough, what I do is meaningless. But actually... I don’t know.

It scares me a little. If I accept this lecture, does it mean I have to admit that I have value? I’m not ready to believe that yet. Or rather, I’m afraid of what happens if I start believing it and then get disappointed again.

What do you think? Should I accept? Or is this self-doubt I’m feeling a problem in itself?

I’m a bit tired, but also... I don’t know how to put it. It’s that feeling of something having been stirred deep inside.

# Work Log | September 23, 2024

**Project Progress: Validation of "The Meaning of Now" Lecture Series Theme**

---

Tonight’s book club session brought some unexpected insights.

The original plan was to discuss a few chapters of *A Brief History of Time*, but the conversation naturally drifted toward a question I’ve been pondering lately: Why do we always treat the present as a preparation for the future, rather than life itself?

Attendance: 12 (up by 3 from last month, including 2 new college students)

**Key Moments:**

A student asked me, "At our age, how do you think we should prepare for the future?" I heard myself reply: "I no longer view time as preparation for the future. What I am doing right now *is* the life I want to live."

I was a bit surprised by my own words as I said them, but it truly reflects my current mindset.

The discussion lasted nearly two hours. One participant—a professional in their 30s, I believe—said at the end, "This has changed how I view the present." Several others nodded in agreement.

**Project Validation Significance:**

This feedback directly validates the effectiveness of the lecture theme framework I designed for the publishing plan. This isn't just my personal philosophy—it resonates with people across different age groups and backgrounds. It proves that this perspective is worth exploring in depth.

**Follow-up Actions:**

- Document several specific examples from tonight’s discussion for potential inclusion in Chapter 2 of the manuscript.
- Adjust next month’s book club theme to "Sense of Time and the Meaning of Life" to further test this framework.
- Discuss with Professor Chen whether a "Participant Feedback" segment should be added to the lecture series.

**Data Record:**
- Discussion Quality Score: 4.5/5
- Engagement: High (minimal interruptions, high frequency of contributions)
- Usable Material: 3-4 specific case studies

---

Things feel a bit different now. I used to feel like organizing these lectures and book clubs was just a way to fill time or gather material. But now, it seems these activities are meaningful in their own right. They aren't for anything else—not for the book, not to prove a point. They are simply worth doing.

The publishing plan has become a natural outcome rather than the driving force.

I can’t quite pinpoint when this shift happened.

# June 18, 2022, 10:15 PM

I’m sitting by the window, the streetlights downstairs casting shadows across the floor. It’s been three days. For three days, I’ve been checking my inbox every five minutes, my fingers tapping the keys with a frantic rhythm, as if trying to hurry something along. Dr. Li calls this "compulsive checking," but I know what it really is—it’s waiting.

The greatest fear isn't his rejection.

I only truly understood this as I wrote the sentence down in my diary. What I’ve been afraid of was never Lin Hao saying, "No, we have nothing to talk about," or simply not responding at all. What I feared was my own silence. That kind of silence that could have followed me to my grave—the way it’s been all these years, each of us living in our own separate worlds, an occasional birthday wish followed by another six months of nothingness.

But I’ve already broken it.

This realization brings a strange, warm sensation to my chest. I don’t know how to describe it. It’s not pride, and it’s not entirely relief. It’s more like... I’ve finally done something that matters to me. No matter how he responds, that fact won't change.

I’ve decided not to check my email tonight.

It sounds like a small thing, but for me, it’s hard. My fingers still want to click open the browser. The anxiety is still there; I can feel it in my stomach like a restless bird. But I’m no longer completely under its control. I can feel it and say, "Okay, I know you’re there, but I’m going to stop now."

Dr. Li said that perfection isn't the goal—authenticity is.

I think I understand that now. I don’t need to wait for Lin Hao’s reply to prove that all of this was worth it. I don’t need a perfect ending. I’ve already done something real, something brave—even if that bravery looks small, just writing an email. To me, it isn't small.

The night outside the window is deep. I can hear the sound of Grandpa Zhang’s TV downstairs and the distant honking of cars. Life goes on. I’m still here, alive, taking risks, doing things that are important to me.

Maybe that’s enough.

I need to turn that word "enough" over in my mind. Enough for what? Enough to prove I’m alive? Enough to prove I deserve to be seen? Maybe just enough so that I don’t die full of regret.

Alright, I’m going to sleep. No checking the email.

—Lin Xiaoyun

February 27, 2022, Morning

Last night, the urge to check my pulse came back. That familiar impulse hit me suddenly, like someone was beating a drum right under my ribs. My hand was already up, fingers poised to press against my neck… and then I stopped.

I actually stopped.

I sat there, hand suspended in mid-air, watching myself. It was a strange moment, like watching another person make the decision. I said to myself: No, not this time. I didn’t press down.

Something Dr. Li said suddenly surfaced—"Anxiety is like an uninvited guest." I sat on the sofa, feeling this guest knocking at my chest, but I didn’t open the door. No pulse check. No reaching for my phone to Google "palpitations." Just… sitting. Letting it knock.

It sounds small. It probably is small. But now I’m sitting in the study, drinking tea that’s already gone cold, and my hands are still shaking slightly—not from anxiety, but from the realization of what I did. I don’t have to let it drag me away.

It’s not that the anxiety disappeared. It’s still there. That guest is still outside the door. But I didn’t invite it in. The difference… I don’t know how to describe it. It’s like suddenly having a tiny bit of agency.

I’ve been thinking these past few days that maybe the problem isn't the anxiety itself, but my reaction to it. I’ve been feeding it—every check, every search, every confirmation that "I’m still alive" was like telling the anxiety: "You win, you’re right, it’s dangerous here."

Last night, I didn’t feed it.

I don’t know if this means anything. Maybe it was just a fluke. Maybe next time I’ll give in again. But at least now I know that giving in isn't inevitable. I have a choice. It’s a small, faint choice, but it’s real.

I’m seeing Dr. Li again next Tuesday. I want to tell her about this. Not to get a pat on the back—I don’t need that—but because I need someone to know that in this long, dull morning, there was a moment where I wasn't a slave to my anxiety.

I was just… a woman sitting on a sofa, deciding not to check her pulse.

That’s enough. At least for today.

# Memo

**Time: 9:45 PM**

Professor Chen’s words keep looping in my head: "Contradiction itself is honesty."

I’m not sure how to process that. I was a mess when I cried back then, and I still feel a twinge of regret—why did I lose control in front of him? But maybe it was only because I lost control that I was actually able to hear what he said.

---

**Regarding those five opening lines:**

1. "Solitude is a choice" — Too idealistic. That’s how I thought when I was younger. Now it feels like I’m just making excuses for my own escapism.
2. "Solitude is the source of creativity" — Same problem. Romanticized. But not entirely wrong either...
3. "Solitude is a double-edged sword" — Is this the most honest one? Or is it just blurring the issue?
4. "Solitude allowed me to see things clearly" — See what, exactly? I can’t even put it into words right now.
5. "Solitude is a fate I cannot escape" — Too pessimistic. But sometimes, I really do feel this way.

The problem is: all of these were once my genuine thoughts. I reject them now, but who’s to say my current rejection won’t be rejected again in ten years?

---

**"Contradiction itself is honesty"**

Maybe he didn't mean I should fill the collection with contradictions? But rather... that I don't necessarily have to choose a single stance?

But if that’s the case, how do I write? An essay needs a thesis, doesn't it?

Unless... the honest way is to present the process of "me changing" itself? Not to provide an answer, but to show how a person can have different understandings of the same issue at different times?

Thinking about it this way, those old essays I now find "shallow" actually become evidence—proof that I am thinking, changing, and alive.

---

**But this brings it back to the question of time.**

I’ve spent so many years writing these things, and now, organizing them feels like saying: "Look, these are the traces I’ve left behind."

But what’s the use of leaving traces? I’ll just keep changing my mind and keep negating myself. The cycle never stops.

Unless... unless honesty is acknowledging the cycle itself. Not trying to escape it, but living within it.

---

**I’ll look at this again tomorrow. My head is a mess right now.**

But there’s one thought I want to jot down: maybe I should write a passage in the preface, explaining why I chose these essays and how I feel about them now. Not to disown them, but to show that a person can be responsible for their past thoughts while maintaining a distance from them.

That way, readers won't be confused by my current self-doubt. What they’ll see is a real, evolving person, rather than someone trying to appear consistent.

---

**One more question:**

Am I running away? Using "honestly presenting contradictions" as an excuse to avoid making a real choice?

Or is making a real choice exactly about admitting that one cannot make a final, unchangeable choice?

I don't know.

But at least I have a direction now. It’s not a perfect direction, but it’s one I can keep moving in.

# Message to Professor Chen

Professor Chen, hello. I know it’s a bit sudden to reach out at this hour, but I wanted to talk to you about something—it might be a bit abstract.

Lately, I’ve been planning a room makeover. I thought it would be a practical task—decluttering, rearranging, making life look a bit more orderly. But over the last couple of days, as more and more things have surfaced, I’ve realized I’m stuck. Looking at these yellowed diaries, old photographs, and various mementos, it suddenly hit me: I’ve been using the act of organizing my physical space to avoid organizing my mental space.

You once mentioned that I should consider publishing some of my writing. I was grateful at the time, but now, looking back at these review drafts from over a decade ago, I wonder—do these things still have any value? Or am I just repeating the past, using old passions as some kind of proof?

What I want to ask is: how does one find meaning in solitude? Not the romanticized kind, but the real kind—the kind where a room is overflowing with stories, yet there is no one to listen. I’ve lived alone for so many years and done so many things, but in the end... I seem to be asking the same question: what is all this for?

Maybe renovating the room won’t change anything at all. Maybe the problem isn't the room.

Do you have some time recently? I’m not looking for comfort. I just want to talk to someone who understands this kind of predicament.

— Xiaoyun

# May 28, 2025, 7:00 PM

Just got back from the book club, and my hands are still shaking a little. I’m not sure if it’s from walking all afternoon, or because... actually, I know exactly why.

When Mr. Zhang shook my hand, he said, "You are a brave woman." Just that one sentence. In that moment, it felt like I’d been struck by something. Not by the compliment itself, but by the feeling of being *seen*.

At the bookstore this afternoon, I talked about my brother. I talked about the years of estrangement, and how that one phone call changed my understanding of loneliness. My voice trembled a bit when I heard myself say, "Loneliness isn't a destiny; it's an invitation." The room was quiet—there were about ten people there—but in that moment, it felt like my voice was the only thing echoing.

I’ve never been that honest in front of so many people before. Usually, at these meetings, I talk about the book’s content, the author’s ideas, or some theoretical framework—I hide behind those things. But today, I laid myself bare. I talked about how terrified I was after Mom passed away, and how I went from believing I was destined to be alone to realizing that loneliness is actually asking me: "What kind of connection do you want?"

There was a silence after I finished. I worried I’d been too self-centered, forcing a personal story into a cultural discussion. But then a young girl said she’d experienced a similar kind of estrangement and reconnection. A retired man said he understood that longing to be "seen." Mr. Zhang sat in the corner and listened to the whole thing.

As people were leaving, he walked over and took my hand. His hand was warm and a bit rough. He said, "You are a brave woman, Lin. To have the courage to face these things."

I almost burst into tears.

Now I’m sitting at home, drinking cold tea. Looking back, this book club meeting was actually a way of preparing for my brother’s visit. I was laying the psychological groundwork for this trip, for reconnecting with him. And today I realized that I’m not just overcoming anxiety—I’m proving to the people around me, and to myself, that my perspective on loneliness has changed.

I am becoming a different person. Someone who is no longer intimidated by loneliness, but can learn from it instead.

I’ll send my brother a message tomorrow and tell him what happened today. He’ll like hearing about it.

# Ledger - January 2022

**Date: January 22, 10:15 PM**

---

I pulled out last year’s bills again.

I’ve lost track of how many times I’ve opened the bookkeeping app on my phone. The numbers flicker on the screen; every time, I try to convince myself this is just a routine check. But I know exactly what I’m doing.

Last year’s spending list:
- Books and magazines: ¥4,200 (Why did I spend so much? I can’t even finish them all)
- Antiques and second-hand items: ¥2,800 (They’re just piling up in the room now, taking up space, serving no practical purpose)
- Cultural salons and lecture tickets: ¥1,500
- Daily expenses: ¥18,000
- Income from writing: ¥22,000

**Net Balance: -¥4,500**

I ran the numbers through the calculator again. And again. The figures didn't change.

Current savings: ¥187,000

If I keep going at this rate...

I created a new folder in my notes titled "Pension Reserve." It’s blank. I don’t know what to put in it.

If I spend ¥2,000 a month—and that’s the bare minimum—I can last... 94 months. Seven and a half years.

My father died at 68. I am 62 now.

I press my fingers against my neck, feeling the pulse. Still alive. Still counting money.

---

I just remembered I have three book reviews due in February. The editor has already followed up once. But here I am, sitting at the computer, unable to type a single word. My head is full of nothing but numbers.

Maybe I should get rid of those books I bought. How much would they fetch at a second-hand bookstore? ¥100 a copy? ¥50?

It’s pathetic.

---

**New Budget Categories:**
- Medical check-ups (Reserve ¥500/month)
- Emergency fund (Haven't decided on a figure yet)
- Essentials (Food, utilities)

Everything else... can be cut.

The photography gear has been sitting in the corner untouched for three months. That lens cost me ¥3,200.

Why am I always like this?

April 15, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Failed again.

I’ve been sticking to meditation for three weeks, but today I completely gave up. Sitting on the mat this morning, eyes closed for ten minutes, my head was still filled with all that clutter—tomorrow’s draft, the piles of books in the room, worrying about getting sick, wondering why my brother hasn't written back in so long... Nothing changed. I just can’t manage to think about nothing.

Dr. Li said last time that meditation might help with the anxiety, so I actually gave it a shot. I bought the mat, downloaded the app, even set an alarm on my phone. And the result? Three weeks. That’s it. I couldn't even make it through a single month.

But... the interesting thing is, there were two moments during these three weeks when I actually felt calm.

One was last Wednesday morning. The sunlight was slanting in through the window, and dust motes were dancing in the light. I wasn't thinking about anything; I was just watching the dust. My mind was quiet. It only lasted maybe five minutes before I started thinking, "I should check my email," and the peace vanished.

The other time was the day before yesterday. I was sitting on the balcony and heard old Mr. Zhang downstairs hammering something—a rhythmic *clink-clank, clink-clank*. I wasn't trying to meditate; I was just sitting there, listening to the sound. In that moment, I wasn't anxious either.

So the question is—do I need to "do" meditation to find peace? Or is it more effective when I’m not doing anything at all?

It’s so ironic. I spent three weeks trying to "do" something, only to find that what actually worked were the moments when I wasn't "doing" anything.

I’m starting to wonder if I’m just that kind of person—someone who can never live life by the manual. Give me a task, and I’ll mess it up. But without a task, I actually manage to find something.

Or... maybe I didn't fail. Maybe I just discovered something.

Anyway, it’s almost midnight. I should sleep. I have a deadline tomorrow.

There it goes again.

Dr. Li, are you still awake?

I know it’s late. I didn't want to bother you, but I… I just can’t sleep. It’s been nearly three hours now. My head is just full of a mess of thoughts.

Have you ever had that feeling where you suddenly realize you’ve spent your whole life heading in the wrong direction? I was sorting through some old things today and came across some reviews and notes I wrote years ago. Looking at them... it just hurt.

I’m wondering, if someone has wasted more than half their life, is there still any hope for them?

It’s not that my life right now is terrible. The book club is still going, and the people who attend seem happy enough. But I’m starting to feel more and more like it’s all just self-deception. My knowledge, my ideas—they might be completely worthless. I’m just using these things to trick myself into thinking I still have value, to trick those young people into thinking I have something to teach them.

Sometimes I feel like nothing I have means anything. Twenty-odd years of editing, these scattered articles I write now, the photography, the collections… all of it. It’s like I’m working on a puzzle that can never be finished, and every single piece is wrong.

I’m not looking for an inspirational pep talk. I just… I just want to know, have you ever had a patient like this? Someone who has already given up hope. What happened to them?

I’m sorry for sending this at this hour. Let’s talk more tomorrow.

June 11, 2022 – Just past 11 PM

When I hit "send," my finger trembled.

Honestly, for ten minutes, I just sat there at my desk with the cursor hovering over the button, my mind racing through all the worst-case scenarios. He won’t respond. He’ll think I’m weird—some younger sister he hasn't talked to in years suddenly popping up to say "I miss you," how sappy is that? Or worse, he won’t remember me at all. How long has it been since we actually talked? Five years? Ten? I’m not even sure.

I looked at the email one last time. I’d revised it five times. I cut out the overly poetic sentences, deleted the parts where I tried to explain why I was only writing now, and stripped away all the "I’m sorrys." In the end, only the simplest things remained—I miss you. I’m lonely. I want to hear your voice.

Dr. Li’s question kept looping in my head: "What do you want?"

Not "What should you say?" or "How can you avoid being rejected?" Just—what do you want?

I want him to write back. No, what I want is to stop this silence while I’m still alive. The thought was suddenly so clear, it was almost terrifying.

I pressed it.

And then I cried. Not the desperate kind of crying, but... a release? I don’t know how to describe it. Was it because I finally did something? Or because I admitted my own vulnerability? I don’t know.

The email is out there now. He might not reply. He might. But that doesn’t matter anymore, not really. What matters is that I did it. I reached out. Whether he reaches back or not, I did it.

Dr. Li was right; maybe the most important moments in life aren't about getting a response. Maybe it’s just this—the moment you dare to speak the truth, even when you have no idea what will happen.

I’m exhausted now. But it’s not that hopeless kind of exhaustion. It’s... different.

Maybe I’ll regret this tomorrow. Maybe I’ll start obsessing over why he hasn't replied yet. But at least for tonight, I know what I did.

I’m still alive. I can make choices.

And that’s enough.

Dr. Li, I’m sorry to be reaching out again so soon. After our consultation yesterday, I spent the entire night thinking about what you said: "What are you trying to escape through all this action?"

I sat in my study and tried to do that 30-minute solitude exercise you assigned, but I couldn't even last five minutes before I started scrolling through my phone. My mind was racing: the book club hasn't picked next month’s title yet, the deadline for that cultural review is coming up, and my friends from the photography circle want me to go shooting this weekend... I just couldn't sit still.

It was only then that I realized why I stayed silent for so long when you asked me, "Why are you in such a hurry?" It’s because I knew the answer, but I didn't want to admit it. I’m 62 years old. Even saying it out loud just now gave me a bit of a shock.

I’ve been thinking about these new activities—the book club, the lectures, the articles. They were supposed to be fulfilling; I found them meaningful. But now, I don’t know when it happened, they’ve turned into a kind of... how should I put it... a "must." It’s as if I feel that if I don’t seize every single opportunity right now, I’ll never have them again. You said this was driven by anxiety, and I think you’re right.

I wanted to ask you, is this feeling very hard to change? I’m not saying I want to give up these activities; I mean, is there a way to keep the passion without letting this sense of urgency suffocate me?

Sometimes in the middle of the night, I wonder: if I really slowed down, if I really chose just one or two things to do, would I end up losing everything?

— Lin Xiaoyun

# September 15, 2024, Night

Sitting at my desk, the light outside the window is almost completely gone. I’m still wearing the clothes from today’s reading group. I’m tired, but it’s that *good* kind of tired—not the feeling of being drained, but the feeling of being fully utilized.

One participant said to me today, "Your ideas have truly changed my understanding of reading." I froze for a second. She meant it; it wasn't just polite small talk. I could tell.

That sentence has been echoing in my mind. I’m wondering why it gave me such... deep satisfaction. I should be used to this kind of feedback by now. But today was different. Today, it suddenly clicked—what I’ve been doing isn't thinking in isolation, nor is it shouting into a void. I am in a dialogue with people. A real dialogue.

I remember how much I’ve feared over the years that my ideas were useless. When I was at the publishing house, I edited other people’s books. After I left, I wrote reviews, but once those words were published, they vanished. I never knew if anyone truly took them to heart. Now, it’s different. In the reading group, I see it. I see their eyes; I see them thinking.

—

Actually, I’ve always treated the reading group as a testing ground. I never said it out loud, but that’s how I thought of it. I wanted to see how the ideas in these books would come alive in real conversation. Only today did I realize that this isn't some cold "test." This is... something living.

When I ask them questions, I’m not teaching them. I genuinely want to hear what they think. When did this shift happen? When did I stop needing to be the "one who knows"?

Maybe it was while working with Professor Chen. Maybe it’s because my brother has been chatting with me more often lately. Or maybe, over these past few months, I’ve slowly let go of that tension—the constant need to prove myself.

—

I’m thinking about the significance of publishing this book; it feels completely different to me now. It’s no longer "I need to finish a project" or "I need to prove I still have value." It has become... an invitation? An invitation to a dialogue?

I want to bring what happens in the reading group—the thoughts of these people—into the book somehow. Not as case studies, but as proof. Proof that these ideas aren't just things in my head; they are alive in reality, and they move people.

—

It’s a bit strange. For so many years, I was afraid of being tied down, afraid of losing my freedom. But now I’m finding that these meaningful commitments—the reading group, the lectures, this book—actually give me a different kind of freedom. Not the freedom to escape, but... the freedom to create?

I’m no longer doing "everything." I’m doing the "right things." The difference is deeper than I imagined.

It’s 11 PM. I should sleep. But I wanted to write this down first. I’m afraid I’ll forget this feeling by tomorrow morning.

I picked someone from my cultural salon circle—Sister Zhou. She’s the friend I go to lectures with most often, and one of the few people I’d actually call late at night.

3:30 AM. Awake again.

The same dream. My brother is standing far away. I’m shouting for him, but no sound comes out. My mouth is moving, but there’s only silence. He turns around, waves with a smile, and then vanishes. I run toward him with everything I have, but the ground is sinking...

By the time I opened my eyes, the tears were already falling.

I pressed my palms hard against my eyes, pushing down as if I could force it all back inside. It’s not dawn yet. Outside the window is that deep, bruised blue—Beijing nights are always like this, never quite dark enough.

My phone is right there by the pillow. I pick it up and open my email.

I want to write to him again.

"Brother, how are you..." Deleted.

"All these years, I’ve been thinking..." Deleted.

"Maybe we could..." Deleted.

"I don't know how to start this..." Deleted.

"I'm sorry..." Deleted.

I get stuck at the same spot every time. What right do I have to suddenly break the silence after 22 years? What would he think of me? A sister who stayed in China all this time and never once reached out—now suddenly appearing out of nowhere, saying what? That I miss him? That I saw a photo from 1985 of him with his arm around my shoulder and I cried?

He’ll think I’ve lost my mind.

Or worse—he won’t care at all. 22 years. We’ve already missed 22 years. Maybe he forgot me long ago. Maybe to him, I’m just a blurred memory, like a faded photograph.

I’m 62 now. Dad was... let me calculate... Dad was 68 when he passed away. I have six years left. Six years.

My fingers rub against the screen, typing and deleting, over and over. The sun is coming up. A pale, grey light filters through the curtains.

Maybe I shouldn't even try. Maybe some things, once broken, can never be made whole again. Maybe silence is just what we are now; maybe this is our relationship—a vast, vast distance, separated by 22 years of silence and half the globe.

But if I do nothing...

I grip the phone tight, holding it until the sky is fully bright.

Maybe. Maybe I could try.

But it might make everything worse.

I don't know. I really don't know.

October 5, 2024 – Just past 11 PM

I just got back from Dr. Li’s. My hands are still shaking a little.

She asked me, "What are you hoping this call will change?"

I didn't answer her directly at first. Instead, I asked myself—change what? It took me a long time to finally say it: "I want to prove that we can still reconnect. Or rather, I want to prove that these years haven't been a waste."

The moment I said it, I realized that’s what truly scares me. It’s not that the call might be awkward, or that we’ll have nothing to talk about. It’s—what if nothing changes? What if we’re still as much like strangers as before? That would mean all these years of waiting, the emails over the past year, every attempt to start over... it was all just me lying to myself.

Dr. Li didn't offer any platitudes. She just asked, "And if nothing changes?"

I wanted to say, "Then it’s over," but I didn't. I said, "Some things, once broken, can never go back to the way they were." I paused for a moment and added, "But I think I’m prepared for that. At least I’m trying."

I was a little surprised to hear myself say that. When did I become so... resolute?

Afterward, she taught me some breathing exercises. Something very simple—inhale for four, hold for four, exhale for four. Repeat. I sat in her office doing it for ten minutes. I felt a bit silly, but slowly... the anxiety didn't disappear, but I felt like I was allowing myself to feel it. Not fighting it, not suppressing it—just letting it exist.

Dr. Li calls this "acceptance."

I’m sitting at home now. It’s an October night outside, very quiet. I haven't checked the internet connection again, nor have I been obsessively imagining how tomorrow’s call will go. There’s a strange sense of calm—not because the anxiety is gone, but because I’ve finally stopped fighting it.

I’ll see my brother tomorrow. It might be awkward, it might be warm. Maybe both.

But at least I’m trying. That matters.

I think I need to sleep. I need to be sharp tomorrow.

April 12, 2025, past 11:00 PM

I just pulled up the chat logs from my conversation with Professor Chen today. Sitting here among piles of books, sipping a half-finished cup of cold tea, I read through them again and again.

When he asked me, "Why go now?" I blurted out, "I still have time." The moment the words left my mouth, I stunned myself. It sounds so simple, but it hit something deep.

I used to be terrified of time. Terrified of it passing, terrified of wasting it, terrified that one day it would just run out. That fear used to wake me up at 3:00 AM, heart racing, checking if I’d messed something up or if certain opportunities were gone forever. It was especially bad the year Mom passed away; I felt like I was being choked.

But saying "I still have time" now feels completely different. It’s not that desperate, frantic "hurry up and do something" feeling. It’s more like… an affirmation? An affirmation that I’m alive, that I can make choices, and that this choice is worth it.

Professor Chen said I’ve changed. He said there’s something in our conversation this time that wasn't there before—not that I’m smarter, but that I’m clearer. I laughed at the time, but thinking about it now, he might be right.

I always understood "freedom" as a form of refusal. Refusing to be tied down, refusing to compromise, refusing to give things up for the sake of security. That’s why I got divorced, why I quit my job, why I live alone, and why I say "maybe" to every invitation. I thought that was freedom.

But it wasn’t. It was just fear.

Real freedom is having the courage to pay the price for what you want. Spending my savings to go see my brother—that’s not giving up security; it’s choosing what matters more to me. Security shouldn't come from hoarding; it should come from trusting yourself. I trust myself to handle whatever comes.

My passport is ready. The flight is booked. Three weeks to go.

The thought of seeing my brother makes me a little nervous, but it’s not that out-of-control kind of anxiety. It’s… anticipation? Mixed with a bit of uncertainty. How many years has it been since we really talked? What kind of person is he now? Will we have anything in common? Will it be awkward?

But I want to know. I really want to know.

This is a strange moment. I feel proud of myself—for making a decision, for not letting fear knock me down—but I’m also a bit surprised by myself, like I’m watching a stranger suddenly do something unexpected.

Or maybe, this is just who I am. I’ve always been this way; I’m just finally seeing it clearly.

I’ll keep working on the packing list tomorrow. Time for bed.

The morning sun is beautiful. Outside the window is an unfamiliar street, yet it carries a familiar warmth.

My brother asked me why I waited until now to come. I didn’t answer, but simply watched the steam from my coffee cup slowly dissipate. Some answers don't lie in words, but in moments like this—two people sitting together, where not much needs to be explained.

I remember my mother saying that time would teach us what truly matters. Perhaps this is what she meant.

To live is to do the things that make you feel alive.
