June 2, 2025, 10:30 PM

It’s been nearly an hour since I hung up the phone, yet I’m still sitting at my desk, my fingers hovering and stuttering over the keyboard. I want to write something down, but I don’t know where to begin.

My brother’s voice is still echoing in my ears. That kind of candor—it’s been so long since I’ve heard it from him. "I’m sorry we’ve been distant for so long," he said, and I could feel his sincerity. It wasn't just politeness or an attempt to ease the awkwardness. He truly regrets it. And so do I. Realizing this makes me want to cry, but it’s not a sad cry. It’s more like... a release?

He said, "I’m looking forward to reading what you’ve written. Maybe we can have more conversations about this topic together." That sentence keeps spinning in my head. He wasn’t just inviting me to visit; he was inviting us into something—a space where we can explore and think together. This is more than just a three-week visit. I suddenly understand: this is a beginning. A foundation we can keep building upon.

I always thought loneliness was my fate, or perhaps the price I paid for choosing freedom. But tonight, talking to my brother, I felt a different kind of intimacy—not the effortless, automatic closeness we had when we were young, wrapped in the safety of family ties. It was... the intimacy of two adults who have experienced life, walked their separate paths, and finally come to understand one another. This kind of closeness feels heavier, more precious.

We’re both getting old. I accepted that fact long ago, but tonight it took on a new meaning. It’s not fear, but—urgency? No, more like clarity. We can’t waste any more time. It’s not a sense of rushing, but a confirmation: we still have a chance, and that chance is real and within reach.

Tomorrow, I’ll start making a packing list. But more importantly, I want to record every detail of this conversation—the things he said, the things I didn't respond to properly at the time but am only now truly understanding. Perhaps I’ll keep chewing over this dialogue in my diary until I can fully capture it in words.

This trip has suddenly taken on much more meaning. It’s no longer just a challenge to "overcome my anxiety," but a genuine reconnection. With my brother, and with myself.

I feel a strange sense of peace right now.

Xiao Zhou, I’ve been wanting to say thank you.

Our conversation at the café today stayed with me all afternoon. When you said, "I’m considering it too," it really hit home. I thought I was the only one waking up at 3:00 AM gripped by some nameless dread; I thought I was the only one who felt so much shame about seeing a therapist. But hearing you say that made me realize, suddenly, that maybe it’s nothing to be ashamed of.

You know, what I fear most isn't the anxiety itself, but the way it makes me feel like an outlier. It’s as if everyone else has their life figured out, while I’m the only one obsessing over trivial details, fearing aging, fearing loss, and fearing that my life has no meaning. But after hearing you speak so candidly today, I finally understand—maybe we’re all in the same boat, and it’s just that no one usually wants to admit it.

I’ve felt so lonely these past few years. My parents are gone, my brother is overseas, and my marriage ended a long time ago. I thought freelancing would give me freedom, but instead, it’s left me feeling more stuck than ever. The unfinished projects piled up at home, the scattered books, the mess of a life… sometimes I look at it all and wonder, what on earth am I doing? But talking to you, I realized you’re asking the same questions, just in a different way.

I want to keep this conversation going. Not the superficial small talk, but real, deep discussions—like what we do at the cultural salons, but perhaps even more honest.

We’re discussing *The Wisdom of Life* at next month’s book club; are you interested? Or we could just find a time to talk one-on-one, like we did today. I think having someone truly understand your struggles is, in itself, a form of salvation.

Thank you for making me feel a little less alone.

Subject: We shouldn't stay silent anymore

Brother,

After receiving your email, I lay awake all night. It wasn't the tossing and turning of insomnia, but a quiet feeling, as if something had deeply touched me. "I regret that there has been so much silence between us"—I read that sentence over and over again.

You know, I always thought it was my fault. I thought I was too stubborn, too self-centered, and that was why the distance between us kept growing. Over the years, I’ve had countless impulses to break this silence, but every time I was held back by something—maybe pride, maybe the fear of being rejected, or maybe just not knowing where to start. Seeing that you feel this regret too, I suddenly realized that this silence was never one-sided. We were both waiting, both regretting, yet neither of us took that first step.

Rereading what you wrote about our parents, my eyes welled up. Do you remember Father’s discussions about books? And the way Mother looked in the library? These memories are both precious and heavy to me now. I often wonder if they were still here, would we have drifted this far apart? But perhaps it is precisely because they are gone that we should cherish each other even more.

I don’t want to waste any more time. Neither of us is young anymore, and I feel the urgency of time more and more. I want to hear more about your life there, to know what you’re thinking and what you’re doing. Maybe we could write more often, or find a time for a video call? I know the time difference might be an issue, but we can always figure something out.

Thank you for breaking the silence. I’ve been waiting for a word like this for a long time.

Xiaoyun

I want to know how these objects and ideas resonate in other people's hearts."

The moment I said it, I even caught myself off guard.

Is that the real motive?

It feels like... I’ve finally found something. Not for the royalties, not to be noticed, just... wanting to do it.

But—

But now, sitting at home, that peace feels a bit fragile again.

Why do I still care if the column editor likes my post? Why am I still calculating if enough people will show up for the book club? Why was my first instinct when I saw Professor Chen’s message to check if he mentioned any feedback on the lecture?

...Is this the anxiety coming back? Or has this always been a part of me?

---

Dr. Li said: sensitivity is not a flaw. I use it to understand, and I use it to create. So... is this craving for feedback just the other side of the same coin?

I need this peace, but I also need to acknowledge this vulnerability.

I can't pretend that I'm completely "cured."

Remember to organize this idea. Might write it into the column.

Professor Chen, are you still awake? I wanted to share something with you that just occurred to me.

Actually, I’ve been wondering why I feel so much guilt over things I haven't finished. Yesterday, I dug out some notes and reviews I wrote ten years ago. My initial thought was, "Here’s another pile of useless junk," but this time, my reaction was different.

I suddenly realized—maybe perfection was never the goal. What I mean is, these pieces, these ideas—they were simply my understanding of the world at that specific moment. They don't need to be published, seen, or even finished. In their own way, they are already complete.

I’ve been thinking about how all these years, I’ve used "publication" or "completion" as the sole metrics of value, as if that’s the only way to prove my thoughts have meaning. But what if I was just writing to record? To understand? To let these ideas be seen by my future self at some point in time?

When you suggested I publish a collection of essays, I was actually quite resistant because I felt it meant making a final judgment—a "final word" on things. But now I’m wondering if there’s another way to look at it. Not as a way to prove something to the world, but as a way to document something for myself.

What do you think? Have you ever felt this way—that the value of certain things has nothing to do with how finished they are or how much recognition they receive?

I’ve been a bit consumed by this thought lately. It feels like I’ve pushed open a door that I never even noticed was there.

Dr. Li, sorry to bother you so late.

Anyway, go back to sleep. We can talk tomorrow.

But... if you do have the time, I’d really like to find a slot to chat.

**Event Title: 4th Consultation with Dr. Li**

**Date:** Sunday, December 3, 2023
**Time:** 14:00–15:00
**Location:** Counseling Clinic (West Nanjing Road)
**Reminder:** Arrive 15 minutes early

---

**Notes:**

Something shifted during this conversation.

I started by saying that I feel like I’m constantly running—running from order, from commitment, from anything that might tie me down. After listening, Dr. Li asked a very simple question: "What are you running from right now?"

I didn't answer right away. I sat there for a long time before saying that I’m running from growing old, from being irrelevant, from being forgotten.

She said that perhaps freedom isn't about running away, but about making a choice. Choosing what is worth committing to, and what is worth letting go.

I thought about my brother’s letter. He said he’s been waiting for me to reach out. I always assumed he didn't care from afar, but in reality, I was running from the possibility of disappointment. I was running from admitting that I need people.

So maybe—maybe true freedom is knowing what you want, and then having the courage to pay the necessary price for it. Including certain forms of order. Including certain forms of commitment.

I told Dr. Li that I want to publish those articles. I want to properly organize them instead of letting them sit in my computer. I want to invite more friends over, to hear their stories and share mine. I want to write to my brother—a real letter, not just perfunctory greetings.

She asked me, "Do these changes scare you?"

I said, "Yes." But I’m starting to feel that this fear might mean these changes are real, and that they matter.

Next consultation scheduled for December 17.

---

**Keywords:** #Counseling #Transformation #Acceptance #Freedom #Commitment

August 18, 2022, shortly after 4:00 PM

It’s been half an hour since I left Dr. Li’s clinic, but I still haven’t gone home. I sat in the cafe downstairs for a while, drinking a latte that had gone cold. My hands are still shaking.

She asked me, "Is it the changes in the market, or how you view yourself?"

I couldn't speak. Then I said, "Both terrify me."

What’s even scarier is that I followed that with something I’ve never said to anyone—"Is it that the market doesn't need me, or that I’m simply not worth needing?" My eyes welled up the moment the words left my mouth. I gripped the armrest of the sofa so hard my knuckles turned white. Dr. Li didn't respond immediately; she just handed me a tissue.

I didn't cry. But that feeling was worse—like something was lodged in my chest, desperate to get out but stuck.

On the way back, I kept wondering: when did it start? When did I become the kind of person who tracks their own rejections in a spreadsheet, like documenting a slow death? I even made a chart for it. At the beginning of August, there were three invitations; now, there’s only one. I drew a downward curve on a piece of paper with a pen, watching my own value depreciate.

Forty years. I spent forty years learning, accumulating, and understanding this world. Literature, art, philosophy—I truly know these things. But now, a 22-year-old blogger can post a few breezy lines and earn more in tips than I do in a month of freelance fees. I’ve seen her content. Honestly, there’s no depth to it.

But why do people love her?

Maybe I never understood this era. Maybe my knowledge was never as valuable as I imagined.

Or—and this is the most terrifying part—maybe I really am obsolete.

I startled myself when I said it. But once it’s spoken, it can’t be taken back. Obsolete. Like an old movie, or a yellowed book. No one needs to look at it.

Dr. Li asked, "Where are these thoughts coming from?"

I couldn't say for sure. Maybe it started with that editor’s email—"Market demands have changed." Maybe it was seeing the balance in my savings account. Maybe it was realizing that at 62, no one is going to hire someone like me.

Maybe it started the moment I decided to quit my job back in 2007.

I always thought that was freedom. Now, it looks more like a trap. I chose instability, and now stability is drifting away from me. What if I had accepted those full-time job offers back then? What if I hadn't been so obsessed with that illusion called "freedom"?

I don't know what to do.

Tomorrow, I still have to write that one remaining commission. I have to pretend everything is fine. Pretend I still have value. Pretend I’m not someone discarded by the times.

But right now, this afternoon, I just want to admit it—I’m afraid. I am truly afraid.

It’s not just the lack of money I fear. It’s the fear that, perhaps, I am simply not worth needing.

Friday Morning, March 28, 2025

Another morning of good sleep. This isn't exactly "news" worth recording anymore, yet I still find myself wanting to write it down. For someone used to insomnia, suddenly being able to sleep through the night feels like the body has finally let go of a heavy burden.

I opened my inbox this morning to find the initial layout proofs for the essay collection from my editor. Seeing my own words presented so formally still feels a bit surreal. These pieces of writing, which I once thought would be washed away by time, are now becoming an actual book. I sat in front of the computer staring at them for a long time, feeling almost moved to tears—not out of regret, but a sense of closure.

I spoke with Professor Chen on the phone the day before yesterday. He said he’s looking forward to the final product and suggested I consider giving a lecture after it’s published. My reaction was interesting—I didn't immediately refuse, nor did I immediately agree. Instead, I said, "Let me see how the book turns out first." It sounds like a mundane response, but what does it mean for me? Perhaps it means I’m finally done running away.

I thought of my brother again last night. In his last letter, he mentioned he was considering coming back for a visit this autumn. Instead of letting the thought torment me over and over as I usually do, I simply wrote back: "That would be great. We can go look at Father’s old books together." Just like that. So many years have passed between us, but perhaps some things—a love for books, for ideas, for the past—are never truly severed.

Lately, I’ve been tidying up the house. Not in a frantic "decluttering" sort of way, but with a gentler approach. I’ve re-categorized some books and organized the photography equipment gathering dust in the corner (though I still have no plans to start shooting again). Mr. Zhang asked me about the room renovation a few days ago, and I told him with a smile, "It’s on hold for now, but maybe I’ll start again sometime." He said, "No rush, take your time." It’s words like those that make me feel like someone understands my pace.

Regarding work, I’m thinking about a new approach. Not returning to that state of being chased by deadlines, but being more selective with projects. I’ve even started reading articles on financial planning for freelancers—something that would have been unthinkable for me before. I used to view financial planning as a constraint on freedom; now I realize it might actually be a form of freedom—knowing exactly how much time and space I have to do what I want.

Sometimes I still wake up in the middle of the night with some unsolvable problem spinning in my head. But the difference now is that I don't let the problem drag me into a whirlpool of anxiety. I’ll get up, have a glass of water, look out the window, and tell myself, "These problems will still be here tomorrow, but right now, I need sleep."

Dr. Li said last time that change doesn't happen all at once. I understand that better now. I haven't suddenly become a completely different person, but I am gradually changing the way I relate to myself and the world. This book, in a way, is a witness to that change.

There’s about a month left until publication. I don’t know how I’ll feel when the time comes, but for now, I can honestly say—I am ready.

Maybe my choices starting from 2007 were wrong. If I had accepted those full-time job offers back then, I’d at least have a pension now. Now, I have neither the fulfillment of freedom nor the security of stability. I am trapped in the middle.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something with you.

I received a reply from my brother this afternoon. I’m not quite sure how to describe the feeling.

Do you remember our last session, when we talked about my self-doubt—how I felt that if my writing had no market value, it had no meaning? I was completely trapped in that loop. Later, I dug out a review I wrote ten years ago and suddenly realized I had been using the market as a yardstick for everything. I wrote to my brother about this, just wanting to share my recent reflections.

His response was unexpected. He said he had gone through a similar struggle, questioning what truly constitutes meaningful work during his years abroad. He mentioned that my articles had once influenced his perspective on certain issues, even though I had long forgotten the details. He wrote, "What you have been doing has never been meaningless to me."

When I read that sentence, I sat in my study and cried for a while. It wasn't a cry of sadness; it was... a sense of relief.

It’s strange. He and I haven't had a deep conversation in years, yet in that moment, I suddenly felt we were still connected on a spiritual level. It was as if we were still those two children growing up in an intellectual household, just thinking about the same questions in different ways.

I think my previous understanding of "value" was too narrow. I had been waiting for external validation—the market, publishers, reader statistics—to prove whether my work was worthwhile. But perhaps value was never meant to be defined that way. Perhaps the fact that I can influence one person’s thinking, or that my ideas can resonate spiritually with someone, is a value in itself.

I also realized that the decision to break years of silence and write that letter to my brother was important in its own right. I’ve always been used to carrying things alone, used to not "bothering" others. But this time, I chose to share. And he chose to respond.

I’m still processing all of this. But I wanted to tell you that the things you said in our sessions—about redefining value and the importance of an internal sense of meaning—they are no longer just theories to me. I’ve experienced them for real in my life.

Thank you.

Hey, I just wanted to say one more thing.

After we hung up, I sat in my room for a long time. I didn't do anything—I just sat there. You know, those things you said—about being interested in my work and looking forward to reading what I’ve written—I don’t think I’ve ever heard anything like that from you before. I’m not blaming you; I just... I felt seen.

I’ve spent years getting used to the silence between us. I thought that was just the way our relationship was. But when you said those things today, I suddenly realized that maybe the silence was never because we didn't care about each other; we just didn't know how to break it.

When you said, "Maybe we can have more conversations about this topic"—I’ve been thinking about that sentence over and over. You know, it changed how I see this visit. It’s no longer just something I have to push through my anxiety to get done. It’s... a beginning. Something we can keep building on.

I think that’s why I’m still awake. I’m thinking about what we could talk about, imagining us sitting somewhere together, just like when we were kids, but this time really listening to each other.

Three weeks is still too short. But at least it’s a start.

Thank you. Thank you for not letting the silence go on.

I was just sitting in a cafe for a while, thinking things over, and I realized I really wanted to talk to you.

After the lecture the day before yesterday, Professor Chen came up to me and said something that I’ve been chewing on ever since. He asked, "Why not consider organizing these ideas into a book?" My first instinct was actually to deflect—you know me, I’ll find every excuse: it’s too late, I don’t have a publisher, who would even read it... But then he added, "The real question is whether you want to leave something behind."

That was it. I was suddenly speechless.

For the past two days, I’ve been wondering: why am I always running away? I’ve been using the word "freedom" to justify my hesitation, but in reality, it might just be fear. Fear of commitment, fear of failure, fear of being tied down. But Professor Chen’s words poked at something else—do I really want to leave something behind?

The answer is yes. I do.

I’m thinking that maybe I could gather what I’ve written over the years and publish them as a collection of essays. Not for sales, and not to prove that I still have "market value." Just... to give these thoughts a place to exist. It sounds simple, but for me, it feels like a massive shift.

I know it sounds a bit crazy—a 62-year-old freelance editor suddenly deciding to put out a book. But the word "late" doesn't feel quite so convincing to me anymore. Instead, I feel like it would be a greater regret not to do it.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this? Or is this actually an idea worth taking seriously?

I’d really love to hear your thoughts.

I feel exhausted today. Not the kind of tired you get from a lack of sleep, but more like... being hollowed out from the inside. When Li Fang called, I really didn't have the energy to deal with it. She asked me exactly what was wrong, but I couldn't say. How do I explain it? Tell her I’m scared? Tell her I’m calculating how many years I have left to live?

Never mind. It’s easier just to mark it as a rest day.

Wait, I should schedule a check-up. Or maybe just book an appointment first? Cardiology? No, maybe a general practitioner first... I could also book another session with Dr. Li. Even though our last talk didn't really solve anything, at least...

Whatever. I’ll just rest for now. I'm doing nothing today.

May 23, 2022, 11:47 PM

I’ve been sitting at my desk for three hours now. My coffee went cold a long time ago. My phone is lying beside me, the email app still open—empty.

Zhou Yu’s words keep circling in my head: "The only thing that’s too late is never trying." I cried in the cafe. I really did. A 62-year-old woman, sobbing like a child in front of strangers. I can’t even remember the last time I cried like that.

I pulled out that old photo. 1985. Lin Hao and me. He had his arm around my shoulder, and we were both laughing. That was before he went overseas. Back then, we could lean into each other without a second thought. And now? Now, I don’t even know how to talk to him.

Twenty-two years. For twenty-two years, we’ve barely had a real conversation. I’ve been waiting. Waiting for what? For him to reach out first? For the perfect moment? Waiting until when—until I’m dead?

I’m 62 now. My father was 68 when he passed away. I have six years left. Six years. That number makes it hard to breathe.

Write the letter. I’ve decided to write it. But my hands are shaking. I’ve already deleted five versions in my head. Every time, I get stuck at the same place—what right do I have to suddenly break the silence after twenty-two years? What will he think of me? Will he think I’m being dramatic? Or will he think I’ve left it too late?

Maybe it is too late.

No. Zhou Yu is right. The only thing that’s too late is never trying.

But what if he doesn't respond? What if he’s moved on, and my letter only disturbs his peace? Will I regret it? If I don’t try now, will I regret that more?

I definitely will.

I’m repeating the same questions. I know. I just can’t help it. Anxiety is fluttering in my chest like a trapped bird. My fingers are tapping on the desk. Left hand. Right hand. Left. Right.

I need to sleep. But I know I won’t be able to. I’ll lie in bed, staring at the ceiling, overthinking every single word. I’ll imagine him opening the email. I’ll imagine his expression. I’ll imagine a thousand possibilities, most of them terrible.

But maybe—just maybe—one of them is good.

I should write this letter.

Now. Before I change my mind.

Lin Xiaoyun

September 11, 2022, 10:45 PM

Insomnia again. But this time, it wasn’t because of some phrase looping in my head; it was because of that Excel spreadsheet.

This afternoon, I got an email from the editor at *Culture Weekly*. They said their column budget for the year has been adjusted and asked if I could lower my rate. I didn’t reply immediately. Instead, I opened my laptop and created a new spreadsheet. I named it "Contribution Invitation Statistics."

A rational move, I told myself.

Then I started filling in the data. Same period last year: 8 invitations. Same period this year: 3. I used different colors to mark the downward trend—green to yellow to red. In the final row, I wrote in red: "It might get even worse after September 2022."

I stared at those words and felt a sense of absurdity. I was writing on the screen with a digital pen as if I were signing my own death warrant.

Then I opened another file to compare the numbers. My newsletter subscribers: 5,000. Those young bloggers I see on Weibo—what do they call them again? Anyway, the ones who write fast, shallow, viral content—they have anywhere from 100,000 to a million followers. I took out my calculator and pressed the buttons over and over, trying to calculate the "efficiency gap."

5,000 versus 1,000,000.

I sat there alone, tapping on the calculator, as if I were quantifying my own incompetence.

Around 11:00 PM, I searched for "how to pivot after 35." Then "what happens to freelancers when they get older." Then "prospects for the cultural criticism industry." I know what I was doing—I was compulsively confirming the existence of the problem rather than looking for an answer. It’s like flicking a light switch repeatedly in the dark, hoping to see something, but really just draining the battery.

Back in 2007, the director at the publishing house asked if I wanted to transfer to become the head of the planning department. I said no. I said I wanted freedom. I said I didn't want to be tied down.

Now I have freedom. I have so much freedom.

How much longer can my savings last? I’ve calculated this countless times. If my monthly expenses stay as they are, if the freelance rates continue to drop... I did some rough division in my head.

The result wasn't pretty.

It’s past midnight now. I should sleep. Tomorrow I still have to reply to those friends from the book club and organize some notes on *The Wisdom of Life*. Those tasks are still there, waiting for me.

But right now, I just want to sit here and stare at those red words: "It might get even worse after September 2022."

Maybe it’s right.

# Diary

**November 28, 2022, 8:00 PM**

I just dug out those diary entries I wrote in early November. My fingers lingered on the pages for a long time. Back then, my handwriting was a mess—in some places, the pen had even torn through the paper. I remember that feeling; it was like being choked, where every breath felt like a calculation of how much longer I could hold on.

Looking back at it now, it feels like reading someone else’s story.

It’s not that I’m completely fine now. I still worry. I still wake up in the middle of the night thinking about an editor who hasn’t replied to my email, or stare into the mirror counting the new wrinkles on my face. But that desperate, "left-behind-by-the-times" feeling... it really seems to have faded.

Dr. Li told me last time that I’ve been measuring myself by market standards. I couldn’t take it in then; I just nodded. But later, while sorting through my old articles, I came across a sentence I’d written: "The significance of cultural criticism doesn't lie in how much money it can make, but in what it allows people to see." I don't remember when I wrote it—maybe ten years ago. Seeing those words made me want to cry.

Maybe I don’t need to be recognized by the entire era. Maybe I just need to be recognized by myself and by those who understand me. Maybe that’s enough.

I’ve written that sentence on a slip of paper and stuck it next to my desk.

My plans for December are already laid out. I’ll keep organizing my essay collection; those pieces might not have market value, but they represent years of my thinking. I’m going to pick up photography again, not for an exhibition, but just... to see. I’ll keep attending the book club and chatting with friends—Professor Chen said he’s looking forward to next month’s discussion, which made me quite happy. I’ve also scheduled my sessions with Dr. Li for every two weeks.

Old Mr. Zhang asked me again the day before yesterday about the room renovation; we decided to wait until next spring. He said, "No rush, take it slow." I like that phrase.

My health is slowly improving, too. My sleep has been much more regular lately, and that inexplicable chest tightness isn't happening as often. I can finish a cup of tea in the morning now, instead of getting restless halfway through.

I’m starting to think that perhaps true freedom isn’t total financial independence, but the ability to live according to one’s own values. I may never be wealthy, but I can choose what matters to me. I can continue doing the things I care about within my limited means.

That seems to be enough.

It’s not that all my problems are solved, or that I’m suddenly some great optimist. It’s just that... I’m starting to believe that maybe my life doesn’t need to meet a certain standard to have value. Maybe I’ve been doing meaningful things all along; I just couldn’t see them before.

I can see them now.

Dr. Li, I wanted to give you an update on how things have been going this week.

Following your suggestion, I started keeping an anxiety journal last week. To be honest, I felt it was a bit of a formality at first, but over the last couple of days, I’ve discovered a few things I’d like to share with you.

I’ve noticed a pattern—every time I receive an email from my editor, I start getting nervous before I even open it. What’s stranger is that I automatically assume it’s a rejection or a complaint about a deadline. In reality, two of the emails this week were just standard editorial feedback, and one was actually praising my choice of topic. It wasn't until I wrote it down in my journal that I realized I hadn't even given them a chance to respond before I’d already staged a whole "rejection" drama in my head.

It reminded me of what you said about "pre-emptive assumptions"—it’s like I’m always preparing for failure, which only makes it easier to spiral into anxiety.

I had another realization, too. Yesterday, I was organizing my bookshelf (I finally started tidying up) and came across some old book reviews I wrote ten years ago. Looking at my own work, it suddenly hit me: maybe I’m not afraid of my current work being unwanted, but rather afraid of becoming irrelevant. This thought popped up several times in the journal.

I have to admit, journaling has helped me see exactly what my anxiety looks like, instead of just vaguely saying "I’m anxious." It feels a bit like... untangling a messy ball of yarn.

You were right; some things really do require me to observe them for myself.

I’ll keep at it next week.

March 22, 2023, 9:30 PM

The house is still a mess. That black trash bag is still sitting in the corner of the living room; it’s been there for three days now. I haven't taken it out.

While sorting through things this afternoon, I unearthed a pile of old picture frames, photo albums, and that notebook I bought back in 2015. I opened it and read a couple of pages—ramblings from my younger self about aesthetics and the meaning of life. I stared at those words for a long time, and suddenly, a strange feeling washed over me: Did I really write this? It felt like someone else’s work. That person was so certain, so passionate; now, they seem like a complete stranger.

I put the notebook into the trash bag, too.

Then I looked at those old frames, the photos I took, the things I once thought were meaningful, and I suddenly started laughing. It was an ugly laugh. I said out loud, "What is all this? It’s just proof of my failure." My voice echoed in the empty room. No one heard me.

Perhaps it’s only because no one heard me that I was able to say it at all.

I don’t know what I’m expecting. Am I waiting for someone to contradict me? To tell me that all of this has value? But no one needs my commentary anymore. No editors are hounding me for copy; there are no deadlines. I thought this would make me free, but it turns out freedom is just an empty room where I can't find my bearings.

The photography, the collections, the written thoughts—what do they become when no one needs them? They become the contents of this trash bag. Or perhaps they never had any value to begin with, and I’ve just been lying to myself all along.

And what about my knowledge? All that I accumulated over twenty-five years as an editor? I thought it was my wealth, but now it feels like a long-running scam. If there’s no market for my voice, does my expertise even exist? Or did it ever exist at all?

I sat on the sofa for a long time. It grew dark. I didn't turn on the lights.

Maybe I should take the trash out. But I didn't. It’s still there, sitting in the dark like a problem I can’t resolve. I want to rid myself of the past, yet I can’t truly let go. What kind of psychology is this? Self-torture? Or is it that I simply don't believe I’m capable of starting anything new?

Sixty-two years old. No professional identity. No one needs me. A house full of the evidence of failure.

Who am I now?

I can’t even bring myself to ask that question out loud.

---

Forget it. I don’t want to think about it anymore. I’ll deal with it tomorrow. It’s not like there’s anything urgent to do anyway.

May 23, 2022, Morning

I didn’t fall asleep until two in the morning. It wasn't insomnia—not the typical kind, anyway. It was more like my brain was on a loop, replaying what the editor said: "This is the most sincere piece of writing I’ve ever read."

I’m sitting at my desk now, staring at the article on my screen. *Living with Anxiety*. I wrote it myself. When I was writing it, I didn't think much; I just... needed to write. It wasn't about a deadline or the pay. It was about the things Dr. Li said—the idea that anxiety isn't an enemy, but some kind of signal. I just needed to get it all down. While I was writing, I even lost track of time.

That has never happened before.

In the past, my drafts were always dragged out of me. I’d procrastinate until the very last second and then rush through them in a panic. Editors got used to my stalling; sometimes they’d call to nudge me. But this time... when I sent it over, I didn't even go through rounds of revisions. It felt as if the words were always meant to be exactly like this.

But now, I’m starting to regret it.

In that article, I wrote too much about myself. The anxiety, the sleepless nights, the fear of aging, the loneliness after the divorce... I’ve never been this honest about these things before. I even hinted at the diagnosis—I didn't explicitly say "I was diagnosed with anxiety," but anyone reading closely would know.

The editor called it "most sincere."

That word is spinning in my head. Sincere. What does that even mean? Does it mean my defenses have slipped? Does it mean I’m exposed?

I don’t know whether to be happy or afraid.

Part of me is happy. It’s the feeling of being seen. Someone understood what I wrote—not just as the critic, but as a person who... has issues. It feels strange, but also... a relief? Like I’ve finally set something down.

But another part of me is screaming. Now people know. This article will be published. Strangers will read it. They’ll know that Lin Xiaoyun—the critic who always seems so insightful, always analyzing others' work—is actually an anxious, fragile, and frightened person.

Am I a "sick" person?

No. I don’t want to define myself that way.

And yet, I wrote it. I admitted it through my words.

Maybe that’s the price of sincerity.

It’s light out now. I should get up. But I’m still sitting here, looking at that article. I want to delete it. I want to tell the editor, "Forget it, don't run it." But I know I won't.

Because he said it was the most sincere piece he’s ever read.

It might take me months to process that sentence.

# Decluttering Notes & Next Steps

**Date: December 12, 2024 – Afternoon**

Spent the whole afternoon sorting things out. I’m a bit tired now, but it feels good.

## Items Donated

- **A stack of expired photography magazines (2015–2018)** — To be honest, I’m never going to flip through these again. The passion I had for learning photography back then has cooled off; keeping them was just lying to myself that "I’ll get back to it."
- **Three old cardboard boxes full of messy notes** — Fragments of ideas from ten years ago. I looked through them and most are long outdated. For the few ideas worth remembering, I’ve taken photos and saved them to my phone.
- **Two broken desk lamps and a bunch of unused household knick-knacks** — Impulse buys from a few years ago. Now they just look like a waste of space.
- **Duplicate books** (I actually had three versions of the same book...)

Donated everything to the community recycling point downstairs. Mr. Zhang helped me get in touch with them; he said they can be given to people in need.

## Items Kept

- **My father’s notebooks and his lecture manuscripts (a few volumes)** — I’m keeping these. Not just because he’s my father, but because these records show how he thought. Every time I come across his annotations, I can almost hear his voice.
- **A few old books my mother brought back from the library, along with her reading notes** — For the same reason.
- **Original manuscripts of all my book reviews and critiques (organized into one folder)** — Professor Chen mentioned I should consider publishing a collection of essays; these are the foundation.
- **Antique and second-hand books with stories behind them** — Not all of them, only the ones that truly remind me of a specific moment or idea. I let go of the rest.

The new standard is clear: **Does this object help me tell my story?**

If the answer is "I’m just storing it, not using or cherishing it," then there’s no point in keeping it.

## Unexpected Takeaways

During the process, I realized that once I started asking "Why am I keeping this?", my attachment to many things began to loosen. I used to think throwing things away was a waste, but now I understand—**letting a useless object occupy my space is the real waste.**

This also seems to be affecting my thoughts on the travel budget. If I can be this rational about physical objects, why can't I be the same with money? Every dollar should have a purpose. This trip has a purpose.

- [ ] **By this Friday:** Message my brother to confirm his vacation dates. Can’t procrastinate anymore; airfares will fluctuate.

- [ ] **Check the visa process** (if required).
- [ ] **Email a few regular editors** to ask if there are any urgent commissions—I want to take on a few more projects before I head out.
- [ ] **Organize this month’s deadlines** to make sure nothing is missed.

The room looks much more spacious now. Even the light seems better. It’s a strange feeling—by clearing things out, I can see what I actually want much more clearly.

I still have a manuscript to review this afternoon, and I might go to a bookstore salon tonight. Time for a short break first.

November 20, 2022, 11:45 PM

My phone is right beside me, and his reply is sitting there on the screen. I’ve read it at least ten times now.

I can’t quite say why I chose this specific moment to send him such a long message. The thought just hit me this afternoon—I’m working on my collected papers, sorting through everything I’ve written over the years, and thinking about what "value" actually means. He’s overseas, and we haven’t truly spoken in years. So, I opened WeChat and typed it out word by word, telling him about my project, my recent thoughts, and even some of my regrets from over the years.

My hands were shaking a little when I hit send.

He replied quickly. It wasn’t a dismissive "keep it up," but a genuine response to what I had said. He told me he’d been thinking about similar things, that he understood why I was doing this project, and that he felt these reflections of mine were meaningful. Finally, he asked: "Do you still remember when we were kids, flipping through Father’s books together?"

I cried. Sitting right here in this room cluttered with books and odds and ends, I just cried.

For years, I’ve felt like there was something between us. Time zones, distance, the completely different trajectories of our lives. I thought those things were permanent. But seeing his reply, I realized—it’s like we never truly disconnected. I had just been pretending we were disconnected because it was easier to explain why we didn't stay in touch, and why I felt so lonely.

He’s on another continent and I’m in this city, yet we’re pondering the same questions. We’re both asking: What is meaningful? What is truly worth doing? We both lived through that era, read those books, and shared those thoughts. These things have no market value. But they are ours.

I suddenly understood something—value doesn't exist just because others recognize it. The connection between my brother and me isn't because we can offer each other financial help or because of how successful our careers are. It’s simply because we can understand what the other is thinking. That, in itself, is enough.

Maybe this is what I’ve been searching for these past few days.

It’s almost midnight now, and I should sleep. But I wanted to record this moment first. This moment feels important.

It’s not a dramatic, sudden epiphany. It’s a slow, steady feeling—like finally finding my footing.

I am not alone.

**Location: Study, a single desk lamp, cold coffee**

---

Just hung up the phone. A friend was going on again about how "your work is valuable." I could hear the kindness in her voice, but I honestly didn't have the energy to accept that kind of comfort. I gave her a cold reply: "My knowledge is worthless. Nobody needs me." After saying it, I was a bit startled by my own words. When they came out of my mouth, they felt like a statement of objective fact rather than an emotional outburst.

But is that really true?

I’m thinking that perhaps the problem isn't the knowledge itself, but my definition of "value." For all these years, I’ve been measuring myself by the standards of publishers, the market, and other people. Was I picked up by an editor? Was there a commission fee? Was I cited? Once those metrics vanish, I feel like I vanish too.

And yet—

At the book club the other day, a girl said that because of a book I recommended, her outlook on life had changed. Her eyes were bright. In that moment, my knowledge sparked something in her. Does that count as value?

I need to admit a fact: I am using absolute language to negate myself. "Worthless," "nobody needs me"—these are absolutes. And reality is never absolute.

Maybe the real issue is that I’ve been avoiding a deeper fear: If I am no longer "useful," then who am I? If my value isn't defined by the market, how am I supposed to define myself?

It reminds me of my father. After he retired, it took him a long time to accept that he was no longer a "lecturer." But then he started giving talks in the community—no pay, no titles. He said it actually made him feel freer. I didn't quite understand what he meant back then.

Now, I’m starting to.

But this doesn't mean my anxiety has disappeared. I still find myself at 3:00 AM calculating how long my savings will last. I still feel obsolete when I see opportunities going to younger editors. I still wonder if I’m just deluding myself—using "spiritual value" as a consolation prize for my financial struggles.

Maybe this is just how I have to live: swinging between two fears. One is "I am useless," and the other is "even if I am useful, it’s not enough to survive on."

I’m divorced. I’ve quit my job. I chose freedom. And now, at 3:00 AM, I ask myself: Were these choices acts of courage or acts of escape? Was I pursuing authenticity or evading responsibility?

Maybe the answer isn't either-or. Maybe I was pursuing authenticity and escaping at the same time. Both can be true.

I need to stop framing my life in terms of "right" or "wrong." Maybe I’m just living, making choices, and bearing the consequences. That is complex enough in itself.

Tomorrow, I still have to email that editor. I still have to prepare for next month’s book club. I still have to keep living.

Maybe that’s enough.

Maybe it’s not.

But it’s 3:00 AM, and I have no other choice.

February 14, 2024, 7:45 PM

I’ve been sitting at my desk for half an hour now. The document on my computer screen is still blank, except for the title: "2024 Financial and Life Planning."

I never thought I’d be the kind of person to open a file like this.

When I was replying to Professor Chen’s email this afternoon, he mentioned there’s been some progress with the publisher regarding the essay collection, but he needs me to confirm some timelines. It suddenly hit me—I don’t even know how long I can sustain myself. It wasn’t that vague, 3:00 AM wake-up-in-a-panic kind of fear; it was a calm, practical problem that needed facing.

So... I started listing numbers.

It’s strange. I used to instinctively avoid this. I felt that being bound by numbers was the opposite of freedom. But now, as I write down the monthly rent, medical expenses, royalties, and savings, I feel a strange sense of clarity instead. It’s not anxiety. It really isn't. It’s more like—how should I put it—finally seeing an outline clearly.

In the email, I told Professor Chen: "I want to create a conservative but feasible plan, so that I can be truly free to do what I want to do."

I paused for a long time after typing that sentence.

It didn't sound like me. Or rather, it sounded like a version of myself I never allowed myself to become.

But maybe that version has been here all along. I just hid her behind the word "freedom." I told myself that accepting a plan was being shackled. That accepting limits was compromising. That accepting reality was giving up on my ideals.

Now, I’m not sure if that holds true anymore.

Maybe real freedom isn't about rejecting everything. Maybe it’s more like... knowing what you want, and then having the courage to pay the necessary price for it. Including certain forms of order. Including certain forms of commitment.

I looked up and out the window. Downstairs, old Mr. Zhang was taking out the trash. He is so pragmatic, so grounded, never anxious about anything. I used to think he was boring. Now I’m wondering if he just understood something much earlier than I did.

I went back to filling in the numbers in the document.

There was no rush of excitement. No dramatic "I’ve finally figured it out" moment. Just a calm, slightly relieved feeling. Like finally letting go of something I’d been fighting against.

Maybe this is what growing up is. Not giving up, but learning when to hold on and when to compromise. When to say "no," and when to say "okay, I accept."

I still have many years ahead of me. But I no longer feel like I have an infinite amount of time to squander. It’s not fear. It’s just... clarity.

I keep typing.

# Publishing Plan Paused · 3-Month Evaluation Period

**Date**: 2024-07-12  
**Time**: TBD (Flexible)  
**Location**: Home  
**Reminder**: 2024-10-12 (Revisit in 3 months)

---

## Memo

It’s decided—I’m not going to rush the publication of that essay collection. Professor Chen’s advice was sound, but I have to admit: if I force this project through now, I’ll be shackled by deadlines, revisions, and the minutiae of proofreading. I’ve been through it too many times; that feeling of being chased by a deadline slowly eats away at my passion for writing itself.

The reading group has just taken off, and the lectures are ongoing. I want to save my energy for these. They are "living" things that offer immediate feedback; every time I see someone nod or fall into thought because of something I said, it feels far more real than publishing a book.

So: **Pause**. Not quit, but pause.

I’m giving myself three months to see where these new activities lead. If by October I feel I still have the capacity—or if these projects have stabilized enough to require less energy—then I’ll reconsider the publication. If not, that’s fine too. At least I’ll know exactly what I’m doing.

---

## Metrics to Monitor

- Reading group attendance and quality of discussion (record monthly).
- Feedback on the cultural column (which topics spark more debate).
- My own energy levels (am I starting to feel exhausted or overwhelmed?).
- Lecture invitations (are there new opportunities coming in?).

---

## Tasks for October 12

Ask myself again:
- Over these three months, have I lost my love for these activities?
- Are my savings still sufficient?
- Do I truly *want* to publish this book, or do I just feel like I *should*?

If the answers are all "yes," then start. If any are "no," keep waiting.

I am 62 years old. I no longer need to live for the "shoulds."

It’s finally official—I’ll be giving a lecture at the university next week titled "A Dialogue Between Reading and Our Times." Professor Chen mentioned that his students need to hear some different perspectives. I initially declined, feeling that my reflections over the years might have fallen out of step with the times. But then I thought, perhaps it’s precisely because of this "falling out of step" that I have something worth saying.

I’ve been organizing my draft these past few days, digging through stacks of old notes and newspaper clippings. Interestingly, some viewpoints I wrote down over a decade ago seem even more relevant today. Maybe I’ve been asking the same questions all along; it’s just that the answers change every time.

To be honest, I’m a bit nervous. But I’m also looking forward to it.

I sat there for two hours, and Professor Chen said something that I’ve been turning over in my mind ever since. She said, "You’re no longer just seeking to be understood."

I froze for a second. Because she was right. I expected you to understand why I chose to freelance, and you perhaps expected me to understand why you had to go abroad. I don’t know if this is the right way to think about it. But I wanted to tell you.

January 29, 2022. Past 11 PM.

Insomnia again. I’ve been lying in bed for two hours, my mind replaying that message from my editor over and over. "Take care of yourself"—just those four words.

Why on earth did I tell her I was "feeling unwell"? I should have just given her the real reason for the delay, but instead, I used that vague excuse. Now look at me. She’s probably wondering if I’m actually sick or just being lazy with my deadline. "Take care of yourself" sounds caring, but could it be disappointment? Does she think I’m unprofessional? Will she even assign me another piece next time?

I opened the calculator on my phone. Again.

If I don't get the payment for this article this month, plus the one delayed from last month... my current savings can last about eight months. Eight months. I keep staring at the number as if it might suddenly grow. It won't.

I dug out last year’s diary and found the entry about my father. He was 68 when he passed away in 2008. I’m 62 now. Six years left. Six years. Why am I calculating it like this? But I can’t stop. Mom was 71. Does that mean I have nine years? Or is the number itself some kind of curse?

What did I write back in 2007 when I quit my job? I found that yellowed notebook. "I want freedom; I won't be shackled"—the handwriting was so forceful back then. Now, I’ve penciled a question mark next to it. Then I crossed it out. Too embarrassing.

Deep down, I know there’s nothing seriously wrong with my health. It’s just that I haven't been sleeping well lately; sometimes my heart races, and my breathing doesn't feel quite right. I’ve read plenty of medical news, and I can match every symptom to a condition. But how can I tell my editor that? How can I tell anyone? They’ll think I’m making excuses, or—worse—they’ll actually start worrying about me, looking at me with that pitying expression.

I don’t want that look.

But I don’t know what I want, either. Security? I gave that up fifteen years ago for the sake of freedom. To keep living freely like this? This kind of freedom is starting to feel like a luxury I can’t afford.

It’s quiet outside. The lights at old Mr. Zhang’s place downstairs have been out for ages. I should be asleep too. But my brain keeps whirring like a broken machine, stuck in the same spot, grinding away.

I have to reply to the editor tomorrow. What should I say? "Thanks for your concern, I’ll submit it next week"? Or keep being vague?

Whatever. I’ll try to sleep. Or try to see if I *can* sleep.

## Notes

**Start writing one week early** ← Important Reminder

This time is different. I asked the editor for specific deadlines; she was a bit surprised, but she gave them to me. I’m putting them here, in plain sight, just so I can see them.

Two pieces a month. Not too many, not too few. Enough to give me breathing room, but enough to hold myself accountable.

In the past, I always waited until the last minute to start, then emailed the editor at 3:00 AM, apologizing while turning in the draft. It felt terrible—it felt like freedom, but it was actually avoidance.

Now it’s different. I want to try another way: choosing discipline, but a discipline of my own making.

Looking at these two dates on the calendar, I feel a strange sense of calm. It’s not the feeling of being bound, but rather... grounded? Perhaps this is what true freedom is—knowing what you want, and having the courage to be responsible for it.

- Sincere response (Probability: 20%) "I’ve been wanting to reach out too, but didn't know how to start."
- Cold or defensive (Probability: 5%) "Did something happen?"—as if he's asking if I'm in trouble.

Which one am I betting on?

- Maybe he won't check his email until tonight.

**Maybe he’ll be surprised and respond.** Maybe he’s been wanting to reach out too, but also didn't know how to start. Maybe he’ll say, "I miss you too."

Lately, I’ve been wondering: do we write to be seen, or simply to speak our minds?

The other day, I was chatting with Professor Chen, and he said something that I’ve been turning over in my head ever since: "What you want to say matters more than who might want to read it." It made me realize I’ve been asking the wrong questions all these years. I was always preoccupied with whether a piece was "marketable" or if my perspective was "novel" enough, and in the process, I forgot why I started writing in the first place.

During my years at the publishing house, I encountered so many authors. Some spent their entire lives waiting for the "perfect moment" to publish, only for that moment never to arrive. Others just wrote—no matter what, they just kept recording what they had to say.

I’m only now beginning to understand that writing in solitude is, in itself, a form of honesty.

As I’ve been sorting through my articles and notes from over the years, I’ve realized I’ve actually been asking the same questions all along, just in different ways. Perhaps it’s time to finally put them in order. Not to prove anything, but just because... it feels like the right thing to do.

I’m a little nervous, but also a little relieved.

I went to see a therapist recently... I feel like I might be overthinking things.

When we were talking yesterday, the doctor just asked, "How has your sleep been lately?" and I just... kind of broke down. It was a bit embarrassing.

Living alone for all these years, it gets incredibly lonely sometimes. Especially in the middle of the night, I find myself spiraling over meaningless things. Then the fear sets in—fear of becoming useless, fear of having no one to look after me when I’m old, fear of... a lot of things, I guess.

Maybe it’s just my age. My parents are gone, and my brother is overseas; we rarely stay in touch. My job isn't stable either. Sometimes I feel like a drifting object with nothing to hold me down.

I don't know if you’ve ever felt this way? Like, even when you're busy, you still feel this profound sense of emptiness.

I think I really need to get myself sorted out. The therapist is quite gentle, so I’m planning to keep going.

It’s just... a bit hard to admit all this. You don’t need to feel pressured to reply; I just wanted to tell someone.

Monday, May 16, 2022, 11:00 PM

Insomnia again.

It’s been three hours since my session ended, and I still can’t sleep. I’m lying in bed, replaying Dr. Li’s words over and over: "A diagnosis isn't the finish line; it's the starting line." I don’t know why that made my eyes well up. I should be terrified, but instead... I feel relieved? It’s absurd.

I spent the entire afternoon in denial. Sitting across from her, I heard myself saying, "A diagnosis is like a label—once it’s on, you can never peel it off." My voice was harsh, like I was picking a fight. I could feel myself getting defensive. Dr. Li didn't push back immediately; she just looked at me calmly and said those words.

I paused. I really stopped in my tracks.

Then I asked her, "So... what should I do?"—in this tiny, tiny voice. Like a child. I hate myself for that.

Lying here now, I realize that what truly scares me isn't whether I have an "illness" or not. It’s being permanently defined. It’s the idea that from now on, when others (or even I) look at me, they’ll be mentally slapping on a label. Lin Xiaoyun: the woman with anxiety. It’s as if my identity has suddenly shifted from "an editor, a critic, a reader" to "a patient."

But maybe... maybe the label was already there. Maybe I’ve just been pretending not to see it.

I don’t know if I should tell my friends. What happens after I tell them? Will they see me differently? Or will they not care at all? I can’t even imagine what it would look like to say it out loud.

Dr. Li said this isn't the end. I wonder if she meant that I still have a chance—or that from now on, everything has to change.

God, I’m overthinking again. That’s exactly the problem.

Maybe... maybe she’s right. Maybe I really do need help. Maybe admitting that isn't a sign of weakness.

Maybe.

But I’m still so scared.

Maybe my life isn't over yet.

I just dug out a dusty old photo album, filled with details I’d long forgotten. Memories blurred by time suddenly snapped back into focus. Some things you think you’ve let go of are actually just obscured by distance.

I’ve only recently realized that solitude isn't a curse. It gives you the time to figure out what truly matters. And when those important people suddenly draw near again, you realize the connection was never really broken.

Maybe there are still new adventures waiting. Maybe I still have time to do the things I’ve always wanted to do but never had the courage to try.

I’m not sure if it’s too late to be thinking this way. But at least now I know—it’s never too late.

My sleep schedule has been a bit of a mess lately. Reading this made me realize that we’ve all probably felt this way at some point. It’s nothing major, just… sometimes it’s hard to untangle your own thoughts, but reading someone else’s words makes you feel a little less alone.

February 11, 2022, Afternoon

Another call from that editor. When I saw the screen light up, my fingers trembled. I didn't pick up. I sent a message instead: "My physical and mental state aren't great right now, can we postpone?"

After hitting send, I just sat on the sofa, staring at the phone screen for five minutes. Will she be angry? Will she think I’m unprofessional? But I really... I really can't do it. How am I supposed to sit at a computer and write cultural criticism? I can’t even get through a full sentence.

I stood in front of the mirror for a long time just now. Looking at my own face. The dark circles are so deep they look like two holes; my complexion is a ghostly grey, not like a living person. The corners of my mouth droop as if I’m crying. I poked my cheek with my finger, trying to confirm—confirm what? Confirm that I’m still alive? Confirm that this isn't a dream?

When I pressed down, I was suddenly terrified. Terrified that if I pressed, it wouldn't spring back. Terrified that my skin has already started to rot.

Why am I so fragile?

I wrote this sentence in my diary with such force that the nib nearly tore through the paper. Then I crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

Father died at 68. Mother at 71. I am 62 now.

Six years. Or nine years.

I keep doing this subtraction in my head over and over. Six years. Nine years. Six. Nine. Like an obsession, I have to calculate it again and again just to make sure I haven't made a mistake. But with every calculation, the fear deepens.

Am I going crazy?

Really. Am I going crazy? Asking myself this repeatedly, checking my pulse like this, searching "is sudden dizziness in a 62-year-old woman cancer" at three in the morning... is this madness?

Or is this just the normal process of aging, and I’m just being too sensitive?

But if I’m too sensitive, then what have I been doing with my life? Twenty-five years at a publishing house, fifteen years freelancing, and now I’m 62, lying in this shabby two-bedroom apartment, so terrified by a single phone call that I can't work. I’ve wasted my life.

Truly. I’ve wasted it.

I chose freedom to escape constraints, only to end up more tightly bound by anxiety. I chose independence so I wouldn't have to rely on anyone, and now I can't even manage to answer a phone. Where is my idealism? Where is my passion for life?

It’s all dead.

Or maybe it was never alive to begin with.

It’s raining outside. I haven't drawn the curtains. The neighbors can see me sitting here, motionless, staring at that strange, aging, frightened woman in the mirror.

I don’t know how to go on.

I really don’t.

Professor Chen, I have a question for you.

There was a young man at the reading group yesterday, probably in his early thirties, who asked me what the point of organizing these discussions is. His point was clear—essentially, we sit there talking about a book, but what does it actually change? Society remains the same, and our lives are no different.

I told him that "understanding itself is a form of change." As soon as I said it, even I felt the answer was a bit hollow. But I couldn't come up with anything more compelling.

Later, when I was home alone, I went over the notes he took, trying to find something in his records. He had written very diligently, circling all the key sentences. I started wondering—perhaps the fact that he’s asking this question proves he’s thinking, and isn't that a form of change in itself? But I’m not sure if I’m just trying to comfort myself or if there’s actual truth to it.

What do you think? Was my answer persuasive? Or rather, where do you think the true meaning of these discussions lies?

I’m not asking this because I want you to reassure me. I’m just—a bit troubled. On one hand, I feel like something is indeed happening in these reading groups; I can feel the texture of that exchange. But on the other hand, I’ve started asking myself the same question that young man asked. Especially at my age, I need to be clear about what I’m doing.

Do you have a moment? Or perhaps we could find a time to chat about this.

# Diary

June 22, 2025, Afternoon

Just got back from the art gallery, and my head is still a mess.

That photograph keeps flashing before my eyes. Black and white, two figures—an elderly woman and a middle-aged man, embracing in some unfamiliar room. I don’t know who they are or where it was taken, but I just couldn't look away. I must have stood in front of that wall for ten minutes. People kept passing by, but I remained rooted there.

The theme of the exhibition was "Family and Distance." How ironic.

It made me think of Lin Hao. It made me think of us.

What has been standing between us all these years? It’s not just geographical distance. Is it time? Is it that we’ve both been too busy, too settled into our separate lives? Or is it because some things, once missed, can never be reclaimed? Looking at that photo, I suddenly felt like crying, but I didn't.

I mentioned the exhibition to Lin Hao tonight. He asked me what I saw. I started describing the photo, describing that feeling—the feeling of looking at a stranger’s story and finding it hauntingly familiar. I said, "I want to write something about this."

He asked, "Are you really going to write it?"

I said, "Yes."

It was as simple as that. But as I said it, I felt a sense of certainty. Not a rational, planned-out certainty, but an instinctive one, rising from deep within. This isn't just my story. It’s the story of so many people. We all use distance to protect ourselves, and we use it to hurt each other, too.

I need to write this down. Not for publication, not for anything in particular—I just need to write. I need to squeeze this feeling, this visceral reaction to the photo, this complex emotion toward Lin Hao, out of my head and onto the paper.

Maybe that’s what I’ve been doing all these years. Turning life into words. Turning pain into reflection. Turning loneliness into a connection.

I’ll start writing tomorrow. Or maybe tonight? I don’t know. But I know I will write.

I’m still alive. I can feel it. And that’s enough.

# MEMO

It’s sent.

It’s actually sent.

The water was scalding. I stood in the shower repeating those words to myself, as if to confirm a fact—I did it. I really did it. My phone is outside; the email is gone, and there’s no taking it back.

Dr. Li asked me what I wanted. I didn’t have a good answer then; I just sat across from her, twisting my fingers together. But it came to me while I was showering—I want to hear his voice. That’s all. No need for forgiveness, no need for explanations. I just want to hear him say, "Hello, little sister."

Five versions were deleted. All those perfect phrases, those meticulously polished sentences—gone. In the end, all I sent was: *I miss you. We haven't spoken in so long. If you're willing, I’d like to hear how you’ve been.*

Terrifyingly simple.

The steam has blurred the mirror. I can’t see my face clearly, but I can feel this strange lightness in my chest. It’s not that I’ve let go of something, but rather—I’ve finally let it be. Dr. Li said to give myself some space, and I think maybe this is what space feels like. It’s not about waiting for a reply; it’s about allowing myself to take the risk.

Maybe he won’t reply. Maybe he will. Maybe he won’t for a very long time.

But at least for now, I’m no longer that person who deletes and rewrites, overthinking and tossing and turning at three in the morning.

I am alive. The email is alive, too, out there somewhere waiting to be opened.

That’s enough. For now, it’s enough.

What you said last weekend has been playing on a loop in my head. "Maybe you need to talk to a professional." At the time, I felt a bit uncomfortable—you know, that feeling of being seen right through. But I couldn’t stop thinking about it afterward.

I went yesterday. I saw a Dr. Li, a counselor at a psychological clinic. To be honest, I was nervous, even a little angry—why should I have to admit I need help? But as I sat across from her, I started talking. It was stiff at first, but then it was like something just opened up. I even said something like, "My entire life has been a mistake." Pretty extreme, right? But in that moment, that’s exactly how I felt.

She didn’t immediately disagree with me, nor did she give me any of that motivational "chicken soup" talk. She just listened and asked some very simple questions. Gentle ones. It caught me off guard—I expected to be judged, but instead, I felt a sense of... relief? I don’t know how to describe it. Just that I said it, someone heard it, and the world didn't fall apart.

I don’t know yet if this will actually help me. It might just be temporary. But at least I took that step. It wasn't easy for me; I think you can understand that.

Have you ever gone through something like this? I mean, how did you... decide to seek help?

Also, thank you for not pushing me directly that day, but for bringing it up the way you did. If you had been too blunt, I probably would have pushed back.

# February 1st, 2:47 AM

Can't sleep again.

I just woke up with a jolt from a dream, my heart pounding. I rushed to the bathroom to check my vitals—heart rate 88 bpm. Blood pressure 140/92. My hands were shaking, the hand holding the monitor.

***Is this a sign of a heart attack?***

I drank a large glass of water, cold water. Rushed back to the bedroom and lay on the bed staring at the ceiling. It’s almost 3 AM now, and I still can’t sleep.

My mind is going in circles. It keeps looping back to the same few questions: When will I die? What will death be like? How much time do I have left?

Dad was still giving lectures at 68. I’m 62 now. Six years left. Mom was 71. Nine years left.

I count these numbers in the dark like I’m counting down a clock. It’s absurd. I know it’s absurd. But I can’t stop.

I turned on the TV and flipped to a random channel. The sound fills the room, fills the void in my head. It’s a rerun of some old drama; I don’t know what’s happening, and I don’t care to know. I just need the noise.

My diary is still on the nightstand. I wrote down the numbers in red ink. 88. 140/92. 2 AM. Three question marks.

The rational part of me knows this is probably just a racing heart caused by insomnia. Probably just a normal fluctuation in blood pressure. Probably nothing at all.

But the other me—the one screaming in the dark—doesn't believe those explanations.

My latest deadline is going to be late again. The editor has already nudged me twice. I look at the emails, but I can’t write a thing. How can I care about some cultural critique? Not when you’re calculating how much time you have left.

The actors on TV are delivering their lines; the sound is jarring. But I need it.

I need anything to fill this hole.

October 16, 2023, past 3:00 AM

I can’t sleep. That nameless dread dragged me out of my dreams again.

This afternoon, I dug out my photography from 2017. The photos were still there on the hard drive; I’d almost forgotten they existed. Corners of the city, profiles of strangers, moments where light and shadow intersected... back when I took them, I felt like I was capturing something important. Now, looking at these images, I just find myself asking—is there really any value in this?

What value is there, anyway? It’s been eight years, and these photos have just been lying there. No exhibitions, no audience, no meaning. They’re just like the piles of books in this room, the unfinished notes, the half-baked ideas. I always thought these were traces of creativity, but now I realize—they are just evidence of failure. One failure after another, piled up to form my life.

I wonder, what if I died right now? What would happen to all this? Who would come to clear out this messy room? Who would look at these photos? Who would understand the meaning behind these stories? No one. I am here alone collecting, organizing, and thinking, and in the end, I am still alone.

The year my mother passed away, I went through the things she left behind. She worked in a library for over thirty years, and in the end, all she left were a few yellowed notebooks. I wonder, what will I leave? These photographs? These half-written reviews? This chaotic room?

I’m starting to doubt my pursuit of freedom. When I left the publishing house, I thought I was chasing some ideal life—unfettered, free to explore my interests as I pleased. But looking back, it was just another form of escape. I escaped a stable job, I escaped marriage, I escaped anything that might limit me. And the result? I got my freedom, but I lost my direction. Everything in this room is mocking me—*Look, this is your freedom. Chaos, incompleteness, meaninglessness.*

Maybe I should have accepted those full-time job offers. Maybe I should have chosen a different life. Maybe... I should never have lived this way.

Is my knowledge still useful? Do those reflections on literature, art, and society still hold any value in this era? I watch young critics voice their opinions online; their perspectives are fresher, more in tune with the trends. And me? Is anyone still reading what I write? Does anyone still care?

It’s three in the morning, and the whole city is asleep. Only I am here, sitting among these piles of belongings, trying to understand how I ended up here.

Maybe I’ve been lying to myself all along.

I realized I’ve accumulated quite a few thoughts on "self-perception and anxiety"—fragmented ideas from reading, discussions, and even daily observations. I never thought about systematizing them before, but now I feel there might be some real value there.

**Reflections**

I’ve been thinking about a question lately: the essence of my work in cultural criticism is helping others understand the world. But what about myself? Am I understanding myself through this work?

The answer seems to be yes. The book reviews and commentaries I’ve written over the years actually reflect my thoughts on life, change, and uncertainty at different stages. Perhaps I should make this process more intentional—not just writing for magazines, but building an intellectual archive for myself.

Professor Chen previously suggested I publish a collection of essays. I didn't take it seriously at the time, thinking my work was too scattered. But looking back at these materials now, I see a clear thread running through them. Anxiety, freedom, meaning—these themes have permeated most of my work over the years.

**Next Steps**

2. Start sorting through key articles from the past three years to see if they can be organized into a thematic collection.
3. Consider reaching out to Professor Chen to seriously discuss the possibility of publishing—not as "completing a task," but as something I genuinely want to do.

**Misc**

*Recorded at 14:45*

Dr. Li, I sent it.

Last night, around 11:00 PM, I was sitting at my computer, my fingers hovering over the keyboard for a long time. The final version of the letter was very simple, stripped of all those flowery words I kept deleting. It just said—I miss you, and I regret that we haven't stayed in touch properly all these years. I don't know what you'll think, but I needed to say it.

And then, I hit send.

The moment I pressed it, my heart was racing. I thought something terrible would happen, but nothing did. The email just flew out. I turned off the computer and sat in the dark crying for a while, but this time was different—I didn't try to stop the tears. I just let them flow, just like you said, allowing myself to feel.

I woke up at 6:00 AM. Then I did something stupid—I kept checking my inbox. Refresh, refresh, refresh. I knew it was pointless, but I couldn't help myself. I even imagined every possible scenario: he might not reply, he might be cold, he might not even see it...

But you know what? I wrote a sentence in my journal: "I don't know what will happen, but at least I tried."

As I wrote that, I realized I’ve truly accepted the uncertainty. It’s not that I’m not afraid—I’m still terrified. But alongside the fear, I feel a sense of relief. It’s like that question you asked me: "What do you want?" This is what I wanted—to no longer stay silent, to stop waiting for the perfect moment.

Sitting here now, drinking cold tea, I feel a bit lost. It’s a strange feeling. It’s as if I’ve finished something monumental, yet at the same time, nothing has changed. The room is still a mess, I’m still anxious, but... something feels different.

What do you think? Should I keep waiting? Or should I do something else to distract myself? My mind is spiraling through all his possible reactions, and I can't seem to stop.

Sorry this is so long. I just... needed to tell you.

Dr. Li, sorry to bother you so late. I thought I should send you a message first about what’s been spinning around in my head lately, just so I don’t end up stammering through it like I did last time we met.

I’ve actually been re-reading my diaries from back in 2007. You know, when I left the publishing house, I wrote all these high-flown words—things like "pursuing spiritual freedom" and "refusing to be shackled by the system." Back then, I was so certain I was doing the right thing. But over the last two months, I’ve started asking myself a gut-wrenching question: Was I really chasing a dream, or was I just running away?

The craziest part is realizing I’ve spent fifteen years trying to prove that decision was right. I threw myself into writing reviews, attending salons, collecting antiques, learning photography… it was like I was saying, "Look how fulfilling my free life is." But now that the work is gone, all of it suddenly feels so hollow. I’m starting to wonder if I’ve just been lying to myself this whole time.

But… something recently changed my perspective. Old Mr. Zhang (the neighbor downstairs) told me during a chat, "Being idle isn't a waste; it’s a chance to figure out what you actually want." And then there are the people at the book club—the way they look at me with such focus when I speak made me realize that maybe my knowledge isn't meant to be "needed," but rather "shared."

This might sound sentimental, but I wrote something in my diary: I no longer need freedom to escape; I can use freedom to create—to create connections, dialogue, and meaning. I added a question mark after I wrote it because I’m not sure I believe it yet. But at least… at least I don’t feel like everything has been a failure anymore.

I wanted to ask you, is this just a new version of self-deception? Or is it possible that I really can redefine what "freedom" means?

Sorry for the long message. I just wanted to get your thoughts on this before our official session.
