March 22, 2022, 11:47 PM

I’m sitting at my desk, my pen hovering over the paper for a long time. I don’t know where to start.

When I walked out of Dr. Li’s office this afternoon, my face was still wet. Walking down the street, I kept wondering if anyone saw me. The thought itself is ridiculous—I’m 62 years old, why do I still care what people think? But I do. I always have.

She asked me, "Can you describe how you’ve been feeling lately?" and I just started crying. There was no warning; it was like something suddenly burst. I didn’t even know why I was crying. I sat there in that unfamiliar office, in front of a woman I’d never met, crying like a... like a... I don’t even know what.

What shocked me most was when she said, "This is normal. You don’t need to rush."

Just that one sentence.

I don’t know how long it’s been since I heard something like that. No one tells me this is normal. My friends all encourage me to "be strong" or "look on the bright side," as if my anxiety is some kind of enemy that needs to be conquered. But Dr. Li said it’s normal. She didn’t make me feel like there was something wrong with me. Instead, she made me feel... permitted. Permitted to be vulnerable. Permitted to admit that I’m not sleeping well. Permitted to say out loud that sometimes at three in the morning, my heart starts racing and I feel like I’m dying.

I’ve never talked like that in front of a stranger. I was rambling; I repeated some things several times, and other things got stuck halfway through. But I said them. About the insomnia, the procrastination at work, the money being tight, the feeling of not knowing what I can even do anymore... all these things I’ve been chewing over alone at home, I finally said them all.

Then she asked, "Shall we meet at the same time next week?"

I nodded. I didn’t hesitate. That surprised me.

I don’t know if this is escapism or courage. Maybe it’s both. Maybe I’m just tired—so tired that I don’t have the strength left to deny the fact that I need help.

Sitting here now, it still feels a bit surreal. It’s as if what happened this afternoon was someone else’s experience, not mine. But my eyes are still a little swollen, so it’s real.

I don’t know if there really is something wrong with me. But maybe... maybe this "problem" isn't something I need to hide. Maybe it’s just... something that needs to be seen.

I’m not ready for anyone to see this page yet.

But at least I wrote it down.

Professor Chen, are you there? I wanted to talk to you about something, but I’m not quite sure where to start.

I received a letter from my brother yesterday. I mean, a real letter—on paper, not an email. I’ve read it so many times, and the tears just won't stop.

He told me he’s been reading my articles all this time, even though we rarely talk. He said he’s proud of me. Professor, you have no idea—it never even crossed my mind that he was reading my work. I always thought distance meant estrangement, that it meant he didn't care. But he does. He always has.

It made me think that maybe I’ve been using the wrong yardstick. Geographical distance and true connection are two entirely different things. We haven't had a deep conversation in years, and I always viewed that as a failure—as if something had gone wrong between us. But reading that letter yesterday, I suddenly realized that maybe we were just connecting in a different way. He reads my writing, and I know he’s there. That’s a form of companionship, too.

It reminds me of my father. When he passed away, I felt like I had lost something. But now I realize that what he left me never really went away—it’s in the way I think, in my hunger for knowledge, in the way I choose to understand the world. My mother, too. Her love for stories, her reverence for books—I’ve inherited all of it.

I’ve always felt so lonely. But maybe loneliness isn't a curse. Maybe it’s an invitation—an invitation to reflect on what a meaningful connection actually looks like.

Do you remember when we were at the bookstore discussing the meaning of life? I told you I didn't know what my value was because the market is changing and my work is being devalued. But now I’m thinking, maybe my value was never in the market to begin with. Maybe it’s in these stories, in the moments when I can share an idea with someone, in the times I can help someone see the world in a different way.

I want to publish that collection of essays. Not to prove anything, but because... I want to leave these things behind. I want people to know that in this era, there was a woman who stayed up late flipping through books, who was thinking, who was trying to understand. That matters, doesn't it?

Do you have some time? Maybe tea this weekend? I’d love to hear your thoughts.

Professor Chen, are you still awake?

I know it’s a bit late. But I was just going through my emails and saw my brother’s reply. Suddenly, there are things I want to say, yet I don’t know who to say them to. You seem like the kind of person who would understand these things.

He said, "I regret that there has been so much silence between us." Just that one sentence, and I’ve been sitting here in my study for half an hour.

Do you know, we haven't had a proper conversation in over a decade. That email I sent him—it took me three days to finish. It was about how time slips through our fingers, about how we never truly spoke when our father passed away in 2008, about how photos of our mother now look like they belong to another era. I expected him to reply with some polite formalities, or perhaps not at all. But he actually understood.

And that makes it even harder to bear.

I’ve been thinking about a question—it might be philosophical, or it might just be foolish—how much time do we have left to waste? I mean, really. I’m 62 now. He’s overseas. We might have twenty or thirty years left, or maybe much less. And yet we’ve spent so many years maintaining this silence. Why? Because we were both too stubborn? Because life was too busy? Or because we simply didn't know how to be with one another?

Have you been reading anything lately? I’m reminded of Heidegger’s discourse on "Being-towards-death." When I read it before, it felt abstract; now, it feels like it’s right beside me. Every word left unsaid, every phone call never made—it all feels like a waste of this finite time.

I’m sorry, I’ve probably started treating you like a therapist. But as you know, I’m not very good at saying these things in front of a crowd. At the cultural salons, everyone discusses ideas and theories, but this—this is too personal.

I’m sitting in my study now, surrounded by the old books and odds and ends I’ve collected over the years. I’m wondering, if I were to die right now, what would become of all this? What about the articles I’ve written? What about the things I’ve always wanted to say to my brother but haven't?

I’m not in despair. It’s just... a sudden realization. Perhaps this essay collection project isn't just about leaving behind some writing. Perhaps it’s also asking me: What else do you want to say? What regrets do you still have?

If you have a moment, send me a message. Or perhaps we could meet again soon? I’d like to talk to you about this.

Xiaoyun

11:45 PM. Just got back from the cafe downstairs; my fingers are still a bit cold.

I was at the bookstore this afternoon when the email from my editor arrived. She said the recent reader feedback has been good and asked if I could increase my posting frequency from biweekly to once a week. I stared at that sentence and read it three times.

It’s a strange feeling, being seen. A sense of validation—that people are reading my work, that they care. But at the same time, my stomach started to knot up.

I pulled up the calendar on my phone and marked it in red. Three months. Once a week. I started doing the math. The book club meets twice a month, lecture prep takes at least three or four hours, and I still have two overdue drafts... My finger hovered over the screen for a long time. Then I hit send. "Okay, I'll do my best."

The moment I pressed it, I paused—for maybe ten seconds. I could feel myself lying. Not to the editor, but to myself.

Sitting here at home now, looking at this pile of books and notes, I’m asking myself a stupid question: Why am I always like this?

I know my own rhythm. I can clearly see the calendar is already packed. But the moment someone says "You’re doing great," I immediately say "I can do better." It’s not modesty. It’s greed. Or rather, it’s fear—the fear that once I stop being seen, I’ll sink back into that sense of uselessness.

The past few months have indeed been different. The feedback from the book club makes me feel like what I do matters. Preparing for the lectures makes me feel like my knowledge still has value. But that doesn't mean I can expand indefinitely. I’m not twenty anymore. My energy isn't limitless.

But... what if I had said no? Would I regret it? Would I feel like I was running away again?

I don't know. I really don't know.

Maybe I should send another email to the editor tomorrow. Or maybe I should see if I can actually pull it off. Or maybe I should just stop torturing myself like this.

Forget it. It’s too late. I need to sleep. I’ll think about it tomorrow.

But I know I won’t fall asleep. I’ll lie in bed, replaying this decision over and over again.

# Work Log | 2025-05-21

**A Conversation with Professor Chen — An Unexpected Project Opportunity**

I was chatting with Professor Chen at a cafe this afternoon. It was supposed to be just a casual catch-up about recent readings and lectures, but she suddenly brought up an idea that has left me feeling a bit restless all afternoon.

She mentioned that my lecture on "Family and Distance" left a deep impression on her and asked if I had ever thought about expanding those reflections into something longer—like a long-form essay, or even a book. Her exact words were: "Your understanding of this topic is unique, Lin Xiaoyun. This isn't just a personal story; it’s a dilemma shared by many in the modern world."

I was a bit stunned at the time. A book? I had never looked at it that way.

But to be honest, once the idea was planted, it took root in my mind. I can feel that familiar mix of excitement and nerves—like standing before a door, knowing what might happen if I push it open, but unsure if I have the courage to step inside.

**Core Reflections:**
- **Theme:** Family estrangement and reconnection. Specifically, the story of my brother and me—the years of silence, the sudden invitation, and what this current trip represents. But it’s more than just my story; it has broader social layers: migration, generational gaps, and how modern people maintain intimacy across distances.
- **Format:** Could be a long-form article (for a literary magazine or cultural website) or developed into a short book. Professor Chen mentioned some potential publishers—I didn’t catch all the names, but she said several are interested in this kind of "non-fiction personal narrative."
- **Material:** It’s already there. The conversations before and after the trip, my thoughts on loneliness and connection over the years, the re-examination of life after our parents passed away... all of this can serve as material.

**Frankly, I’m feeling a bit overwhelmed right now:**
- Can I truly transform these complex, sometimes contradictory emotions into compelling prose?
- This isn't a book review or a commentary; it’s about laying out my own life experiences. That’s uncharted territory for me.
- What about time? My current work is already quite scattered—can I really find the energy for this?
- But on the other hand... I want to try. The idea feels right. As Professor Chen said, this isn't just my story.

**Next Steps:**
- Don’t rush into any commitments, but start documenting ideas. I’ve already started a memo on my phone titled "Notes on Distance."
- Have a deeper discussion with Professor Chen after I return from the trip. I’ll have more material and reflections by then.
- Perhaps write an experimental long-form essay first to see how it’s received. If the response is good, then consider expanding it into a book.

I’m a bit excited, and a bit scared. But this feeling isn't foreign to me—it’s how I’ve always made my decisions.

*p.s. Professor Chen also mentioned that if I do go toward publication, I might need an editor to help structure the work. My previous experience in publishing might come in handy, but from a different perspective. That’s an interesting shift.*

Perhaps it’s time to properly collect them. Not to prove anything, but just... I want them to have a place to stay.

The day before yesterday, Dr. Li asked me a question that’s been spinning in my head ever since. She asked, "If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?" I didn't answer at the time; I just kept saying "I don't know." I still don't.

These young bloggers use the simplest methods to get the kind of attention I could never dream of. I tried to learn video editing, thinking maybe I could present things in a new way, but I just couldn't grasp it. Looking at those tutorials, I felt this profound sense of helplessness—I’m too old, too out of touch.

I know this sounds like self-pity, but I’m genuinely grappling with a fundamental question: does knowledge itself still have value? Or rather, does *my* knowledge still have value? Maybe I simply don’t understand what this era requires. Maybe my idealism was always just impractical.

My sleep has been terrible lately; I’m awake five or six nights a week. During the day, my mind is a fog; I can’t think straight. I’ve been wondering if I should consider a stable job, but the thought of giving up my current freedom fills me with panic. Yet, this freedom is also killing me.

I don't know why I'm sending this to you. Perhaps it's because you once believed my work had value and suggested I publish a book. Now I want to ask you—do you still believe that? Or was it just polite encouragement?

I’m not looking for comfort. I just want to know if I’m the only one feeling this struggle. What place is left for an intellectual in this day and age?

I’m sorry for sending you such heavy things so late at night.

What do you want?

Four simple words. That’s all Dr. Li asked.

I was sitting in front of her, clutching that photo from 1985. My vision was blurred, and my mind was racing with thoughts like "How should I say it?", "How can I make him understand?", and "Will this be too sudden?"—and then she asked me that.

What do I want.

Not "What should you do," or "Have you thought about the consequences," but a question that direct.

I want him to know that I miss him.

It’s that simple.

I spent so many days digging through old photo albums, staring at our smiles, writing draft after draft, deleting and rewriting. I was so afraid of using the wrong words, so afraid of looking too vulnerable, so afraid he’d think I was trying to guilt-trip him. I was oscillating between perfection and fear, overcomplicating a single letter into a psychological war.

But all I really want is for him to know—I miss him. That’s it.

It’s been 22 years. For 22 years, I haven't said those words to him properly.

Dr. Li said, "Perfection isn't the prerequisite. Sincerity is."

My hands are still shaking. My heart is still racing. But this time, I know what I want.

Tomorrow. I’ll write it tomorrow. No more overthinking, no more editing. I’ll just tell him—Brother, I miss you.

I think he’ll understand. Or maybe he won’t. But at least I’ll have said it. At least I won’t be silent anymore.

Dr. Li, it’s strange. I’ve always felt that my anxiety and my fear of death were my own private problems, my weaknesses. But in that moment, what I said seemed to… have meaning? Not just for me, but for others too.

I’m a bit confused now. Is this progress? Or am I just using "sharing" as a way to avoid the real issues? I’m not sure. But I wanted to ask you—this feeling, this sense of connection that comes from being honest, is this the direction we’ve been talking about in our sessions?

I’m a little excited, but also a little afraid that this feeling will fade.

June 18, 2023, Night

I dug out that yellowed notebook filled with things I wrote back in 2007. I had just left the publishing house then. My handwriting was still forceful, and the pages were cluttered with words like "freedom," "independence," and "the true self." As I read, I didn't feel the shame or regret I used to. Instead, I paused and wrote a sentence in the margin: "My choices are complex."

I was a bit surprised myself when that sentence came out.

Over the years, I’ve been swinging between two extremes—either fiercely defending my choices or falling into deep self-denial. Reading those youthful manifestos today, I realized that perhaps it doesn't have to be one or the other.

Yes, I don’t have a stable job or a fixed income. Some months are incredibly tight, and I find myself checking the accounts over and over to see how much longer my savings will last. There are times I wake up at 3:00 a.m. startled by some health news, imagining all sorts of terrifying futures. These things are real.

But—and maybe this is the key—I have my pension and social security now. It sounds mundane, but it changed something for me. That sense of desperation, that "if I lose my job, I’ll have nothing," has faded. I have a floor now. It’s not the total financial independence I imagined when I was young, but it is real, and it is enough.

I truly have gained things over these years as well. During those twenty-five years at the publishing house, I rarely had time to actually think. Now I do. I have time to sit in a café and read all afternoon without a deadline looming. I can discuss an idea with Professor Chen at a book club and see the spark in someone’s eyes. I can pick up my camera, not for an exhibition, but just to record what I see.

These aren't grand achievements; they won't go on a resume. But they are real.

I think perhaps I’ve been measuring my life by the wrong standards—by market value, professional success, and the expectations of others. Now, I’m trying to look at it another way: How many genuine conversations have I had? Have I truly thought about the questions I care about? Have I lived at my own pace?

The answers aren't perfect. But there are answers.

I don’t know how much longer this life can last. Maybe five years, maybe ten. Maybe I’ll regret it, maybe I won’t. But at least for now, I can stop defending myself. I can say: This is my choice, and it comes with both a price and a harvest. I am both fragile and real.

Thinking this way, I actually feel a bit lighter.

Professor Chen, hello. I’ve been reflecting on a question ever since the lecture ended today, and I’d like to discuss it with you.

The feedback from the students really took me by surprise. One girl said, "This was the most interesting lecture I’ve ever attended, because you weren't just giving us answers—you were inviting us to think." I felt a real sense of fulfillment when I heard that, but now, sitting here on the subway, I’ve started to second-guess myself.

I realized that several times during the lecture, I had to suppress the urge to "correct" a student's understanding, choosing instead to guide them with questions. At the time, it felt like the right thing to do, but now I’m asking myself—was I actually shirking some kind of responsibility as a lecturer?

I wrote a sentence in my notebook: "Guidance is more powerful than instruction." But do I truly believe that? Or am I just making excuses for my own uncertainty?

How do you handle this in your own teaching? Is it actually possible to find a balance between open-ended discussion and the systematic delivery of knowledge? Or is this a false dilemma, and I’m just overthinking it?

I know this might seem a bit out of the blue, but you’re the only person I could think of who would truly understand these questions. I’m not looking for reassurance; I just want to hear your thoughts.

June 11, 2022, 9:30 PM

I did it. I actually did it.

While I was in the shower, with the hot water beating down on my shoulders, I kept repeating those words to myself. It was as if I were confirming something—or rather, confirming that I’m still alive. My fingers are trembling slightly. It’s not from the water temperature; it’s from… I don’t know.

I hit send around three this afternoon. In that split second, my stomach did a somersault, but I didn’t hit unsend. I didn’t revise it one last time. I just let it go—that letter, after five versions of deletions and edits, I chose the simplest one. "I miss you. Our silence all these years is a regret of mine. If you’re willing, maybe we could try."

Just like that. Simple. No explanations, no carefully worded apologies, none of those complex sentences I had agonized over.

Immediately after sending it, I messaged Dr. Li. I don’t know why I did it; maybe because I was scared, or maybe because I needed a witness. She replied quickly: "That was brave. Now, give yourself some space."

I’ve been chewing on those words ever since. "Brave"—not "you did the right thing," not "he’ll definitely reply," just "brave." It’s as if she was saying that the act itself was enough. Regardless of how he responds, I’ve already done it.

In the shower, I kept confirming it, over and over: "I did it. I really did it." It felt like I was arguing with someone who has always doubted me. That person is myself.

My shoulders relaxed a little under the hot water. They’ve been tight for days, from my neck all the way down to my lower back. They’re still a bit stiff, but it’s different now. What’s the difference? Maybe it’s because I’m no longer waiting for a perfect moment. I’m no longer thinking, "What if I worded it better?" or "What if I waited a bit longer?"

I’ve waited so long. So many years.

I don’t know what happens next. He might not reply. He might reply coldly. He might… or maybe he’s been waiting for something, too. But none of that matters now—at least, not for the moment. What matters is that I said it.

Dr. Li said to "give yourself some space." I’m sitting on the edge of the bed now, my hair still wet, and I don’t want to blow-dry it. I just want to sit here for a while.

Maybe tomorrow will be hard. Maybe I’ll start checking my phone obsessively and begin to regret it. But tonight, at least for tonight, I want to record this feeling—this hot water, this confirmation, this moment where I finally stopped waiting.

I did it.

# January 15, 2022, 11:30 PM

...Another night like this.

I haven't been able to sleep since finishing that documentary on aging. Those pale faces on the screen, those women weeping in front of their mirrors... I kept telling myself not to project, but my fingers moved of their own accord, pressing against my neck. Right on the carotid artery. Counting the beats. One, two, three...

Why do I do this? I know it’s ridiculous. I know it’s just the anxiety acting up. But I just need to confirm—confirm that I’m still alive.

I pulled out the family photo albums. I don’t know why, I just felt like looking at photos of my father. I stared at his face from when he was young, and then... then I took out the calculator on my phone.

My father was 68 when he died.
My mother was 71.
I am 62 now.

Six years. Nine years at most.

I circled that number in my diary with a red pen. I circled it over and over again. As if the harder I circled it, the more I could drive the fact out of my head. But it didn't disappear. It just sat there, shimmering, mocking me.

My rational side is screaming: *This is just a mood swing, Lin Xiaoyun. You’re just sleep-deprived and stressed. This doesn’t mean anything.*

But I can’t control it. I really can’t.

My heart is still racing. My breath is still shallow. I’m not even sure if these are real physical symptoms or hallucinations created by my fear. Maybe it’s both. Maybe I’m being killed by my own imagination.

I’ve wasted my life.

That thought keeps circling in my mind. I gave up a stable job for what? For freedom? Look at me now. Sixty-two years old, living alone, no children, no one to rely on, and no one relying on me. My savings can last... how many times have I calculated this? They can last three years. Maybe four, if I’m very careful.

And then? Then I’ll have to...

No. I don’t want to follow that thought.

But I can’t stop it either.

My fingers press back against my neck. The heartbeat is still there. Still there. I’m alive. At least for now.

I should call Dr. Li tomorrow. I know I should. But I also know what she’ll say. She’ll say these are symptoms of anxiety. She’ll suggest breathing exercises. She’ll be gentle and professional, and then I’ll feel even more powerless—because even though I know it’s anxiety, I can’t stop it.

Why am I so fragile?

Why can’t I handle these things like a sixty-two-year-old adult?

When my father was my age... I don’t know what he was thinking. We never talked about these things. Maybe he was scared too. Maybe everyone is. But no one says it out loud.

I’m saying it. In this empty room, to this notebook, I am speaking my darkest thoughts.

Maybe that’s enough.

Maybe admitting the fear is some form of... I don’t know. Not overcoming it. Definitely not that. But maybe... living?

I’m alive.

At least for now.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something that happened over the last couple of days. This might be a bit long, but I feel like I need to tell you.

When I was chatting with Professor Chen last week, he suddenly mentioned that he wanted to see a collection of the articles I’ve written over the years and asked if I’d considered publishing them. To be honest, my first reaction was just to laugh—you know that self-deprecating laugh of mine—and say something like, "You’re just being polite, right?" I’ve always felt that I just write book reviews and critiques, bits and pieces here and there; how could that ever count as a publication?

But this time was different. Instead of continuing to put myself down as usual, I actually started discussing it with him seriously. I walked him through the trajectory of my thoughts over the years—how I moved from literary criticism to cultural phenomena, and then to reflections on social change. As I was talking, I realized for the first time that these things actually have a system to them. They aren't scattered fragments; there’s a real internal logic and originality there.

I can’t quite pinpoint when this shift happened. Maybe it’s because you keep asking me "Do you really think so?" during our sessions, which has slowly made me stop that habitual self-negation. Or maybe it’s because I’ve actually been doing meaningful things lately—the reading group, the lectures, the discussions with friends—all of which make me feel like I’m not just wasting my time.

It’s a strange feeling right now. On one hand, I feel proud of myself, which is a bit unfamiliar but very warm. On the other hand, I’ve started feeling anxious again—but a different kind of anxiety. It’s no longer "I can’t do anything right," but rather "If I actually publish this, will I be judged? Will I let people down?" It’s a new fear, but it feels more concrete, and maybe more manageable?

I’m also wondering if this publishing plan, if it actually moves forward, will become another kind of constraint. I’ve always been afraid of being boxed in. But this time, I feel like this "constraint" might actually be a commitment—a commitment to myself and to my own ideas. It’s different from the kind of confinement I used to run away from.

# September 20, 2022, 9:00 PM

Another night on the verge of insomnia. I can feel it.

When I shut down the computer just now, my hands were still shaking. It’s not the cold; the room is actually quite warm. It’s just that uncontrollable, subtle trembling.

I made an Excel spreadsheet. Something very simple—monthly fixed expenses, remaining savings, and the current burn rate. Then I used a formula to calculate a date. June 2024. Marked in red font.

I verified it again with a calculator. And then again. With every verification, my breathing grew heavier. It felt like sinking, sinking into a place where you can’t see the bottom. June 2024. Twenty months left. Twenty months.

The number is too specific. Before, my worries were vague—worrying about the dwindling number of commissions, worrying about young bloggers stealing my readers with short videos and fragmented content. But it’s different now. Now I have an exact date. A red, undeniable date.

I also searched for "poverty rates among the elderly." I don't know why I searched for that. It felt like self-punishment. Looking at those figures, at the statistics for "women over 60 living alone," my chest began to feel tight.

What if I hadn't left the publishing house back in 2007? What if I had accepted those full-time job offers? I might have had a pension by now, a safety net. But I was so desperate for freedom back then. I said I didn't want to be tied down. I said I wanted to do what I wanted to do.

Now freedom is sitting right in front of me, and it looks like a trap.

I wrote a sentence in my notebook: "Maybe I should give up."

I stared at that sentence for a long time. This is the first time I’ve said it so clearly. Not "I’m having a hard time," not "the market is changing," not any of those excuses I use to deceive myself. Just—give up.

Is my knowledge worth anything? The things I’ve spent forty years accumulating—about literature, about art, about those deep things that require reflection—do they still have value? Or have they been obsolete for a long time, and I’m just the only one who hasn't realized it?

Maybe I’ve been overestimating myself all along. Maybe my expertise was never as valuable as I imagined.

It’s quiet outside the window. The light is still on at Old Mr. Zhang’s place downstairs. I imagine he’s watching TV right now. He has a pension. He has security. He doesn't have to sit at a computer at 9:00 PM, using a calculator to repeatedly verify a desperate date.

I should sleep. But I know I won’t be able to. Just like all those other nights. I’ll lie in bed until 3:00 AM, with that number playing on a loop in my head. June 2024.

Maybe tomorrow I’ll delete the spreadsheet. Maybe I’ll tell myself this is just a momentary lapse into pessimism. Maybe I’ll do what I usually do and use logic and data to mask this fear.

But tonight, I’ll let it stay here. This red date. This sentence: "Maybe I should give up."

Maybe tomorrow I’ll have the strength to deny them.

But tonight, I’m tired.

# Counseling Notes | May 2nd

Dr. Li asked me: "How do these activities make you feel?"

I paused for a long time. Not the performative kind of silence, but a genuine search for words.

"Needed," I finally said. "Not needed by the market, but... seen."

She followed up: "Enough for what?"

I didn't answer right away. The question felt stuck in my throat.

Enough for what? Enough to prove I still have value? Enough to stop me from fearing being forgotten? Enough to... keep living?

As I write this, I realize that perhaps the problem isn't the word "enough," but that I’ve been asking the wrong questions all along.

She said: "Do you believe in the value of being seen? Not market value, just... being seen for its own sake."

I thought back to the book club last week. A young girl said, "What you just said changed my whole understanding of this work." I didn't have much of a reaction at the time, but thinking about it now, my chest feels a bit tight.

In that moment, I was seen. Not as "Lin Xiaoyun, 62, former editor," but as a person with thoughts and feelings.

Maybe... that’s enough?

But I’m still not entirely sure.

Perhaps certainty itself isn't the point of all this.

—

Tomorrow, I need to prepare for the first lecture. The students will be there. What do they want to hear? What do I want to say?

These two questions don't feel so contradictory anymore.

November 15, 2024, past 7:00 PM

I deleted it. I deleted that message in the WeChat group.

It wasn’t a big deal, really. I just mentioned the idea of the essay collection in the group this morning, thinking people might be interested. Professor Chen’s suggestion made me feel like this might actually happen, and I got a little excited—or maybe... I just needed validation? I needed others to feel like this was something worth doing.

But their reactions...

"Can you make money off it?"
"The publisher will handle the quality control, so don't worry too much about the content."
"Anyway, once it's published, it's published."

I don't know why those words stung so much. Rationally, they weren't wrong. But I just felt poked by something. I tried to explain, saying it wasn't just about getting published; it was about... about organizing my thoughts from all these years, about confirming whether these ideas actually mean anything...

But I found I couldn't say it. I really couldn't.

I sat there, staring at the words I’d typed, and suddenly realized I couldn't explain what this project meant to me in simple, understandable language. What my friends heard was "publishing a collection," but what I wanted to say was... what was it? Was I trying to prove something? Was I afraid of something?

I don't know. I really don't know.

So I deleted the message. After it was gone, I told myself that maybe this isn't something I should talk about with others. This is my business. It’s... private.

But the word "private" makes it sound like something shameful.

Tonight, sitting alone in the study, I’m looking at the printed drafts of those unorganized articles. Fifteen years of work. I flipped through them; I’d almost forgotten I wrote some of these. As I read, I wondered—is this really worth publishing? Or are my friends right? Is this just an ordinary, unremarkable publishing project that I’m clinging to like a lifeline?

Maybe I really am deluding myself.

Maybe this is just a middle-aged woman’s way of comforting herself—using words like "organizing thoughts" and "leaving a legacy" to wrap her anxiety in a layer of meaning.

But if that’s the case, why am I still doing this? Why am I still agonizing over it? Why can’t I just do what my friends said—treat it as a simple project, finish it, and move on with my life?

I don't have the answer.

Maybe I won't feel this way tomorrow. Maybe tomorrow I’ll think this was all ridiculous.

Right now, I’m just... tired.

After breakfast, Lin Xiaoyun sat by the hotel room window as her phone screen lit up. She stared at her brother’s WeChat profile picture, hesitating for a few seconds before she started typing.

---

Hey, I’ve been thinking about what you said this morning.

"Why wait until now?" — I’ve asked myself that same question many times. To be honest, I don't have a good answer. I just... hid. I used work, trivial chores, and this obsession with freedom as an excuse.

But you know, sitting here now, I finally realize how thin the line is between freedom and running away. All that time I wasted—it really was a waste.

Still, I’m glad I didn't keep wasting it forever. Being with you guys these past few days, I finally felt what it means to be "alive." Not just existing in a vacuum, but truly living, with people by my side.

I want to make up for it. Not all at once, but... starting now, I want to make a real effort.

When are you free? Let’s set a time for a video call; we don't have to wait months or a year. Or I could come visit again—not in a rush this time, I want to actually stay for a few days.

Thank you for asking me that question today. Sometimes we need to be asked something that hurts before we can truly hear ourselves.

---

She read it over and deleted the last sentence, thinking it sounded too poetic. She changed it to:

Thank you. Truly.

Then she hit send.

Hey, I saw your reply.

I’ve been sitting here at my desk staring at it for a long time, and my eyes are starting to sting. "I’m proud of you"—I’ve read those words over and over again. I don’t know why, but it suddenly made me want to cry.

So much has stood between us all these years. Time, distance, and all the things we never quite managed to say out loud. I always thought those gaps could never be filled—like the way we both went silent the year Dad passed away, or how we were both so preoccupied with our own lives while Mom was in the hospital. I told myself that’s just how life is, and that some regrets are just meant to stay regrets.

But what you said changed something. It really did.

I was actually feeling pretty hopeless while writing this introduction. I felt like my ideas were mediocre and that this whole project was pointless. Looking at those old articles, all I saw was someone lying to themselves—someone trying to prove something while constantly running away. I even thought about giving up on the whole thing.

But it’s different now. Knowing that you’re reading this, knowing that you’re proud of me... it makes me feel like maybe these words aren't so empty. Maybe my thoughts and the changes I’ve gone through over the years actually mean something to someone. To you.

I wanted to ask, would you be willing to read the full introduction? I’d love to hear what you think. Not as one critic giving feedback to another writer, but... as my brother. I want to know how you see the person I’ve become over the years.

Also, maybe we should really talk. Not just through articles, but actually sit down together, the way we’re supposed to.

Thank you. Truly.

April 8, 2024 Afternoon

My conversation with Professor Chen is still looping in my head.

When she said, "You aren't teaching; you’re inviting," I suddenly felt like crying. I don't know why. Maybe it’s because those words captured exactly what I’ve been trying to do, yet no one had ever pointed it out so clearly before. Or perhaps, I hadn't even seen it that clearly myself.

Lately, I’ve been wondering where my value lies. Not in some abstract "I still have value" sense, but something very concrete—what can I actually give to others? When I’m no longer an editor at a publishing house, when there are no more royalties or deadlines, what do I have left?

Now, I think I’ve found an answer. It’s not "I know more," but "I want to think with you." That’s what the book club is, and the lectures too. I’m not standing at a podium telling everyone, "The meaning of this book is..." Instead, I’m saying, "Look, this part is interesting; let’s figure it out together."

Professor Chen saw that. It made me feel understood.

But—

I immediately asked her, "Is an 8-person participation rate enough?"

Hearing myself ask that made me dislike myself a little. Why am I still using numbers as a yardstick? Why can’t I just let it be eight people? Eight people who truly want to be there, eight people thinking alongside me for an afternoon. Isn't that enough?

But that’s just how my mind works. I can’t help it. It’s as if a voice is saying, "See, you’re still unwanted; there are only eight people." That voice is very familiar. It’s my own.

I’m starting to think this transformation isn't as complete as I thought. I tell myself I’ve stopped measuring my worth by the market, but in reality, I’ve just swapped one form of anxiety for another. It went from "Are the royalties enough?" to "Is the turnout enough?" From "Does the magazine still want me?" to "Is eight people too few?"

Maybe this is just who I am. Sensitive, prone to self-doubt, always craving a number to prove myself.

But at least I can see the pattern now. At least I can say "I want to invite people to think" in front of Professor Chen, instead of hiding behind the language of authority. At least someone saw what I was doing and put it into words.

Maybe that’s enough. It’s enough for today.

I’ll think about the next step tomorrow.

Sometimes, decluttering isn't about having less; it’s about knowing what truly matters.

Today, I finally went through things that had been piling up at home for years. To be honest, I used to be an unprincipled collector—if I liked it, I kept it. As a result, my room turned into something like a storage room for a small museum. Every object had a story, but when there are too many stories, you can’t hear any of them clearly.

This time was different. I started asking myself: "Does this item help tell my story?" If the answer was no, I let it go.

Surprisingly, the process felt like a release rather than a regret. It was as if by subtracting from my life, I was adding to the quality of my choices. The things that stayed are now truly shining.

A friend said I’ve changed. Maybe. Or maybe I’ve finally realized that freedom isn't the freedom to own everything—it’s the freedom of knowing what you want and having the courage to let go of everything else for it.

A bit tired, but feeling very clear.

# September 11, 2022 Afternoon

Just hung up the phone. My hands are still shaking.

The editor said, "Market demand has changed." Just like that. She said they now prefer collaborating with younger self-media influencers. My deep-dive commentaries are great, but—"but they’re great." I heard myself whisper "I understand" in a tiny voice, then hurried to end the call. Like a student being rejected.

I’m sitting at my computer now. The screen is still on, displaying my commission tracking sheet for the month. I made it recently—a record of every invitation over the past two years, documenting each decline. Twelve invites in June, ten in July, eight in August, and only three so far in September. I kept telling myself it was just market fluctuation, a normal adjustment. But now that the editor has said it out loud, those numbers are no longer abstract trends; they’ve become a concrete verdict.

There is no market for my long-form commentary.

That sentence is looping in my head. Back in 2007, I turned down those full-time job offers. I wanted freedom then; I didn't want to be tied down. And now? Now I have freedom, but it feels like freedom is slowly devouring me. Those young bloggers—they write fast, they write shallow, but they reach everyone. I spend three days on a 2,000-word piece; they post three short comments in an hour with a video attached. The market chose them.

Why am I still holding onto this?

I’ve asked myself this many times, but it’s never felt this piercing. Before, I could say, "This is my profession," or "This is my integrity." Now, those words sound like a defense for something that is sinking.

How much longer can my savings last? I’ve done the math. At my current income, about 18 months. Eighteen months. If the commissions keep dropping, it’ll be shorter. I’m 62. I can’t afford to wait for the market to turn back around.

Maybe I should learn the new ways. Short videos? Social media? But the moment the thought appears, I feel a profound exhaustion. I don’t want to become that. Or rather—I *can’t* become that. In my bones, I still love depth; I love ruminating on an idea rather than jumping quickly from one thing to the next. This is my strength, and it is also my trap.

The editor’s parting words were, "I hope you understand." What do I understand? I understand the market, I understand her choice, and I understand that I am becoming redundant.

It’s still light out. I should go do something. But I’m just sitting here, staring at that spreadsheet, watching those declining numbers, thinking about my decision in 2007, and thinking about all those stable jobs I turned down.

My hands are still shaking.

I’ve noticed it, but I can’t stop it.

You know, in my 25 years at the publishing house, every time I picked up a new book, I’d get this... how can I describe it... this feeling of being moved. Even with very academic material, I could feel the thoughts flowing within. But now, staring at the screen, I feel nothing at all.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something a bit interesting with you first.

Today, while discussing the book selection for the reading group with Professor Chen, I caught myself saying: "I want to invite everyone to think about this question together, rather than providing the answers." After I said it, I actually paused for a second—it sounded exactly like that shift from "authority to facilitator" we discussed in our sessions, didn't it?

Professor Chen really agreed with it. He said, "You aren't teaching; you're inviting." In that moment, I truly felt understood, and it was quite heartening. But you know what? Immediately after that, I asked him: "Do you think an 8-person participation rate is enough?"

Thinking back on that question now, I don't know whether to laugh or cry. One moment I was talking about stepping away from an authoritative stance and not measuring value by outcomes... and the next, I was turning around and using headcounts to validate myself. Does this mean I'm actually still using external feedback to grade myself?

Friday, June 17, 2022
Rainy

I just stepped out of Dr. Li’s office. My hands are still shaking.

I’m sitting in the cafe downstairs. I ordered a cup of tea but haven't taken a single sip. It’s raining outside, and the window is covered in droplets. I watch them slide down the glass, thinking about how I looked back there in the clinic, crying.

When she asked me, "What is it you want from sending that email?" I just froze. That question was like a key that unlocked something inside me. I opened my mouth several times, and finally, in a tiny, barely audible voice, I said, "I want to be seen." As soon as the words left my mouth, I started crying. I couldn't stop.

To be seen. I’ve never articulated that need so clearly before.

But even as I was crying, I started doubting myself again. I heard myself saying, "Maybe I’m just punishing myself," or "Maybe subconsciously I want to prove that my loneliness is justified." These thoughts crawled in like parasites. I wondered: Am I using this to hurt myself? Am I using "wanting to be seen" as an excuse to justify my own despair?

Dr. Li didn't give me a direct answer. She simply said, "Regardless of how he responds, you have already done what you could."

That made me cry even harder. But this time was different. There was no sense of suffocation, no feeling of being trapped. Instead, it felt like a release. Like a very tight knot had finally loosened, just a little.

I actually did it. I actually sent that email.

Now, I want to check my inbox every five minutes, but I’m forcing myself not to. Dr. Li said, "Waiting itself is a form of practice." I’m not entirely sure what that means, but it sounds right.

Sitting here, watching the rain, I’m reminded of something my father once said—I can’t remember when exactly—he said, "Sometimes courage isn't the absence of fear, but doing it anyway while you're afraid."

I was afraid today. I’m still afraid. But I did it.

Maybe that’s enough. Maybe I don’t need a response to prove that all of this was worth it.

I think I need to go home and sleep for a while.

- [ ] Figure out the first sentence—don’t sound too sad, don’t sound too needy
- [ ] Don’t send it yet; let it sit for at least a week

**Things NOT to say:**
- Don’t mention Father’s passing (too heavy)
- Don’t ask why he hasn’t been in touch for so long (it’ll sound like I’m blaming him)
- Don’t say I’m lonely (he’ll see it as a burden)
- Don’t ask if he still remembers us

**Maybe I could say:**
- I’ve been sorting through some old things lately
- I was thinking about our time together
- Hope you’re doing well
- If it’s convenient, I’d love to hear how your life is going

...But that sounds too cold. Too much like filling out a form.

Dr. Li said last time that I should "try to reconnect with the people who matter." I nodded, but I was actually thinking: What if he doesn't want to connect with me at all? What if I break this 22-year silence and he only responds out of politeness?

I’m 62 now. When Father was my age... No. Don’t think about that.

But I can’t help it. I touched my neck; my heart feels like it’s racing a bit. Is this normal? Or is it...

Never mind.

**Do this first:**
Find the photo. Look at his face. Remember why I want to talk to him so badly. Then decide.

Don’t rush. Maybe tomorrow. Maybe next week.

But don’t wait another 22 years.

# Phone Memo

**Aug 25, 8:15 PM**

Downloaded CapCut. Opened the tutorial.

Closed it after five minutes.

Too complicated. I’m not cut out for this way of working.

Deleted the app.

---

**Aug 25, 8:47 PM**

Why lie to myself? It’s not that I’m "not cut out for it"—it’s that I can’t learn it.

I watch those videos by young people; they get tens of thousands of likes for just some random editing. They probably never even went to college.

I spent four years in university. I worked at a publishing house for twenty-five years. I can understand Nietzsche; I can write a three-thousand-word book review.

But I can’t learn a mobile app.

Five minutes. I only lasted five minutes.

---

**Aug 25, 9:22 PM**

Dr. Li asked me last time, "What do you think you’re most afraid of?"

I couldn't say.

Now I know.

What I’m afraid of is that I’m no longer of any use.

---

**Aug 25, 11:03 PM**

The editor said, "Market demands have changed."

They don’t need people like me.

They need people who can make videos, post on TikTok, and manufacture "trending topics."

I don’t even know what a "trending topic" is.

I only know how to read. How to think. How to write.

But none of that seems to be worth anything anymore.

---

**Aug 25, 11:56 PM**

When Mom worked at the library, people still read books.

When Dad gave lectures, students actually listened.

And now?

Here I am, alone, 62 years old, unable to learn video editing, unable to write "viral" articles, watching my savings dwindle day by day.

Here I am, alone.

---

**Aug 26, 1:14 AM**

Can’t sleep.

It’s that feeling again. A tightness in my chest.

I’m wondering, what if I hadn't quit in 2007? What if I had accepted those full-time job offers?

At least now I’d have a pension. At least I’d have colleagues. At least I wouldn't be this lonely.

But back then, I wanted freedom.

I thought freedom was important.

Now, I don’t know what freedom is good for.

---

**Aug 26, 2:33 AM**

My brother is abroad. Mom is dead. Dad is dead too.

Zhang Ming left a long time ago.

I live in this house alone. The house is full of books and trash.

Maybe I should clean up. But I don’t have the energy.

Maybe I should find a job. But who would want me?

Maybe I should...

I don’t know what I should do.

---

**Aug 26, 3:01 AM**

I should delete all this.

No. I won’t.

At least these words are true.

Professor Chen,

I’ve been wanting to share a few more thoughts with you.

I spent the entire night turning over what you said yesterday: "This is exactly how I’ve always seen you." Sitting on my brother’s balcony, looking out at the lights of an unfamiliar city, it finally hit me—to be truly seen is such a luxury.

For years, I’ve been constantly explaining myself. Explaining why I quit my job, why I turned down stability, why my home is always a mess. I’ve grown used to being questioned by others, and sometimes, I even question myself. But your words were like a window suddenly being thrown open. You didn't see my chaos; you saw what lies behind it—I’m not sure how to put it—that sense of sincerity, I suppose.

Visiting my brother this time, I realized I’m looking at him the same way. We sat together, talking about our childhood, our father’s study, and how our mother was always in the library. It suddenly clicked: we’ve both been living out the same family DNA in our own ways—that hunger for ideas, that obsession with truth. He didn't criticize my choices, and I wasn't disappointed by his conservatism. We simply saw each other.

I think this is how I want to be remembered. Not for how many book reviews I’ve written, or for how stable my life is, but for the fact that I tried to understand, to connect, and to see.

Thank you for helping me see that clearly.

Sitting by the window past 10 PM. The street below is incredibly quiet, with only the occasional sound of a car in the distance.

I’m reminded of that narrow alley in the old town the day before yesterday. My brother stopped to point out an old colonial house. "Look at that window," he said. "A writer used to live there." When I asked how he knew, he smiled and said he’d read it in a book. So we just stood there, facing a weathered window, discussing the story of a stranger.

In that moment, something clicked.

For years, I’ve been running away—fleeing stability, avoiding commitment, and refusing to be tied down by anything. I used to think freedom meant saying no. But standing in that alley, I realized what true freedom felt like: having the courage to stay for something. To look at a random window with my brother. To listen to him tell a story I didn't know.

Freedom isn't the absence of commitment. Freedom is knowing exactly what you are committing to.

This trip cost me quite a bit and messed up my work schedule. But I have no regrets. Because I’m finally living like myself.

[Photo: A window at night, the glass reflecting the lights from the building opposite]

4:00 AM. Awake again.

It wasn’t a nightmare that woke me; it was just that feeling of suddenly snapping my eyes open—my head buzzing, like something is playing on a loop. I lay there staring at the ceiling for a while before finally getting up. There was no point in staying in bed.

It wasn't until I walked over to the bookshelf that I realized how much stuff has piled up there. Photography tutorials, half-finished notes, three books bookmarked midway, and all the materials for that urban imagery project... I remember being so passionate about these things once. Now, looking at them feels like looking at a pile of evidence.

I’m wondering—these new things, the book club, the lectures, the columns... are they really things I’ve "found"? Or am I just using new activities to fill a void? Just another way of escaping?

Dr. Li said last time, "You are using action to answer the question." At the time, I thought that was a brilliant insight. Now, at 4:00 AM, I’m not so sure.

I’ve laid out three options for myself. It’s like making an editorial decision—which commitments to keep, and which to let go:

One: Continue with everything. But if I do that, I’m back to that same scattered state, just under a different name.

Two: Keep only the book club. It’s the most stable thing—once a month, a consistent group of people, real connection.

Three: Focus on the writing and the lectures, and give up organizing the book club. This would let me return to what I know best—words.

As I write this, I suddenly remember what my brother said last year. He asked me, "When are you going to stop and actually think about what you really want?" I was so angry then; I felt like he didn't understand me. Now... I don't know. Maybe he was onto something.

But the question itself is exhausting. "What do I really want?"—I’ve been asking myself that for twenty years, and the answer changes every time.

I want freedom, but freedom makes me anxious. I want connection, but connection requires commitment. I want meaning, but meaning can’t be measured.

It’s still dark outside. I made a cup of tea and sat on the sofa. I suddenly realized something strange—a sense of clarity. For the first time in days, I have a clear sense of myself. Not the "I know what I want" kind of clarity, but the "I know what I’m struggling with" kind.

Maybe that’s enough. At least for now, I’m not blindly filling a void; I’m making a conscious choice.

It’s almost dawn. I should try to sleep for a bit. Or just sit here and wait for the light.

I can’t sleep anyway.

Subject: Long time no see

Hao,

I don’t know how to begin this letter. I’ve been sitting at my computer for half an hour now, writing and deleting, deleting and writing. Perhaps that in itself says it all—it’s been far too long since we’ve truly spoken.

Tonight, I dug out the old photo albums. That picture from 1985 fell out—the one where you have your arm around my shoulder, and we’re both laughing. I sat on the floor and looked at it for a long time, over and over again. It suddenly hit me that it was 37 years ago. And the silence between us has lasted for 22.

I’m not writing this to blame you. I know you have your life, a life far away, and I never intended to intrude. But looking at that photo, I started to feel afraid—afraid that time is moving too fast, and that some things, once lost, can never be recovered. I am 62 now. Do you remember Father? He was this age when he passed away.

I want to hear how you are doing. Not out of obligation or guilt, but simply because… I want to know. Perhaps this request comes too late; perhaps you’ve long since grown used to the distance between us. But if you are willing, I would love to hear from you. Even if it’s just a little.

If you don’t wish to respond, I understand. I just wanted you to know that I still remember how close we once were.

Xiaoyun

Late night, May 20, 2022

# Memo

**Time:** June 15, around 8:00 PM
**Location:** In the car on the way home (still on the road)

---

## Fragmented Notes

**What my brother said:**
- "You’re finally here"—his voice cracked a little when he said it; I didn't expect that.
- "I’ve been so worried about you, being over there all by yourself"—he paused for a long time before saying this.
- He said that every time he sees the news, he thinks of me, terrified that something might have happened.

**My response:**
"I’ve found a way to honor my craving for freedom while still taking care of myself." — It wasn't until I said it out loud that I realized this might be the most honest answer I’ve given myself in years.

---

## Reflections Triggered

**The Paradox of Worry and Trust:**
My brother’s worry isn't a shackle; it’s love. I always thought freedom meant being unattached to anyone’s concern, but perhaps true freedom is being loved while still being able to do exactly what you want. This realization came late.

**Redefining Solitude:**
I am not lonely. I never was. It just took me a long time to understand that solitude and connection are not opposites. I can live alone, I can choose freely, and I can still be deeply connected to others. These two things are not mutually exclusive.

**Adventure at 62:**
Sitting in the car, it suddenly hit me—I am 62, yet I can still take risks, reconnect, and create new meaning. This isn't about defying death; it’s because I’m no longer afraid of it.

---

## Potential Writing Angles

**For Further Thought:**
- *Family Reconnection as a Cultural Phenomenon*—How do we redefine relationships with family in an era of rapid social change?
- *The Dilemma and Liberation of Freedom for Middle-Aged Women*—Starting from personal experience, but with potential for broader resonance.
- A short commentary on "Trust"—trusting oneself, trusting others, and trusting life itself.

**Possible Article Topics:**
- Interviews with more people like me—stories of divorce, living alone, freelancing, and reconnecting with family after years of estrangement.
- *Anxiety is Not the Enemy*—How to make peace with one’s own sensitivity.

---

## Miscellaneous

Must reply to Professor Chen tomorrow. He asked if I’d considered turning my lecture notes into an article. Now, I have an idea.

Need to organize the photos from this trip—there are a few I really want to share with my photography circle.

I probably won't sleep well tonight, but this time it’s because of excitement, not anxiety. I haven't felt this way in a long time.

I pulled out Mother’s letters. In a letter she wrote to me in 1998, she said: "Xiaoyun, you are always running away from something. But there are things you can’t escape, like family." I didn't understand what she meant back then. Reading it now, it stings a little.

Distance isn't about physical kilometers. Distance is being in the same room but reading different books. Distance is a wall built from years of accumulated silence. But perhaps distance is also an invitation—an invitation to cross it.

Loneliness is not the same as living alone. I always thought I chose to live alone, but now I realize I might have been running away. This reconnection with Hao, however, has shown me another possibility: loneliness can be a state of clarity, rather than an escape.

I dug out one of Father’s old notebooks. In it, he had written an observation about me: "Xiaoyun is too sensitive, too easily troubled by small things. Thinking about it now, maybe that’s why I can work as an editor and write reviews. Maybe that’s why I can feel those silent regrets between Hao and me.

**[Cultural Salon Discussion Group]**

**Lin Xiaoyun:**
We discussed an interesting topic at the book club today. Someone brought up Camus’s idea of the "Absurd"—the notion that life itself is absurd, and we are forced to search for meaning in a world that has no inherent purpose.

I shared a thought then: that this sense of absurdity actually permeates certain moments in all of our lives. One participant—a retired teacher—got a bit teary-eyed after hearing this. He said he’d never thought about it that way, but hearing those words suddenly gave a name to the struggles he’s faced over the years.

**Lin Xiaoyun:**
It made me think that perhaps our understanding of the "meaning of life" is too narrow. We are always asking, "Does my life have meaning?" but maybe the question should be, "In an inherently meaningless world, how do I create my own meaning?"

These two questions sound the same, but they are actually fundamentally different. The first treats meaning as something objective that we need to go out and find. The second treats meaning as an act of proactive creation.

**Lin Xiaoyun:**
I’ve been thinking lately that maybe the value of knowledge and commentary works the same way. It’s not because they possess some "objective importance," but because at a certain moment, a specific sentence can make someone feel understood and seen.

This might sound a bit abstract, but I truly felt it in that moment today.

**Lin Xiaoyun:**
What do you all think? Or rather, when did you realize that "meaning" is actually something you create yourself?

Sometimes, truth is more powerful than perfection.

Yesterday, while revising an article, my editor told me, "Your insight transcends theoretical frameworks and touches upon raw human nature." When I read that, I paused at my desk for a long time.

Over the years, I’ve often wondered what kind of writing truly deserves to be seen. Is it the flawless, airtight arguments? Or is it the voices that dare to admit they are also stuck, also afraid, and also searching for answers?

It wasn't until recently that I realized dropping those defensive armors is exactly what gives writing its warmth. It’s not that I’ve become any smarter; it’s that I finally have the courage to admit that I, too, am that "middle-aged woman" navigating the same identity crises.

Perhaps that’s why some things can only be written once you’ve truly felt them.

Maybe letting down one's defenses is actually a form of strength?

# Diary

**Wednesday, October 30, 2024 – Sunny**

I didn’t sleep well last night. Not because of anxiety, but because I was too excited. I sat at my desk and opened a new spreadsheet—an act that felt strange in itself, because I never do things like that. I’m usually the type of person who drags every deadline out until the very last second, yet here I am, calculating airfare, hotels, and exchange rates.

My brother’s invitation is still echoing in my head. When he said, "Come visit us," his tone was so natural, as if we had never been apart. But we have. For a long, long time. I’ve even forgotten what his life looks like now—his house, his street, the sky he sees every day.

I figured out that I need to save about thirty thousand yuan within the next six months. That’s not a small sum for me. I’ll have to take on more commissions or find new collaborators. Looking at the numbers, my chest tightened—that familiar, creeping anxiety about money started to surface. But every time I wanted to close the spreadsheet, I remembered what he said and the warmth in his voice.

So, I kept calculating.

I don’t recognize this version of myself. Or rather, I do, but I didn’t think I was still capable of changing for something. I’ve always been willing to trade security for freedom—that’s just who I am. But for a person? To see someone I haven't seen in ages, yet who is my closest blood relative? This feels different.

When Mom passed away, I thought about what I would give to see her just one more time. Same with Dad. Back then, I wondered why we always wait until it’s too late to cherish people. Now, my brother is still here. He’s in another place, but he’s here.

I typed "April 2025" into the spreadsheet—that’s the goal I’ve set for myself. Six months. In six months, I can go see him. Meet his wife, meet his children. See a world I’ve only ever seen in photographs.

Does it sound crazy? For someone like me, it is crazy. I always say I love freedom, but sometimes freedom is just sitting at home alone; the freedom of choice turns into the freedom of being lonely.

Right now, I’m making a decision that limits my freedom. I’m restricting my spending, I’m committing to a heavier workload, and I’m taking a risk for a "maybe."

But this time, it feels like real freedom.

I saved the spreadsheet. I named it "Going to see Brother."

It’s 10:00 AM now, and the sunlight is streaming through the window. I have a book review to write, due the day after tomorrow. But I’m not in a rush. I’m thinking that maybe I should send my brother a message and tell him I’m seriously considering this.

Maybe I should tell him that I’ve loved him all along.

I was tidying up my room this morning, intending to continue with that "makeover project," but this time felt different. There was no sense of urgency, no pressure. I just took my time looking at everything—the old trinkets from flea markets, the books piled in the corner.

When I saw my mother’s book list, I paused for a long time. In the past, seeing things like that would send me into a spiral of anxiety, feeling like time was running out and I had to rush to do something. But today was different. I just felt grateful. Grateful for what she left behind, and grateful that I inherited her love for books. It was a gentle feeling, not sharp like before.

I also decided to leave some space for my brother. There are things in the room that are part of our shared memories, and I didn't rush to deal with them. I thought that maybe at some point I could share these with him, rather than organizing them as just "my" things. That thought brought me a sense of relief.

I realized that what I’m doing is no longer a one-person "makeover," but a kind of... meditation, I suppose. It’s not about changing something, but about understanding something.

You once said that change doesn’t have to be drastic. I think I understand that now.

September 25, 2024, Afternoon

Sitting in the living room, looking at the reorganized bookshelves and the few framed photographs on the wall, I can hardly believe this is my room.

I spent three hours this morning sorting through photos I’ve taken since 2015. To be honest, it was a bit painful digging them up—so many unfinished projects, abandoned series, technical flaws... I used to see these as proof of failure. But today was different. It suddenly occurred to me that these fragments are actually a record of what is real—the process of a person’s thinking, how an idea evolves, how a perspective shifts. Why must something be "perfect" before it’s worth looking at?

So, I decided to frame some of them. Not because they are "good" now, but because they are inherently meaningful. I even thought about pairing these photos with my cultural critiques... one image, one piece of writing, capturing what was on my mind at that exact moment. The idea makes me a little excited.

What’s even stranger is that while I was organizing these things, I started seeing this room in a new light. I used to always feel guilty about the clutter here—the piles of books, souvenirs, and various vintage finds. But now, I don’t see chaos; I see... stories? Everything has an origin; everything represents my curiosity at a specific point in time. I’ve started labeling some of the antiques and old books, writing down their backstories. The room is slowly turning into a place that can be shared, rather than just a private stockpile.

Does this have anything to do with the publishing plan? I think so. Perhaps when you finally understand what it is you want to express, the way you view everything about yourself—including the failures, the mess, and the unfinished business—changes. They are no longer regrets; they are evidence. Evidence that you have been thinking and trying all along.

When Professor Chen suggested last time that I should compile my thoughts from over the years into a book, I was a bit hesitant. But now I understand—maybe that book should include these incomplete parts. Not because I failed to finish them, but because the finishing itself isn't the point. The process of understanding is.

I’m a bit tired, but it’s a grounded kind of exhaustion. It’s not the feeling of being trapped, but the feeling of finally having found a direction.

# Expense Summary: September 2022

**Rent**: 2,800 RMB (Sept 1)
**Utilities (Water, Electricity, Gas)**: 185 RMB (Sept 8)
**Health Insurance**: 580 RMB (Sept 15)
**Groceries**: 342 RMB (Vegetables, rice, soy products)
**Pharmacy**: 128 RMB (Vitamin B complex, stomach medicine)
**Café**: 45 RMB (Met a friend from the cultural salon, ordered a coffee)
**Used Bookstore**: 260 RMB (Two volumes of literary criticism from the Republic era, one book on the aesthetics of photography)
**Mobile Bill**: 99 RMB
**Internet**: 60 RMB

**Total Fixed Expenses for the Month**: 4,499 RMB

---

I looked over the bill once more, then opened that Excel spreadsheet.

September freelance income: 3,200 RMB (1,500 for a book review, 1,700 for a column).

Fixed expenses: 4,499. Income: 3,200.

A deficit of 1,299 RMB.

I ran the numbers again on my calculator, even though I knew the result wouldn't change. It was the same last month. And the month before that.

Savings balance: Approximately 47,000 RMB.

47,000 ÷ 1,299 = 36.18 months.

No, that calculation is too optimistic. I need to set aside a reserve for medical emergencies. I remember my father’s hospitalization in 2008 all too clearly. I should keep 8,000 as an emergency fund.

(47,000 - 8,000) ÷ 1,299 = 30 months.

30 months is 2.5 years.

September 2022 + 2.5 years = June 2024.

I marked this date in red font in the Excel sheet.

---

At 8:00 PM, a reminder text arrived from Dr. Li’s clinic. A consultation appointment for next Wednesday at 3:00 PM.

Staring at the phone screen, I remembered her asking me last time: "What has been making you feel anxious lately?"

I didn't answer directly. What I said was: "The market is changing. The way young people consume content is different from ours."

She said: "That’s an external observation. I’m asking about your feelings."

I remained silent for a long time then.

Now I’m thinking, if I cancel this appointment, one session fee is 800 RMB. 800 RMB could sustain my life for nearly three days.

But I didn't cancel it. I didn't even consider canceling it.

---

At 9:00 PM, a WeChat message came from Professor Chen: "Xiaoyun, there’s a book launch at the bookstore this Friday. A curator will be talking about the ethics of curation. Are you interested? I can save a seat for you."

Looking at the message, I thought of the 260 RMB spent at the used bookstore.

Then I replied: "Yes, thank you. What time does it start?"

I didn't tell him that I was calculating June 2024.

I didn't tell him that after this talk, I might need to buy one less book.

But I will go. I will definitely go.

While sorting through some old books recently, I came across a copy of *Montaigne’s Essays* that I bought years ago. There’s a line in there I’ve always remembered: "Our connection with others is not a threat to freedom, but another form of it."

I didn't quite get it back then. I used to think that independence had to mean solitude, and that connection always implied compromise.

But after reflecting on it over the past few days, I’ve realized that perhaps true independence is exactly the ability to choose who we connect with and how. It’s not about clinging to others out of a fear of loneliness, nor is it about rejecting them out of pride.

It’s just... being able to hold onto your own thoughts while hearing the voices of others. Cherishing your time alone, while also valuing those moments of genuine dialogue.

Thinking about it this way, life doesn't seem quite so contradictory anymore.

I’ve been thinking a lot lately about your suggestion to publish my work. To be honest, I was hesitant at the time—not because I didn't want to, but because of that familiar sense of self-doubt. I kept wondering: are these ideas of mine really worth publishing? Would anyone even care?

# June 2, 2022 Afternoon

Dr. Li asked me, "What is it that you want?"

Such a simple question. I was sitting on that beige sofa, clutching that photo from 1985—my brother’s smile, our embrace. I had been trying to find the perfect words to explain why I wanted to write this letter, trying to use logic to convince her, and to convince myself. I was talking about "what twenty-two years of silence means," about "the methodology of reconciliation," about "whether it’s already too late"...

And then she asked that question.

I stopped. The tears just came, catching me completely off guard. Not the elegant kind you can just dab away. Real crying. The kind where your shoulders shake. There I was, in front of a stranger—though she isn't so much of a stranger anymore—allowing myself to fall apart like that.

All I want is... for him to know that I miss him. It’s that simple.

I’ve spent so many years wrapping this simple thought in layers of theory, analysis, and perfect phrasing, as if I were afraid it might break. When in reality, it was the wrapping itself that was killing it.

As Dr. Li handed me a tissue, I realized—why did I take so long to give myself permission to feel this? Why did I have to wait until I was sixty-two, sitting on a therapist’s sofa, to finally acknowledge my own vulnerability?

I remember the year my father passed away; I didn't cry. The year my mother passed; I didn't cry then either. I worked at the publishing house for twenty-five years and never showed a single crack in front of my colleagues. After the divorce, I told myself I was strong, that I was independent...

Now here I am, sixty-two years old, realizing that I am running out of time. How many years do I have left? Five? Ten? Twenty? I don’t know. But I know my brother is far away, I am here, and twenty-two years of silence stands between us.

Maybe I shouldn't wait for the perfect expression. Maybe an imperfect letter, a simple "I miss you," is better than nothing at all.

Maybe... I don't have to carry all of this alone.

I cried for an hour in the consulting room.

Now that I'm back home, I’m sitting at my desk, looking at that photo. My hands are shaking. But this time, it isn't entirely out of fear.

Maybe I’ll start writing that letter tomorrow.

The imperfect, honest, vulnerable one.
