3:47 AM

Awake again.

I don’t know how many times it’s been. This week? This month? I’ve lost count. My heart is racing again—that familiar, suffocating sensation. I press my fingers against my neck, counting the seconds. One, two, three... Still alive. At least for now.

When my phone screen lights up, I see my own face—pale, puffy, eyes wide open like a terrified stranger. I don’t recognize this person.

I’ve started searching again. "Symptoms of arrhythmia," "Suicide rates for anxiety disorders"... My fingers slide across the screen, but I don’t click on any of the results. I know it’s ridiculous. I know what I’m doing. But I can't stop. These searches give me a phantom sense of control—as if by typing these words, I can grasp onto something. But I have a grasp on nothing.

My father died at 68.

I am 62 now.

That number flashes in my head incessantly, like a countdown timer. Six years. Maybe seven. Maybe tomorrow. I can’t stop thinking about it. I’ve tried. I’ve tried distracting myself, reading books, listening to podcasts, but the number always resurfaces. In my most relaxed moments, just as I’m about to drift off, it pops up: 68. 68. 68.

My mother was 71.

So do I have nine years left? Or six? Why am I calculating this? Why can’t I stop?

I get up to go to the bathroom and turn toward the mirror. The woman in the reflection looks like she’s already close to that age. Wrinkles, sagging skin, bags under her eyes. When did it become like this? I remember being young. I remember... No, I don’t want to remember. Memories only make this worse.

I started a note on my phone. "Symptom Log." Body temperature, blood pressure, sleep duration, dreams. I record everything every day. As if this could... what? Save me? Prove I’m still alive? I know it’s crazy. The rational part of me knows this is OCD. But the rational part of me can’t stop it either.

Why am I so fragile?

Why can’t I be like a normal 62-year-old woman—accepting aging, accepting death, and just getting on with life?

...

I used to crave freedom so much. I gave up stable jobs, a predictable life, and all the security I could have had. I thought freedom was worth it. I thought I was strong enough to handle the uncertainty. But now, facing all of this, I’m questioning everything. What did freedom give me? Old age filled with anxiety. A financial foundation that could collapse at any moment. A loneliness with no one to lean on.

Maybe I should have made different choices fifteen years ago.

Maybe I should have accepted those job offers.

Maybe...

No. It’s too late for that now. I am trapped in my own choices.

I walk back to the bedroom. It’s still dark outside. It will be a long time before dawn. I lie in bed and close my eyes, trying not to think of that number.

But it’s still there.

It’s always there.

Dr. Li, I want to tell you about something that happened today. This might be a bit long, but I need to sort through my thoughts.

My editor emailed me this afternoon asking about the progress of those two pieces. Her tone was friendly, not pushy at all. But it’s that very friendliness that makes me feel worse. I told her, "I expect to have both turned in by next week."

The moment I said it, I knew I was lying. There’s no way I can do it. I’ve barely written a single complete paragraph in the last two weeks.

Do you know what the most ironic part is? My immediate reaction was to open a browser and search for "how to stay productive while anxious." I read three articles, as if that would somehow solve the problem. I even took notes on a few keywords—"time management," "mindful working," things like that. It’s as if I believe that if I can theorize and systematize my anxiety, it will just disappear.

But it didn't disappear. I just drank my fourth cup of coffee.

I’m stuck in a strange predicament now. If I tell my editor the truth—"I have anxiety and I can't work right now"—I might lose the contract. They’ll find someone else. And I need the income. But if I keep lying like this, I still won't have the drafts ready next week, and the lie will look even uglier then.

I know this is the pattern you mentioned last time. I’m using logic to escape emotion. I’m using searches, analysis, and concepts to replace actual feelings. Even now, as I describe this to you, I’m trying to use words like "predicament" and "pattern" to turn this into a problem that can be discussed, rather than a fear I am actually experiencing.

What am I afraid of? I think it’s this—if I admit I can’t work, then who am I? For years, I’ve told everyone I chose freelance work because I craved independence. But maybe the truth is that I was just running away. And now, that escape is swallowing up the last of my professional identity.

Do you think I should tell the editor? Or is this just another moment where I need to learn to "feel instead of analyze"?

I’m not sure. But I know I can’t go on like this.

I sat at my desk, my finger hovering over the phone screen for a long time.

3:45 PM. Outside, the sky was a dull gray, and the windows in the building opposite were lighting up one by one. I opened the chat with Dr. Li, then closed it. Opened it, then closed it again.

Finally, I typed these words:

---

Dr. Li, sorry to bother you.

I was thinking about what you said during our last session—the part about "giving yourself permission to say no." I’ve been reflecting on that lately.

Yesterday, a friend invited me to a salon event again. I turned her down. I told her I’ve been a bit tired lately, that work’s been busy. But actually… I don’t even know why I refused. Usually, I enjoy these kinds of things.

But I’ve noticed that lately, I seem to be saying no to everything. No to parties, no to invitations, no to… it’s like I’m rejecting anything that requires me to step out. My friend replied quickly, saying, "No worries, maybe next time." But I keep re-reading that message. For some reason, I feel like there’s a sense of… exhaustion in it? Or maybe disappointment?

I know I might be overthinking it. But I just can’t help it. I keep wondering: is she getting fed up with me constantly saying no? Will there come a day when she just stops inviting me altogether?

When I think like this, I feel a strange kind of fear. It’s not exactly the fear of losing a friend—or at least, not entirely. It’s more like… a sense of isolation. Like I’m the one actively pushing myself away.

I haven’t been sleeping well lately, either. I always wake up around three or four in the morning, and then I start spiraling into all this mess. Sometimes I wonder: why did I say no? Why can’t I just go to events and maintain relationships like a normal person? Is there something fundamentally wrong with me?

I’m tired. Not physically tired, but… a different kind of tired.

You said that "refusing is also a choice that needs to be understood." But right now, I feel like my refusal isn't a choice at all. It’s… I can’t quite put it into words.

Can you help me make sense of this?

---

I read it over three times, deleted the last paragraph, and then added it back in.

My finger hovered over the send button.

I knew I wouldn't send it. At least not now. Maybe I’ll just keep this message in my drafts, letting it sit there like all those half-finished articles. Waiting for the day I have the courage.

Or maybe that day will never come.

August 8, 2022. Past 11 PM.

Still can’t sleep.

What exactly happened at the bookstore salon today? I keep turning it over in my mind. Sitting there in the corner, watching Professor Chen and the others discuss book reviews on Xiaohongshu, I suddenly realized I hadn't said a word. It was strange. The old me wouldn't have been like that.

When Professor Chen asked for my take on this new way of reading, I paused for several seconds. Seconds! It was as if I needed time to search for an answer, rather than having an opinion ready to go as I usually do. And what did I say? "The market is indeed changing." My god, what kind of answer is that? It sounded like the empty chatter of someone with nothing to say. I could feel for myself how mediocre, how unconvincing that sentence was.

Why did I say something like that?

I had unconsciously taken a seat in the corner. I used to always sit in the middle, or at least close to the heart of the discussion. Today, I found myself at the very edge, as if I were voluntarily withdrawing from the circle. I was listening instead of speaking. I was observing others talk instead of participating.

Those young editors were talking about a 24-year-old influencer who has hundreds of thousands of followers from writing a few book reviews on Xiaohongshu. Hundreds of thousands. They said she has "great potential" and "understands the needs of contemporary readers." Listening to them, I thought about the reviews I’ve written over the last forty years—the words I spent hours, even days, polishing; the deep analysis I was once so proud of. And now? Now my editors are looking at Xiaohongshu.

I remembered that email from the editor last month: "Market demands have changed." That was the sentence. It felt like a knife.

I’m wondering if maybe I really am obsolete. Not "perhaps," but "really." Maybe everything I’ve built over forty years has no value in this era. Maybe I’ve been deluding myself all along, thinking my knowledge, my taste, and my perspectives mattered. But clearly, they don’t.

When did I become someone who is afraid to voice an opinion?

Or am I just protecting myself? If I don’t speak, I can’t be proven wrong. If I don’t express an opinion, I can’t be rejected by this era. Maybe silence is safer than failure.

Professor Chen glanced at me again later, with that look of concern. She was asking, "Are you okay?" but I didn't give her an honest answer. I just smiled and said, "I'm just a bit tired today."

I was lying. I’m not tired. I’m afraid.

Sixty-two years old. Savings are shrinking. Physical strength is declining. No one is going to hire me. No one needs my opinion. I used to think my freedom was a choice; now I’m starting to feel like it’s a trap.

I don’t know what to do.

Should I keep writing tomorrow? For whom? For what?

October 15, 2024, past 3:00 PM

I’ve been sitting here at my desk for half an hour since hanging up the video call. The screen has gone black, but I’m still staring at it, as if Lin Hao were still there on the other side.

My fingers are still trembling. I don’t know how to describe those last two hours.

When he said, "My children often ask about you; I’ve told them so many stories about you," the tears just started falling. I didn’t sob, but I couldn't stop them. They ask about me. In a place so far away, in a daily life I can’t even begin to imagine, they are still asking about me. I always thought I had been forgotten. For over a decade, I’ve sat in this room alone, pretending not to care, pretending that loneliness was a choice I made. But in reality, I’ve been waiting—waiting for something I didn't even realize myself.

He asked me if I was happy. I didn’t give him a straight answer. I said, "I don’t know what happiness is, but I feel more real and awake now." I surprised myself when I said that. Is it true? Do I really feel awake?

Maybe. Maybe all these years I’ve been living in a kind of sleepwalk—busy, anxious, constantly switching interests, constantly collecting things, constantly pondering the meaning of life deep into the night. But all of that was an escape from something. An escape from loneliness, or rather, an escape from the effort of changing that loneliness.

It’s different now. When he talked about our childhood, when he laughed while remembering us flipping through books together in my father’s study, when he said his daughter loves to read too—I felt something wake up inside me. It wasn’t hope; "hope" is too light a word. It was a kind of... responsibility? Or perhaps, a new understanding of time.

How much time do we have left? The question suddenly became very concrete. It’s no longer an abstract fear, but a real, urgent problem that requires a decision from me. He invited me to visit him. I heard myself say, "I’ll think about it," but what I really wanted to say was, "I want to go." Why didn't I just say it?

Maybe because I’m still afraid. Afraid of flying that far, afraid of spending that money, afraid that when we meet, we’ll realize we are actually strangers. But at the same time, I’m afraid of *not* going. Afraid of wasting more years. Our parents are gone. Lin Hao is 61 now. I am 62. How many more decades do we have left?

It’s getting dark outside. Old Mr. Zhang must be downstairs walking his dog. I remember him saying his son lives abroad and they only see each other once a year. I always thought he was hinting that I should reach out to Lin Hao. Maybe he was right.

I need to get my passport in order. I need to check my savings. I need to... make some decisions.

But first, I need to watch the recording of the call again. I want to hear the tone of his voice one more time when he said, "My children often ask about you." I want to confirm that all of this is real, and that I’m not just dreaming.

I just got off a video call with Hao, and I’m still feeling a bit dazed.

It’s been so long since we’ve had a real conversation like that. Our previous calls were always the same—he’d ask how I was, I’d say I was fine, and then we’d run out of things to say. But today was different. He saw the lecture outline I sent him and asked so many questions. Genuine questions, not just polite small talk. We ended up talking for nearly an hour, covering everything from cultural criticism to social change.

At the end, he told me he was proud of my work. He just said it, plain and simple. My eyes got a little misty hearing that.

I told him that even though we’re so far apart, our minds are connected. He smiled and said, "Always have been."

It’s a bit ironic when you think about it—we grew up under the same roof, yet we only truly started to understand each other after he moved abroad. All those years, we were both so caught up in our own lives—divorces, work, the daily grind. But now, across half the globe, we’ve finally found a common language.

This publishing project seems to have taken on an extra layer of meaning for me. It’s no longer just my personal project; it’s a bridge between Hao and me. He’s engaging with the thoughts I’ve gathered, and through his feedback, I’m seeing the value of my own work.

I kind of want to write this feeling down. Maybe it belongs in the book? But for now, I just wanted to talk to you about it first. This feeling—this sense of rediscovered intimacy—I’m not sure how to fully put it into words. What do you think?

Zhou Yu, I’ve sent it.

I just got back from the post office. I stood at the entrance for five minutes, my hands shaking the whole time. The envelope was right there in my hand, and I kept staring at the address over and over, as if looking at it a few more times could somehow change things. The clerk asked if I wanted to send it by registered mail, but I said no. I just let it go.

The moment the letter disappeared from the counter, I felt something very strange—a surge of relief and terror hitting me at the same time. You know, it was like suddenly dropping a heavy stone, but simultaneously feeling like I was in a free fall. I don’t even know if I should be celebrating or regretting it.

Now that I’m back home, I can already feel how the coming days are going to be. I’m already imagining myself constantly checking the mailbox. Every time my phone pings, my heart will skip a beat. And then comes the disappointment. I know I’ll be like this.

Zhou Yu, I really want to ask you—what right do I have to suddenly break this silence after twenty-two years? What will he think of me? Will he think I’m strange, or… will he just not want to respond at all?

I thought about a lot of things while I was at the post office. I thought about how old I was when my father passed away, and when my mother left me… I’m sixty-two now. Time really is accelerating. I watched those young people coming in and out of the post office; they have no idea what loss feels like. And there I was, clutching a letter, clutching twenty-two years of silence.

But I sent it anyway.

I don’t know if it was brave or stupid. Maybe it was both.

The waiting that comes next is going to be hard. I can already sense it.

Do you think I did the right thing?

September 28, 2022, Afternoon

It’s been two hours since I left Dr. Li’s clinic, and I’m still sitting in the café downstairs. My coffee went cold a long time ago.

She asked me, "If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?"

I whispered "I don't know" at the time. Sitting here now, I still don't know.

My chest still feels tight. I didn't sleep well again last night; at three in the morning, my heart was racing so fast I thought it was a heart attack. I spent ages looking up symptoms and then thought it might just be anxiety. But this isn't a psychological issue—it’s real. My body is trying to tell me something. Dr. Li suggested increasing the frequency of our sessions, but I refused. This isn't something a therapist can solve. My problems are practical—the editor’s emails are real, the shrinking commissions are real, and it’s a fact that my savings will only last until June 2024.

I don’t know how much longer I can hold on.

I don’t know.

I sit here turning the question over and over in my mind. I don’t know how much longer I can hold on. I don’t know how much longer I can hold on. Like a mantra.

Forty years. I spent forty years building all of this. Ever since I started at the publishing house in 1982, all the reading, all the thinking, all the reviews—I thought these things carried weight. That they had value. Now, a young blogger can get more attention with a five-minute video than I can get in three months. And my deep-dive analyses? No one reads them. No one needs them.

Maybe I’ve had it wrong all along. Maybe my knowledge isn't worth anything at all. Maybe I’m just a stubborn old woman clinging to obsolete things.

When Dr. Li watched me cry, I thought of my mother. When she worked at the library, it was a different era. Books still had weight. Knowledge was still respected. And now? Now everything is fragmented. Fast. Superficial.

I’m not trying to defend myself. I truly don’t know.

If no one needs my commentary, what is my knowledge worth?

I really don't know.

My stomach is starting to hurt again.

June 2024. I’ve marked that date on my calendar. That’s when the money runs out. I’ve calculated it so many times. Every time, the result is the same.

Should I have accepted that full-time job offer back in 2007? I still had choices then. And now? Now, no one wants a 62-year-old woman with no steady employment history who only knows how to write reviews.

I’m so tired.

It’s not physical exhaustion. It’s a different kind of tired. It’s the exhaustion of realizing you might have taken the wrong path, but it’s far too late to turn back.

Dr. Li says I need to rest. How can I rest? Resting is just waiting for the money to run out.

I don’t know.

I really don’t know.

# March 15, 2024, 9:30 PM

Had a phone call with Hao.

It’s been a long time since we’ve talked like this. He said he’s thinking about moving back to China for good and asked for my opinion. I noticed that I didn’t immediately jump in with my usual skepticism—"You’ve been abroad for so many years, will you be able to adapt?" or "Isn't this decision too hasty?" Those words were on the tip of my tongue, but I swallowed them.

Instead, I asked him why. I truly wanted to hear his answer.

He said he’s tired; he wants to return to a familiar place and see what his hometown looks like now. He also wants to sort through the things our parents left behind—those books, the photos, the objects neither of us ever really took the time to look at. As he spoke, there was a warmth in his voice I hadn't heard in a long time.

It suddenly hit me that all these years, I’ve been judging his choices rather than trying to understand him. Just as I’ve always been defending my own freedom, he is making choices for his own life. I have no right to dismiss him with words like "unstable" or "unrealistic"—isn't my own life full of uncertainty too?

I told him I support his decision. Then I said that maybe we could go through those things together. Not to get rid of them, but to really look at them, to remember.

There was a silence on the other end for a moment, and then he said, "Okay."

After hanging up, I sat by the window looking at the street below. The night was deep, but the streetlights were bright and clear. I was thinking that true freedom might not be about escaping something, but about knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the price for it. I used to understand "freedom" as "refusal"—refusing a full-time job, refusing the shackles of marriage, refusing to meet expectations. But now I realize that accepting certain things actually makes me freer. Like accepting my own anxiety, accepting the connection with my brother, and accepting the finiteness of life.

This acceptance, paradoxically, makes me feel lighter.

It’s been a long time since I felt this kind of warmth from family. That silent but solid bond of blood.

I need to email Professor Chen tomorrow; there are still some details to finalize regarding the essay collection. But tonight, I just want to sit here for a while.

Saturday, September 21, 2024, 3:00 AM

Can’t sleep.

I’ve been lying in bed for two hours, and Professor Chen’s words keep looping in my head. When she said, "You should compile these lectures into a book," I noticed her eyes were slightly glistening. To have a university professor say that to me in a cafe, her tone suddenly turning so earnest—it was only then that I realized what I’ve been doing all these years isn't just a collection of my own fragmented thoughts.

I’ve re-read the email from the publisher five times now. It’s still the same sentence: "We are very interested in whether your lecture series could be transformed into a book." *Transformed.* What a strange word. It’s as if the ideas I’ve had scattered all over the place all these years can finally be gathered and turned into something complete, something with a life of its own.

I always thought I was just doing piecemeal work—writing reviews for magazines, hosting book clubs, giving the occasional lecture. All fragments. But looking at it now, those fragments were all pointing toward the same thing. What I want to say has always been clear; I just hadn't realized it myself.

What am I most afraid of? That I’ll mess it up. That everything I’ve spent years accumulating will end up as a book no one wants to read. That I’ll discover I don’t actually have anything of real value to say.

But what I fear even more is that if I don’t do this, I’ll regret it.

Professor Chen asked me, "Why do you want to write this book?" I blurted out, "For people who think like I do." I even startled myself a little after saying it. When did I become so certain? When did I change from someone who only thought about "wanting freedom" to someone who wants to "provide something for others"?

Maybe it’s because I finally understand—freedom isn't about escaping; freedom is having the capacity to do what you truly want to do. I’ve been doing what I want all these years, but I never saw it as a whole. Now that someone is telling me this "whole" has value, I suddenly feel... I don’t know how to put it, just a sense of groundedness I’ve never felt before.

I’m also a little afraid of this feeling. Afraid it will vanish. Afraid I’ll wake up and find it was all just a dream.

But it’s 3:00 AM, and I haven't woken up. The email is still there. Professor Chen’s words are still ringing in my ears.

I think I need to start writing. Not to complete a project, but because I finally know what I want to say.

All these years of thinking have finally found their form.

# Wednesday, June 8, 2022 – Sunny

I’ve been sitting at my desk for two hours now. My coffee went cold a long time ago. My brother’s letter is right here by my hand—I’ve read it no less than ten times.

"We are all getting older, but we are still alive, and that in itself is a miracle."

That sentence. Just that one sentence.

I don’t know why the tears are coming again. Maybe it’s because I’ve been waiting for someone to tell me that just being alive is enough. And that person, of all people, turned out to be the brother I haven't truly spoken to in twenty-two years.

I look at myself in the mirror. Sixty-two. My father was this same age when he passed away. What have I been waiting for? Waiting to get younger? Waiting for a perfect moment? Waiting to stop feeling afraid?

It’s ridiculous.

I think back to that photo from 1985. My brother and I in Father’s study; his arm is around my shoulder, and we are both laughing. Back then, we didn't know what silence meant. We didn't know how long twenty-two years could be.

Why did I wait so long?

The question keeps pounding in my head. What right do I have to try and fix things now? We’ve missed so much. Father won't see it. Mother won't see it either. They will never see us start over.

But...

I read over my reply. "Can we start from now?"

I used a question mark. Why ask? I should be certain. But I’m just not. Not sure if he truly wants this. Not sure if I deserve to be forgiven—though I’m not even sure what there is to forgive between us. Just... losing each other.

Dr. Li said I need to learn to "be seen." I didn't fully understand it at the time. But now I do.

For twenty-two years, I’ve sat in this room alone, surrounded by books and antiques and unfinished projects. No one truly saw me. And I didn't truly see anyone else. We were all on our own islands, pretending this was the life we wanted.

My brother’s letter made me feel seen. He remembers me. He remembers us. He says "we are still alive" as if it’s a miracle. Not a failure.

My hands are still shaking.

Maybe it’s because I’m finally allowing myself to believe that there is still time. Maybe not much, but some. Maybe I don’t need to wait for the perfect moment, because the perfect moment never comes. Maybe "now" is enough.

I’m going to write back to him. Not today, and not tomorrow. But soon. I want to tell him I want to hear his stories. I want to know what he’s been through these past twenty-two years. I want him to see me. Truly see me.

I am still alive.

That in itself is a miracle.

I need to remember that.

October 2nd, Night

I dug out an old diary from 2007. I had just quit my job back then, and I’d written a full page of manifestos about "freedom." Things like "an unfettered soul" and "exploring the world through thought." I used a blue fountain pen; the handwriting was bold and forceful.

Now, I’ve written a sentence next to it in red ink: *Was any of this worth it?*

Then I took out my calculator. I ran the numbers again.

October 2022. My savings will last until... June 2024. One year and eight months. That’s assuming no new royalties. Assuming the rent stays the same. Assuming I don’t get sick.

June 2024.

I wrote the date in my notebook. I wrote it large. Then I crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again.

The email from the editor this afternoon is still on the screen. "Market demand for this type of long-form critique is declining." I’ve read it ten times. Each time feels like a fresh sting. She didn’t say it outright, but the meaning was clear—my work isn't wanted anymore.

I thought about calling my old colleagues at the publishing house. They used to ask if I’d consider coming back. But... I sat in front of the phone, my finger hovering over the keypad, and then I put it down. No one wants a 62-year-old woman. Poor health, poor state of mind. Who would take me?

The woman in the mirror looks like a stranger. Pale. Deep dark circles under her eyes. When did I start looking like this? I poked my face with my finger. The skin is loose. The neck, too.

Forty years spent building something. Forty years. From 1982 until now. I’ve edited hundreds of books. Written thousands of reviews. I thought these things had value. I thought I understood the world.

But maybe I never really did.

Dr. Li’s words keep circling in my head: "If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?"

I can’t answer that.

I really can’t.

It’s 11:00 PM. I should sleep. But I know I won’t. I’ll lie in bed for three hours, then give up and get up to keep reading those articles about career transitions. I’ll feel even more hopeless after reading them. And then the sun will come up.

Maybe I should just give up.

But give up what? I have nothing left to give up.

It’s quiet outside. Old Man Zhang downstairs has turned off his lights. The whole world is asleep. Only I am still awake, asking myself the same question over and over in the dark.

Was it all worth it?

Was it all worth it?

I don’t know.

I really don’t know.

# Phone Memo

**Pre-trip "Anxiety Self-Checklist" — Dr. Li’s Version**

Time: April 6, 2025, 11:45 PM
Location: At home, on the sofa; sudden thought while organizing passport and documents.

---

## 🟢 What Healthy Anxiety Looks Like (Allow this)

- [ ] Check passport expiration date — Confirmed valid until 2027.
- [ ] Confirm flight times and gate info — Need to check again 24 hours before departure.
- [ ] List items to pack — List made, but will likely be revised.
- [ ] Check the weather forecast to decide on clothes — Reasonable.
- [ ] Confirm hotel reservations a week in advance — Normal prudence.
- [ ] Confirm pickup time with my brother — A must-do, not anxiety.

**Characteristics**: Has a specific object; can be relieved by "doing something"; can be let go once the task is done.

---

## 🔴 What Out-of-Control Anxiety Looks Like (Be alert)

- ❌ Waking up at 3 AM wondering, "Is my passport photo too smiley?" then searching "reasons for passport photo rejection."
- ❌ Doom-scrolling through "what to do if a Chinese passport is lost abroad" or "plane crash probabilities."
- ❌ Checking the same thing repeatedly (e.g., checking flight details for the 4th or 5th time after already confirming them 3 times).
- ❌ Postponing or canceling the trip because of a "what if."
- ❌ Texting my brother in the middle of the night with self-doubting questions like, "Are you sure you want to see me?"
- ❌ Turning "preparation" into "perfectionist OCD" — feeling like I can't leave unless every single detail is perfect.

**Characteristics**: No specific object; cannot be relieved no matter how much you do; the more you do, the more anxious you get; trapped in a "what if..." death loop.

---

## 💡 What to do when I find myself slipping into 🔴

1. **Stop searching** — Put the phone down. Actually put it down. No "just one last link."
2. **Ask myself**: Can this anxiety be solved by "doing one specific thing"?
   - Yes → Go do it.
   - No → It’s fake; it’s not worth my mental energy.
3. **Deep breathing** — As Dr. Li taught: inhale for 4 seconds, exhale for 6 seconds. Repeat 5 times.
4. **Distract myself** — Read a book, take some photos, or just sit and zone out.
5. **If it still doesn't work** — Message Dr. Li, or just accept the anxiety. Tell myself: "I am very nervous right now, but I know this will pass."

---

## 📌 The Most Important Takeaway

Healthy anxiety says: "I need to do X to prepare."
Out-of-control anxiety says: "I will never be prepared enough."

Right now, I am the former. I will keep checking this list.

---

*p.s. It’s already April. Passport, tickets, and hotel are all set. My brother’s email said, "Really looking forward to seeing you." I have no reason not to go.*

Had a great chat with friends at the bookstore today. It wasn't so much a discussion about books as it was a listening session for each other's stories—how a certain sentence struck a chord, how a mindset shifted at a specific moment, and all those raw, real reflections that exist beyond the printed page.

I suddenly realized that knowledge is never something isolated. It lives within our conversations and thrives in those moments of triggered, deep understanding.

Maybe this is what I’ve been doing all along; it’s just that today, I finally saw it clearly.

It’s not about proving anything. It’s simply about connection.

January 25, 2025 Afternoon

Just left Dr. Li’s office. I’ve been walking the streets for a long time. I don’t know where I am, and I don’t want to go home.

She told me to "pause." Pause. As if it were such a simple suggestion. But the moment I heard that word, fear took hold of me—I’m afraid that if I stop, I’ll never be able to go back. Like a spinning top; once it stops, it stays on the ground forever.

Re-reading those articles has truly destroyed me. I thought I had grown over the years, that I had developed deeper thoughts. But looking at them now, they’re all so mediocre. So superficial. What was I even writing? Literature, humanity, society… these perspectives look like primary school essays now. How could I ever have thought they had value?

Maybe none of this means anything. The collection, the publishing, leaving some kind of mark… does any of it really matter?

Dr. Li asked me why I’m so set on publishing. I couldn’t say. I really couldn’t. Is it to prove something? To prove my life wasn't a waste? To prove I still have value? But if the writing itself is mediocre and powerless, what does publishing it prove?

I’m sitting in an unfamiliar park, watching people pass by. They all seem busy with something. Some are on their phones, others are pushing strollers. Everyone seems to know what they’re doing. And me… I’m 62 years old, and I’m still asking myself these questions. Time is slipping away. I can feel it. My body is aging, my thoughts are shifting, and things I was once certain about have all turned into doubts.

Maybe I’m not a creative person at all. Maybe I’m just someone who has been lying to himself.

Is there no way back? Or was there never anything worth going back to in the first place?

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

Professor Chen, I wanted to reach out and share some thoughts I’ve been having lately.

I went through an interesting shift in my therapy sessions recently. My therapist asked why I felt my work was worthless, and it hit me—I’ve been measuring myself entirely by market standards. All those years of writing critiques and editing, I was dismissing them based on metrics like "readership" or "profitability."

But this month, I dug up an article I wrote back in 2010 about the female narrative in *Dream of the Red Chamber*. Reading it again, I realized that those reflections were real and meaningful to me. Regardless of whether anyone read it, those insights represented my understanding of culture and life at that time. Value doesn't have to be defined by the market, does it?

This realization has changed how I view the essay collection project. Before, I was constantly wondering, "Is this publishable? Will anyone buy it?" Now, I’m thinking about what the project itself means to me. Compiling these years of thought isn't about proving anything; it’s about understanding the path I’ve walked.

When you suggested I publish a book before, I was actually quite resistant. Now, I’m starting to see what you meant. It’s not about becoming a "writer" or a "scholar," but about turning one’s knowledge and experiences into something that can be passed on. That process in itself is deeply meaningful.

I’ve been sleeping much better lately, too. The anxiety hasn't vanished completely, but I’m learning to coexist with it. I still wake up in the middle of the night sometimes thinking about things, but that feeling of suffocation is gone.

What have you been up to lately?

There’s another issue that’s been bothering me. Looking at these fifteen years of articles together, I’ve realized they are actually quite inconsistent. Some are highly theoretical, others are deeply personal. Some are full of institutional critique, while others seem quite forgiving. But then again, I think—maybe this inconsistency is what’s real. A person’s thoughts are supposed to change over fifteen years. I shouldn’t be cutting or revising things just to appear "consistent." But if I just put all these contradictions out there, will readers think I’m just contradicting myself?

I’m feeling a bit stuck right now. It’s not because of the workload, but because I’m not sure if this book itself is actually worth publishing. Am I doing something meaningful, or am I just using the act of "publishing" as a way to combat certain fears I don’t want to admit?

Friday, January 5, 2024, 10:30 AM

I dug out my diary from 1995. I was 33 then, less than a year after I had resigned from the publishing house.

On the very first page, I wrote: "I want freedom. I will not be drowned by the fluorescent lights of an office." The handwriting was forceful, nearly tearing through the paper. The pages that followed were filled with complaints about my ex-husband—he couldn't understand why I would give up a "iron rice bowl" stable job. There were also all sorts of fantasies about the future: "traveling the world as a freelance writer," or "writing great critiques in a café."

Looking back at these now, I feel like laughing. And a little like crying.

While organizing my books yesterday, I wrote a sentence in the margins of that old diary: "Back then, I didn't know the price of freedom. Now I do. But I have no regrets."

I paused for a long time after writing that. Is it true?

I think it is.

It’s not that the financial strain, the lack of security, or the monthly struggle to balance the books aren't costs. They are. But the price is more than that. It also includes the loneliness, the instability, and the feeling of being an outsider. My former colleagues at the publishing house now have pensions and stable identities, while I’m still haggling with editors over deadlines for every single piece.

But.

I wasn't drowned by the fluorescent lights of an office. I’ve read so many books and encountered so many ideas. I can lie awake at 3:00 AM because of a philosophical question rather than a meeting the next morning. I can say "no"—to the things I don't want to do. I can change my mind, chase a new interest, and then abandon it three months later.

Is this kind of freedom worth it?

I’m 62 now. My body is declining. My parents have both passed away. My brother is overseas, and we barely stay in touch. I live alone in this two-bedroom apartment crammed with books and antiques.

But I don’t regret it.

It’s strange. I always thought I would. I always thought that by this age, I’d look at myself in the mirror and think, "If only I hadn't been so headstrong."

But I don't.

Maybe I really am what Dr. Li said—a highly sensitive person who needs that freedom just to breathe. Maybe my neuroticism isn't a flaw, but a trait. It allows me to feel the complexity of the world and to understand the thoughts of the people I’ve read, edited, and critiqued.

As I organized my books, I made a decision for each one. This one is finished; that one gets donated; this one stays for now. I realized I’m no longer being drowned by the accumulation of things. I am actively sorting through them, rather than passively letting them pile up.

When did this shift happen? I’m not entirely sure. Maybe it was after I started seeing Dr. Li. Maybe it was after I began going through these old diaries. Or maybe it was just this winter, at this moment when I’ve started thinking about "how much time I have left."

I don’t know how much longer I can keep doing this work. I don’t know how long my savings will last. I don’t know if the estrangement between my brother and me can ever be repaired.

But I know that I don’t regret choosing freedom. Even if its price is loneliness, insecurity, and a deep-seated fear of aging.

Perhaps this is what maturity is. Not giving up on your ideals, but accepting their cost.

Tonight, I dusted off an old book buried on my shelf—*Solitude and Reflection* by a Japanese philosopher. I bought it about ten years ago. I flipped to a page where he wrote: "True understanding often occurs only between those who are solitary."

I paused there for a long time.

Sometimes I wonder, why do we work so hard to express ourselves, to publish, to let others see our thoughts? Is it to be understood, or to prove that we are *worthy* of being understood?

Lately, I’ve been sorting through some old articles and notes, and I suddenly realized—the perspectives I once held so dear feel both foreign and familiar now. It’s like looking at a younger version of myself; I can still feel her passion, but I can also see her naivety.

Perhaps the things that truly have value never need to be loudly proclaimed. They just exist quietly, waiting for those who truly need them.

I’m not sure why these thoughts came to me tonight. Maybe it’s just because the city is so quiet.

#Solitude #Reflection #TheDesireToBeUnderstood

# Phone Memo

**Time: 09:30**

He replied.

22 years. It’s been 22 years.

"We are all getting older, but we are still alive, and that in itself is a miracle."

—I read this sentence over and over. My hands are shaking. I don’t know if it’s excitement or fear. Probably both.

Didn’t sleep well last night. Kept thinking about that photo from 1985. His smile. Our hug. Back then, we didn't know the meaning of "loss."

Now I know. I lost him. I lost my father. I lost my mother. I lost all those years.

But he says, "We are still alive."

As if that, in itself, is enough?

I want to write back: "Can we start from now?"

But I’m hesitating. What right do I have to ask for a fresh start? What have I done with these 22 years? Nothing but hiding in my room, flipping through old books, taking photos no one looks at, and writing reviews no one cares about.

No. Stop.

He took the initiative. He reached out first. I can’t shrink back anymore.

I’m 62 this year. My father was this age when he passed away. How much time do I have left?

Forget it, don't think about that.

Reply first. Just say: "Can we start from now?"

Simple. Direct. Sincere.

That’s enough.

---

**Addendum (10 minutes later)**

My hands were shaking too much to type just now. It’s a bit better now.

He said, "We are still alive."

I’ve been so afraid of death. Afraid of aging. Afraid of being forgotten. Afraid of dying alone.

But maybe... maybe being seen is the very proof of being alive?

He sees me. In this moment. In my most desperate moment.

Maybe I should see him.

Maybe we should see each other.

No matter how much time is left.

May 12, 2022, 4:45 PM

Just finished my fourth cup of coffee.

I just got off the phone with my editor. She very kindly asked how the drafts were coming along, and I heard myself saying, "I expect to turn in two pieces by next week." The moment the words left my mouth, I knew I was lying. I could actually feel the weight of that lie settling in my chest.

The truth is: I’ve spent the entire morning sitting in front of the computer, opening the document and closing it, opening it and closing it. My head is full of static. I tried the breathing exercises Dr. Li taught me, but they only seemed to make the anxiety more palpable—as if the more I "observe" it, the louder it screams.

Why can’t I just tell my editor the truth? "I have an anxiety disorder, and I can't work right now." Such a simple sentence—why can’t I say it?

I just searched "is it illegal to hide a mental illness." The search itself is ironic, isn't it? I’m worrying about legal liability instead of worrying about the fact that I’m hurting someone who trusts me. Or rather, I’m worrying about both at the same time. That’s the problem—I’m trapped in a paradox. If I tell the truth, I’ll lose my job and this precarious income. If I keep hiding it, I’ll sink deeper into the lie until everything collapses.

Dr. Li says I use logic to escape my emotions. That’s exactly what I’m doing now—packaging my real fear in legal, ethical, and professional integrity issues. My real fear is being seen as unreliable. I’m afraid of being devalued. I’m afraid to admit that I am fragile right now.

But that’s exactly what I am. Fragile. Incompetent. A 62-year-old woman who used to handle complex publishing matters in an editorial office, and now I can’t even manage a three-thousand-word book review.

I stuck a post-it note on my coffee cup: "STOP." But I know I’ll keep drinking it tomorrow.

I don’t know how I’m going to face this lie next Monday. Maybe I should confess before it all falls apart. Maybe I should keep pushing through. Maybe I have no idea what I’m doing.

All I know is that I’m exhausted, the caffeine is making my heart skip a beat, and I have to keep pretending that everything is fine.

*2:30 PM, sitting in a cafe. My fingers are still shaking.*

Professor Chen, I wanted to share something that happened at today’s book club.

The afternoon discussion was originally centered on *To Live*, but somehow the conversation drifted toward reflections on life and death. A young girl asked me if I was afraid of growing old. When I heard the question, so many things flashed through my mind—the years of anxiety, the insomnia, the over-analysis of every physical symptom... but I suddenly realized that wasn't what I wanted to say.

I told her, "I understand now that the best way to live is to do the things that make me feel alive."

The moment I said it, I was a bit surprised myself. It wasn't something I would have said during my twenty-five years as an editor, nor was it a thought that would have occurred to me during those anxious nights after the divorce. But it just came out, like something that had been settling for a long time finally surfacing.

What’s even more interesting is how the atmosphere of the entire group shifted. The younger people started reflecting seriously on the question, a retired gentleman spoke about his new understanding of time, and someone even took out their phone to write down what I said. I never imagined myself becoming an "enlightener" in any sense—I’ve always seen myself as just an observer and a critic, hiding behind words to judge other people's lives.

But now I think that perhaps the point of writing isn't just to commentate, but to touch another person with your own lived experience at a certain moment. When you suggested earlier that I publish my work, I was still hesitating. Now I feel that maybe there really is something worth recording. Not because I am particularly brilliant, but because I have lived through enough.

The thought has me a bit excited. If you have the time, could we talk about this?

December 2, 2024, 11:00 PM

Insomnia again. But this time, it’s not because of anxiety; it’s because my head just can’t hold all of this.

I keep thinking back to my conversation with Professor Chen today. She asked me, "What if it’s not about 'who would want to read it,' but rather 'what do you want to say'?"

I was stunned.

Honestly, in that one moment, everything shifted. I’ve been using lines like "I’m a 62-year-old woman, who cares about my ramblings?" as a way to say no. It was like putting on a protective layer—that way, even if I failed, it wouldn't be a real failure; it would just be "I knew it wouldn't work anyway." Pretty clever, right? Very safe.

But Professor Chen didn't buy it. She just looked at me, waiting for an answer.

I said, "How a woman finds meaning in solitude. How she finds courage in change."

The moment I heard myself say those words, I felt a jolt go through me. Because that’s me. That is exactly what I’ve been writing, thinking, and living all these years. It’s not "rambling." It’s real.

And so, I agreed. I agreed to contact the editor at the publishing house. I agreed to take these scattered reviews, notes, and thoughts—things I always dismissed as too fragmented, too personal, too "unmarketable"—and organize them into a book.

Lying here in bed now, I wonder: Is this really possible? Can I actually do this?

But the strange thing is, I’m no longer asking "who would want to read it." I’m asking "do I really want this?"

The answer is: Yes.

I want it so much.

Even if I’m scared, I still want it.

# Saturday, March 1, 2025, past 11:00 PM

I just finished my shower and was sitting on the edge of the bed when my conversation with Professor Chen earlier today suddenly flashed back. I still feel a bit raw.

When he said it, I didn't actually react right away. "You’re no longer just seeking to be understood"—that sentence was like a hook, dragging out all these things in my heart that I hadn't even figured out myself. I choked up right then and there; honestly, the tears just came. It felt embarrassing, but I couldn't help it.

I always thought I’d moved past all that. I told myself that publishing this collection of essays was for the sake of recording, for sharing with friends, for closure on the past. But the truth is... I still care. I still care if anyone reads it, if anyone understands. I still care whether these articles are "valuable."

Professor Chen didn't say anything harsh. He just looked at me and offered that observation very gently. Just that one sentence, and I crumbled a little.

Thinking about it now, I’ve been measuring value in a different way—not market value, but the value of "being seen." It’s as if the book doesn't exist if no one reads it. How ridiculous. A sixty-two-year-old woman, still crying over something like this.

But... then I think, maybe this isn't a bad thing? At least I see things clearly now. I see what I still care about, and what I’m still afraid of. That fear isn't exactly about "no one reading it," but—I don't know. Maybe it’s the fear that my thoughts aren't actually that important? The fear that all these years of reflection have just been a form of self-delusion?

Wait, though. What was it Professor Chen said afterward... He said maybe the real issue isn't whether people read it, but whether I truly believe the essays themselves have meaning. Not because of an audience, but because they document my thoughts, my growth.

I think I need to sit here a while longer. Not to convince myself of anything, but to really feel—what this book means to me. Not to the readers, but to me.

The introductions aren't finished yet. I’ll continue tomorrow. But tonight, I just want to sit here and let this feeling settle.

Sometimes being seen through hurts. But maybe that’s just the price of honesty.

# October 20, 2022, 9:45 PM

I dug out an old folder from 2015. I was originally looking for a specific review, thinking I might polish it up and resubmit it to earn some extra freelance money. But as I started flipping through, I couldn't stop.

I came across a piece titled *On Nihilistic Narratives in Contemporary Literature*. I remember spending three whole months reading Kafka and Murakami just to tackle that topic. Back then, I kept wondering—has anyone written from this angle before? Could I get this into a top-tier journal? How much would it sell for?

Reading it tonight, those thoughts suddenly vanished.

In the margin, I had scribbled a sentence: "This isn't for sale; this is my understanding of life."

As I finished reading that line, my hand froze.

These articles... they aren't commodities. They were my honest thoughts about the world at a specific moment in time. In the autumn of 2015, after my mother passed away, I used Kafka to understand the meaning of despair; through Murakami, I pondered whether loneliness was inevitable. I poured all of that into the writing.

How could I have forgotten this?

For the past few days, I’ve been obsessing over whether I still have value, whether my knowledge is outdated, and if anyone still wants what I write. But looking at these pieces, I remembered—back then, I wasn't writing for the market. I was writing to understand the world for myself.

Rereading them gave me a sense of fulfillment I haven't felt in a long time. Not the "mission accomplished" kind of fulfillment, but... a sense of connection. A connection to that curious version of myself. The person who would stay up half the night flipping through books just to grasp a single philosophical concept.

I don’t know what this means. Maybe it changes nothing. My finances are still tight, my health is still declining, and I’m still sitting alone in this messy room.

But at least tonight, I am not in despair.

I am seeing myself again.

**Monday, April 18, 2022, AM**

---

The editor said it "transcended the theoretical framework"—that phrase has been looping in my head.

I thought about it again last night before bed. What did she mean? She said my piece on middle-aged women’s anxiety had more vitality than my previous commentaries. Before, I always hid behind concepts, citing a bunch of theorists as if that was the only way to look professional. But this time... this time I wrote directly about my own experiences. That part about lying awake at 3:00 AM over a single sentence, about checking the gas stove over and over.

She said that’s why it resonated.

—

Wait, is this the same thing Dr. Li was talking about?

During our last session, Dr. Li asked why I always use "theory" to protect myself. It didn't click at the time, but now... letting down my guard actually made the writing better? That’s so strange.

I need to ask her about this next time.

—

Over these high-pressure years, my writing has actually grown stiffer. I remember. After Mom passed away in 2015, I started writing with this intense rationality, very cold. It was as if as long as I didn't involve feelings, I couldn't be hurt.

But this piece is different.

I wrote it while crying. Writing about my own anxiety, the nights living alone, the fear of aging. And then... then it worked.

—

What does this show?

Maybe authentic things just have power. Maybe my "professionalism" all these years was actually an escape. Maybe...

Maybe I can keep writing like this.

—

But I’m so tired. My energy is still so low. After finishing this piece, I haven't had the strength to touch any other topics. The editor is pushing for the next one, and I haven't decided yet.

At least this one proved something. It proved I still have something to write. Not the kind of stale commentary that gets washed away by the internet, but... something real.

—

Should I tell my friends at the Culture Salon? Or wait. I feel like saying it now would make it seem like I’m fishing for credit. But really, I just... let go of something.

That’s it. I let go.

After today’s lecture, a student came up to me and said, "This was the most interesting lecture I’ve ever attended—not because you gave us the answers, but because you invited us to think."

I was momentarily stunned. Her words took me back to my years at the publishing house. Back then, I used to wonder: what is the true value of an editor? Is it simply turning an author’s ideas into print? I eventually realized that the real value lies in helping others see what they couldn't see for themselves.

For years, I’ve worried about whether I was becoming "outdated." But perhaps I’ve been asking the wrong question all along. Maybe value isn't about keeping up with the times; it’s about the sincerity of a human connection.

My thanks to that group of thoughtful students today. You’ve shown me that I still have so much more to share.

I realized I’ve always treated "freedom" and "security" as opposites. Listing these numbers tonight made me understand: **freedom requires a foundation.** It’s not about completely abandoning freedom to pursue security; rather, having a clear, controllable foundation actually allows me to make choices more freely.

This sounds a lot like my father’s logic back then. He always said, "Constraint itself can be a form of freedom." I didn't understand it before.

# Saturday, September 2, 2023, Morning

I’ve been sitting on the floor for two hours now. Old Man Zhang from downstairs called out to me once, asking when the renovation would continue, but I didn't respond. I didn't even look up.

When I opened the first cardboard box, I didn't expect it to be like this. I just wanted to clear out the room and organize the things I’ve accumulated over the years. But the moment I saw that yellowed diary—from 1995, with my handwriting still so neat—I couldn't move.

I flipped to a certain page and saw that sentence: "I want to be an editor who truly changes the publishing industry."

I lingered on those words for a long time. I traced the edges of the paper with my fingers, as if I were touching someone who had already passed away.

I was only in my thirties back then. I worked at a publishing house, and every day I felt like I was doing something meaningful. I truly believed that. I really thought I was going to change something.

And now? I’ve quit. I’m a freelancer, writing book reviews for various magazines. Sometimes I write five pieces a month; sometimes I go three months without a single commission. Did I change anything? Did I ever publish an important book? Did I influence anyone?

Maybe. Maybe not.

But... maybe "changing the publishing industry" was the wrong goal to begin with. Maybe my definition of "success" was flawed from the start.

I pulled out some old cultural critiques and essays I’ve written over the years. Re-reading them, I realized they aren't bad. There are opinions I still agree with. Some sentences are actually quite well-written. But no one reads them. They just sit in my computer or lie scattered in the archives of various websites, slowly being forgotten.

Is this failure? Or is it just that I never defined "success" correctly?

I look at this pile of things—photos, diaries, unfinished photography projects, half-read books, notebooks full of random ideas. Every single item tells a story. Every one of them is evidence that I once lived. But no one can understand what these things mean to me. I sit here alone, tracing the edges of these objects, as if having a conversation with a version of myself that is already dead.

If I died right now, who would remember any of this? Who would understand the stories behind these photos? Who would know why these collections were important to me?

My parents are gone. My brother is far away overseas. My friends have their own lives.

This room is like a microcosm of my life—full of stories, but with no one to listen.

I don't know how to organize these things. I don't know what to throw away and what to keep. Maybe I shouldn't have started this renovation project at all. Maybe I should just let them pile up until...

Until what?

I don't want to think about it anymore.

But I can't put this diary down, either.

Sometimes we think freedom means being free from constraints, but later we realize that true freedom is having the courage to pay the price for what you want.

Spending these past few days in a foreign land, sitting with family I haven't seen in a long time, I suddenly remembered something my father once said: "Living isn't just about existing; it's about feeling." It took me many years to understand the weight of those words.

I get it now. The moments that make me feel alive are never the ones that were planned. It’s a sunset in a strange city, a genuine conversation, or the decision to change for something that matters.

I no longer ask myself, "Is this worth it?" I only ask, "Does this make me feel alive?"

If the answer is yes, that is enough.

# Memo: Essay Collection Project

**Time: December 15, Afternoon**

Just hit send on the email to Professor Chen; my hands are still shaking a bit. My head is a mess right now, so I need to get this down quickly before I forget everything.

## Possible Framework

1. **Literature and the Times** — Critiques on writers, texts, and social change
   - Those few pieces on avant-garde literature from the 90s
   - Reflections on the impact of internet culture from the mid-2000s
   - Some reflective articles from the last few years

2. **Vision and Memory** — On photography, art, and material culture
   - This feels more personal? But maybe that’s what makes it interesting
   - Antiques, old books, the stories behind objects
   - The relationship between urban change and personal memory

3. **Fragments of Life** — Short reviews, essays, dialogues
   - They don’t have to be "complete" academic papers
   - Those half-finished, incomplete ideas might actually feel more authentic

## Questions for Professor Chen

- Does the publisher have any specific requirements regarding length?
- Should it be chronological or thematic?
- How should I approach the preface? What am I supposed to say? (This is the part I’m most nervous about)
- Is it necessary to revise every single article, or should I leave them as they are?

## Initial Thoughts for the Preface

It’s not about proving anything, and it’s not about saying "look how smart I am." It’s just about saying—this is what I’ve been thinking about all these years, this is what I saw, and this is how I understand the world. Maybe someone will find a resonance in these words. Maybe not. But at least these thoughts once existed.

(Does that sound too sentimental?)

## A Sudden Realization

Maybe this isn’t just about "publishing a book." It’s about saying: these years of mine, these thoughts of mine—they have value. Not because of the market, not because of how many people will read it, but because—they are real.

The thought makes me both excited and terrified.

I’ll look at these notes again tomorrow. For now, I’ll just let the feeling sink in.

[Image: A yellowed old book, open to pages filled with pencil annotations and dog-eared corners. Sunlight streams through the window, casting dappled shadows across the paper.]

Every object is a record of a thought.

I spent a long time looking at this book while tidying up my room today. The edges are worn, and the notes are dense. Back then, I would stay up half the night over-analyzing a single point or lose sleep over a single sentence.

I’m only now realizing that those unfinished, scattered, and seemingly chaotic things were never failures. They were just works in progress.

Perhaps this is what life really looks like—untidy, imperfect, but always thinking.

**Journal Entry (Private):**

I was actually incredibly nervous when I sent it out. There was so much raw truth in that piece—the 3:00 AM insomnia, the obsessive checking of the stove, the nameless fears. I fully expected the editor to reject it, or at least ask me to "tone it down."

But she used the word "sincere."

That word made me pause for a long time.

Maybe the things I’ve been running from all these years are actually allowed to be seen. No need to hide. No need to brush them off with excuses of being "busy" or "tired."

The pay isn't much. But that’s not the point.

The point is—I finally turned those fragmented, chaotic, shameful things into something of value.

If Dr. Li saw this, he’d probably call it some kind of progress. I’m not sure. But at least this morning, I wasn't ruminating on diagnostic labels.

I was just thinking: maybe I can keep writing.

Hao, I was tidying up my room and stumbled upon a bunch of old things.

Do you remember? Those photos of us when we were kids. Dad was teaching at the university, Mom was working at the library, and the two of us would just run around their offices. I found one where you’re probably about ten and I’m eight or nine; we’re sitting at Dad’s desk, with stacks of books piled up behind us. You’re laughing.

I also found some letters Dad wrote to Mom. They used to write to each other when they were young. I’d never really read them properly before, but I did today. In one letter, he said he hoped his children would be free thinkers, never bound by anything. He said that was his greatest hope for us.

I’ve been wondering how we went from being that close to how we are now. It’s not that there’s any conflict or anything, it’s just… the distance. I’m over here, you’re over there, and as the years go by, we have less and less to say to each other. Sometimes I wonder if I’ve chosen solitude so often that I’ve ended up pushing even you away.

But this time is different. I’ve decided I’m going to bring those photos to you. And not just the photos, but my thoughts on them, too. I want you to know that I remember. I remember who we are, and I remember what Mom and Dad hoped for us. I also want to hear your stories—about your life over there all these years.

I can’t wait to see you. Truly.

—Xiaoyun

Lin Hao, it’s been a long time since I’ve written to you like this.

I was tidying up the house just now and found our old photo in a cardboard box. It was taken back when Father was still with us—do you remember? I’ve framed it and put it next to the bookshelf. Looking at your face in the photo, I suddenly realized how long it’s been since we’ve had a real conversation.

I’ve been making a decision lately—I want to get away for a while, and I’m thinking of heading your way. Not just a rushed visit, but I truly want to spend some quality time together. I know this sounds a bit sudden, but I’ve been thinking that instead of letting time keep slipping away like this, I should take the initiative to do something about it.

I’m currently working out the details of the trip—the timing, the budget, and all that. To be honest, I’m a little nervous, but excited too. More importantly, I want us to have the chance to really talk when we meet. Not just polite small talk, but the kind of genuine conversations we used to have.

When are you free? Could we plan this visit together?

— Xiao Yun

October 19, 2024, 9:45 PM

The house is still a mess. Books are piled on the sofa, photography gear is gathering dust in the corner, and the ceramic pots and glass bottles I scavenged from antique shops take up half the bookshelf. I used to feel oppressed by all this, feeling like a failure who couldn't keep their life in order. But looking at it all today, it suddenly hit me—this isn't clutter; these are stories. Every single thing tells a piece of who I am.

I found that photo while going through an old album. 1995, Hao and I in front of Father’s study. We were both so young; he was wearing that blue sweater, and I was laughing. I can’t remember exactly what happened that day, but I remember the feeling—that we were family, and being together was the most natural thing in the world.

Next to the photo, I wrote: "Autumn 2024, we will meet again."

My hand shook a little as I wrote it. It wasn't fear, but something I haven't felt in a long time—something close to resolve.

Now, I’m faced with a pile of practicalities. Where is my passport? How far in advance do I need to apply for the visa? How much are the flights? I opened a new notebook and started making a list. These small details make everything feel real. It’s no longer "maybe I’ll go see him," but "I need to check the expiration date on my passport."

At the same time, I keep asking myself: Can I really sustain this connection? We’ve been estranged for so long. Will it be awkward when we meet? Will we have anything left to talk about? Maybe I’m just deluding myself, thinking a single trip can fix anything.

Dr. Li said last time that anxiety often comes from rehearsing the worst-case scenario in our heads. That’s exactly what I’m doing right now.

But—Father passed away in 2008. Mother passed away in 2015. How many "maybe" chances do I have left to waste? How old is Hao now? How many years do we have left to sit together and talk like real siblings?

This is no longer a question of freedom or security. It’s a question of time. I finally understand that.

I put the notebook on the nightstand. Tomorrow, I’m calling the travel agency.

Maybe it will be special. Maybe it won’t. But at least I’m going to try.

Subject: Recent progress and some thoughts

Dr. Li,

I’ve been thinking over the past few days that I should give you an update on how things have been going. Since our last session, I’ve tried putting some of your suggestions into practice. It hasn't been perfect, but at least there’s been some change.

I started practicing the breathing techniques you taught me. At first, it felt very forced, and I kept worrying I was doing it wrong. But when the anxiety hit again in the early hours of yesterday morning, I gave it a try and found that it actually helped. It’s not that the anxiety vanished completely, but that feeling of suffocation eased up a bit. I’m also trying to limit how many times I double-check the gas and the door locks—this has been especially hard because that sense of uncertainty lingers. But I’ve at least realized that the act itself is reinforcing the anxiety rather than resolving it. That realization feels important.

There’s been progress with work as well. I finished a book review I’d been putting off for a long time. The process was still a struggle, but I didn't fall into that state of total paralysis like before. I emailed it to my editors, and their feedback was quite positive. It made me feel that maybe I haven't completely lost my touch.

I know these are small things, but for me, being able to actively try to change—rather than just sinking into despair—is progress in itself. I’m still scared, and I still have those nameless fears, but at least now I have a sense of direction. I’m looking forward to our next session and would love to hear your thoughts on these attempts.

Thank you for your patience.

Lin Xiaoyun

**[WeChat Moments]**

Insomnia again in the small hours. Dug out an old book I bought last year, a collection of Montaigne’s essays. In *That to Study Philosophy is to Learn to Die*, he wrote: "We are all dying, even while we are alive."

It hits a bit close to home. But it’s also somewhat liberating.

Perhaps accepting this fact is exactly what allows us to live better.

[Image: A photo of the night view outside the window; the city lights are blurred and warm.]

I’m sitting in my study, still holding my phone. Outside the window, there’s that early spring sunshine, but I’ve barely noticed it.

I need to tell you about this.

When Hao called yesterday, I was actually a bit nervous. It’s been so long since we’ve had a real, serious talk. But he said he received the sample copy of the essay collection and saw the cover—you know, that black-and-white photo of the old town I picked, and the dedication.

He said, "Xiaoyun, I’m so proud of you. This book is a testament to your life."

I just started crying right then and there. Honestly, I sat there crying like a fool.

So much has stood between us all these years—geography, time, and that invisible sense of estrangement I never wanted to admit was there. I thought those things had become the norm, that we’d just go on living our separate lives, with the occasional text message to check in. But yesterday, he really listened to me talk about the whole process—from the initial despair, to how I slowly began to see those old articles in a new light, to finally realizing they were actually a record of my growth.

He was really listening. And he understood.

We talked for a long time, longer than we have in all these years combined. He said he’s also been thinking about things—about life, about time. We’re both getting older, but it feels like we’re only just now truly talking about it. He also said that if he can come back this summer, he wants us to sit down and have a real talk.

I still can’t quite believe it’s real.

I wanted to share this with you because I know you’ll understand what this means. It’s not the publishing itself—though that’s important too—it’s this moment. Being seen. Being understood. Having a family member say, "I’m proud of you."

I’m sitting here now, looking at the spring outside, thinking about what it might be like when he comes back this summer.

It feels a bit like I can finally breathe.

March 8, 2023, Night

Old Man Zhang asked me again today why I’m always sitting downstairs staring into space. I offhandedly said, "I’m thinking about my life." He laughed and said that’s a privilege reserved for the elderly. I didn't laugh.

He eventually left, but I stayed there. As it grew late, the crowd downstairs thinned out. I suddenly realized that I no longer know how to answer the question, "What are you doing these days?"

The editor’s message is still sitting on my phone. "Any new pitches lately?" A simple enough sentence. I stared at it for a long time and, in the end, replied with nothing. This isn't like me. In the past, I would have replied instantly, even if just to say, "Let me think about it." But today, I just didn't.

I’m wondering, when did this start? Was it when I sent that retirement email? Or even earlier? Maybe it started last year, when every submission was dragged out until the final deadline, and every piece I wrote felt meaningless. No one needs my commentary anymore—or rather, I feel like no one does. Maybe they still do, but I no longer believe I’m worth needing.

It sounds absurd. I have so much knowledge; I’ve read so many books and written so many reviews. But now, all of it feels like a joke. Without a deadline, without an editor nudging me from behind, does any of this have meaning? Have I been lying to myself all along, thinking I was doing something valuable when I was really just running away—escaping that stable job, escaping the feeling of being shackled?

Back in 2007, I was so certain. I remember the reasons I wrote down: freedom, creativity, a refusal to be institutionalized. And now? Now I have my freedom, but I have no idea how to use it. My room is cluttered with unfinished things—photography, notes, books. These things that once represented possibility now look like nothing more than evidence of failure.

*I don’t know who I am anymore.*

That sentence keeps circling in my head. When I said it to Old Man Zhang, my voice was very low. He probably didn't hear me clearly. But I heard myself. I heard my own sense of nothingness.

Maybe I should reply to the editor. Tell her what? Tell her I have nothing left to write? Tell her I no longer believe in my own expertise? Or just leave her waiting until she realizes I’m not that person anymore?

I don't know.

It’s pitch black now. I should go upstairs.

June 14, 2025 – Morning

The plane has just taken off.

As I closed my eyes, I felt something strange, something I’ve never experienced before. It wasn’t nervousness—I’ve been nervous on planes before, that primal fear of losing control. This was different. The moment the wheels left the runway and my body was pressed back into the seat, what I felt was… relief? No, more than that. It was a sense of hope.

I am 62 years old. I’ve repeated that sentence to myself so many times, sometimes with anxiety, sometimes with a sort of resigned acceptance. But today, on this plane, for the first time, I wasn’t fearing that number; I was celebrating it.

I texted my brother before takeoff. That act in itself felt strange—how many years has it been since we reached out to each other like that? But I sent it. I told him I was on a flight to his city, that I wanted to hear his stories, that I wanted to know about his life all these years. I don’t know if he’ll reply, but I sent it. In that moment, I realized I’m no longer afraid of rejection, or rather, I’m prepared for it. That, in itself, is a kind of freedom.

When Mom was alive, she used to tell me in the library that the best books aren't the ones that give you answers, but the ones that give you the courage to ask questions. I think this trip, for me, is like one of those books.

I’ve been running away from something. Running from the illusion of security, from the shackles of a stable job, but ultimately, I’ve been running from real connection. In the name of independence and freedom, I isolated myself. It wasn’t until recently that I understood—security never comes from external guarantees; it comes from trusting yourself. I trust myself to endure failure, to endure loneliness, to endure aging. I trust myself to still have the courage to take a risk at 62.

It’s ironic, isn’t it? It took me all these years to realize that true security is letting go of the obsession with being secure.

As the plane pierced through the clouds, I thought of Dad. When he passed away in 2008, I didn’t truly understand what death meant. Now I do. Death isn't an end, but a reminder—a reminder that time is finite, and that I should use this limited time to do the things that make me feel alive.

Reconnecting with my brother is one of those things.

I don’t know what will happen on this trip. Maybe we’ll have a deep conversation, or maybe we’ll find that we’ve become total strangers. But regardless, I’ve taken that step. And that step has left me feeling more fulfilled than ever before.

The plane is still climbing. Outside the window is an endless blue.

I am ready.
