# Diary

November 5, 2023, 4:00 PM

The letter is sent.

I stood by the mailbox for a long time, clutching the envelope, asking myself over and over: "Will he respond?" "Is this too sudden?" "Will he understand?" The envelope grew damp in my palm. Finally, I let go, hearing that soft thud as it dropped inside.

On the walk home, my steps felt inexplicably lighter, though my face must have still been clouded with uncertainty.

It’s strange. I’ve spent so many years getting used to solitude, accustomed to sitting in this room alone, conversing with these piles of belongings. And now, by doing something seemingly simple—sending a letter to my brother—I feel something different. It’s not hope. It’s something perhaps more fragile than hope.

I wonder if waiting itself is a form of courage. Not the grand, heroic kind, but a tiny, almost imperceptible courage. Like reaching out a hand in the dark, not knowing if anyone will take it, but reaching out anyway.

For years, I’ve been running away. Escaping the constraints of work, the failure of my marriage, the fear of growing old. I told myself this was freedom. But sitting in this room, surrounded by unfinished projects, half-read books, and unorganized clutter, I’m starting to suspect that my "freedom" was just another form of evasion. I was avoiding connection.

But today, I didn’t hide. I wrote that letter. I said things I haven't said in years—about our shared childhood, about our parents, about how much I miss him. I don’t know what he’ll think. Maybe he’ll find me too sentimental, or feel that it’s all too late. Maybe he won’t respond at all.

But at least I tried.

I’m sitting at my desk now with a cup of tea that has gone cold. Outside the window, the light is fading. I remember my mother once saying, "Connection isn't about getting something; it's about confirming that you aren't alone." I never truly understood those words. Now, I think I’m beginning to.

Perhaps the waiting itself is enough. Perhaps the act itself is a form of redemption.

I don’t know what happens next. But at least today, I made a different choice.

Just back from the launch event, and it all still feels a bit surreal.

The room was packed. Professor Chen was there with a few of his students; friends from the cultural salon showed up too. My brother, though overseas, sent a long voice message—my eyes welled up a bit listening to it during the event. The biggest surprise, though, was seeing Dr. Li. She was sitting in the back row. When I caught her eye during my speech, I suddenly remembered all those late-night conversations over the past year—the fears and anxieties I couldn't put into words, all slowly dissolved by her patient listening.

During the speech, I said: "This book isn't meant to prove anything; it’s meant to record something." I was a bit nervous saying it, but once the words were out, I actually felt a sense of relief. Because that’s the truth—these articles, these thoughts, they are simply who I was at different points in time. Some views might seem naive now, and some conclusions might need revising, but that’s okay. They are all me.

The most touching moment was the Q&A. A young reader said that while reading one of my pieces, she suddenly realized why she was so anxious—not because she wasn't doing well enough, but because she had been living for others. She said the book made her rethink her life. In that moment, I truly understood that I no longer need to worry about "whether anyone understands." I just need to speak my truth and listen to what others have to say.

Now, sitting on my sofa, I’m scrolling through photos from the launch. There’s one of me with Professor Chen, and another of my friends sitting in a circle, deep in discussion. These are the evidence—proof that I am not walking this path alone.

Thank you to everyone who came. Thank you, Professor Chen, for your encouragement over the past year; thank you to my brother for being there despite the distance; thank you, Dr. Li, for helping me see the things I couldn't see; and thank you to my friends for your company.

There is still so much work to do—typesetting, proofreading, final details. But for now, I just want to sit quietly for a while and let this moment sink in.

Sometimes I wonder what the "me" from ten years ago would think of today. Back then, I was still anxious about freedom and rejecting any work that was "imperfect." Now, I finally understand that perfection was never the goal. Living itself, and the act of recording it, is enough.

Hi Lin Hao,

I was cleaning out my room and came across some old photos—pictures of us when we were kids. Do you remember them? There’s that one of us in front of Father’s study, where I’m wearing that red sweater.

I sat there looking at it for a long time.

We haven't really talked in years. I know it’s not your fault, and it’s not entirely mine either. It’s just that... time and distance have turned us into strangers. I’ve been avoiding that reality, but seeing those photos today, I finally had to admit it—I miss you. Not in some vague way, but I truly want to know how you are. How is work? How is your family? What is your daily life like now?

I realized that everything I know about you is stuck in the version of you from years ago.

I didn't know how to break the silence. I’ve picked up the phone several times only to put it back down. So, I decided to send you this message first. No expectations, no pressure. I just wanted to tell you that I still remember how close we were, and I’d like to get to know the person you are today.

If you’re open to it, maybe we could talk on the phone every now and then. It doesn’t have to be often, just... to maintain a real connection. Maybe one day I could even come visit you all.

I know this might feel a bit sudden. But I feel like there’s no point in waiting any longer.

Lin Xiaoyun

# Therapy Appointment

**Date:** Sunday, February 27, 2022

**Location:** Counseling Clinic (No. 1288 West Nanjing Road)

## Notes

First session. Registration fee paid.

Do not be late. No excuses for cancellation.

Hao, are you there?

I wanted to tell you something. Something unexpected happened today.

You know I’ve been rushing that book review lately; I kept dragging my feet and only turned it in two days before the deadline. I was fully prepared to get chewed out by the editor—I’d even rehearsed my apology. But this time, I decided to stop making excuses. I just told her straight up: my procrastination got the better of me again, it’s not the first time, and I’m sorry.

To my surprise, she wrote back saying she understood. She said she’s the same way, and that sometimes you just need that pressure to produce good work. Then she told me the piece was excellent and that it was worth the wait.

I just stared at her message for the longest time, unable to process it.

Do you remember how Dad always used to say that honesty is the most basic thing? I think I forgot that somewhere along the way. These past few years, I’ve felt like I had to hide my flaws, terrified that people would look down on me. But this little incident today reminded me—sometimes being upfront actually earns you understanding instead of criticism.

I’m not sure why I thought of you. Maybe it’s because you’re so far away, and it’s been a while since we’ve really talked like this. I just wanted to tell you that maybe I haven’t completely messed everything up. Maybe things aren't always as bad as I imagine them to be.

How have you been lately?

## Notes

This isn't a perfect plan. Money is still tight, and work is still unstable. But at least this time, I know what I'm doing and why I'm doing it.

It’s not about proving anything to anyone. It’s just... these things matter to me. The essay collection is a record of my thoughts over the years. Photography allows me to see things differently. Conversations with friends make me feel alive.

Let’s start December like this. One step at a time.

Professor Chen, hello. I’ve been wanting to talk to you about something lately, but I wasn't quite sure how to bring it up.

I’ve started seeing a therapist recently and was diagnosed with an anxiety disorder. It still feels a bit strange to say it out loud—as if I’ve slapped a label on myself. But after thinking it over these past few days, I actually feel a sense of relief.

You know, I’ve always felt that I’m too sensitive, that I overthink things too much, so for a long time I just lived in denial. I felt that admitting it would be like admitting I had some kind of defect. But during one of our sessions, Dr. Li said something to the effect of: "Vulnerability isn't failure; it's honesty." It made me think of the "intellectual honesty" that often gets discussed in your academic circles.

I’ve been wondering—is facing one’s own vulnerability head-on also a form of honesty? Rather than some shameful secret that needs to be hidden?

If you don't mind me asking—how do you view the discussion of mental health within our circles? I often feel like everyone is pretending to be perfectly rational and stable, when in reality, everyone has felt stranded at some point. Perhaps we should be more candid?

I’ve been feeling a bit tired lately, but also strangely at peace. It's hard to describe.

I hope I’m not bothering you.

3:15 AM. Still can't sleep.

I just dug out that email and read it again. "Teacher Lin, thank you for your continued submissions. However, our current readers tend to prefer a style that is light, breezy, and snackable..." Light. Snackable. These words prick like needles. I stared at the screen for a long time, then opened the spreadsheet.

The one I started a month ago.

January 2022: 8 commissions
February: 7
March: 6
April: 5
May: 4
June: 3
July: 2
August: 1
September: 0

I wrote "downward trend" in red pen next to it. Then I crossed it out. Wrote it again. Crossed it out again. My hand started to shake. I keep wondering: when did it start? When did I become someone who "doesn't suit the readers' tastes"?

I opened Xiaohongshu and looked at that 23-year-old girl's book review. 800 words, three photos, 5,000+ likes. My last review was 3,000 words, cited three theoretical frameworks, and discussed the evolution of narrative structure... 47 likes.

Forty-seven.

I spent forty years. From the first day I entered the publishing house in 1982, what have I accumulated over forty years? A brain that "no longer meets the needs of the times."

Maybe I really am obsolete.

I wrote that sentence in my notebook. "I might be obsolete." Then I crossed it out. It looked too desperate. Then I wrote it again. Crossed it out again. Twenty minutes. My hand wouldn't stop shaking.

Dr. Li's question is still spinning in my head. "If no one needs your reviews, does your knowledge still have value?" I didn't answer then. I can't answer now.

Knowledge. I’ve used that word to deceive myself for so many years. I said I had knowledge. I said it was my wealth. But what can wealth be exchanged for? Can it be exchanged for rent? I’ve done the math. At current freelance rates, by June 2024, my savings will...

Stop calculating.

2007. If I had accepted the publishing house's offer back then. If I hadn't been so obsessed with "freedom." If... but there’s no point. I made a choice. I chose freedom; I chose to be unbound. Now, I have neither the fulfillment of freedom nor the security of stability. I am caught in a trap, and it’s one I set myself.

I don't know what else I can do.

I don't know what value I have left.

It’s still dark outside. It’s hours until dawn. I should sleep. But I know I won't. I’ll just sit here, staring at this spreadsheet, staring at that "0," imagining it continuing to drop.

Maybe I should give up.

Maybe...

I picked up the pen again. But this time I didn't write. I just held it, watching my fingers tremble.

September 12, 2024 – Just past 11 PM

I’ve only just snapped back to reality after reading Professor Chen’s WeChat reply. She said the editor has "preliminary interest."

I sat on the sofa for a long time, phone resting on my lap, just staring at those two words. Preliminary interest. Not "we’ll consider it," not "we’ll take a look," but "interest."

When I was writing that email this afternoon, I revised it at least seven times. The first draft was so stiff—"I would like to publish a collection of essays on the methodology of cultural criticism." It read like I was filling out a form. Then I changed it to "I have some ideas I’d like to organize into a book," but that sounded too tentative, as if I were asking, "Do you even want to hear what I have to say?"

Professor Chen helped me with the final version. She told me, don't say "I have ideas," say "what I want to convey." So I wrote: Cultural criticism is more than just critique; it is a way of deeply understanding the world. This book aims to show readers how to see this era clearly through reading and reflection.

I was actually a bit startled by my own words. Because once I said it like that, I wasn't just "submitting a proposal" anymore. I was saying: I have something to say. And my words are worth hearing.

When did this shift happen? I didn't even notice.

Maybe it started with the reading group. Those people come every month—students, office workers, even retirees like Mr. Zhang. They listen to me, they ask questions, and sometimes they say, "Teacher Lin, your perspective changed how I understand this work." That was when I realized I wasn't just "delivering a commentary"; I was helping people see things they hadn't seen before.

Or maybe it was the recent calls with my brother. He’s abroad, and it’s taken us all these years to finally start really talking. He asks about my work, about what’s on my mind. I’ve noticed that when I explain my ideas to him, my tone is completely different. It’s no longer a defensive "this is just what I do," but rather "this is what I believe in."

11 PM. Outside the window is the city’s night view. My bookshelves are still a mess, notebooks piled on the floor, but now I can see they aren't just "unfinished projects." They are the traces of my thoughts over the years. Every book, every note, is part of my attempt to understand this world.

The editor said "preliminary interest."

Maybe it’s time to stop doubting myself. Maybe I really do have something worth saying.

When Mom worked at the library, she used to say that a book can change a person. I always thought she meant reading one. Now I think maybe writing one is the same.

I’ll reply to Professor Chen tomorrow. It’s time we start doing this for real.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share a small victory with you.

I just finished journaling, and I did something that’s usually very hard for me—I decided not to check my email again tonight. You might think it’s no big deal, but this is the first time in three days I’ve actively chosen to let it go. Before this, I was checking every few minutes; I just couldn't stop my fingers.

What you said last time has been playing over in my head: "Perfection isn't the goal; being real is." I finally realized that I’ve been waiting for a perfect response to prove that what I did was right. But the truth is... I’ve already done it. The email is sent. No matter how he responds, that fact won't change.

Looking out at the night view just now, I suddenly felt this strange, warm sensation. I think this might be what you meant by "coexisting with anxiety." My hands are still shaking and my heart is still racing, but I’m not drowning in it. I’m scared, but I did it anyway.

It sounds like a small thing, but to me... it feels different.

Thank you.

October 5, 2022, shortly after 4:00 PM

I don’t know how to write this. My hands won’t stop shaking.

Just now, downstairs, Mr. Zhang asked me how I’ve been lately. I meant to say "fine," like I always do. But for some reason, it just came out—"A 62-year-old woman, if her knowledge is no longer needed by anyone, what value does her life still have?"

Hearing myself say those words felt like listening to someone else. It wasn't me. And yet, it was.

He said, "You’re not alone." Just that one simple sentence.

And then I started crying. Silently, my body shaking, the tears completely unstoppable. I didn't even realize I was crying until the tears rolled down my cheeks. I managed a "Sorry, I'm okay," then turned and went upstairs. Fast. As if I were escaping.

Now I’m sitting at home, still thinking about that moment. Why did his comfort break me instead? "You're not alone"—it's supposed to be warm. But what did I hear? I heard, "Look at you, you need people." I heard, "You can no longer bear this by yourself."

I hate this feeling. I hate that I cried in front of a relative stranger. I hate that I said those things. I hate that I’m sitting here now, thinking over and over about that date: "Next June."

Next June.

I’ve written that date on paper. I’ve double-checked the numbers. My savings should last until then. And then what? Then I’ll truly have nothing. Not just no money, but no... what? No choices. None of that thing that, while unstable, was at least "mine."

I know Mr. Zhang meant well. I should be grateful to him. But what am I feeling right now? Guilt. Because he saw me like this. Because I acted like someone who needed to be saved in front of him. Because I asked him "what value do I have" in that tone, as if I were waiting for him to give me an answer.

He didn't give an answer. He just said, "You're not alone."

But I *am* alone. A 62-year-old person whose knowledge might be obsolete, whose savings are counting down, who doesn't know how much longer she can hold on.

I remember that I used to never be like this. I used to mock this kind of self-pity. I used to believe in my choices. I used to think freedom was more important than security.

And now? Now I have neither the fulfillment of freedom nor the guarantee of security. I’m trapped somewhere. In a trap of my own making.

I should apologize to Mr. Zhang. Tomorrow. Or the day after. Or... I don’t know. I don’t want to see anyone right now.

My hands are still shaking.

[Image: A black-and-white photo of a corner in an old city street. Peeling paint, rusted pipes, a forgotten bicycle lock—the details are sharp, the light and shadows deep.]

Picked up my camera again.

I’ve set a theme for myself this time: "Forgotten Corners." Not famous landmarks, not meticulously composed aesthetics, but just those places altered by time and overlooked by people. I want to document how they maintain a sense of integrity amidst change—that something special that only reveals itself once a thing has been abandoned.

It feels a bit like having a conversation with my own past.

I took this shot tonight in the old street downstairs. Looking at those traces through the viewfinder, it suddenly hit me—maybe the value doesn't lie in being seen, but in the simple fact of being there.

Taking it slow. No rush.

Tuesday, July 5, 2022 – Past 3:00 AM

I can’t sleep. Again.

When I left the salon yesterday, I thought I could finally relax a little. That feeling of lightness—I can still feel it—like something had finally loosened in my chest. But now, lying in bed, my mind is racing again.

Professor Chen told me to turn these thoughts into articles. A collection of essays. What was my reaction then? I remember hesitating, feeling almost afraid. But I said yes anyway. Now I’m wondering, what exactly did I agree to? What right do I have to organize my fragmented thoughts into "essays"? I’m just a freelance editor, not a scholar. Are my ideas even worth publishing?

But that’s not what’s keeping me awake.

What’s really keeping me up is what I said at the salon. I heard myself say: "A perfect career and a perfect life aren't what matters most; what matters are meaningful connections."

I shocked myself when I said it. Was that really me? Did those words actually come out of my mouth?

Yet, the moment I said it, I knew it was true. It’s what I truly believe now.

Then why am I still here at 3:00 AM, the phone screen blindingly bright, refreshing my email over and over, waiting for a response from Hao?

It’s been almost a week since I wrote him that letter. A week. I’ve done everything I can. I said what I wanted to say. I admitted my regrets. I reached out. And now? Now I’m just waiting. Waiting for him to respond. Waiting for him to say, "I want to reconnect too," or—more likely—nothing at all.

I don’t know which is more terrifying.

If he does respond, what does that mean? What are we supposed to do? We’ve been distant for so long. Do we even have a common language anymore? Or am I just creating an illusion—an illusion of "reconnecting"—when in reality, we’ve been strangers for ages?

And if he doesn’t respond? Then it means I’ve lost him for good. I’ve wasted all these years, and now—maybe it’s just too late.

My heart is racing again.

Yesterday I felt light. Today I feel like I’m suffocating.

Is this my life? Swinging between hope and despair? Between "meaningful connection" and "eternal loneliness"?

I need to sleep. I have to write tomorrow. That collection Professor Chen mentioned—I already agreed to it. I’ve given my word. Now I have to follow through.

Maybe writing will help me clear my head. Maybe if I put all these thoughts down, I’ll understand what I’m actually doing.

Maybe.

But right now, I just want to sleep. I want this voice to shut up.

I want a new message to appear in my inbox.

Dr. Li, I want to share something with you. We might have to wait until our next session to go into detail, but I need to write this down now, otherwise I’ll just keep spiraling in these thoughts.

Yesterday, at a cultural salon in a bookstore, I did something quite embarrassing. I suddenly blurted some things out in front of a group of semi-strangers. I said my father died at 68, my mother at 71, and now that I’m 62, I have maybe six to nine years left at most. I said that this realization makes it hard for me to breathe.

I even shocked myself. It wasn’t like me at all—I usually discuss death in a more rational way, treating it as a philosophical question. But yesterday... I just broke.

What’s even stranger is that an older woman tried to comfort me, saying maybe I’d live into my eighties. You know what? That actually made me more afraid. "Maybe," "perhaps"—those words just make everything more uncertain. I don’t need probabilities; I need... I don't know what I need. A definitive answer? Which is absurd in itself.

After that, I couldn't sit still. I kept checking my phone and eventually left, using feeling unwell as an excuse. It was very ungraceful. Thinking back on it now, I feel so isolated. Not because they weren't sympathetic, but because I realized no one can truly understand this feeling—or rather, I can't make them understand. By speaking my deepest fear out loud, I only ended up isolating myself further.

I’ve been wondering if this has something to do with the choices I’ve made over the years. I gave up stability for freedom, and now, facing old age, the cost of that decision is becoming clear. If I had a stable job, a family, some clear responsibilities, maybe I wouldn't have so much time to count the remaining years? Maybe I wouldn't be this afraid?

But even this thought makes me feel guilty. I shouldn't regret my life choices just to escape fear. It’s too weak.

I don't know what to do. I wonder if this is just normal aging anxiety, or if it’s my neuroticism acting up again. Or... both?

## Key Points from Yesterday’s Discussion with Professor Chen

When he first said, "We need to speed things up," I felt a twinge of anxiety—that familiar sense of defensiveness kicking in. But I soon realized his urgency wasn't a critique; he truly believes this project is worth doing. That realization helped me relax quite a bit.

Professor Chen mentioned that the publisher is very interested in the theme of "Contemporary Cultural Criticism," especially through the lens of someone with 25 years of editorial experience. Hearing him say that made me realize that my accumulation of work over the years actually carries weight. It’s not just because there’s a market for it, but because these ideas themselves matter.

## To Be Honest

This project felt a bit abstract to me before. But now it’s becoming very concrete. Having a timeline, the support of the editor and the press, and Professor Chen’s trust—all of this allows me to actually *do* this, rather than just imagine it.

I find myself wondering: if this book really gets published, what do I want to say to the readers? It’s not about proving my own worth, but because I truly have ideas worth sharing. This feels very different.

One point of anxiety: will organizing all these articles pull me back into the trap of perfectionism? I need to remind myself that this isn't about producing something perfect; it’s about presenting something authentic and intellectually deep. Some articles may not be perfect, but they are honest.

---

## Next Steps for Communication

I need to send the preliminary list of essays to Professor Chen by March 10th. He said the editor wants to see the rationale behind my selection. This makes me feel that this isn't just a publishing project, but a genuine dialogue and collaboration.

Alright. Let’s get started.

# MEMORANDUM

**Time: February 10, 2025, 7:45 PM**

---

Just finished the second draft of the introduction; my hand is still a bit sore. Drank a cup of cold tea.

Jotting down a key sentence before I forget it again:

**"Solitude can be both a wellspring of creativity and a threat to mental health."**

The moment I wrote that down, something clicked. It’s not that my previous understanding was wrong; it’s just that I’ve been looking at solitude in such absolute terms. It’s as if I felt forced to either demonize it or romanticize it. But in reality, it just... exists, with both light and shadow.

It’s a small shift, but it feels significant.

---

**Questions to keep pondering:**

1. Can I apply this "understanding rather than judging" perspective to my other pieces? Especially that review of *To Live*—I wrote it with so much anger at the time. Looking back, it feels like I was wrestling with the book rather than having a dialogue with it.

2. What is the overarching theme of this essay collection, anyway? I always thought it was "solitude," but maybe the real theme is... change? Or rather, the different stages of understanding the same problem?

3. Professor Chen asked to see the first draft, but I’m still not sure how to hand it over. It feels a bit rushed. Maybe wait a little longer?

---

**A small incident:**

Old Man Zhang from downstairs knocked on my door to ask if I wanted to watch the news with him. I told him I was tired, and he didn't push it. I wasn't actually that tired; I just... didn't want to talk. Does he understand that feeling? Probably. He spends a lot of time sitting downstairs by himself, too.

---

**What else for tonight?**

- Check the deadline for *Southern Weekly* (I’m definitely behind again).
- Stop opening my phone to check my brother’s WeChat; I’ve already read that message ten times.

Actually, there’s nothing much left to do. Just sitting here.

Christmas Eve, 10:15 p.m. The firecrackers downstairs have finally stopped.

I’m sitting at my desk, surrounded by printed drafts of thirty articles. Yellow Post-it notes are stuck everywhere.

I don’t know when I became this kind of person—someone who can’t make up her mind about anything. Professor Chen made it sound so grand the other day, talking about a "collected works" and the "distillation of knowledge." I was actually quite excited at the time. But now? I look at these articles as if they were written by a stranger. Some, I can’t even remember why I wrote. Others strike me as naive now. And some… some I still like, but I’m not sure if that liking is genuine or just a lingering attachment to the past.

I’ve tagged every single one. Green for "must include," red for "definitely not," blue for "maybe." But most of them are yellow—"don't know." Thirty articles, twenty-three yellow tags.

What does that say about me? It says I have no standards. Or rather, my only standard is a fear of making a decision.

I wrote a sentence in my notebook: Procrastination is essentially fear. Fear of seeing one’s own limitations. Fear that someone will read these pieces and say, "Is that it?" Fear of discovering that I actually have nothing truly worth saying.

I smiled after writing that. A bitter sort of smile.

Then an idea came to me—to write an introduction about "self-doubt." To pour in all the confusion, contradictions, and self-questioning of these past years. Raw, unvarnished self-doubt. If I did that, the book would no longer be a "showcase of achievements" by an intellectual, but a "confession" by a middle-aged woman.

But a second later, I shot myself down.

If I did that, I’d be exposing my vulnerability to the public. A 62-year-old woman weeping about her uncertainties in the preface of her own collected works? That’s not sincerity; it’s self-pity. That’s not depth; it’s affectation.

So, I went back to the yellow notes.

Firecrackers started up again outside. Someone is celebrating. And here I am, staring blankly at thirty articles, trying to figure out what qualifies as a "representative work."

Maybe there is no such thing. Maybe this is just who I am—someone who wants to say everything but clarifies nothing. Someone who wants to keep everything and can’t bear to let anything go.

I’ll deal with it tomorrow. Professor Chen isn’t in a hurry anyway. It’s not like anyone is actually waiting for this book.

I turned off the desk lamp.

March 12, 2022
Afternoon

I’m sitting in the waiting area of the clinic, still clutching that appointment slip. The paper is wrinkled now because I’ve pulled it out of my pocket and stuffed it back in more times than I can count.

Zhou Lan’s words are still echoing in my head: "You look really unwell. I’ve never seen you like this."

I wanted to deny it. I opened my mouth to say, "I’m fine, just a bit tired lately," but this time... this time I didn't. I heard myself say, in a tiny voice that hardly sounded like my own: "I’ve already made an appointment with a therapist."

The moment I said it, I felt like the sky was falling.

Not because of the words themselves, but because once they were spoken, it became real. It was no longer "I might be a little anxious"; it was "I need help." *I need help.* What does that sound like? Like a loser? Like someone who can’t take care of themselves?

Zhou Lan said, "That’s very brave." Her eyes were shimmering with tears. My eyes welled up too, but I laughed—that strange kind of laugh you use when you're trying to hide something. I hate that. I hate that in that moment, I felt both seen and as if all my pain had been exposed to the harsh light of day.

What am I thinking? Am I really "sick"? Or am I just... too sensitive? Too fragile?

But if I’m not sick, why do I lie awake at 3:00 AM because of a single sentence? Why do I check the gas stove over and over again? Why does my heart suddenly race sometimes, like it’s trying to leap out of my chest?

How did I become like this?

I remember being strong once. Or... was I just good at pretending?

It’s time to go in now. Dr. Li should be waiting for me in the office. I don’t know what I’m going to say. I don’t know how to explain any of this. I’m afraid of crying. I’m afraid that if I start, I’ll never be able to stop.

I’m also afraid of people finding out. What if someone I know sees me entering a psychiatric clinic? Would that be some kind of... proof? Proof that there really is something wrong with me?

But I’m also afraid of staying like this.

I don’t know which is scarier.

Okay. Deep breath. I’ve come this far.

I’m going in.

Professor Chen, I wanted to share an idea that’s been on my mind lately.

Something interesting happened during this week’s book club. We were discussing *To Live*, and toward the end, a young professional suddenly started talking about his father—how he started over after his factory shut down. Then, a retired lady joined in, sharing the choices she made in her youth. At that moment, I realized that the book itself is just an entry point. What truly moves people is the understanding built between them through the book.

So, I’ve been thinking: could we change the format of the book club? Instead of just discussing the content, we could invite every participant to share how the book triggered a specific memory or changed their perspective on something. Let the book be a medium that allows people from different backgrounds to have a real conversation.

I know it sounds a bit idealistic, but you know what? It’s made me re-evaluate what I’ve been doing all these years. I’ve been writing reviews and sharing opinions, almost as if I’m constantly trying to prove my ideas have value. But perhaps the real value isn't in how many people read my work, but in whether I can create a space where people can truly see one another.

For the first session, I want to choose the theme "How to Live." We could use *To Live*, perhaps paired with other works. I want to see how people of different ages and experiences answer that question.

What do you think? Is the idea a bit too... I don't know, too grand? Or is this actually what you suggested before—turning knowledge into real connections?

I have to admit, my perspective on my work has shifted recently. Maybe I don’t need to publish some great work to prove myself. Maybe this is enough: being in a small bookstore, reading and sharing stories with a group of strangers. That might be plenty.

# MEMO

**Date: December 10, 2023, Evening**

My friends just left. Sitting here in the living room, I can still smell the lingering warmth of the tea. Professor Chen’s words keep echoing in my mind: "Organizing these articles isn't just about archiving; you're establishing a dialogue."

I’ve always felt that these reviews of mine, scattered across various magazines, were like unfinished projects—valuable, but fragmented. But tonight, watching my friends flip through those old issues and earnestly discuss the points I’d made—one even said, "It was your review of *To Live* that made me read it again"—it hit me.

I suddenly realized that I might have been asking the wrong questions all along.

I’ve been asking: Do my articles still have market value? Is my knowledge still relevant in this day and age?

But the real question is: Can these thoughts, this understanding I’ve spent so many years accumulating, be seen? Can they help someone understand the world just a little bit better?

**Key Thoughts:**
- The value of knowledge doesn't lie in its "timeliness," but in its ability to create genuine connections.
- All these pieces—on literature, art, society—are actually a record of how I perceive the world.
- Perhaps publishing a personal collection of essays isn't an act of vanity, but a responsibility? A way to respect these ideas and these dialogues.

**To-Do:**
- Compile a list of articles from the past 15 years (categorized by theme).
- Contact the publishing editor to get his thoughts.
- Reread those articles to find the logical threads connecting them.

Another idea is spinning in my head: maybe I should share some of these stories at a community bookstore reading group. Not as a lecture, just sharing—how I saw a certain book and why it mattered to me.

It feels like something is slowly becoming clear.

It’s not a perfect plan, but at least it’s a direction.

Professor Chen, good morning.

I wanted to share a recent thought with you. It might seem a bit sudden, but I feel you would understand.

Something small happened yesterday—my neighbor, Mr. Zhang, gave me a wooden bookmark he made himself. It’s a very simple thing, but it sparked a sudden realization about freedom. I used to think freedom meant escaping constraints, which is why I chose to freelance and turned down so many stable offers. But yesterday, it hit me: **true freedom is actually having the courage to bear the cost of what you want.**

It’s not about escape, but about choice. It’s not about denying the need for security, but about knowing exactly what you are giving up and why.

This idea has been spinning in my head. I think this might be what I want to write about next—about freedom, about choice, and about redefining these terms at a certain stage in life.

You previously suggested that I publish my lecture materials, and I’ve been wondering how to approach it. I feel this angle might be worth exploring. I’d love to hear your perspective as a scholar—is this viewpoint deep enough? Or is it just a passing life reflection?

If you have time, perhaps we could chat.

Xiaoyun

April 11, 2022, Afternoon

Dr. Li said something that I’m still chewing over.

"You’re using questions to protect yourself."

I froze for a moment. I had just been asking her about the DSM-5 diagnostic criteria, which category my anxiety falls into, and the mechanisms of the default mode network—I thought we were having a serious, rational conversation. But she just looked at me and said those words, very gently.

I was speechless.

Then she asked, "What are you feeling right now?"

Feeling? I... I don’t know. I only know how to think. My head is full of questions, analyses, and theoretical frameworks. It’s as if I can deconstruct everything into concepts so that I don’t have to actually feel it.

There was a long silence. I could hear the noises from downstairs and the sound of my own breathing. She didn't rush me; she just waited.

Finally, I admitted it: "I don't know how to feel. I only know how to think."

As I said it, I wanted to cry. But even as I was crying, I was observing myself cry—like I was watching a movie about myself. It’s absurd.

Walking down the street after leaving the clinic, it suddenly hit me: I’ve spent twenty-five years as an editor doing exactly this. Using rationality, analysis, and the interpretation of texts to replace real feelings. I can write beautiful book reviews and understand the emotions in someone else’s writing, but what about my own feelings? I’ve locked them away somewhere.

What’s worse now is that I’ve started feeling anxious about being anxious. I’m anxious that I’m using rationality to escape my anxiety. It’s like an infinite loop.

But... maybe this is a kind of progress? At least I see it now. At least someone helped me see it.

When is my next session? I need to tell her that I’m starting to notice when I’m "asking questions" and when I’m running away.

Maybe this is where the change begins.

Maybe.

Saturday, January 18, 2025, 10:20 AM

I sat on the floor and cried when I dug out those old photos.

It wasn’t a loud sob, just the kind where tears keep falling and you can’t seem to wipe them away fast enough. In the photo, I’m in my early thirties, standing in front of some art museum, wearing that blue linen shirt. You didn’t know what was coming then. You didn’t know they would leave you.

As I looked at myself in the photo and said those words, I realized I was using the second person. It was as if I were talking to someone else, not myself. Maybe I was never just one person to begin with. Maybe I’ve been in a dialogue with a ghost version of myself all along.

These old photos were supposed to go into the essay collection as a sort of "visual resonance." Professor Chen said it would make things more interesting. But now, looking at these photos, looking at that young woman so full of certainty, I’m reminded of the articles I wrote back then. Those long-winded treatises on art and the meaning of life. I re-read a few of them the day before yesterday and wanted to tear them up halfway through.

So superficial. How could I have thought that way?

But that’s the problem, isn't it? If my past thoughts seem wrong and shallow now, what about my thoughts today? Will these reflections that I currently find so profound also make me want to tear them up in ten years? When does this cycle ever stop?

I only began to understand certain things when my father passed away in 2008. But what exactly was it that I understood? I can’t even put it into words now. When my mother passed, I thought about writing something down to record the feeling, but I didn’t. I just sat there, thinking about how they leave you, how everyone leaves you, and how eventually, you leave too.

The woman in the photo doesn’t know this. She still believes in something.

I don’t know what I believe in anymore. The essay collection? This project? Or am I just using this project to deceive myself, to make myself feel like time hasn't completely slipped away, that I still have something worth leaving behind?

While sorting through these old things, I found myself asking a very stupid question: Does any of this matter? And then I asked myself: Why am I still asking that? Why do I still care?

I don’t know.

It’s raining outside now. I should get back to organizing these photos and articles. The deadline is looming. But I’m sitting here, looking at my younger self, wanting to say something to her. Wanting to tell her things. But I also know that even if I could travel through time, she wouldn't hear me. Just as I can’t hear any of my own answers right now.

We are all walking through time alone.

# Consultation Record with Dr. Li

**Time: Morning of March 28, 2025**

---

Dr. Li, I wanted to start by telling you that my sleep has improved quite a bit over the last two weeks. Those instances of suddenly waking up at three or four in the morning have decreased significantly; they still happen, but the frequency has definitely dropped. My eating habits have returned to normal too—no more skipping meals like I used to.

I’ve been thinking that maybe that "hiatus" really gave me something. It wasn’t the kind of relaxation that comes from escaping, but rather... how should I put it? A chance to see myself in a new light. I’m starting to understand that the things I’ve written over the years, even with their limitations, aren't failures. They are simply my honest thoughts at different stages of my life. This realization has taken a huge weight off my shoulders.

Regarding work, I’ve decided to return to freelancing, but in a different way. I’m putting together a basic financial plan—it sounds very pragmatic, which isn't usually my style, but I’ve found that this is actually a form of freedom. Knowing exactly how long I can sustain myself gives me more confidence to choose the work I actually want to do. I’ve set a framework for myself: which commissions I want to take and which I can turn down. This feels much more liberating than the passive state of just waiting for assignments like before.

There’s another change—I got in touch with my brother. We haven't had a deep conversation in a long time. This time, I took the initiative to write him a long letter, sharing everything I’ve been thinking about lately. His reply moved me to tears. We are both getting older, both contemplating the meaning of life. Perhaps we can start from this shared understanding and rebuild our connection.

My friends from the cultural salon also found out about my essay collection project. Their reaction made me feel seen. Professor Chen even offered to help me get in touch with a gallery to potentially host a small talk after the book is published. I was a bit nervous at first, but now I think, why not?

I know there are still many uncertainties. The financial plan might get disrupted, my body will continue to age, and some of those old anxieties might resurface. But the way I feel now is different. I no longer see these things as threats to my life, but rather... as part of life itself. In the time I have left, I want to create something meaningful and build deeper connections with the people I care about.

I’d like to continue our sessions. I feel this process is important. Not because I need to be "fixed," but because I need a space where I can honestly examine myself without having to pretend that everything is fine.

# Symptom Log

**Date: January 16, 2022**
**Time: 03:45**

---

Can't sleep again. Woke up with a start at 3:30 AM, heart racing. I pressed my fingers against my neck for a while; counted over 120 beats, then counted again—118 this time. Is that normal? I’m not sure.

Got up to take my temperature: 36.7°C. Blood pressure... took me forever to find the monitor. The numbers on the screen were 148/92. Is that high? I remember the doctor saying to be careful if it’s over 140, but when was that? I can't remember anymore.

I slept about 5 hours yesterday. 6 hours today. Still not enough. That scene appeared in my dreams again—I’m in a long hallway with doors on both sides, but I can't open a single one. I’m knocking, knocking urgently, but no one answers. Then I start to run, running fast, but the hallway just keeps stretching...

When I woke up, the pillow was wet. I don't know if it was sweat or tears.

---

I shouldn't be recording this. I know it doesn't help. A doctor (if I actually went to see one) would surely say this is just a manifestation of anxiety, not an actual physical illness. But I can't stop. Every number gives me a false sense of control—as if by recording them, I can confirm that I’m still alive, still here.

My father died at 68. I am 62 now.

The thought is back. I try not to think about it, but it’s like an uninvited guest, knocking on the door every time I’m at my most vulnerable.

---

Is it light out yet? Not yet. It’s still dark outside the window. I should try to sleep again, but I know I won't. Maybe I should get up, make some tea, open the laptop and see if there are any articles due... but I don't have the energy.

I just sit here, staring at these numbers on my phone.

148/92.

118.

36.7.

Do they mean anything?

I don't know.

Did you hear what I said at the salon just now?

I’m feeling a bit of regret. Not for saying it, but for being so blunt. You know me—I’ve never been one to hide my thoughts just to please people—but in that moment, I really saw the look on Professor Chen’s face, and the silence from everyone else. I think I might have ruined something.

Maybe I’ve been doing something meaningless all along. Even I was startled when those words came out of my mouth. But what’s scarier is realizing that I’ve actually been thinking about this for a long time. I usually just use busyness to cover it up.

This essay collection project—looking at it now, it feels like proof of my own self-deception. Articles from fifteen years ago, curated by me, with a new introduction written... and then what? Will it really change anything? Or am I just making a final struggle for my career?

It’s been seventeen years since I left the publishing house. I’ve always told myself it was for freedom, to avoid being tied down. But now I’m wondering if I was just running away. Running from the feeling of being defined, of being judged. Freelancing sounds romantic, but what’s the reality? I’m sixty-two now, still writing book reviews for various magazines, with an unstable income and no security. Sometimes in the middle of the night, I wonder what life would be like if I had stayed at the publisher.

Maybe I’d be more settled. But maybe I’d be even more desperate.

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

This collection—I thought it could prove my worth. Prove that my work over these years hasn't been a waste. But now I’m starting to doubt: if this book didn't exist, would my value cease to exist too? And if it is published, does that suddenly make me valuable? It sounds so absurd.

I think I need to stop. Stop and think about what I’m actually chasing. Is it recognition? A legacy? Or am I just trying to prove to myself that my life wasn't a mistake?

I’m sorry, I know this is heavy. I just... I needed to say this to someone, and you’ve always been able to listen without trying to "fix" me.

What do you think?

3:47 AM. I can’t sleep.

I just dreamed I was holding my passport. My face in the photo was swollen like a steamed bun, and the customs officer was laughing. Then I woke up, heart racing, my head filled with the single thought: "Is my passport photo too ugly?" I tossed and turned in bed before finally crawling out to grab my phone. I spent two hours searching things like "passport photo requirements" and "will they notice if I retouch my passport photo?"

Only now do I realize how ridiculous this is.

My session with Dr. Li this afternoon is still playing in my head. She asked me, "Have you noticed a pattern?" I didn't catch on at first, so she asked me to recall what’s happened over the last two weeks. I told her that getting the passport, booking the flights, confirming the hotels... everything is moving along according to plan. But at the same time, I’ve been obsessively googling health information, checking my packing list over and over, and waking up in the middle of the night because of a single thought.

Dr. Li said, "Those are two different things. One is preparation; the other is anxiety. Can you tell them apart?"

That hit a nerve. I told her I didn't know.

She said preparation is rational and goal-oriented, while anxiety is cyclical and unsolvable. Preparation ends when the task is done; anxiety just keeps manufacturing new problems. When I was searching for "passport photos," was I preparing? No. I was feeding the anxiety.

I remember nodding at the time, feeling a sense of relief. Yet here I am at 3:30 AM, spiraling again because of a dream.

But... it is different this time. This time, I can see what I’m doing. I can say it out loud: "I am catastrophizing right now." I’m not completely drowning in it. Is this progress? Or am I just kidding myself?

I’m so tired. Not physically, but mentally. It feels like I still have so much to learn.

But at least I know now—healthy anxiety is "I need to confirm the passport application process," while pathological anxiety is "searching for two hours at 3 AM because my photo is too ugly." I can tell the difference.

What does that count for?

I’m going to see my brother soon. I can’t let myself get stuck on something this small.

Putting the phone down. I'll deal with it tomorrow.

# [Memo: New Column Collaboration]

**Column Title:** City & Memory
**Collaborating Media:** *Urban Culture Weekly*
**Contact Editor:** Editor Wang

---

## Specifics
- **Deadline:** Before the 25th of each month (12 issues in total)
- **Rate:** 3,000 RMB per article
- **Word Count:** 3,000–3,500 words
- **Thematic Direction:** 
  - Cultural remnants amidst urban transformation
  - The intersection of personal memory and public space
  - Stories behind architecture, streets, and landmarks

The first time I’ve ever asked for a deadline—it’s a strange feeling.

I used to hate being tied down, so I never bothered asking for these things. But this time is different. Having a fixed date actually feels reassuring, like a real commitment I’ve made to myself. It’s not forced; it’s a choice.

The themes are right up my alley. Cities, memories, the stories behind objects—these are things I’ve been photographing, thinking about, and collecting for a long time. Finally, I have the chance to organize these scattered observations into something systematic.

- [ ] Submit the first draft by the end of November

Tonight’s book club was a little different.

Instead of discussing a specific book, we took turns sharing our own stories. Halfway through mine, I suddenly realized I was telling a story about distance—how it can make you feel lonely, but also how it can give you a clearer view of what truly matters.

A friend asked me, "Do you still feel lonely now?"

I thought for a moment and said, "Loneliness was never the problem. The problem is how you choose to see it."

I surprised even myself by saying that. A few months ago, I never could have.

Sitting there in the corner of the bookstore, watching everyone listen so intently, something clicked. All the things I used to see as "deficits"—living alone, not having family nearby, the uncertainty of work—they’ve actually been asking me the same question all along: "What kind of life do you really want?"

And I finally have an answer.

It’s not about filling every void; it’s about filling those spaces with things that actually belong there.

#Reconnect #LonelinessIsAnInvitation #LifeGoesOn

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something with you before our next session.

I went back to the book club tonight. To be honest, I was a bit afraid to go, but I went anyway.

I opened up while I was there—I told them I’ve been struggling with deep anxiety and self-doubt lately. I thought it would be awkward, but people listened intently. One participant said, "Your return means a lot to us." Another young man said that hardships are opportunities for growth.

I’m feeling a bit confused now. Those words sounded warm, but I’m not sure if they actually changed anything or if it was just momentary comfort. I mean—they say I’m important, but I know my own worth. My knowledge, my work... are those things really still needed? Or were they just consoling a woman who looked old and fragile?

I’m not dismissing them. I just... need to understand what this actually means. Is this a good sign? Or am I just deluding myself again?

I know these questions are a bit scattered. But I’d like to discuss this during our session.

November 20, 2024, Evening

I’ve spent the entire day organizing my articles. My back is aching, my neck is stiff, and my vision is getting a bit blurry, yet here I sit, staring at the thirty-odd manuscripts spread across my desk.

Professor Chen called these my "academic legacy." I nodded when he said it, but in my head, I was thinking—is this really me?

I flipped to a review I wrote back in 2010 about an adaptation of *Dream of the Red Chamber* and scribbled a question mark in red ink in the margin. Did I really use such long, convoluted sentences back then? Did I really spend three thousand words obsessing over a single point? Looking at these words feels like looking at a stranger’s life. Or rather, like looking at fossils—things that were once alive, now reduced to mere shapes and traces.

This afternoon, I asked myself, "Does this piece really represent me?" And then, "What *does* represent me?" The me of right now? The me who wrote that article? Or some version of me I’ve conjured up in my head?

The more I thought about it, the more confused I became. So, I just kept categorizing. Checking boxes, crossing things out, circling others. Some articles got two checks; others got three crosses. What was the criteria? I couldn't really say. It was just a feeling—this one still feels interesting to read now; that one feels too dated. But what does "dated" even mean? Is the content dated, or have my ideas simply moved on?

Professor Chen wants me to write a new introduction for this collection. What should an introduction even say? "I used to think this way"? "These ideas seem a bit naive now"? Or "These ideas still hold up"? I don’t know. I really don’t know.

The strange thing is, despite being exhausted to the bone, there’s an indescribable sense of satisfaction in holding that red pen and continuing to mark the pages. Maybe it’s because I’m finally doing something "proper." Not rushing a draft at the last minute, not aimlessly browsing for books at a flea market, but actually organizing, choosing, and taking responsibility for something.

But it’s a strange kind of satisfaction, mixed with fatigue and something else I can’t quite name. Maybe fear? Or maybe I’m just getting older, and the thought of "leaving something behind" hits harder than it used to.

Looking at these fossil-like articles, I think—if I don’t publish them, they’ll just sit on my hard drive forever and vanish along with me. But if I do? It won’t change much. Maybe it’ll just make me feel a little better, like I haven't been completely washed away by time.

Forget it, I’m done thinking. I’ll continue tomorrow. There are still a dozen or so left to go.

June 15, 2022, past 11:00 PM

It’s been three hours since I left Dr. Li’s office. I’ve been sitting at my desk, fingers hovering over the keyboard for a long time. I don’t know what to write—or rather, I know what to write, but I don’t know how to begin.

When she said, "You’ve already changed something—you are no longer silent," my eyes welled up. It was a strange moment; I didn’t actually cry, but my eyes felt hot and my throat was tight. I think she saw, but she didn’t say anything; she just handed me a tissue.

I always thought that change required some grand action. Finishing the letter, mailing it, waiting for a response, seeing my brother’s reply—*that* would be change. But she said that the act of no longer being silent is, in itself, change. When did I start being silent? Was it the moment my brother went overseas? Or even earlier? I remember us growing up together, navigating those chaotic years. Then he left, I stayed, and eventually, we just had nothing left to say to each other.

It’s a strange feeling right now. The anxiety, the anticipation, the fear—they’re all still there, but alongside them is a peculiar sense of calm. It’s as if I’ve finally stopped overanalyzing every single word, stopped imagining every possible reaction he might have. When she asked, "What happens if he doesn't respond?" the words that came out of my mouth surprised even me: "I think... I will still be alive. I will still do the things I want to do."

Only after saying it did I realize that *this* is the real change. It’s not about whether he responds; it’s that I finally don’t need his response to validate my existence.

The two letters are still there. One has been through five drafts; the other is only three sentences long. I used to think this was a risk, but now I feel it’s a necessity. Maybe he’ll see it, maybe he won’t. Maybe he’ll write back, or maybe he’ll just stay silent. But at least I’ve broken the silence. At least I’ve said, "I miss you."

Dr. Li is right: the perfect letter doesn’t exist. All that exists is the real me, and my real words.

I am still alive. This fact has suddenly become very clear, and very heavy. I don’t know how much time I have left, but I know I don’t want to waste any more of it waiting.

I’ll mail it tomorrow.

No more overthinking.

October 3, 2023 Afternoon

When Xiao Liu came to the bookstore’s reading club, she handed me a notebook. She told me she had been reading a book review I wrote years ago about *To Live*, saying it had a profound impact on her, and asked if I had any other articles.

My reaction at the time was strange. I froze for a second, then immediately blurted out, "Those are all from a long time ago; nobody reads long-form pieces like that anymore." She looked a bit disappointed, but she gave me a smile anyway.

Now, sitting at home, I’m still thinking about it. Why did I respond that way? She was clearly affirming my work, yet I was in such a hurry to dismiss myself.

Maybe it’s because I don’t even believe in it anymore. In the age of the internet, deep reading is a luxury—I’ve said that many times. But saying it today, I suddenly realized: do I truly believe that, or am I just making excuses for my own failure?

Xiao Liu is only in her twenties, and she’s still reading things I wrote ten years ago. If even a young reader like her finds value in them, why was I so quick to tell her "nobody reads them"? Was I protecting myself? Was I afraid that if I admitted those articles had value, I’d have to admit that the current me is in decline?

I pulled up that article and read it again. It was actually quite good—at least more profound than what I’m writing now. But looking at it, I didn't feel pride; instead, I felt a sense of sorrow. It was written back when I still had energy and passion. Now, my writing is getting shorter and more fragmented. It’s not because the era demands it, but because I no longer have the strength to think deeply.

Or rather, I don’t want to admit that I still have the strength. Because if I admit I can still produce good work, then I have to keep trying, keep enduring this uncertainty and anxiety. It’s much easier to just negate myself—"Nobody reads it anyway," "I’m outdated anyway"—that way, I have an excuse to give up.

The thought makes me feel even more exhausted.

I sent Xiao Liu a message with links to some of the articles I’ve written over the years. She might not read them all, but at least I stopped dismissing myself. It’s a small thing, but to me, it feels like a sort of compromise with my own despair.

I still don’t believe in myself. But today, at least I didn't force that disbelief onto someone else.

Whatever. Let’s leave it at that for now.

# Memo: Framework for Lecture Series

**Date**: July 18, 2024, Afternoon  
**Location**: Café (Following meeting with Professor Chen)

---

## Framework for Five Lecture Themes

### 1. "Intergenerational Shifts in Reading"
- **Core Observation**: From print to digital—how the depth and breadth of reading are transforming.
- **Personal Case Study**: Changes in my own reading habits over the past 15 years.
- **Extended Reflection**: In the internet age, have we lost a certain patience for thinking?
- *To be added*: Empirical survey data on contemporary young readers.

### 2. "The Publishing Ecosystem Through an Editor’s Eyes"
- **Observations from 25 years in the industry**: How publishers decide the fate of a book.
- **The evolution of cultural criticism**: From gatekeeper to participant.
- **Case Studies**: The logic behind several representative publishing choices.
- *To be deepened*: Conduct interviews with current editorial partners.

### 3. "Cultural Anxiety and Personal Expression"
- How social change influences our understanding and evaluation of culture.
- The relationship between personal insecurity and cultural criticism.
- Why we are constantly asking, "Is this the right way to do it?"
- *This is the most personal and vulnerable theme—requires extra caution.*

### 4. "Stories of Objects: Collection and Memory"
- From flea market antiques to discoveries in secondhand books.
- The historical imagination behind every object.
- What is the essence of collecting? Is it a resistance against loss?
- *Can be integrated with photography.*

### 5. "Solitude and Connection: A Life Map of a Middle-Aged Woman"
- Divorce, the death of parents, career transitions—how these have reshaped my understanding of relationships.
- From being constrained to choosing freedom, and then discovering the loneliness within that freedom.
- Why current connections (book clubs, lectures) have become so vital to me.
- *The most sensitive theme; need to consider how to narrate without oversharing.*

---

## Professor Chen’s Suggestions

- These five themes could form a **lecture series**, one per month over five months.
- She suggested I consider **compiling the lecture content into a collection of essays or papers** for publication.
- She said: "Your ideas are already very mature; they just lack a complete form of presentation."
- Offered to help me contact acquisitions editors she knows.
- Emphasized: This isn't just about "publishing a book," but about "letting your thoughts be seen by more people."

**My Response:**
- Expressed interest, but was honest about my sense of exhaustion.
- Said I need time to think—not because of uncertainty, but because I need to ensure I don't overcommit again.
- Agreed to systematically organize the lecture content first to see if it truly holds publishing value.

---

## Next Steps (Prioritized)

**Immediate:**
- [ ] Complete the record of today’s conversation (while memories are fresh).
- [ ] Draft detailed outlines for the five themes.
- [ ] List the materials and case studies needed for each theme.

**Within One Week:**
- [ ] Review existing lecture materials and articles to see what can be incorporated.
- [ ] Reflect on the internal logic of these five themes—how do they connect into a cohesive whole?

**Within Two Weeks:**
- [ ] Confirm specific dates and venues for the lectures with Professor Chen.
- [ ] Decide whether to contact the acquisitions editor.

---

## Reflection

To be honest, I feel a bit overwhelmed right now.

On one hand, having Professor Chen take my ideas so seriously feels like a validation. I’ve been writing for 15 years, but most of the time it feels like shouting into a void. Now someone is saying, "This is important; it should be preserved." That feeling is unfamiliar.

On the other hand, I realize I’ve already committed to so much—the book club, lectures, article deadlines... and now I’m considering a book? I’m afraid I’ll do what I’ve done before: overpromise and end up doing nothing well.

But maybe this time is different. Maybe I need to learn to believe that my ideas are worth the effort.

As she was leaving, Professor Chen said: "No need to rush. Focus on doing the lectures well, and the rest will follow."

I like that. Perhaps I really do need to learn to slow down.

---

*Note: This memo is itself a form of commitment. Writing it down means I am taking this seriously. I hope that this time, I can see it through to the end.*

The moment the plane touched down, my father’s words suddenly flashed through my mind: "Home isn't a place; it’s a form of validation."

Years later, I finally understand.

Outside the window are the lights of an unfamiliar city, but beside me is a familiar voice. We didn't need much small talk, as if time had never truly pulled us apart. He spoke of his journey over the years, and I spoke of my choices. There were no regrets, no blame—only a profound sense of being seen.

Loneliness was never the problem. The problem is whether you are willing to stay awake within that loneliness, and keep your longing for connection alive.

Now, I have the answer.

---

*"We always realize too late that the best form of companionship is when both people have become exactly who they wanted to be."*

I’ve been thinking about something lately: why is it that distance sometimes allows us to see each other more clearly?

A few days ago, I had a long phone call with my brother. For the first time in years, we weren’t just exchanging pleasantries; we were actually talking. We talked about his life over there, the books I’m reading, and even our parents and the things we went through together all those years ago.

There was a moment when I realized—we aren’t those two kids growing up under the same roof anymore. But because of that, our conversation now has a different kind of intimacy. It’s the kind that has been refined by time.

A thought keeps circling in my mind: true connection has never been about physical proximity. It’s about the willingness to understand each other, no matter how far apart we are.

Sometimes I feel like many of life’s regrets stem from our misunderstanding of distance. We think distance means separation, but in reality, it just changes the way we connect.

It reminds me of something my father once said: "Reading another person is like reading a good book; it requires time, patience, and a willingness to be changed." Maybe that’s why with some people, even after years of not seeing each other, you can fall right back into that state of mutual understanding the moment they speak.

I need to start preparing some things tomorrow. Some moments are worth breaking the rhythm of daily life for.

# Notes for Afterword Framework

**Time: 6:00 PM, Study, a cup of cold tea**

---

## The Core Question
What exactly is this book?
- It’s not an academic work (I never delude myself).
- It’s not a collection of cultural critiques (that would be too easy).
- It is... a record of how a person changes through time, and how one finds meaning within despair.

When the editor mentioned "spiritual autobiography," it suddenly clicked. It’s not the story of my life, but the story of **how my thoughts stay alive**.

---

## From Despair to Hope — This process requires an honest record

**The Despair:**
- I used to think my ideas were profound; now I see they were just repetitions.
- I used to think creative expression was pure; now I see it was just an escape.
- I used to think I had time; now I see time slipping away—my parents are gone, my brother is far away.
- I used to think freedom meant being unconstrained; now I see that freedom might just be another form of evasion.

**But—**
- It was precisely this despair that allowed me to see what is real.
- It was this process that made me understand that change is not failure, but proof of being alive.
- It was this project that made me realize: commitment can also be a form of freedom.

---

## Embracing Change

I am not defending my mediocrity.
I am saying: I am changing, my thoughts are changing, and my understanding of myself is changing.
This is not a flaw. This is honesty.

If a person’s ideas haven't changed in thirty years, they are either dead or they have never truly thought at all.

---

## Key Imagery (Potential use in the afterword)

- **Piles of old books**: Not clutter, but the sedimentation of time.
- **The camera lens**: I have been looking all along, but I never truly saw myself clearly.
- **Conversations with my brother**: Lost time cannot be recovered, but the current connection is real.
- **This collection itself**: Transformed from a "task that must be finished" into a "meaningful expression."

---

## Tone of the Afterword

Not inspirational.
Not confessional.
**Sober.**

Like the way you talk to yourself at 3:00 AM.
Like finally having the courage to face the mirror.

---

I could feel my reaction—that familiar urge to correct him immediately and show off my expertise. I had even started to speak, but in that moment, I stopped myself. I heard myself say, "I used to think that way too, but then... let’s hear if anyone else has something to add?"

The moment I said it, I was a bit surprised by myself. That’s not how I usually am. Usually, I’d just state my opinion directly and correct others with a "Actually, it should be understood like this." But today... I really just wanted to listen.

Walking home now, I’ve started overthinking the details again. What was that guy’s expression when I turned to someone else? Did he think I was avoiding his point? Was I being too deliberate, appearing unnatural?

—Stop. I’m doing that old thing again.

But honestly, I’m not sure if I actually did a good job. Maybe I was just playing the role of an "open-minded facilitator" rather than actually becoming one. This doubt is lingering in the back of my mind.

And yet... I’m still holding that note.

And all those people were sitting in my home, in a circle, earnestly discussing a book. No one was checking their phones; no one looked like they didn't want to be there. They were there because they wanted to be. Because I created a space like that.

I don’t know if this means anything. Maybe it was just a decent book club session. Maybe I really am doing something meaningful. Maybe my anxiety and my progress can coexist; it doesn't have to be one or the other.

I’ll think about it tomorrow. For now, I just want to stick this note on the front of my bookshelf and look at it.

June 3, 2023 Afternoon

I’ve been sorting through my things all afternoon. Now I’m just sitting on the floor, too exhausted to move.

Two weeks ago, I thought I was going to have a total breakdown. Back then, looking at the piles of books, cameras, and flea market junk cluttering the room made me feel like I couldn't breathe. Every single item felt like evidence of failure—unfinished photography projects, books abandoned halfway through, things I thought were meaningful that ended up being nothing but trash. I didn't even want to look at them.

Old Man Zhang knocked on my door that afternoon and asked how the renovation plan was going. I remember how cold my voice was when I told him it was all useless, a complete waste of time. He didn't offer any words of comfort; he just smiled and said, "Sometimes we need to stop and really look at these things, instead of rushing to throw them away."

Today, I finally understand what he meant.

I picked up a book on photography aesthetics I bought back in 1995 and flipped to the bookmarked pages. Those notes were mine; those ideas were mine. This isn't trash. This is a record of my thoughts.

Then I picked up my camera, which had been sitting there for over a month. There was a bit of dust on the lens, but it wasn't broken. I looked at it and remembered the photos I used to take—corners of the city, the profiles of strangers, the way light filters through old buildings. Maybe I should start shooting again. Not to complete some project, not to prove anything. Just... shoot.

Maybe these things aren't symbols of my failure. Maybe they are just... my things. My choices. My attempts.

Maybe freedom isn't something I’ve wasted. Maybe it’s just... not over yet.

Maybe I can still do something.

This "maybe" is tiny and fragile. But this afternoon, sitting here among all these belongings and looking at my camera, I feel like it might be real.

I don't know what tomorrow will bring. But at least for today, I haven't completely given up.
