# Friday, April 18, 2025 – Sunny

My passport arrived.

I picked it up from the post office yesterday afternoon. I held that kraft paper envelope in my hands for a long time, just looking at it. When I flipped it open, there was my face—an ID photo taken five years ago. I was wearing a navy blue sweater, my expression a bit stiff. Looking at that photo now feels like looking at a stranger.

The passport is real. What does that mean? It means I’m not dreaming. It means I am actually going to see my brother.

I’ve written "I’m going" in this diary many times, but back then it was more of an imagination. It’s different now. Now I have this little blue booklet with my name, my date of birth, and the seal of the Chinese government. This is no longer a "maybe"; it’s a "certainty."

I’m a little scared.

It’s not the flight—though I’ve never been on one that long. It’s not the luggage or the jet lag. What I’m afraid of is that when I finally see him, I’ll find we don't actually have much to say. We haven’t had a deep conversation in over a decade. He lives over there, with his friends, his job, his lifestyle. I’m over here, with my books, my salons, my solitude. Are we still the same two people who grew up in the same household? Or have we become two strangers who just happen to share the same parents and childhood memories?

And yet, I’m full of anticipation. I’m looking forward to seeing his face, hearing him speak, sitting together for tea or a walk. I hope he can understand me, just like when we were kids. I hope someone can truly *see* me—not as "Lin Xiaoyun, the freelance editor," or "that woman who attends cultural salons," but as his sister.

Professor Chen said last time that I should treat this trip as a new beginning. I laughed then, saying, "What new beginning? I’m 62 years old." But now I’m thinking, maybe he was right. It’s not a new beginning, but a turning point. I’ve spent so many years affirming my freedom, refusing to be tied down. Now I’m doing the opposite—I’m spending money, time, and energy on something that isn't "rational" for my bank account or my schedule.

I am becoming a person with a plan. It makes me feel both relieved and uneasy.

The passport is sitting on the bookshelf, right next to *Life and Death Are Wearing Me Out*. Every now and then, I glance at it to make sure it’s still there. To make sure all of this is real.

I’m not sure if I’m truly ready. But then again, I suppose no one ever really is. Perhaps it’s in this very uncertainty that we must do the things that make us feel alive.

I’m going to the book club at the bookstore tonight. Maybe I’ll tell them about it. Maybe I won’t. I’m not sure yet.

I sit in my darkened bedroom; outside the window, everything is still silent. The glow of my phone screen stings my eyes. I open WeChat, then close it. Then open it again.

Finally, I find the chat group.

---

**Sent to: Cultural Salon Friends**

It happened again. Just after 3:00 AM. My heart started racing, and I felt like I couldn't breathe—like something was pressing down on my chest. I’ve been sitting here on the edge of the bed in the dark for an hour, too afraid to move.

I know you all told me to see a doctor. I did; I made an appointment. But... be honest with me. If the doctor finishes the checkup and says "your heart is fine," does that mean the problem is really in here? (Pointing to my head)

I don’t know which is scarier.

This is the second night in a row. I’m okay during the day, just exhausted. But as soon as the fatigue sets in, I start thinking, and as soon as I start thinking, I get scared. And then at night... well, you know.

I’m not trying to freak you guys out. It’s just... I need to ask someone. Do you think there’s actually something wrong with me? Or am I just being too sensitive, scaring myself over nothing?

I know how stupid this question sounds. My head is just a mess right now.

---

I stare at the message for a long time. The delete button is right there. My finger hovers over the screen.

In the end, I hit send.

The sky hasn't brightened yet, but I can no longer bear to sit in the dark alone.

At tonight’s salon, a question came up: do we truly need to change ourselves to fit into the world, or should we insist on being that "lonely person"?

A friend said that perhaps the problem isn't loneliness itself, but how we perceive it. Loneliness can be a curse, or it can be an invitation—an invitation to reflect on what kind of connection is actually authentic.

It reminded me of something I’ve been working on lately. I’m planning a trip to see someone I haven’t spoken deeply with in years. To be honest, I’m scared. Scared of the awkwardness, scared of disappointment, and scared of discovering that we no longer have a common language. But I’ve decided to go anyway.

Maybe that’s what courage is. It’s not the absence of fear, but the choice to act in spite of it.

Thank you to my friends tonight. Your stories showed me that we are all, in our own ways, answering the same question: how do I want to live?

# Memo

**Date: November 26, Afternoon**
**Location: At home, at my desk**

---

Just got back from the book club. My head is still buzzing.

A young girl asked me how one can maintain freedom while still feeling secure. I heard myself say: "True freedom is the selective acceptance of order."

The moment the words left my mouth, I froze. Where did that come from?

Thinking about it now, it seems like something I’ve only come to understand slowly over the years. I always thought freedom meant being unconstrained, which is why I turned down so many full-time job offers. But the instability of these past few years—the irregular income, the lack of discipline, the projects that never seem to end—is that really freedom? Or is it just another form of being trapped?

Maybe order isn't the enemy of freedom. Maybe order is its foundation.

It’s like right now, as I’m organizing these old objects, old books, and old stories—I’m realizing that having a clear framework actually allows me to understand and share them better. The limitations have actually opened something up.

It’s so strange. I spent so many years running away from restrictions, only to find now that certain forms of order make me feel... free?

No, it’s not order itself. It’s the **selective** acceptance of it. That’s the key. I choose when to work, I choose who to collaborate with, and I choose how to organize my life. That choice, in itself, is freedom.

It reminds me of my brother’s letter. He said he’s always done what he chose to do. Maybe that’s what we have in common—this hunger for autonomy. It just took me this long to admit that autonomy needs some kind of structure to truly exist.

Should I include this in the essay collection? The idea is too fresh; I haven’t decided yet. But I need to at least write it down.

Maybe tomorrow I’ll look at those manuscripts I’ve been putting off. Not because I have to, but because I want to.

---

*P.S. Passed by that old bookstore again while getting coffee and spent half an hour browsing. I bought a collection of aesthetics essays from the 1980s. The previous owner wrote their name and the date on the title page. I wonder, if someone ever stumbles upon my writing one day, what will they think?*

Professor Chen, I’ve been meaning to talk to you about something lately.

A while back, I was tidying up my room and came across some articles and reviews I’ve written over the years. To be honest, I initially thought they were all outdated—who even reads these kinds of "paper-based" reflections in the internet age? But after reading through them again, I realized some of the ideas are actually still quite interesting—things about culture, reading, and social change.

It’s not that these pieces are anything extraordinary, but... I want to know if these scattered thoughts can be integrated into something more systematic.

I’d like to get your take on this. If I were to actually go through with it, where do you think would be a reasonable place to start? Should I be revising these old articles, or should I write some new prefaces to tie them all together? Do you happen to have any contacts at publishing houses?

I know this might seem a bit unrealistic for a freelance writer, but I want to try. At the very least, I want to confirm whether my work over these years has truly been of any value.

If you have some time, could you take a look for me?

Professor Chen, I wanted to share something that happened at today’s reading group.

During the discussion session this afternoon, a young girl—likely a junior or senior—suddenly asked me, "Professor Lin, why do you always talk about heavy topics like death and meaning? Does doing this really help us live better lives?"

To be honest, I was momentarily caught off guard. I could feel the genuine doubt in her voice, and I understand why someone in their early twenties would find these subjects a bit depressing. But instead of giving her a "correct answer," I paused for a moment and told her:

"We don't talk about death to be profound; we talk about it to live with greater clarity."

I told her I wasn't trying to scare anyone, but rather to help us see what truly matters. If a person never contemplates the fininitude of life, they often get trapped by trivialities. But if we can face this fact calmly, we actually become freer—clearer about what we want, who we want to be with, and what we want to create.

After the event ended, the girl came up to talk to me. We spoke for a long time. She shared some of her own confusion, and I just listened intently without trying to provide answers. Finally, she said, "I think I understand now. You’re not talking about death itself; you’re talking about... what it means to have truly lived."

My eyes welled up a bit at that moment.

It reminded me of when you suggested I publish my collection of essays, and I was still hesitating, wondering if anyone would even want to read them. But today made me realize that it’s not a question of "marketability." It’s about whether it can help someone see things a little more clearly. That girl’s question, and her eventual understanding, is proof enough of the value of this kind of dialogue.

I think this is the real reason we do what we do. It’s not to appear deep, but to help another person live a more conscious life at a certain moment in time.

What do you think?

April 26, 2025, 10:30 PM

Lying in bed, phone resting on my chest. The city sounds outside the window are finally fading, but sleep feels miles away.

It’s not about the trip anymore. The passport, the tickets, all those details I’ve been obsessively checking for the past two weeks—they seem trivial now. In their place is this strange, warm restlessness.

I sent that long WeChat message to Lin Hao this afternoon. I sat at my desk for nearly an hour, typing and deleting, deleting and typing. It started with polite small talk like "How have you been lately?" but in the end, I just asked it: "Have you been happy over there all these years? Why haven't we actually talked in so long?"

My hand was shaking when I hit send. It felt like doing something incredibly brave, yet incredibly stupid.

His reply came quickly. Only three sentences, but I’ve read them at least ten times.

"I’ve been wanting to talk to you too, but I didn't know how to start."

I’ve been lying here for a long time, just thinking about that one sentence.

All these years, I assumed he didn't care. Or rather, I pretended that *I* didn't care. With so many years and so much silence between us, I treated it as a natural drifting apart—people grow up, they go their separate ways, it’s normal. I even took pride in my independence. Not needing anyone, not needing to be needed.

But he said he’d been wanting to talk, too.

What does that mean? Does it mean all these years of silence weren't because of indifference, but because we were both waiting? Both afraid to be the first to speak?

I’m lying here in the dark now, imagining a conversation with him. Not the polite, "checking-in" kind, but a real one—the kind where you actually say what’s on your mind. Do we have that much to say? Or have we long since run out of things to talk about?

I don't know. But at least for now, that thought is occupying my mind far more than "How long will my savings last?" or "Is it safe to go to a strange place alone?"

It’s strange. I thought I’d gotten used to being lonely. But now, a possible connection—even just the beginning of one—is enough to shift the entire direction of my anxiety.

I feel a little proud of myself. I also feel a little scared.

Maybe I really do need this trip. Not just to see the world, but to see my brother. To see if, at our age, we can still start something over.

My phone screen just lit up. Another message from Lin Hao.

I don’t have the courage to open it yet.

Professor Chen said something today that I’ve been turning over in my mind ever since.

She said, "Life itself is the story; words are merely the record. But sometimes, the act of recording is what changes the life."

We sat in the café for two hours. She seemed to listen so intently as I talked through the things I’ve been dwelling on—about distance, about time, and about those connections we thought we had missed. I didn't expect to find myself saying so much, things I’ve never told anyone, all in a single afternoon.

Finally, she asked me, "And what about you? How do you plan to record all of this?"

I don’t have an answer yet. But I know I need to write these thoughts down. Not for publication, not for any particular reason, just... because I need to.

Sometimes, being seen and truly heard gives you a strange kind of courage. Not the courage to change something, but the courage to acknowledge it.

Lately, I’ve found myself looking forward to something new. Maybe it’s a new direction in my writing, or perhaps a new way of understanding. Nothing is clear yet, but that very sense of haze is what makes me feel excited.

Grateful for those who are willing to sit and listen when you’re feeling lost.

# MEMORANDUM

**Date: November 3, 2024, Afternoon**

---

## The Paradox of Consistency

I just got off the phone with Professor Chen. She said the preliminary framework for the essay collection has been discussed with the publisher. I should be happy, but a question is stuck in my head—

By putting these pieces together, will I come across as inconsistent?

It’s been fifteen years. The commentary I wrote in 2009 about the anxieties of modernity and the article I wrote in 2023 about urban memory are worlds apart in stance, tone, and even value judgment. Back then, I was angrier. Now, I am more... contemplative? Or am I just more exhausted?

**Questions to Ponder:**
- Consistency vs. Evolution—where is the line between the two?
- If my thoughts are constantly shifting, is the very notion of "my viewpoint" a lie?
- Will readers think I’m contradicting myself? Or rather, *should* they think that?
- Affectation vs. Sincerity—when did I start using these two words to judge my own writing?

---

## Issues to Address in the Introduction

Perhaps I shouldn't hide this inconsistency. Perhaps I should confront it head-on—just say it: my thoughts have indeed changed over these fifteen years. My understanding of culture, society, and myself has evolved. This isn't a flaw; it’s... what? Honesty? Or just an admission of my own mediocrity?

Professor Chen calls it an "intellectual journey." But I’m not sure if my journey is a meaningful progression or if I’m just circling back on myself.

---

## Technical Issues (Which Are Actually Philosophical)

- Should I add the year of writing to each article?
- Should I write footnotes to explain shifts in thought?
- Or should I arrange them by theme rather than chronologically? (Would that hide the inconsistency? But wouldn't that also be a form of dishonesty?)

---

## The Deep-Seated Fear

What does publishing this book actually mean? It means fixing all my contradictions, all the moments I changed my mind, and all the "I used to think this, but I don't anymore" instances into print for others to see.

It feels like I’m building an archive for my thoughts. Or perhaps, a tombstone.

---

## To-Research List

- How do the introductions of other essay collections handle this issue?
- Have any theorists discussed the problem of "intellectual consistency"?
- Should I actually read through all the articles instead of just judging them by memory?

---

**Think more about this tonight. My head hurts right now.**

After today’s lecture, a girl came up to me and said she never imagined a talk could be like this—not a speaker lecturing from the stage while the audience takes notes, but everyone thinking together.

To be honest, I decided to change the format on a whim. Midway through, I noticed people’s eyes starting to wander, and I thought: why not just ask them what’s on their minds? Once the floor opened up, the whole atmosphere came alive. People brought in completely different perspectives, shared their own stories, and even asked questions I had never considered before.

For the final hour, I barely "lectured" at all. I was just listening, responding, and thinking alongside everyone. It was exhausting, but the kind of exhaustion that feels deeply fulfilling.

Doing these lectures and book clubs over the past few months, I’ve slowly realized something—I’m not actually "teaching" anything; we are exploring together. This feeling is so much more real than writing articles alone in my study.

Thank you to all the friends who came. And I look forward to more of you joining the discussions ahead.

June 20, 2022. Past 3:00 AM.

I don’t know how to start writing this. My hands are still shaking.

Yesterday afternoon in the cafe, when I opened my inbox, I saw my brother’s reply. The subject line was "Re: Little Sister." Just like that. Simple. My fingers completely froze in that moment; the cursor hovered there, and I couldn't even bring myself to click it right away. I just sat there, staring at those three words, feeling like I was suffocating.

Then I opened it.

"Little Sister, I was so happy to receive your letter. I miss you too."

Just those few words. But I cried right there in the cafe. I just cried. I didn’t hide, I didn’t run to the restroom, I didn’t try to conceal it. The tears just fell, and I didn’t even reach to wipe them away. There was a girl looking at me, and an elderly gentleman—I could feel their eyes on me, but I didn't care at all. This feeling of indifference was strange—the old me would have been so self-conscious about how others saw me, but in that moment, I just wanted to cry.

I took a screenshot on my phone. Then another. And another. After I got home, I printed it out. Now, the printed version of that email is sitting on my desk, right next to my notebook. I keep looking at it, as if I’m afraid it might vanish. Virtual things are too surreal. I need it to be real. I need to be able to touch it with my fingers.

He said, "I miss you too."

Those words are echoing in my head over and over. What does it mean? Does it mean that all these years, he’s been missing me too? Then why did we waste so much time? Why did we let this longing turn into silence? I’m 62 now. He isn’t young anymore either. How much time do we have left?

But at the same time, I feel like maybe it’s not too late. Maybe it really isn't.

I’ve been thinking about all those years I chose to live alone, chose "freedom," chose to keep everyone at a distance. I thought that was freedom. But what was it, really? In truth, I was just running away. I ran away from my connection with him, just as I ran away from so many other things. And he—he was there all along, missing me too.

I don’t know how to reply to him. I’ve written a dozen versions of an email, but I haven't sent any of them. Every version seems like too much or too little. Too much explanation, too little sincerity. Or the other way around.

It’s 3:00 AM now, and I’m still awake. My heart hasn’t settled down yet. I look at that printout, staring at those words: "I miss you too."

I think that maybe my life hasn't been as lonely as I imagined. Maybe some connections, even in silence, never truly break.

I need to get some sleep. But I can't bring myself to put this piece of paper down.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something with you before our next session.

Yesterday, I dug up a book review I wrote back in 2010 about *To Live*. At the time, I’d been away from my corporate job for two or three years and was still adjusting to life as a freelancer. Reading my own words from back then, I expected to feel embarrassed—to find the perspectives naive and the writing clumsy.

But strangely, I didn't. Instead, I was actually a bit moved.

I wrote a passage in that piece: "The value of a life doesn't lie in how many people see it, but in how you choose to see it yourself." I was writing for a small magazine back then; the pay was tiny, and almost no one read it. But re-reading it now, I realize I was actually saying those words for myself.

I’ve been wondering lately: have I become too dependent on external validation over the last decade? Every time I miss a deadline, every time a book club has a low turnout, every time a photo I post gets no likes, I feel completely worthless. But that article reminded me—I never started writing those things to be judged.

So, I wanted to ask you: does this mean... I don't need external recognition to define my worth? Or am I just lying to myself, using "inner meaning" to rationalize my failures?

I’m not sure. But the thought alone has made it a little easier to breathe this morning.

11:45 PM. I can’t sleep.

It’s been three days since I received Lin Hao’s email. I’ve read it five times over, and my heart races every single time. He said he wants to video call and asked if I’m free this weekend. It was a brief email, but there was a sense of caution between the lines—as if we’re both testing the waters to see if the other is still willing to get close.

I replied saying okay. And then the insomnia started.

Just after 3:00 AM, I got up and stood in front of the mirror for a long time. I was trying to find the "right" expression. It sounds ridiculous, I know. But I just kept wondering: when he sees my face, will he feel disappointed? Do I look too old? Too tired? I caught myself wondering if I should wear makeup, change my hair, or dress more formally... then I realized what I was doing. I wanted to laugh, and I wanted to cry.

I wrote "Don't bring up past regrets" in my notebook. I thought we could just talk about the present and avoid those awkward silences. But after writing it, I crossed it out.

Why avoid it? So many years have passed between us. If we don’t even dare to talk about the past, what else is there to talk about? Maybe it’s precisely because of those years that this call has become so important. Maybe we need to acknowledge the things we left unsaid, the moments we missed.

When Mom passed away, he didn’t come back. I understood his situation—he was overseas, busy with work, plane tickets were expensive. But still... I still felt a bit of resentment. Looking back now, that resentment has lingered for so long that I’ve almost forgotten the reason for it; I only remember the feeling of being abandoned.

Dad has been gone for sixteen years. It’s been nearly ten since Mom passed. I can’t get back the years between Lin Hao and me. If I don’t take action now, if I keep hiding behind this safe distance, then... then maybe there will never be another chance.

I don’t know how this call will go. Will we have anything in common? Will we fall into awkward silences? Will we end up arguing?

But what scares me more is the fear that I’ve lost the ability to maintain a connection. I’ve grown used to being alone. I’ve grown used to talking to myself in the middle of the night instead of communicating with a real person. What if I come across as stiff or cold during the call? What if he senses that I’m actually afraid of getting close?

In his email, he mentioned inviting me over to his place. He said he wants me to meet his children. I haven't even met my own nephew and niece. The thought both excites and terrifies me. I need to check my passport. I need to see how much money I can save. I need to...

I need to believe that this is possible.

It’s almost midnight now. I should sleep. But I know I’ll just lie in bed, playing out tomorrow’s conversation over and over in my head. I’ll check the internet connection. I’ll adjust the camera angle. I’ll change my mind at the last minute, and then change it back.

Whatever. I’ll stop here. Maybe writing this down will help calm me.

Or maybe it won't. But at least I tried.

I don’t know what this proves. Maybe it shows that I’m not entirely without value? Maybe the definition of value really isn't just about whether the market needs it or not?

I paused for a long time.

For years, I’ve been constantly questioning myself—does my writing have any meaning? Are my thoughts deep enough? Am I just wasting my time? But perhaps that was never the point. Perhaps the meaning doesn't lie in what we say, but in whether we truly hear one another.

In that moment, I realized I don’t need to be some great critic. I just need to be here—reading, thinking, making mistakes, and changing my mind alongside everyone else. That, in itself, is enough.

Grateful to those who are willing to sit here with me.

**Private Journal | June 24, 2025, 10:15 PM**

Hao and I sat in a cafe for a long time this morning. He asked me why I decided to come this time, saying he could sense something "different" in my emails. I didn't give him a straight answer; instead, I asked him why we hadn't had a real conversation in all these years.

He was silent for a moment, then said he didn't know. Maybe it was the distance, maybe we were both just too busy, or maybe it was that awkwardness of "not knowing where to start."

I remembered a sentence I once wrote in my diary: *True freedom is having the courage to choose what is worth committing to.* At the time, I was thinking about work and lifestyle. But sitting across from him, I suddenly realized that this applies to relationships, too.

I’ve spent all these years running away. Running from being tied down, from failed marriages, from the fear of growing old. I understood freedom as "non-commitment." But now I see clearly that it wasn't freedom; it was just hiding.

True freedom should be this: knowing what I want, and then having the courage to pay the price for it.

I paid a price for this trip—I pushed back two deadlines and lost some commission fees. But what did I gain? I gained the experience of sitting across from my brother, listening to him talk about his daughter’s school life, his frustrations at work, and how he has actually been missing me all this time. It’s that feeling of "we still have so much to say to each other." It’s understanding that the opposite of loneliness isn't "the absence of loneliness," but "meaningful togetherness."

When Mom passed away, I wondered about the meaning of life. I did the same when Dad died. But I never had a real answer. Now, I think the answer might be just this—to do the things that make you feel alive. To see the people who matter. To commit to the things worth committing to.

I don't know what will happen between Hao and me after this trip. Maybe we’ll go back to our separate lives, maybe not. But at least for now, we both know the other is still there, still thinking of each other. That’s enough.

Tomorrow we’re going to the park they always visit. I want to take some photos. Not to post on social media, but just to capture this moment. To record the way we are starting over.

 I feel a bit tired, but it’s a good kind of tired. Like I’ve finally stopped running.

I’m sorry to be messaging you this late... I know you’re probably already asleep.

I received an email from my brother. He said he wants me to go stay with him for a while, and that he wants me to meet my nephew and niece. I’ve read it five times now, and my heart races every single time. It’s a bit ridiculous, isn't it?

I can’t sleep. I’m just tossing and turning in bed, and my head is full of questions like "How do we even start talking?" or "What am I supposed to say?" I even searched "how to make a video call" online—it’s so embarrassing. I’ve been an editor for 25 years, yet I don’t know how to talk to my own brother.

It’s been so long since we’ve actually talked. I mean, *really* talked. Not those polite New Year’s greetings, but... I don’t know how to describe it. The kind of conversation that makes you feel like the other person still knows who you are. I’m terrified that when I see him, I’ll realize we’ve become strangers. Or worse, I’m scared he’ll realize I’ve turned into a lonely, somewhat stubborn old woman.

But at the same time, I really want to go. I want to see his children. I want to hear about what he’s been doing all these years. I want to know if he’s been thinking of me, too.

Do you think I’m overthinking this? That’s my problem—always coming up with a million things at three in the morning.

Sorry, I’m really sorry for bothering you so late. I just... needed to get this out.

April 29, 2022, Afternoon

It’s been two hours since I left the clinic. I’m sitting in the café downstairs; my coffee went cold a long time ago.

"Generalized Anxiety Disorder with Adjustment Disorder"—I keep staring at those eight words on the diagnosis. Dr. Li said them in such a gentle tone, but the words felt like they were being nailed into me. I asked her how accurate the diagnosis was, if there was any chance of a misdiagnosis. She just gave a small smile and said it was based on our sessions and the scales I filled out.

The scales. I filled out a questionnaire, and just like that, I was given a label.

She suggested medication. Anti-anxiety meds. I refused immediately—my attitude was probably a bit sharp. I told her I didn’t want to take pills, that drugs would alter my way of thinking, and that I needed to resolve this through my own efforts. She didn’t push; she just said we could try meditation and yoga first.

But now I’m wondering, why did I react so violently?

I lingered in the clinic’s stairwell for three minutes. I just stood there, reading those four words over and over. I could feel my breathing quicken, like something was pressing down on my chest. I suppose that’s exactly what she meant by "anxiety symptoms." A diagnosis that immediately validates itself. It’s ironic.

I know what I’m doing. I’m using rationality to escape. Using knowledge, analysis, and skepticism of medical authority to dodge a simple fact: I am actually ill. Or rather, something is actually wrong with my brain.

Not "maybe" wrong. Actually wrong.

What does taking medication mean? It means admitting that I cannot control my own thoughts through willpower alone. It means relying on some external chemical substance. It took me sixty-two years to become who I am—this way of thinking, this sensitivity, this obsession with detail; all of this is me. If medication changes my thoughts, who is it changing? Is it still me?

But I also know that this thought itself is absurd. I’m overthinking. This is exactly what Dr. Li said—I’m always analyzing, theorizing, turning everything into an intellectual problem instead of just feeling it.

I don’t want to admit that I need help. I’ve always relied on myself.

Now I’m sitting here, looking at this cup of cold coffee, remembering that I have two book reviews due. The deadline is tomorrow. I should go home and write, but I can’t write a single word right now.

Maybe I should call Dr. Li and ask how to use that meditation app. Maybe I should consider the medication. Maybe I should accept the fact that I am not omnipotent.

But for now, I don’t want to do anything. I just want to sit here and let this diagnosis slowly sink in.

Professor Chen, something interesting happened today that I wanted to share with you.

During my session with Dr. Li this afternoon, she made a suggestion—that perhaps we don’t need to continue our regular, scheduled consultations anymore. At first, I felt a bit anxious, wondering if something had gone wrong. But she explained that she’s noticed I’ve built a very complete support system recently, and this system is actually already helping me process a lot of things.

She listed a few examples: the book club, the lectures, our discussions, and even the fact that I’ve reconnected with my brother lately. I was a bit stunned as I listened—I had never looked at these things as being connected like that. I always thought of them as separate, independent activities, but she said they are actually part of a whole.

That realization brought me a sense of relief. It’s not that I no longer need help, but rather that I’ve realized I already know how to seek it. More importantly, I’m not dealing with things in isolation. You, my friends from the book club, my brother—these conversations and connections between us are all there.

I think my previous understanding of "independence" was a bit off. I used to think being independent meant not relying on anyone, but it should actually mean knowing what you need, knowing when to ask for help, and having the capacity to build and maintain those connections.

Your role in this system is very important, you know? Not just because we discuss culture and ideas, but because these discussions are a form of support in themselves. They make me feel understood and give my thoughts a place to resonate.

A lot has happened this month, but this might be the one realization that has made me feel the most grounded.

# May 15, 2023, 9:00 PM

I’m sitting here among piles of books writing this. The lamp in the room is a bit dim, but I can’t be bothered to get up and turn on the overhead light.

Another day spent spinning my wheels. I opened my inbox this morning to find an editor telling me that my piece "doesn't quite fit the current readers' tastes." I read through it again and thought it was actually pretty good, but they just don't want it. Then this afternoon, I got a new commission—3,000 words, but the fee they’re offering is the same as it was ten years ago. Ten years ago! My rent has gone up, the cost of living has gone up, yet my value is somehow depreciating.

What am I even thinking? Honestly.

I organized two sessions for that book club this month. People showed up; people actually listened to what I had to say. At the time, I felt quite happy, like I could still contribute something. But thinking about it now, what does it even matter? A group of strangers gathering to chat, and then what? Then I come back to sit in this run-down apartment, watching my savings slowly being eaten away by daily life. I’m 62. It’s FOREVER TOO LATE.

I’m not self-pitying. I’m just stating facts.

I sorted through those photographs, thinking maybe I could enter them into an exhibition. But looking at them now, they suddenly feel so hypocritical. All that talk about "urban loneliness" or "the poetry of light and shadow"—it’s all a sham. I’m just an old woman wandering around with a camera, trying to slap an "artistic" label on my own idleness.

And what about my knowledge? Everything I learned during those years at the publishing house, the books I’ve read, the reviews I’ve written—it’s all worthless. Some random influencer born in the 90s has more impact than I do. A lifetime of accumulation, and now I can’t even trade it for a decent freelance fee.

I am a failure.

But the strange thing is, thinking this way actually brings me a sense of peace. It’s as if all expectations have been dropped, all efforts have become meaningless, and I can finally stop struggling. This peace is comfortable. It’s dangerous, I know. But it’s very comfortable.

Maybe this is just how it is. Maybe I don't need to pretend I still have "possibilities" anymore.

I’m going to sleep. I have to write that 3,000-word piece tomorrow, even though I already know it won’t change a thing.

Found an old letter while clearing out some old belongings; the handwriting has faded a bit. Some things from those years I’ve already forgiven; others, I’m only just beginning to understand.

Time is a strange thing—it washes so much away, yet brings certain things into sharper focus. The things I once considered regrets now seem like essential parts of how I became who I am today.

It’s not necessarily about letting go, but finally being able to coexist with them. Like these old objects, they don’t need to be discarded or enshrined; they just need to be seen and understood.

Distance changes perspective. Sometimes the best connections come from the places that once felt the furthest away.

Dr. Li, I don’t really know how to start. Something happened yesterday while I was tidying up my room, and I’m still feeling… it’s hard to describe.

I opened the first cardboard box. It was filled with things from 1995—photography, journals, and just various bits and pieces. I had intended to go through it quickly, but I ended up sitting on the floor, flipping through everything for a long, long time.

You know, I came across a sentence in my diary—written in my younger handwriting: "I want to be an editor who truly changes the publishing industry." As I looked at those words, my eyes welled up. It wasn't out of being moved; it was just… a very strange feeling. I realized that this goal was never achieved. I worked as an editor for 25 years, and then I quit. And then what? And then, this is it.

I started wondering, if I were to die right now, what would happen to all this? These photos, these journals, the second-hand books and antiques I’ve collected—who would care about the stories behind them? My brother is overseas; we haven't had a real conversation in years. My parents have passed away. My friends have their own lives. Do these things, these memories, just vanish like that?

I’ve started questioning all my life choices. I always felt I was pursuing freedom, but now, looking at this cluttered room, I’m wondering—is this really freedom? Or have I just been running away? I turned down so many full-time job offers to stick with freelancing, but what do I have now? An unstable income, a life with no structure, sitting here alone in the middle of all this stuff.

I also dug out some cultural critiques I wrote when I was younger. Re-reading them, I realized they’re actually quite good. But at the same time, I thought—what’s the point? In this internet age, who still needs critiques like mine?

I’m not looking for you to give me answers. I just… I needed to say it somewhere. I’m alone in this room, and there’s no one to go through this with me.

How am I supposed to face all these unfinished things? Should I just throw it all away, or should I… I don’t know.

Hao, there’s something I’ve been wanting to say to you for a long time.

At the book club today, someone asked me if I was afraid of this trip. I answered without thinking too much, but the moment the words came out, I surprised even myself. I said: "I understand now that the best way to live is to do the things that make me feel alive."

I’ve been thinking about that sentence ever since. At 62, to still be able to think this way, to be packing my bags and changing my plans just to see you… the person I was a few years ago couldn't have imagined this. Back then, I was always calculating—counting how much time I had left, weighing what I was still capable of doing. Anxiety was eating away at me; I didn't even dare to imagine what it would be like to meet you.

But things are different now.

I’m not saying I’m no longer afraid. You know what kind of person I am—I’m still the one who lies awake at 3:00 AM overthinking a single sentence or over-analyzing a news report. But fear and being alive don't necessarily have to be at odds with each other. I’ve learned how to coexist with my anxiety instead of letting it drown me.

This trip is about more than just seeing you. It’s true, we haven’t had a real conversation in far too long, and I regret that. But more importantly, I want you to know that I am ready. Not just in terms of packing bags and booking flights, but mentally and spiritually ready. I’m not as afraid of loss or loneliness anymore. I’ve built real connections here—with friends, with my community, and with myself.

I wanted to ask, have you been thinking about similar things? About life, about time, about the distance between us? I don’t know what your state of mind is right now. But I hope that when we meet, we can talk about these things honestly, like two adults.

The ticket is booked. I’m almost finished packing.

Looking forward to seeing you.

# Memo: Progress on the Essay Collection Project

**Date: December 25, 10:15 PM**

---

## Selection Criteria (Still figuring it out)

I’ve gone through them once using sticky notes. Here’s where things stand:
- **Green (Confirmed):** About 8–10 pieces? Mostly those that are relatively complete with clear arguments. For example, the one on *One Hundred Years of Solitude* and the few pieces reflecting on the publishing industry in the internet age.
- **Yellow (Undecided):** This is the bulk of it, maybe around 15 pieces. What’s the issue?
  - Some have good points but are too long; they feel a bit redundant now.
  - Some are quick commentaries—lacking depth, yet capturing a certain "spirit of the times."
  - Some cover topics that seem a bit dated now. Or is that exactly where their value lies—as a record of that era?
- **Red (Discard):** 5–7 pieces, mostly early works that are honestly just not well-written.

**Key Question:** What exactly is the criterion for "selection"?
- Representativeness? Or quality?
- If it’s representativeness, should I keep some of the more radical or controversial pieces?
- If it’s quality, how do I define "quality"? My definition of a "good article" today is different from what it was 15 years ago.

**Decision:** Organize the "Green" ones first, then re-read the "Yellow" ones. I might need to discuss this with Professor Chen.

---

## How to write the Introduction?

This is even more of a headache. A few ideas:

**Option A: Chronological**
- Start from leaving the publishing house in 2007.
- Review the shifts in my thinking over these 15 years.
- Problem: Will it be too autobiographical? Readers might not care about my personal history.

**Option B: Thematic**
- Group the essays into themes (literary criticism, cultural observations, thoughts on publishing, etc.).
- Write a mini-intro for each theme.
- Problem: Then it’s not one introduction, but several. It doubles the workload.

**Option C: A "Reflective" Introduction**
- Directly confront how my understanding of "cultural criticism" has evolved over the last 15 years.
- Be honest about certain confusions: What did I believe when I wrote these? Do I still believe it now?
- Problem: Will it come across as insecure? The publisher might worry that such an intro would hurt sales.

**Preference:** Maybe a mix of A and C? But I haven't figured out the specifics of how to blend them.

---

## Communication with the Publisher

I have a call with the editor next week. Things to confirm:
- [ ] Final word count/article count—30 pieces or trim it down to 25?
- [ ] Timeline—when is the first draft due?
- [ ] Format of the introduction—do they have any suggestions?
- [ ] Do any articles need revisions, or should they be published as originally written?
- [ ] Details on cover design, publication date, etc.

**Internal Monologue:** I’m a bit afraid they’ll say "this project might not be feasible" or "the market outlook is unclear." If they say that, what will I do? Persist or give up?

---

## Timeline (Subject to change)

- **By end of Dec:** Finalize the selection (target: 30 pieces).
- **Mid-Jan:** Hand in the first draft of the introduction.
- **By end of Jan:** Revise based on editor feedback.
- **Feb:** Finalize manuscript.
- **March–April:** Layout and proofreading.
- **May:** Publication?

But this timeline is optimistic. I know my habits—I always push deadlines. I probably need to build in more buffer time.

**The Likely Reality:**
- Selection might take until mid-January.
- Introduction might not be ready until February.
- The rest... we'll see.

---

## Lingering Questions

1. Once this book is out, will anyone actually read it?
2. If no one reads it, how disappointed will I be?
3. Why am I so set on publishing these as a book? Is it because of Professor Chen’s suggestion, or because I truly want to?
4. Will this project interfere with my freelance work? How much time do I need to sink into this?
5. What does publishing this book mean to me? Is it a summary of past work, or a final validation of my professional value?

**11:00 PM Update:** Just did some more thinking while lying in bed. I might be overthinking this. Tomorrow, I’ll start by re-organizing the sticky notes and re-reading the "Yellow" ones. Some of those pieces aren't as bad as I remembered.

Maybe I just need to give myself a hard deadline and force myself to move forward. Otherwise, if I keep spiraling like this, nothing will get done.

# January 22, 2024, 9:15 PM

It’s that time again, right before the insomnia kicks in. Instead of reaching for the sleeping pills, I’m sitting at my desk with a list of articles I’ve compiled over the last few days spread out in front of me.

To be honest, I still can’t quite believe what I’m doing. Publishing a collection of essays? The idea has been rattling around my head for nearly two weeks. It started as "this is way too presumptuous," but now it’s evolved into a real plan with specific dates and an editor’s name. Dr. Li told me last time, "Maybe you need to channel your anxiety into action." I rolled my eyes at her in the clinic, thinking it sounded like typical self-help fluff. But now… maybe she was onto something.

I’ve listed twenty-three pieces. They range from a 2010 book review of *To Live* to an essay I wrote last year about memory and forgetting. Looking at this list, it hit me—I didn’t write these for the paychecks or to satisfy a publisher’s requirements. I wrote them because I truly had something to say. Each one represents my understanding of the world at a specific moment in time—or rather, my understanding of myself.

I’ve finished a rough draft of the preface. One sentence is still sitting there on my screen: "These essays are my understanding of the world. They may not change anything, but they have changed me."

I cried when I finished writing that. It’s strange, because I’m not someone who cries easily. But in that moment, I suddenly understood—for years, I’ve been worrying about whether my value is being recognized, whether I’m still marketable, whether I can still make money. This project has shown me another possibility: maybe value doesn't lie in those things at all. Maybe the value lies simply in the fact that I thought, I wrote, and I tried to understand.

It makes me think of my mother. She worked in that library for so many years and never felt her work was meaningless, despite the meager pay and the lack of gratitude. She was just there, quietly organizing books and helping strangers find what they needed. I’m only now realizing that she wasn't teaching me how to be successful; she was teaching me how to give my own work meaning.

There’s been another strange shift. I find I’m not as afraid of death anymore—at least, not that old medical fear of "what if I just drop dead." The fear now is more… philosophical? I’m afraid of a meaningless life. And this collection, in a way, is like saying: "Look, I lived, I thought, I left something behind."

Professor Chen said he could put me in touch with an editor at the publishing house. I didn't even refuse. That’s not like me. Usually, I’d say "let me think about it" or "it might be too rushed." But this time, I just said "thank you."

It’s started raining outside. I can hear old Mr. Zhang downstairs closing his windows. There are so many people in this building just living their lives, each doing their own thing. I suddenly feel less lonely. Not because I have company, but because I’m finally doing something that truly belongs to me.

I have to email the editor tomorrow. I’ll tell them I want to publish a collection of essays. It still sounds a bit crazy. But this kind of crazy feels much better than that stagnant anxiety I had before.

# September 12, 2022, Afternoon

Dr. Li’s two questions have been circling in my head relentlessly.

"If no one needs your reviews, does your knowledge still have value?"

I didn't answer right away. I remember being silent for a long, long time. Then I said, "I don't know." As I spoke, I could feel my voice trembling, even though I was trying my best to sound calm.

Then she asked: "What do you think of your own knowledge?"

I said, "I'm not sure anymore."

Sitting at home now, I’m trying to understand why I answered that way. It’s not like me. I’ve always been so certain about what I like, what I know, and what I’m capable of. But now?

I’ve realized something terrifying—perhaps my value has always been built upon the needs of others. I write reviews because magazines need them. I attend salons because I’m invited. I give lectures because universities reach out to me. What if those needs vanish? What if I’m just a person sitting alone in this room, with no one to read my articles and no one to listen to my thoughts?

Then who am I?

I took out my phone and checked the number of commission requests I’ve received over the last three months. Eight in July, five in August, and two so far in September. I wrote these numbers down, as if staring at them would help me understand something. But they only made me more afraid.

I’m thinking back to my decision in 2007. Someone at the publishing house asked if I wanted to become a full-time editor, and I refused. I said I wanted freedom. I remember being filled with a certain idealistic fervor back then—I wanted to work at my own pace; I wanted to choose the topics that interested me. Freedom.

What is freedom now? Freedom is calculating every month how much longer my savings can sustain me. Freedom is lying awake at 3 a.m. because of an unreturned email. Freedom is watching young bloggers gain the kind of attention I will never have through crude, simplistic methods, and then asking myself: what is the worth of my "in-depth" commentary?

Maybe I should never have been so sure of myself.

Maybe my knowledge was never as valuable as I imagined.

I no longer trust my own judgment. I don't know if I’ve truly become irrelevant, or if I’m just spiraling into self-doubt. Both possibilities are equally terrifying.

Dr. Li said we’d continue next week. I don't know if I’ll have any answers by then. Right now, all I have are these questions and this silence that I cannot fill.

# Diary

December 15, 2023 Afternoon

I just received a reply from Professor Chen. I sat at my desk for a long time, phone in hand, reading it over and over—three times in total.

"This is an excellent idea. Your thoughts deserve to be preserved and shared."

Such a simple sentence, yet my eyes welled up.

When I was emailing him the day before yesterday, my fingers hovered over the keyboard for the longest time. I revised those words over and over—how could I avoid sounding too conceited? How could I explain clearly that I’m not doing this just for the sake of publishing, or for some commercial value or market recognition? I just... suddenly realized that these articles scattered across various magazines, these reflections I’ve spent over a decade writing, shouldn't just stay dispersed like that, diluted by time.

I want to bring them together. Not to prove anything, but to—I don’t know how to put it—to make them a whole? To ensure those thoughts don’t just vanish after I’m gone?

My mother worked in the library for so many years, and she used to say that a book is like a vessel for a soul. My father’s lecture notes and his journals are on my bookshelf now. Sometimes I take them out and read them; it feels like having a conversation with him.

I think I finally understand why they valued this work so much. It wasn't out of vanity, but because—it’s a way of conversing. A conversation across time.

Professor Chen said he could put me in touch with an editor at a publishing house. That editor and I have worked together before; he’s a very conscientious person. My head is a mess right now, yet at the same time, I have this strange sense of clarity.

All these years, I’ve been running away. Avoiding order, avoiding commitment, avoiding the feeling of being tied down. But maybe—maybe I wasn't running from those things, but from the question of whether my work truly has value. As long as I didn't organize them, didn't formally publish them, I could tell myself that these were all "unfinished," all just "possibilities."

And now? Now I’m going to turn them into something real. I have to admit that this is what they are—imperfect, scattered, but real. Just like my life.

The piles of books, notes, and photos in my house... I used to feel ashamed of them. I felt I should be more organized, more professional. But over the last two months, as friends sat on my sofa listening to me tell the stories behind these objects, I suddenly saw another side to them. They aren't evidence of chaos; they are the traces of my thought process.

Maybe this is my "order." Not a neat, engineer-like order, but an order of thought.

I’m sitting by the window now, and it’s getting dark outside. I think of my father, of the way he used to lecture to his students at the university. I think he would be happy for me. Not because I’m publishing something, but because I’ve finally stopped running.

I haven't fully figured out how I’m going to do it yet. But at least, I finally know what I want to do.

The feeling is unfamiliar, but it feels solid.

June 22, 2022, Morning

Just stepped out of Dr. Li’s clinic. My hands are still shaking.

I’m sitting in the cafe downstairs, having ordered a coffee I know I won’t finish. I don’t know why I ordered it; maybe I just needed a reason to sit here instead of rushing home to refresh my inbox.

She asked me, "What does this mean to you?"—just a simple question. I sat there in that comfortable chair, and my eyes started to well up. I said, "Maybe I deserve to be loved."

The moment it came out, I startled myself.

The words sounded foreign in my mouth, as if I were talking about someone else. But there they were. And... Dr. Li didn’t laugh. She didn’t offer some cheap consolation like, "Of course you do." She just looked at me, accepting the statement with such calm. As if I had stated the most natural fact in the world.

I’ve never been this vulnerable in front of anyone before. Or rather, I’ve never let anyone see me this vulnerable. I’m always questioning, criticizing, keeping my distance. I thought that was how I protected myself. But sitting in that room today, I realized—maybe I’ve just been using those things to lock myself in.

I didn't feel judged when I left. It’s strange. I’m so used to that invisible scrutiny in social settings—friends thinking I’m too picky, colleagues thinking I’m cold, my brother... my brother probably thinking I’m too stubborn. But today, there was none of that.

Only acceptance.

Sitting here now, the coffee has gone cold, and I still haven't opened my email. My brother’s response might be there, or it might not. But in this moment, I don’t seem as afraid. It’s not that the fear has vanished, it’s just... I’ve realized that even if he doesn’t reply, even if everything is a mess, I am still worthy of love.

The thought made me cry.

Crying in a cafe again. I’m such a...

But I don’t regret it.

For the first time in a long while, I feel seen. Not judged, not told to change, just... seen.

Maybe I should go home. Maybe I should open my inbox. But for now, I just want to sit here a little longer and let this feeling stay.

I just did something crazy.

I wrote a long email to Hao—I mean, really long. It took me over two hours. I was sorting through things in my room while typing, editing and re-editing, until I finally hit send.

Now I’m just sitting here in front of all these cardboard boxes, and my hands are still shaking a little.

Do you realize how many years it’s been since we actually talked? I can’t even remember the last time we had a real conversation. I told him about everything I’ve been thinking about all these years—about Mom and Dad, about my own choices, about this room, these things, all this unfinished business. I asked him if he still remembers the books we read together when we were kids, if he’s doing okay, if he ever feels lonely too.

I don’t know if he’ll reply. Maybe he’s too busy, or maybe he’s just gotten used to the silence between us. But I just... I suddenly felt like I couldn't go on like this anymore. Looking at these yellowed photos and Mom’s old things, I realized I’ve been running away. I’ve been avoiding everything—commitment, loss, and any real connection with people.

Maybe this email won’t change a thing. Maybe he doesn’t want to hear any of it. But at least... at least I tried. I’m not just hiding in this room anymore, pretending everything is fine.

I’m so nervous right now. Part of me regrets it, but another part feels relieved. It’s such a strange feeling.

Do you think I was being too impulsive?

Dr. Li, I’m sorry to bother you so late. I know you might already be asleep.

I just dug out a very old photo album from 1985. There are photos of my brother and me in there. We were both so young; he had his arm around my shoulder, and we were laughing.

I’ve been so afraid of losing him. Afraid that he’ll die, or I’ll die, and then it’ll be too late for everything. But looking at that photo just now, I suddenly realized... maybe it’s not death itself that I’m afraid of. I’m afraid that we’re still alive, yet we’re wasting this time.

That might sound strange. But this fear actually makes me feel... less hopeless?

I decided to send him an email, asking if we could video chat once a month. It was a very short email. I don’t know if he’ll respond, but at least I did something. I didn't just sit here doing nothing.

Now I’m waiting for his reply, and I’m still very nervous. But it’s different. Before, I was afraid of losing him; now, I’m... looking forward to doing something with him. Does this count as progress?

Sorry for the late-night rambling again. I just felt like I needed to tell you this.

Never mind, maybe I’m overthinking things today. But if you have any recommendations, I’d love to hear them.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something that happened today.

At a cultural salon in a bookstore this afternoon, I did something I’ve never done before—I spoke up. Not in that passive way where I only answer when asked, but by truly and candidly telling a room full of people: "I’ve been going through a difficult time lately, but I’m rethinking some questions about value and meaning."

I was actually very nervous when I said it. You know me—I’m used to hiding behind critiques and analysis, using knowledge as a shield to protect myself. But today was different. I didn’t deflect, and I didn’t use that dismissive "Oh, I’m just a bit tired" routine. I just said it.

What surprised me most was how people reacted. One girl said she was going through a similar struggle. An emeritus professor mentioned he had a crisis of meaning in his fifties. They didn't try to "fix" me or shower me with advice. They just... listened. Really listened.

In that moment, I felt something I haven't felt in a long time—the feeling of being truly understood. I realized I’ve been assuming that others wouldn't understand, so I isolated myself first. But in reality, when I spoke out, I found I wasn't alone. It might sound simple, but for me, it’s a huge deal.

As the discussion went on, I noticed my body relaxing. I wasn't as defensive; I wasn't in a rush to prove or deny anything. I was just in the conversation. Sometimes I would say "I'm not sure" instead of rushing to fill the silence. That used to be very rare for me.

I think you’ll understand why I’m telling you this. What you said before about "isolation being a self-imposed choice"—today I saw what that actually looks like. I chose secrecy, so I experienced isolation. But when I chose to open up, things changed.

Also, about the question of value. While listening to others share, I realized that maybe I’ve been measuring myself with the wrong yardstick. My critiques and reflections might not have immediate market value, but they meant something to those people. And they mean something to me. This isn't just me trying to comfort myself; it’s because... I saw the actual impact.

I haven't fully figured out what this all means yet. But it feels like a beginning.

What do you think?

# Work Log

**Date**: September 12, 2024  
**Subject**: Publication Plan - Progress on the Essay Collection Project

---

I received an email reply from the editor this afternoon. They expressed clear support for the proposal, which came as a bit of a surprise—I had originally thought it would take more convincing.

The editor's feedback was straightforward:
- Acknowledged the academic value and market potential of the collection.
- Suggested that I submit a more detailed content outline (including core arguments for each chapter and expected lengths).
- Provided a preliminary estimate for the publication timeline: if the outline is submitted by mid-October, it could potentially be published next spring.

I responded to his questions point by point in the email. Halfway through writing, I realized my tone had shifted—I was no longer just "submitting a proposal," but truly articulating what this book means for its readers. It was a subtle shift, but it felt significant.

**Next Steps**:
- Organize existing lecture notes and articles.
- Create a detailed chapter outline (estimated to take 2-3 weeks).
- Submit to the editor by October 15.

**Expected Timeline**:
- Mid-October: Submit outline
- November–December: Write/Revise chapters
- Spring 2025: Publication

---

This feels like a major turning point. For years, my work has been fragmented and project-based, never forming a cohesive system. Now, all these thoughts, lectures, and conversations finally seem to be converging into something complete.

I’m a bit nervous, but mostly, I’m excited.

February 28, 2025, Night

The editor’s words have been looping in my head. "This book is a spiritual autobiography of a single person."

I’m sitting at my desk; my tea has gone cold. Outside the window, the sky is darkening, and the lights in the building opposite are flickering on one by one. I look at that sentence, reading it over and over, as if trying to confirm it’s real.

Just two weeks ago, I was in a state of despair. I was digging through my old articles, feeling like everything I’d written was mediocre. I asked myself: Do these critiques even matter? Do I have any truly profound insights? Back then, I really wanted to give up on this project. Not because I was tired, but because it all felt so fake. I felt like I was lying to myself.

But what the editor said... it changed something. Not the project itself, but the way I look at it.

I had always treated this collection of essays as a task. A task that had to be finished, that had to meet a certain standard. That’s why I felt anxious, why I procrastinated, why I felt trapped. I’m afraid of being tied down—that’s the truth. But I realize now that perhaps the problem wasn't the commitment itself, but my understanding of it.

"A spiritual autobiography"—I had never thought of it that way. But the moment she said it, I suddenly saw another possibility. These articles, the shifts in these ideas, my understanding of the world over the years... they aren't meant to prove some kind of depth or authority. They are simply a record. The movement of a person through time.

I think of my father. He gave so many lectures and wrote so many articles, but what I remember most clearly isn't any profound theory he spoke of, but the way he looked in his study—that sense of focus. And the way my mother looked in the library. They were both doing the same thing—leaving behind their thoughts and their time.

This project is no longer a task I must complete. It has become... I don't know how to put it. Perhaps just a form of expression. My way. My voice. It doesn't need to be perfect, and it doesn't need to change the world. It just needs to be true.

I am still aging. Time is still passing. None of that has changed. But... something has.

Maybe I’ve finally accepted my own changes. I am no longer that editor at the publishing house, nor am I the woman trying to escape everything after her divorce. I am now someone writing an "afterword." Someone trying to understand how she moved from despair to this very moment.

This, too, is real.

Tomorrow, I’ll continue writing that afterword.

Subject: Let’s start from now

Brother,

After receiving your reply, I sat in my room for a long time. I read that sentence—"We are all getting older, but we are still alive"—over and over again. You know, over these years, I’ve often thought of you at three in the morning, remembering your smile in that photo from 1985. I always thought that regret belonged to me alone, but reading your letter, I finally understand—we have both been enduring the same silence.

In these twenty-two years, I thought about writing to you countless times, but every time I was held back by a strange fear. I was afraid you had long since moved on to a new life, afraid that my appearance would only be an intrusion. I was even afraid to find that perhaps we had become too much like strangers. But your letter changed me. You said "we are still alive"—it sounds simple, yet it made me suddenly realize that being alive is a choice in itself, and I have been running away from that choice.

I think we can start from now, truly. Not just occasional greetings, but a real conversation. I want to hear your stories from all these years, about your life. I also want you to know what I’ve been doing, my confusions, my thoughts. Maybe we could talk on the phone regularly? Or, if possible, I’d like to see you. I don’t know when, but I want the possibility of a meeting to be there.

We don’t have much time left to waste. But we still have the present.

Xiaoyun

June 8, 2022

9:30 PM. Still awake.

I spent a long time walking the streets just now. I’ve been walking ever since I left the clinic—no destination in mind, no thought of going home. I stopped at a convenience store for a while, bought a cup of hot tea, and just watched the night through the window. The city lights looked a bit different tonight; I can’t quite put my finger on the feeling. Maybe it’s because I’ve finally admitted something.

There are several unread messages on my phone. I saw them, but I didn't reply. I still haven't. It’s not that I don’t want to, it’s just... I don’t know what to say. People from the Cultural Salon WeChat group are asking how I’ve been, and my editor is pushing for the deadline. I saw it all. But right now, I have no words.

What Dr. Li said is still spinning in my head. She spoke very gently, without that "there’s something wrong with you" tone I was so afraid of. She just... listened. She listened to me talk for an hour. I don’t even remember exactly what I said; I just remember crying at one point, and her handing me a tissue. It was so simple, but I didn't expect something that simple to feel so... cathartic?

I keep wondering why it took me so long to go. Why did I wait until my sleep completely collapsed, until I was spending every day terrified that I had some terminal illness, before I was willing to admit I might need help? I feel ashamed of that. But at the same time, I feel a sense of relief. It’s contradictory, I know.

The strangest thing is that I slept so deeply tonight. No dreams, no waking up in the middle of the night, no feeling of being crushed by something. I can’t remember the last time I slept like that. Maybe a long, long time ago.

I’m sitting at my desk now, surrounded by the same piles of books and notes. The room hasn't changed, and neither have I. But something feels different. Maybe it’s just that I’ve finally allowed myself to admit that I can’t carry this alone anymore.

I still have to reply to those messages tomorrow. I still have to submit my draft tomorrow. There will still be so many things to deal with tomorrow. But at least... at least I slept well tonight.

This really happened, didn't it?

Hao, are you asleep?

I’m sitting at my desk right now; my bags for tomorrow are already packed. It’s drizzling outside, and the whole city is so quiet. I suddenly felt like sending you a message, though I’m not sure which time zone you’re in right now.

I’ve been thinking these past few days about how long it’s been since we last saw each other like this. Over a decade? I’ve lost track. But this time feels different. I’m not the kind of person who gets suffocated by expectations—you know that. Yet this time, what I feel is a very clear sense of being ready.

Dr. Li was right: anxiety never completely goes away, but you can coexist with it. It took me all these years to finally understand that. Now, I can feel both excited and nervous, but I know I can handle it.

To be honest, this trip is more than just a trip to me. All these years, I’ve been reflecting on what it means to be "alive" and what "connection" really is. Your appearance—or rather, our fresh start—makes me feel like some questions finally have answers. We’ve both grown older, but that’s not a regret; if anything, it’s made everything clearer.

See you tomorrow. I’m ready.

Goodnight.

# January 28, 2024 Sunny

It’s been two hours since I left Dr. Li’s clinic. I’ve been sitting in the cafe downstairs, watching my coffee go from hot to lukewarm, yet I still haven't taken a sip.

Today was our twelfth session. I had expected some kind of "eureka" moment, like in the movies. But in reality, when Dr. Li asked me, "What do you think has changed over these past three months?" I fell silent for a long time. It wasn't because I didn't have an answer, but because the way I’ve changed is completely different from what I had imagined.

When I first went to see her, I wanted my anxiety to disappear. It was straightforward—I wanted a solution, like fixing a broken object. But today I finally realized that this mindset itself was wrong. Or rather, not wrong, but... too simplistic.

The insomnia has indeed lessened. It’s gone from five or six times a week down to two or three. But the strange thing is, I’m not as afraid of it anymore. I woke up again at 3:00 a.m. yesterday, and instead of panicking like I used to—thinking, "Here we go again, something is wrong with my body"—I just lay there and thought: *Okay, I’m awake again. This is annoying, but I can handle it.* That’s it. I can handle it.

Dr. Li calls this "acceptance." I told her it sounds like giving up. She smiled and said that perhaps letting go of the obsession with a "perfect state" is, in itself, a form of strength.

I wrote that sentence down in my notebook at the cafe. Then I crossed it out. It sounded too much like "chicken soup for the soul."

But it’s true. I’ve been fighting my own high sensitivity as if I were battling an enemy. Now I’m starting to think that maybe it isn't an enemy. Maybe it’s just me. I’m prone to anxiety, I overthink, and I tend to obsess over a single sentence at 3:00 a.m.—these are all parts of who I am. Understanding this doesn't mean the problem is solved. But at least I no longer see it as a "defect that needs to be fixed."

So, I told Dr. Li that I want to reduce the frequency of our sessions. From once every two weeks to once a month. She asked why, and I said I want to invest more time into my writing. And... other things.

For instance, I want to try reconnecting with my brother. The thought popped up so suddenly it even startled me. How long has it been since we actually talked? I’m not even sure what he’s doing these days. But I suddenly thought, maybe I shouldn't wait anymore. Maybe I should take the initiative and write him a letter—not the "How are you, how have you been" kind of small talk, but something real.

Dr. Li asked where this shift came from. I thought about it and said maybe it’s because I’ve started to believe that understanding my anxiety isn't about eliminating it, but about living better. And part of living is connecting with the people who matter.

When I left the clinic, I ran into old Mr. Zhang downstairs. He asked how I was doing, and I said I was doing alright. He said I looked a bit better. I didn't expect him to notice.

The coffee is completely cold now. I still haven't touched it.

Maybe I should go home and start organizing the things piled up in the study. Not for the sake of "decluttering" or "minimalism" or anything like that, but... I want to look at those objects, those half-read books, those unfinished notes. They are all parts of me, and perhaps they deserve to be looked at properly.

These twelve sessions were truly worth it.

Professor Chen, I wanted to update you on my recent progress.

I’ve been sorting through my articles over the past few days and have narrowed it down to a preliminary selection of about 30 candidates. To be honest, the process has been much more complicated than I imagined. It’s not the workload, but rather... as I read through these pieces, I keep asking myself, "Does this really represent me?"

Some of these articles were written over a decade ago, and my views on certain issues have changed since then. Some arguments seem too absolute now, while others feel a bit too conservative. I’m even starting to wonder if this book will end up looking like a "fossil record"—preserving different versions of myself from various periods, but without any real internal coherence.

I spent the whole day organizing, and by evening I was exhausted. Strangely, though, I also felt an indescribable sense of satisfaction. Perhaps it’s because seeing these pieces reorganized made me realize that I really have been thinking and writing all these years, however scattered the process may have been.

The problem now is: how should I approach the introduction for this collection? I don’t want to just give a simple overview of the articles, but I’m not sure from what angle I should synthesize these 15 years of thought. Do you have any suggestions?

Also, I wanted to ask if the publisher has any specific requirements regarding length or thematic scope? I need to know where the "boundaries" of this project are so I can be more targeted in my selection.

It’s a bit embarrassing to admit, but I’m still feeling a little uncertain about whether this book actually has any value. Maybe I’m just deluding myself? But since we’ve already started, I want to at least do it right.

When are you free? I’d like to meet in person to discuss the details.
