# March 25, 2022, 3:00 AM

Can’t sleep. Again.

It was already dark when I left Dr. Li’s office yesterday; I didn't even notice how the time had slipped away. Now, lying in bed, my head is filled with that hour of conversation, looping like an endless tape recording.

She just asked one thing: "How has your sleep been lately?" Just that. I don't know why the tears started coming. Maybe because no one has asked me that in years—or rather, no one has actually wanted to hear the answer. I started talking about the insomnia, about waking up at 3:00 AM and never being able to drift back off, about that nameless dread, about aging, about death—it was only when my mother passed away that I realized I was getting old too, which is such an absurd thought. I talked about my brother, about him being overseas, about how we haven't truly opened up to each other in years, and about that faint sense of loneliness, like being slowly strangled by something.

Then I talked about work, about the internet’s impact on the publishing industry, about how fewer and fewer people read my articles, about whether I’ve become useless...

When I said, "I feel like I’m becoming a useless person," I broke down. I just sat there in front of a strange woman and sobbed my heart out.

Thinking back on it now, I still feel ashamed. That wasn't me. I don't do that. I’ve always been Lin Xiaoyun—the independent, rational one who doesn't need anyone's pity. But yesterday, I just cried, and once I started, I couldn't stop.

The strangest thing was that Dr. Li didn't try to comfort me. I thought she’d say something like, "You aren't useless," or give me some advice. But she just sat there, handed me a tissue, and said nothing. That silence actually made me feel... heard? It sounds strange.

I don't know what to think right now. On one hand, I feel like something has been released, as if a years-long blockage suddenly found an outlet. On the other hand, I’m terrified by my own loss of control. I was such a mess in that clinic; what if someone I knew had seen me?

She told me to come back next week. I didn't refuse.

But it’s 3:00 AM now, and I still can’t sleep, my mind still replaying those words. Maybe this is who I am now—a 62-year-old woman whose sleep is broken, whose career is a blur, who is estranged from her family, and who can be reduced to tears by a single question.

Is this the real me? Or is it the me I’ve been denying all along?

I don't know.

I really don't know.

December 18, 2023. Late night, 23:45.

When I pulled out that cardboard box, I didn't expect it to take this long.

I was originally just looking for an old contract, but an hour has passed and I’m still sitting on the floor, surrounded by photos, journals, and all sorts of mementos. My 1982 graduation photo was tucked right at the bottom. I picked it up and stared at it for a long time—that girl in the dark blue dress with the voluminous hair, her eyes sparkling. What was she thinking? Probably about how she could finally leave school and become a real editor.

Now, I look at myself in the mirror and then back at the photo. Forty-one years. There are many more fine lines on my face now, and my eyes aren't as bright. But what shocks me most isn't aging itself; it’s the sensation that time really is passing—not as an abstract concept, but as a concrete, cold reality.

I decided to write a chronology of my life. Like organizing an archive, I’ll list all the important moments. 1982: Graduated from university. 1982–2007: Worked at the publishing house. 2005: Divorced. 2007: Resigned. 2015: Mother passed away.

After the 2007 entry, I wrote a sentence: "Left for freedom, but at what cost?"

I stared at those words for a long time. All these years, I’ve been defending that decision—to my friends, to myself. Freedom. What a beautiful word. I didn't want to be tied down; I didn't want to suffocate in an office. So, I left.

But now I admit, this choice gave me not only freedom but also instability. An irregular income. No security. Living alone in this old house, piled high with books and souvenirs, with no one who truly knows what’s on my mind. My brother is overseas; we hardly speak. My ex-husband has long been a stranger. My parents are both gone.

I’m not saying I regret it. I’m just admitting that freedom comes with a price. And I’ve been pretending there wasn't one.

The thought makes me feel uneasy. But it also brings a certain sense of relief.

Perhaps the real question isn't "How can I avoid aging and death?"—which is what I’ve been asking myself all these years. Perhaps the real question is "How do I want to live?" What kind of life do I want? Not to live longer, but to live more authentically.

I put the photo down and continued flipping through the journals. I’ve forgotten what I wrote on some of the pages. Re-reading them feels like having a conversation with my younger self. She was in such a hurry, so desperate to prove something. I want to tell her: you don’t need to prove that much. But at the same time, I’m grateful for her persistence. It was that very persistence that made me who I am today—imperfect and full of regrets, perhaps, but at least real.

Who should I share these thoughts with? Dr. Li? Or my friends from the cultural salon?

No, maybe it’s not the right time yet. I need to sort these thoughts out here first, on paper.

It’s almost dawn. I should get some sleep.

But I will keep working on this chronology.

December 22, 2023, 8:00 PM

Just got back from the gathering. Sitting alone in front of my piles of books. My hands are still shaking.

I’m not sure when it started, but I just began talking. Maybe someone asked what I’ve been up to lately, and I mentioned Dr. Li. Then it was like a dam breaking—I said I was in therapy, that I’ve been using rationality to escape my emotions. After I finished, the whole living room went silent. I could feel that silence.

I thought it would be awkward. But it wasn’t.

One friend said, "I have anxiety too." Another said, "I’m seeing a doctor as well." Then everyone started sharing their own stories. No one thought it was strange. Instead, it felt like some kind of... I don’t know how to describe it... like a moment of permission?

I’ve always maintained this persona of the "intellectual woman." At these gatherings, I’m always the one who can quote theories and analyze cultural phenomena. When I speak, there’s always this sense of distance, as if I’m critiquing someone else’s life rather than talking about my own. Today, I broke that. I admitted I’m fragile, admitted I’m anxious, admitted I use knowledge as armor.

I was actually terrified while I was speaking. Terrified of being seen through, terrified of losing that "insightful Lin Xiaoyun" persona. But looking back now, being seen through feels more like a... release?

I also talked about my brother. I don’t know why I thought of him at that moment. Maybe because I finally admitted my own vulnerability, I could also admit that regret—that we haven’t had a real conversation in so many years. I don’t even know what’s on his mind these days. My voice cracked a little as I spoke, and a friend handed me a tissue.

It’s strange. I always felt that rational understanding was the same as control. I’ve read so many articles about anxiety and neurotic traits; I thought if I understood myself, I could change myself. But today I realized that understanding and changing are two different things. Sometimes what you need isn't more knowledge, but... to be heard?

On the way home, I wondered: is this real change? Or am I just putting on another performance—this time performing the "honest self"? I’m not sure. But at least for that moment at the gathering, I wasn’t thinking about how I looked. I was just there, speaking the truth, hands shaking, eyes a bit wet.

I have to reply to the magazine's email tomorrow; another deadline is about to pass. But tonight, I don’t want to think about that.

I want to sit here a little longer.

Hi, I just got some feedback from the editor and wanted to share it with you.

Regarding that piece on "Identity Anxiety in Middle-Aged Women," the editor said the reception has been incredibly high. She told me, "Your understanding transcends theoretical frameworks and touches upon true human nature." I was actually a bit stunned when I read that.

To be honest, I let down a lot of my defenses while writing this. I didn't hide behind theories or use fancy academic language to protect myself. I just... very bluntly wrote down some personal feelings and observations. I was actually a bit nervous, worried that doing so might come across as too subjective or "unprofessional."

But looking at it now, it seems it was exactly that honesty that gave the article its power.

It made me realize that all those rigorous theoretical analyses I've written over the years—while technically sound—always felt like they were behind a layer of glass. This time, there was no barrier.

Do you ever feel this way? That when you truly let something go, you actually become more persuasive? I always thought I had to maintain distance and objectivity to be taken seriously, but now... I’m not so sure.

It’s a strange feeling. A mix of pride and vulnerability.

Thursday, June 15, 2023
Night

Professor Chen’s words have been looping in my head.

I sat in the cafe for two hours this afternoon; the coffee went cold. She said I could compile my lectures and articles from over the years into a book, and that she could help me get in touch with publishers. She was very earnest, saying my perspectives are truly inspiring for students.

I didn’t say yes right away, but I didn’t say no either. I just said, "Let me think about it."

It was a strange reaction. The old me would have either refused immediately—out of a fear of failure, a fear of being judged, hiding behind the guise of being a "freelancer"—or I would have jumped at the chance, desperate to prove myself and chase that feeling of being "validated." But today, I just... paused.

Sitting there in the cafe, I asked myself: Do I really want to publish this book?

Not "should I," not "would this help my career," but truly asking myself—do I want it?

It’s been a long time since I’ve asked myself a question like that.

To be honest, I don’t know. This "not knowing" isn't born of fear or avoidance. I genuinely just don’t know. I realized that I no longer need a publication to prove my worth. Even as I say that, I’m a little surprised myself. But it’s true.

The people in the reading group come every month, and it’s not because I’ve published some major article. They come because in that room, we can talk honestly. What I say resonates with them. Not because I have a title, not because my name is printed on a book, but simply... because it’s a real conversation.

Maybe my value has always been right here. In these dialogues, in these genuine connections with people. Not on the title page of a book.

But I didn’t reject the idea entirely, either. Because maybe... maybe this book could bring these ideas to more people. Maybe it’s just another form of conversation. I’m really not sure.

Dr. Li once said that uncertainty isn't a bad thing. Allowing yourself to dwell in uncertainty is, in itself, a form of growth.

I think I’ll just stay here for now. No rush to decide. I’ll let this idea sit in my heart for a while longer and see what it turns into.

Maybe I’ll think about it again next week. Maybe next month. Maybe it will never be published, or maybe it will. I don’t need to know the answer right now.

This feeling is unfamiliar. But it’s also very... peaceful.

[Message to Dr. Li]

Dr. Li, I... I took out that camera today.

It’s just sitting here on my desk. I didn't put it back.

I don’t really know why. My head was a mess when I woke up this morning, and then I just walked over to the cabinet and opened it. The camera was still there, with a layer of dust on the lens.

I stared at it for a long time.

Maybe this doesn't change anything. I know that. And it’s not like I’m trying to take photos or anything. I just... took it out.

You told me to pay attention to small changes. Does this count? Or am I just kidding myself, thinking that doing this proves I’m still alive?

I’m sitting here now, looking at the camera, and it feels strange. Like I’m looking at something belonging to a stranger.

Sorry, this message probably doesn't make much sense. I just... wanted to let you know.

Professor Chen, do you have a moment? I wanted to share a rather strange discovery with you.

While tidying up my room yesterday, I stumbled upon my diary from 2007. You know, the year I left the publishing house. There’s an entire page dedicated to a "manifesto" I wrote—the incredibly idealistic kind, all about freedom, independent thinking, and not wanting to be shackled by the system. Reading it made me want to laugh a little, but it also hurt a bit.

I thought I’d fall into that trap of self-deprecation—you know, "Look how naive I was back then." But I didn't. Instead, I wrote down a sentence: "My choices are complex."

That phrase has been spinning in my head ever since.

You see, the person I was back then wasn't wrong—I did need freedom, and I did need time to think. But I didn't foresee how heavy that freedom would be. I didn't realize that sixteen years later, I’d be obsessively revising a draft just for a freelance fee, or that financial anxiety would follow me like a shadow. So, in a way, my choices certainly came with a price.

But—and this is the point I wanted to make to you—I’ve also gained something. The work with the reading club over the past two years has made this very clear to me. The people who come to hear me speak don't do so because of how many articles I’ve published; they come because I can have a real conversation with them. My thoughts, my questions, and my perspectives mean something to them. That sense of connection is something I never truly felt during my twenty-five years at the publishing house.

So, I’ve stopped asking myself, "Did I make the right choice?" Instead, I ask, "What did this choice give me, and what did it take away?" Both answers are true.

I’m not sure why I felt like sharing this with you specifically. Perhaps it’s because you’ve always understood that the "value of knowledge" isn't something black and white.

What do you think?

Dr. Li, I’ve been thinking about our conversation since I hung up, and I felt I should send you a message just to confirm.

Wednesday at 3 PM—I’ve got it down. I’ll be there on time.

To be honest, I’m not sure what I’m expecting, or maybe I’m expecting too much. But at the very least, I found the courage to make that call, which wasn't easy for me. I think I might need more help than I realized.

Thank you for taking my call.

Dr. Li, I need to tell you something.

At the book club today, I made a decision that I’m still feeling a bit shaken by. I came right out and told everyone about my anxiety diagnosis. Just like that.

I hadn't planned on it. We were discussing a book on psychological resilience, and someone mentioned their own experience with anxiety, and I just... I don't know how to describe it, it was like something suddenly snapped loose. I said things like how a diagnosis isn't the end but a starting point. I spoke so calmly, almost as if I were telling someone else's story.

The strangest part was that three people came up to talk to me privately afterward. One of them said, "Thank you for being so open; I have anxiety too." In that moment, Dr. Li, I realized how absurd my previous fears were—I always thought speaking out would scare people away, make them think I’m unstable, or change how they see me. But actually... it brought us closer somehow.

But now I’m wondering, is this a good thing? Or was I just acting on impulse, using some kind of "educational" framework to justify oversharing? You know my personality; I tend to make decisions based on emotion. Am I truly accepting the diagnosis, or am I just seeking sympathy and validation?

And... I’m feeling a bit conflicted now. If I’ve already said it at the book club, do I still need to keep it a secret? Should I tell more people? Or is this enough? I’m afraid this "courage" is only temporary and that I’ll just shrink back into my shell next time.

What do you think?

Friday, March 18, 2022. Rainy.

Another sleepless night. The editor’s voice from yesterday’s call is still ringing in my ears.

"This is the third extension."

I heard myself saying "I haven't been feeling well" in a tiny, fragile voice, as if defending my own incompetence. But defending what? They need a manuscript, not my excuses. I could feel the coldness on the other end of the line—that professional, thinning patience. "We need to know if you can still do this."

Can I?

I lie on the sofa, staring at the crack in the ceiling. That crack has been there for two years. I’ve never fixed it. Just like I’ve never finished that piece.

Is it my health? Or... have I truly become incapable? I don't know. My head has been a mess for months. I can't focus on reading; I can't produce any writing. The very thing I was best at now feels like it’s trapped behind a veil. Sometimes I sit in front of the computer for three hours, and the screen remains blank.

Then I start doing the math. If I lose this client, how much longer can I hold out? Three months? Four? My savings are shrinking; they shrink every single month. I’m 62. I should have a stable retirement plan, not be sitting here calculating survival.

Maybe I shouldn't have left the publishing house. Maybe I overestimated myself. "Freedom" sounds beautiful, but freedom comes at a price, and that price is swallowing me whole right now.

I think about what Dr. Li said. She said anxiety can sometimes affect one's ability to work. But I’m not sure—is the anxiety causing the struggle to work, or is the struggle to work causing the anxiety? The two are so entangled now that I can't tell them apart.

I should call the editor back. I should give him a definitive answer. But I don't know what to say. I don't know if I really can go on.

Or... maybe I just don't want to know.

It’s still raining outside.

# Self-Adjustment Plan 2022.4.29

I’ve taken Dr. Li’s advice to heart, but medication is a path I cannot take. It’s not out of stubbornness; it’s because I know clearly that once I become dependent on drugs, I lose the right to understand myself. I need to resolve this in my own way.

**Starting this month:**

**Meditation & Body**
- 15 minutes of meditation every morning (not to clear my mind, but to observe my thought patterns).
- Attend yoga classes on Wednesdays and Fridays at that community center Uncle Zhang recommended.
- Stop scrolling through medical news on my phone before bed—this habit is the most lethal.

**Reading Plan**
- Finish *Cognitive Behavioral Therapy for Anxiety* (it’s been sitting there for two months).
- Reorganize the bookshelf and list out the "unfinished" books; finish at least one per week.
- This isn't just leisure; it’s using knowledge to understand myself.

**Social & Connection**
- Organize a book club meeting next week; invite Professor Chen and friends from the cultural salon.
- Take the initiative to email Lin Hao—stop waiting. Even if he’s overseas, it’s time for a deep conversation.
- Schedule an outdoor shoot with my photography friends; no more flaking out.

**Work**
- That review on contemporary anxiety literature must be submitted by May 15th.
- Stop pushing back deadlines; the delay itself is a source of anxiety.
- The publishing plan Professor Chen mentioned—I should give it serious consideration.

**The Bottom Line:**
No medication. I’m not dismissing Dr. Li, but I need to prove that I can change the status quo through understanding, action, and connection. If there’s still no improvement by the end of June, I’ll reconsider. But for now, I’m doing this my way.

Perhaps the problem isn’t that I can’t control my anxiety, but that I’ve been treating it the wrong way. To understand it, accept it, and then coexist with it—that sounds much harder than taking a pill, but it feels more like me.

Let’s begin.

February 8, 2024, Afternoon

Professor Chen’s words have been looping in my head.

"The key is whether you want to leave something behind."

I’m sitting in my study, surrounded by piles of old articles from over the years—book reviews and cultural critiques published in various magazines. I never used to take them seriously; I saw them as nothing more than fragmented pieces written for the sake of a paycheck. But this afternoon, hearing Professor Chen say those words in the café, I felt a sudden surge of something unfamiliar.

It wasn’t anxiety. It was something else.

My initial reaction was instinctive avoidance—"It’s too late to publish anything now. Who’s going to read a collection of essays by a 62-year-old woman?" I heard myself saying these things, my voice thick with the kind of self-deprecation I despise most. But Professor Chen didn’t take the bait. She just looked at me and asked, "Is it that you truly don’t want to, or are you just afraid?"

I didn’t answer right away.

Sitting here now, I finally understand what that question was really about. She wasn't asking if there was a "market" for it; she was asking if I *wanted* to. There is an entire world between those two questions.

For years, I’ve been measuring value—how much an article could sell for, whether a topic was still trending, how the internet has gutted traditional publishing, whether my expertise was still worth anything. I treated my thoughts as commodities, and when I saw they weren't being "consumed" enough, I began to doubt the validity of their very existence.

But what if it isn't about sales?

What if it’s just about giving these thoughts a place to exist?

I said this to Professor Chen, and even I was a bit surprised by the words as they came out. It wasn't planned; it just spilled out of me. "I want to record my thoughts from all these years—not for the sales, but to give these ideas a place to exist."

It sounds like I’ve finally understood something.

Maybe I have. I’m thinking that if I start organizing these articles now, writing a preface, weaving them together... it wouldn't be "publishing a book." It would be something far more intimate—making peace with my own mind. Making peace with all the years of reading and thinking.

When my father passed away, I didn't truly go through his things for sixteen years. It was the same after my mother died. Now, it suddenly occurs to me that perhaps I should find a home for my own thoughts while I still have the time. Not for anyone else, but for myself. To prove that these years weren't spent in vain.

This thought brings me a strange sense of relief.

Not all the anxiety has vanished. I still worry about whether I can see this project through; I still doubt if there’s any point to it. But now, these worries no longer feel like reasons *not* to do it. They are just the background noise to the thought: "This is hard, but I want to try."

Maybe I still have time to do something meaningful.

That thought alone is enough to make today feel different.

Dr. Li,

I sat downstairs for a long time after leaving your office.

I remembered how I couldn't find the words when you asked me what I feared most. Now, I want to write down the words that were stuck in my throat.

What I fear most is that I’ll never change. That I’ll always be a failure. And—that no one needs me.

As I write this, I realize these words sound so absolute, almost like I’m giving up on myself. But I’m starting to see that these might not be facts at all, but mental traps. I’ve been using these thoughts to protect myself, using despair to justify giving up. If I’m already at my worst, then I don’t have to try anymore, right?

But that logic is a lie.

I’ve been organizing a book club for the past two months, and people show up every time. Last week, a girl told me she started reading again because of our discussions. Then there’s Mr. Zhang, who said I gave him a reason to leave the house. Professor Chen even suggested I publish a collection of my essays. These things actually happened.

So, is it really true that "no one needs me"?

I know there are still many things I haven't figured out—my savings, my health, the widening gap between my brother and me. But what I want to ask now is: Can I stop using the worst-case scenarios to negate every good possibility?

Your silence gave me the space to think about all of this. I want to continue this conversation.

I don’t need to be comforted. I need to be asked the right questions.

Lin Xiaoyun

I’ve been debating whether to send this message for a long time. I’ve deleted and rewritten it at least five times.

You know, I did something yesterday that I haven’t done in years—I went to see a therapist. I know it sounds like no big deal, but for me... I don’t know how to explain it. It’s like I finally admitted to the things I’ve been running away from.

Dr. Li asked me a lot of questions. There’s one thing she said that I keep replaying in my head. She asked, "But you’re here now, aren’t you?" Such a simple sentence. I paused for a long time, and then the tears just started falling. I realized that while I can’t change the time I’ve wasted in the past, I am doing something to change the present. It might sound silly, but for me, it was... I don’t know, some kind of breakthrough.

I’ve always felt like an outsider. Even in a crowd, even during those cultural salons where we’d debate until midnight, I’d still go home and sit there alone among my piles of books and antiques, feeling incredibly lonely. I’ve never really told anyone this. I’m always on the defensive—afraid of being seen through, afraid of appearing vulnerable, afraid of being a burden.

But yesterday in that clinic, I was "seen" by a stranger. It was strange, but also such a relief. When I walked out, I felt this unprecedented sense of... being understood? I don’t know if that’s how therapy is supposed to feel, but in that moment, I felt like I wasn’t carrying it all by myself.

I know this might sound overly sentimental. You might think I’m making a mountain out of a molehill. But I needed to tell someone, and I thought of you. Because in all our late-night discussions, you never once made me feel like my thoughts were ridiculous.

I’m still scared. Scared that this is just temporary, scared that I’ll sink back into it again. But at least for now, I know—I don’t have to carry this alone.

How have you been lately?

Professor Chen, I wanted to talk to you about something. This might come a bit out of the blue.

I ran into Old Li at a second-hand bookstore today, and he said something that’s been stuck in my head: "Everything here has a story; maybe you can find a shadow of yourself in someone else's story." It sounds a bit poetic, but I feel like he hit on something.

Lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I’ve misunderstood my own work all along. It’s not about how many reviews I write or how many people see them, but rather... about understanding something through the process of understanding and recording those stories. When you suggested I publish a collection of my essays before, I was actually a bit resistant. I felt it was too formal, too much like building a "monument" to myself. But now I’m wondering, maybe it’s not a monument, but a record of a conversation?

I’m rambling a bit. What I mean is, do you think there’s any meaning in this direction? I mean, if I were to reorganize what I’ve written over the years, not to prove anything, but as a... record? A dialogue?

Maybe I’m being a bit sensitive right now, questioning the worth of everything. But your opinion still matters a lot to me.

Professor Chen, the reading group just wrapped up, and I’m still sitting here in the cafe, trying to process everything.

Something truly unexpected happened today. Do you remember Xiao Li? The young man who always sits in the corner and rarely speaks up? Today, he suddenly said something that caught me completely off guard. He said, "Professor Lin, your ideas have truly changed my understanding of what reading is."

I didn't know how to react at first. He went on to explain that he used to read just to passively absorb information, but through our discussions, he’s begun to realize that reading can be a dialogue rather than a one-way street. He even said he’s going to re-read several books he previously found "boring."

You know, hearing that meant more to me than meeting any manuscript deadline ever could. It’s the very core of what I tried to express in my book—but I was never quite sure if the message was actually getting across. Today, his transformation proved that it was.

It got me thinking that maybe this is why I keep doing these seemingly "unstructured" things. The reading groups, the lectures, the articles... they aren't isolated efforts. They feel like a way of testing out a dialogue, gathering feedback, and letting these ideas become clearer and more vital.

By the way, I wanted to ask you something. Have you ever considered adding a segment to your lecture series where participants can share their own reading experiences? I feel like combining your academic perspective with this kind of "democratized" discussion could create a really fascinating chemistry. What do you think?

When are you free to sit down and properly discuss our next steps? I have so many ideas I’d love to bounce off you.

I had a long talk with Editor Zhou yesterday. When she asked what I’ve been up to lately, I realized I’ve spent the past few years oscillating between being "busy" and being "idle," without actually accomplishing anything.

I said something I haven't said in a long time: "I want to make a change."

The moment the words left my mouth, I felt a twinge of fear—like a middle-aged person suddenly talking in their sleep. But she didn't laugh. Instead, she offered me a few new project opportunities.

I’ve been reading a book about freedom lately. The author argues that true freedom is never about escaping something, but about consciously choosing to shoulder something. I had it backwards. I used to think freedom meant being free from constraints, so I was always running away. Now I finally understand that perhaps freedom is simply knowing what you want, and having the courage to pay the price for it.

Even if that price is a commitment.

Subject: Five years later, I want to talk to you properly

Hao,

I’ve been sitting here for a long time before hitting send. It’s 2 a.m., and I just dug out your email from five years ago—"Sis, are you doing okay over there?"—and it suddenly hit me that maybe you’ve been waiting for a real response all these years. I feel truly sorry for that.

These past few years haven't been as good as they might have seemed on the surface. I’m still as disorganized as ever with work, always turning in drafts at the very last minute. But more than that, I’ve started to fear aging, fear death; sometimes I lie awake at night because of some health news I read. I keep thinking how much better it would be if Mom and Dad were still here. It was only after they passed that I realized some things shouldn't be left unsaid. With you overseas and me here, it’s like we’ve both just been waiting for the other to speak first.

I know it’s been a long time since we’ve had a real conversation. My stubbornness and my independence can make me seem cold sometimes, but it’s not because I don’t care. On the contrary, I think about you all the time—wondering how you’re doing over there, wanting to hear about your life.

I don’t know if this email will change anything, but I want to try. Maybe we could write to each other more often. We don't need perfect words; we just need the truth. I want to hear your voice, even if it’s only through text.

Maybe it’s time we both stopped waiting.

Thinking of you,

Xiaoyun
Late night, June 13, 2022

Dr. Li, are you still awake?

...I’m sorry to bother you so late. I know this isn't appropriate.

I watched a documentary tonight, about aging. I only meant to watch a little bit, but... I just couldn't stop. It was all very heavy stuff. I’m sitting on the sofa right now, and my heart is racing a little. I think I’m just spooked.

I felt the pulse in my neck and counted my heart rate. It’s within the normal range. I know that. But I still feel a bit... how should I put it... unsettled.

My father passed away when he was 68. I’m 62 now. That number keeps spinning around in my head. I know it’s irrational to think this way, but I just can’t help it.

Are you there? I’m not looking for medical advice right now. It’s just... I don't really want to be alone.

I’m sorry, I’m doing this again. I hate being this vulnerable.

Hey, I’ve been thinking, and I really wanted to share some things with you.

I’ve spoken with a therapist a few times lately, and she helped me realize something simple that I’d never quite grasped: I’ve been so afraid of getting hurt that I chose to distance myself. But the real risk, it turns out, is missing out on a connection.

After our last call, I stayed up all night thinking. You still remember me—you remember details I thought had long been washed away by time. In that moment, I realized the distance between us isn't actually that great. I’ve just been using the "we’re both busy" excuse as a way to protect myself.

I don’t want to do that anymore.

I was wondering if we could talk more often? It doesn’t have to be long, deep conversations—just... speaking truthfully, like this. Maybe we could set a fixed time each month? Or you could just message me whenever something comes to mind?

Also, I’m seriously considering flying out to visit you all. I know it sounds a bit sudden, but I feel like I need to see your life for myself and meet your family. I’ve missed out on too much over the years.

Maybe I’ve had it backwards all along. Freedom isn’t about staying away; it’s having the courage to move closer to the people who truly matter.

What do you think?

March 27, 2022 – Just past 11:00 PM

I was lying in bed just now, but my mind wouldn’t stop racing. I decided to get up and write something down; maybe it’ll help me settle.

When I was having tea with Sophie this afternoon, I don’t know where the courage came from, but I just blurted out that I’ve been going to therapy. I’ve actually been three times now, but I hadn’t dared to tell anyone. It felt like admitting to some kind of failure. I had all these explanations ready, trying to figure out how to make it sound… less bad.

But Sophie didn’t react the way I expected. She didn’t pity me, and she didn’t pry for details. She just said, "It’s actually very brave of you to share that so honestly."

My eyes welled up right then and there. It wasn’t a cry of despair, it was… the feeling of being seen? I’ve always thought that admitting something is wrong with me was synonymous with failure. Being independent, self-sufficient, not relying on anyone—I’ve held onto those things for so many years. But today I suddenly realized that maybe I had it all wrong.

Honesty is not weakness.

I wrote that sentence over and over in my journal, as if trying to convince myself.

My sleep has been terrible lately. I’m still awake at three or four in the morning, ruminating on meaningless things—like something a friend said ten years ago, or worrying that there’s something wrong with me again. Dr. Li says this is a manifestation of anxiety. I’ve known that for a while, but knowing it and admitting it are two different things.

I realize now that my anxiety has seeped into every corner of my life. It’s not just the insomnia; it’s the hyper-fixation on my health, the overanalyzing of every social interaction, the inexplicable dread of the future. I’ve been trapped in this room with these things for a long time.

Sophie asked me what my plan was. I told her I’m going to keep going to therapy. She just nodded, as if it were the most natural thing in the world.

Maybe… maybe I don’t need to treat this like a secret. Maybe I don’t have to carry this alone.

It’s almost midnight now, but my heart feels calmer than it has in months. Not a fake calm, but a sense of—being allowed to be vulnerable.

Onward tomorrow.

Dr. Li, I was thinking about what you said while I was in the shower just now.

"Very brave. Now, give yourself some space."

I don’t know why, but those words carried much more weight than I expected. I think I was waiting for a more complex response—like you telling me what I should do next, or what I should prepare for. But you didn’t. You just called it "brave," and then told me to stop.

The problem now is, I don’t really know how to "give myself space." I mean—the email has already been sent; all I can do is wait. But the waiting itself is consuming me. I keep wondering if my brother will see it, when he’ll see it, and what he’ll think once he does. I’m even wondering if I should have phrased things differently, if maybe I should have...

But I know there’s no point in thinking like that. It’s already done.

What I want to ask is, what does "giving yourself space" actually mean in practice? Does it mean I shouldn’t check my inbox? Or that I should do something else to distract myself? Or does it mean I should just allow myself to feel anxious, instead of trying to "fix" it?

I’m sitting on the sofa right now with my phone right next to me. I can feel the urge to refresh it over and over again. But I’m trying my best not to.

You’re right, I did do something brave. But what comes after the bravery?

Uncle Zhang, I wanted to say thank you to you one more time.

That phone call yesterday really helped me out a lot. I was sitting in front of my computer, my fingers were literally shaking, and my mind was a total mess—I had no idea what to do. You just listened to me so calmly and said those words: "What is there to be afraid of? Whatever will be, will be." I actually laughed when you said that, even though my eyes were a bit watery.

I’ve been thinking that there really aren't many people like you. You don't try to "fix" my problems, and you don't say those things that sound right but are actually just empty. You just stay there, you listen, and then you say something real and warm.

I’m talking to my brother tomorrow. I feel much better now than I did yesterday. I’m still nervous, but it’s not that feeling of drowning anymore. It’s more like... anticipation, I guess. Maybe a little bit of fear too, but I feel like I can handle it.

You know, I’ve lived in this building for so many years, and I never imagined a neighbor could become someone like this. I mean, someone who truly shows up right when you need them most.

I’ll cook something for you one of these days—that dish you like best—as my way of saying thanks. Don't try to be polite and turn it down; I really want to make it.

Thank you, Uncle Zhang. Truly.

Dr. Li, I have to be honest with you. I’ve given up on meditation.

It’s the one you suggested I try last time. I stuck with it for three weeks, but yesterday, I just quit entirely. I know that sounds like a failure, but I felt I should be upfront with you about it.

The first five times I tried, my mind was like a bird trapped in a cage, just constantly crashing into the sides. I’d sit there, close my eyes, and think, "Okay, I’m meditating now," but my head would be full of everything—should I check my email, that editor still hasn't gotten back to me on my draft, was it this time of year when my mom passed away... it just wouldn't stop. I’d open my eyes to check the clock, and only two minutes had passed.

But here’s the strange part. Twice—I remember it clearly—I just... relaxed. It wasn't because I did anything right; it was because I gave up. Once, I was sitting by the window thinking, "Forget it, I just can't do this," and instead, I felt this very strange sense of calm. Just for a moment. Another time was in the middle of the night; I wasn't even trying to meditate, I was just sitting there drinking tea, and I suddenly realized I hadn't thought about anything for a long time.

So now I’m a bit confused. Does this mean I’m just not cut out for meditation? Or... am I misunderstanding something? Those two times it actually worked, I wasn't really doing anything. I had just given up. Does that even count?

I’m not trying to make excuses. I really did try. But I’d like to hear what you think.

I sat in front of the bookshelf, holding a sticky note written in blue ink: "Bought *Being and Nothingness* at a secondhand bookstore in 1987. I was still at the publishing house back then, and I used to go to that shop every weekend."

Behind me was the result of an entire afternoon’s work—every item now had a label next to it. Some were long, others just a few words. I realized that as I was writing them, my tone had completely shifted. It was no longer "I never got around to organizing this," but rather "This represents what I was thinking at that moment."

It’s 8:15 PM now. It’s quiet outside the window. I opened my phone and found the chat window for someone I know from the Cultural Salon’s WeChat circle.

---

**I thought for a moment and started typing:**

I’ve been sorting through my things lately and discovered something interesting. Behind every item I couldn't bring myself to throw away, there’s a story—some about myself, some about the era we lived through. I’ve written labels for all of them, recording these stories in the gentlest language I can find.

To be honest, the process has left me feeling a bit overwhelmed. But at the same time, there’s this indescribable sense of fulfillment.

I was wondering, would you be interested in coming over sometime to take a look? Nothing formal, just... to sit for a while, and I can tell you the stories behind these objects. You might find it boring, but I really want to share this. These past few days, I’ve realized that maybe connection is as simple as that—just having someone willing to listen.

What do you think? (No pressure at all if you're busy.)

---

I paused and reread it. I deleted "If you have time" and changed it to "What do you think?"

Then I added one more line:

Lately, I’ve been thinking that maybe I’ve been misunderstanding something. These unfinished things, these messy collections... maybe they are complete just as they are.

---

I stared at the screen, not sending it immediately. This tentative invitation made me a little nervous, but also a little hopeful. I know they might decline, and I know they might come. But either way, I want to try.

# Phone Memo

**The Breathing Technique Dr. Li Taught Me — Write this down so I don't forget**

Time: October 5, 2024, Morning

---

## Steps (Must follow the order)

1. Find a comfortable position to sit, lean against something.
2. Inhale through the nose, count to 4 (take it slow, don't rush).
3. Hold your breath, count to 7.
4. Exhale through the mouth, count to 8 (this is the most important part, go slow).
5. Repeat 5-10 times.

Dr. Li says it's called the "4-7-8 technique." Sounds simple, but it actually works.

---

## When to Use It

- Before bed (tried it last night, it really helped).
- When feeling anxious (like before the call with Lin Hao this afternoon?).
- When my heart starts racing.
- At 3 AM when I start overthinking everything again.

---

## First Impressions

It’s strange. It’s not that the anxiety disappears—it’s still there. But while I’m doing the breathing, it’s like... I’m allowing myself to feel it? Instead of constantly fighting it.

Like Dr. Li said: "It’s not about making the anxiety go away; it’s about learning to coexist with it."

I never thought about it that way before. I always felt like anxiety was this "bad thing" I had to get rid of immediately. But when I slow down and count the numbers one by one, the anxiety becomes... something I can observe? Rather than something that drowns me.

It sounds a bit "woo-woo," but it really works.

Video call with Lin Hao at 2 PM.

Hands are shaking a bit.

## Notes

December 12, 2024, Evening

I spent an hour on the phone with Professor Chen. She’s still encouraging me, saying that reflection itself has value and that my writing deserves to be seen by more people. As I listened to her, I kept asking myself: what has reflection actually changed? Has it really changed anything at all?

I told her I wanted to give up on the essay collection project. She seemed a bit startled and asked me why. I couldn't give her a clear reason, but the thought just won't go away—I feel like I’m trying to use this book to prove my worth. And that, in itself, is a mistake.

Saying it out loud even startled me. But that is how I truly feel.

I pulled out those old articles and read through them over and over. Some are decent; others seem too long-winded and wordy now. How am I supposed to choose? Which ones are actually worth keeping? I don’t know. And—who is going to read this book anyway? Will anyone actually read it? Or am I just deluding myself, imagining that someone cares about what I’ve been thinking all these years?

Professor Chen said this book is something I want to leave behind. Hearing those words made my heart ache. Yes, I want to leave something behind. But I also wonder, is there really any meaning in leaving these things? Or am I just wrestling with my own mortality?

I need to commit time to this project and give up some freelance opportunities. But I’m also afraid of losing that flexibility. I’m afraid of being tied down. Then again, maybe I shouldn't even have this fear. Maybe I should just let it go and return to my old life—keep writing articles, attending salons, and hunting for books at flea markets. At least that kind of life doesn't require me to prove anything.

But I also know that if I really give up, I’ll regret it. I’ll wake up at three in the morning thinking about this decision, overanalyzing every "what if."

So here I am, spinning in this contradiction. I want to quit, yet I can't let go. I crave encouragement, yet I doubt every word of it.

Professor Chen’s final words were to give myself some time. Not to rush into a decision.

But I don’t have that much time left. That is the truest thing I feel right now.

March 25, 2022 – Past 11:00 PM

I’m sitting on my bed, holding that appointment slip. The paper is already crumpled from my grip.

At 2:30 this afternoon, I sat in a cafe for an hour. I originally just wanted a cup of coffee to steady my nerves before going, but I ended up going to the restroom four times. Four times. Each time it was because my chest felt tight, like something was lodged there. The person in the mirror looked terrible. I asked myself: Lin Xiaoyun, what are you doing?

I even opened my phone, wanting to text Dr. Li to say I wasn’t coming. My finger hovered over the screen for a long time. I could think of a hundred excuses—a last-minute draft to finish, feeling unwell, or simply saying this whole thing was a mistake. But I didn't hit send.

 those twenty minutes in the waiting room felt longer than twenty hours. My legs were shaking. Honestly, I pressed down hard on my knees to try and hide it, but the trembling came from deep inside my bones; I couldn't control it. I stared at a landscape painting on the opposite wall—some place I’ve never been, with blue skies and white clouds. It felt incredibly ironic.

Then Dr. Li came out. She just said one thing: "I know how hard this step is."

Just that one sentence.

And the tears just started falling. I didn't even realize I was crying.

We talked for an hour. I said a lot—about the sleep, about those 3:00 AM terrors, about how I ended up like this. I sounded like I was defending myself, but I was actually accusing myself. She didn't interrupt me, nor was she in a rush to give me answers. Finally, she said that maybe we could keep talking and "see what’s happening."

"See what’s happening"—it’s a strange way to put it. As if I don’t even know what’s happening to me.

Now that I’m back home, I feel both relieved and terrified. Relieved because I finally said it out loud. Terrified because… what if this becomes an official diagnosis? What if there really is something "wrong" with me? I’ve been in denial, perhaps because I knew that once I admitted it, there would be no going back.

But I can’t go back anyway. I know that.

My hands are still shaking slightly.

I need to sleep. I hope I can fall asleep tonight.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something that happened recently.

The day before yesterday, Professor Chen invited me for coffee. He mentioned that he hopes I can compile my lecture materials from over the years into a book for publication. It’s not the first time I’ve heard this suggestion, but my reaction this time was very different.

I didn’t immediately refuse like I used to, nor did I jump at the chance with excitement. I simply said, "Let me think about it."

Sitting there in the café, I suddenly realized what that response meant to me. In the past, I would have either declined out of a fear of failure or rushed to agree out of a hunger for validation. But this time, I was truly just asking myself—do I actually want to do this? I wasn't asking, "Am I worthy of being published?" or "Will others approve of me?"

What’s even stranger is that as I sat there for a long time, a thought came to me: I no longer need to prove my worth through a publication.

I was a bit surprised when that thought surfaced. I could feel that it wasn't about giving up or being self-defeating. It was... I don't know how to describe it. It’s just the feeling that my value is already there, whether a book exists or not.

When the people in the reading group listen to me speak—their expressions, their questions, the way they say, "Your perspective made me rethink this"—in those moments, I can feel my own worth. It doesn't need an ISBN.

I wanted to ask you, does this mean anything? Is this progress? Or am I just kidding myself, using "no need to prove anything" as a way to mask a deeper fear?

I’m a bit unsure. But I wanted to tell you this because even this sense of uncertainty doesn't seem as scary as it used to be.

---

## Why am I thinking about this now?

While tidying up my room yesterday, I stumbled upon articles I’ve written over the past decade. My first instinct was to toss them, but as I started reading... there’s actually something there. Professor Chen’s words have been echoing in my head—maybe I really should try publishing a collection?

I don’t know if it’s just because I’ve been so exhausted lately, but I suddenly feel that if these pieces remain scattered across various magazines and websites, they’ll eventually leave no trace behind.

---

## Potential Articles (Rough Timeline)

- **2007–2012**: The years right after I left the publishing house; my most prolific period. Mostly commentaries on contemporary literature and shifts in the publishing industry. They seem a bit dated now, but... perhaps some perspectives still hold water?
- **2013–2018**: The middle period; writing was more sporadic. Some pieces on photography and visual culture, some book reviews. The quality is a bit hit-or-miss.
- **2019–2023**: The recent years. Mostly social commentary; some of it is quite good, though some pieces feel rushed.

**The problem is**: Exactly how many pieces are there? I need to track them all down. They’re likely scattered across different email accounts, old computers, and even hard copies. That in itself is a massive undertaking.

---

## Possible Publication Formats

- **Academic Press**: More formal, but the process is long and the requirements are strict. Plus, strictly speaking, my work isn't "academic papers"...
- **Cultural/Trade Publisher**: Probably a better fit? But it’s competitive, and I’m not sure if anyone would be interested.
- **Self-Publishing**: Do I have the money? No. And it feels a bit... I’m not sure I want to go down that road.
- **Online Release** (E-book or platform): Low cost, but it carries a strong sense of "self-distribution." It feels a bit like giving up.
- **Joint Publication**: With other authors? But among the people I know... there don't seem to be any such opportunities.

**The most realistic direction**: I should probably ask Professor Chen first. He said he was impressed; maybe he has connections or advice. There’s also that editor who handled my articles before—what was their name?—maybe I could consult them.

---

## To-Do List (If I actually move forward with this)

1. **Compile the article list** ✗ (Haven't started)
   - Find every published piece.
   - Organize them chronologically or by theme.
   - Evaluate which are worth including and which are too outdated.

2. **Contact Professor Chen** ✗ (A bit nervous)
   - Ask if he still thinks it’s a good idea.
   - See if he knows anyone in publishing.
   - Get his honest opinion on the work (not just polite encouragement).

3. **Reread and Revise** ✗ (This will be time-consuming)
   - Some articles might need updates or additional context.
   - Check for outdated views that need adjusting.
   - Standardize style and formatting.

4. **Define Theme and Framework**
   - Chronological or thematic?
   - Should I add a preface or an afterword?
   - What is this collection trying to say?

---

## Honest Doubts

- Is this actually worth publishing? Or am I just kidding myself?
- Will anyone read it? Or is this just a way to reassure myself that "I’ve done something"?
- Is it worth the time and energy, especially given my current workload?
- What if it gets rejected? Can I handle that?

...But if I don't try, I'll never know. And these articles are, in a way, a record of my life over these years. Maybe that’s enough in itself.

---

## Next Steps

Try to find all the articles this weekend. Not necessarily every single one, but at least get a list going. Then... we’ll see.

Maybe email Professor Chen first? Just to see if he remembers his suggestion.

---

*(11:15 Update: Just found a 2015 piece on urban memory. It’s actually quite good. It makes me feel that maybe there really is something here worth preserving.)*

July 12, 2024, Friday, past 11:00 PM

My conversation with Professor Chen is still looping in my head. I’m sitting at my desk, holding a cup of tea that went cold long ago, looking out at the city lights.

He said, "The best creative work comes from having enough space and time to think."

My eyes welled up a bit when I heard that. I’m not entirely sure why.

This afternoon, I told him I’ve decided to put the publication of that essay collection on hold. The editor has been pushing for it, and Professor Chen had previously suggested I compile it for publication. I was tempted myself—after all, it represents a kind of recognition, an "achievement." But over the last couple of days, I kept thinking: if I really commit to this project, I won’t have the time to properly prepare for my lectures, nor the headspace to think about next month’s reading group theme. I’d be shackled by deadlines, proofreading, and endless logistics.

What I fear is that I’ll turn back into that person who is always in a rush, doing things just for the sake of finishing them, with all my passion drained by the deadlines.

When I shared this with Professor Chen, he didn’t try to change my mind. Instead, he said those words.

Thinking about it now, this might be the first time I’ve proactively said "no" to an "opportunity." It wasn't out of anxiety or a fear of failure, but because I wanted to protect something—I wanted to protect my love for what I do. The reading groups, the lectures, the reviews—these things feel real and alive to me right now. I don’t want to turn them into just another item on a to-do list.

And yet... I keep asking myself: is this just my habitual retreat disguised as something else? Am I truly protecting my passion, or am I just finding another excuse to avoid things that require commitment and perseverance?

Sixty-two years old, and I’m still wrestling with this. Sometimes I feel incredibly mature, like I truly understand the meaning of "knowing when to stop." But then I’ll turn around and think that maybe I’m just finding a poetic explanation for my own laziness and fear.

Still... the look in Professor Chen’s eyes today was sincere. He wasn’t just patronizing me; he genuinely believed what I said. It makes me feel that perhaps this decision isn't an escape, but a choice of authenticity.

I have enough space to think now. The feeling is unfamiliar, but precious.

Tomorrow, I’ll get back to refining the opening for the lecture.

October 18, 2022, Afternoon

Just stepped out of Dr. Li’s clinic. Walking down the street, my head is still a mess.

She asked me a question that I’m still chewing over—"If no one needs your commentary, does your knowledge still have value?"

I paused for a long time. A very long time. I could feel all my defenses crumbling during that silence. It hit on something I’ve been asking myself repeatedly these past few days, but from a completely different angle.

I said... maybe it does. Maybe value isn't defined solely by the market.

It sounded weak. I could hear the uncertainty in my own voice. But I said it.

This is entirely different from what I’ve been repeating to myself at home: "My knowledge no longer has any value." Back then, I was certain. As certain as if I were stating a fact. But now... now I’m not so sure.

Maybe it’s precisely because I’m not sure that I feel somewhat alive?

I don’t know. I really don’t know.

Back home, sitting in front of my bookshelf. Looking at these books, everything I’ve accumulated over the years. Some I’ve read three times; some are covered in dense notes. Others I’ve only flipped through a few pages before setting aside. But they are all there. They have all existed.

Maybe... maybe this is the value itself? Not because someone is buying my commentary, but because... because I actually thought about these things. I actually cared.

I’m still thinking about that "maybe."

It’s a word that has felt foreign to me lately. I’ve grown used to absolute sentences. "Must," "already," "forever." But "maybe"—it opens a crack. A tiny, fragile crack.

I don’t know if this crack will close. But for now, it’s still open.

I’m exhausted. But at least I’m still thinking.

I was just on the phone with Professor Chen, and he was pushing me again to keep working on the essay collection. He said things like, "This is the culmination of your career," and "Your ideas deserve to be seen." It all sounds right, but the more I listened, the more uncomfortable I felt.

I’ve realized that I might be doing something quite absurd. I want to publish this book not because I truly believe these articles are so important, but because I’m trying to use it to prove something to myself—to prove that my years of work haven't been a waste, that my choices were right. The more Professor Chen talked, the more clearly I could see this. It makes me feel like a hypocrite.

You know, I’m even starting to want to give up on the whole project. Just let it go. Because I feel like if I keep going, I’m just lying to myself. I’m using a book to fill some kind of void—maybe it’s a fear of aging, or a fear of death. But is there really any meaning in doing that?

I’m not looking for comfort. I’m asking: is this a form of escapism? Am I using the excuse that "it’s meaningless" to run away from what I’m truly afraid of—like failure, or being judged?

I’m very confused right now. I don’t know if I should push through or give up. More importantly, I don’t know why I spiral into self-doubt so easily.

Could you find some time to talk before next week? Or how do you think I should handle this state of mind?

November 3, 2022, Evening

Just got back from the bookstore. I’m a bit tired, but it’s a different kind of tired. Not that hollow, hopeless exhaustion, but… how should I put it? It’s the kind of fatigue you feel after something inside you has been activated.

I was actually really nervous when I spoke up at the salon today. Sitting there, looking at everyone’s faces, I almost lost the courage to open my mouth. It’s a familiar feeling—for years, all I’ve done is wrap myself up tight, pretending everything is fine and that I’m perfectly content with my life. But today, I just… said it. "I’ve been going through a difficult time lately." It sounds so simple, but I could feel myself trembling as the words came out.

The most surprising part was that no one turned away. No one gave me that "you should be more positive" kind of advice. They just… listened. One friend said she’d gone through a similar period of feeling lost; another said he’s currently rethinking the meaning of his work. In that moment, I realized I’d always assumed I was alone, but in reality… maybe I’ve just been sitting in the dark by myself for too long.

What Dr. Li said suddenly felt concrete. "Value isn't only defined by the market"—it sounds like an empty platitude, but when my friends listened intently to my "market-worthless" ideas, I suddenly understood. They weren't assessing my commercial value; they were… understanding me. And that, in itself, is a form of value.

On the subway home, I was thinking: I’ve been running away all these years. I ran from constraints, so I chose to freelance; but at the same time, I was running from connection, using "that’s just who I am" as an excuse to isolate myself. Freedom turned into loneliness.

Maybe true freedom isn't about being unconstrained, but about being able to be your authentic self—even when that self is fragile, confused, and imperfect. Today was the first time I showed that self to a group of people.

Tired, but not the bad kind of tired.

I want to give Professor Chen a call tomorrow. I’m not sure why, but I suddenly want to talk to him about those questions regarding "value."

Good morning, Dr. Li. I know it’s very early, but I just couldn't help messaging you.

I woke up at 6:30 AM and couldn't get back to sleep at all. The first thing I did was grab my phone to check my email—you know, the one I sent to my brother. I’ve been re-reading it over and over, and my fingers haven't stopped shaking. I’ve started second-guessing my wording, feeling like some sentences sound too harsh, or maybe too weak—just not right.

Then I started calculating the time difference. He’s in Europe, so it should be around 11 PM there? Or is it the middle of the night? I’ve calculated it over and over, trying to figure out if he’s seen it yet. If he has, what is he thinking? If he hasn't, when will he? I’m just lying here in bed with a timer going off in my head, forcing myself to do the math again every few minutes.

It’s strange, isn't it? I’m aware that I’m doing this, but I can’t seem to stop. I know it’s pointless—him reading the email won't change just because I’m calculating the time—but I just can’t control it. My fingers keep tapping on the table, and they’re still shaking now.

I wanted to ask you, is this a symptom of anxiety? Or is it just a normal nervous reaction? How should I handle these intrusive thoughts? Is there any way to stop myself from checking my email repeatedly?

But… I also wanted to tell you that despite how miserable I feel right now, I’m proud of myself. I actually sent it. Regardless of how he responds—or if he doesn't respond at all—I’ve broken the silence. That’s a victory in itself, right?

But right now, I’m just so restless. I’m sorry for bothering you this early.

Next Wednesday. Another week to wait.

Dr. Li, I wanted to reach out before our next session. Something happened yesterday, and I’m still not quite over it.

While I was organizing some old drafts for my collected essays, I came across some photos from over twenty years ago. They were just ordinary shots—me in a bookstore when I was younger, some photos from publishing house gatherings. I looked at myself in those pictures, and suddenly... I just broke down.

My hands started shaking. I sat on the floor and cried for about half an hour, not even sure what I was crying about. Later, I realized I was talking to the version of myself in the photos in the second person—"You had no idea what was coming," "You didn't know they were going to leave you." I was talking about my parents.

I know it sounds a bit strange. But that was the feeling—looking at my younger self, I suddenly became acutely aware that time is moving forward, while I am looking backward. My father passed away in 2008, and my mother in 2015. I’m 62 now. This essay collection project has forced me to dig all of this up, to see my own transformation, and that change... it scares me.

I re-read some of the pieces I wrote over twenty years ago. To be honest, I look down on those ideas now. They were too shallow, too idealistic. How could I have thought that way? But that sparked another fear—if my past thoughts seem so flawed now, will my current thoughts be dismissed by my future self as well? It feels like a loop that won't stop.

I’m starting to think that maybe this project itself was a mistake. Maybe I shouldn't be trying to preserve these things. Maybe no matter what I do, time keeps slipping away, death is waiting, and leaving these words behind won't change a thing.

Is it normal to feel this way? How should I handle this?

I’m still feeling a bit... unsure of what I’m even thinking right now.

The article went live today.

I felt a lump in my throat when I saw one particular comment: "You said exactly what I’ve been wanting to say but couldn't find the words for."

While I was revising the draft these past few days, I kept agonizing over the wording. I cut out a lot of the academic, rigorous phrasing and eventually left this sentence: "In reading, I found the meaning of solitude, and the possibility of connecting with strangers." At the time, I wondered if it was too personal, not "professional" enough.

But seeing that comment, I suddenly realized—maybe being "professional" was never the most important thing. What matters is whether you actually said something real.

I’ve been feeling this at the book club lately, too. Someone will say, "I never thought of it from that angle," or just sit there quietly with a certain spark in their eyes. Those moments make me feel more fulfilled than any view count ever could.

Maybe this is what I’ve been looking for all along—not just to be seen, but to truly connect with someone.

Goodnight. Back at it tomorrow.

A bit nervous. A bit excited, too.

I hope I can stick with it this time.

Professor Chen, do you have a moment? I’d like to talk to you about the essay collection.

To be honest, I’ve been going through those old articles over the past few days, re-reading them. It’s been a bit of a painful process, if I’m being frank. I’ve realized that many of the ideas seem quite shallow now; there are even some viewpoints I no longer agree with. You know, some of those pieces felt so profound when I wrote them, but looking at them now, they just feel... how should I put it? Ordinary. Utterly ordinary.

I’ve been wondering: is this kind of work really worth publishing? I mean, if even I find these ideas problematic, won't readers see it as nothing more than the self-indulgence of a 62-year-old woman?

When you first suggested I publish this collection, I felt very encouraged. But now I’m... I don’t know, I’m starting to have doubts. Perhaps I overestimated the depth of my own thinking. Maybe all these years of writing haven't amounted to any real substance, just a repetition of clichés.

I’m also behind schedule. I originally wanted to have the first draft ready before the Spring Festival, but that’s looking unlikely now. I keep getting stuck on this one question: should I even go on? What is the point of continuing?

It’s not that I’m giving up, it’s just... I’d like to hear your thoughts. Do you still think this project is worth doing? Or rather, how should I judge whether something truly has value?

Are you busy lately?

I kept opening and closing WeChat on my phone, over and over again.

Lin Hao’s chat window remained unchanged—the last message was a single "Mhm" he sent two weeks ago.

I was sitting in a taxi after leaving the clinic. Outside the window were the familiar city streets, but my heart was still back in Dr. Li’s warm office. When she said, "Maybe you deserve to be loved," I cried. That isn't something I usually allow myself to do.

Now, I wanted to tell him.

I opened the chat, deleted, and opened it again.

"Hey, I wanted to tell you something. I saw a therapist today. I know it sounds... I don't know. But I was thinking, maybe this is what we need too—to be seen, rather than judged."

I read it three times. It was too much. Too exposed.

Delete.

"I’ve been thinking about us lately. About connecting more."

That felt too light, like I was pretending everything was simple.

I deleted it again.

What I actually wanted to say was: I realized today that I might be worthy of love, and it made me think of you, and all those years we’ve wasted. I don’t want to wait anymore. But I’m also scared—scared that you still won’t respond, scared that saying it out loud will only make things worse.

My finger hovered over the screen, but I didn't press down.

Outside the car window, someone was laughing. I watched them, then looked back at my draft.

Maybe in a little while. Maybe I need to breathe first. Maybe...

I put my phone down. But I didn't close the chat.

# Work Log 2024.01.15

**Email Exchange with the Editor — New Protocol for Deadline Management**

Just replied to the follow-up email from the editor at *Cultural Horizons*. This time, I didn't make excuses. I was blunt: my deadline management has always been terrible.

My fingers paused over the keyboard for a second as I typed that. But I kept going.

---

**New Framework (Let's see if I can stick to it):**

1. **Advance Notification Mechanism**
   - Confirm whether I can meet the deadline within 48 hours of receiving a topic.
   - If there’s a risk, notify them a week in advance instead of flaking out at the last minute.
   - No more vague phrasing like "I might be a bit late."

2. **Work Breakdown**
   - Break a 3,000-word draft into three 1,000-word segments.
   - Set individual deadlines for each segment (instead of piling all the pressure onto the end).
   - Send the first two segments to the editor as they are finished to allow room for feedback and adjustments.

3. **Buffer Time**
   - Move the submission target up by two days.
   - Leave space for self-review and revisions.
   - Stop starting the "I think this point could be deepened" phase at 2:00 AM on the due date.

---

**To be honest, none of this is a new discovery. I know what my problems are. But this time is different—I didn't defend myself, and I didn't blame it on "the inherently unpredictable nature of creative work."**

I just said: This is my problem, and I need to fix it.

The editor replied quickly. She said, "Thank you for your honesty."

That sentence stung a little. Not because of guilt, but because—why did it take me so long to do something so simple?

---

**To-Do:**
- [ ] Organize the three current topics and re-plan them according to the new framework.
- [ ] Add this protocol to my calendar reminders (don't just rely on memory).
- [ ] Check progress next Monday.

**Reflections:**
Maybe I’ve always treated "freelancing" as a synonym for "doing whatever I want." But freedom isn't the absence of boundaries. Freedom should be the ability to choose when I work and what I write—but once a commitment is made, I must be responsible for it.

This sounds like something my father would say. He always said, "Freedom and discipline are two sides of the same coin." I thought it was a cliché back then.

Now, at 62, I finally realize he was right.

Thursday, May 2, 2024  Sunny

I just stepped out of Dr. Li’s clinic. Walking down the street, her words are still looping in my head.

She asked me, "What do these activities make you feel?" I could name the feelings—the book club makes me feel heard, the lectures make me feel like I still have something to share, and writing that cultural column makes me feel... needed? No, that’s not quite it. Then I just said it, a bit abruptly, a bit self-consciously: "I want to be seen. I want to know that my voice is being heard."

The moment I said it, I wanted to laugh at myself. Talking like this at my age.

But Dr. Li didn't laugh. She just nodded and said, "But you *are* being seen right now. The people at the book club are listening to you, the students at the lectures are listening, and the people reading your articles are listening too."

I should have felt comforted. Instead, I countered with a question: "The question is, do you believe there is any value in being seen this way?"

She paused for a moment, then asked, "Do you?"

I didn't give her a direct answer.

Now, sitting at home and looking out the window, I’m wondering—why is it that I can do all these things—organize book clubs, write articles, give lectures—and yet I still don’t believe any of it has value? It’s as if I’m performing the role of a "person of value," but I don't believe the act myself.

In the past, my anxiety came from feeling like I wasn't enough—not successful enough, not recognized enough by the market. Now I’ve done these things, people show up, people listen, but the anxiety hasn't disappeared; it’s just wearing a different mask. Now I worry—does this actually count? An unpaid book club, a cultural column that nobody reads, a lecture for a few dozen students... does any of this truly matter?

Or am I just using the word "meaningful" to lie to myself, to convince myself that I haven't been completely phased out?

I don't know.

Tomorrow I have to prepare for the first session of the book club. I chose *The Unbearable Lightness of Being*. It’s a bit ironic. Right now, I’m the one bearing this weight—the weight between believing and not believing.
