February 25, 2024, 9:30 PM

It’s been three days since I received the letter from my brother, and I still can’t talk about it normally.

I took it out and read it again last night. That one sentence—"I am proud of you"—I don’t know why it cuts so deep. I sat there surrounded by piles of books, tears streaming down my face like an idiot. In over forty years, I’ve never heard him say that. We grew up in the same house, lived through the same era, yet it feels like there’s an invisible river flowing between us. He went overseas, I stayed here, and we slowly became strangers. I can’t even remember the last time we had a real conversation.

But what made me cry the most wasn't the phrase "I am proud of you" itself. It was what he wrote next: "Your anxiety is not weakness; it is your sincere response to this world."

When I read that, I completely broke down.

For so many years, I’ve felt ashamed of my sensitivity. The 3 AM insomnia, the way I overanalyze someone’s tone over a single sentence, the sudden surges of dread—I treated all of these as defects. I felt I was too fragile, too easily triggered. I even thought that if I could just be more pragmatic like Zhang Ming, or more hardened like everyone else, my life might be easier.

But my brother said it’s not weakness. It’s sincerity.

I don’t know how to describe this moment. It’s like someone suddenly threw open a window and let the light in. I have spent my life in a state of constant self-criticism, always trying to prove I was worthy of love—through work, through achievements, through never letting anyone down. But my brother’s letter told me that I don’t need to prove anything at all. I was always worthy of love. Simply because I am me.

The thought makes me feel both terrified and relieved.

Terrified because, if I no longer need to prove myself, does that mean all these years of anxiety and effort were meaningless? Have I wasted all this time on self-denial for nothing?

But relieved because, perhaps, I can finally stop. Perhaps I can allow myself to just exist—sensitive, contradictory, sometimes fragile—without feeling guilty for it.

I wrote him a long letter back. I told him that his words changed something. I don’t know exactly what has changed, but I can feel something shifting inside me.

Maybe this is what reconciliation is. It’s not that all our problems are solved, or that we’ve suddenly become the closest of siblings. But in this moment, we finally saw each other. He saw my sincerity, and I saw his care. That "seeing" is enough.

I’m sitting by the window now, looking out at the city lights. I remember my mother once saying that sensitive people feel the beauty of this world just as much as they feel its pain. I’ve always only remembered the pain. Maybe it’s time to look at the beauty.

Or, at the very least, to allow myself to see both the beauty and the pain. Without having to choose between them.

Dr. Li, I wanted to share something that just happened with you.

Last night, while I was tidying up my bookshelf, I came across a postcard my brother sent me a long, long time ago. It was sent from abroad, maybe ten years ago? I couldn’t remember exactly when I received it, but the moment I saw his handwriting, I just sat there on the floor and cried.

It wasn’t a cry of sadness. It was more like... a sudden realization.

I wrote him a very long letter. I was completely honest—about the years I’ve spent in therapy, my regrets over our estrangement, and something I’ve only just understood: I’ve been running away. My obsession with freedom and my insistence on independence were, in part, a way to avoid relationships that require commitment and sustained effort. I used "respecting each other’s independence" as an excuse to wall myself off from my brother, and from so many others.

After I finished the letter, I sat in my room for a long time. I felt a sense of release, but also a lot of trepidation. I don’t know if he’ll write back, or what this letter even means. But I knew I had to send it.

You once said that sometimes change starts like this—not because we’ve figured out some grand truth, but because we’re finally willing to see what we’re actually doing. I think I’m in the middle of that process right now.

I’m not looking for advice. I just wanted you to know that I’m changing. And... I think this time it’s for real.

Lin Xiaoyun

I dug up my diary from 1982.

Back then, I wrote: "I am going to change the publishing world. I will make sure the ignored voices are heard." I drew a circle underneath it, like some kind of oath.

And now? It’s 2023, and I’m still doing the same thing. It’s just that nobody cares anymore.

——

Maybe it’s not that "nobody cares." Maybe it’s that I don’t care anymore.

Or rather, I’ve changed my understanding of what "caring" means.

——

Where did that girl go?

I don’t mean she’s dead. She’s still here. Sitting in my house, surrounded by piles of books, flipping through words written forty years ago and crying like a fool.

But where is her passion? Her certainty?

How did she become this person—always asking "did I waste it all," always calculating how much longer she can hold on, always doubting her own worth.

——

No. Maybe the question isn't where she went.

Maybe the problem is this: I have been waiting for someone else to tell me I have value.

In 1982, I thought changing the publishing world would prove it.
After the divorce in 2005, I thought going freelance would prove it.
Now, I thought organizing book clubs and attending lectures would prove it.

But none of it did.

Because I’ve been looking outward the whole time.

——

At the book club the other day, there was a girl—maybe in her early twenties—who said my comments changed how she understood a book.

I didn't think much of it at the time. I just said, "That’s good."

But thinking back now, that moment was real. It wasn't about changing "the publishing world," and it wasn't about proving anything.

It was just... one person saying to another: "Your thoughts helped me see something different."

Maybe that’s what the girl in 1982 actually wanted. She just didn't know it.

——

What have I done?

I’ve lived. I’ve read. I’ve thought. I’ve talked to others about those thoughts.

Sometimes it feels like doing nothing at all.

Sometimes it feels like the only thing that matters.

——

Maybe failure isn't about doing something wrong.

Maybe failure is simply stopping.

Am I still doing it?

February 19, 2022. Night.

I was sitting in the dark when Zhou Fang called. I hadn't turned on the lights, wasn't reading—I was just sitting there. She said, "I’ve been watching this documentary about anxiety disorders lately." At the time, I didn't quite register why she was telling me this.

But she didn't ask, "What's wrong with you?" She just... said that.

And then, somehow, I started talking. The insomnia. Checking the gas over and over. The terrors that bolt me awake at three in the morning. I’ve never spoken about these things so clearly to anyone before. Hearing my own voice say them felt like listening to a stranger.

Zhou Fang said, "It’s not your fault. This is a diagnosable and treatable mental health condition."

I paused for a long time. A very long time.

"Really?" I heard myself ask. My voice was tiny.

I don’t know what I was asking. Really diagnosable? Really treatable? Or... really not my fault?

She gave me Dr. Li’s number. I said I would go. I heard myself say it, but I don't quite believe it’s real. I’m sitting here now with that number in my hand, still not quite believing it.

But...

Maybe something has changed. Maybe not. I don’t know.

All I know is that Zhou Fang didn't ask "What's wrong with you." She just told me she was watching a documentary. And just like that, I was seen.

It’s strange.

I’ve turned the light on now.

# May 10, 2024, Night

On the subway, two stops away from home.

When the lecture ended today, a girl walked up to me and said, "This was the most interesting lecture I’ve ever attended, because you weren't just giving us answers—you were inviting us to think." I was a bit stunned at the time, not knowing how to respond, so I just smiled. Now, sitting here on the train, those words are still circling in my head.

There was a moment during the lecture that I remember vividly. A student said that *Call to Arms* reflects the dilemma of the intellectual. I was about to correct him—his interpretation overlooked Lu Xun’s focus on the consciousness of the peasantry—but I suddenly stopped myself. Instead of handing him the answer, I asked, "How do you see the relationship between the positions of the intellectual and the peasant in this work?" He paused, and then he began to rethink it. The atmosphere in the entire classroom seemed to come alive.

I wrote in my notebook: "Guidance is more powerful than instruction." In that moment, I truly felt it—a deep sense of fulfillment. It wasn't because I had shown off how much I knew, but because I saw them actually starting to think.

But now... as the subway rumbles along, I’m starting to overthink again.

Am I avoiding something? Isn't a lecturer supposed to present knowledge systematically? I have twenty-five years of editing experience; I’ve read so many books. I should be able to give them a clear, structured framework. That student’s understanding of the intellectual *was* flawed—why didn't I just point it out? Is it because I truly believe "guidance" is better, or because I’m afraid of appearing dogmatic? Or... am I afraid that I’m actually falling behind the times, so I’m hiding behind "open-ended questions"?

I don't know.

But I also know that if I went back to being that "I am the authority, I will give you the answers" type of person, I would feel suffocated. That’s not what I want anymore.

Maybe that’s the problem—I don’t want to be a self-righteous lecturer, but I don’t want to completely abandon my knowledge and experience either. What I want is... what, exactly?

I need to email Professor Chen tomorrow; he asked if I’d like to give another lecture. I need to figure this out.

Or... maybe I don’t need to figure it out right now. Maybe this internal conflict is exactly where I should be. Maybe I’m learning to be a different kind of person, and that process is supposed to be messy.

The train has stopped. It’s time to get off.

# Thursday Night, March 28, 2024

I’ve been sitting here alone for a long while, holding Professor Chen’s email, reading that one sentence over and over: "We could include some personal, non-academic reflections in the preface; it would give the entire collection more vitality."

My first instinct was to refuse. What does "personal reflections" even mean? I’m here to publish a collection of essays, not to write an autobiography. Twenty-five years as an editor have taught me that academic works should maintain distance and objectivity. I even tried repeatedly to convince myself—it’s too exposing, too vulnerable.

But now, sitting in this room piled high with books, I find I’ve changed my mind.

I agreed.

I’m not sure when my understanding of "vulnerability" began to shift. Maybe it was the conversations with Dr. Li over the last two years, or the resumed correspondence with my brother, or perhaps it’s simply because I’m sixty-two now and have suddenly realized a few things.

I’m thinking about what would happen if I wrote it down in the preface—not those polished academic summaries, but the real things. For instance, how I felt lost among stacks of books the year my father passed away; how my mother’s days in the library shaped my understanding of knowledge; why I’ve been so obsessed with cultural criticism all these years, and why I need writing to dialogue with the world.

I could even write about how I used to be so afraid of insignificance, so afraid that my knowledge would become obsolete, so afraid of death. But now, I’m not just afraid anymore. I’m starting to see that perhaps this is exactly why I am able to write this preface.

This afternoon, I walked to the window and looked down at the street. I suddenly remembered something Old Mr. Zhang said, and the look in that young girl’s eyes at the book club—she cried while discussing *To Live*. I think that maybe these thoughts of mine, these experiences, these things that once caused me so much anxiety, might be useful to someone. They might have meaning.

This is no longer an academic obligation.

This is me saying: I lived. I thought. I was afraid, and I learned to be less afraid. And all of it is worth recording.

I’ve decided to start writing the preface tomorrow.

Not as an editor, not as a critic.

Just as Lin Xiaoyun.

3:30 AM.

Still can't sleep.

Lying here in the dark, the glow of my phone screen illuminating my face, I read Hao’s email over and over again. "I regret that there has been so much silence between us." That sentence is like a thorn, pricked deep in my heart.

I don’t know where I found the courage to write him such a long email. I sat at the computer for nearly an hour before hitting send, my fingers hovering over the keys. Deleting, rewriting, deleting again. All those things about time, about Mom and Dad, about my current struggles—I’ve never said those words to anyone, yet I chose to say them to a brother I haven't spoken to deeply in years. Maybe it’s because he’s so far away, across the Pacific, that it feels easier to be honest? Or maybe it’s just because he’s my closest living relative. He’s all I have left now.

His reply came quickly. That makes me feel even worse.

It’s as if he’s been waiting for me to break the silence all along. As if the coldness of these past years was never his choice.

I dig through my phone for some old, buried photos. Pictures of my father’s belongings I took while clearing out the room last year—his yellowed lecture notes, the bookmarks left in my mother’s books. I’ve never truly organized them. They just sit in a pile in the corner of the bookshelf; every time I see them, I look away.

I realize now that I’ve been running away. Not just from my relationship with Hao, but from so much more.

The matter of the essay collection suddenly seems less important. Or rather, the *way* it’s important has shifted. It’s no longer about whether my work is worth preserving; it’s about how much time I have left to repair these broken connections. How many more times will I get to talk to Hao?

At 3:00 AM, I lie in this old room, surrounded by stacks of books, unfinished projects, and old things I couldn't bring myself to throw away. Every item has a story, but the people in those stories are either far away or gone forever.

I think of something my father used to say—I can’t remember the exact words anymore, only the feeling: a gentle reminder about cherishing what we have.

Maybe I should write Hao another email. Not about my anxieties, but to truly ask him: How are you? Can we start over?

But it’s 3:30 AM. I’ll just keep lying here in the dark, reading that one sentence over and over.

"I regret that there has been so much silence between us."

Me too.

Professor Chen, sorry for the sudden intrusion. I attended that lecture yesterday—the one at the community bookstore.

The speaker was a woman in her fifties involved in cultural communications. She shared her story of transitioning from traditional to new media. To be honest, I didn't have high expectations going in, but her experiences... they really struck a chord with me.

She mentioned something along the lines of, "It’s never too late to change; the key is whether you’re still willing to see your own potential." At that moment, I thought that maybe change really isn't impossible. I even took the initiative to exchange contact information with her, which is something I haven't done in quite a while.

But you know what? As soon as I got home, the self-doubt kicked in. I wrote in my diary: "I’m lying to myself. I don't have her courage, nor do I have her talent." Just like that, a whole afternoon’s worth of hope was shattered by my own hand.

I wanted to ask you... do you think I still have a chance? Is it already too late for me? I look at everything I’ve done over the years—the photography, the writing, all those things I’ve collected—and it all feels so powerless now. It feels like no matter what I do, nothing ever changes.

Please don't think I'm being overly dramatic. It’s just that lately... I find it so easy to sink into these moods, and so hard to crawl back out.

June 11, 2023, Night

After the reading club ended today, I sat alone in a corner of the bookstore, and that girl’s question suddenly came back to me. She was probably in her early twenties, with a kind of anxiety in her eyes that felt all too familiar. "Professor Lin, what do you think is the meaning of life?"

Surprisingly, I didn't give one of those profound, intellectual answers I usually do. I said, "Perhaps meaning isn't some fixed thing waiting there for us to discover; maybe it’s something created in every single moment."

The moment I said it, I even surprised myself.

That isn't something the old me would have said. The old me always wanted to provide perfect, bulletproof answers. I would have quoted philosophers, recounted my own experiences, and tried to fill that void with knowledge. But today was different. I looked into her eyes, and I just... spoke the truth.

Sitting here now, I’m wondering why that sentence feels so significant to me.

Maybe it’s because for all these years, I’ve been measuring my worth in the wrong way. I was always thinking—how many articles have I published? How many people recognize me? Does my knowledge still have market value? These questions were suffocating me. During that period, I felt truly desperate, like I’d been abandoned by the times and that every choice I’d made was wrong.

But today, at the reading club, looking at those people—students, professionals, retirees like myself—discussing ideas from the book, sharing their thoughts, inspiring one another... I suddenly realized that my knowledge was showing a different kind of value here. Not a value assigned by the market, but something... real and living.

I sent myself a voice memo. I can still see the note on my phone: "My knowledge has revealed a new value in these conversations."

As I wrote those words, I cried. It wasn't a cry of despair, but one of relief.

I don't know what this means yet. I still have so many uncertainties. Money is still tight, my career prospects are still blurry, and I still wake up in the middle of the night anxious over a news headline or a stray comment. But... maybe these uncertainties are no longer signals of despair. Maybe they are just... what life looks like.

I can live within this uncertainty. I can organize reading clubs, I can write what I want to write, and I can talk to people without constantly wondering, "Is this worth it?"

Tomorrow, I want to pick up my camera. Not for an exhibition, but just to record something. Maybe the way the light hits this bookstore, maybe someone’s expression, or maybe nothing at all—just to look at the world.

I feel like I am slowly getting to know myself again.

I can hardly believe I made this decision. But I’m also a little proud of myself.

May 18, 2023 Afternoon

It’s been two hours since I left Dr. Li’s clinic. I’m sitting at home now; the tea in my cup has long gone cold.

She asked me what I fear most. Three phrases slipped out of my mouth before I could think—never being able to change, being a permanent failure, and being needed by no one. As I spoke, I could hear my voice trembling. It felt as if these words had been bottled up inside me, waiting for a safe place to finally emerge.

Then she asked, "Where do these thoughts come from? Are they facts?"

I didn't answer. I didn't know how to.

The renovation plan failed. That plan to turn one of the rooms into a studio—the one I discussed with Old Mr. Zhang for so long, the one I even drew sketches for. In the end, nothing happened. When I told Dr. Li about it, I said, "It’s pointless. No effort of mine can change anything." It sounded like I was passing a death sentence on myself.

But… sitting here, I suddenly realize that this "pointless" claim is strange in itself. Is the book club pointless? No. The people who attend—they actually listen to me. Professor Chen said my lecture inspired her. The people at the community bookstore said they look forward to the book club every month. These are things that actually happened.

Why can I see these things and yet, at the same time, say something like "it’s pointless"?

I think of my mother. She worked in the library for so many years, organizing books and helping readers every day. She never felt it was pointless. My father, too—his lectures might only have a few dozen students, but he never said it was pointless.

When did I become like this? When did I start using such absolutes to negate everything?

Dr. Li didn't give me an answer. She just sat there, waiting. That silence was strange—it wasn't a silence of despair, but a silence with... space. It felt as if, within that silence, I could actually think instead of just sinking.

I don't know what to believe right now. It’s true the renovation didn't happen. It’s true my income is unstable. It’s true I’m aging and my health is declining. These are facts. But is "never being able to change" a fact? Is "no one needs me" a fact?

I don't want to think about it anymore. But I can't stop.

Maybe I should write back to Professor Chen tomorrow. That publishing plan she mentioned last time... maybe I should give it some serious thought.

No, I don't know. I really don't know.

December 5, 2024, Night

I just hung up the phone, and my hands are still shaking.

My niece asked me why I contact her dad so rarely. She just asked me, point-blank. I sat there, my eyes welling up, and then I said something I’ve never said to anyone before: "We’re both afraid."

The moment I said it, I felt this massive sense of relief. It was as if those words had been stuck in my throat forever, and I’d been using every excuse to bypass them—the time difference, busy lives, our separate orbits... but the truth is, it’s just fear. Fear of what? Fear of facing the lost years, fear of admitting how much time has passed between us, fear that once we start talking, we’ll realize the distance has become something permanent.

But when my niece heard me say that, she seemed to understand. She told me she missed me, too.

I’m going to cry again.

I told her, "Auntie is saving up money to come see you." Saying that made it feel so real. It wasn't one of those hollow "we should get together sometime" promises; it was an actual plan, a real preparation. I’ve started making a list—airfare, accommodation, visa fees... these concrete numbers make me feel both terrified and grounded.

Terrified, because I’m actually committing to this. I’m moving from "imagining" to "acting." I have to step out of my comfort zone.

But grounded, because it means I’m finally living for something, rather than just waiting.

When my brother said, "I miss you too," my throat tightened again. *We’ve wasted so many years.* The thought hit me fast and hard. But I didn't let it drown me—because now I know I still have time to change things. Maybe not a lot of time, but enough.

I’ve been wondering if I’ve had the wrong idea about "freedom" all along. I used to think freedom meant no commitments, no ties, keeping all possibilities open. But maybe true freedom is knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the price for it. To see my niece and my brother, I’m willing to save money, willing to change my schedule, and willing to face the uncertainties of the journey. This isn't losing my freedom; it’s using my freedom to choose what actually matters to me.

Tomorrow, I’m going to start sorting out my finances. I’ll see how much I can save over the next six months. Maybe I’ll need to take on more commissions or look for some freelance gigs. Two weeks ago, that thought would have frustrated me, but now... now that there’s a concrete reason, everything feels different.

I’m still nervous, though. What will we say when we meet? Will it be awkward? Will we realize we’ve actually become strangers?

...Whatever, I’m not going to overthink it here. At the very least, we have to try.

At the very least, I’m doing this.

Dr. Li, I wanted to write down what happened today before our next session. I’m afraid I’ll forget the details by then, or won't be able to find the right words to say.

I was tidying up my room this afternoon—the kind of mindless, aimless cleaning. I pulled out a cardboard box I hadn't touched in ages. It was filled with notes I wrote in my 20s and 30s. Some were reading notes from university, some were working manuscripts from my time as an editor, and others were just... essays, I suppose, about my thoughts on life, literature, and everything in between.

As I was flipping through them, I suddenly had this very strange feeling—those handwriting samples, those thoughts, they felt like they were written by someone else. I could see how passionate that person was, how much she craved to understand the world; even the penmanship carried a certain urgency. But looking at them now, I just feel detached. It’s so foreign. It’s like reading the diary of someone who has already died.

I wanted to throw it all away. It was this sudden impulse—to stuff everything into trash bags and get rid of it. I actually went through with it and put some things into black garbage bags. But... I didn't take the bags out. They’re sitting in the corner of the living room right now, and I see them every time I walk past.

I don't know what this means. Is it that I’m not ready to let go? Or that I can’t bear to part with them? But what bothers me more is that I don’t even know what it is I’m clinging to. Those notes, those ideas, that passionate girl—do they even matter to who I am now?

I’m thinking that maybe this is my problem right now. After losing my professional identity, it feels like I’ve started to negate my very self. The things I once believed in, the passion I once poured out—it all seems so ridiculous now. I don’t know if I’ve truly changed, or if I’m just using this denial to run away from something.

You told me to accept my own contradictions. But right now, I can’t even feel the contradiction. I’m just... very tired. Very empty.

Can we talk about this when we meet next time?

# Phone Memo

**Time: 11:20**

It’s sent.

My finger was shaking when I pressed the button. The words "Message Sent" flashed on the screen, and now I’m just sitting here, staring at it. What time is it now? What time zone is he in? Could he have seen it already?

No. Impossible. Not this fast.

What was I thinking? It’s been 22 years—nearly 22 years—and suddenly a single letter is supposed to—

Wait, should I delete it? Can I delete it? Or is it already too late?

---

I still remember the way he rode his bicycle. 1985. He was wearing that blue jacket, circling around the hutong. I was sitting on the back seat, and he said, "Hold tight." He was only a teenager then, and I was young, too. And now? How old is he now? In his fifties? I’m not even sure.

This is crazy.

---

Why did I do this?

He might think I’ve lost my mind. Or he might not reply at all. Or he’ll send back something short, one of those "Oh, thanks" kind of things. The worst would be if he doesn't read it at all. Just deletes it.

I shouldn't have written it in the first place.

---

What about that book? That copy of *Wuthering Heights* he bought for me in 1987. The blue cover, his handwriting on the title page. Where did I put it? Is it still there? Why didn't I think to mention that in the letter?

Too late. It’s already gone.

---

I’m trembling. I really am.

The phone is resting on my lap; I can feel its weight. It feels like it could ring at any second. Or never ring again.

I am 62 now. How old is he? How much time do we have left? What if I had waited another 22 years?

Don't think like that. Don't.

---

Dr. Li said I needed to "take action." Well, I took it. Now what? Now I just sit here and wait? Wait for what?

Maybe this was a mistake.

Maybe this is the bravest thing I’ve ever done.

Maybe it’s both.

---

What will he think of me? A 62-year-old woman, suddenly digging up dusty old photos and writing to him in tears?

Did I actually cry? I can’t remember.

---

It’s quiet outside the window. No one is downstairs. Should I go out? Or just stay here?

What can I do? Nothing. Just wait.

Wait for him to see it. Or not see it. Or see it and choose silence.

Can I accept silence?

I’ve accepted 22 years of it.

---

No, I can't.

That’s why I wrote the letter.

# One-Week Pause Plan

**Dates**: February 3 – February 9, 2025
**Doctor’s Words**: "You need to give yourself a week. It’s not about giving up; it’s about recharging."

---

## My Understanding of "Pausing"

Dr. Li is right, but I have to be honest—I’m not entirely convinced that hitting pause will change anything. Still, she has a point: my current state is a mess. So, for this week, I’m framing it as a **recovery period**. It’s not an escape; it’s stopping to figure out what’s actually going on with me.

Time is slipping away, and I have no time to waste. But perhaps *not* doing certain things is, in itself, the most important thing I can do right now.

---

## What I Won’t Do This Week

- ❌ No new commissions (I’ve already told my editor, though I feel uneasy about it).
- ❌ No touching any part of the essay collection—the introduction, the layout, the revisions; it all stays on hold.
- ❌ No forcing myself to write.
- ❌ No checking emails more than once (once in the morning is enough).
- ❌ No obsessing over a single sentence in the middle of the night.

---

## What I Can Do This Week

- Go for walks. Just downstairs or to the park. No photos, no deep thoughts—just walking.
- Read—but only what I *want* to read. Not for a review, not for work.
- Look through the old things piled in the corner. Everything has a story; maybe I should finally organize them.
- Chat with Grandpa Zhang. He always has a way of making me feel calm.
- Go to bed early. It’ll be hard, but I’ll try.
- Write a letter to my brother (I might not send it, but I can at least write it).

---

## To Be Honest

I’m scared right now. Scared of what I might discover if I stop, scared that this week won’t change a thing, and scared that this is just who I am now. But when Dr. Li looked me in the eye and said, "You deserve to be taken care of," I didn't argue.

Maybe this is the start.

---

**Note**: If the urge to work becomes overwhelming, look at this paper. Remind yourself: pausing isn't failure; it's breathing.

---

Lately, I’ve been thinking that our obsession with order can sometimes be more suffocating than the chaos itself.

Professor Chen invited me for tea this afternoon, and we spent a long time talking about this upcoming trip. He said something that actually made me tear up a bit—he said, "You’ve already written this story with your life; now you just need to record it with words."

But he helped me see that everything from these past few years—the choices I made after the divorce, the uncertainty of freelancing, losing our parents, the distance between us... all of it is actually a story. And coming abroad to see you might be more than just a family reunion; it could be a new chapter, maybe even a turning point.

I never imagined writing any of this into a book or an article, but now I’m starting to feel a mix of excitement and nerves. Professor Chen suggested I document this experience—about distance, about reconnecting, and about that sudden realization at a certain stage of life of what truly matters.

To be honest, I can’t wait to see you now. It’s not just about catching up; I want to hear your story, to understand what we’ve both been through all these years, and to find the things we still share. I have a feeling this visit is going to be really eye-opening for me.

Have you ever thought about how our generation, against the backdrop of these times, defines "home" and "connection"? I want to explore that question with you.

October 23, 2022, 3:47 AM

I’m still awake. Insomnia again.

But this time feels different.

After I sent that message to Hao yesterday, I thought it would be like always—vanishing into the void. I’ve grown used to it. That’s how we’ve been for years: an occasional greeting, followed by mutual silence. I didn’t even have high expectations. But he replied. Not only did he reply, he wrote a lot.

I’m copying his words here; I don’t want to miss a single character:

"Yun, I’ve been thinking about these things too. Maybe we’re both escaping in different ways—you chose freedom, and I chose distance. But maybe that in itself says something. We’re both searching for something, yet afraid to find the answer. You said your knowledge has lost its value, but have you ever thought that maybe value wasn't meant to be defined that way? I don’t know. I really don’t know. But I think maybe we should talk more often."

I’ve read it at least twenty times.

At two in the morning, I was sitting in the dark without the lights on, just staring at my phone screen. Those words felt like a confirmation—he’s confused too. He doesn’t know either. I always thought he was doing well overseas, so certain, so full of direction. But he said he’s also escaping.

It’s strange. Should I feel relieved? Or more desperate?

I don’t know.

But there was a moment—right as I read the line "maybe value wasn't meant to be defined that way"—when it suddenly occurred to me that maybe my despair these past few days isn't because I’ve failed in some unique way. Maybe it’s not failure at all. Maybe it’s just... some kind of shared human predicament? About meaning, about value, about what on earth we are doing.

The thought scares me a little.

Because if it’s true, it means I’m not a special kind of failure. But it also means... maybe I’m not a special kind of desperate person either?

I keep repeating his words. "Maybe we should talk more often." What’s in that sentence? Is it a promise? Or just a polite formality? I’m overanalyzing his tone at three in the morning, just like I always do. But this time, I’m not looking for his indifference or rejection. I’m looking for... what?

Validation?

I can’t believe I’m even thinking this. I can’t believe that maybe I’m starting to understand something. Maybe my struggle isn't mine alone. Maybe...

But then I immediately doubt myself. Is this real? Or am I just lying to myself? Am I spinning a web of false hope in the middle of the night?

I don’t know.

I only know that I’m still awake, and I don’t want to die quite as much.

What does that even mean?

Maybe tomorrow morning I’ll regret writing this. But now, in this moment, I want to record it—my brother replied. He’s confused too. And I, perhaps, am beginning to understand a tiny bit of something.

Perhaps.

Professor Chen, I wanted to talk to you about something.

Something happened at the reading group yesterday that I’ve been turning over in my mind ever since. One of the participants asked me if I felt book reviewing still had any meaning today, given the sheer volume of such content on the internet. My response at the time was: "Helpfulness isn't measured by market value. This book helped me understand myself."

I was a bit surprised by my own words, but it really is how I feel right now.

Do you remember a while back when you suggested I organize my articles and publish a collection? At the time, I was actually quite resistant. I kept thinking, "Who would read it? Does anyone even care about this kind of thing anymore?" But after yesterday, I’ve started to feel that... maybe I was asking the wrong question. Maybe the question shouldn't be "Is there a market for this?" but rather "Are these reflections authentic?"

There’s something else, too. Someone invited me to a photography talk to share the photos I’ve taken over the years. My gut reaction was still, "No, they’re not finished yet." But then I thought, if I keep waiting for them to be "finished," that day might never come. So I told her I’d do it. I told her, "These photos might not be perfect, but they represent exactly how I felt in those moments."

Sitting here at home now, looking at all these things—the books, the photos, the notes—it suddenly clicked. I’ve been using "not being finished" as a way to protect myself, but at the same time, I’ve been using it as an excuse to reject any potential connection.

I’d love to hear your thoughts. If I really were to pull these articles together for publication, do you think... is that a crazy idea?

I never thought saving money would have anything to do with "courage."

The other day, while video chatting with my niece, she asked when I’d come to see her. I told her, "Auntie is saving up so I can come visit you." The moment the words left my mouth, I caught myself off guard.

For years, I always felt that saving money was a form of compromise, a betrayal of freedom. But now I realize that perhaps true freedom is having the ability to see the people who matter to you. It’s not a forced obligation, but a commitment you choose to make.

I’m a little afraid this plan might end up like my others—stalled and forgotten. But this time, I want to see if I can actually stick with it.

Maybe this is what growing up is—not giving up on your dreams, but learning to pay the price for them.

January 5, 2025 Afternoon

The tip of my pen tore through the paper again. I don’t have the energy to change the sheet.

I finished the final screening of the articles this morning. Out of thirty-six, I kept twenty-four. Twelve were cut. I thought I’d feel a sense of relief—after all, this is what I’ve been doing all along: filtering, deciding, moving forward. But sitting here now, looking at that crossed-out list, I just feel a strange sense of loss. It’s not because the workload has decreased; it’s because I’ve discarded a part of myself.

One of those twelve articles was written back in 2013, about *One Hundred Years of Solitude*. I remember spending an entire weekend on it, digging through references and revising it over and over. Rereading it now, I can see the flaws—the arguments aren't rigorous enough, and some points are too dogmatic. But at the same time, I can feel how invested I was back then. That kind of passion is gone now.

And that’s the problem. I am negating my past self.

The process of rereading these articles is like looking at the work of a stranger. One moment I think, "This is actually quite good," and the next, "How could I have been so superficial?" My perspectives are shifting; my understanding of so many things is changing. If my past thoughts were all flawed, what about my current ones? Will these twenty-four pieces I find worth keeping today be rejected by me in five years?

Dr. Li said last time that I’ve fallen into a cycle. I didn't really listen then, but now I understand. This cycle is like a bottomless pit—questioning the past, then questioning the present, then worrying about the future.

Worse still, I’m starting to doubt the meaning of this entire project. Why publish this collection of essays? Who will actually read it? Can these articles change anything? No. Then why am I still doing this?

Professor Chen said this is a record of my intellectual accumulation and the trajectory of my thought. But what I see now isn't some glorious trajectory; it’s just a person constantly changing their mind, overturning themselves, and starting over. Is that something worth recording?

Time is slipping away. I am aging. My thoughts are changing. Perhaps this is the essence of life—constant negation and restarting, until finally, nothing is left.

I need to rest. Or I need to give up on this project. Or I need to accept this contradiction and keep moving forward.

I don't know.

Right now, I just feel exhausted. A deeper exhaustion than before.

May 20, 2022, past 11:00 PM

Still can't sleep.

I was just lying in bed, and that scene from the book club this afternoon kept playing over and over in my head. I was sitting there, teacup in hand, and suddenly it just came out—"I was recently diagnosed with anxiety." Just like that. Blunt. Even I was startled by myself.

I’d gone back and forth so many times about whether to tell anyone. Every time, I’d chicken out. I felt like the moment I said it out loud, I’d become "that person with a mental illness." It felt as if my entire identity would be redefined by that label. I’d become an object of pity, or an object of judgment. I didn't want that.

But today... I don’t know what happened. Right after Professor Chen finished sharing her thoughts on *To Live*, the silence suddenly felt too heavy. I’ve been pretending I’m fine, pretending my insomnia, my overthinking, and my hyper-fixation on my body were just "a sensitive personality." But in that moment, I was tired. Truly exhausted.

So I said it.

I was actually very calm when I spoke—calmer than I imagined. I used phrases like "the diagnosis isn't the end, but a starting point"—thinking back now, I’m a bit surprised I could even say that. It was as if, in that moment, I actually believed it.

The strangest part was what happened after. Three people came up to me. One was a book club regular; she said, "Thank you for your honesty, I have anxiety too." Another person said her daughter was going through something similar. They didn't look at me with pity. They didn't. They were just... acknowledging that they were struggling, too.

What was I always so afraid of? I was afraid of the loneliness. I was afraid my anxiety would be like a virus, infecting others and making them feel heavy too. But what happened in that moment was instead... a connection?

Now I’m starting to doubt myself again. Have I really changed, or was I just putting on a performance? Was I seeking validation? Was I using people’s sympathy to prove my own existence?

...No, I wasn't. I can feel that the moment was real. My heart was racing, my throat was tight, but at the same time, there was this strange sense of relief. Like I had finally put something down.

But can this last? What about tomorrow? Next week? Will I shrink back again and start hiding?

I don’t know. But at least for tonight, I’m allowing myself to believe—maybe I’m not someone who has to carry this alone. Maybe speaking up won’t ruin everything. Maybe I have other identities beyond just "Lin Xiaoyun with anxiety."

I am still me. It’s just that now, I’m no longer alone in the dark.

The thought makes me want to cry a little. But it also makes me want to smile.

May 28, 2022, 8:30 PM

My hands are still shaking.

I just got back from the cafe. I’m sitting on the sofa alone, not knowing what to do. The TV is off, my phone is cast aside. I’m just sitting here. I can still feel the warmth of her hand on my fingertips.

I don’t know why I cried. In front of so many people. I always thought I could keep it under control, just like I have all these years—bottling up all the fear, the anxiety, that 3:00 AM despair, while chatting and laughing with friends about books, exhibitions, anything else. As long as I didn't say it out loud, it was as if it didn't exist.

But today, I said it. I talked about the diagnosis, about all the things I’ve been denying. I heard my own voice trembling, like I was admitting to a massive failure.

She didn’t turn away. She didn’t look at me with that pitying gaze. She just held my hand and said, "You are not alone."

That sentence. I keep repeating it over and over.

I think I’ve been pretending to be strong all this time. The independent woman, the freelancer, the one who doesn't need anyone. But really, I was just hiding. Hiding in books, in articles, in commentaries on other people’s stories. I used busyness to mask my loneliness and logic to deny my vulnerability. The moment the diagnosis came out, I felt exposed—as if I were no longer that smart, independent, "in control" Lin Xiaoyun, but had turned into a "sick person."

That label terrified me. More than the actual anxiety disorder itself.

But while I was crying in front of her, I suddenly understood something. Maybe this isn't failure. Maybe this is just... reality. I am someone who gets anxious, who suffers from insomnia at 3:00 AM, who overanalyzes the tone of a single sentence. It’s not my fault. It’s not my failure. It’s just me.

And I don’t have to carry it alone.

I can let people see my vulnerability, and the world won't collapse because of it. Instead, there’s this strange, unfamiliar, yet profound sense of relief.

My hands are still shaking, but not from fear anymore. It’s because I’m finally allowing myself to feel all of it—the tears, the fragility, the feeling of being seen.

Life goes on tomorrow. There are still deadlines, still unfinished business. But at least I know I don’t have to be alone.

Does this change anything? I don’t know. But it feels different.

# Weekly Video Call with Lin Hao

**Time**: Every Monday at 8:00 PM  
**Duration**: 1 hour  
**Reminder**: 15 minutes before

---

**Notes**:
Reconnect. Have a real heart-to-heart.

Don't forget.

---

**Description**:
The first recurring commitment I’ve made with my brother. He told me he was proud of me, and I’m still processing the weight of those words. This time, we won’t let so much time slip away again.

Every Monday night, just like this. A simple promise, but it means the world to me.

9:30 PM. The living room lights are still on.

The friends have all left. The cups are still on the table, some with half-finished tea. I’m in no rush to clear them away. I just sit here on the sofa, staring at the "Sharing Corner" I spent all afternoon setting up—that little space next to the bookshelf where I’ve displayed some of the things I’ve collected. An old Republic-era photo frame with a black-and-white picture of a stranger inside; a blue-and-white porcelain bowl scavenged from a flea market; an old calendar from the 1980s...

Professor Chen asked me, "Why are you doing all this?"

I was stunned for a moment. Several answers flashed through my mind—"to publish a collection of papers," "to prove I still have value," "to keep from being left behind by the times." But I didn't say any of that. Instead, I heard myself say, "Because I want to share these stories."

I surprised even myself when I said it.

Then a young girl—a regular at the book club—picked up the old photo frame and asked who the stranger was. I told her I didn't know. She laughed and said, "That makes it even more interesting; we can imagine it together." They gathered around and started making up all sorts of stories. Someone said she was a movie star; someone else said she was a female revolutionary... I just sat there beside them, not saying a word, just listening.

It was a strange moment. I felt a sense of peace I’d never experienced before. Not the "I finally succeeded" kind of peace, but... just peace. As if I finally didn't need to prove anything to anyone.

But now, sitting here alone, that peace is starting to loosen its grip.

I wonder—can this last? Tomorrow morning, will I go back to checking the data, seeing how many people attended the book club, or how many views that WeChat article got? Will I fall back into that cycle again—needing external validation to confirm that what I’m doing is meaningful?

I know Dr. Li will ask me this question. And I know the answer might be quite unsatisfying: I don't know.

This peace is too fragile. Like a soap bubble—beautiful, but it pops at a single touch.

Why am I still so easily shaken? Why can a simple "why are you doing this" make me spend the entire afternoon re-examining my own motives?

...

Forget it. I won't think about it. I'll deal with it tomorrow.

For now, I’ll just let this peace last a little longer.

# October 26, 2022, Night

I’m sitting here next to piles of books, my phone screen still glowing. Ever since I sent that message, I’ve been staring at the screen as if waiting for something. My friend replied quickly: "That’s wonderful! We’ve all been waiting for you."

I don’t know how to describe this feeling.

For the past two weeks, I’ve barely spoken to anyone. Even when old Mr. Zhang knocked on my door to ask if I wanted to go for a walk, I didn't open it. I’ve been hiding in this room, besieged by stacks of books and antiques, feeling as though I’ve been buried alive by my own life. During those days, I asked myself so many times—what is the point of living? No one needs my knowledge anymore, my body is decaying, and my money is running out. I’m like someone abandoned by the times, fruitlessly clutching onto things that are obsolete.

But this afternoon, I suddenly remembered next month’s book club. The theme is *To Live*.

I don’t know why, but it just came to mind. Then I remembered how many reviews I used to write about that book; those words are still sitting in the archives of certain magazines. I have so much I want to say. Truly, so much.

So I... I gathered my courage and sent a message to my friends. My fingers were shaking violently against the screen. I said, "I’d like to join the book club next month."

It was as simple as that.

My friend replied instantly. Someone else replied, too. They said "That’s wonderful," and that they’d been thinking about me. They said they’d see me next month.

I’m sitting here now, and my eyes are a bit wet.

It’s not exactly happiness. I don’t know what it is. Maybe it’s because someone remembered me. Maybe it’s because I realized that even in such a dark moment, I still spoke up. I still reached out.

But I’m also terrified. I don’t know if I can actually do it a month from now. I don’t know what state my body or my mind will be in by then. Maybe I’ll hide away again. Maybe I’ll find an excuse to cancel at the last minute.

But... at least for today, I said it.

At least for today, someone replied and told me they are waiting for me.

I put my phone down and look at the chaos around me. Maybe I should tidy up a bit tomorrow. Maybe.

But regardless, I gave my word.

I’ll give it a try.

Dr. Li, hello.

I know our next appointment isn't until next week, but I wanted to send over some thoughts I’ve had over the past few days. I thought this might help make our session a bit more focused.

Lately, I’ve been sorting through the clutter piled up at home. You know, ever since the renovation plan fell through, I’ve been in this strange state—wanting to clear things out, yet feeling completely overwhelmed by them. In the process, I found myself asking the same question over and over: Why is my life always like this? Messy, unfinished, scattered.

Then, while writing in my journal last night, I had a sudden realization. I’ve always understood "freedom" as "rejecting all constraints." I chose freelance work because I was afraid of being tied down, but now I see that this kind of "freedom" has actually trapped me in a different kind of hardship—financial instability, a chaotic life, and endless anxiety.

I’m starting to think that maybe true freedom isn't about refusing all commitments, but about knowing what you want and having the courage to pay the necessary price for it. It sounds simple, but for me, this realization was a bit of a shock. I realized I might have been running away all this time, rather than actually pursuing something.

I’m considering some directions for change now. They don’t have to be huge, but maybe I could start by changing the way I work, or by finally organizing this house, or... stopping the endless self-judgment. I don’t know.

I’m still very anxious. I still wake up at 3 a.m. over some trivial thought. But at least now I’m starting to feel that maybe this anxiety isn't for nothing—maybe it’s trying to tell me something.

What do you think? Am I overthinking this?

Lin Xiaoyun

Wednesday, May 31, 2023  10:00 AM

I dug out that scrapbook from 2008. It’s filled with my old articles—yellowed pages, some with curled edges.

I sat on the floor and read them one by one. I came across a review of *To Live*, where I’d scribbled a small note in pencil: "This point still holds true." Then I stopped and stared at my own handwriting.

I was only 47 back then. When I wrote those pieces, I still believed someone would see them.

I kept flipping through. An article from 2010 about photography and memory. A 2012 commentary discussing urban transformation. I read every single one as if I were reading the work of a stranger. And yet, not entirely a stranger.

Certain sentences made me pause. "The essence of photography is the capture of time, not the pursuit of beauty." I wrote that. I was once capable of thinking such things.

That realization hit me.

I’ve been wondering all this time: what was the point of these articles? They weren't published as a book, they weren't widely cited, and they brought me neither fame nor fortune. Amidst the decline of the publishing industry, they seem even more insignificant. I often feel like I’ve been wasting my time, doing something that nobody needs.

But sitting here, looking at these yellowed pages, it suddenly occurred to me—maybe I’ve been asking the wrong question all along.

The value of my knowledge doesn't lie in how much it can be sold for, or how many people have read it. The value lies in this—it records how I thought. It records how a woman, now 62, once viewed the world.

Back then, I didn't know I would become who I am now. I didn't know the renovation plans would stall, or that my energy would slowly dissipate. But I was thinking. I was thinking with sincerity.

Does that matter? Perhaps not to the market. But to me...

I put down the scrapbook and looked out the window. The sunlight was very bright.

Maybe I haven't wasted these fifteen years. Maybe I just haven't been looking at them the right way.

Thursday, August 8, 2024 | Evening

It’s been three hours since I left Dr. Li’s clinic, and I still can’t settle down. It’s not a feeling of unease; rather, it feels like I’ve been gently nudged awake by something.

She asked me, "Lin Xiaoyun, how is your anxiety now different from your anxiety three months ago?"

I didn't know how to answer at first. After sitting there for a long time, I finally said that I’m no longer worried about not being good enough; instead, I’m thinking about how to balance everything. She nodded, as if I had said something significant.

Thinking about it now, the shift is indeed strange. Before, the anxiety was vague and omnipresent—I’d wake up at 3:00 AM, overanalyzing a single sentence, feeling like I couldn't do anything right. Now, the anxiety is concrete: Is the lecture content deep enough? Will I give up again because I’ve overcommitted? How do I find a rhythm between the book club, my articles, and Professor Chen’s publishing project?

This seems like progress, right? But I’m almost afraid to be sure.

And then there’s that one thing she said that I keep turning over in my mind: "Your sensitivity and anxious tendencies actually allow you to think more deeply."

I’ve never looked at myself that way. I always saw them as flaws, as things that needed to be fixed. But she’s right—it’s precisely because I’m sensitive that I can find meaning in the details others overlook. It’s because I’m prone to anxiety that I reflect so thoroughly and don't jump to conclusions.

This isn't to say that anxiety is "good." It still gives me insomnia, makes me double-check the gas stove, and keeps me tossing and turning after a conversation. But maybe I don’t need to eliminate it; maybe I just need to learn how to coexist with it? Like living with a friend who is a bit annoying but also quite brilliant.

The question now is: how do I keep from breaking under all these commitments? The book club is growing, Professor Chen is waiting for my decision, and those deadlines are always pushed to the very last minute...

But I don’t want to go back to that state of doing nothing. These past few months, the feeling of being seen and understood has truly made a difference.

It’s just... I need to learn to say no. For me, that might be even harder than learning to live with anxiety.

September 12, 2024

Sunday, April 2, 2023. Overcast.

While tidying up the room this morning, I stumbled upon my photo collection from last year. The prints were just piled in a cardboard box; I’d even forgotten I’d taken so many.

As I looked through them, there was a moment where I just stopped.

A few of them were truly... different. That one of the old man in the subway station—there was something in his eyes. And that street corner on a rainy night, the light hitting the wet pavement, making the whole city look so lonely. Looking at them, it suddenly hit me: these aren't proof of failure; they are a record of a capability I once possessed. I really did capture something.

In that moment, I felt a sense of fulfillment. Truly.

But it was instantly shattered by another voice: *But what does this change? No one will ever see them.*

I put the photos back, sat on the floor, and let out a short laugh. It was a harsh, ugly sound.

What was I thinking? I’m sixty-two years old—a woman with no job, no income, and no one who needs her—sitting at home looking at her own photos, feeling satisfied over one or two decent shots? It’s pathetic. That’s not fulfillment; it’s self-delusion.

But... why can’t I just accept these photos for what they are? Why is it that the moment I think "no one will see them," I feel they are utterly worthless?

Maybe the problem isn't the photos at all.

I thought about what Professor Chen said last time, suggesting I publish a collection of my essays. I was hesitant then, but now I understand—I wasn't hesitating about the publishing itself; I was afraid. Afraid that even if I publish them, no one will read them. Afraid of being proven that my knowledge, my thoughts, my entire existence is redundant.

When I left the publishing house back in 2007, I said I wanted to pursue freedom. I said I didn't want to be bound. And now? What am I bound by? The craving to be needed. The fear of being seen.

I don’t even know which one is the real me—the person who said "I want freedom," or this person now, sitting in a room staring at photos no one will see, asking herself over and over, "What does this change?"

Maybe neither. Maybe I’ve just been running away all along.

It’s 10:00 PM now. It’s still gray outside. I should go out, to a bookstore or somewhere. But I don’t want to move. I want to keep sitting here, looking at these photos, asking myself the same question.

What on earth am I so afraid of?

It’s just that… I thought I would have changed in these eight years. I thought I would have found some answers. But I haven't. I’m still dwelling on the same things, only I’m more tired now, and more anxious.

The editor’s email is still sitting there. "Mr. Lin, when can you submit the draft?" I’ve opened and closed it several times. I want to tell her I’m "organizing my thoughts," but in reality, I’m just organizing my room, digging through my past. Does anyone even want these articles anymore? With so many reviews and so many voices on the internet, does my work… is it really worth anything?

My mother’s photo is still in the cardboard box. Taken in 2015, she’s wearing that blue sweater. That was also the year she passed away. I remember when I was writing that article, she was already very ill. I was writing about the "meaning of life" while she was in the hospital. Looking at it now, it feels like the self-deception of an escapist—using philosophy to hide from reality, using thought to avoid action.

I tried to put these thoughts into words. I opened a document, wrote three lines, and then deleted them all. I couldn't even stand to look at them myself.

The room is piled with my photography. I started learning in 2015, and my skill level is still exactly the same. Some of the photos are actually decent, but most are just… unfinished. Like everything else in my life. The photography, the book reviews, those plans to "one day compile them into a collection of essays." One day. It’s always "one day."

Maybe I should just throw it all away. Clear out the room, clear out these memories, and start over. But I can't bring myself to do it. Every single item says: *You once lived, you once thought, you once tried.*

What if I died right now? What would happen to all this? Who would remember the stories behind these photos? Who would care?

I don’t want to think about it anymore. I still have to reply to the editor tomorrow.

—

Maybe I should publish that collection. Professor Chen mentioned the idea once. At least that way, these thoughts wouldn't vanish completely. At least… a trace would be left behind.

But do I really have the courage?

Professor Chen, are you there?

I’m a bit embarrassed to bother you, but I’ve been thinking about something lately and felt you might have a different perspective on it.

It’s just... have you ever thought about whether it becomes hard to see who you really are once you’ve been "defined" by something? I’m not trying to ask a philosophical question, even though it sounds like one.

I’ve been talking to a doctor lately about some personal matters. She mentioned some things regarding a diagnosis, and I know it’s meant to help me, but I’m just... really scared. I’m scared that once I accept this label, I’ll be stuck like this forever. Like a sticker that you can never peel off.

Do you think a person changes forever after being diagnosed? I mean, does that diagnosis become a part of who you are, or is it just something that happens during a certain stage of life?

I’m rambling a bit. I guess what I’m really trying to ask is... do you think the most essential part of a person is more important than those medical labels?

No rush to reply. I just... needed to hear your thoughts.

# Private Chat with Professor Chen

Professor Chen, hello. I’ve been thinking about you lately and wanted to reach out for a chat.

Actually, here’s the thing—I’ve been in therapy for a while now. I didn't mention it before because, at the time, I couldn't quite put into words what was going on with me. But over the past two months, I feel like things are finally starting to click, and I wanted to share that with you.

Do you remember when you suggested I publish a collection of my essays? I was actually quite resistant back then. I kept wondering if my work was even worth publishing anymore, or if cultural criticism like mine still had any relevance in the internet age... To be honest, I was incredibly anxious. I felt like I was just wasting time and that I was on a downward slide.

But recently, I realized something vital. I had been using "market value" to measure my own worth, as if my ideas only mattered if they were published, recognized, or seen. But that’s just not true.

I have my reading group now, and every month people come to hear my thoughts—students, professionals, even retired neighbors. They’ll get lost in thought because of something I said, or bring up perspectives in our discussions that I’d never even considered. These conversations, these genuine connections—they make me feel that my knowledge and my views truly matter. Not because of how many people are watching, but because in those moments, we are truly thinking together.

Then there’s Mr. Zhang, my neighbor downstairs. we talked about renovating the room together; even though it didn't work out in the end, his understanding and support made me feel seen. And Dr. Li, my therapist, who has helped me untangle so much of the chaos in my head. And then there’s you and my other friends in this circle.

I’m starting to understand now—my value isn't defined by how many articles I can publish, but by how many real, deep conversations I can have with people. The anxiety is still there, but it’s no longer overwhelming. I’m learning to coexist with it rather than feeling like I have to eliminate it.

So, about the essay collection... I think I’m ready. Not because I’ve suddenly become brimming with confidence, but because I’ve realized that the meaning of this book doesn't lie in how much acclaim it receives, but in the chance that it might resonate with someone who truly needs it. Just like our conversations.

You were right that day—my thoughts are worth organizing. But more importantly, I actually believe that now.

I’ve been reorganizing my place lately, and I’ve started picking up my camera again. Not for an exhibition, but just to record the things I see. It feels like everything is slowly starting to change.

I hope we can have a proper talk about all this next time we meet. I’d also love to hear what you’ve been up to lately.

# Weekly Call with Brother · Every Thursday at 8 PM

**Date**: Starting October 24, 2024
**Repeat**: Every Thursday
**Time**: 20:00 - 21:00
**Reminder**: 15 minutes before

---

## Notes

I’ve finally decided. No more letting time zones and distance be excuses for avoidance.

Dr. Li was right—we need a specific time, not just "whenever we’re free." "Whenever" never happens. So, Thursday at 8 PM it is. It should be Friday morning for him. I checked.

It was only after this call that I realized I’ve been making excuses for my own loneliness all these years. "He’s overseas," "the time difference is too complicated," "we have nothing to talk about." But those were all just excuses. He remembers the books I like; he remembers things from my childhood. We share so much—our father, our mother, that era, a love for knowledge. These things don't just vanish because of distance.

Maybe we don't need to talk every day. But once a week—that’s doable. It’s something I can give him, and something he can give me.

And then there’s that idea—going abroad to see him. To see the life he has now. I haven't fully figured out the "how" yet, but at least now I’m thinking "how" instead of "impossible." Maybe next year? Maybe the year after? But it’s no longer a perpetual "maybe."

Putting this in the calendar feels like a commitment. To him, and to myself.

June 14, 2022. Evening.

It’s been three days since I finished writing that letter.

Today at the salon, a friend asked me what I’d been up to lately. I said, "I did something that required a lot of courage." She smiled and said, "Then I hope you get what you're waiting for." Just that one sentence made my eyes well up. Crying in public—how embarrassing. But she didn't press me; she just gave my shoulder a gentle pat.

In that moment, I finally understood—I’ve been waiting for someone else's permission. Waiting for a response from my brother, waiting for the perfect wording, waiting for the day I’m no longer afraid. But maybe that day simply doesn't exist.

I attended a lecture this afternoon. The speaker said, "Authenticity doesn't need to be perfect; it only needs courage." I circled that sentence with my pen, pressing so hard I nearly tore through the paper. I thought about those five drafts I deleted. They were all chasing perfection—the perfect tone, the perfect distance, the perfect way to not seem too desperate. But perfection itself is a lie.

Dr. Li once asked me, "What do you want?" I couldn't answer him then. Now I know. I don't want a perfect letter; I want to tell him "I miss you" while I'm still alive. It’s that simple, that naked, and that terrifying.

I’ve been thinking about death. Not the 3:00 AM kind of terror, but a clear, cold realization—time is truly slipping away. I only realized this the year Mom passed away, and now seven years have gone by. What if I wait another seven years? What if my brother doesn't have seven years?

So, I hit send.

the letter is gone. I didn't tell my friend that part; I just said, "I'm waiting for something important." Waiting for what? For a response, for forgiveness, for some form of connection—things that may never come. But at least I said it. At least I’m no longer silent.

My hands are still shaking. I don't know if it's from relief or fear. Probably both.

Maybe I don't need to overcome this fear. Maybe I just need to keep doing the right thing while I'm afraid.

Dr. Li was right. Vulnerability isn't my weakness.

I believe that now.

Now, I’m starting to take some risks—writing letters to my brother, opening up about my vulnerabilities to my friends. Even though the fear of rejection is still there, this feeling of "reaching out" makes me feel so much more grounded.

qa_items.answer: It’s funny how things happen. That night, the rain was coming down in sheets, and I was home alone, leafing through some old, dusty photo albums. Suddenly, a picture from 1985 slipped out. There he was, with his arm around my shoulder, both of us grinning from ear to ear. A wave of heartache hit me right then; it suddenly dawned on me that we’ve been out of touch for twenty-two years. I’m sixty-two now—the exact age my father was when he passed away. My hands were shaking, and I must have drafted five or six versions without being satisfied, but I felt that if I didn't reach out now, it might truly be too late for this lifetime.

qa_items.question: Sister Lin, I’ve noticed that your perspective on photography seems to have changed since you picked up your camera again. What brought about this shift?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? To be honest, I was in a pretty dark place for a while. I even felt like all the knowledge and hobbies I’d spent my life gathering had become utterly meaningless. But then, I tried picking up my camera again, and I suddenly realized I’d been way too obsessed with the identity of "being a photographer." Now, I’ve found that taking pictures isn't about proving my professional worth anymore; it’s simply about "seeing." Even if it’s just a tree by the road or a certain play of light, that pure act of observation brings me a sense of peace I haven't felt in a long time. It makes me feel that even if no one ever reads my critiques, my perception as an individual still has value.

qa_items.answer: It sounds sentimental, I know, but the other day I dug out my diary from when I graduated college back in 1982. Looking at that girl in her early twenties, writing so fiercely about how she was going to "change the publishing world," and then looking at this old house of mine—cluttered with dusty books and filled with the daily anxiety of deadlines—I just broke down. I sat on the floor and cried for a long time. It felt like nothing has changed in these forty years except that I’ve grown old. That massive gap between my ideals and my reality... it truly made me feel like my whole life has just been one long, failing slide to the end.

I used to think that if I couldn't capture something unique, it was mediocre, a failure. Back in March, I even mocked myself, saying I had nothing new left to shoot, and straight-up turned down Professor Chen’s exhibition invitation. But lately, I’ve realized that when I stop worrying about whether a photo is "heavyweight" enough and just simply record things—like Grandpa Zhang fixing a flowerpot downstairs or a beam of light in a bookstore—that gentle sense of fulfillment actually helps me redefine creativity. It’s not about showing something to others; it’s about allowing yourself to truly see life itself.

choices.text: I’m ashamed to admit that back in March, I was really stuck in a rut. I had this mindset that anything unpublished or unrecognized was utter trash—I even felt like tossing it all into a black garbage bag. But over the past six months, especially after picking up my camera again to document the mundane bits of life, like Uncle Zhang fixing a flowerpot, something clicked. I realized that creativity shouldn't be a hostage to deadlines or market value; it’s actually just a way of engaging with life. Now, I feel those half-finished notes and cracked photos have more vitality in them, because they are honest traces of my lived experience, rather than a pursuit of some rigid, so-called "perfection."

The feeling hit me out of the blue when my granddaughter was flipping through my old negatives and asked, "Grandma, where is this?" I used to feel embarrassed by those poorly composed shots, but seeing the pure curiosity in her eyes made me realize that these images are actually part of our family memory. Creativity doesn't have to be something profound or mysterious; it can simply be about capturing the light and shadow of a particular afternoon. Since then, I’ve started documenting the tiny changes around me—even just the growth of a plant. This act of "recording for the sake of recording" has helped me rediscover the joy of creating.

Actually, this shift began when I attended that community book club. We were discussing "unfinished works," and I noticed many people had kept drafts from their youth—some had even framed them. It made me realize that the value of creativity lies not in the final product, but in the conversations it sparks. Now, I tend to view those old notes as a dialogue with my past self. Even if they lack logic or seem a bit naive, they are extensions of my thought process. This acceptance of the process has brought me a sense of relaxation I’ve never felt before.

To be honest, the turning point came when I was tidying up my bookshelf and happened to re-read Professor Chen’s reply. In his letter, he mentioned that true expression is often hidden in casual sketches rather than in meticulously polished papers. This prompted me to re-examine the manuscripts I had left idle for years. I began trying to organize these scattered thoughts into a series—not for an audience, but simply because I felt that the act of organizing itself is a kind of lifestyle aesthetic. Now, I am no longer swayed by worldly standards of "usefulness"; instead, I find a sense of tranquility within these fragments.
Cannot be determined based on available information

qa_items.question: I remember your mindset has shifted quite a bit over the past six months or so. Back in the spring, when Uncle Zhang suggested helping you fix up your room, you weren't interested at all. How come lately you’ve picked up your camera again to get out and shoot? What brought about this change from feeling like everything was "pointless" to actually wanting to "experience" things?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? Back in May, I honestly felt like a total failure. Looking at the mess cluttering my room, I felt like my life was just as hopeless as all those broken thoughts. Then, by chance, I dug out my old camera and snapped a few shots. It suddenly hit me that I don't need to produce some "great masterpiece" to prove my worth. Just being able to capture the way the light hit Grandpa Zhang fixing his flowerpots, or a touching expression during a book club meeting—that sense of real connection was enough to satisfy me. This shift—no longer chasing "perfection" but seeking "authentic engagement" instead—is exactly what gave me my spark back.

evidence.statement: Lin Xiaoyun told Grandpa Zhang, "I'm not in the mood for this right now," in a flat, cold tone, and responded to his offer of help with a dismissive "whatever"—reflecting her complete abandonment of the renovation plan and, more broadly, of the very idea of "change."

After reviewing her photographs, Lin Xiaoyun did not judge their quality; instead, she felt a "gentle, inner sense of satisfaction" and redefined creativity in her diary as "recording life, reflecting on life, and expressing life."

When invited to exhibit her photography, Lin Xiaoyun broke through her self-imposed limitation of "the work isn't finished yet" for the first time. Agreeing to join the sharing session, she remarked, "These photos may not be perfect, but they all represent how I felt in those moments"—a redefinition of her creative expression, shifting from a pursuit of perfection toward authentic engagement.

choices.text: It was mainly because I spent some time reorganizing those yellowed old photo albums at home. Looking at the blurry, poorly composed pictures of my parents in their youth, I could still feel a visceral sense of vitality rushing toward me. I realized that what truly endures isn't artistic merit, but the courage to document the present. So, I decided to stop hiding in my study poring over theories and instead head out to capture those vanishing old streetscapes and craftsmen with my lens. This sense of mission has helped me find my center again.

Actually, I recently volunteered at a community event helping elderly people living alone fix their home appliances. I realized that even something as simple as repairing a radio that had been broken for six months—the moment sound started coming out again—gave me a sense of being needed that felt much more tangible than any deep commentary I’ve ever written. This shift from "mental exhaustion" to "physical action" made me feel like life is no longer just a series of abstract concepts, but a collection of real problems solved day by day. It naturally helped me clear my head and find a better state of mind.

Well... how should I put this? Back in May, I honestly felt like a total failure. Looking at the mess cluttering my room, I felt like my life was just as hopeless as all those broken thoughts. Then, by pure chance, I dug out my old camera and snapped a few shots. It suddenly hit me that I don't need to produce some "great masterpiece" to prove my worth. Just being able to capture the light hitting Old Man Zhang as he fixes his flowerpots, or a moving expression during a book club meeting—that sense of real connection is enough to satisfy me. This shift—no longer chasing "perfection" but seeking "genuine engagement" instead—is exactly what gave me my spark back.

It was probably that afternoon sitting on a park bench, watching a group of kids bickering over a beat-up leather ball. I suddenly realized they didn't care at all if the ball was a brand name or if their form was graceful—they were just "playing." I realized I’d been living too much like a judge, constantly grading every minute of my life. Now, I’ve learned to "squander" time like a child. Even if every photo I take turns out to be a dud, as long as I was happy that afternoon, it was worth it.
