qa_items.question: If you had actually steeled yourself during that big spring cleaning in March and tossed that bag of "proof of failure"—full of old frames and photo albums—into the dumpster, how do you think you would view your past now?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? Thank goodness I didn't throw them away back then. If I had really tossed that bag out that day, I might have truly "buried" that chapter of my life. At the time, I saw them as evidence of failure, but looking back now, those undeveloped negatives and fragmented notes are actually the traces of me having lived. They’re a mess, sure, but that’s the real me, isn’t it?

evidence.statement: Lin Xiaoyun began clearing out her belongings, but in the end, she didn't take the trash bag out, leaving it instead in a corner of the living room—this act of incomplete purging symbolized her inner conflict, a desire to let go of the past coupled with an inability to truly part with it.

While sorting through her belongings, Lin Xiaoyun looked at the items that once represented her potential and interests—old picture frames, photo albums, notebooks—and dismissed them as "proof of failure." With a self-deprecating remark, "What even are these? They're just monuments to my failure," she revealed a complete repudiation of her past creative pursuits.

When Lin Xiaoyun said, "These books aren't junk; they are the records of my thoughts," she redefined the items from "symbols of failure" to "symbols of value," reflecting a newfound validation of her own creative expression.

choices.text: Man, if I had actually thrown it away, I’d probably regret it for the rest of my life. That’s why I eventually went out and bought a locked metal trunk to seal everything away, all neatly categorized. Even now, I still don’t quite have the nerve to open it, but whenever I feel like my life is spiraling out of control, just touching that box lets me know my roots are still there. It’s more than just evidence; it’s like a "memo" reminding me that even at my lowest point, I never truly gave up on my obsession with aesthetics.

To be honest, once it’s gone, it’s gone—I’ve always been one to look forward. If I hadn't cleared out all that negative energy, I’d probably still be stuck in that pit of self-pity, feeling like an "unrecognized genius." It was only by clearing out my physical space that I finally had the mental room to sign up for that woodworking class. In the solid, rhythmic sound of hammering, I actually found a sense of groundedness that I never had back when I was writing. You have to empty your hands first to catch what life throws at you next, right?

To be honest, I think I’d feel a sense of relief. That "feeling of failure" is often just a psychological cue triggered by these old belongings—they’re constant reminders that I didn’t become the person I wanted to be. If I had just called a moving company that day and had everything hauled away at once, I probably would have moved to that small southern town six months earlier. No one knows me there, and I wouldn't have to carry the baggage of being a "former editor." Maybe on those beaches, I’d have already picked up my camera again to photograph the fishermen, instead of sitting here reflecting on the past.

Well... how should I put it? Thank goodness I didn't throw them away back then. If I had really tossed that bag out that day, I might have truly "buried" that whole chapter of my life. At the time, I saw them as evidence of failure, but looking back now, those undeveloped negatives and fragmented notes are actually the traces of me having lived. They’re a mess, sure, but that’s the real me, isn't it?

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, if you hadn't pushed yourself to go talk to Dr. Li back in mid-May—when you were feeling your most hopeless, or even sinking into that "strange calm"—do you think you would have ever been able to pick up your camera and start documenting life again?
qa_items.answer: Honestly, if I hadn't untangled the mess of those old diaries back then, I think I would have just completely "burned out." That kind of stillness is actually terrifying—it’s like you’ve resigned yourself to fate, believing your life is just over and that you’re a total failure. I really have to thank Dr. Li for helping me piece together those scattered emotions; he made me realize I wasn't just wasting time, but actually trying. Without that understanding, I wouldn't have the heart to go out and shoot the morning mist on the old streets. My camera would probably still be gathering dust in the cabinet.

evidence.statement: In her conversation with Dr. Li, Lin Xiaoyun traced the emotional crisis triggered by the renovation plan back to the specific moment of "seeing those old diaries," indicating a preliminary understanding of the causal chain behind her psychological breakdown.

After writing down these words of extreme self-negation, Lin Xiaoyun felt a "strange sense of calm"—a tranquility born from the total abandonment of hope, signaling that her psychological state had moved beyond despair into a dangerous stage of "acceptance."

Picking up the camera she had set aside for a month, Lin Xiaoyun said, "Maybe I should start shooting again." This marked a revival of her creative drive, shifting from "creative burnout" toward "new possibilities."

Lin Xiaoyun picked up the camera she had set aside for a month and said, "Maybe I should start shooting again," marking a revival of her creative drive as she shifted from "creative burnout" toward "new possibilities."

choices.text: To be honest, what I lacked most back then was someone to talk to. If it hadn't been for that photography class at the senior college organized by the community, where I met a few sisters who loved tinkering with cameras as much as I do, I probably would have sold my lenses long ago. It was them who dragged me to the park every day to stake out the sunrise; that lively atmosphere pulled me right out of my listless state. With that companionship, I finally felt like life was worth living again, and I started focusing on composition and lighting once more. Otherwise, that camera would have just been sitting there gathering dust.

Honestly, if I hadn't untangled the mess of those old diaries back then, I think I would have just completely "flamed out." That kind of stillness is actually terrifying—it’s like you’ve resigned yourself to fate, believing your life is just over and that you’re a total failure. I really have to thank Dr. Li for helping me string those scattered emotions together; he made me realize I wasn't just wasting time, I was actually trying. Without that understanding, there’s no way I’d have the heart to go out and shoot the morning mist on the old streets. My camera would probably still be gathering dust in the closet right now.

To be honest, if I hadn't stumbled upon that book on color psychology at a secondhand bookstall, which suddenly gave me a fresh perspective, I might still be spiraling in self-doubt. There was one sentence in that book that really struck a chord with me: "All stagnation is preparation for a deeper breakthrough." It made me realize that my previous slump was actually a period of gathering inspiration. After that, I tried to throw away all the rules and conventions. I stopped chasing so-called "artistry" and started clicking the shutter simply to please myself. Ironically, that’s how I found my way back to the pure joy I felt when I first picked up a camera.

Honestly, if I hadn’t gone on that sketching trip to the outskirts, I might have actually given up. An old colleague of mine practically dragged me to the mountains to clear my head; I had zero interest at first. But once I got there and saw those centuries-old trees swaying in the wind, I suddenly felt like my little troubles were nothing in the face of nature. That sense of openness just washed away everything that was weighing me down. When I came back, I decided to point my lens at things with more vitality, instead of dwelling on my own failures all day long.

qa_items.question: When you were reorganizing those photographs recently, I remembered how you used to self-deprecatingly call them "proof of failure." What made you suddenly change your tune and start calling them "records of thought" instead?
qa_items.answer: I’m ashamed to admit that I used to be quite stubborn, obsessed with the idea that if a project didn't become a series or get published, it was a waste of time. But the other day, as I sat on the floor flipping through those old street scenes and portraits, it suddenly hit me: even though the technique was raw, every single frame was an honest record of my thoughts and struggles at the time. Just like Uncle Zhang said, they aren't trash; they are the footprints of a life truly lived. This state of being "unfinished" is, in its own way, a form of completeness.

evidence.statement: While sorting through her belongings, Lin Xiaoyun looked at the items that once represented her potential and interests—old picture frames, photo albums, and notebooks—and dismissed them as "proof of failure." With a self-deprecating remark, "What even are these? They're just proof of my failures," she revealed a complete negation of her past creative endeavors.

When Lin Xiaoyun said, "These books aren't trash; they are records of my thoughts," she redefined the objects from "symbols of failure" into "symbols of value," reflecting a newfound validation of her own creative expression.

When Lin Xiaoyun said, "These books aren't junk; they are the records of my thoughts," she redefined the objects from "symbols of failure" to "symbols of value," reflecting a newfound validation of her own creative expression.

While organizing her photography, Lin Xiaoyun shifted her perspective on these "unfinished" works—moving from seeing them as "failures" to viewing them as "reflections of my thoughts at that moment"—and assigned a personal meaning to every single frame.

choices.text: Mainly, I visited a contemporary art exhibition, and the curator's concept of "the process is the work" really struck a chord with me. Looking back at these photographs, I realized that even though they were never published as a collection, the research I did and the essays I wrote to understand light, shadow, and composition have already been internalized into my current aesthetic. They document my journey from a complete novice to someone with a self-defined aesthetic system; every discarded shot was a stepping stone to where I am now.

It’s actually because I participated in a blind review session in an online photography community a while back. I tried submitting a few of my early street snapshots, and to my surprise, the feedback was that those shots—unstudied in composition and even slightly out of focus—actually captured the loneliness of urban life with pinpoint precision. It made me realize that I used to be too obsessed with so-called professional standards. These works are, in fact, my private observations of that era—irreplaceable slices of thought.

I’m ashamed to admit that I used to be quite stubborn, obsessed with the idea that if a shoot didn't turn into a series or get published, it was a waste of time. But the other day, as I sat on the floor flipping through those old street scenes and portraits, it suddenly hit me: though the technique was raw, every single frame was an honest record of my thoughts and struggles at the time. It’s just like what Uncle Zhang said—they aren't trash; they are the traces of a life truly lived. This state of being "unfinished" is, in its own way, a form of completeness.

I actually have my niece to thank for this—she’s an art history major. She was over at my place, flipping through these old works, and she actually said the graininess and that sort of clumsy narrative style felt exactly like an indie film. She even wanted to use them as material for her final project. Seeing that look of fresh wonder in a young person's eyes made me realize I used to be far too pragmatic, always obsessing over "success." I’ve moved past that now. These pieces capture my purest, least ambitious moments, and that’s what makes them truly precious.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I’ve noticed that every time you start going through those old photos or notes, you always end up calling them "proof of failure" and even want to throw them all away. Have you realized that you’re being incredibly hard on yourself?
qa_items.answer: Sigh... maybe you’re right. Sometimes, looking at a room full of half-finished drafts and photos, I really feel like I’ve just been fooling myself all these years. Like when I was going through those photo albums a while back—looking at them one by one, all I saw was stiff composition and a total lack of originality. I even started thinking that if I were gone one day, all this unwanted "junk" would just be a burden to everyone else. I know that’s an extreme way to think, but in that moment, this sense of existential void just completely swallows me up.

evidence.statement: While browsing through her photography, Lin Xiaoyun rated most of her photos a 3/10 and dismissed her creative output with labels like "mediocre" and "uninspired"—a reflection of her profound self-doubt regarding her professional value and creative talent.

While sorting through her belongings, Lin Xiaoyun looked at the items that once represented her potential and interests—old picture frames, photo albums, and notebooks—and dismissed them as "proof of failure." With a self-deprecating remark, "What even are these? They're just proof of my failures," she revealed a complete negation of her past creative endeavors.
While examining her photographic works, Lin Xiaoyun fell into a fundamental questioning of her creative value, viewing these unfinished projects as proof of a failed life.

choices.text: Maybe so. I have this habit of projecting my frustrations onto these things. Not long ago, I dug out some sketches from a painting class I took years ago, hoping to find some inspiration. Instead, the more I looked at them, the more ridiculously clumsy the brushwork seemed. I stared at those frames, wondering why I’d wasted so much money and energy on something I clearly have no talent for. I even had this sudden urge to paint over every single canvas in black. That moment of clear-eyed realization—seeing your own mediocrity and being powerless to change it—really made these years of effort feel like a joke. I just wanted to erase them from my life entirely.

Sigh, you’re absolutely right. It’s just like last month when I planned to organize those handwritten essays from years ago to submit for publication. But as soon as I flipped through a few pages and saw that naive rhetoric and those melodramatic reflections, I felt so ashamed I wanted to shove them into the shredder immediately. I keep feeling like those words can’t possibly carry my current thoughts; instead, they’re like a thick wall, trapping me in my past mediocrity. I feel like if I don't completely clear out this "baggage of growth," I’ll never be able to truly start something new. This suffocating feeling of being negated by my own past is truly unbearable.

Sigh... maybe you're right. Sometimes, looking at a room full of half-finished drafts and photos, I really feel like I’ve just been kidding myself all these years. Like when I was going through those photo albums a while back—looking at them one by one, all I saw was stiff composition and a total lack of originality. I even started thinking that if I were gone one day, all this unwanted "junk" would just be a burden for someone else to clean up. I know it’s an extreme way to think, but in that moment, this overwhelming sense of worthlessness—of having no real identity—just completely swallows me whole.

You caught me—my personality can be quite exhausting. A few days ago, I tried sorting through the dusty professional books and old work notes on my shelves, thinking I’d leave them to my former students. But as I flipped through them, I realized these outdated theories and shallow musings were nothing more than a disservice to anyone reading them. In that moment, I even started to doubt if I’d just been running in circles for my entire decades-long career. Eventually, I piled them all up in the entryway, planning to just have the scrap collector take them away. That feeling of having accomplished nothing—where even the traces you leave behind feel like a burden—is, I admit, rather extreme.

qa_items.question: I’ve noticed a pattern with you: whenever you try to make a change—like your room makeover or your writing—and things start to fluctuate, you quickly retreat into a sort of "nothing matters anyway" calmness. It seems like this has become your way of protecting yourself.
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? Your observation is so spot on that it’s actually making me feel a bit exposed. When the room renovation came to a halt a while back, I did tell old Mr. Zhang next door, "Whatever, I don't care." In that moment, I truly felt like all the effort was pointless. That so-called "calm" was actually more of a self-defeating defense mechanism—as if by being the first to admit I’m a loser, nothing could ever hurt me again. But after talking with Dr. Li recently, I’ve realized how absurd this absolute negation really is. I mean, there are people at the book club who still take earnest notes on what I say, yet I was choosing to look right past that.

evidence.statement: Lin Xiaoyun told Uncle Zhang, "I'm not in the mood for this right now," in a flat, indifferent tone, and responded to his offer of help with a dismissive "whatever"—reflecting her complete abandonment of the renovation plan (and, more broadly, of "change" itself).

After writing down these extreme self-negations, Lin Xiaoyun felt a "strange sense of calm"—a tranquility born from the total abandonment of hope, signaling that her psychological state had shifted from despair into a dangerous stage of "acceptance."

Lin Xiaoyun used absolutist language, such as "pointless" and "none of my efforts will change anything," to express her total rejection of the reform plan and all personal endeavors. This indicates that her thinking has become trapped in a mode of absolute pessimism.

choices.text: Sigh, you saw right through me. To be honest, this "calmness" of mine is more like a form of damage control. Remember when that long article of mine was rejected last time? I dragged every single draft on my computer straight into the recycling bin and even turned down Professor Chen’s invitation to serve as a journal consultant. I’ve always felt that it’s better to destroy everything myself than to wait for others to reject me. But later, while helping out at the community greenhouse, seeing those plants wither and then come back to life made me realize how extreme my mindset had become—especially when those old friends I regularly correspond with are still waiting to hear from me.

That’s exactly it—you hit the nail right on the head. It reminds me of when I tried to teach myself French last year. I only lasted three months before hitting a wall with the grammar and giving up entirely. I even packed up all the original novels I’d bought and gave them to Mr. Wang, the local recycler, while lying to myself that I’d "never end up in Paris anyway." That kind of defense mechanism made me feel safe, until I happened to run into a young guy at a second-hand bookstall who was also learning French. He was actually flipping through a notebook filled with my old scribbles, and in that moment, I realized just how absurd my "preemptive failure" really was.

Hmm... you’re certainly observant. That feeling was especially intense when the preparations for my previous photography exhibition were cut short. At the time, I just locked all the developed prints in the attic and told Mr. Zhou—the mentor who first introduced me to the craft—that I never wanted to touch a camera again. That so-called "calmness" was actually just a sense of frustration, a fear that no matter how hard I tried, I’d never keep up with the younger generation. It wasn't until my niece’s wedding a few days ago, when I saw she’d printed an old photo I took years ago on her invitations, that I realized those "meaningless" pieces I had dismissed might actually hold a certain warmth in the eyes of others.

qa_items.question: I have noticed that you often find yourself plagued by intense doubts about your dreams and talents late at night or when you are alone, sometimes even sitting on the floor in tears. Does the cyclical nature of these emotions leave you feeling exhausted?
qa_items.answer: Well, you caught me... It’s true, that feeling is exactly like falling into a bottomless pit. Sometimes in the middle of the night, I’ll flip through my old journals and see that younger version of me, so fired up to "change the world." Then I look around at this room full of unfinished negatives and outdated reviews, and I honestly start to wonder if my whole life has just been one big act of self-delusion. That feeling of sitting on the floor and crying—it’s not just exhaustion; it’s more of this hollow sense of grasping at thin air. It’s as if the freedom I was so proud of ended up amounting to nothing at all.

evidence.statement: Beside the diary entry about "future dreams," Lin Xiaoyun wrote, "I don't know what dreams I have left," and then sat alone on the floor crying—a reflection of her utter disillusionment with her creative expression and the direction of her life.

Confronted with the stark contrast between her youthful declaration, "I am going to change the publishing world," and her current self-questioning, "Am I still doing the same thing?", Lin Xiaoyun broke into tears and asked desperate questions like, "What have I done? Where has my life gone?"

When revisiting her 2017 photography, Lin Xiaoyun questioned its worth with self-deprecating language ("Is this truly of any value?"), and in her diary, she described her entire life's creative endeavors as "self-deception."

choices.text: It’s true. Especially every time I finish organizing a community book club and see the hopeful eyes of those young people, I look back at the yellowed academic notes on my own bookshelf and feel a profound sense of displacement. The exhaustion comes from realizing that the "idealism" I try to impart to them feels so pale and powerless against the reality of the publishing market. Sometimes I look in the mirror and ask myself: have the aesthetics and standards I hold onto become nothing more than clichés that hinder others' progress? Those moments of self-doubt are truly painful.

Sigh, you’re right—that kind of cyclical recurrence definitely exists. I remember Professor Chen once brought up the topic of "intellectual evolution" with me; he felt my current state of contemplation was a sign of progress, but I’m not so sure myself. Every time I try to compile these fifteen years of freelance experience into a book and look at those drafts—where the logic is so disjointed from start to finish—I fall into a state of profound self-doubt. I always feel like I’m just running in circles, and that my so-called "independent thinking" is really just building myself a magnificent tombstone. It’s a feeling that becomes oppressively heavy whenever I’m alone.

It’s true—that sense of loneliness reached its peak in the period following my mother’s death. Although I had always prided myself on pursuing absolute freedom, sitting in my living room late at night and looking at those antiques from flea markets—things I once thought represented a "lifestyle aesthetic"—I suddenly realized they couldn't fill the void inside me. I began to wonder if my decision to leave the publishing house back then was merely an escape from responsibility rather than a search for myself. This doubt about my life’s trajectory often kept me awake all night long.

Looking at those undeveloped rolls of film and half-baked projects, I saw them only as evidence of my own flightiness—I’d even stuffed them into trash bags, ready to throw them away. But looking through them again recently, I suddenly realized that every single photo honestly captured my thoughts at the time. Even if they were unrefined, they are a part of my life. Messiness isn't necessarily failure; it’s actually a visual trail of my mind jumping from one idea to the next. Thinking about it now, that state of being "unfinished" is a kind of completeness in itself.

I used to think that "creativity" had to be something earth-shattering—a perfect piece of work worthy of an exhibition. That’s why back in March, looking at my photos, all I saw was mediocrity; I even felt like packing away my camera for good. But lately, I’ve gradually realized that perfection is often "dead." Those flawed moments—capturing the old man downstairs fixing a flowerpot or a particular streak of afternoon light—those are what’s truly alive. I’ve come to feel that creativity isn't about chasing some final "result" anymore; it’s about authentic engagement and truly seeing. As long as I felt something in that moment, it has value.

When I got home and looked at the dusty, half-finished projects on my bookshelf, I suddenly felt there was no longer any need to hide them. I began to categorize them—not to tidy up, but to see how these "weeds" actually grew. This sense of peace made me realize that I’ve always had the courage to refuse being defined by standard measures.

The turning point came when I decided to clear out that old cloud drive. Looking at those incredibly crude web design sketches from over a decade ago, I didn't feel ashamed; instead, I was moved by their clumsy vitality. I realized that the reason I viewed my past work as a "failure" was that I kept trying to use a perfect endpoint to negate the entire journey. So, I bought a set of elegant archival boxes and carefully filed away both physical and digital drafts, writing down my creative reflections from those moments. This sense of ritual helped me understand that I don't need to "liquidate" my past to prove my current progress. True freedom lies in embracing every version of myself.

I have my college-aged niece to thank for this. She came to stay for a week during her break and ended up digging out all my old sketches and discarded proposals—things I had fully intended to throw away. She was so enthusiastic, asking me about the story behind every single piece. Seeing that look of pure curiosity in her eyes made me realize that what I dismissed as "scrap paper" might actually hold a spark of inspiration for someone else. I started trying to re-examine these projects from an outsider’s perspective, even adding written captions to them. I’ve come to realize that those so-called "failures" are actually coordinates left behind while I was exploring different paths; they are proof that I have never stopped pushing the boundaries.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I remember you were so fired up about renovating that old house. How is it that right around the time you decided to scrap the plan, after flipping through an old diary from 1982, you suddenly felt like none of it mattered anymore?
qa_items.answer: How can I even put this into words... The other day, I stumbled upon that yellowed diary and saw my twenty-something self grandly declaring that I was going to "revolutionize the publishing world." Then I looked around at this cluttered, dilapidated mess of a house that I can't even manage to renovate, and the sheer gap between the two just crushed me. I sat on the floor and cried for a long time, feeling like I’ve just been running in circles for forty years. Those so-called ideals have long been worn away by time. If I couldn't even change my life, what’s the point of changing a room?

That sudden, violent sense of disconnection between our worlds and lifestyles just made me break down. I realized I’d been trying to simulate that ideal life by renovating this old house, but it was all just self-deception. Changing my surroundings can't cure this deep-seated sense of displacement in my bones. So, I figured, why bother anymore?

It’s quite ironic, actually. I was in the middle of taking measurements when a radio interview about "minimalist living" came on. I stopped what I was doing and looked around this half-finished mess of an old house, and it hit me—I was exactly the kind of "escape artist" they were describing. I realized that what I truly needed wasn't a new room, but to learn how to live with these old scars. So, right then and there, I decided to walk away from the project for good.

qa_items.question: I remember you were quite frustrated with your photography before. How is it that after talking with Dr. Li for a while, you’re willing to pick up the camera again? And from the way you’re talking now, it sounds like you’ve started to feel that those "imperfect" works actually have some value?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? It’s actually because my mindset has changed. I used to always measure myself by "market value," feeling like any time spent without winning an award or holding an exhibition was just a waste. But after talking with Dr. Li, I suddenly realized that I don't have to be a perfect photographer—I don't even need to be a "successful" person. I’ve started to embrace my own aimlessness and anxiety, treating my camera as a tool to record life rather than a weapon to prove myself. Like when I was photographing Grandpa Zhang fixing his flowerpots; in that moment, I just felt that life itself is beautiful. That kind of authentic connection is more fulfilling to me than any exquisite composition.

In that moment, I realized that the value of a photograph doesn't lie in its artistic merit, but in the chemistry it creates between people. That kind of genuine interaction is what gave me the motivation to start clicking the shutter again.

qa_items.answer: Sigh, you caught me. To be honest, sometimes when I look at those photos, I’m not thinking about how beautiful the composition is, but rather, "Does this actually have any value?" When Professor Chen invited me to join the exhibition last year, I just stared at those street corners and play of light and shadow, feeling they were deathly mediocre. In the end, I made an excuse and turned him down. I’ve always felt that if I haven't captured something truly groundbreaking, it’s not worthy of being seen. This mindset is honestly exhausting; it’s even started making me wonder if I’ve just been kidding myself all these years.

You're right, that mindset has definitely cost me quite a few opportunities. Not long ago, Editor Wang from the publishing house reached out to discuss publishing a photography book. But as I flipped through those thick notebooks and developed prints, I couldn't help but feel that the expression wasn't profound enough—I even started to feel like this way of recording is already behind the times. In the end, I politely declined his offer. I always feel that if I haven't unearthed some deeper philosophical meaning, these images are just pale, lifeless records. This kind of self-doubt can be truly exhausting sometimes.

You caught me—that sense of anxiety has actually been with me all along. I remember last summer, I signed up for an international documentary photography competition. But on the night of the deadline, as I looked at my selected "Urban Fringe" series on the screen, the more I stared, the more I felt they were too polished, too artificial—they lacked that raw power that hits you right in the heart. I stared at the upload progress bar for a long time, but in the end, I cleared the attachments. I feel that if I can't produce work that completely wins me over, submitting it is just wasting the judges' time. This mindset has indeed made it very hard for me to find my way back to that initial purity I had when I first started creating.

Sigh, it’s true. For instance, last month I was supposed to give a lecture at an art school to share my creative experiences over the years. But while I was preparing the slides, I kept looking at my representative works—pieces I used to think were quite good—and suddenly felt they might be full of flaws under a professional academic lens, hardly worth discussing at all. That pressing fear of being "seen through" made me back out at the last minute, using poor health as an excuse. This almost harsh self-demand really makes it difficult for me to simply enjoy the moment of pressing the shutter.

qa_items.question: I remember back when you’d pull out those old frames and photo albums, you’d mock them as "proof of failure" and even turned down the exhibition. What changed lately? How is it that you’re now okay with things being imperfect, and even willing to share those photos that capture nothing but "fleeting moments"?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, looking back on this really brings up a lot of emotions for me. I used to feel that if something wasn't finished or wasn't perfect, it was just a meaningless piece of junk. Even early last year, right after I retired, looking at those half-finished notebooks and picture frames around the house filled me with nothing but a sense of failure. But then it clicked—if I insisted on waiting for "perfection" before showing my face, I’d probably spend my whole life hiding in my shell. The truth is, those photos represent exactly what moved me in that moment, and that’s enough. There’s no need to self-censor just to meet some arbitrary standard.

evidence.statement: While tidying up her home, Lin Xiaoyun looked at the items that once represented her potential and interests—old picture frames, photo albums, and notebooks—and dismissed them as "proof of failure." With a self-deprecating remark, "What even are these? They're just monuments to my failure," she revealed a total rejection of her past creative endeavors.

When invited to exhibit her photography, Lin Xiaoyun broke through her self-imposed limitation of "the work isn't finished yet" for the first time. Agreeing to join the sharing session, she remarked, "These photos may not be perfect, but they all represent how I felt in those moments"—a redefinition of her creative expression, shifting from a pursuit of perfection toward authentic engagement.

I’ve spent my whole life studying composition and lighting, yet I’d forgotten that the most primal function of photography is to record emotion. I realized that if even a child could be moved by the feeling in a frame, why should I obsess over all those rules and regulations? So, I decided to pull together all these "warm" moments and stop worrying about technical specs.

I finally see that my previous refusal to hold an exhibition was just vanity; I was afraid of exposing my flaws. Now, I’ve come to terms with it. I don’t need to prove I’m a master; I just want to be an honest chronicler and present these raw, unfiltered moments of the shutter.

qa_items.question: Sister Lin, I remember you were quite frustrated a while back, even doubting if you had any talent at all. But looking at your recent notes, it seems you now feel that "participating and experiencing" is the true goal of creativity. How did you manage to make that mental shift?
qa_items.answer: Well… how should I put it? I think I’ve finally been worn down by the word "perfection." After twenty-five years in publishing, I got used to the idea that everything must result in a proper "product." So, for a while last year, looking at my unfinished photos and half-written reviews made me feel like I was just wasting my life; it was incredibly frustrating. It wasn’t until I was flipping through a book the other day that it hit me: those cracks and unfinished marks are actually the evidence of my life. It’s like my mother used to say—a book is at its most beautiful when it’s being read and annotated, not the moment it comes off the press. Now, I feel that as long as I’ve felt it and been part of it, those half-finished works aren't failures anymore; they are a living, breathing part of my life.

evidence.statement: In her diary, Lin Xiaoyun immediately wrote, "I am deceiving myself. I don't have Ms. Li's courage, nor her talent," showing that the denial of her own creative abilities still dominated her thinking, and this new glimmer of hope instantly collapsed under the onslaught of self-doubt.

Lin Xiaoyun began writing personal, unpublished reflections, noting that "perhaps perfection is not the goal of creativity; rather, it is participation and feeling that matter"—marking a fundamental shift in her understanding of the essence of creativity.

Lin Xiaoyun began writing personal, unpublished reflections, noting that "perhaps perfection is not the goal of creativity, but rather participation and feeling"—marking a fundamental shift in her understanding of the essence of creativity.

choices.text: As it happened, I was passing by an old warehouse under renovation that day when I saw a group of young people doing some impromptu graffiti. They would finish a piece and then immediately paint over it. At the time, I couldn't wrap my head around it—it seemed like a complete waste of effort. But then, one of the kids said to me, "The process is the work itself." In that moment, it felt like a wall inside me just crumbled. I thought back to my days as an editor-in-chief, how I was always obsessed with the final draft, forgetting that the happiest moments were actually those spent arguing over outlines with authors at roadside food stalls. Now, I just want to be that "kid with the spray paint" and simply live for the moment.

It’s probably because I’ve recently started sorting through the old newspaper clippings my father left behind. He never published a book in his life, but he clipped every article that caught his interest, pasting them neatly and filling the margins with annotations. I used to think he was just wasting his time, but the other day, looking at those yellowed pages, I suddenly realized that this was his way of having a conversation with the world. He wasn't looking for achievement; he was simply "living." It made me realize that my own failed photographs are also my dialogue with the world—they don't need a perfect ending.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, looking back at your journey over the past year, how has your fundamental understanding of "what constitutes meaningful creation" changed between now and when you first retired?
qa_items.answer: I’m a bit ashamed to admit that right after I retired, I was incredibly anxious. Looking at all those unfinished photo albums and notes around the house, I couldn't help but see them as "proof of failure." But after a year of wrestling with it all, I’ve finally found peace. I’ve come to realize that a perfect finished product is actually "dead"; it’s the things with cracks, the things left unfinished, that are truly breathing with life. Real creation doesn't have to be for an audience or for publication. As long as I was whole-heartedly present and feeling in that moment, the act of recording is a meaning in itself.

Value no longer belongs to the individual creator; instead, it is generated through this constant cycle of feedback and iteration.

To be honest, when I first retired, I was quite dismissive of "hobbyist" activities, seeing them as nothing more than a way to kill time. But this year, I started trying my hand at copying ancient paintings, and I’ve realized that the value doesn't lie in how closely I can replicate them, but in that "dialogue across time and space." When I hold my breath and focus entirely on tracing the brushstrokes of the ancients, I feel a sense of tranquility unlike anything I’ve ever known. I’ve come to feel that the true value of creation lies in its power as a form of self-cultivation, helping me withstand the loneliness and trivialities of living alone.

I used to believe that true value meant creating something like I did in my youth—something that could shake up the industry or be cited by the next generation. But this past year, while helping the community archive historical records, I realized that real creation is actually a form of "relay." Even if all I’m doing is typing up scattered oral histories, as long as that information is preserved to smooth the way for those who follow, this utility as a bridge is far more significant than any work bearing my own name.

qa_items.question: Lin Xiaoyun, after all these ups and downs, if you were to meet a former colleague who is also struggling with the transition to retirement and feeling like they are "no longer needed by society," what advice would you give them? Based on your own reflections during this time, how would you tell them to deal with that sense of "uselessness"?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I’ve been through that sense of "uselessness" too—I’ve even cried like a complete fool in the middle of the night. But if I were to talk to him now, I’d tell him to stop fixating on those grand goals like "how many books I’ve published" or "how I’ve changed the world." It’s just too exhausting. In reality, even if you just spark a new insight in one young person at a book club, or talk through a small idea with a neighbor, that genuine connection is where the value lies. To participate and to feel is, in itself, proof of being alive. We don’t have to be perfect; even those unfinished, broken thoughts are a living, breathing part of our lives.

qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? These past fifteen months have been quite a transformation. I stopped thinking about exhibitions or recognition and just focused on capturing things like Uncle Zhang next door fixing his flowerpots, or the way people’s eyes light up during our book club meetings. I’ve realized that creating isn't about proving how professional you are; it’s about that genuine connection. Like I wrote in my column, as long as I can voice something a stranger has been longing to express, that pure desire to share is enough to keep me going.

It’s like when I was recently revising that piece on *One Hundred Years of Solitude*; once I stopped obsessing over sounding "professional" and instead wrote honestly about the sense of connection I found within solitude, I realized that my work as a critic is actually a bridge between my own isolation and the outside world. It allows me to find a genuine resonance with readers I’ve never even met.

I used to feel like I had to seize every opportunity that came my way, like I had something to prove, but I just ended up making myself incredibly anxious. After that talk with Professor Chen, though, it suddenly hit me: if I lose my passion for literature just to hit deadlines and deal with all those tedious editing tasks, then what’s the point of publishing a book at all? So, I’ve learned to say "no." I’m prioritizing my energy for the reading club and those real, live interactions. This feeling of taking control of my own pace—of no longer being held hostage by what I "should" be doing—is much more grounding. I still have these little moments of doubt, wondering if I’m just making excuses, but I feel so much more at peace.

So, after talking it over with my family, I decided to stop forcing out words just to chase that grant. Now, I only spend an hour a day writing whatever comes to mind, leaving the rest of my time for my family and my life. This feeling of taking back control is far more fulfilling than any title could ever be.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, do you remember that time you woke up in the middle of the night, looked at those old projects on your bookshelf, and started having second thoughts? If you hadn't seen them as a burden back then, but instead forced yourself to finish every single plan, do you think that would have given you a sense of achievement, or would it have just dragged you deeper into that feeling of being a "failure"?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I actually did struggle with it at the time. But I think if I had forced myself to fill in all those gaps, that sense of "loser" frustration would have felt even heavier. I used to think that leaving things unfinished was a waste of time, a sign of incompetence—I even wrote in my diary that I was worthless. But in the early hours of that morning, it suddenly clicked: creativity isn't about how "perfectly" you finish something; it's about the fact that you were part of it. If I had finished it just for the sake of finishing, I would have locked myself right back into that rigid system again. And that’s not the kind of freedom I’m looking for.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I’ve noticed a recurring pattern whenever you look back at your work: before you can organize your old notes or start conceptualizing something new, you always seem to go through this intense period of self-doubt. But recently, I spread them all out on the floor and re-read them, only to realize that those scattered thoughts have actually been in conversation with one another all along. They form the most authentic trajectory of my thinking over the past twenty-odd years. Looking back now, I suppose that period of self-denial was just the necessary growing pains before "breaking out of the cocoon."

qa_items.question: I’ve noticed that when you’re alone with your thoughts, you often deeply doubt your own creativity—to the point where you feel like deleting everything you’ve just written. Is this habit of ‘self-censorship’ perhaps your way of dealing with anxiety?
qa_items.answer: Sigh, you caught me. To be honest, that night I sat in front of the computer and managed to squeeze out three lines with great effort, but the more I looked at them, the more I felt they missed the mark. So, I just deleted the whole thing. This "self-censorship" feels more like a defense mechanism to me—I feel like if I don't put them out there, I won't be judged by this fast-paced era, nor will I have to face the possibility that I might already be "obsolete." This feeling hits especially hard around three or four in the morning. Looking at a room full of half-finished film negatives and drafts, they really do start to feel like evidence of my failures.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, looking back at your year-long struggle with photography and writing since retirement, how has your understanding of the word "talent" changed?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to think talent had to be the ability to shoot a perfect cinematic masterpiece or write something earth-shattering. That’s why last year, looking at all those mediocre photos, I even thought about giving up entirely, feeling like there was nothing new left to capture. But after a year of trial and error, I’ve come to feel that talent is actually a kind of "authentic engagement" with life. It’s like recently, when I spread all those messy notes across the floor, I realized those imperfect fragments actually form the most genuine thread of my thoughts. Now, I no longer obsess over whether I’m professional enough or if there’s a market for what I do. As long as I can create that "you get me" connection with a stranger through my words, that is the most meaningful form of expression for me.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, if a former colleague had just retired and was feeling anxious because they felt "useless," based on everything you've been through lately, what advice would you give them on how to handle that sense of nihilism?
qa_items.answer: Well... how can I put this? I know that feeling all too well. Last year, I spent many late nights crying like an absolute idiot because I felt like I was "no longer needed." But if I were to talk to him now, I’d tell him: don’t rush to prove your "social value," and don't let yourself be held hostage by data or titles. Real meaning is often hidden in those tiny connections—like a single remark you make at a book club that inspires someone, or an old item you share that sparks a moment of resonance. These moments of "being seen" are what’s truly real. We need to learn to embrace our anxiety; treat it like a friend who’s just reminding you, "Hey, this really matters to me." Then, try to find yourself again through these small acts of sharing.

evidence.statement: Beside the diary entry about "future dreams," Lin Xiaoyun wrote, "I don't know what dreams I have left," and then sat alone on the floor crying—a reflection of her utter disillusionment with her creative expression and the direction of her life.

In her sessions, Lin Xiaoyun redefined anxiety as something to coexist with rather than eliminate, stating that "while anxiety is still present, it is no longer overwhelming"—a sign of her maturing understanding of mental health.

But after talking with Professor Chen recently, I suddenly realized that the fragments of all these years aren't trash; they have an echo. When I realized that my ramblings could make people who also struggle with insomnia, who are also told they "think too much," feel less alone, it gave me a sense of groundedness that is truly different. I used to be obsessed with "my" freedom, but now, I want to leave something behind for "us."

But if you hadn't stopped then, and had instead forced yourself to churn out that collection of essays on schedule, do you think you would still find these creative activities "enjoyable" today?
qa_items.answer: Well... I think it definitely would have lost its essence. To be honest, Professor Chen’s remark about how "the best works need space" really saved me. If I had forced myself to push through all those proofs and deadlines, I’d probably be staring at a pile of manuscripts right now, feeling miserable—maybe even starting to hate writing altogether. Back then, I realized that I was far more captivated by the raw, immediate spark of ideas at the reading clubs than by the empty prestige of publishing a book. That’s a feeling no deadline can ever provide. So, I’m just glad I protected that little flame instead of letting it turn into another chore on my to-do list.

evidence.statement: Lin Xiaoyun decided to put her publishing plans on hold, reasoning that "I don't want to lose my love for these activities by overcommitting"—a reflection of her shift from pursuing more opportunities to protecting her mental health and creative passion.

To be honest, I might have already started to regret it. I was this close to signing a cross-over collaboration project to turn the ideas from my essay collection into a series of short-form video courses. If I hadn't pulled back then, I’d probably be spending every day now coordinating scripts with a film crew, agonizing over how to dumb down profound theories for a general audience. While that would have certainly brought more influence, that kind of fragmented dismantling just to chase traffic would make me feel like I was desecrating my own thoughts. The pure joy I find in them would have simply vanished.

To be honest, I was quite torn at the time. I had originally planned to send the first draft to a few senior colleagues for their input. Had I not stopped myself, I would probably be swamped right now, revising the outline point by point based on their feedback and trying to plug every theoretical loophole. While that might have made the collection more rigorous, I likely would have spiraled into a cycle of endless self-doubt—feeling that my thoughts were never quite "perfect" enough—and in the process, I would have lost the fluidity and intuition that sparked these words in the first place.

That fear of having to lay myself completely bare for others to see... it left me sitting there in the dark, unable to move.

qa_items.answer: To be honest, I’m starting to feel more and more that this so-called "inconsistency" is actually the most precious part. I used to strive for a kind of airtight professionalism, but everything I wrote ended up sounding like I was reciting a thesis—incredibly stiff. It wasn't until I spread all my notes from over the years across the floor and saw myself evolving from anger to reflection, constantly overturning and reshaping my own ideas, that I realized this "chaos of evolution" is the truest color of life. It’s just like what Professor Chen said: this is an "intellectual journey." If someone’s thoughts after fifteen years were exactly the same as they were fifteen years ago, now that would be a real shame.

evidence.statement: In the process of revising her essay, Lin Xiaoyun shifted from academic jargon to a more personal voice, ultimately retaining the expression: "In reading, I found the meaning of solitude, as well as the possibility of connecting with strangers." This reflects a transformation in her creative approach—a shift from pursuing "correctness" to pursuing "authenticity."

While organizing her work, Lin Xiaoyun took photos of her notes and materials scattered across the floor, describing the scene as "chaotic yet meaningful." This suggests a shift in how she evaluates her own creative process—moving from viewing "chaos" as a flaw to seeing it as a valuable and authentic state of creativity.

Lin Xiaoyun’s concern that putting these pieces together would make her "seem inconsistent" suggests she is judging her creative expression by external standards of consistency, rather than embracing the evolution and multifaceted nature of her own thoughts.

choices.text: To be honest, I used to be troubled by these inconsistencies as well, and I even considered deleting all those early, immature writings. It wasn't until I attended a private alumni seminar last year and heard a veteran editor share the concept of a "fluid perspective." He mentioned that the coherence of a work shouldn't be a static conclusion, but rather the growth of the author’s persona. This brought me a sense of complete closure. I decided to keep those contradictions and conflicts, as they represent the continuous expansion of my understanding of the world. Looking back now, these "cracks of growth" possess far more vitality than any deliberate rigor.

It was indeed a painful process. At one point, I locked myself in a library study room, trying to force the logic of these fifteen years into a coherent mind map. But as I looked through the handwritten letters from my readers, I realized that what moved them most were the "ramblings" I had written in moments of sheer frustration. I came to see that chasing logical perfection often comes at the expense of genuine emotional resonance. Rather than being a cold academic machine, I would rather embrace this imperfect evolution and view that "chaos" as a necessary detour on the path to truth.

Actually, while I was putting this together, I specifically sought advice from a therapist. I had been feeling that the way I’ve "changed" was a form of betrayal. My therapist told me that this inconsistency is actually an inevitable stage of "self-integration." Later, during a long conversation late one night, I realized that this chaos is actually a manifestation of courage—the courage to overturn past conclusions. Now, I no longer strive for that so-called seamlessness. Instead, I choose to organize these "fragments" into a collection, much like an artist assembling a mosaic. This "collaged truth" is what most honestly reflects the integrity of a creator.

qa_items.question: Sister Lin, overall, if a young editor who also pursues freedom but is currently mired in a sense of "uselessness" were to ask you for help, how would you advise her to measure the value of her work?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to be fixated on external metrics like "completion" and "impact," but now I feel that the act of participating and experiencing is a value in itself. I would tell her that those unfinished drafts or seemingly fruitless efforts are actually vivid traces of thought in one's life. It’s like what I eventually came to realize: creativity isn't about achieving some kind of perfection; it's about sincere sharing. As long as your words can resonate with another person, that sense of connection outweighs any grand narrative.

evidence.statement: Lin Xiaoyun began writing personal, unpublished reflections, noting that "perhaps perfection is not the goal of creativity; rather, it is participation and feeling that matter"—marking a fundamental shift in her understanding of the essence of creativity.

When asked "Why are you doing this?", Lin Xiaoyun initially wanted to say "to publish a book" or "to build influence," but she ultimately said "because I want to share these stories"—indicating that her motivation is shifting from being externally driven (market, recognition) to internally driven (a genuine desire to share).

Lin Xiaoyun added, "I'm not just writing for myself; I'm writing for those who think like me," indicating that she has transformed this creative project from a pursuit of personal self-fulfillment into a broader form of creative expression rooted in connection and sharing.

Confronted with the stark contrast between her youthful declaration, "I am going to change the publishing world," and her current self-questioning, "Am I still doing the same thing?", Lin Xiaoyun broke down in tears and asked desperate questions like, "What have I done? Where has my life gone?"

choices.text: Actually, I would suggest she keep a record of those "moments of rejection." I used to be terrified of being turned down by authors or having my story ideas rejected. But later, I realized that every killed proposal was actually helping me sharpen my intuition. For instance, I once spent six months tracking a topic that ultimately never made it to print. Although the final result was zero, the interviewing experience and the perspective on the world I gained during that process have become an internalized part of my professionalism—and that is far more important than any byline.

I believe the most important thing is to seek "authenticity in feedback." I would tell her not to wait for distant, grand praise, but rather to observe the specific notes a reader leaves in the margins, or to happen upon someone browsing a book she edited in a bookstore. That kind of immediate, real-world physical collision makes you realize that your work has truly entered someone else’s life. This concrete evidence, more than any traffic data, is what sustains you through those late nights of self-doubt.

Instead of searching for value solely within words, it’s better to reaffirm your existence as an individual through these tiny, controllable physical feedbacks.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I’ve noticed you’ve been spending a steady hour or two every day in your "project workspace" in the study. It reminds me of how lost you seemed right after you retired last year. How did you manage to turn things around and find this rhythm?
qa_items.answer: Speaking of which, I really have to say. When I first retired last year, it felt like I had fallen into a bottomless abyss. I would sit on the floor in the middle of the night, looking at the piles of old books and notes and just cry, feeling like every attempt I’d made in my life was a failure—I even started to doubt if I had any talent at all. It wasn't until a neighbor, Old Mr. Zhang, said something that snapped me out of it. He made me realize that these unfinished works aren't "trash," but rather the footprints of my thoughts. Now, I’ve gathered all those messy ideas onto a whiteboard. It’s still a jumble, but that sense of certainty—the feeling that "I am doing this"—has truly put my mind at ease.

evidence.statement: Beside a diary entry about "future dreams," Lin Xiaoyun wrote, "I don't know if I have any dreams left," and then sat alone on the floor crying—a reflection of her utter disillusionment with her creative expression and the direction of her life.

When Lin Xiaoyun said, "These books aren't trash; they are the records of my thoughts," she redefined the objects from "symbols of failure" into "symbols of value," reflecting a newfound validation of her own creative expression.

Lin Xiaoyun set up a dedicated "project workspace," using a whiteboard to track chapter outlines and progress, and established a daily routine of working for one to two hours. This demonstrates that she has transformed her publishing plan from a mere "idea" into a "daily practice," and from "sporadic interest" into a "systematic commitment."

Now, I’ve set a little goal for myself called the "Hundred People Interview" project; every afternoon, I head to the community garden right on time to interview an old neighbor. My technical skills are still a bit rough, but that sense of rhythm—having new material to process every single day—has truly pulled me out of that feeling of being lost and adrift.

I really have to thank that online writing camp Old Li recommended. Right after I retired, I felt like all the experience I’d built up over the years had become useless, and I was in a real slump. But then I met a group of like-minded "classmates" at the camp—all retirees. We started critiquing each other’s drafts and cheering each other on, and that’s when I realized I’ve still got it. Now, I make it a point to sit in my study for two hours every single day, without fail, just to finish my assignments and peer reviews. Having this kind of structured environment with constant feedback has helped me find that sense of being needed again. Life doesn't feel like a total blank anymore.

I’ve now converted half of my study into a succulent lab, where I observe the growth of each leaf every day and keep detailed logs. qa_items.question: Remember how this time last year, you wanted to toss those old frames and notes out like trash, even calling them "proof of failure"? Now, you’re treating them like treasures and actually planning to publish them. What do you think has changed in the way you look at your past?
qa_items.answer: I’m ashamed to admit it, but back when I had just retired last year, I felt completely useless. I saw everything I’d done as a failure and a burden, and I actually went as far as stuffing all those notebooks into trash bags. But later, I tried spreading them all out across the living room floor and reading through them page by page. I realized then that those fragments were actually coherent; they recorded a living, breathing person who never stopped thinking. Now I feel that those imperfections and unfinished pieces aren't failures at all—they are the map of my mind, the marks of my true existence. That’s why I want to organize them into a collection of essays; it’s my way of giving an account of my decades of reflection.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I feel like your perspective on creation has changed so much over the past year. You used to be so fixated on whether your work was perfect or if anyone was watching, to the point where you’d even doubt your own self-worth because of it. But now, you seem to value the "participation and the experience" much more. How has this shift in mindset influenced your current creative state?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? I used to be held hostage by the need for "completeness" and the desire to feel "needed." I always felt that a work no one read was just a pile of scrap paper. But over the past year or so, I’ve slowly come to terms with it. Just as my mother used to say that a book’s vitality lies in the act of reading, the meaning of creation actually lies in my own participation and breathing in that very moment. Now, I no longer feel guilty about those half-finished drafts; instead, I feel that these imperfect traces are what make it feel alive. Realizing that I’m writing for people who "think like I do" has given me a sense of groundedness—a peace of mind I never experienced back when I was chasing some hollow sense of perfection.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I often wonder—if you hadn't put that question mark after the line "maybe I still have something to give" in your diary, but had simply believed in that possibility instead, do you think you would have emerged from that "strange calm" much sooner?
qa_items.answer: Well... you see, that question mark was actually my truest color back then. That night, as I was flipping through my old journals, I kept talking about using freedom to create connections, but deep down I was terrified. I felt like that hope was a thin thread that would snap at the slightest breeze. As for that "strange calm" that followed—to be honest, it was just a form of self-numbness that comes after completely giving up the struggle. If I had been bold enough to believe in myself then, maybe I wouldn't have fallen into that dangerous acceptance of "I’m just a loser anyway" and gone looking for evidence that "life is meaningless." But that’s just how life is; you have to be pulled back and forth by that uncertainty for a while before something real can slowly start to grow.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, if you hadn't decided to put that publishing project on hold last summer, but had instead forced yourself to rush the book out on schedule, do you think you’d still be able to feel that sense of "genuine connection" when you’re giving lectures and hosting book clubs now?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I feel like that sense of connection would have definitely lost its flavor. To be honest, if I had forced myself to grind through those deadlines and all that tedious proofreading, I probably would’ve reverted back to being that person who’s just anxious about ticking off tasks, with all my passion drained away. Take my lecture in August, for example—halfway through, I suddenly didn't want to read from the script anymore, so I just stopped and asked everyone what they thought. That spark in people's eyes at that moment was the most authentic thing. If my head had been filled with the pressure of publishing, how could I have ever been in the right headspace for that kind of spontaneous interaction? That raw, living feeling of everyone thinking together is, to me, far more precious than any sense of achievement printed on paper.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, after looking back through those old pieces of yours from over a decade ago, what is it that has caused you to feel such profound self-doubt and disappointment in who you are today?
qa_items.answer: Sigh… it’s a pretty gut-wrenching feeling. Looking back at the reviews I wrote in 2009, I realized my writing back then was so sharp and vigorous—every line was brimming with passion and that naive belief that literature could actually change the world. But look at me now. My head is filled with nothing but doubt and exhaustion. I keep thinking, if I were to publish these pieces as a book, what if everyone realizes I haven’t actually grown at all in these fifteen years? Or worse, what if they see that my current depth of thought doesn't even live up to the spirit I had back then? Wouldn't that just prove that a lifetime of accumulation has been nothing but self-deception?

evidence.statement: After rereading her old articles from 2009, Lin Xiaoyun felt a sense of nostalgia for her past fervor and disappointment with her present self—believing that she now has "only doubts" and has lost the "passion and innocence" of those years. I can't quite put my finger on it; perhaps it’s a kind of "linguistic atrophy." Looking back at those long, complex sentences from over a decade ago—so vibrant, sharp, and even tinged with poetry—and then looking at the dry, mechanical, bureaucratic commentary I churn out now, it feels like watching a once-spirited child slowly go bald and turn mediocre. I’ve realized that it’s not just my mindset that has changed; even my most basic ability to express myself has been ground away by the trivialities of life over these past ten-odd years. This realization—that my talent has utterly evaporated—is more soul-crushing than any failed publication could ever be.

What actually hurts the most is that sense of "disconnection." I realized that back in 2009, I was incredibly radical; every line I wrote was a critique of the old aesthetic system. Yet now, I’ve somehow become mild, even somewhat conservative. The other day, I sat there staring at my computer screen, thinking that if I were to force these articles—spanning over a decade—into a single collection, readers would surely feel I have no firm convictions at all, that I’m just a trend-chasing opportunist. This total negation of my own value system makes me feel like all the reading and learning I’ve done over the past ten-plus years has been for nothing.

Sigh, it all comes down to that seminar that never happened. Looking back at my old work, I realized that the prediction I made in 2009 about "online literature narrative" seems laughably naive now—a total slap in the face by the way the industry actually evolved. I always thought of myself as an observer at the forefront of the era, but those words are a living record of my shortsightedness. Now, being asked to bind these "pieces of evidence" into a book and send them to a publisher feels like nailing my most embarrassing failure to a pillar of shame for everyone to see. I truly can't face that self-righteous version of myself.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I’ve noticed that your recent writing has become so much more emotional. It’s no longer packed with those stiff academic terms like before. You used to care so much about sounding professional and being "correct"—what made you suddenly want to pursue this kind of "authentic expression"?
qa_items.answer: You caught me! To be honest, I used to feel like I had to hide behind rigorous terminology, as if that were the only way to seem professional. Even right after I retired last year, I’d look at my own photos and find them painfully mediocre, sinking deep into self-doubt. But through counseling and reflection, I’ve gradually realized that creativity isn’t about chasing perfection or meeting some standard—it’s about connection. Like I wrote in a recent article, I "found the meaning of solitude in reading." It might not sound academic, but when I see someone comment that I’ve captured exactly how they feel, that "moist" sensation of being understood makes me feel more grounded than any professional title ever could. I’ve come to believe that imperfect but authentic expression is actually far more powerful.

qa_items.question: After this journey from despair to rebirth, if a young editor—someone also facing retirement and drowning in self-doubt—were to ask you what "creativity" truly is, how would you explain it to her?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put this? I used to be held hostage by concepts like "completion" and "perfection," to the point where I’d cry in my diary, feeling like my life was an absolute failure. But now, I would tell her that creativity isn't actually about producing some earth-shattering masterpiece; it’s about truly seeing life itself. It’s not about how many academic terms are in your writing or how high your comment count is—it’s about the raw honesty of the moment you record it, and that late-night connection you make with a stranger through your words. As long as you are still feeling and still participating, this imperfect reality is the most vibrant form of creativity there is.

Lin Xiaoyun added, "I'm not just writing for myself; I'm writing for those who think like I do," indicating that she has transformed this creative project from a pursuit of personal self-fulfillment into a broader form of creative expression rooted in connection and sharing.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, I’ve noticed your recent writing has moved away from the academic jargon you used to rely on; it feels so much more heartfelt and authentic now. I remember right after you retired, you were always writing things like "I have no talent" or "it’s all meaningless." Have you developed a different perspective on what "creativity" really means?
qa_items.answer: You still remember that? It’s true, I was incredibly anxious right after I retired. I felt like if I wasn't writing some profound theory, I was somehow lacking, and I even went as far as berating myself in my diary for having no talent and being a fraud. But now I’ve realized that true creativity isn't about chasing "correctness" or "professionalism"—it’s about being real. Take the draft I’ve been revising lately: I stripped away all the academic jargon and left only the rawest, most direct feelings. Ironically, readers told me I’d captured exactly what they were thinking but couldn't put into words. That kind of connection, the kind that transcends the page, is so much more meaningful than any cold, clinical terminology.

qa_items.question: In short, you used to mock your old notes and photographs as "proof of failure" and even wanted to throw them all away. What made you decide to organize and publish them now? How did you come to terms with those imperfections and contradictions?
qa_items.answer: Well... how should I put it? I used to find those half-finished plans quite embarrassing—I even stuffed them into trash bags once. But later, as I sat on the floor looking at those dense pages of notes, it suddenly hit me: these imperfections are the most authentic traces of my life. Instead of chasing some fake consistency, I’d rather honestly show the evolution of my thoughts over the past fifteen years. Even if they seem a bit self-contradictory, they are proof that I’ve truly been thinking.

qa_items.question: Xiaoyun, based on your recent insights, if an old colleague who is also facing retirement anxiety and feeling like their talent has run dry came to you for advice, how would you use your journey "from despair to engagement" to guide them?
qa_items.answer: I would tell him not to rush to prove anything, but to first try and embrace this sense of "losing control." I used to be trapped in the fear of my "talent running dry," feeling as though no one needed my voice anymore. But later, I realized that creativity isn't about completing a perfect project; it's about authentic sharing and connection. I’d suggest he talk to different people—even if it’s something like the book clubs I host. Even if you only impact one person, that sense of connection will help you rediscover that your experiences and perspectives are still very much alive.

evidence.statement: In her sessions, Lin Xiaoyun redefined anxiety as something to coexist with rather than eliminate, noting that "while the anxiety is still there, it is no longer overwhelming"—a sign of her maturing understanding of mental health.

Lin Xiaoyun added, "I'm not just writing for myself; I'm writing for those who think like I do," indicating that she has transformed this creative project from a personal endeavor of self-fulfillment into a broader form of creative expression rooted in connection and sharing.

Lin Xiaoyun took the initiative to exchange contact information with the speaker, Ms. Li, saying, "Your story made me realize that perhaps change isn't impossible after all." This was the first time since her retirement that she had reached out for a potential connection and inspiration.
